<PAGE><|det|>title [391, 71, 589, 110]<|/det|>Preface
<|det|>text [70, 171, 892, 386]<|/det|>At its core, grammar is the set of rules in a language that changes the form of words and joins them into sentences. Grammar is now seen, not simply as theory, but as an instrument that enables language use. The Revised Cornerstone: Grammar and Composition Skills for 1-8 helps students to learn the nuances of a language which is not their mother tongue, and the rules of which therefore need to be reiterated for better retention. This series presents grammatical facts lucidly and informally, leads the student to assimilate them thoroughly, and more importantly, to apply them to communication in everyday life. In addition, this book provides young learners with ample opportunities to expand their dexterity in language use through the comprehension, composition and vocabulary components.
<|det|>text [71, 396, 897, 545]<|/det|>Each concept of grammar is introduced through detailed explanations and exercises which will help the students to grasp its functions. To make the learning of grammar an enjoyable process, we have introduced concepts through comic strips, stories and poems. This also acknowledges that grammar is not just a subject, the rules of which must merely be committed to memory, but also that which enhances language usage. The use of different stories, poems and informative pieces in exercises also ensures that children learn grammar in actual contexts of use.
<|det|>text [71, 553, 897, 814]<|/det|>The grammar chapters in these wonderfully illustrated books are interspersed with sections on vocabulary, composition, comprehension and conversation. The sections dealing with composition are designed to develop creative writing skills. To begin with, the learners are not only given the themes or subjects, but are also given suggestions along the lines of which the composition can be drafted. The comprehension passages test the literal and evaluative comprehension of the learners through an array of texts, fiction and non-fiction. The conversation sections begin with an example of a dialogue or a conversation on topics that are appropriate to the level of the class. Thereafter, topics are suggested around which further conversation practice can be carried out. This also ensures collaborative learning and group work. The vocabulary pages help students to extend their active and passive vocabulary by introducing them to new words through exercises and games. They are also encouraged to develop reference skills.
<|det|>text [71, 821, 899, 888]<|/det|>The teaching notes in the primary classes provide guidelines to both teachers and parents on how to introduce or revise concepts in a meaningful and interesting way and to carry out the activities that are given.
<|det|>text [73, 903, 289, 925]<|/det|>Rupidkha semugpta
<|det|>text [76, 927, 217, 946]<|/det|>Nupur Ghosh

<PAGE><|det|>title [54, 56, 372, 90]<|/det|>The Sentence
<|det|>page_number [876, 58, 907, 93]<|/det|>1
<|det|>image [53, 140, 108, 186]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [119, 149, 690, 172]<|/det|>Mark whether these statements are true or false.
<|det|>list [120, 183, 905, 392]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 183, 625, 209]<|/det|>☐ A sentence begins with a capital letter.
<|det|>text [121, 211, 903, 237]<|/det|>The words in a sentence are arranged in the alphabetical order.
<|det|>text [122, 239, 640, 264]<|/det|>☐ A sentence always ends with a full stop.
<|det|>text [122, 265, 521, 289]<|/det|>□ A question is also a sentence.
<|det|>text [122, 291, 780, 336]<|/det|>□ Sentences can end in a full stop, a question mark or an exclamation mark.
<|det|>text [122, 340, 821, 365]<|/det|>☐ The words in a sentence need not follow a logical order.
<|det|>text [122, 368, 668, 393]<|/det|>☐ A sentence may or may not contain a verb.
<|det|>image [55, 409, 108, 448]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 406, 885, 453]<|/det|>You have learnt that a group of words that makes complete sense is called a sentence. A sentence always begins with a capital letter
<|det|>text [54, 454, 810, 498]<|/det|>and ends with a full stop, a question mark or an exclamation mark. A sentence must always contain a verb.
<|det|>title [54, 515, 345, 538]<|/det|>Subject and Predicate
<|det|>text [54, 545, 823, 569]<|/det|>Every sentence consists of two parts—the subject and the predicate.
<|det|>text [53, 587, 531, 678]<|/det|>The part which names the person or thing we are talking about is called the subject of the sentence. The subject usually comes at the beginning of the sentence.
<|det|>text [52, 716, 541, 810]<|/det|>The part which tells us more about the person or thing, or what they do, forms the predicate of a sentence. The verb is always a part of the predicate.
<|det|>image [55, 838, 88, 864]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [125, 840, 867, 864]<|/det|>Circle the subject and underline the predicate in each sentence.
<|det|>list [126, 873, 634, 940]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [126, 873, 634, 896]<|/det|>1. The train reached the station at 9 p.m.
<|det|>text [126, 897, 491, 918]<|/det|>2. What a lazy boy you are!
<|det|>text [126, 919, 612, 940]<|/det|>3. The roses in the garden smell sweet.
<|det|>image [720, 592, 774, 630]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [598, 639, 875, 699]<|/det|>The subject, however, need not always come at the beginning of the sentence. For example,
<|det|>list [624, 700, 874, 760]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [625, 700, 858, 718]<|/det|>- Down dropped the sail.
<|det|>text [625, 720, 874, 739]<|/det|>• How beautiful the rose is!
<|det|>text [625, 741, 856, 760]<|/det|>Did you see my watch?
<|det|>image [820, 930, 884, 964]<|/det|>
<PAGE><|det|>text [171, 48, 693, 71]<|/det|>4. You are required to attend the meeting.
<|det|>text [171, 72, 920, 94]<|/det|>5. Alexander the Great marched into India with a large army.
<|det|>text [171, 95, 832, 117]<|/det|>6. The lazy boys loitered in the corridor of the school.
<|det|>text [171, 119, 719, 141]<|/det|>7. Across the forest path, lay a huge python.
<|det|>text [171, 142, 880, 163]<|/det|>8. Socrates was a Greek philosopher who died in 339 BCE.
<|det|>text [171, 165, 926, 210]<|/det|>9. The children, happy to be allowed into the garden, danced about.
<|det|>list [171, 48, 926, 210]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [94, 230, 150, 273]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [158, 227, 942, 275]<|/det|>Sometimes, the subject of a sentence can consist of more than one word. For example,
<|det|>text [97, 282, 620, 306]<|/det|>- Luv and his friend are jogging in the park.
<|det|>text [97, 307, 699, 331]<|/det|>- Books, magazines and newspapers are sold here.
<|det|>list [97, 282, 699, 331]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 345, 957, 416]<|/det|>In some sentences, the subject may consist of several words, but there may be one word which is more important than the other words. This is called the subject-word. The subject-word is always a noun, or a group of
<|det|>text [93, 417, 532, 555]<|/det|>words that does the work of a noun. The subject is usually qualified by an adjective or a group of words that does the work of an adjective. This adjective or adjective phrase is called the attribute. For example,
<|det|>list [98, 556, 536, 625]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 556, 500, 576]<|/det|>• The warm weather will not last.
<|det|>text [98, 577, 536, 625]<|/det|>- The house in the woods belongs to my uncle.
<|det|>title [589, 439, 645, 461]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [731, 426, 804, 468]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [582, 468, 890, 550]<|/det|>The predicate, like the subject, may also consist of several words. When the predicate consists of a single word, it is always a verb. For example,
<|det|>list [584, 551, 721, 592]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [584, 551, 721, 570]<|/det|>- Dogs bark.
<|det|>text [585, 572, 721, 591]<|/det|>- Bees sting.
<|det|>text [94, 634, 848, 682]<|/det|>Here, the words that are underlined are the subject-words and the highlighted words are the attributes.
<|det|>image [65, 694, 147, 741]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [174, 697, 858, 743]<|/det|>Identify the subject-word and the attribute in each of these sentences.
<|det|>list [174, 751, 858, 933]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [175, 751, 760, 774]<|/det|>1. The captain of the football team has resigned.
<|det|>text [175, 775, 486, 796]<|/det|>2. My father is a doctor.
<|det|>text [175, 798, 725, 818]<|/det|>3. The writer Ruskin Bond lives in Dehradun.
<|det|>text [175, 821, 736, 842]<|/det|>4. The hot and humid weather is affecting me.
<|det|>text [175, 844, 857, 865]<|/det|>5. The priceless diamond of the queen is in the museum.
<|det|>text [176, 867, 682, 888]<|/det|>6. The cunning vazir tricked the emperor.
<|det|>text [176, 889, 711, 911]<|/det|>7. The leaping flames engulfed the building.
<|det|>text [176, 911, 813, 932]<|/det|>8. Printed notes will be available at the school office.
<|det|>page_number [135, 943, 153, 958]<|/det|>2
<PAGE><|det|>text [135, 45, 856, 68]<|/det|>9. A girl carrying a basket of flowers walked into the room.
<|det|>text [130, 69, 646, 91]<|/det|>10. A confident man will always succeed.
<|det|>text [130, 92, 670, 115]<|/det|>11. The patient man explained the problem.
<|det|>text [130, 116, 737, 139]<|/det|>12. The geography teacher entered the classroom.
<|det|>list [130, 45, 856, 139]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [50, 157, 304, 178]<|/det|>Kinds of Sentences
<|det|>text [48, 185, 811, 209]<|/det|>A group of words that makes complete sense is called a sentence.
<|det|>text [50, 210, 658, 231]<|/det|>Sentences are of four kinds. Let us learn about them.
<|det|>text [49, 248, 306, 269]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>text [50, 271, 345, 293]<|/det|>- Wolves live in forests.
<|det|>text [51, 295, 374, 316]<|/det|>• The sun rises in the east.
<|det|>list [50, 271, 374, 316]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [48, 337, 855, 385]<|/det|>These sentences state or declare something. They are called assertive sentences and they end with a full stop (.)
<|det|>text [49, 411, 901, 435]<|/det|>Assertive sentences can be affirmative (positive) or negative. For example,
<|det|>text [51, 436, 424, 457]<|/det|>• It's warm today. (affirmative)
<|det|>text [52, 458, 438, 481]<|/det|>• It's not warm today. (negative)
<|det|>list [51, 436, 438, 481]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [65, 504, 111, 533]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [75, 506, 143, 531]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [336, 500, 383, 533]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [73, 545, 621, 565]<|/det|>Sentences that contain not or no are called negative sentences.
<|det|>text [51, 594, 371, 615]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>text [52, 617, 286, 638]<|/det|>- Do you like tea?
<|det|>text [52, 639, 358, 660]<|/det|>• Who will do the work?
<|det|>list [52, 617, 358, 660]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [50, 677, 871, 723]<|/det|>These sentences ask questions and end with a question mark (?). Such sentences are called interrogative sentences.
<|det|>text [51, 739, 371, 760]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>text [52, 761, 514, 783]<|/det|>- Leave the room immediately. (order)
<|det|>text [52, 784, 537, 806]<|/det|>- Please lend me some money. (request)
<|det|>text [52, 807, 589, 830]<|/det|>• Study hard if you want to succeed. (advice)
<|det|>list [52, 761, 589, 830]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [50, 852, 885, 898]<|/det|>These sentences give orders, commands or advice, or make requests and are called imperative sentences.
<|det|>page_number [850, 933, 868, 950]<|/det|>3
<PAGE><|det|>text [87, 45, 378, 66]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [89, 69, 436, 116]<|/det|>

<|det|>text [85, 133, 834, 183]<|/det|>These sentences express strong or sudden feelings and are called exclamatory sentences. They end in an exclamation mark (!).

<|det|>text [160, 363, 495, 388]<|/det|>2. Please, do sit down.
<|det|>text [160, 393, 441, 416]<|/det|>3. What can I do? T

<PAGE><|det|>title [92, 35, 127, 55]<|/det|>H
<|det|>text [174, 31, 970, 350]<|/det|>Rewrite this passage inserting capital letters, full stops, interrogation marks and exclamatory marks wherever required, while we were walking together along the river, I asked him to tell me some stories the good man suddenly became animated, transfigured, eloquent almost poetical in his heart there was one great passion—the river he had many memories of the life of the people associated with the river flowing beside us he told me about the fishermen and the sailors were all these stories true how fantastic they were I listened with rapt attention I saw the moon come up in the eastern sky how enchanting it looked I did not care to go home what was I waiting to hear frankly I do not know who was this man who had cast a spell upon me what was his name I dared not ask
<|det|>text [171, 381, 970, 500]<|/det|>There are 16 sentences in the above passage. Now that you have put in the appropriate punctuation marks and capital letters, you know that there are various kinds of sentences in the passage. Number the sentences from 1 to 16 and identify them as affirmative, negative, exclamatory, interrogative or imperative.
<|det|>image [202, 522, 914, 900]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [135, 933, 153, 950]<|/det|>6
<PAGE><|det|>title [45, 55, 367, 88]<|/det|>The First Bus
<|det|>header [722, 0, 999, 142]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>text [42, 161, 870, 208]<|/det|>Here is an interesting story about how the villagers of Bhoganpur got their first means of state transport.
<|det|>text [42, 221, 892, 336]<|/det|>An entire village lines up by a nearly non-existent road to welcome its first bus service. Excitement is so high that a man who hasn't left his house for years due to illness, makes an effort to stir out just to witness this historic occasion. Women throng the route to garland the driver and the conductor.
<|det|>text [45, 350, 818, 398]<|/det|>This isn't a clip from pre-independent India. This is the village of Bhoganpur, near Hubli. The date is 7 November 2008.
<|det|>text [45, 410, 908, 503]<|/det|>When the first bus service from the North West Karnataka Road Transport Corporation rolled into Bhoganpur, the villagers' joy knew no bounds. Never mind that it came sixty-one years after India became independent and weeks after India sent an independent mission to the moon.
<|det|>text [47, 515, 892, 631]<|/det|>Over three hundred people gathered at the village bus stop, all geared to clamber aboard the 11:20 a.m. bus. In a few moments, it was packed. Others sat on the top or clung to the ladder. Hanumanthappa Basavarajappa Bhoganpur—the ailing gentleman—came to see the bus aided by his friends.
<|det|>text [47, 644, 895, 828]<|/det|>Earlier, the village was connected by a market-day jeep service that ran once a week. In 1977, the construction of a 2.7 km road connecting Bhoganpur to the nearest city began but its completion got delayed due to various factors. To get daily service between Bhoganpur and Hublí via Navalgund, the villagers resorted to the ultimate weapon available to dispossessed Indians—boycott. They boycotted the last assembly elections as their demand for a public transport facility was falling on deaf ears.
<|det|>text [48, 841, 844, 912]<|/det|>They prevented candidates from campaigning in the village. The local MLA Shankar Patil Munenkoppa, however, dared to meet the villagers and promised to look into their demands if they voted.
<|det|>page_number [864, 930, 883, 948]<|/det|>7
<PAGE><|det|>text [92, 32, 977, 85]<|/det|>Sometimes, promises are indeed kept as the thrilled villagers have discovered.
<|det|>text [92, 97, 980, 217]<|/det|>MLA Munenkoppa, who flagged off the service, was moved: 'I have participated in several inaugurations but have never witnessed such public enthusiasm.' Now, Bhoganpur has more cause to be thrilled—he has promised to build a road at a cost of forty lakh rupees by using the MLA fund.
<|det|>title [93, 240, 470, 265]<|/det|>A. Answer these questions.
<|det|>list [172, 272, 963, 442]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [173, 272, 963, 345]<|/det|>1. Why was there so much excitement on a non-existent road? When did the first bus service start from Bhoganpur and where did it go?
<|det|>text [172, 346, 852, 369]<|/det|>2. Who was the ailing gentleman and what did he do?
<|det|>text [173, 370, 898, 393]<|/det|>3. What did the villagers resort to as an ultimate weapon?
<|det|>text [173, 394, 963, 416]<|/det|>4. Who dared to meet the villagers and what was the promise?
<|det|>text [174, 417, 860, 441]<|/det|>5. Who flagged off the service and why was he moved?

<|det|>text [174, 565, 871, 587]<|/det|>3. The excited villagers have discovered ____.
<|det|>text [174, 588, 844, 609]<|/det|>4. By using the MLA fund, Munenkoppa has promised to
<|det|>title [93, 657, 598, 679]<|/det|>C. Mark the sentences as true or false.

<|det|>text [174, 685, 878, 732]<|/det|>1. The excitement for the first bus service was at a village named Bhoganpur near Hoogli.
<|det|>text [174, 733, 730, 755]<|/det|>2. Bhoganpur is a small village in Karnataka.
<|det|>text [174, 755, 951, 800]<|/det|>3. The construction of a 7.2 km-road connecting Bhoganpur to the nearest city started in 1977.

<PAGE><|det|>title [55, 53, 212, 85]<|/det|>Nouns
<|det|>page_number [879, 51, 917, 88]<|/det|>2
<|det|>image [55, 157, 118, 206]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [127, 158, 885, 207]<|/det|>You have learnt that a noun is a word that names a person, place, animal, action, thing, quality or feeling.
<|det|>title [55, 236, 262, 258]<|/det|>Kinds of Nouns
<|det|>text [55, 267, 562, 290]<|/det|>Nouns are usually classified into four kinds.
<|det|>list [55, 291, 884, 474]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [55, 291, 809, 337]<|/det|>◆ A proper noun denotes a particular person, place or thing. For example, Italy, Mount Everest, Mohit, Taj Mahal.
<|det|>text [55, 337, 815, 383]<|/det|>◆ A common noun denotes a person, place or thing in general. For example, city, mountain, boy, monument.
<|det|>text [55, 383, 884, 428]<|/det|>◆ A collective noun denotes a collection of people, animals or things. For example, army, herd, bunch.
<|det|>text [55, 429, 847, 474]<|/det|>An abstract noun denotes a quality, an action or a state of being. For example, wisdom, honesty, wickedness.
<|det|>text [57, 506, 851, 552]<|/det|>There is a fifth kind of noun which names the material of which things are made.
<|det|>text [55, 574, 844, 622]<|/det|>A material noun denotes the matter or substance of which things are made. For example, jute, copper, paper.
<|det|>title [80, 666, 150, 691]<|/det|>Note.
<|det|>text [80, 703, 834, 764]<|/det|>Proper nouns, collective nouns, abstract nouns and material nouns can also be used as common nouns. They are then used in the plural number or have an article placed before them.
<|det|>list [81, 766, 625, 850]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [81, 766, 625, 806]<|/det|>• Mother Teresa served the poor of India. (proper noun) She is the Mother Teresa of my country. (common noun)
<|det|>text [81, 808, 567, 850]<|/det|>- Death is a great leveller. (abstract noun)
The media reported many deaths. (common noun)
<|det|>page_number [856, 932, 874, 949]<|/det|>9
<PAGE><|det|>image [104, 42, 153, 66]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [189, 40, 917, 91]<|/det|>Pick out the nouns in the passage and classify them. Place the nouns under the categories given below.
<|det|>text [186, 99, 957, 240]<|/det|>I visited Nainital when I was eight years old. We put up at a hotel by the lake. The beauty of the surrounding hills left a lasting impression on me. I distinctly remember the tiny boats that used to ply tourists and the shops selling delicious pastries. The Indian army had a camp nearby and every evening, officers in smart uniforms could be seen strolling about.
<|det|>text [186, 240, 970, 384]<|/det|>Every evening, young ladies draped in saris from Kancheepuram and children wrapped in woollens would come and sit on the benches that lined the lakeside. A batch of students from a nearby residential college was often spotted. My mother purchased a bangle from a silversmith and a few bronze statuettes. The kindness of the local people and the beauty of nature made the trip to Nainital memorable.
<|det|>text [230, 406, 943, 430]<|/det|>Proper Common Collective Abstract Material
<|det|>image [114, 580, 182, 631]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [186, 577, 925, 600]<|/det|>You have learnt that abstract nouns can be formed from adjectives
<|det|>text [189, 601, 860, 650]<|/det|>and verbs as well as from common nouns by adding suffixes. For example,
<|det|>text [114, 654, 585, 678]<|/det|>- sweet—sweetness (from an adjective)
<|det|>text [114, 679, 525, 700]<|/det|>- move—movement (from a verb)
<|det|>text [114, 702, 421, 747]<|/det|>- boy—boyhood (from a common noun)
<|det|>list [114, 654, 585, 747]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [622, 648, 687, 671]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [760, 640, 811, 678]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [620, 685, 934, 750]<|/det|>Some other suffixes that are used to form abstract nouns are -sion, -al, -ship, -dom, -th, -tion and -cy.
<|det|>text [114, 784, 701, 808]<|/det|>8. Now, form abstract nouns from these words.
<|det|>text [194, 819, 331, 842]<|/det|>1. captain
<|det|>text [414, 819, 521, 842]<|/det|>2. deep
<|det|>text [616, 818, 717, 839]<|/det|>3. idiot
<|det|>text [815, 818, 925, 838]<|/det|>4. bore
<|det|>text [195, 842, 296, 864]<|/det|>5. high
<|det|>text [414, 842, 519, 862]<|/det|>6. false
<|det|>text [616, 841, 720, 861]<|/det|>7. child
<|det|>text [815, 841, 960, 861]<|/det|>8. deceive
<|det|>text [195, 865, 329, 886]<|/det|>9. dismiss
<|det|>text [404, 865, 534, 886]<|/det|>10. cheap
<|det|>text [602, 864, 714, 887]<|/det|>11. king
<|det|>text [801, 863, 937, 882]<|/det|>12. friend
<|det|>page_number [150, 937, 179, 954]<|/det|>10
<PAGE><|det|>header [39, 41, 92, 89]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>text [113, 35, 888, 128]<|/det|>You know that a collective noun is used to denote several persons or things regarded as one group. Sometimes, collective nouns can be considered as singular or plural, depending on the context. For example,
<|det|>list [70, 142, 884, 214]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [70, 142, 880, 166]<|/det|>- The jury agrees that the accused is guilty. (Jury acts as a unit here.)
<|det|>text [71, 166, 884, 214]<|/det|>- The jury disagree that the accused is guilty. (Jury acts as a unit, but the people in the jury are acting as individuals.)
<|det|>title [115, 242, 616, 266]<|/det|>C. What do these collective nouns describe?
<|det|>list [117, 275, 907, 351]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 275, 245, 299]<|/det|>1. pack
<|det|>text [318, 277, 437, 299]<|/det|>2. shoal
<|det|>text [599, 277, 715, 299]<|/det|>3. sheaf
<|det|>text [775, 277, 886, 299]<|/det|>4. bevy
<|det|>text [119, 303, 231, 326]<|/det|>5. gang
<|det|>text [319, 303, 427, 325]<|/det|>6. mob
<|det|>text [599, 303, 711, 325]<|/det|>7. stack
<|det|>text [775, 303, 884, 325]<|/det|>8. suite
<|det|>text [119, 327, 245, 349]<|/det|>9. clump
<|det|>text [305, 327, 528, 351]<|/det|>10. congregation
<|det|>text [582, 327, 692, 349]<|/det|>11. den
<|det|>text [765, 327, 905, 351]<|/det|>12. colony
<|det|>title [115, 385, 840, 431]<|/det|>D. Fill in the blanks with suitable collective nouns. Choose from those provided within brackets.
<|det|>list [139, 443, 895, 902]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 443, 829, 490]<|/det|>1. An ____ of poems by Rilke is on display. (library, anthology, auction)
<|det|>text [139, 490, 854, 535]<|/det|>2. A ____ of policemen entered the premises. (panel, posse, band)
<|det|>text [140, 535, 871, 580]<|/det|>3. The ____ sang beautifully during the Sunday mass at the Church. (company, coterie, choir)
<|det|>text [140, 580, 871, 626]<|/det|>4. The ____ were unwilling to leave port as the ship needed repairs. (captain, crew, convoy)
<|det|>text [140, 626, 895, 671]<|/det|>5. The ____ waited expectantly for the performer to arrive. (society, audience, horde)
<|det|>text [140, 671, 897, 717]<|/det|>6. The ____ of trees at that corner will have to be cut down. (clutch, clump, truss)
<|det|>text [140, 717, 895, 763]<|/det|>7. The ____ gave its verdict and the criminal was sent to jail. (judge, jury, board)
<|det|>text [140, 763, 836, 808]<|/det|>8. A ____ of musicians arrived for the show. (band, troop, soiree)
<|det|>text [140, 808, 819, 856]<|/det|>9. A ____ of robbers attacked them on the highway. (group, gang, galaxy)
<|det|>text [127, 856, 888, 901]<|/det|>10. Place the ____ of eggs in this basket. (cluster, clutch, brood)
<|det|>page_number [848, 924, 876, 941]<|/det|>11
<PAGE><|det|>title [112, 46, 212, 67]<|/det|>Gender
<|det|>text [111, 75, 915, 100]<|/det|>Nouns may be in the masculine, feminine, neuter or common gender.
<|det|>text [112, 130, 856, 179]<|/det|>A noun is said to be in the masculine gender if it refers to a male. For example, man, tiger, uncle.
<|det|>text [112, 217, 880, 266]<|/det|>A noun is said to be in the feminine gender if it refers to a female. For example, woman, tigress, aunt.
<|det|>text [117, 299, 946, 371]<|/det|>A noun is said to be in the neuter gender if it refers to things that are neither male nor female. Nouns referring to non-living things are of the neuter gender. For example, stone, grass, sand.
<|det|>text [119, 410, 893, 458]<|/det|>A noun is said to be in the common gender if what it refers to can be a male or a female. For example, student, orphan, baby.
<|det|>title [145, 497, 207, 521]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [512, 490, 561, 525]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 535, 925, 594]<|/det|>Objects associated with strength and power are sometimes regarded as male. For example, Sun, Death. Objects associated with beauty and gentleness are usually regarded as female. For example, Nature, Earth.
<|det|>title [115, 650, 920, 696]<|/det|>E: Identify the gender of the highlighted nouns. In case the noun is masculine or feminine, name its opposite too.
<|det|>list [194, 705, 948, 885]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [195, 705, 948, 729]<|/det|>1. The jury declared its verdict and the criminal was sent to jail.
<|det|>text [195, 729, 809, 749]<|/det|>2. The hunter aimed and shot at a doe but missed it.
<|det|>text [195, 750, 780, 773]<|/det|>3. He is a businessman while his wife is a lawyer.
<|det|>text [195, 773, 780, 795]<|/det|>4. The emperor asked the lady to speak her mind.
<|det|>text [195, 795, 773, 818]<|/det|>5. The prince arrived with his retinue of servants.
<|det|>text [194, 818, 948, 841]<|/det|>6. My uncle has come from America with his wife and a cousin.
<|det|>text [194, 841, 938, 885]<|/det|>7. The viceroy threw a party in honour of the duchess who was visiting India with her child.
<|det|>page_number [145, 927, 180, 948]<|/det|>12
<PAGE><|det|>text [138, 45, 896, 88]<|/det|>8. This successful author is an orphan who was brought up by his teacher.
<|det|>text [138, 91, 918, 136]<|/det|>9. My great-aunt waded through soft mud and got into the boat in which her husband and her friend were already seated.
<|det|>text [129, 137, 918, 183]<|/det|>10. The hero of the film walked up to the director, who was sitting on the grass, and said that he needed to meet a friend.
<|det|>list [129, 45, 918, 183]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [47, 204, 172, 225]<|/det|>Number
<|det|>text [46, 233, 770, 259]<|/det|>In English, a noun may either be singular or plural in number.
<|det|>text [45, 280, 886, 328]<|/det|>When we speak about one person, one animal or one thing, we use the noun in the singular form. For example, boy, tiger, box.
<|det|>text [45, 361, 862, 409]<|/det|>When we speak about two or more people, animals or things, we use the noun in the plural form. For example, boys, tigers, boxes.
<|det|>text [46, 426, 858, 473]<|/det|>Singular nouns can be changed to plural in various ways. Look at this table and revise the rules that you have studied before.
<|det|>title [305, 499, 359, 520]<|/det|>Rule
<|det|>text [65, 537, 452, 563]<|/det|>by adding -s to the singular form
<|det|>text [65, 598, 543, 643]<|/det|>by adding -es to nouns ending in -x, -ch, -s, -sh
<|det|>text [65, 704, 560, 752]<|/det|>in nouns ending in -y, by changing -y into -i and adding -es
<|det|>text [65, 763, 539, 813]<|/det|>in nouns ending in -y but preceded by a vowel, by adding s
<|det|>title [631, 499, 870, 523]<|/det|>Singular Plural
<|det|>text [604, 538, 848, 580]<|/det|>garden
stone

watch
gas

<|det|>title [172, 759, 955, 784]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the plural forms of the nouns in the brackets.
<|det|>text [174, 794, 797, 816]<|/det|>1. The ____ (buffalo) sauntered away.
<|det|>text [174, 817, 885, 861]<|/det|>2. The ____ (dormouse) scampered off at the sound of ____ (footstep).
<|det|>page_number [142, 932, 172, 948]<|/det|>14
<PAGE><|det|>text [131, 51, 815, 98]<|/det|>3. We were stung by ____ (mosquito) and ____ (wasp).
<|det|>text [132, 98, 822, 166]<|/det|>4. The ____ (child) as well as the ____ (woman) of the ____ (colony) walked to the mayor's office.
<|det|>text [132, 167, 903, 214]<|/det|>5. The ____ (deer) ran into the forest as they were being chased by ____ (wolf).
<|det|>text [132, 214, 777, 284]<|/det|>6. The ____ (goose) wandered into the ____ (pond) where some ____ (calf) were bathing.
<|det|>text [132, 288, 871, 333]<|/det|>7. The ____ (elf) stitched the ____ (shoe) in record time.
<|det|>text [132, 335, 590, 429]<|/det|>8. The ____ (footman) ran down the ____ (stair) to stop the ____ (carriage).
<|det|>image [596, 330, 900, 445]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [60, 441, 114, 485]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [134, 453, 472, 475]<|/det|>Now, read some more rules.
<|det|>text [60, 496, 490, 567]<|/det|>◆ Compound nouns generally form their plurals by adding -s to the main noun. For example,
<|det|>text [117, 567, 474, 588]<|/det|>- father-in-law—fathers-in-law
<|det|>text [117, 589, 485, 612]<|/det|>- step-daughter—stepdaughters
<|det|>text [60, 617, 493, 687]<|/det|>◆ Some words are generally used in the singular form. For example, poetry, information, furniture,
<|det|>list [60, 496, 493, 687]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 687, 848, 710]<|/det|>scenery, mathematics, politics, advice, machinery, alms, innings.
<|det|>text [60, 710, 888, 776]<|/det|>◆ Nouns that describe things having two similar parts are considered to be plural nouns. For example, jeans, glasses, tongs, socks, trousers, scissors.
<|det|>text [60, 778, 892, 824]<|/det|>Some nouns denoting a specific quantity or amount are considered to be singular. For example, three dozen apples, two hundred miles.
<|det|>text [60, 824, 901, 893]<|/det|>Some words in the English language have been borrowed from Latin. Some nouns of Latin origin that end in -us form their plurals by adding -es. For example, circuses, campuses, viruses.
<|det|>list [60, 710, 901, 893]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [599, 485, 648, 515]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [704, 485, 754, 515]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [845, 936, 872, 953]<|/det|>15
<PAGE><|det|>text [124, 46, 951, 120]<|/det|>- In the table below, there are some more Latin words that are used in the English language. Observe the pattern which they follow when changed from singular to plural.
<|det|>table [126, 131, 964, 519]<|/det|><table><tr><td colspan="2">Singular</td><td>Plural</td></tr><tr><td>larva</td><td></td><td>larvae</td></tr><tr><td>bacterium</td><td></td><td>bacteria</td></tr><tr><td>axis</td><td></td><td>axes</td></tr><tr><td>curriculum</td><td></td><td>curricula</td></tr><tr><td>criterion</td><td></td><td>criteria</td></tr><tr><td>basis</td><td></td><td>bases</td></tr><tr><td>alumnus</td><td></td><td>alumni</td></tr><tr><td>focus</td><td></td><td>foci</td></tr><tr><td>phenomenon</td><td></td><td>phenomena</td></tr><tr><td>stimulus</td><td></td><td>stimuli</td></tr><tr><td>oasis</td><td></td><td>oases</td></tr></table>
<|det|>text [122, 538, 938, 586]<|/det|>- Some Latin nouns have two plural forms, one English and one Latin. For example,
<|det|>table [124, 598, 964, 907]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Singular</td><td>English plural</td><td>Latin plural</td></tr><tr><td>cactus</td><td>cactuses</td><td>cacti</td></tr><tr><td>radius</td><td>radiuses</td><td>radii</td></tr><tr><td>hippopotamus</td><td>hippopotamuses</td><td>hippopotami</td></tr><tr><td>stadium</td><td>stadiums</td><td>stadia</td></tr><tr><td>syllabus</td><td>syllabuses</td><td>syllabi</td></tr><tr><td>index</td><td>indexes</td><td>indices</td></tr><tr><td>antenna</td><td>antennas</td><td>antennae</td></tr><tr><td>millennium</td><td>millenniums</td><td>millennia</td></tr><tr><td>gymnasium</td><td>gymnasiums</td><td>gymnasia</td></tr></table>
<|det|>page_number [152, 943, 183, 960]<|/det|>16
<PAGE><|det|>text [45, 44, 898, 91]<|/det|>- Some words of French origin too have both English plurals and French plurals. For example,
<|det|>table [50, 100, 905, 224]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Singular</td><td>English plural</td><td>French plural</td></tr><tr><td>bureau</td><td>bureaus</td><td>bureaux</td></tr><tr><td>plateau</td><td>plateaus</td><td>plateaux</td></tr><tr><td>chateau</td><td>chateaus</td><td>chateaux</td></tr></table>
<|det|>title [45, 268, 867, 293]<|/det|>G. Correct the errors in these sentences. Make necessary changes.
<|det|>list [141, 300, 903, 568]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [142, 301, 551, 323]<|/det|>1. Our planet rotates on its axes.
<|det|>text [141, 324, 558, 346]<|/det|>2. I need some advices from you.
<|det|>text [142, 347, 648, 369]<|/det|>3. The beggar asked the emperor for alm.
<|det|>text [142, 370, 648, 392]<|/det|>4. The Chinese are hardworking peoples.
<|det|>text [143, 393, 657, 414]<|/det|>5. Measles are a highly infectious disease.
<|det|>text [143, 415, 807, 437]<|/det|>6. The beautiful scenarios was captured by the camera.
<|det|>text [144, 438, 752, 458]<|/det|>7. Ten thousands mens marched to the Town Hall.
<|det|>text [143, 460, 862, 482]<|/det|>8. A numbers of crisis has crippled the official machineries.
<|det|>text [143, 483, 900, 526]<|/det|>9. We need to gather more informations regarding the poetries by Wordsworth.
<|det|>text [134, 527, 886, 573]<|/det|>10. The phenomena of day and night occurs because the earth moves around the sun constantly.
<|det|>image [110, 592, 871, 905]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [867, 930, 898, 948]<|/det|>17
<PAGE><|det|>image [19, 0, 313, 145]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [535, 48, 966, 90]<|/det|>Paragraph Writing
<|det|>image [118, 159, 177, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [190, 156, 840, 205]<|/det|>You are already familiar with a paragraph. Let us quickly recapitulate the characteristics of a good paragraph.
<|det|>list [117, 219, 930, 405]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 219, 528, 242]<|/det|>• A paragraph has one basic idea.
<|det|>text [117, 242, 871, 286]<|/det|>- The sentence which contains the basic idea is called the topic sentence.
<|det|>text [117, 288, 928, 313]<|/det|>- The topic sentence usually comes at the beginning of a paragraph.
<|det|>text [118, 313, 841, 359]<|/det|>- The topic sentence provides the base on which to build the paragraph.
<|det|>text [118, 359, 930, 405]<|/det|>- Each paragraph has unity. Very often this is provided by the way in which sentences stand together and illustrate the main idea.
<|det|>title [117, 420, 460, 445]<|/det|>Let's examine this paragraph.
<|det|>text [116, 456, 968, 660]<|/det|>My curiosity about birds dates back to a long time. In my childhood, we had a pet mynah at home and I had taught it to pronounce over a hundred Bengali words. I believed then that the birds did not really know the meaning of what they only mechanically repeated. But I had to revise my opinion as my mynah did something amazing. One afternoon, as I was about to sit for lunch, the mynah started screeching 'Earthquake! Earthquake!' We felt nothing, but next morning, there was a report in the paper about a mild tremor. Ever since, I have maintained an interest in birds.
<|det|>image [119, 677, 177, 725]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 676, 915, 744]<|/det|>Here, the topic sentence is at the beginning of the paragraph—'My curiosity about birds dates back to a long time.' The other sentences illustrate the writer's interest in birds.
<|det|>text [116, 759, 958, 806]<|/det|>The sentences in a paragraph are interrelated. Each sentence prepares the ground for the next. A linkage is provided—
<|det|>text [118, 817, 922, 862]<|/det|>- by theme—the sentences exemplify the basic idea contained in the topic sentences.
<|det|>page_number [149, 928, 177, 946]<|/det|>18
<PAGE><|det|>text [50, 49, 482, 163]<|/det|>- by linkers like I, we, but. The pronouns refer to something that has been discussed whereas the conjunctions emphasise thematic connections.
<|det|>title [567, 63, 636, 90]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [714, 58, 768, 93]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [564, 102, 882, 163]<|/det|>The sentences must follow a logical sequence to make the paragraph a coherent one!
<|det|>title [45, 206, 842, 276]<|/det|>A. Describe the picture in a single paragraph of about 80–100 words. Give a title to it. The following clues may help you develop the paragraph.
<|det|>list [127, 285, 525, 513]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [127, 285, 480, 331]<|/det|>- Where is the action taking place?
<|det|>text [127, 333, 523, 401]<|/det|>- How many people do you see in the picture? What are they doing?
<|det|>text [128, 403, 485, 426]<|/det|>- Describe the surroundings.
<|det|>text [128, 427, 800, 449]<|/det|>• Is there a place similar to this in your neighbourhood?
<|det|>text [128, 450, 487, 470]<|/det|>• Do you visit it? How often?
<|det|>text [127, 472, 890, 514]<|/det|>- Would you like to be any of the people in the picture? Why/Why not?
<|det|>title [567, 277, 636, 304]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [564, 315, 892, 399]<|/det|>Never start the paragraph with a sentence like, 'In this picture, I see...'. Remember, the picture is a mere guideline!
<|det|>image [85, 528, 891, 888]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [853, 935, 884, 953]<|/det|>19
<PAGE><|det|>text [130, 53, 942, 101]<|/det|>B. In your notebook, attempt to write paragraphs of about 100 words, following the guidelines given below.
<|det|>text [205, 111, 567, 135]<|/det|>1. Complete the paragraph—
<|det|>text [272, 135, 921, 179]<|/det|>It was 10 o'clock at night. I looked out of the window and noticed a shadow on the wall outside...
<|det|>text [202, 181, 893, 230]<|/det|>2. Here are some newspaper headlines. Expand each into a paragraph.
<|det|>text [377, 250, 942, 278]<|/det|>The great blaze that destroyed the heart of the city
<|det|>text [283, 317, 705, 341]<|/det|>Thousands stranded due to heavy rains
<|det|>text [536, 379, 938, 405]<|/det|>School children decide to go green!
<|det|>text [203, 418, 905, 485]<|/det|>3. Amit is going to represent his school at an Environmental Summit to be held in Germany. His programme is given below.
<|det|>list [273, 488, 852, 600]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [273, 488, 660, 510]<|/det|>• Departure by Lufthansa : 15 July
<|det|>text [274, 511, 650, 531]<|/det|>• Destination : Berlin
<|det|>text [274, 534, 655, 556]<|/det|>• Duration of stay : 7 days
<|det|>text [274, 557, 850, 599]<|/det|>- Purpose : Indian ambassador at the Environmental Summit
<|det|>text [270, 602, 920, 647]<|/det|>Using the above information, write a paragraph describing Amit's itinerary.
<|det|>text [205, 648, 926, 740]<|/det|>4. Imagine a Royal Bengal Tiger speaking to you about the marked decline of the tiger population and the reasons for it. Narrate in a paragraph what you heard and describe how you felt on hearing it.
<|det|>image [384, 756, 667, 910]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [167, 937, 197, 953]<|/det|>20
<PAGE><|det|>title [63, 55, 291, 85]<|/det|>Pronouns
<|det|>header [821, 14, 986, 144]<|/det|>3
<|det|>image [62, 147, 125, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [134, 147, 796, 193]<|/det|>You have learnt that words that take the place of nouns are called pronouns.
<|det|>text [61, 208, 417, 230]<|/det|>Pronouns are of different kinds.
<|det|>list [61, 233, 386, 346]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [62, 233, 322, 255]<|/det|>◆ personal pronouns
<|det|>text [62, 256, 341, 277]<|/det|>◆ possessive pronouns
<|det|>text [62, 278, 384, 300]<|/det|>◆ demonstrative pronouns
<|det|>text [62, 301, 366, 323]<|/det|>◆ interrogative pronouns
<|det|>text [62, 324, 321, 346]<|/det|>◆ reflexive pronouns
<|det|>list [474, 233, 774, 325]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [474, 233, 774, 255]<|/det|>◆ emphasising pronouns
<|det|>text [475, 256, 740, 277]<|/det|>◆ indefinite pronouns
<|det|>text [475, 278, 752, 300]<|/det|>◆ distributive pronouns
<|det|>text [475, 301, 713, 323]<|/det|>relative pronouns
<|det|>text [62, 361, 285, 382]<|/det|>Read this sentence.
<|det|>list [61, 385, 884, 456]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 385, 811, 406]<|/det|>- Mohit is an athlete. He practises regularly. The coach likes him.
<|det|>text [61, 407, 884, 456]<|/det|>In the above sentence, he and him take the place of Mohit. These pronouns take the place of persons or things.
<|det|>text [62, 488, 545, 535]<|/det|>Pronouns that take the place of people and things are called personal pronouns.
<|det|>text [62, 573, 580, 596]<|/det|>We use personal pronouns depending upon—
<|det|>list [64, 597, 562, 667]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [64, 597, 562, 619]<|/det|>◆ number—singular or plural. For example,
<|det|>text [111, 620, 534, 667]<|/det|>- The children went to a party. They enjoyed it.
<|det|>text [64, 675, 622, 699]<|/det|>◆ gender—male, female or neuter. For example,
<|det|>list [111, 699, 701, 745]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 699, 639, 722]<|/det|>• Mahima is in the pool. She loves swimming.
<|det|>text [111, 722, 701, 745]<|/det|>- Tanish is a good boy. He always obeys his parents.
<|det|>text [64, 755, 838, 779]<|/det|>◆ person—first person, second person or third person. For example,
<|det|>list [111, 780, 534, 825]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 780, 534, 801]<|/det|>• I told you not to tell her the secret.
<|det|>text [111, 802, 513, 825]<|/det|>• They agreed to meet us at 8 a.m.
<|det|>text [64, 835, 531, 858]<|/det|>◆ case—subject or object. For example,
<|det|>list [111, 858, 717, 904]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 858, 717, 881]<|/det|>- Gaurav is a good student. He is also good in sports.
<|det|>text [111, 882, 424, 904]<|/det|>• Jayant gave me the book.
<|det|>page_number [834, 933, 864, 951]<|/det|>21
<PAGE><|det|>title [156, 69, 220, 95]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [532, 60, 578, 98]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [151, 109, 370, 128]<|/det|>The pronoun, it is used—
<|det|>list [151, 130, 677, 256]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [151, 130, 470, 150]<|/det|>• for non-living things. For example,
<|det|>text [189, 152, 517, 171]<|/det|>• Here is your knife. Keep it carefully.
<|det|>text [151, 173, 553, 193]<|/det|>• to refer to an earlier statement. For example,
<|det|>text [189, 195, 458, 212]<|/det|>• He must leave. He knows it.
<|det|>text [151, 215, 676, 235]<|/det|>• to emphasise a noun or pronoun that follows it. For example,
<|det|>text [189, 237, 508, 257]<|/det|>• It was I who promised to help you.
<|det|>text [129, 302, 432, 323]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [131, 326, 430, 370]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 326, 378, 346]<|/det|>• This chair is mine.
<|det|>text [131, 349, 430, 369]<|/det|>• Those books are theirs.
<|det|>text [129, 372, 743, 396]<|/det|>The highlighted pronouns show possession or belonging.
<|det|>text [128, 428, 860, 451]<|/det|>Pronouns that show possession are known as possessive pronouns.
<|det|>text [128, 488, 430, 509]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [129, 511, 611, 604]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 511, 436, 533]<|/det|>• This is a beautiful dress.
<|det|>text [130, 535, 441, 556]<|/det|>• Those are ripe mangoes.
<|det|>text [128, 557, 611, 603]<|/det|>Pronouns like this, that, those and these are used to point out certain objects.
<|det|>text [128, 627, 560, 696]<|/det|>Pronouns that are used to point out the objects which they refer to are known as demonstrative pronouns.
<|det|>text [129, 731, 424, 751]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [129, 753, 644, 800]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 753, 644, 775]<|/det|>• What would you like to have—tea or coffee?
<|det|>text [131, 776, 547, 798]<|/det|>• Which is the way to the university?
<|det|>text [129, 799, 648, 845]<|/det|>Pronouns like what, who and which are used to ask questions.
<|det|>text [130, 871, 599, 916]<|/det|>Pronouns that are used to ask questions are called interrogative pronouns.
<|det|>title [698, 496, 761, 521]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [768, 495, 816, 526]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [696, 539, 924, 643]<|/det|>This and these are used to refer to things that are close by. That and those are used to refer to things that are far away.
<|det|>title [699, 762, 761, 787]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [768, 760, 816, 792]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [696, 801, 927, 905]<|/det|>Who is used to refer to persons. Which is used to refer to persons or things. What is used only for things.
<|det|>page_number [159, 950, 190, 966]<|/det|>22
<PAGE><|det|>text [55, 43, 281, 64]<|/det|>Read this sentence.
<|det|>list [57, 67, 500, 158]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [57, 67, 292, 87]<|/det|>• He hurt himself.
<|det|>text [57, 90, 334, 110]<|/det|>• The cat licked itself.
<|det|>text [55, 113, 500, 158]<|/det|>The highlighted pronouns tell us that the subject is the receiver of the action.
<|det|>text [55, 186, 474, 280]<|/det|>The highlighted pronouns are called reflexive pronouns because the action of the subject reflects upon the subject itself.
<|det|>title [540, 55, 602, 81]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [687, 49, 741, 86]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [535, 95, 886, 159]<|/det|>The reflexive pronoun is used when the subject and the object of the verb refer to the same person or thing.
<|det|>text [535, 160, 852, 200]<|/det|>A reflexive pronoun can never be the subject of a sentence.
<|det|>list [537, 202, 877, 285]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [537, 202, 877, 242]<|/det|>- My sister and myself went to Kolkata. (incorrect)
<|det|>text [537, 242, 839, 285]<|/det|>• My sister and I went to Kolkata.
(correct)
<|det|>text [56, 312, 372, 333]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [58, 336, 497, 382]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [58, 336, 497, 358]<|/det|>• He himself should finish the work.
<|det|>text [59, 360, 386, 382]<|/det|>• I myself closed the door.
<|det|>text [57, 383, 611, 407]<|/det|>The highlighted pronouns are used for emphasis.
<|det|>text [56, 439, 865, 486]<|/det|>Pronouns that are used for the sake of emphasis are called emphasising pronouns.
<|det|>title [58, 527, 890, 576]<|/det|>A. Fill in the blanks with pronouns that refer to the highlighted word/group of words.
<|det|>list [131, 581, 896, 838]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 584, 893, 606]<|/det|>1. The man left behind two jackets. Where are ____?
<|det|>text [135, 608, 803, 654]<|/det|>2. Mr Rahim is my teacher. ____ teaches us geography.
<|det|>text [135, 655, 891, 698]<|/det|>3. The kittens are playing in the sun. ____ are three months old.
<|det|>text [134, 700, 870, 723]<|/det|>4. Mr Biswas, how are ____ feeling this morning?
<|det|>text [135, 725, 870, 767]<|/det|>5. I will meet you at the station. ____ can discuss the matter there.
<|det|>text [134, 770, 882, 793]<|/det|>6. Jamal will be attending the conference by ____.
<|det|>text [135, 794, 769, 815]<|/det|>7. Here is your axe. Keep ____ carefully.
<|det|>text [134, 816, 894, 837]<|/det|>8. Mr Das is our accountant. You could consult ____.
<|det|>page_number [838, 932, 868, 949]<|/det|>23
<PAGE><|det|>title [98, 17, 143, 40]<|/det|>B.
<|det|>text [175, 16, 972, 67]<|/det|>Tick the correct option and mark whether the pronoun you have chosen is a reflexive pronoun (RP) or an emphasising pronoun (EP).
<|det|>list [179, 77, 974, 328]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 77, 783, 102]<|/det|>1. Jaya himself/herself/yourself locked the door.
<|det|>text [180, 105, 888, 130]<|/det|>2. Did you do the homework yourself/herself/ourselves?
<|det|>text [180, 135, 857, 159]<|/det|>3. I yourself/myself/themselves made the reservations.
<|det|>text [180, 164, 752, 188]<|/det|>4. I cut my hair ourselves/myself/themselves.
<|det|>text [180, 193, 867, 217]<|/det|>5. She looked at itself/herself/themselves in the mirror.
<|det|>text [180, 222, 797, 247]<|/det|>6. You express himself/herself/yourself very well.
<|det|>text [180, 250, 901, 275]<|/det|>7. They themselves/ourselves/yourself started the fight first.
<|det|>text [180, 279, 842, 327]<|/det|>8. Roshni doesn't care about anyone. She only thinks about himself/herself/themselves.
<|det|>image [117, 345, 177, 390]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [190, 355, 500, 378]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [123, 394, 606, 440]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [123, 394, 540, 416]<|/det|>- Somebody has left the door open.
<|det|>text [124, 418, 606, 440]<|/det|>• Few escaped punishment that morning.
<|det|>text [123, 440, 942, 465]<|/det|>These highlighted pronouns do not refer to any specific person or thing.
<|det|>text [119, 487, 951, 536]<|/det|>Pronouns that refer to some person or thing in a general way and do not refer to any person in particular are called indefinite pronouns.
<|det|>text [117, 573, 550, 709]<|/det|>The other indefinite pronouns are—all, another, any, anybody, anyone, anything, each, everybody, everyone, everything, few, many, nobody, none, one, several, some, somebody, someone.
<|det|>title [580, 589, 641, 614]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [743, 582, 788, 616]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [577, 625, 925, 667]<|/det|>The indefinite pronoun one, if used in a sentence should be used throughout.
<|det|>text [580, 668, 940, 686]<|/det|>• One must never abandon one's duties.
<|det|>text [114, 760, 965, 808]<|/det|>C. There are errors in each of these sentences. Rewrite the sentences correctly.
<|det|>list [196, 818, 655, 891]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [196, 818, 525, 841]<|/det|>1. They blamed themselves.
<|det|>text [196, 843, 520, 864]<|/det|>2. I am shorter than him.
<|det|>text [196, 866, 653, 890]<|/det|>3. Which is the news that you bring?
<|det|>page_number [145, 925, 177, 943]<|/det|>24
<PAGE><|det|>text [140, 45, 652, 66]<|/det|>4. It was him who should have protested.
<|det|>text [141, 68, 743, 89]<|/det|>5. I and Ram have been chosen to lead the team.
<|det|>text [141, 91, 604, 113]<|/det|>6. Which is the purpose of this plan?
<|det|>text [141, 114, 653, 135]<|/det|>7. She and me are to blame for this mess.
<|det|>text [141, 137, 664, 182]<|/det|>8. The members of the jury was divided in its opinion.
<|det|>text [141, 183, 621, 226]<|/det|>9. Between you and I, there is no truth in this.
<|det|>text [132, 228, 609, 274]<|/det|>10. One must be careful about what he says.
<|det|>text [132, 276, 562, 322]<|/det|>11. Anybody was there to save the drowning child.
<|det|>text [132, 323, 595, 368]<|/det|>12. My uncle asked my sister and I to accompany him.
<|det|>text [132, 369, 687, 391]<|/det|>13. My aunt and myself decided to see a film.
<|det|>text [132, 392, 775, 438]<|/det|>14. Any of the children are ready to participate in the programme tomorrow.
<|det|>text [132, 438, 808, 460]<|/det|>15. Our players were better prepared than that of China.
<|det|>text [132, 461, 860, 507]<|/det|>16. The money has been stolen by anybody who entered the room when we were away.
<|det|>list [132, 45, 808, 507]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [682, 107, 907, 368]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [52, 535, 370, 556]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [53, 558, 567, 678]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [53, 558, 542, 581]<|/det|>• Each of us must contribute generously.
<|det|>text [53, 582, 542, 603]<|/det|>• Either of you can take down the notes.
<|det|>text [53, 605, 560, 627]<|/det|>• Neither of them attended the ceremony.
<|det|>text [53, 628, 567, 671]<|/det|>- Either of the two students may be sent to the competition.
<|det|>text [53, 673, 534, 716]<|/det|>- Neither of the coats is suitable for the occasion.
<|det|>title [631, 536, 698, 563]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [727, 530, 777, 568]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [629, 575, 902, 699]<|/det|>Either and neither can be used only when speaking about two persons or things. Either means one or the other of two. Neither means not one nor the other of two.
<|det|>text [50, 728, 920, 772]<|/det|>The pronouns each, either and neither refer to persons or things one at a time.
<|det|>text [51, 797, 900, 845]<|/det|>Pronouns that refer to nouns one at a time are called distributive pronouns. Distributive pronouns are always in the singular and take singular verbs.
<|det|>page_number [845, 933, 876, 950]<|/det|>25
<PAGE><|det|>text [125, 47, 411, 67]<|/det|>Now, read this sentence.
<|det|>text [126, 67, 938, 93]<|/det|>- The boy who you were talking to is the captain of the cricket team.
<|det|>text [123, 110, 957, 202]<|/det|>In this sentence, who is a relative pronoun that refers to the noun boy that comes before. The noun is thus the antecedent of the pronoun. The relative pronoun should ideally be placed close to its antecedent in the sentence.
<|det|>title [150, 244, 212, 269]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [536, 235, 582, 273]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [145, 282, 919, 365]<|/det|>Generally, who is used while referring to people, which for non-living things or animals and that is used for people and things. As the relative pronoun refers to a noun or a pronoun, it must be of the same number and person as its antecedent. It is the verb that shows the number and person of the relative pronoun.
<|det|>text [146, 367, 617, 386]<|/det|>Sometimes the relative pronoun is omitted. For example,
<|det|>text [147, 388, 558, 408]<|/det|>• This is the house (that) my grandfather built.
<|det|>title [125, 455, 885, 501]<|/det|>D. Fill in the blanks with an appropriate distributive or relative pronoun.
<|det|>text [217, 510, 848, 533]<|/det|>1. ____ of the girls was given a certificate.
<|det|>text [217, 534, 848, 556]<|/det|>2. This is the best film ____ he ever made.
<|det|>text [217, 557, 955, 579]<|/det|>3. This is the man ____ has three sons in the army.
<|det|>text [217, 580, 945, 625]<|/det|>4. The crowd ____ had gathered near the market, grew restive.
<|det|>text [217, 626, 801, 648]<|/det|>5. They ____ are gifted do well in life.
<|det|>text [217, 649, 933, 693]<|/det|>6. The boy ____ was elected captain of the team has resigned.
<|det|>text [217, 694, 822, 716]<|/det|>7. ____ of the roads will be empty now.
<|det|>text [217, 717, 908, 759]<|/det|>8. The tornado ____ struck the town destroyed several houses.
<|det|>text [217, 761, 860, 785]<|/det|>9. I hate people ____ throw stones at dogs.
<|det|>text [205, 786, 888, 828]<|/det|>10. These are the boys ____ were called to his office.
<|det|>text [205, 831, 860, 853]<|/det|>11. The good ____ men do, lives after them.
<|det|>text [205, 855, 805, 877]<|/det|>12. The answer ____ I gave is incorrect.
<|det|>text [205, 878, 860, 900]<|/det|>13. ____ of the programmes may be chosen.
<|det|>text [205, 901, 960, 922]<|/det|>14. This is the teacher about ____ we were speaking.
<|det|>page_number [159, 933, 186, 949]<|/det|>26
<PAGE><|det|>title [40, 68, 87, 90]<|/det|>E.
<|det|>text [113, 66, 867, 111]<|/det|>Join these sets of sentences with relative pronouns. One is done for you.
<|det|>list [113, 118, 891, 330]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 121, 807, 165]<|/det|>1. The boy arrived late. He was scolded by his teacher. The boy who arrived late was scolded by his teacher.
<|det|>text [116, 167, 805, 190]<|/det|>2. The tiger was pursuing a buck. The buck was injured.
<|det|>text [117, 191, 607, 213]<|/det|>3. Bring me the purse. It is on the bed.
<|det|>text [117, 214, 657, 237]<|/det|>4. This is the prize. He won it for his essay.
<|det|>text [117, 238, 847, 259]<|/det|>5. This is the man. We were saved by his presence of mind.
<|det|>text [117, 260, 885, 283]<|/det|>6. Unfortunately, I missed the bus. It was to take me to Shimla.
<|det|>text [117, 284, 892, 306]<|/det|>7. Here is the surgeon. He will operate on my friend tomorrow.
<|det|>text [117, 307, 813, 330]<|/det|>8. Let us visit the old market. It was set up by the British.
<|det|>image [102, 351, 858, 613]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [845, 933, 876, 951]<|/det|>27
<PAGE><|det|>header [19, 0, 252, 146]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [616, 46, 974, 85]<|/det|>Seeking Advice
<|det|>image [120, 160, 182, 208]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [197, 157, 905, 205]<|/det|>Read the conversation between a teacher and a student who wants to know how to prepare for the board exams.
<|det|>text [120, 218, 884, 263]<|/det|>Vrinda: Good afternoon, ma'am. May I speak to you for a few minutes?
<|det|>text [122, 264, 744, 287]<|/det|>Mrs Das: Good afternoon, Vrinda. What is it about?
<|det|>text [122, 288, 963, 357]<|/det|>Vrinda: Ma'am, since the study leave is beginning from tomorrow and we won't be coming to school anymore, I wanted some suggestions about how to study for the board exams.
<|det|>text [127, 357, 928, 402]<|/det|>Mrs Das: Yes, studying in a disciplined manner before the exams is very important. Have you made a timetable for yourself?
<|det|>text [128, 402, 611, 424]<|/det|>Vrinda: No, ma'am. How do I do that?
<|det|>text [129, 425, 963, 558]<|/det|>Mrs Das: It's better to wake up early because starting bright and early is always a good idea. In the morning, you can begin with a difficult subject, say, physics. Study the relevant topics till 8:30 or so. Then, have breakfast. After breakfast, for the next one-and-a-half-hours, solve some of the questions from the chapters that you have studied earlier.
<|det|>text [128, 559, 744, 581]<|/det|>Vrinda: But ma'am, our exams are for three hours.
<|det|>text [127, 582, 943, 784]<|/det|>Mrs Das: Well, yes. But since you will be studying a number of subjects every day and then working out their questions, it would be best if you answered questions from a section of the sample papers. You should time yourself to see that you can finish answering in time. After that, move on to another subject, say, geography and study that till lunch time. But after lunch, don't fall asleep immediately. Solve questions from the geography question papers for the next hour and a half.
<|det|>text [120, 786, 905, 810]<|/det|>Vrinda: But why, ma'am? Anyway, I feel very sleepy after lunch.
<|det|>text [120, 811, 965, 877]<|/det|>Mrs Das: A nap right after lunch makes one very lethargic for the rest of the day. So, solve some questions first. It can be a revision of what you've studied earlier. Then take a break.
<|det|>text [120, 880, 585, 903]<|/det|>Vrinda: How long can the break be?
<|det|>page_number [148, 927, 182, 946]<|/det|>28
<PAGE><|det|>text [58, 48, 907, 136]<|/det|>Mrs Das: Well, about an hour or so. Once your batteries are recharged, start by solving a maths paper. Vrinda, you're a little weak in maths. So, you should try to solve an entire paper every day in three hours.
<|det|>text [58, 139, 847, 185]<|/det|>Vrinda: But that way, I'll finish solving all the sample papers long before the board exams begin!
<|det|>text [58, 186, 906, 370]<|/det|>Mrs Das: Well, that is never a problem. Once you finish solving them once, solve them again. Practise makes perfect, you know! After doing maths, study something light. Maybe English or history. Since you love both subjects, you'll enjoy doing that till it's time for dinner. After dinner, move on to another subject. But be sure to finish by 11 o'clock. Don't stay up after that as you'll have to get up early the next day and proper sleep is very important.
<|det|>text [58, 371, 804, 416]<|/det|>Vrinda: How many chapters from each subject should I cover every day?
<|det|>text [58, 417, 881, 555]<|/det|>Mrs Das: Well, try to cover three to four chapters each day. But you don't have to stick to this order of studying each day. Say, if you begin with physics on Monday, you can begin with biology on Tuesday and chemistry on Wednesday. Don't try to cover all the subjects each day, spread it out between two days, but you must do some maths every day.
<|det|>text [58, 556, 856, 600]<|/det|>Vrinda: Well, thank you very much, ma'am. Your suggestions have been invaluable and will help me a great deal.
<|det|>text [58, 601, 895, 668]<|/det|>Mrs Das: I'm sure they will, Vrinda. You are a sincere student and if you follow this timetable rigorously, then I am confident that you will do well in your exams. All the best and study hard.
<|det|>text [58, 670, 414, 692]<|/det|>Vrinda: Thank you, ma'am.
<|det|>title [54, 724, 600, 748]<|/det|>Now, work in pairs and enact these situations.
<|det|>text [55, 757, 910, 848]<|/det|>1. Satish has an important football match coming up. But his final exams are also around the corner. He approaches his football coach for suggestions. The coach advises him on how best he can divide his time between these two activities.
<|det|>text [54, 850, 905, 920]<|/det|>2. Meera wants to apply for a scholarship so that she can continue her college education. She talks to the college counsellor to find out more about the scholarship.
<|det|>page_number [851, 935, 884, 952]<|/det|>29
<PAGE><|det|>header [111, 38, 152, 78]<|/det|>4
<|det|>header [710, 40, 967, 82]<|/det|>Adjectives
<|det|>image [108, 153, 164, 203]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 152, 917, 224]<|/det|>Words that are used to describe a noun or a pronoun are called adjectives. Adjectives help us to know more about nouns and pronouns by expressing their qualities.
<|det|>title [108, 242, 361, 266]<|/det|>Kinds of Adjectives
<|det|>text [108, 273, 616, 297]<|/det|>There are six kinds of adjectives. They are—
<|det|>list [108, 297, 401, 365]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [109, 297, 386, 319]<|/det|>◆ adjectives of quality
<|det|>text [110, 320, 397, 342]<|/det|>◆ adjectives of quantity
<|det|>text [110, 343, 395, 365]<|/det|>◆ adjectives of number
<|det|>list [559, 297, 895, 365]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [559, 297, 895, 319]<|/det|>◆ demonstrative adjectives
<|det|>text [560, 320, 872, 342]<|/det|>◆ interrogative adjectives
<|det|>text [560, 343, 872, 365]<|/det|>◆ emphasising adjectives
<|det|>text [108, 381, 361, 402]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [110, 405, 397, 450]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 405, 397, 426]<|/det|>• He is a brave soldier.
<|det|>text [110, 428, 377, 450]<|/det|>• Mana is a kind girl.
<|det|>text [108, 451, 967, 474]<|/det|>The highlighted adjectives tell us what kind of noun is being spoken about.
<|det|>text [107, 498, 915, 525]<|/det|>Adjectives of quality express the kind or quality of a person or a thing.
<|det|>text [107, 549, 421, 571]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [108, 573, 577, 617]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 573, 577, 595]<|/det|>• I need some money to buy stationery.
<|det|>text [110, 596, 550, 616]<|/det|>• There is not much ink in the bottle.
<|det|>text [108, 618, 825, 642]<|/det|>The highlighted adjectives tell us how much of a thing is meant.
<|det|>text [107, 668, 781, 694]<|/det|>Adjectives of quantity tell us how much of a thing is meant.
<|det|>text [108, 724, 384, 744]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [108, 747, 548, 791]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 747, 483, 769]<|/det|>• This box contains ten apples.
<|det|>text [110, 770, 546, 791]<|/det|>• Today is the last day of the festival.
<|det|>text [107, 793, 958, 816]<|/det|>The highlighted adjectives answer the question how many or in what order.
<|det|>text [104, 836, 935, 884]<|/det|>Adjectives of number tell us how many people or things are meant or in what order a person or a thing stands.
<|det|>page_number [138, 937, 169, 953]<|/det|>30
<PAGE><|det|>text [55, 42, 525, 65]<|/det|>Adjectives of number are of three kinds.
<|det|>text [55, 66, 867, 110]<|/det|>- Definite numeral adjectives tell us the exact number of people or things. They are of two kinds—
<|det|>text [103, 112, 914, 158]<|/det|>- cardinals state the number of people or things. For example, forty thieves, twenty-five years.
<|det|>text [103, 158, 907, 203]<|/det|>- ordinals give the order of people or things in a series. For example, first man on the moon, last day of the week.
<|det|>text [55, 213, 844, 261]<|/det|>- Indefinite numeral adjectives do not indicate the exact number. For example, few children, many people.
<|det|>text [55, 269, 829, 293]<|/det|>- Distributive numeral adjectives refer to each one of a number.
<|det|>list [103, 294, 694, 341]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [103, 294, 593, 317]<|/det|>• Every person must attend the meeting.
<|det|>text [103, 317, 694, 341]<|/det|>• Each girl should participate in the competition.
<|det|>title [85, 369, 149, 394]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [478, 366, 526, 400]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [80, 408, 856, 451]<|/det|>Some adjectives can be classified as adjectives of number as well as adjectives of quantity depending on their usage in a sentence. Look at the examples given below.
<|det|>title [160, 465, 359, 487]<|/det|>Adjective of quantity
<|det|>text [102, 493, 324, 514]<|/det|>He has lost all his money.
<|det|>text [102, 521, 327, 542]<|/det|>He did not drink any water.
<|det|>title [572, 466, 756, 487]<|/det|>Adjective of number
<|det|>text [446, 493, 754, 514]<|/det|>All participants must submit a letter.
<|det|>text [446, 521, 733, 542]<|/det|>Are there any charts in the book?
<|det|>text [55, 581, 345, 601]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [57, 603, 586, 648]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [57, 603, 378, 625]<|/det|>• This book is expensive.
<|det|>text [57, 626, 585, 648]<|/det|>• These apples are from Himachal Pradesh.
<|det|>text [55, 669, 838, 693]<|/det|>Demonstrative adjectives point out which person or thing is meant.
<|det|>text [55, 717, 378, 737]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [57, 740, 480, 784]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [57, 740, 480, 763]<|/det|>• Which pen do you want to buy?
<|det|>text [57, 764, 354, 784]<|/det|>• Whose house is that?
<|det|>text [54, 785, 905, 831]<|/det|>The highlighted adjectives are used to ask questions about the nouns that follow them.
<|det|>text [54, 847, 816, 895]<|/det|>Words like what, which and whose when used with nouns to ask questions are called interrogative adjectives.
<|det|>page_number [859, 928, 889, 946]<|/det|>31
<PAGE><|det|>text [94, 42, 447, 64]<|/det|>Take a look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [99, 67, 617, 113]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [99, 67, 590, 89]<|/det|>• The campaign was a complete success.
<|det|>text [99, 90, 617, 112]<|/det|>• This is the very dress that I wanted to buy.
<|det|>text [99, 112, 958, 137]<|/det|>The highlighted adjectives are used to lay emphasis on the nouns that follow.
<|det|>text [100, 154, 926, 207]<|/det|>The adjectives that we use to emphasise what we want to say are called emphasising adjectives.
<|det|>title [125, 250, 190, 275]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>list [149, 284, 907, 682]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 284, 907, 305]<|/det|>- Some and any are used in affirmative and negative sentences, respectively. For example,
<|det|>text [180, 307, 429, 326]<|/det|>• I have bought some pens.
<|det|>text [181, 328, 473, 347]<|/det|>• I have not purchased any pens.
<|det|>text [150, 347, 871, 368]<|/det|>• Little is used with uncountable nouns and few with countable nouns. For example,
<|det|>text [181, 370, 475, 389]<|/det|>• There is little hope of recovery.
<|det|>text [181, 390, 648, 408]<|/det|>• There were a few chairs scattered around the room.
<|det|>text [150, 410, 695, 430]<|/det|>• Few means not many, while a few means some. For example,
<|det|>text [181, 432, 565, 450]<|/det|>• Sometimes, a few birds perch on this tree.
<|det|>text [181, 452, 506, 471]<|/det|>• Few students were at school today.
<|det|>text [150, 471, 893, 510]<|/det|>- Older and oldest indicate age and are used to compare people or things. Elder and eldest are used with reference to family members. For example,
<|det|>text [181, 512, 501, 531]<|/det|>• My eldest brother will arrive today.
<|det|>text [181, 532, 453, 551]<|/det|>• Jagriti is older than Mahesh.
<|det|>text [181, 552, 527, 572]<|/det|>• This is the oldest house in the colony.
<|det|>text [150, 573, 724, 592]<|/det|>• Later and latest denote time and latter and last denote position.
<|det|>text [181, 593, 576, 612]<|/det|>• I am rather busy now and will see you later.
<|det|>text [181, 613, 468, 633]<|/det|>• Which is the latest magazine?
<|det|>text [181, 634, 456, 653]<|/det|>• Go and sit on the last bench.
<|det|>text [180, 654, 924, 675]<|/det|>• Jaya and Rajeev are both good swimmers, but the latter is a more sporting loser.
<|det|>text [150, 676, 630, 695]<|/det|>• Nearest denotes distance and next denotes position.
<|det|>text [181, 696, 488, 716]<|/det|>• How far is the nearest bus stop?
<|det|>text [181, 717, 450, 736]<|/det|>• Sita's house is next to mine.
<|det|>title [180, 782, 701, 805]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the correct alternative.
<|det|>text [181, 816, 752, 839]<|/det|>1. ____ men are shy. (many/much)
<|det|>text [181, 840, 761, 862]<|/det|>2. I like ____ sweets. (Turk/Turkish)
<|det|>text [181, 863, 800, 886]<|/det|>3. He has ____ money. (enough/much)
<|det|>text [181, 887, 830, 909]<|/det|>4. He ate ____ biscuits. (few/a few/little)
<|det|>page_number [133, 928, 165, 946]<|/det|>32
<PAGE><|det|>list [149, 56, 918, 331]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 56, 744, 78]<|/det|>5. ____ citizen must vote. (all/every)
<|det|>text [150, 79, 733, 100]<|/det|>6. Bring me ____ chair. (these/that)
<|det|>text [150, 101, 862, 123]<|/det|>7. Mr Roy is a man of ____ words. (few/a few)
<|det|>text [150, 124, 874, 147]<|/det|>8. It was a ____ opportunity. (terrific/terrifying)
<|det|>text [150, 148, 877, 169]<|/det|>9. The plan was an ____ failure. (total/absolute)
<|det|>text [142, 170, 917, 193]<|/det|>10. He is ____ to me by a few years. (senior/superior)
<|det|>text [142, 194, 898, 215]<|/det|>11. He is the ____ man in command. (one/second)
<|det|>text [142, 216, 847, 259]<|/det|>12. This building is ____ than the one we live in. (older/elder)
<|det|>text [142, 262, 777, 284]<|/det|>13. What is the ____ news? (later/latest)
<|det|>text [142, 286, 895, 308]<|/det|>14. You can take ____ of the four toys. (either/one)
<|det|>text [142, 309, 914, 331]<|/det|>15. I have warned the boy ____ times. (some/several)
<|det|>title [58, 364, 345, 388]<|/det|>Participal Adjectives
<|det|>text [58, 395, 343, 415]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [58, 417, 545, 461]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [58, 417, 545, 439]<|/det|>• We stood in front of a running stream.
<|det|>text [59, 440, 468, 461]<|/det|>• We heard the interesting news.
<|det|>text [58, 462, 801, 486]<|/det|>The highlighted words are present participles (verb + -ing form).
<|det|>text [58, 501, 375, 522]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [58, 523, 600, 568]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [58, 523, 558, 544]<|/det|>• People say that this is a haunted house.
<|det|>text [58, 546, 600, 568]<|/det|>• The burnt cottage was razed to the ground.
<|det|>text [58, 568, 816, 591]<|/det|>The highlighted words are past participles (verb + -ed/-t/-n form).
<|det|>text [54, 605, 911, 652]<|/det|>You will notice that in these sentences, the present participles and the past participles have been used as adjectives.
<|det|>text [54, 683, 840, 730]<|/det|>Present participles and past participles, when used as adjectives, are called participial adjectives.
<|det|>title [55, 775, 895, 822]<|/det|>B. Underline the adjectives in this paragraph. Identify the participial adjectives.
<|det|>text [130, 832, 885, 901]<|/det|>I reached the forbidding building on Park Avenue in a black car. I knocked on the closed door and a short bald man opened the door. I entered a bright room lit with glowing candles. By the fireplace,
<|det|>page_number [848, 935, 880, 953]<|/det|>33

<PAGE><|det|>text [184, 53, 960, 265]<|/det|>there was a big easy chair with a shining tea table and a smaller chair beside it. A girl in a pretty dress and a cheerful smile appeared from nowhere. I felt shy. On the other side of the barred window, the glorious sun was setting the evening sky aflame. Suddenly, I noticed a sombre man sitting on an upholstered divan with a Persian rug over his knees. Behind him was a spectacular painting by Van Gogh. The crimson flowers and the golden cornfields had been painted with passion. I was impressed by the interesting décor of the room. However, the reigning silence was somewhat disturbing.
<|det|>title [185, 292, 938, 339]<|/det|>Pick out the participial adjectives from these sentences. Not all sentences will have them.
<|det|>list [194, 348, 792, 925]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [200, 351, 656, 373]<|/det|>1. I had watered the plants yesterday.
<|det|>text [200, 374, 669, 396]<|/det|>2. The injured students got off the bus.
<|det|>text [200, 398, 663, 420]<|/det|>3. Ashwini played the winning stroke.
<|det|>text [200, 421, 645, 443]<|/det|>4. The tiger was seriously wounded.
<|det|>text [200, 444, 609, 466]<|/det|>5. She is an experienced teacher.
<|det|>text [200, 467, 624, 489]<|/det|>6. The frightened rabbits ran away.
<|det|>text [200, 489, 789, 513]<|/det|>7. The hungry children ate up all the sandwiches.
<|det|>text [200, 513, 645, 535]<|/det|>8. Why do you have a worried look?
<|det|>text [200, 536, 565, 558]<|/det|>9. She is wearing a gold ring.
<|det|>text [190, 559, 684, 581]<|/det|>10. You should not read in a moving bus.
<|det|>text [190, 582, 666, 604]<|/det|>11. People walked over the frozen lake.
<|det|>text [190, 605, 489, 628]<|/det|>12. Today is a cold day.
<|det|>text [190, 628, 757, 650]<|/det|>13. The man must provide supporting evidence.
<|det|>text [190, 650, 791, 671]<|/det|>14. The man knocked on the door that was closed.
<|det|>text [190, 672, 881, 695]<|/det|>15. The pressing need of the hour is a roof over our heads.
<|det|>text [190, 695, 940, 717]<|/det|>16. I escaped through the broken window of the ruined house.
<|det|>text [190, 718, 960, 741]<|/det|>17. The wounded deer escaped from the clutches of the leopard.
<|det|>text [190, 741, 912, 764]<|/det|>18. The erupting volcano poured out large quantities of lava.
<|det|>text [190, 765, 701, 787]<|/det|>19. Mt Everest is the highest peak on Earth.
<|det|>text [190, 788, 661, 810]<|/det|>20. He is the richest man in the village.
<|det|>text [190, 811, 772, 833]<|/det|>21. The kitten was playing with the falling leaves.
<|det|>text [190, 834, 817, 856]<|/det|>22. The police caught the prisoner who had escaped.
<|det|>text [190, 857, 666, 879]<|/det|>23. It was such a long and boring flight.
