<|det|>text [190, 902, 535, 924]<|/det|>25. What a frightening film!
<|det|>page_number [148, 945, 179, 962]<|/det|>34
<PAGE><|det|>title [54, 55, 370, 77]<|/det|>Degrees of Comparison
<|det|>text [52, 83, 844, 130]<|/det|>You have learnt that adjectives have three degrees of comparison—positive, comparative and superlative. Let us revise what we know.
<|det|>text [54, 143, 312, 163]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [56, 166, 620, 234]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [56, 166, 345, 187]<|/det|>• This is a good violin.
<|det|>text [56, 189, 618, 211]<|/det|>• My violin is better than the one you bought.
<|det|>text [56, 212, 597, 234]<|/det|>• Bobby's violin is the best of all our violins.
<|det|>text [52, 266, 898, 313]<|/det|>The adjective good is used when we talk about one person or thing. It is said to be in the positive degree.
<|det|>text [54, 339, 732, 362]<|/det|>The positive degree is used when no comparison is made.
<|det|>text [54, 395, 922, 464]<|/det|>The adjective better is used to compare two things. It conveys a higher degree of quality than that of the positive. The adjective better is said to be in the comparative degree.
<|det|>text [54, 491, 571, 536]<|/det|>The comparative degree is used to compare two people or things.
<|det|>text [54, 567, 570, 636]<|/det|>The adjective best is used to compare more than two things. The adjective best is said to be in the superlative degree.
<|det|>text [54, 664, 512, 709]<|/det|>The superlative degree can be used to compare more than two things.
<|det|>text [56, 710, 525, 733]<|/det|>It denotes the highest degree of quality.
<|det|>title [614, 494, 678, 518]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [757, 489, 807, 520]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [607, 532, 890, 611]<|/det|>When we use the comparative degree, we always use the word than to compare and contrast two things.
<|det|>text [607, 624, 881, 682]<|/det|>The superlative degree, on the other hand, is always preceded by the word the.
<|det|>title [55, 772, 905, 818]<|/det|>D. Fill in the blanks with the correct forms of the adjectives given in the brackets.
<|det|>list [133, 829, 918, 925]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [133, 829, 825, 851]<|/det|>1. He is the ____ student in the class. (bold)
<|det|>text [133, 854, 917, 876]<|/det|>2. My fever is ____ than what it was yesterday. (bad)
<|det|>text [134, 879, 852, 924]<|/det|>3. It was certainly the ____ moment of his life. (proud)
<|det|>page_number [863, 925, 893, 942]<|/det|>35
<PAGE><|det|>text [187, 25, 970, 56]<|/det|>4. It is surely ____ to preach than to practise. (easy)
<|det|>text [189, 56, 893, 102]<|/det|>5. The ____ proposal came from a little child.
(good)
<|det|>text [188, 102, 874, 149]<|/det|>6. Very few people have been as ____ as he.
(lucky)
<|det|>text [188, 151, 925, 175]<|/det|>7. He believes he is ____ than his sister. (smart)
<|det|>text [188, 177, 963, 203]<|/det|>8. Sandy is the ____ member of his family. (young)
<|det|>text [188, 204, 817, 226]<|/det|>9. This is the ____ church in India. (old)
<|det|>text [175, 228, 943, 253]<|/det|>10. She looked ____ in the evening gown. (lovely)
<|det|>text [175, 254, 837, 278]<|/det|>11. This machine is ____ than that. (heavy)
<|det|>text [175, 279, 835, 304]<|/det|>12. My sister is ____ than my mother. (tall)
<|det|>title [91, 333, 603, 359]<|/det|>Interchange of Degrees of Comparison
<|det|>text [90, 363, 968, 412]<|/det|>Degrees of comparison of adjectives in sentences can be changed without any change in the meaning of the sentence. For example,
<|det|>list [93, 412, 842, 505]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 412, 842, 435]<|/det|>- No other boy in the class is as intelligent as Gitesh. (positive)
<|det|>text [93, 436, 790, 482]<|/det|>- Gitesh is more intelligent than any other boy in the class. (comparative)
<|det|>text [93, 482, 807, 505]<|/det|>• Gitesh is the most intelligent boy in the class. (superlative)
<|det|>title [163, 530, 823, 576]<|/det|>Now, change the degrees of comparison according to the instructions.
<|det|>list [179, 585, 856, 951]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 586, 862, 632]<|/det|>1. Shakespeare is greater than all other English dramatists. (superlative)
<|det|>text [180, 633, 844, 679]<|/det|>2. Sita is more popular than all other girls in the school. (superlative)
<|det|>text [180, 679, 844, 724]<|/det|>3. The Taj Mahal is the most beautiful monument of our country. (comparative)
<|det|>text [180, 725, 675, 748]<|/det|>4. Shimla is cooler than Delhi. (positive)
<|det|>text [180, 748, 725, 794]<|/det|>5. Kolkata is one of the biggest cities in India. (positive)
<|det|>text [180, 794, 585, 840]<|/det|>6. Gita is the best student in this group. (comparative)
<|det|>text [180, 840, 570, 886]<|/det|>7. He is cleverer than his sister. (positive)
<|det|>text [180, 886, 585, 933]<|/det|>8. The ostrich is the biggest bird. (comparative)
<|det|>image [645, 781, 958, 945]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [130, 936, 156, 952]<|/det|>36
<PAGE><|det|>text [135, 57, 682, 78]<|/det|>9. Lead is the heaviest metal. (comparative)
<|det|>text [123, 80, 785, 102]<|/det|>10. Gautam is not as tall as his brother. (comparative)
<|det|>list [123, 57, 785, 102]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [117, 125, 862, 193]<|/det|>Each of the following sentences contains an error in the use of positive, comparative and superlative adjectives. Rewrite the sentences correctly.
<|det|>list [133, 202, 910, 422]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [138, 204, 637, 226]<|/det|>1. This is the tallest of the two buildings.
<|det|>text [137, 228, 736, 249]<|/det|>2. My assignment is most prestigious than yours.
<|det|>text [137, 250, 750, 272]<|/det|>3. The wind grew strongest as the day progressed.
<|det|>text [137, 273, 645, 294]<|/det|>4. Who is the older? Rima, Sima or Rita?
<|det|>text [137, 295, 907, 318]<|/det|>5. This poem is not as best as the one you published last month.
<|det|>text [138, 319, 704, 340]<|/det|>6. I have two aunts. The youngest is a teacher.
<|det|>text [138, 341, 727, 363]<|/det|>7. This is definitely the hotter day of the season.
<|det|>text [138, 365, 613, 386]<|/det|>8. It seemed dark than it actually was.
<|det|>text [138, 387, 672, 409]<|/det|>9. We live in the taller building of the area.
<|det|>text [123, 410, 657, 432]<|/det|>10. She is the most lively of the two sisters.
<|det|>page_number [848, 924, 881, 942]<|/det|>37
<PAGE><|det|>header [19, 0, 302, 136]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [638, 48, 969, 88]<|/det|>Essay Writing
<|det|>image [119, 152, 177, 202]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 153, 932, 337]<|/det|>An essay, or a composition, can be an expression of your opinions, feelings or experiences. It is not, however, a rambling piece of writing, but a very structured form. An essay is usually divided into paragraphs. The first paragraph is called the introduction and the final paragraph is called the conclusion. The paragraphs in between the introduction and the conclusion constitute the body of the composition. These paragraphs help to develop the thoughts and ideas of the composition.
<|det|>text [123, 352, 637, 375]<|/det|>What are the qualities of a good composition?
<|det|>list [127, 375, 966, 808]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [127, 375, 928, 420]<|/det|>◆ Unity of thought: The composition must develop one theme with a definite purpose.
<|det|>text [127, 420, 961, 511]<|/det|>◆ Order: The composition must follow a certain ordered line of thought and come to a definite conclusion. It must not consist of haphazard reflections put down anyhow. Therefore it is of utmost importance to plan out a line of thought before beginning to write.
<|det|>text [128, 511, 966, 600]<|/det|>- Brevity: A composition should not be very long. What is important is whether the composition is well written or not. Though there can be no strict rule regarding the length of a composition, one can say that it ought to be roughly around three hundred words.
<|det|>text [128, 600, 961, 737]<|/det|>- Style: A composition should be written in simple and correct English. The sentence construction should be simple, direct and natural. The secret of clear writing is clear thinking. Slang, colloquial terms and free and easy constructions ought to be avoided. A composition which ‘moves’ the reader is supposed to be a good piece of writing. The words chosen ought to be justified and necessary.
<|det|>text [122, 738, 953, 806]<|/det|>- Personal touch: A composition must reveal the personal feelings and opinions of the writer. Without the personal touch, the composition becomes devoid of individuality.
<|det|>text [121, 822, 564, 845]<|/det|>How do we write a good composition?
<|det|>list [119, 846, 966, 916]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [119, 846, 727, 868]<|/det|>◆ Step 1: Think about the topic. What does it mean?
<|det|>text [119, 868, 966, 916]<|/det|>- Step 2: Write down the thoughts and questions that arise in your mind after seeing the topic.
<|det|>page_number [150, 928, 180, 946]<|/det|>38
<PAGE><|det|>text [55, 46, 891, 91]<|/det|>- Step 3: Arrange the thoughts and questions in a logical sequence, so that one may lead to the other.
<|det|>text [55, 92, 886, 160]<|/det|>- Step 4: Once the facts have been organised one can concentrate on the style. How do we make the facts appear attractive? This can be done in several ways.
<|det|>list [55, 46, 891, 160]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 162, 856, 207]<|/det|>- Start the composition with the help of a creative and arresting introduction by using one of the various methods.
<|det|>text [138, 209, 540, 231]<|/det|>- by setting the scene of the story
<|det|>text [138, 232, 535, 255]<|/det|>- by plunging straight into action
<|det|>text [138, 256, 547, 278]<|/det|>- by an arresting or unusual detail
<|det|>text [138, 279, 334, 301]<|/det|>- by a dialogue
<|det|>list [138, 209, 547, 301]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 302, 821, 345]<|/det|>- The style of writing also includes a meaningful conclusion. Remember—
<|det|>text [138, 349, 588, 371]<|/det|>- you should round off your narration
<|det|>text [138, 372, 588, 395]<|/det|>- you could aim for a surprise ending
<|det|>text [138, 396, 888, 460]<|/det|>- you must avoid ending your composition with sentences such as, 'In conclusion...', 'It can be seen that...' or 'Summing up, I'd like to state...'
<|det|>list [138, 349, 888, 460]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [55, 463, 905, 576]<|/det|>- Step 5: Keep in mind the length of the composition. An ideal composition would be about five paragraphs of 60 words each. A little more or less does not make much of a difference. Remember, it is not how much you say but what you say and how you say it—that is what creates an impact!
<|det|>text [55, 577, 898, 646]<|/det|>- Step 6: Do not forget the personal touch. Remember what you are writing is a composition and not an impersonal report. Therefore your opinion and views are most important.
<|det|>list [55, 463, 898, 646]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [80, 677, 143, 703]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [461, 673, 514, 711]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [75, 716, 892, 877]<|/det|>At this point, one has to understand the difference between a descriptive composition and a narrative composition. Certain topics like 'A Village Fair' may seem a little confusing. The confusion arises in whether the composition ought to be written in the first person or in the third person. 'A Village Fair' may be written in the first person. However, the description of the fair is more important than the narrator's experience of it, which may come in as part of the conclusion! If the topic reads, 'How You Spent a Day in a Village Fair', then the narrator's experience becomes as important as the description of the fair. Remember that descriptions are a part of a narrative essay and descriptive essays may or may not be written in the first person.
<|det|>page_number [848, 930, 882, 947]<|/det|>39
<PAGE><|det|>text [105, 48, 942, 96]<|/det|>Write a composition (300–350 words) on these topics with the help of the given guidelines.
<|det|>list [108, 106, 900, 480]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 106, 497, 128]<|/det|>1. Pandemonium at the Picnic
<|det|>text [174, 129, 896, 198]<|/det|>(A picnic or an excursion is an enjoyable experience if shared, especially if something exciting takes place. Describe such an occasion.)
<|det|>text [106, 200, 415, 221]<|/det|>2. Going to the Market
<|det|>text [174, 223, 767, 245]<|/det|>(Write a description of a market scene in your area.)
<|det|>text [106, 246, 380, 268]<|/det|>3. An Old Favourite
<|det|>text [174, 270, 880, 338]<|/det|>(Write about a book or a television programme that you have enjoyed and try to analyse what particular features gave you pleasure.)
<|det|>text [105, 340, 466, 363]<|/det|>4. A Wedding in the Family
<|det|>text [174, 364, 931, 409]<|/det|>(You have recently attended your cousin's marriage. Describe the event, the customs and what you liked/disliked about it.)
<|det|>text [105, 411, 617, 432]<|/det|>5. My Favourite Extra-curricular Activities
<|det|>text [174, 434, 918, 480]<|/det|>(What do you enjoy doing in your spare time? How do you feel these activities contribute to the development of your character.)
<|det|>image [235, 501, 816, 920]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [143, 932, 175, 948]<|/det|>40
<PAGE><|det|>title [45, 56, 248, 90]<|/det|>Adverbs
<|det|>header [720, 0, 999, 144]<|/det|>5
<|det|>image [46, 150, 108, 198]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 150, 828, 198]<|/det|>You have learnt that an adverb is a word which modifies the meaning of a verb, an adjective or another adverb.
<|det|>title [45, 213, 281, 236]<|/det|>Kinds of Adverbs
<|det|>text [45, 235, 405, 259]<|/det|>There are six kinds of adverbs.
<|det|>text [45, 280, 809, 306]<|/det|>Adverbs of time show us the time at which an action takes place.
<|det|>text [45, 323, 202, 345]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [47, 347, 497, 393]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [47, 347, 384, 367]<|/det|>• I have met Neha before.
<|det|>text [47, 369, 497, 393]<|/det|>• The principal will meet you today.
<|det|>text [45, 412, 766, 438]<|/det|>Adverbs of frequency show us how often something happens.
<|det|>text [47, 453, 202, 475]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [47, 476, 438, 521]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [47, 476, 343, 498]<|/det|>• He visits us regularly.
<|det|>text [47, 498, 438, 521]<|/det|>• My father exercises every day.
<|det|>text [45, 538, 666, 563]<|/det|>Adverbs of place show us where something happens.
<|det|>text [47, 578, 201, 600]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [47, 601, 364, 647]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [47, 601, 356, 621]<|/det|>• Sam went downstairs.
<|det|>text [47, 623, 364, 647]<|/det|>• The man has gone out.
<|det|>text [44, 666, 670, 690]<|/det|>Adverbs of manner show us how something happens.
<|det|>text [47, 707, 202, 729]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [47, 730, 456, 775]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [47, 730, 334, 751]<|/det|>• Rahim writes neatly.
<|det|>text [47, 752, 456, 775]<|/det|>• The students worked diligently.
<|det|>title [73, 801, 137, 825]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [460, 795, 514, 830]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [65, 838, 648, 859]<|/det|>Sometimes, an adverb can modify an entire sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [68, 860, 434, 880]<|/det|>- Luckily, she was not seriously injured.
<|det|>page_number [845, 922, 876, 940]<|/det|>41
<PAGE><|det|>text [118, 59, 823, 109]<|/det|>Adverbs of degree show the intensity or the degree of an action or a quality.
<|det|>text [120, 124, 272, 145]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [121, 147, 601, 191]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 147, 354, 167]<|/det|>• I am so satisfied!
<|det|>text [121, 168, 601, 191]<|/det|>• What you have heard is absolutely true.
<|det|>text [119, 207, 921, 256]<|/det|>Interrogative adverbs are adverbs which are used at the beginning of a sentence to ask questions.
<|det|>text [120, 277, 269, 298]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [121, 298, 520, 392]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 298, 590, 320]<|/det|>- When are you reaching Kolkata? (time)
<|det|>text [122, 322, 503, 344]<|/det|>• Where can I meet you? (place)
<|det|>text [122, 345, 520, 367]<|/det|>• How did you get here? (manner)
<|det|>text [122, 368, 456, 392]<|/det|>• Why are you sad? (reason)
<|det|>title [119, 414, 848, 459]<|/det|>A. Fill in the blanks with the kind of adverbs mentioned in the brackets.
<|det|>list [199, 468, 948, 884]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [201, 469, 948, 493]<|/det|>1. She arrived ____ for the class. (adverb of time)
<|det|>text [201, 494, 908, 516]<|/det|>2. This mango is ____ ripe. (adverb of degree)
<|det|>text [201, 517, 938, 560]<|/det|>3. We walked ____ in the direction of the school. (adverb of manner)
<|det|>text [201, 562, 945, 585]<|/det|>4. The grains were scattered ____. (adverb of place)
<|det|>text [201, 586, 945, 608]<|/det|>5. She will return ____ from Delhi. (adverb of time)
<|det|>text [201, 609, 842, 652]<|/det|>6. I cannot read the notes. He writes ____. (adverb of manner)
<|det|>text [201, 653, 862, 676]<|/det|>7. The child wept ____. (adverb of manner)
<|det|>text [201, 677, 856, 721]<|/det|>8. Rajiv ____ spoke ill of others. (adverb of frequency)
<|det|>text [201, 722, 882, 765]<|/det|>9. Breakfast is ____ ready. (adverb of degree)
10. He ____ visits us in May. (adverb of
<|det|>text [201, 766, 856, 809]<|/det|>frequency)
11. He will get a job ____. (adverb of place)
<|det|>text [201, 810, 935, 854]<|/det|>12. She ____ travels by bus. (adverb of frequency)
12. ____ do you usually come home?
<|det|>text [264, 855, 585, 877]<|/det|>(interrogative adverb of time)
<|det|>text [201, 878, 940, 901]<|/det|>14. She is ____ ill to go to work. (adverb of degree)
<|det|>text [201, 902, 933, 943]<|/det|>15. The father looked ____ at the baby. (adverb of manner)
<|det|>page_number [157, 941, 186, 958]<|/det|>42
<PAGE><|det|>title [56, 49, 353, 71]<|/det|>Formation of Adverbs
<|det|>text [54, 77, 922, 101]<|/det|>Most adverbs of manner are formed from adjectives in the following ways.
<|det|>list [55, 101, 926, 309]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [55, 101, 815, 125]<|/det|>Most adjectives can be changed to adverbs by adding -ly. For
<|det|>text [92, 125, 815, 148]<|/det|>example, warm—warmly, sincere—sincerely, quiet—quietly.
<|det|>text [55, 148, 926, 215]<|/det|>- When the adjective ends in -y preceded by a consonant, -y is changed to -i and -ly is added. For example, pretty—prettily, steady—steadily, lucky—luckily.
<|det|>text [55, 216, 895, 263]<|/det|>- When the adjective ends in -le, the -le is changed to -ly. For example, possible—possibly, able—ably, gentle—gently.
<|det|>text [55, 263, 783, 309]<|/det|>- When the adjective ends in -ic, we add -ally. For example, basic—basically, scientific—scientifically.
<|det|>title [80, 345, 147, 370]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [474, 339, 526, 373]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [77, 384, 720, 405]<|/det|>Some adverbs and adjectives have the same form, but are used differently.
<|det|>list [78, 406, 520, 487]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [78, 406, 412, 425]<|/det|>• We need fast bowlers. (adjective)
<|det|>text [79, 426, 325, 445]<|/det|>• He bowls fast. (adverb)
<|det|>text [79, 447, 363, 466]<|/det|>• We must be quick. (adverb)
<|det|>text [78, 467, 519, 487]<|/det|>• The smart child gave a quick reply. (adjective)
<|det|>title [55, 535, 867, 560]<|/det|>B. State whether the highlighted words are adverbs or adjectives.
<|det|>list [149, 568, 752, 910]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 568, 747, 590]<|/det|>1. The teacher dictated the notes in a loud voice.
<|det|>text [150, 592, 585, 614]<|/det|>2. There is enough milk in the pot.
<|det|>text [150, 615, 517, 637]<|/det|>3. He visits his mother daily.
<|det|>text [150, 638, 585, 660]<|/det|>4. I have worked enough for a day.
<|det|>text [150, 661, 619, 683]<|/det|>5. The early riser enjoys a longer day!
<|det|>text [150, 684, 604, 705]<|/det|>6. Work hard if you wish to do well.
<|det|>text [150, 706, 537, 728]<|/det|>7. The politician spoke loudly.
<|det|>text [150, 729, 751, 751]<|/det|>8. Exercise should be a part of your daily routine.
<|det|>text [150, 752, 615, 772]<|/det|>9. Late arrivals were sent back home.
<|det|>text [140, 775, 414, 797]<|/det|>10. He arrived early.
<|det|>text [140, 798, 515, 820]<|/det|>11. The children sang merrily.
<|det|>text [140, 821, 534, 841]<|/det|>12. He reached the station late.
<|det|>text [140, 843, 485, 865]<|/det|>13. This truly is a hard nut!
<|det|>text [140, 866, 493, 889]<|/det|>14. This will be a long wait.
<|det|>text [140, 889, 416, 911]<|/det|>15. She hit him hard.
<|det|>page_number [857, 930, 890, 947]<|/det|>43
<PAGE><|det|>title [105, 48, 370, 69]<|/det|>Position of Adverbs
<|det|>text [104, 76, 966, 146]<|/det|>- Adverbs of manner, which answer the question How?, are generally placed after the verb. If the verb has a direct object, then the adverb is placed after the direct object. For example,
<|det|>list [150, 147, 510, 194]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 147, 509, 169]<|/det|>• The actor is singing loudly.
<|det|>text [150, 170, 465, 194]<|/det|>• He plays the flute well.
<|det|>title [130, 215, 195, 241]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [522, 207, 571, 245]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [122, 254, 945, 317]<|/det|>The direct object is the person or thing that receives the action of the verb. The direct object follows the verb. We can identify the direct object by asking the question Whom? or What? after the verb. For example,
<|det|>list [122, 319, 601, 361]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [122, 319, 601, 339]<|/det|>• The boy recited a poem. (What did the boy recite?)
<|det|>text [122, 340, 585, 361]<|/det|>• Sudha is biting her nails. (What is Sudha biting?)
<|det|>text [104, 388, 960, 460]<|/det|>- Adverbs of place and adverbs of time are also placed after the verb. If the sentence has a direct object, then the adverb is placed after the direct object. For example,
<|det|>list [150, 461, 480, 504]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 461, 430, 483]<|/det|>• It snowed yesterday.
<|det|>text [150, 484, 426, 504]<|/det|>• Put the chairs there.
<|det|>text [142, 516, 972, 562]<|/det|>When there are two or more adverbs after a verb, the normal order is adverb of manner followed by adverb of place and then adverb of time.
<|det|>list [144, 564, 960, 734]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [144, 564, 578, 584]<|/det|>• Rima will come here next month.
<|det|>text [98, 595, 960, 664]<|/det|>- Adverbs of frequency, adverbs of degree and sometimes adverbs of manner are normally put between the subject and the verb if the verb consists of only one word. For example,
<|det|>list [144, 666, 612, 734]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [145, 666, 463, 687]<|/det|>• I quite like these shoes.
<|det|>text [145, 689, 516, 710]<|/det|>• I always tell him to exercise.
<|det|>text [145, 712, 612, 734]<|/det|>• She happily accepted the suggestion.
<|det|>title [125, 761, 191, 786]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [144, 797, 945, 838]<|/det|>- If the verb consists of more than one word, then the adverb comes after the first verb. For example,
<|det|>text [175, 840, 563, 859]<|/det|>• The man had just entered the auditorium.
<|det|>page_number [135, 938, 165, 955]<|/det|>44
<PAGE><|det|>text [94, 63, 816, 84]<|/det|>• If the verb is am/are/is/was, the adverbs are placed after the verb. For example,
<|det|>text [126, 86, 348, 103]<|/det|>• They are always late.
<|det|>text [126, 106, 340, 125]<|/det|>• The rice is just right.
<|det|>text [95, 138, 905, 179]<|/det|>- When an adverb modifies an adjective or another adverb, the adverb usually comes before it. For example,
<|det|>text [126, 181, 432, 200]<|/det|>• He lifted the stone quite easily.
<|det|>text [126, 202, 363, 221]<|/det|>- It is a rather warm day.
<|det|>text [95, 222, 838, 242]<|/det|>• The adverb enough is always placed after the word which it modifies. For example,
<|det|>text [126, 244, 414, 263]<|/det|>• Is this good enough for you?
<|det|>text [126, 264, 533, 284]<|/det|>• Is the box large enough for all your books?
<|det|>text [95, 297, 782, 318]<|/det|>- The word only should be placed before the word it modifies. For example,
<|det|>text [126, 319, 434, 338]<|/det|>• I only have four bags right now.
<|det|>text [95, 352, 843, 392]<|/det|>- For emphasis, adverbs of time may also be placed at the beginning of the sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [126, 394, 392, 412]<|/det|>• Tomorrow, we shall meet.
<|det|>title [135, 460, 885, 484]<|/det|>C. Insert the adverbs given in the brackets in their correct positions.
<|det|>text [151, 494, 528, 516]<|/det|>1. I am late for school. (often)
<|det|>text [151, 518, 593, 539]<|/det|>2. Sam agreed to the plan. (readily)
<|det|>text [151, 541, 606, 561]<|/det|>3. He has finished the work. (almost)
<|det|>text [151, 564, 574, 584]<|/det|>4. He disagrees with me. (seldom)
<|det|>text [151, 586, 558, 607]<|/det|>5. She cycles to school. (usually)
<|det|>text [151, 609, 580, 630]<|/det|>6. He did well in the exam. (fairly)
<|det|>text [151, 632, 580, 653]<|/det|>7. I asked for a glass of milk. (only)
<|det|>text [151, 654, 619, 675]<|/det|>8. We shall meet you soon. (certainly)
<|det|>text [151, 677, 739, 698]<|/det|>9. I like the book that I bought yesterday. (rather)
<|det|>text [142, 700, 799, 722]<|/det|>10. He was brave to challenge the authorities. (enough)
<|det|>image [745, 491, 907, 672]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [55, 742, 355, 765]<|/det|>Use of Simple Adverbs
<|det|>text [55, 771, 512, 792]<|/det|>Note the use of these common adverbs.
<|det|>text [55, 794, 856, 840]<|/det|>◆ Very is intensively used with an adjective or another adverb in the positive degree. For example,
<|det|>text [108, 842, 436, 862]<|/det|>• He is a very nice person.
<|det|>text [108, 864, 396, 886]<|/det|>• I typed very carefully.
<|det|>page_number [846, 932, 878, 949]<|/det|>45
<PAGE><|det|>text [139, 38, 867, 88]<|/det|>- Very with a superlative is used to indicate the highest degree. For example,
<|det|>text [186, 89, 633, 111]<|/det|>• The coffee is of the very best quality.
<|det|>text [186, 113, 675, 136]<|/det|>• I can reach by 7 a.m. at the very earliest.
<|det|>text [139, 138, 538, 184]<|/det|>- Too is used to mean more than required or excess. For example,
<|det|>text [186, 186, 525, 208]<|/det|>• I am too tired to play now.
<|det|>text [186, 210, 576, 255]<|/det|>- It is too cold to go for a walk in the woods.
<|det|>text [186, 258, 535, 331]<|/det|>- Much is used with an adverb or an adjective in the comparative degree. For example,
<|det|>text [186, 332, 600, 355]<|/det|>• Things are much better in the city.
<|det|>text [186, 355, 785, 379]<|/det|>• You are much stronger than I thought you would be.
<|det|>image [600, 156, 804, 201]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [601, 200, 818, 222]<|/det|>We say, It is very hot today.
<|det|>text [601, 223, 875, 244]<|/det|>We do not say, It is too hot today.
<|det|>text [598, 256, 878, 278]<|/det|>We say, It is too hot to go cycling.
<|det|>text [598, 278, 948, 300]<|/det|>We do not say, It is very hot to go cycling.
<|det|>title [130, 401, 785, 426]<|/det|>D. Fill in the blanks with very, too or much as required.
<|det|>text [224, 437, 948, 461]<|/det|>1. Mayank is ____ young to go for a swim alone.
<|det|>text [225, 462, 717, 485]<|/det|>2. I am ____ glad to see you.
<|det|>text [225, 485, 754, 508]<|/det|>3. Ishaan plays tennis ____ well.
<|det|>text [225, 509, 871, 533]<|/det|>4. I am ____ sleepy to go for a jog tonight.
<|det|>text [225, 533, 903, 555]<|/det|>5. Her handwriting is ____ worse than mine.
<|det|>text [225, 556, 920, 579]<|/det|>6. The movie was ____ better than I expected.
<|det|>text [225, 579, 925, 623]<|/det|>7. You have to be ____ careful when you go to the beach in the rainy season.
<|det|>text [224, 625, 945, 648]<|/det|>8. The watch wasn't ____ expensive, so I bought it
<|det|>text [225, 648, 940, 690]<|/det|>9. When we went to Goa, the weather was ____ nice.
<|det|>text [211, 693, 945, 719]<|/det|>10. The eagle swooped upon the rabbit ____ quickly.
<|det|>text [211, 719, 664, 739]<|/det|>11. It's dark outside.
<|det|>text [211, 741, 748, 765]<|/det|>12. That was a ____ good answer.
<|det|>title [136, 782, 489, 802]<|/det|>One Word for Several Words
<|det|>text [135, 811, 875, 857]<|/det|>We often use an adverb in place of a group of words. So adverbs are useful when we wish to express something concisely. For example,
<|det|>text [136, 857, 935, 896]<|/det|>◆ I reminded Ravi again and again that he had to pick up his sister from school.
<|det|>text [136, 899, 937, 927]<|/det|>◆ I reminded Ravi repeatedly that he had to pick up his sister from school.
<|det|>page_number [165, 941, 195, 958]<|/det|>46
<PAGE><|det|>title [50, 59, 88, 80]<|/det|>E.
<|det|>text [135, 57, 885, 103]<|/det|>Rewrite the following sentences replacing the highlighted words with adverbs from the box given below.
<|det|>text [167, 121, 823, 175]<|/det|>independently politely manually abnormally finally beautifully unusually immediately
<|det|>text [139, 198, 735, 221]<|/det|>1. I agreed to accept his offer without any delay.
<|det|>text [138, 222, 898, 244]<|/det|>2. At the end, after some discussion, we signed the agreement.
<|det|>text [139, 246, 633, 268]<|/det|>3. I want to start something on my own.
<|det|>text [139, 269, 615, 291]<|/det|>4. He spoke to me in a polite manner.
<|det|>text [139, 292, 747, 314]<|/det|>5. Suman operated the heavy machinery by hand.
<|det|>text [139, 315, 858, 358]<|/det|>6. The interior designer has decorated my flat in a beautiful manner.
<|det|>text [139, 360, 865, 404]<|/det|>7. The water of the river has risen beyond the usual limit for summer.
<|det|>text [139, 406, 771, 450]<|/det|>8. Scientists believe that bacteria often behave in an uncharacteristic manner.
<|det|>list [138, 198, 898, 450]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [851, 933, 884, 951]<|/det|>47
<PAGE><|det|>header [6, 0, 268, 150]<|/det|>The image is too blurry to recognize any text content.
<|det|>header [702, 55, 967, 88]<|/det|>Post Haste
<|det|>text [100, 162, 945, 232]<|/det|>What are the things you need to post a letter? Read an interesting story about how the writer goes out of his way to help his neighbour to post a letter.
<|det|>text [101, 246, 969, 296]<|/det|>'I say, I am pleased to see you!' declared the little man standing dejectedly by the post box.
<|det|>text [104, 309, 642, 335]<|/det|>'Oh, hullo!' I said, stopping. 'Simpson, isn't it?'
<|det|>text [103, 345, 968, 461]<|/det|>'Yes, that's right! I wonder if you could lend me five rupees.' he said, 'You see, my wife gave me a letter to post, and I've just noticed it isn't stamped. My wife was most insistent about it going tonight. She said I wasn't to... It's...Well, I am not sure that it's extraordinarily important, but I'd better post it, if you know what I mean.'
<|det|>text [104, 474, 807, 520]<|/det|>'I understand—I've got a book of stamps at home!' I suddenly remembered.
<|det|>text [104, 534, 741, 559]<|/det|>'We'd better hurry, or we shall miss the post,' I told him.
<|det|>text [104, 570, 951, 639]<|/det|>We hurried. It was just as well that we did hurry, because it took rather a long while to find the book of stamps. But it wasn't worth finding after all the trouble. It was empty.
<|det|>text [104, 651, 718, 677]<|/det|>'Funny!' I said. 'I could have sworn it was nearly full!'
<|det|>text [104, 688, 731, 713]<|/det|>'But what about my letter!' asked Simpson, mournfully.
<|det|>text [103, 726, 963, 773]<|/det|>'What else can you do? Post it unstamped. The other chap will have to pay double postage on it when he receives it, but that can't be helped.'
<|det|>text [103, 786, 801, 810]<|/det|>Then, I added, 'Now, hurry, or you'll miss the last collection.'
<|det|>text [101, 824, 875, 848]<|/det|>Much flustered by this reminder, Simpson went swiftly up the street.
<|det|>text [101, 862, 567, 887]<|/det|>'Hey! The other way!' I roared after him.
<|det|>text [101, 899, 951, 925]<|/det|>'Sorry!' he panted, returning. '1...I rather think I've forgotten the way again.'
<|det|>page_number [132, 938, 161, 956]<|/det|>48
<PAGE><|det|>text [54, 35, 877, 130]<|/det|>I didn't even try to explain. I just took him firmly by the arm and accompanied him to the post office, in time for the midnight collection. I knew it would save me time in the end. He dropped in his letter and then, to finish my job properly, I took him home.
<|det|>text [54, 143, 855, 193]<|/det|>He said, 'Thank you. I...I can't think what I should have done without you. That letter—it's only an invitation to dinner, to...good gracious!'
<|det|>text [56, 205, 438, 229]<|/det|>'Why, what's the matter?' I asked.
<|det|>text [56, 244, 551, 269]<|/det|>'Nothing. Just something I've remembered.'
<|det|>text [54, 282, 862, 329]<|/det|>He stared at me for a moment, jerked out a 'Good Night!' and popped indoors.
<|det|>text [54, 345, 900, 415]<|/det|>All the way home, I was wondering what he'd remembered. But I stopped wondering the next morning, when I had to pay the postman ten rupees, for a familiar looking envelope.
<|det|>title [58, 435, 422, 458]<|/det|>A. Complete the sentences.
<|det|>text [138, 468, 757, 516]<|/det|>1. Mrs Simpson had insisted that her husband should ____.
<|det|>text [138, 515, 790, 538]<|/det|>2. The author suddenly remembered ____.
<|det|>text [138, 538, 874, 561]<|/det|>3. The book of stamps wasn't worth finding ____.
<|det|>text [138, 562, 810, 584]<|/det|>4. The author suggested that the letter ____.
<|det|>text [138, 585, 701, 607]<|/det|>5. Next morning, the author ____.
<|det|>title [58, 628, 418, 650]<|/det|>B. Answer these questions.
<|det|>text [138, 661, 546, 684]<|/det|>1. Who was in a hurry and why?
<|det|>text [138, 684, 763, 707]<|/det|>2. How much money did Simpson ask for and why?
<|det|>text [138, 707, 600, 729]<|/det|>3. What did the author do ultimately?
<|det|>text [138, 729, 696, 752]<|/det|>4. Why did Simpson exclaim 'good gracious'?
<|det|>title [58, 772, 870, 821]<|/det|>C. Choose the word that is similar in meaning to these words from the story.
<|det|>text [138, 830, 720, 854]<|/det|>1. dejectedly obediently/miserably/quietly
<|det|>text [138, 854, 905, 876]<|/det|>2. insistent forcing action/unable to do/examine carefully
<|det|>text [138, 876, 757, 899]<|/det|>3. extraordinarily intelligently/unusually/minutely
<|det|>text [138, 899, 785, 924]<|/det|>4. mournfully sorrowfully/mischievously/happily
<|det|>page_number [845, 929, 876, 946]<|/det|>49
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 999, 50]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>text [208, 45, 851, 69]<|/det|>5. panted gasped for breath/hurried/worried
<|det|>text [210, 71, 794, 94]<|/det|>6. flustered surprised/confused/desperate
<|det|>text [210, 95, 775, 120]<|/det|>7. swiftly promptly/eagerly/carelessly
<|det|>list [208, 45, 851, 120]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [135, 150, 525, 173]<|/det|>D. Choose the correct answer.
<|det|>text [211, 183, 601, 209]<|/det|>1. Simpson's wife had sent him
<|det|>list [278, 211, 504, 303]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [278, 211, 490, 230]<|/det|>a. on an errand.
<|det|>text [278, 233, 501, 256]<|/det|>b. to post a letter.
<|det|>text [278, 258, 506, 279]<|/det|>c. to call a friend.
<|det|>text [278, 282, 507, 305]<|/det|>d. to buy a stamp.
<|det|>text [211, 321, 610, 345]<|/det|>2. Simpson and the author were
<|det|>list [278, 346, 470, 440]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [278, 346, 421, 365]<|/det|>a. friends.
<|det|>text [278, 369, 430, 389]<|/det|>b. cousins.
<|det|>text [278, 393, 440, 412]<|/det|>c. relatives.
<|det|>text [278, 415, 469, 440]<|/det|>d. neighbours.
<|det|>text [211, 453, 712, 478]<|/det|>3. Who got the bigger surprise in the end?
<|det|>list [278, 479, 477, 569]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [278, 479, 434, 500]<|/det|>a. Simpson
<|det|>text [278, 501, 454, 522]<|/det|>b. the author
<|det|>text [278, 525, 429, 544]<|/det|>c. the wife
<|det|>text [278, 546, 476, 569]<|/det|>d. the postman
<|det|>text [135, 600, 924, 625]<|/det|>E. Find a suitable word or group of words from the passage for—
<|det|>list [208, 633, 552, 723]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [208, 633, 556, 656]<|/det|>1. with strong determination
<|det|>text [209, 657, 537, 679]<|/det|>2. to move away suddenly
<|det|>text [209, 680, 545, 702]<|/det|>3. an expression of surprise
<|det|>text [209, 702, 416, 723]<|/det|>4. the last post
<|det|>page_number [175, 936, 206, 952]<|/det|>50
<PAGE><|det|>title [60, 46, 238, 80]<|/det|>Articles
<|det|>header [768, 46, 895, 113]<|/det|>6
<|det|>image [59, 156, 121, 201]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 166, 790, 190]<|/det|>You have learnt that there are three articles—a, an and the.
<|det|>title [58, 209, 296, 231]<|/det|>Indefinite Articles
<|det|>text [56, 239, 834, 307]<|/det|>The articles a and an are called indefinite articles because they do not point to a particular person or thing. Let us look at the uses of the indefinite articles.
<|det|>text [58, 319, 826, 365]<|/det|>We use a and an before countable nouns in the singular number. For example,
<|det|>list [103, 367, 264, 412]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [103, 367, 225, 385]<|/det|>- a desk
<|det|>text [103, 390, 264, 412]<|/det|>- an orange
<|det|>title [80, 445, 147, 470]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [461, 437, 511, 474]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [76, 483, 843, 524]<|/det|>We do not say a milk or a lemonade because they are uncountable nouns. However, we can say a cup of milk or a glass of lemonade.
<|det|>list [58, 572, 885, 905]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [58, 572, 885, 617]<|/det|>We use a before words that begin with a consonant sound. We use an before words that begin with a vowel sound. For example,
<|det|>text [102, 619, 305, 640]<|/det|>- a thirsty crow
<|det|>text [468, 619, 662, 640]<|/det|>- a good friend
<|det|>text [102, 642, 329, 664]<|/det|>- an empty bottle
<|det|>text [468, 642, 744, 663]<|/det|>- an independent state
<|det|>text [56, 673, 875, 720]<|/det|>We use a before words that begin with e and u because they have an initial 'y' sound. For example,
<|det|>text [102, 723, 282, 744]<|/det|>- a university
<|det|>text [102, 746, 277, 767]<|/det|>- a European
<|det|>text [56, 777, 774, 799]<|/det|>◆ We use a before words that begin with o but sound like 'w'.
<|det|>text [99, 802, 251, 825]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [102, 826, 392, 849]<|/det|>• a one-storey building
<|det|>text [56, 858, 821, 881]<|/det|>We use an before words that begin with a silent h. For example,
<|det|>text [102, 883, 225, 903]<|/det|>- an heir
<|det|>text [468, 882, 735, 903]<|/det|>- an honest politician
<|det|>page_number [834, 931, 865, 950]<|/det|>51
<PAGE><|det|>text [132, 44, 936, 91]<|/det|>- We use an before abbreviations, if consonants begin with a vowel sound. For example,
<|det|>text [180, 93, 300, 112]<|/det|>- an MA
<|det|>text [544, 92, 655, 110]<|/det|>- an MP
<|det|>text [132, 124, 934, 171]<|/det|>- We use a or an before a singular countable noun which is used to represent a class of things. For example,
<|det|>text [180, 173, 506, 193]<|/det|>• A cow is a useful animal.
<|det|>text [132, 207, 675, 229]<|/det|>- We use a or an to indicate one. For example,
<|det|>text [180, 231, 521, 251]<|/det|>• Not a bird could be heard.
<|det|>text [132, 265, 835, 286]<|/det|>- We use a or an to make a common noun of a proper noun.
<|det|>text [178, 289, 327, 311]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [180, 312, 570, 333]<|/det|>• We have a Daniel in our midst!
<|det|>text [132, 346, 855, 369]<|/det|>◆ We use a or an when we speak of a profession. For example,
<|det|>text [180, 371, 387, 390]<|/det|>• He is a doctor.
<|det|>text [132, 402, 953, 449]<|/det|>- We use a before words like dozen, quarter, thousand, few, little and lot of. For example,
<|det|>text [180, 450, 558, 472]<|/det|>• Can you give me a little sugar?
<|det|>text [180, 474, 517, 496]<|/det|>• He bought a few mangoes.
<|det|>text [180, 496, 674, 519]<|/det|>- It is a quarter of a kilometre to the school.
<|det|>image [104, 535, 177, 575]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [211, 541, 513, 564]<|/det|>Insert a/an where required.
<|det|>text [221, 576, 928, 621]<|/det|>1. What ____ shame! He cannot make it to the reunion.
<|det|>text [220, 623, 953, 667]<|/det|>2. He is ____ honourable man but has been largely misunderstood.
<|det|>text [221, 670, 953, 715]<|/det|>3. I met ____ Assamese gentleman while travelling from Agra to ____ hill station.
<|det|>text [220, 717, 938, 739]<|/det|>4. He has ____ headache and needs to take rest.
<|det|>text [221, 741, 902, 786]<|/det|>5. The workers formed ____ union in order to discuss their problems.
<|det|>text [220, 788, 847, 811]<|/det|>6. Can you give me ____ one-rupee note?
<|det|>text [221, 812, 779, 834]<|/det|>7. ____ honest person will never lie.
<|det|>text [220, 836, 885, 858]<|/det|>8. She wants to buy ____ South Indian saree.
<|det|>text [220, 859, 948, 902]<|/det|>9. ____ aunt of mine, who lives in Egypt, will visit us in summer.
<|det|>text [207, 904, 942, 950]<|/det|>10. Our train is late by ____ hour. We may have to change our plans.
<|det|>page_number [157, 945, 192, 963]<|/det|>52
<PAGE><|det|>text [136, 45, 842, 68]<|/det|>11. Could you add ____ little salt to the salad?
<|det|>text [137, 69, 870, 91]<|/det|>12. I need ____ lot of money to fulfil my dreams.
<|det|>list [136, 45, 870, 91]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [61, 111, 281, 133]<|/det|>Definite Articles
<|det|>text [60, 139, 917, 187]<|/det|>The is called the definite article because it points to a particular person or a specific thing. Let us learn the uses of the definite article.
<|det|>text [60, 197, 916, 244]<|/det|>◆ We use the when we speak of a particular person or thing, or one that has already been referred to. For example,
<|det|>text [108, 245, 810, 266]<|/det|>• A thirsty crow found a pitcher that contained some water.
<|det|>text [148, 267, 911, 314]<|/det|>The clever crow picked up some pebbles in his beak and dropped them one by one into the pitcher.
<|det|>text [60, 324, 839, 369]<|/det|>- We use the when a singular common noun is used to indicate a whole class. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 370, 530, 394]<|/det|>• The lion is the king of the jungle.
<|det|>text [60, 403, 600, 427]<|/det|>◆ We use the with superlatives. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 427, 812, 449]<|/det|>• Sachin is one of the most popular sportsmen in the world.
<|det|>text [60, 460, 725, 483]<|/det|>◆ We use the with objects that are unique. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 484, 641, 504]<|/det|>• the sun • the moon
<|det|>text [60, 515, 820, 561]<|/det|>- We use the before phrases like first, last, only, next, following and previous. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 562, 474, 583]<|/det|>• She is sitting in the last row.
<|det|>text [60, 594, 580, 617]<|/det|>◆ We use the before ordinals. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 617, 668, 638]<|/det|>• Her examination starts on the third of March.
<|det|>text [60, 650, 761, 695]<|/det|>◆ We use the when we speak of the population of a nation. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 696, 604, 719]<|/det|>• The Japanese are warm-hearted people.
<|det|>text [60, 729, 905, 752]<|/det|>◆ We use the before the names of trains, planes and ships. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 753, 830, 775]<|/det|>• the Titanic • the Golden Temple Express
<|det|>text [60, 785, 888, 831]<|/det|>◆ We use the before the names of well-known buildings and institutions. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 832, 666, 853]<|/det|>• the Parliament House • the Red Fort
<|det|>page_number [854, 931, 885, 949]<|/det|>53
