<PAGE><|det|>text [117, 45, 848, 96]<|/det|>We use the before the names of newspapers and magazines. For example,
<|det|>text [164, 96, 430, 117]<|/det|>• the Deccan Herald
<|det|>text [527, 94, 682, 114]<|/det|>• the Hindu
<|det|>text [117, 129, 923, 179]<|/det|>- We use the with adjectives, when we use them to refer to a whole group of people. For example,
<|det|>text [164, 179, 648, 202]<|/det|>• We should help the poor. (poor people)
<|det|>text [117, 213, 917, 261]<|/det|>- We use the with the names of rivers, seas, oceans, mountains and deserts. For example,
<|det|>text [164, 262, 341, 285]<|/det|>- the Ganges
<|det|>text [524, 260, 758, 281]<|/det|>• the Indian Ocean
<|det|>text [165, 286, 384, 306]<|/det|>• the Arabian Sea
<|det|>text [524, 285, 760, 305]<|/det|>• the Sahara Desert
<|det|>text [117, 320, 954, 367]<|/det|>- We use the before points of the compass that are used as geographic areas. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 368, 384, 388]<|/det|>• the Middle East
<|det|>text [521, 367, 657, 387]<|/det|>- the West
<|det|>text [117, 401, 732, 425]<|/det|>• We use the before points on the globe. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 426, 340, 448]<|/det|>• the equator
<|det|>text [518, 425, 713, 448]<|/det|>• the south pole
<|det|>title [117, 463, 376, 485]<|/det|>Omission of Articles
<|det|>text [117, 494, 395, 514]<|/det|>We do not use articles—
<|det|>text [117, 515, 945, 560]<|/det|>- before expressions like last night, last week, last year, next week and next month.
<|det|>text [117, 572, 537, 595]<|/det|>• before proper nouns. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 596, 458, 617]<|/det|>• I am going to Australia.
<|det|>text [165, 617, 504, 640]<|/det|>• Have you spoken to Hema?
<|det|>text [117, 650, 603, 673]<|/det|>- before names of languages. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 673, 541, 695]<|/det|>• They speak German quite well.
<|det|>text [117, 705, 790, 728]<|/det|>- before plural countable nouns that refer to the entire class.
<|det|>text [165, 729, 306, 750]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [165, 751, 438, 772]<|/det|>• I do not like bananas.
<|det|>text [117, 783, 550, 806]<|/det|>• before material nouns. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 806, 546, 828]<|/det|>- Platinum is an expensive metal.
<|det|>text [117, 838, 853, 861]<|/det|>• before abstract nouns when used in a general way. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 861, 414, 883]<|/det|>• Honesty is a virtue.
<|det|>page_number [150, 937, 180, 953]<|/det|>54
<PAGE><|det|>text [53, 44, 701, 68]<|/det|>- before the names of games and sports. For example,
<|det|>text [99, 69, 654, 92]<|/det|>• Golf is gaining in popularity in India of late.
<|det|>text [99, 92, 487, 115]<|/det|>• Rakesh loves playing hockey.
<|det|>text [53, 123, 570, 148]<|/det|>◆ before the names of meals. For example,
<|det|>text [99, 148, 500, 171]<|/det|>• I ate breakfast quite late today.
<|det|>text [53, 180, 831, 203]<|/det|>◆ before expressions such as all day, all night, by air and by sea.
<|det|>text [99, 204, 254, 226]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [99, 227, 758, 251]<|/det|>• The contingent for the Asian Games will travel by air.
<|det|>title [80, 291, 143, 317]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [468, 286, 521, 322]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [75, 330, 882, 371]<|/det|>In certain cases, there is a need to repeat an article to make the meaning clear. Look at these sentences carefully.
<|det|>text [99, 373, 437, 393]<|/det|>• The Trustee and Secretary arrived.
<|det|>text [99, 394, 468, 414]<|/det|>• The Trustee and the Secretary arrived.
<|det|>list [99, 373, 468, 414]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [75, 426, 868, 446]<|/det|>The first sentence implies that only one person who was both Trustee and Secretary arrived.
<|det|>text [75, 447, 812, 468]<|/det|>The second sentence states that two persons arrived—the Trustee and the Secretary.
<|det|>title [53, 527, 692, 551]<|/det|>B. Insert, omit or change articles where necessary.
<|det|>list [141, 559, 760, 928]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [147, 562, 560, 585]<|/det|>1. French is not a easy language.
<|det|>text [146, 586, 520, 607]<|/det|>2. Moon is shining in the sky.
<|det|>text [147, 608, 395, 630]<|/det|>3. Don't play fool.
<|det|>text [146, 631, 480, 650]<|/det|>4. This is a best of the lot!
<|det|>text [146, 652, 723, 675]<|/det|>5. Kindness that he showed was truly touching.
<|det|>text [146, 676, 753, 698]<|/det|>6. The privileged of community should help poor.
<|det|>text [146, 699, 465, 720]<|/det|>7. I hope to be the actor.
<|det|>text [146, 721, 767, 743]<|/det|>8. I met a Austrian at an university in the Germany.
<|det|>text [146, 744, 519, 766]<|/det|>9. He thinks he is the Tagore!
<|det|>text [135, 767, 767, 789]<|/det|>10. My father admires the truth and honesty in men.
<|det|>text [135, 790, 506, 835]<|/det|>11. The aeroplanes can fly at unbelievable speed.
<|det|>text [135, 836, 425, 858]<|/det|>12. I play the football.
<|det|>text [135, 859, 617, 881]<|/det|>13. We enjoyed meal that you cooked.
<|det|>text [135, 882, 645, 904]<|/det|>14. Govind is better swimmer than golfer.
<|det|>text [135, 905, 667, 927]<|/det|>15. America is richest country in the world.
<|det|>image [631, 795, 914, 875]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [854, 930, 887, 949]<|/det|>55
<PAGE><|det|>header [100, 4, 136, 28]<|/det|>C.
<|det|>text [186, 4, 977, 56]<|/det|>There is an article missing in each line. Mark me space where the article should be inserted and write it down in the space provided.
<|det|>text [186, 68, 777, 96]<|/det|>Sam and I were playing in sand pit in our backyard.
<|det|>text [189, 100, 812, 128]<|/det|>We were building sand castle. We lined it with bricks
<|det|>text [190, 133, 744, 160]<|/det|>and bits of grass. We left some space in middle
<|det|>text [191, 165, 714, 192]<|/det|>in order to pour in bucket of sand. I decided
<|det|>text [191, 198, 744, 224]<|/det|>to go to beach first. Sam was the creative artist.
<|det|>text [191, 229, 783, 256]<|/det|>He was designer in our group. He had once made
<|det|>text [193, 262, 695, 288]<|/det|>a model of unicorn for a school exhibition.
<|det|>text [193, 293, 813, 321]<|/det|>It became centre piece that year. Suddenly, two dogs
<|det|>text [196, 326, 770, 354]<|/det|>appeared. They could destroy our artwork in jiffy.
<|det|>text [196, 358, 804, 384]<|/det|>Our friends, who were in vicinity, rushed to our aid.
<|det|>text [198, 390, 753, 416]<|/det|>Foundation of our grand castle had to be saved!
<|det|>text [198, 422, 657, 446]<|/det|>Then, Sam rose. He let out loud scream.
<|det|>text [196, 453, 733, 478]<|/det|>Dogs ran as fast as their legs could carry them.
<|det|>text [193, 484, 739, 540]<|/det|>Moment of crises was over. That day, we learnt that hell hath no fury as artist scorned.
<|det|>image [306, 574, 816, 825]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [143, 927, 177, 946]<|/det|>56
<PAGE><|det|>title [68, 44, 381, 85]<|/det|>Diary Writing
<|det|>image [795, 0, 986, 145]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [62, 156, 118, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 156, 870, 227]<|/det|>Read this extract from the 1864 science-fiction novel by Jules Verne (1828-1905) called the Journey to the Interior of the Earth (also called Journey to the Centre of the Earth).
<|det|>text [73, 252, 876, 438]<|/det|>Monday, August 17: I am trying to recall the peculiar instincts of the monsters of the pre-adamite world, who, coming next in succession after the molluscs, the crustaceans and the fishes, preceded the animals of mammalian race upon the earth. The world then belonged to reptiles. Those monsters held the mastery in the seas of the secondary period. They possessed a perfect organisation, gigantic proportions, prodigious strength. The saurians of our day, the alligators and the crocodiles, are but feeble reproductions of their forefathers of primitive ages.
<|det|>text [73, 445, 879, 584]<|/det|>I shudder as I recall these monsters to my remembrance. No human eye has ever beheld them living. I saw at the Hamburg museum the skeleton of one of these creatures thirty feet in length. Am I then fated—I, a denizen of earth—to be placed face to face with these representatives of long extinct families? No; surely it cannot be! Yet the deep marks of conical teeth upon the iron pick are certainly those of the crocodile.
<|det|>text [72, 591, 861, 753]<|/det|>My eyes are fearfully bent upon the sea. I dread to see one of these monsters darting forth from its submarine caverns. I suppose Professor Liedenbrock was of my opinion too, and even shared my fears, for after having examined the pick, his eyes traversed the ocean from side to side. What a very bad notion that was of his, I thought to myself, to take soundings just here! He has disturbed some monstrous beast in its remote den, and if we are not attacked on our voyage.
<|det|>text [72, 761, 885, 810]<|/det|>I look at our guns and see that they are all right. My uncle notices it, and looks on approvingly.
<|det|>text [72, 817, 879, 886]<|/det|>Already widely disturbed regions on the surface of the water indicate some commotion below. The danger is approaching. We must be on the lookout.
<|det|>page_number [836, 927, 865, 944]<|/det|>57
<PAGE><|det|>header [104, 38, 160, 90]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>text [160, 38, 948, 87]<|/det|>What you have just read is a very different form of writing which is written in the first person.
<|det|>text [107, 87, 926, 131]<|/det|>This form of writing is called diary writing. This is yet another form of creative writing and it does have some special features. Let us list them.
<|det|>list [109, 133, 942, 438]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 133, 881, 179]<|/det|>- It is written in the first person and the day and date are carefully recorded.
<|det|>text [110, 180, 923, 226]<|/det|>- It is not only a chronicle of the events that have taken place on that day and date, but also a spontaneous expression of emotions.
<|det|>text [110, 226, 907, 274]<|/det|>- Sometimes the diary is given a name. Anne Frank called her diary, 'Kitty'.
<|det|>text [109, 274, 938, 367]<|/det|>- Usually, a diary is maintained by an individual and is not meant for public viewing. So, one can really be honest to the diary and write without inhibitions. However, in a classroom situation, perhaps one has to be diplomatic.
<|det|>text [110, 367, 944, 438]<|/det|>- When writing in a diary, you should keep in mind the normal rules of good writing. Be careful about sentence constructions, paragraphs, ornamentations in language, etc.
<|det|>title [135, 459, 198, 484]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [519, 454, 564, 490]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 497, 925, 580]<|/det|>Mind the format! Every entry must begin with the date and day of the week. Diary entries may also be signed off like letters. You have a wide choice of tenses to use. You may start with the past as you are trying to recall the incidents of the day and end with the present or the past when you are trying to sum up your emotions.
<|det|>title [113, 615, 838, 640]<|/det|>Now, attempt writing diary entries for the following situations.
<|det|>list [119, 649, 961, 932]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 650, 958, 720]<|/det|>1. Suppose your parents have left town and you are alone at home for a week. Write a diary entry for each day of the week describing what you did and how you managed.
<|det|>text [119, 720, 961, 790]<|/det|>2. Imagine your plane has crashed in the Amazonian rainforest. You are the lone survivor. In the form of a diary, narrate how you spent your days (at least seven) in the wilderness before you were rescued.
<|det|>text [119, 790, 951, 931]<|/det|>3. Imagine you have been selected to represent the school at some event (it could be a sports meet, a debate or a quiz). Write three diary entries—the first describing how you prepared for the selection process, the second describing how you felt after you were selected and the third describing what happened when you participated in the competition.
<|det|>page_number [142, 935, 172, 951]<|/det|>58
<PAGE><|det|>title [54, 45, 388, 85]<|/det|>Simple Tenses
<|det|>page_number [870, 46, 907, 83]<|/det|>7
<|det|>image [55, 155, 114, 202]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [118, 152, 890, 194]<|/det|>You have learnt that in a sentence, the form of the verb indicates the time when the action is taking place. Therefore, we say that
<|det|>text [55, 195, 888, 293]<|/det|>there are three tenses of a verb—the present (the time now), the past (the time then) and the future (the time to come). These tenses are further subdivided. Let us now look at the simple tenses—simple present, simple past and simple future.
<|det|>text [55, 307, 839, 331]<|/det|>This chart gives us an idea about the verb forms of these three tenses.
<|det|>table [59, 345, 902, 568]<|/det|><table><tr><td></td><td>First person</td><td>Second person</td><td>Third person</td></tr><tr><td>Simple present</td><td>I drive cars.</td><td>You drive cars.</td><td>He/She drives cars.</td></tr><tr><td>Simple past</td><td>We drive cars.</td><td>You drive cars.</td><td>They drive cars.</td></tr><tr><td>Simple future</td><td>I drove cars.</td><td>You drove cars.</td><td>He/She drove cars.</td></tr><tr><td>Simple past</td><td>We drove cars.</td><td>You drove cars.</td><td>They drove cars.</td></tr><tr><td>Simple future</td><td>I shall drive cars.</td><td>You will drive cars.</td><td>He/She will drive cars.</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>We shall drive cars.</td><td>You will drive cars.</td><td>They will drive cars.</td></tr></table>
<|det|>title [55, 594, 345, 617]<|/det|>Simple Present Tense
<|det|>text [45, 623, 451, 645]<|/det|>. We use the simple present tense—
<|det|>text [53, 645, 805, 668]<|/det|>◆ to say something that is always or generally true. For example,
<|det|>list [101, 669, 425, 711]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 669, 361, 688]<|/det|>• The earth is round.
<|det|>text [101, 691, 425, 711]<|/det|>• The sun sets in the west.
<|det|>text [53, 724, 865, 771]<|/det|>◆ to describe something that has been happening for a long period of time and is still continuing. For example,
<|det|>list [101, 772, 331, 815]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 772, 337, 792]<|/det|>• We live in Delhi.
<|det|>text [102, 795, 324, 815]<|/det|>• He is a teacher.
<|det|>text [53, 827, 831, 874]<|/det|>◆ to refer to something that happens regularly or to something that somebody does habitually. For example,
<|det|>list [101, 876, 331, 918]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 876, 291, 897]<|/det|>• A dog barks.
<|det|>text [102, 898, 330, 918]<|/det|>• He travels a lot.
<|det|>page_number [842, 930, 872, 948]<|/det|>59
<PAGE><|det|>title [124, 46, 361, 70]<|/det|>Simple Past Tense
<|det|>text [124, 76, 481, 99]<|/det|>We use the simple past tense—
<|det|>text [125, 100, 576, 170]<|/det|>◆ to refer to an action that has already been completed in the past. For example,
<|det|>list [174, 171, 520, 217]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 171, 425, 193]<|/det|>- It rained yesterday.
<|det|>text [174, 194, 519, 217]<|/det|>• She exercised last evening.
<|det|>image [768, 47, 816, 78]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [631, 54, 693, 80]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [621, 91, 955, 175]<|/det|>Adverbs and phrases like yesterday, a week ago, four months ago, etc. are usually added to sentences that describe past action.
<|det|>text [127, 227, 768, 251]<|/det|>◆ to indicate a habitual action in the past. For example,
<|det|>list [174, 252, 677, 300]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 252, 677, 275]<|/det|>• My father always played golf on Sundays.
<|det|>text [174, 276, 570, 299]<|/det|>• As a young man, I trekked a lot.
<|det|>title [131, 316, 393, 339]<|/det|>Simple Future Tense
<|det|>text [131, 345, 497, 367]<|/det|>We use the simple future tense—
<|det|>list [133, 367, 895, 536]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [133, 367, 893, 412]<|/det|>◆ in order to say what we think or know will happen in the future. For example,
<|det|>text [180, 413, 468, 433]<|/det|>• I shall swim tomorrow.
<|det|>text [180, 436, 517, 456]<|/det|>• Suman will visit next week.
<|det|>text [131, 468, 961, 513]<|/det|>- in order to state what will happen or will be done as a matter of habit or course. For example,
<|det|>text [174, 514, 783, 537]<|/det|>• During the lunar eclipse, the river will be turbulent.
<|det|>title [127, 566, 722, 591]<|/det|>A. Fill in the blanks according to the instructions.
<|det|>text [200, 599, 870, 668]<|/det|>1. As a child, I ____ regularly, but now I ____ daily. (simple past of swim, simple present of exercise)
<|det|>text [198, 669, 958, 737]<|/det|>2. I ____ the news with interest and ____ to learn more about the elections. (simple past of read and resolve)
<|det|>text [198, 737, 935, 783]<|/det|>3. Horrible accidents ____ place because of fire-crackers. (simple present of take)
<|det|>text [198, 784, 895, 853]<|/det|>4. He ____ his full support to the team and ____ to do so in the future. (simple past of extend and promise)
<|det|>text [198, 854, 948, 900]<|/det|>5. I ____ for my colleagues next Monday. (simple future of cook)
<|det|>page_number [155, 934, 186, 951]<|/det|>60
<PAGE><|det|>text [165, 51, 874, 98]<|/det|>6. They ____ themselves at the summer camp.
(simple future of enjoy)
<|det|>text [166, 98, 895, 142]<|/det|>7. We are very sorry and ____ how you ____. (simple present of understand and feel)
<|det|>text [166, 143, 862, 189]<|/det|>8. Hari ____ not ____ anything without his father's permission. (simple future of do)
<|det|>text [166, 189, 874, 258]<|/det|>9. ____ you ____ time to go through the draft? We ____ it to the council tomorrow. (simple future of find and submit)
<|det|>text [158, 258, 876, 328]<|/det|>10. He ____ at night, ____ during the day and ____ for four hours in the afternoon. (simple present of study, work and sleep)
<|det|>text [158, 328, 862, 397]<|/det|>11. I usually ____ up early in the morning, but tomorrow I ____ till nine in the morning. (simple present of got, simple future of sleep)
<|det|>text [158, 397, 880, 464]<|/det|>12. He ____ only a glass of milk every morning. However, from tomorrow, he ____ an apple. (simple present of drink, simple future of eat)
<|det|>text [73, 497, 641, 521]<|/det|>B. Rewrite the paragraph in simple past tense.
<|det|>text [157, 530, 914, 625]<|/det|>I live in a small apartment in Mumbai. I travel by local train to the suburbs every day. I cook my own meals. I wash my own clothes. I train young girls at the Women's Football Association. I get a monthly salary. It is not much, but I live happily.
<|det|>text [73, 652, 810, 698]<|/det|>C. Rewrite the paragraph using simple future tense wherever possible.
<|det|>text [154, 708, 870, 825]<|/det|>I drive to work every day. I take a bag, some books and a water bottle to work. On my way, I pick up a colleague. He is studying law. I encourage him to work sincerely. My friends at the office are planning a picnic. Our manager likes the idea. He agrees to accompany us on the trip. It is a group of forty.
<|det|>page_number [854, 929, 885, 947]<|/det|>61
<PAGE><|det|>header [19, 0, 307, 150]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>title [452, 48, 959, 85]<|/det|>The Chinese New Year
<|det|>text [121, 173, 885, 220]<|/det|>How do you celebrate the New Year? Read about how the Chinese celebrate New Year.
<|det|>text [122, 234, 961, 374]<|/det|>Many people around the world celebrate the New Year on 1 January. For over a billion others, it is just a dress rehearsal. The real festivities begin when, according to the Chinese calendar, the first new moon of the year makes its appearance in the night sky. In 2010, China and many other East Asian countries like Vietnam, Korea and Mongolia celebrated the lunar New Year on 14 February.
<|det|>text [130, 387, 954, 526]<|/det|>The Chinese New Year is a time for families to get together. Hundreds of millions of people in China, and hundreds of thousands elsewhere, hurry back to their home towns for the celebrations. Many will camp at airports and stations in order to get a ticket home on crowded buses, boats and trains. China's railways alone are expected to carry over 150 million people during the fifteen-day festival.
<|det|>text [127, 538, 960, 766]<|/det|>An ancient Chinese legend about a man-eating beast called Nien tells us how the celebration started. To scare off the creature, people covered their homes with the colour red and filled the air with loud noises. This led to the first New Year celebrations, and those traditions have continued for over 5,000 years. Even today, people paint parts of their homes red, hang red paper with lines expressing good wishes for wealth and happiness, and set off thousands of firecrackers. Other traditions include the distribution of red envelopes filled with money to children and unmarried adults; feasts of sweets, chicken and fish; and parades of dancing dragons and lions.
<|det|>text [125, 781, 902, 873]<|/det|>Every Chinese New Year is associated with one of the twelve animals found in the Chinese zodiac: the rat, ox, tiger, rabbit, dragon, snake, horse, sheep, monkey, rooster, dog and pig. 2010 was the year of the Tiger.
<|det|>page_number [157, 933, 188, 949]<|/det|>62
<PAGE><|det|>title [73, 48, 114, 70]<|/det|>A.
<|det|>title [156, 47, 403, 70]<|/det|>Answer the questions.
<|det|>list [156, 80, 896, 238]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [156, 80, 697, 102]<|/det|>1. When do the Chinese celebrate New Year?
<|det|>text [156, 104, 510, 125]<|/det|>2. How do they celebrate it?
<|det|>text [156, 126, 858, 169]<|/det|>3. What was the Chinese legend which talks about how the celebration started?
<|det|>text [156, 172, 753, 195]<|/det|>4. What other traditions do the people still follow?
<|det|>text [158, 195, 898, 238]<|/det|>5. What are the other things that the Chinese year is associated with?
<|det|>title [76, 272, 435, 295]<|/det|>B. Complete the sentences.
<|det|>text [157, 306, 754, 331]<|/det|>1. For more than a billion people, 1 January is just ____.
<|det|>text [157, 352, 817, 374]<|/det|>2. On 14 February 2010, China and ____.
<|det|>text [158, 376, 835, 398]<|/det|>3. In order to get a ticket home, many ____.
<|det|>text [158, 399, 910, 420]<|/det|>4. The Chinese New Year with all its traditions ____.
<|det|>text [158, 422, 799, 444]<|/det|>5. According to the Chinese, 2010 ____.
<|det|>title [79, 474, 773, 497]<|/det|>C. State whether these statements are true (T) or false (F).
<|det|>list [160, 504, 911, 667]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [165, 507, 907, 530]<|/det|>1. People around the world get together on the Chinese New Year.
<|det|>text [166, 531, 865, 576]<|/det|>2. During the fifteen-day festival, China's railways carry over 150 million people.
<|det|>text [165, 576, 890, 620]<|/det|>3. People paint their homes red and make loud noises to scare off the man-eating beast.
<|det|>text [164, 621, 867, 664]<|/det|>4. The fox, the snail and the tortoise are some of the animals found in the Chinese zodiac.
<|det|>title [79, 696, 808, 720]<|/det|>D. Find a synonym from the passage for each of these words.
<|det|>list [159, 728, 816, 868]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [163, 730, 359, 751]<|/det|>1. celebration
<|det|>text [163, 754, 369, 774]<|/det|>2. of the moon
<|det|>text [163, 776, 350, 798]<|/det|>3. to frighten
<|det|>text [163, 800, 352, 819]<|/det|>4. connected
<|det|>text [163, 821, 571, 843]<|/det|>5. having a lot of people or things
<|det|>text [163, 844, 813, 866]<|/det|>6. a story handed down from one generation to another
<|det|>page_number [851, 921, 882, 938]<|/det|>63
<PAGE><|det|>title [102, 42, 143, 82]<|/det|>8
<|det|>title [530, 48, 974, 83]<|/det|>Continuous Tense
<|det|>image [112, 150, 177, 201]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [187, 150, 928, 199]<|/det|>Verbs in the continuous tense will denote actions that have not been completed.
<|det|>text [112, 212, 974, 284]<|/det|>You have learnt how the form of the verb changes in the continuous tense, depending on the time. Take a look at the chart given below and revise how the verb forms change.
<|det|>table [117, 292, 967, 653]<|/det|><table><tr><td></td><td>First person</td><td>Second person</td><td>Third person</td></tr><tr><td>Present continuous</td><td>I am watching TV.</td><td>You are watching TV.</td><td>He is watching TV.</td></tr><tr><td>Past continuous</td><td>We was watching TV.</td><td>You were watching TV.</td><td>They are watching TV.</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>I was watching TV.</td><td>You were watching TV.</td><td>He was watching TV.</td></tr><tr><td>Continue</td><td>We were watching TV.</td><td>You were watching TV.</td><td>They were watching TV.</td></tr><tr><td>Future continuous</td><td>I shall be watching TV.</td><td>You will be watching TV.</td><td>He will be watching TV.</td></tr></table>
<|det|>title [113, 663, 465, 684]<|/det|>Present Continuous Tense
<|det|>text [112, 692, 570, 715]<|/det|>The present continuous tense is used—
<|det|>text [111, 717, 725, 763]<|/det|>◆ to describe an action that is happening now, at the time of speaking. For example,
<|det|>text [150, 765, 512, 787]<|/det|>• He is reading a book now.
<|det|>text [150, 789, 639, 812]<|/det|>• A nightingale is singing in the woods.
<|det|>list [150, 765, 639, 812]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [749, 678, 952, 802]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [130, 830, 192, 857]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [264, 827, 321, 868]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [127, 872, 880, 914]<|/det|>The present continuous tense draws attention to the continuity of action. It is usually associated with adverbs or phrases of present time such as now, at this moment, etc.
<|det|>image [749, 678, 952, 802]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [145, 943, 177, 960]<|/det|>64
<PAGE><|det|>text [62, 51, 911, 118]<|/det|>to describe an action that has been going on over a period of time, including the present, but may not be happening at the time of speaking. For example,
<|det|>list [113, 119, 567, 168]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 119, 393, 141]<|/det|>- I am learning Arabic.
<|det|>text [113, 142, 567, 168]<|/det|>• Swami is attending classes regularly.
<|det|>text [62, 175, 890, 200]<|/det|>◆ for an action that is going to take place in the near future. For example,
<|det|>list [113, 200, 606, 246]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 200, 482, 222]<|/det|>• I am going to listen to music.
<|det|>text [113, 223, 605, 246]<|/det|>• Wait, we are reaching in a few minutes.
<|det|>title [62, 261, 361, 283]<|/det|>Past Continuous Tense
<|det|>text [62, 291, 470, 313]<|/det|>The past continuous tense is used—
<|det|>list [62, 314, 878, 456]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [62, 314, 878, 360]<|/det|>◆ to describe an action going on at a point of time or over a period of time in the past. For example,
<|det|>text [113, 360, 495, 384]<|/det|>• We were strolling all evening.
<|det|>text [113, 384, 321, 406]<|/det|>- I was sleeping.
<|det|>list [62, 416, 878, 506]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [62, 416, 878, 461]<|/det|>◆ to describe an action that was in progress when another action took place. For example,
<|det|>text [113, 462, 740, 485]<|/det|>• They were working in the laboratory when I arrived.
<|det|>text [113, 485, 588, 508]<|/det|>- When I met him, he was baking cakes.
<|det|>text [61, 511, 895, 579]<|/det|>In these two sentences, we see that the verbs in the past continuous tense are describing longer actions, whereas the verbs in the simple past are describing shorter actions.
<|det|>title [62, 594, 391, 616]<|/det|>Future Continuous Tense
<|det|>text [62, 623, 427, 645]<|/det|>The future continuous is used—
<|det|>list [62, 646, 905, 772]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [62, 646, 905, 691]<|/det|>◆ to denote an action that will be in progress at some point in the future. For example,
<|det|>text [113, 692, 590, 715]<|/det|>• Grandfather will be eating at that time.
<|det|>text [61, 725, 717, 749]<|/det|>◆ to denote future events that are planned. For example,
<|det|>text [113, 749, 675, 772]<|/det|>• The Cabinet will be announcing the next plan.
<|det|>text [62, 795, 892, 866]<|/det|>A. Mark whether the highlighted verbs in these sentences are simple present (SPr), present continuous (PrC), simple past (SP), past continuous (PC), simple future (SF) or future continuous (FC).
<|det|>list [133, 875, 580, 921]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [133, 875, 580, 898]<|/det|>1. Why are you not carrying a bag?
<|det|>text [133, 898, 580, 921]<|/det|>2. The cook will cut the vegetables.
<|det|>page_number [845, 935, 876, 953]<|/det|>65
<PAGE><|det|>text [198, 51, 642, 74]<|/det|>3. She finished the letter. I posted it.
<|det|>text [199, 75, 642, 98]<|/det|>4. We were thinking of visiting you.
<|det|>text [200, 98, 938, 121]<|/det|>5. Mira sold her paintings at an exhibition hall built last year.
<|det|>text [201, 122, 705, 144]<|/det|>6. When Sita arrived, I was taking a bath.
<|det|>text [201, 145, 668, 167]<|/det|>7. The train will be leaving in an hour.
<|det|>text [200, 167, 942, 191]<|/det|>8. He was studying in the bedroom when the lights went out.
<|det|>text [201, 192, 652, 214]<|/det|>9. Sam visits his uncle every Sunday.
<|det|>text [194, 214, 965, 237]<|/det|>10. We shall be trekking in the area before he leaves for London.
<|det|>text [194, 237, 875, 284]<|/det|>11. When I visited the monument the last time, I found it crumbling.
<|det|>text [194, 284, 956, 327]<|/det|>12. My sister is washing a lot of clothes as she will be going out of town.
<|det|>text [194, 329, 918, 375]<|/det|>13. The children are playing outside, while their mothers are drinking tea.
<|det|>text [195, 375, 862, 421]<|/det|>14. Rahim was walking down the road when he noticed something strange.
<|det|>text [195, 421, 900, 466]<|/det|>15. Jiya's father was driving the car when the accident took place. No one was injured.
<|det|>title [117, 490, 970, 536]<|/det|>B. Change the tense of the verb in each of the sentences according to the instructions in the brackets. Make necessary changes.
<|det|>text [207, 545, 885, 589]<|/det|>1. We tied his hands and feet and held him fast. (present continuous)
<|det|>text [207, 590, 838, 613]<|/det|>2. The police guarded the roads. (future continuous)
<|det|>text [207, 613, 950, 636]<|/det|>3. He ran so fast that he became breathless. (past continuous)
<|det|>text [207, 636, 956, 658]<|/det|>4. They pushed and jostled in the crowd. (present continuous)
<|det|>text [207, 658, 945, 681]<|/det|>5. I had not received any letter from him. (future continuous)
<|det|>text [207, 681, 969, 727]<|/det|>6. I read an article in the newspaper about what the government plans to achieve in five years. (present continuous)
<|det|>text [207, 727, 907, 769]<|/det|>7. My father sold the old car and bought a new one. (future continuous)
<|det|>text [207, 771, 668, 818]<|/det|>8. The dog happily chases its own tail for hours. (present continuous)
<|det|>text [207, 818, 643, 865]<|/det|>9. He left for Delhi. He took a new job. (future continuous)
<|det|>text [194, 866, 641, 935]<|/det|>10. He came to my house to tell me what has happened. (present continuous)
<|det|>image [720, 768, 951, 935]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [143, 939, 175, 958]<|/det|>66
<PAGE><|det|>title [65, 51, 425, 91]<|/det|>Rishi's Day Out
<|det|>header [735, 0, 999, 142]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>text [62, 156, 897, 225]<|/det|>Rishi goes to a restaurant with his parents to celebrate his birthday. His parents have asked him to order what he wants for dinner. Read the conversation between Rishi and the waiter.
<|det|>text [61, 240, 762, 266]<|/det|>Waiter: Good evening, sir. Have you decided on the order?
<|det|>text [62, 278, 851, 350]<|/det|>Rishi: Yes, we have. To begin with, we'd like two prawn cocktails. My mother is a vegetarian. So, she'll have a tomato juice. Could you spice it up a little with some tabasco sauce?
<|det|>text [61, 362, 885, 388]<|/det|>Waiter: Yes, of course. Would you like some appetiser to go with that?
<|det|>text [62, 399, 822, 425]<|/det|>Rishi: Yes, why not. Something crunchy maybe and vegetarian!
<|det|>text [61, 437, 780, 462]<|/det|>Waiter: Well, the stuffed mushrooms are our chef's speciality.
<|det|>text [62, 475, 601, 499]<|/det|>Rishi: How many pieces does a plate have?
<|det|>text [61, 513, 271, 535]<|/det|>Waiter: Six, sir.
<|det|>text [61, 550, 738, 575]<|/det|>Rishi: That should do nicely for us. Then, a plate of that.
<|det|>text [58, 588, 767, 613]<|/det|>Waiter: Very good, sir. Will you order the main course now?
<|det|>text [58, 625, 885, 694]<|/det|>Rishi: Yes, we've already decided. I'll have the Chef's Grill Platter. My father will have the Chicken Sizzler. And my mother wants to try the Frivolous Fettuccine. What is that?
<|det|>text [56, 709, 890, 757]<|/det|>Waiter: It's a pasta dish with zucchini and sun-dried tomatoes baked in a cheesy sauce.
<|det|>text [58, 770, 744, 794]<|/det|>Rishi: Well, my mother likes cheese. So, she'll have that.
<|det|>text [56, 808, 903, 897]<|/det|>Waiter: Let me just repeat it all. Two prawn cocktails and a tomato juice. One stuffed mushrooms as the appetiser. And for the entree, one Chef's Grill Platter, one Chicken Sizzler and one Frivolous Fettuccine.
<|det|>page_number [843, 932, 876, 948]<|/det|>67
<PAGE><|det|>text [105, 51, 354, 76]<|/det|>Rishi: That's right.
<|det|>text [105, 90, 540, 113]<|/det|>Waiter: And what about dessert, sir?
<|det|>text [105, 127, 942, 174]<|/det|>Rishi: Well, the one called Mango Euphoria sounds nice. But, I think we'll order the dessert later.
<|det|>text [105, 190, 940, 215]<|/det|>Waiter: Very good, sir. Would you like mineral water or normal water?
<|det|>text [105, 229, 956, 279]<|/det|>Rishi: A bottle of mineral water, please. And how long will it take? I'm quite hungry.
<|det|>text [105, 293, 567, 316]<|/det|>Waiter: We'll serve in five minutes, sir.
<|det|>text [105, 331, 427, 355]<|/det|>Rishi: Good. Thank you.
<|det|>text [105, 370, 390, 394]<|/det|>Waiter: Thank you, sir.
<|det|>text [105, 424, 609, 447]<|/det|>Now, work in pairs and enact this situation.
<|det|>text [104, 458, 951, 528]<|/det|>Hafiz and Jimmy decide to celebrate their big win at the debate competition. They go to a South Indian restaurant for lunch. Work in pairs and enact the roles of the two friends discussing what to order.
<|det|>page_number [152, 945, 186, 963]<|/det|>68
<PAGE><|det|>title [62, 35, 395, 70]<|/det|>Perfect Tenses
<|det|>header [863, 38, 896, 78]<|/det|>9
<|det|>image [62, 144, 118, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 144, 766, 191]<|/det|>The perfect tenses show that an action has already been completed. The word perfect means completed.
<|det|>list [61, 201, 837, 456]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 201, 837, 248]<|/det|>The present perfect tense tells us that an action is complete with respect to the present. For example,
<|det|>text [114, 248, 817, 294]<|/det|>- I have seen this movie twice this week. (And the week isn't over yet.)
<|det|>text [61, 306, 905, 354]<|/det|>The past perfect tense tells us that an action took place before another action in the past. For example,
<|det|>text [114, 354, 611, 375]<|/det|>• Mala had left before I reached the office.
<|det|>text [61, 387, 823, 435]<|/det|>- The future perfect tense talks about an action that will occur in the future before some other action. For example,
<|det|>text [114, 435, 631, 458]<|/det|>• I will have arrived before you come home.
<|det|>title [62, 485, 351, 508]<|/det|>Present Perfect Tense
<|det|>text [62, 516, 396, 539]<|/det|>The present perfect is used—
<|det|>list [61, 540, 837, 636]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 540, 837, 588]<|/det|>to indicate that an action has been completed just now or a little while ago. For example,
<|det|>text [114, 588, 667, 611]<|/det|>• He has just finished correcting answer scripts.
<|det|>text [114, 611, 495, 635]<|/det|>• The sun has risen a while ago.
<|det|>text [58, 644, 823, 690]<|/det|>to express an action whose time is not given and is not definite. For example,
<|det|>text [114, 691, 590, 715]<|/det|>• We have known her to be very helpful.
<|det|>text [57, 725, 868, 774]<|/det|>to describe a past event when we think more about its effect on the present. For example,
<|det|>text [111, 774, 750, 798]<|/det|>• I have broken my glasses. (So, I cannot see properly.)
<|det|>text [55, 807, 871, 856]<|/det|>to denote an action that started some time ago and is continuing till the time of speaking. For example,
<|det|>list [111, 857, 635, 904]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 857, 635, 879]<|/det|>• We have known the family for many years.
<|det|>text [111, 880, 527, 904]<|/det|>• Manju has been ill since Sunday.
<|det|>page_number [845, 935, 876, 950]<|/det|>69
<PAGE><|det|>title [136, 35, 201, 64]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [521, 26, 574, 66]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 75, 929, 160]<|/det|>Both the simple past tense and the present perfect tense express an action completed in the past. But the simple past tense expresses an action completed in the past, but not linked to the present. The present perfect tense, however, denotes an action that is completed but connected to the present.
<|det|>text [132, 161, 926, 202]<|/det|>The present perfect tense is never used with adverbs or phrases of past time like yesterday, last month, etc.
<|det|>text [132, 205, 599, 225]<|/det|>We do not say. He has gone to the museum yesterday.
<|det|>text [132, 227, 502, 248]<|/det|>We say, He went to the museum yesterday.
<|det|>title [114, 281, 355, 302]<|/det|>Past Perfect Tense
<|det|>text [114, 314, 586, 336]<|/det|>We use the past perfect tense to denote—
<|det|>text [114, 337, 911, 384]<|/det|>◆ an action completed before a given point of time. This moment or point of time may be indicated or may be understood.
<|det|>text [158, 385, 710, 406]<|/det|>• When I reached the station, the train had left.
<|det|>text [158, 408, 627, 432]<|/det|>• I had completed my training by noon.
<|det|>title [135, 464, 201, 490]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [516, 457, 564, 495]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 504, 928, 546]<|/det|>If two actions occurred in the past, with one action taking place earlier than the other, the past perfect is used for the earlier action and the simple past for the later action. Thus, we say,
<|det|>text [158, 547, 581, 565]<|/det|>• The exam had started when I reached school.
<|det|>text [132, 566, 916, 607]<|/det|>The past perfect tense is used with such verbs as hope, expect, think to indicate that a past hope or desire remained unfulfilled.
<|det|>text [158, 608, 686, 627]<|/det|>• I had believed that I would win the tournament but I did not.
<|det|>title [114, 667, 385, 688]<|/det|>Future Perfect Tense
<|det|>text [114, 697, 494, 720]<|/det|>The future perfect tense is used—
<|det|>text [114, 721, 945, 789]<|/det|>to refer to the completion of an action by a certain time in the future. The tense is normally used with a time expression beginning with by, such as, by that time, by then or by next week.
<|det|>text [158, 790, 888, 812]<|/det|>• The Minister will have announced his decision by next week.
<|det|>text [158, 813, 856, 836]<|/det|>• They will have completed the assignment by next summer.
<|det|>text [158, 837, 802, 860]<|/det|>• The jury will have given its verdict by noon tomorrow.
<|det|>text [158, 860, 893, 906]<|/det|>- By next week, we shall have reached an agreement regarding the factory.
<|det|>page_number [142, 931, 177, 950]<|/det|>70
<PAGE><|det|>text [58, 44, 856, 112]<|/det|>A. A verb is highlighted in each of these sentences and its tense is mentioned in the brackets. State whether the tenses mentioned are correct. If not, correct them.
<|det|>text [148, 121, 842, 144]<|/det|>1. When I arrived, the members were eating. (simple past)
<|det|>text [148, 147, 582, 169]<|/det|>2. He failed last year. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [148, 171, 842, 193]<|/det|>3. I shall have done this exercise by 9 a.m. (future perfect)
<|det|>text [148, 196, 695, 219]<|/det|>4. They are going to the zoo. (simple present)
<|det|>text [148, 221, 725, 244]<|/det|>5. Rima was working in the office. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [148, 246, 777, 269]<|/det|>6. Kolkata stands on the Hooghly River. (simple past)
<|det|>text [148, 270, 876, 293]<|/det|>7. The show commenced after we had reached. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [148, 295, 800, 338]<|/det|>8. When I reach home, father will be cooking. (present continuous)
<|det|>text [148, 341, 890, 365]<|/det|>9. Dr Sen has taught many children in this area. (present perfect)
<|det|>text [137, 367, 828, 410]<|/det|>10. Rahul was driving his bike when it started to rain. (past continuous)
<|det|>text [136, 413, 872, 436]<|/det|>11. She has been a social activist for many years. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [136, 438, 890, 460]<|/det|>12. The tennis tournament commences next week. (simple future)
<|det|>text [137, 462, 805, 485]<|/det|>13. I have finished this canvas just now. (present perfect)
<|det|>text [137, 487, 736, 510]<|/det|>14. I was cycling the whole morning. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [137, 511, 844, 557]<|/det|>15. The criminals had fled before the security guards arrived. (past continuous)
<|det|>text [55, 583, 836, 607]<|/det|>B. Fill in the blanks according to the instructions in the brackets.
<|det|>text [136, 615, 896, 661]<|/det|>1. We ____ in Delhi for ten years. (present perfect tense of live)
<|det|>text [135, 664, 880, 711]<|/det|>2. The plumber ____ repairing the damaged pipe when it started to rain. (past perfect tense of finish)
<|det|>text [136, 712, 842, 758]<|/det|>3. Before you reach the station, the train ____. (future perfect tense of leave)
<|det|>text [136, 760, 819, 806]<|/det|>4. You ____ the task by the time your guests arrive. (future perfect tense of complete)
<|det|>text [136, 807, 867, 852]<|/det|>5. Ravi ____ for Mumbai. (present perfect tense of depart)
<|det|>text [137, 854, 842, 920]<|/det|>6. Sarah ____ a brilliant article for the science magazine, but it wasn't published. (past perfect tense of write)
<|det|>page_number [828, 927, 865, 948]<|/det|>71
<PAGE><|det|>text [214, 55, 896, 100]<|/det|>7. He ____ never ____ such a wonderful sight. (past perfect tense of see)
<|det|>text [214, 102, 948, 148]<|/det|>8. Villagers ____ the breach in the embankment by then. (future perfect tense of notice)
<|det|>text [214, 150, 928, 196]<|/det|>9. Heera said, 'I ____ my homework.' (present perfect tense of do)
<|det|>text [204, 198, 921, 243]<|/det|>10. You ____ the news by then. (future perfect tense of hear)
<|det|>text [204, 246, 960, 293]<|/det|>11. The yacht ____ before the rescue team arrived.
(past perfect tense of sink)
<|det|>text [204, 294, 950, 340]<|/det|>12. We ____ him to be a generous man. (present perfect tense of know)
<|det|>text [127, 377, 935, 446]<|/det|>C. Rewrite these sentences using the tense specified within the brackets. At least one verb will have to be changed. Make other necessary changes.
<|det|>text [204, 454, 900, 501]<|/det|>1. The play will begin when you reach the theatre. (future perfect)
<|det|>text [203, 503, 948, 526]<|/det|>2. The boss arrived before the employees came. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [203, 528, 968, 551]<|/det|>3. The politician had just arrived for the meeting. (present perfect)
<|det|>text [203, 552, 830, 575]<|/det|>4. We had known him for a while. (present perfect)
<|det|>text [203, 576, 963, 600]<|/det|>5. We shall be reaching the station around then. (future perfect)
<|det|>text [203, 601, 915, 645]<|/det|>6. I had been working for two hours when he arrived. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [201, 648, 835, 671]<|/det|>7. You will see me before you leave. (future perfect)
<|det|>text [201, 673, 913, 720]<|/det|>8. The Town Hall had been closed when we reached. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [120, 753, 905, 800]<|/det|>D. Join these pairs of sentences using the past perfect tense and making changes where necessary.
<|det|>text [201, 808, 862, 833]<|/det|>1. She finished sweeping the floor. The telephone rang.
<|det|>text [201, 834, 650, 857]<|/det|>2. The students left. It began to rain.
<|det|>text [201, 858, 890, 881]<|/det|>3. The thieves wiped the place clean. We reached home.
<|det|>text [201, 883, 908, 907]<|/det|>4. Ratul submitted an application form. He left for college.
<|det|>page_number [149, 945, 183, 963]<|/det|>72
<PAGE><|det|>text [128, 44, 890, 68]<|/det|>5. The cat jumped upon the table. I did not have time to react.
<|det|>text [129, 70, 909, 93]<|/det|>6. I made up my mind. My parents arrived to discuss the matter.
<|det|>text [129, 94, 740, 117]<|/det|>7. I reached the station. Usha reached before me.
<|det|>text [129, 118, 640, 143]<|/det|>8. I planned it. I executed it successfully.
<|det|>list [128, 44, 909, 143]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [128, 175, 643, 201]<|/det|>Change the following sentences as directed.
<|det|>list [131, 204, 918, 881]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [145, 208, 911, 232]<|/det|>1. The doctor has just left the clinic. (present continuous tense)
<|det|>text [145, 234, 830, 258]<|/det|>2. We leave for London this evening. (simple past tense)
<|det|>text [145, 260, 911, 283]<|/det|>3. We leave for London this evening. (future perfect continuous)
<|det|>text [145, 285, 876, 309]<|/det|>4. They have been trying to contact you. (past perfect tense)
<|det|>text [145, 310, 904, 355]<|/det|>5. Grandmother went for a walk every morning. (simple future tense)
<|det|>text [145, 358, 761, 382]<|/det|>6. I walk to school every day. (simple future tense)
<|det|>text [145, 384, 767, 407]<|/det|>7. I have cleared the cupboard. (past perfect tense)
<|det|>text [145, 409, 809, 432]<|/det|>8. They have finished their work. (future perfect tense)
<|det|>text [145, 434, 806, 478]<|/det|>9. Rakesh was waiting at Nirula's for his friend. (future continuous tense)
<|det|>text [131, 482, 788, 505]<|/det|>10. We are watching a movie. (past continuous tense)
<|det|>text [131, 507, 682, 530]<|/det|>11. He speaks rudely. (present perfect tense)
<|det|>text [131, 531, 908, 578]<|/det|>12. I will have been living in Delhi for many years. (past perfect continuous)
<|det|>text [131, 580, 845, 604]<|/det|>13. When does your school reopen? (simple present tense)
<|det|>text [131, 605, 895, 629]<|/det|>14. My grandmother exercises regularly. (simple present tense)
<|det|>text [131, 630, 901, 654]<|/det|>15. The river is flowing under the bridge. (simple present tense)
<|det|>text [131, 655, 861, 679]<|/det|>16. The police are looking for clues. (past continuous tense)
<|det|>text [131, 680, 865, 728]<|/det|>17. I am listening to the president's speech. (past continuous tense)
<|det|>text [131, 730, 870, 754]<|/det|>18. The children are going to school. (past continuous tense)
<|det|>text [131, 756, 654, 779]<|/det|>19. I want to go home. (simple past tense)
<|det|>text [131, 780, 867, 825]<|/det|>20. Reema will go to the library after school. (simple present tense)
<|det|>text [131, 828, 905, 877]<|/det|>21. My mother is making dinner and my father is working in the garage. (future tense)
<|det|>page_number [856, 945, 890, 963]<|/det|>73
