<PAGE><|det|>title [106, 42, 136, 63]<|/det|>F
<|det|>text [182, 42, 761, 63]<|/det|>Choose the correct verb from those in the brackets.
<|det|>list [181, 72, 885, 451]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [182, 72, 885, 119]<|/det|>1. I ____ in Delhi for many years. (have been living/am living)
<|det|>text [181, 120, 880, 164]<|/det|>2. The dogs ____ all night. (are barking/have been barking)
<|det|>text [181, 165, 852, 210]<|/det|>3. I ____ him since we left school last year. (haven't seen/didn't see)
<|det|>text [180, 213, 930, 259]<|/det|>4. Our school ____ on the first of July. (reopens/will reopen)
<|det|>text [180, 261, 945, 306]<|/det|>5. We ____ for London this evening. (are leaving/ leave)
<|det|>text [179, 309, 910, 354]<|/det|>6. I ____ a phone call from him yesterday. (had received/received)
<|det|>text [179, 357, 835, 406]<|/det|>7. I ____ when the phone rang. (had been sleeping/was sleeping)
<|det|>text [178, 407, 866, 453]<|/det|>8. It ____ for two hours. (is raining/has been raining)
<|det|>page_number [120, 929, 152, 948]<|/det|>74
<PAGE><|det|>title [76, 35, 406, 75]<|/det|>Letter Writing
<|det|>header [714, 0, 978, 135]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>image [75, 147, 136, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 144, 893, 214]<|/det|>You have learnt that we write informal letters to friends and relatives and formal letters to principals of school and colleges and to official bodies.
<|det|>text [75, 228, 506, 252]<|/det|>Here is a sample of an informal letter.
<|det|>text [99, 287, 252, 309]<|/det|>Top Sea View
<|det|>text [102, 311, 282, 331]<|/det|>47 Bandra Point
<|det|>text [102, 334, 296, 354]<|/det|>Mumbai 400 007
<|det|>text [100, 371, 304, 394]<|/det|>26 February 20XX
<|det|>text [101, 410, 231, 430]<|/det|>Dear Mona
<|det|>text [99, 447, 882, 496]<|/det|>I have just returned from my holiday in Shimla. I thought I would write to you immediately and tell you all about my trip.
<|det|>text [98, 508, 880, 716]<|/det|>Although I was most reluctant to be away from home for a week, the trip turned out to be most memorable. It was quite a long train journey from Mumbai to Shimla, but since I slept most of the way, time passed by quickly. Two of my cousins in Shimla were my constant companions. What I liked most during my stay there was the location of my aunt's house where we had put up. Their home was on a slope overlooking a pine forest. Coming from the congested city of Mumbai, breathing the pure fresh air of the hills was an extremely welcome change.
<|det|>text [97, 729, 869, 825]<|/det|>Another thing that I loved about the place were the small tracks built all over the area because of which we could go on long walks and be surrounded by nature. It was an extremely enjoyable feeling to take an average of three walks a day with my cousins.
<|det|>text [98, 837, 845, 886]<|/det|>I recommend Shimla as a vacation destination especially for someone who loves nature as much as you do, because of its clean
<|det|>page_number [845, 927, 876, 946]<|/det|>75
<PAGE><|det|>text [125, 53, 927, 126]<|/det|>surroundings, its beauty and the friendly people. For city folks like you and me, such a trip is essential to realise that we are missing a lot by living in Mumbai and Kolkata.
<|det|>text [129, 140, 800, 166]<|/det|>I would be happy to make a trip with you in the near future.
<|det|>text [130, 180, 258, 203]<|/det|>Yours truly
<|det|>text [132, 205, 195, 225]<|/det|>Anita
<|det|>image [108, 258, 168, 306]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [176, 256, 948, 300]<|/det|>You have learnt what the parts of an informal letter are. It is very important to remember each of these. In your exams, marks will be
<|det|>text [110, 301, 948, 375]<|/det|>allotted for the layout of the letter as well as the content and, of course, the language. Look at the marks that will be allotted to each of these parts in the exam.
<|det|>text [114, 391, 372, 413]<|/det|>◆ Layout of the letter:
<|det|>list [159, 415, 501, 528]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 415, 501, 435]<|/det|>- sender's address: 1½ marks
<|det|>text [161, 438, 350, 457]<|/det|>• date: ½ mark
<|det|>text [161, 460, 406, 481]<|/det|>- salutation: ½ mark
<|det|>text [161, 484, 433, 505]<|/det|>- subscription: ½ mark
<|det|>text [160, 514, 396, 535]<|/det|>- signature: ½ mark
<|det|>text [114, 544, 773, 568]<|/det|>◆ Content of the letter: (in the context of the sample letter)
<|det|>list [159, 569, 798, 614]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [159, 569, 625, 590]<|/det|>• what you found most exciting: ½ mark
<|det|>text [160, 591, 798, 614]<|/det|>• two reasons explaining why you were excited: 2 marks
<|det|>text [115, 627, 472, 650]<|/det|>◆ Language and style: 5 marks
<|det|>text [114, 666, 785, 689]<|/det|>A. Now, attempt these informal letters in your notebook.
<|det|>text [186, 695, 938, 809]<|/det|>1. You have got the address of someone in Japan who is looking for a pen friend. Fortunately, he/she can read and write English. Write your first letter to him/her saying that you would like to be his/her pen friend. In your letter, you are expected to give your interests and other relevant details.
<|det|>text [185, 810, 911, 923]<|/det|>2. Write a letter to your friend in Delhi describing to him/her how two of your school friends and you got together and watched the FIFA World Cup Finals. Tell him/her how much you missed his/her company and the things that you could have done had he/she been present.
<|det|>page_number [135, 928, 171, 946]<|/det|>76
<PAGE><|det|>text [150, 38, 856, 104]<|/det|>3. A friend from abroad is coming to visit you. Write a short letter giving him/her directions to enable him/her to reach your house.
<|det|>text [151, 105, 888, 152]<|/det|>4. Write a letter to your friend wishing him for his birthday and explaining why you cannot attend his party.
<|det|>text [152, 152, 905, 293]<|/det|>5. A friend of yours is a very good actor on stage. You have organised a Harry Potter theme party for some underprivileged children. Write a letter to your friend requesting him/her to impersonate any character from any one of the novels in order to entertain the children. Give him/her other details of the programme which he/she requires.
<|det|>text [155, 293, 880, 363]<|/det|>6. You have just celebrated Teacher's Day in school. Write a letter to your friend in another city about the things you did on that day to make your teachers feel special.
<|det|>image [78, 381, 141, 428]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [157, 386, 544, 410]<|/det|>Here is a sample of a formal letter.
<|det|>text [108, 469, 314, 493]<|/det|>St John's Academy
<|det|>text [111, 494, 316, 514]<|/det|>12 Central Avenue
<|det|>text [111, 516, 291, 535]<|/det|>Kolkata 700 012
<|det|>text [109, 553, 288, 574]<|/det|>25 March 20XX
<|det|>text [109, 590, 260, 613]<|/det|>The Principal
<|det|>text [110, 614, 318, 636]<|/det|>St Vincent's School
<|det|>text [111, 638, 317, 660]<|/det|>15 Aliipore Avenue
<|det|>text [110, 661, 294, 681]<|/det|>Kolkata 700 029
<|det|>text [106, 698, 706, 723]<|/det|>Subject: Invitation to judge One-Act Play Competition
<|det|>text [111, 737, 205, 757]<|/det|>Dear Sir
<|det|>text [116, 774, 877, 871]<|/det|>hank you very much for your letter dated 12 March 20XX, requesting me to be one of the judges at the prestigious One-Act Play Competition to be held in your school on 15 April 20XX at 5 p.m. appreciate your invitation and the honour you have reposed on me.
<|det|>page_number [845, 930, 876, 946]<|/det|>77
<PAGE><|det|>text [135, 58, 892, 130]<|/det|>However, due to a prior engagement, I regret my inability to attend the One-Act Play Competition and act as one of the judges for the same. The inconvenience caused is regretted.
<|det|>text [138, 145, 322, 219]<|/det|>Thanking you
Yours faithfully
Kiran Deshpande
<|det|>image [141, 264, 191, 301]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [143, 269, 205, 295]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [141, 307, 600, 328]<|/det|>Note the difference in tone in formal and informal letters.
<|det|>image [512, 262, 558, 300]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [713, 262, 758, 300]<|/det|>
<|det|>table [141, 330, 925, 678]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Name</td><td>Dear Mr/Mrs/Ms</td><td>Hi/Hello Kiran</td></tr><tr><td>Previous contact</td><td>This is with reference to your letter dated...</td><td>Thanks for your letter...</td></tr><tr><td>Reason for writing</td><td>I am writing in connection with...</td><td>Please note that...</td></tr><tr><td>Asking for information</td><td>Could you give me some information about...</td><td>Please send me...</td></tr><tr><td>Requests</td><td>I would be grateful if...</td><td>Please could you.../Could you...</td></tr><tr><td>Closing</td><td>I am looking forward to.../ Regards</td><td>Bye.../Take care</td></tr><tr><td>Complimentary close/Subscription</td><td>Yours faithfully/ Yours sincerely</td><td>Yours lovingly/ Your loving</td></tr></table>
<|det|>image [119, 713, 177, 763]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 712, 911, 780]<|/det|>You have learnt what the parts of a formal letter are. It is very important to remember each of these. Look at the marks that will be allotted to each of these parts in the exam.
<|det|>text [117, 795, 378, 817]<|/det|>◆ Layout of the letter:
<|det|>list [160, 819, 667, 888]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [161, 819, 567, 839]<|/det|>- writer's address and date: 1 mark
<|det|>text [161, 841, 666, 862]<|/det|>• name and address of the receiver: ½ mark
<|det|>text [161, 864, 545, 886]<|/det|>- subject and salutation: ½ mark
<|det|>page_number [142, 930, 175, 948]<|/det|>78
<PAGE><|det|>list [111, 35, 737, 192]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 35, 397, 58]<|/det|>- subscription: ½ mark
<|det|>text [111, 60, 361, 83]<|/det|>• signature: ½ mark
<|det|>text [62, 98, 737, 122]<|/det|>◆ Content of the letter: (in the context of the sample letter)
<|det|>list [111, 123, 518, 192]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 123, 518, 143]<|/det|>• date of the competition: ½ mark
<|det|>text [111, 145, 432, 166]<|/det|>• venue and time: ½ mark
<|det|>text [111, 168, 515, 192]<|/det|>• reason for not attending: 1 mark
<|det|>text [62, 208, 432, 232]<|/det|>◆ Language and style: 5 marks
<|det|>title [62, 250, 730, 273]<|/det|>B. Now, attempt these formal letters in your notebook.
<|det|>list [145, 278, 896, 790]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [145, 278, 862, 325]<|/det|>1. Write a letter to the local police complaining about a theft that took place the previous night at your neighbour's flat.
<|det|>text [145, 326, 896, 442]<|/det|>2. You are the Secretary of the Photography Club of your school. You plan to organise an exhibition of some of the activities of your club during the year. Write a letter to the principal of your school inviting him/her to inaugurate the exhibition, stating some of the items that will be displayed.
<|det|>text [147, 443, 874, 583]<|/det|>3. You are a budding tennis star but there aren't enough facilities for the improvement of your genius. Write a letter to the Secretary of the Lawn Tennis Association of your state bringing to his notice the lack of facilities and suggesting what could be done to encourage young tennis players like you, stating some of the incentives that could be provided.
<|det|>text [147, 583, 836, 696]<|/det|>4. You have recently purchased a book from a famous bookstore. On going home, you discover you have been given a different book. Write a letter to the bookstore complaining about their services and requesting them to change the book at the earliest.
<|det|>text [146, 697, 895, 789]<|/det|>5. Write a letter to the municipal authorities of your town objecting to the construction of a supermarket close to your house, giving reasons as to why the construction work should be abandoned.
<|det|>page_number [838, 935, 868, 952]<|/det|>79
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 293, 146]<|/det|>10
<|det|>header [414, 44, 959, 78]<|/det|>Modals and Auxiliaries
<|det|>image [105, 159, 164, 208]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 158, 945, 206]<|/det|>An auxiliary is a helping verb. In a sentence, an auxiliary is always used with another verb. There are two types of auxiliary verbs.
<|det|>list [105, 213, 957, 378]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 213, 957, 282]<|/det|>◆ primary auxiliaries: The words be, have and do are called primary auxiliaries. The forms of be are is, am, are, was and were. The forms of have are have, has and had. The forms of do are do, does and did.
<|det|>text [105, 283, 933, 378]<|/det|>- modal auxiliaries: The words can, could, may, might, shall, should, must, will, would, used, need, dare, ought express attitudes like permission, possibility, etc. Modal auxiliaries are also called modals. They do not change their forms.
<|det|>title [110, 401, 365, 425]<|/det|>Primary Auxiliaries
<|det|>title [110, 433, 148, 453]<|/det|>Be
<|det|>list [105, 460, 891, 722]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 460, 840, 484]<|/det|>◆ It is used in the formation of continuous tenses. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 485, 516, 506]<|/det|>• Mother is knitting a sweater.
<|det|>text [104, 516, 890, 562]<|/det|>It is used to show that the action is more important than the doer. For example,
<|det|>text [150, 563, 590, 585]<|/det|>• The cupboard was opened by Kirti.
<|det|>text [101, 595, 890, 641]<|/det|>- When followed by an infinitive, it is used to indicate a plan or an arrangement. For example,
<|det|>text [149, 642, 618, 663]<|/det|>• We are to finish the work in a month.
<|det|>text [101, 675, 721, 698]<|/det|>It is also used to denote a command. For example,
<|det|>text [150, 700, 545, 722]<|/det|>• You are to pick it up right now.
<|det|>title [102, 748, 175, 768]<|/det|>Have
<|det|>list [101, 775, 834, 900]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 775, 735, 798]<|/det|>This is used to form the perfect tenses. For example,
<|det|>text [150, 799, 595, 821]<|/det|>• The artists have painted beautifully.
<|det|>text [101, 830, 865, 877]<|/det|>It is used with the infinitive to indicate some kind of obligation. For example,
<|det|>text [150, 877, 565, 899]<|/det|>• They have to return immediately.
<|det|>page_number [134, 932, 165, 949]<|/det|>80
<PAGE><|det|>title [48, 42, 92, 63]<|/det|>Do
<|det|>text [47, 65, 851, 111]<|/det|>It is used to form negative statements and interrogative sentences. For example,
<|det|>list [98, 113, 625, 162]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 113, 451, 136]<|/det|>• He doesn't agree with me.
<|det|>text [98, 137, 625, 162]<|/det|>• Did you manage to finish the assignment?
<|det|>text [46, 172, 905, 222]<|/det|>In imperative sentences, it is used to make the statement more forceful. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 223, 290, 247]<|/det|>- Do be quiet.
<|det|>title [47, 269, 291, 292]<|/det|>Modal Auxiliaries
<|det|>title [47, 302, 246, 325]<|/det|>Can and Could
<|det|>list [47, 335, 714, 407]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [47, 335, 714, 361]<|/det|>◆ Can usually suggests potential or ability. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 361, 515, 384]<|/det|>• Ram can take you to the airport.
<|det|>text [98, 385, 340, 407]<|/det|>- Can you lift this?
<|det|>text [46, 420, 708, 445]<|/det|>◆ Can is also used to indicate permission. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 445, 477, 468]<|/det|>• You can join from tomorrow.
<|det|>text [46, 480, 691, 505]<|/det|>◆ Could is used to ask a polite question. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 505, 541, 528]<|/det|>• Could you help me solve the sum?
<|det|>text [46, 538, 903, 563]<|/det|>◆ Could is used as the past tense of can in indirect speech. For example,
<|det|>list [98, 564, 514, 612]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 564, 450, 588]<|/det|>• Toto said, 'I can help you.'
<|det|>text [98, 588, 514, 611]<|/det|>• Toto said that he could help me.
<|det|>text [46, 623, 750, 648]<|/det|>◆ Could is used to indicate ability in the past. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 648, 576, 671]<|/det|>• He could sing well once upon a time.
<|det|>title [46, 686, 248, 712]<|/det|>May and Might
<|det|>list [46, 716, 840, 884]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [46, 716, 838, 742]<|/det|>◆ May is usually used to request permission formally. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 742, 401, 766]<|/det|>• May I use your eraser?
<|det|>text [46, 777, 654, 802]<|/det|>◆ May is also used to express a wish. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 802, 530, 827]<|/det|>• May you always enjoy good luck!
<|det|>text [46, 836, 645, 861]<|/det|>◆ May is used to indicate possibility. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 861, 570, 885]<|/det|>• There may be some rain this evening.
<|det|>page_number [838, 970, 868, 987]<|/det|>81
<PAGE><|det|>text [120, 28, 880, 79]<|/det|>◆ May suggests a weak possibility and might suggests a very weak possibility in the future. For example,
<|det|>text [164, 79, 802, 100]<|/det|>• The match schedule may be announced next Monday.
<|det|>text [165, 102, 639, 125]<|/det|>• We might return sooner than expected.
<|det|>text [165, 126, 509, 148]<|/det|>• She might arrive tomorrow.
<|det|>title [120, 168, 482, 194]<|/det|>Shall, Should, Will and Would
<|det|>text [120, 198, 898, 247]<|/det|>- shall is usually used in the first person and will in the second and third person. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 248, 532, 271]<|/det|>• I shall be pleased to visit you.
<|det|>text [165, 272, 467, 295]<|/det|>• You will agree with me.
<|det|>text [120, 305, 945, 354]<|/det|>- shall is used in the second and third person to express a command, a promise or even a threat. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 355, 619, 377]<|/det|>• He shall not work here again. (threat)
<|det|>text [165, 378, 662, 401]<|/det|>• You shall certainly be rewarded. (promise)
<|det|>text [120, 410, 951, 459]<|/det|>- shall is used in interrogative sentences with the first person to indicate an offer or a suggestion. For example,
<|det|>text [166, 459, 562, 482]<|/det|>• Shall I carry these books for you?
<|det|>text [121, 492, 801, 516]<|/det|>Will is used to express a decision or a choice. For example,
<|det|>text [166, 516, 501, 539]<|/det|>• I will do the cooking today.
<|det|>text [121, 548, 643, 572]<|/det|>• Will is used to indicate a habit. For example,
<|det|>text [166, 572, 537, 595]<|/det|>• He will go swimming as usual.
<|det|>text [121, 605, 834, 628]<|/det|>- Will is used in interrogative sentences to suggest an invitation.
<|det|>text [165, 630, 306, 652]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [166, 652, 569, 675]<|/det|>• Will you go on a holiday with us?
<|det|>text [121, 684, 942, 731]<|/det|>- In clauses starting with if, should is used to express a supposition that may not be true. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 731, 680, 752]<|/det|>• If it should rain, the match will be called off.
<|det|>title [120, 773, 322, 798]<|/det|>Must and Ought
<|det|>text [121, 803, 724, 825]<|/det|>- Must expresses necessity or obligation. For example,
<|det|>text [166, 826, 474, 845]<|/det|>• We must remember this.
<|det|>text [121, 859, 714, 881]<|/det|>- Must can express fixed determination. For example,
<|det|>text [165, 882, 690, 904]<|/det|>- I must have an opportunity to do what I wish.
<|det|>page_number [148, 930, 179, 948]<|/det|>82
<PAGE><|det|>text [57, 46, 761, 69]<|/det|>Must and ought also express certainty or strong possibility.
<|det|>text [108, 71, 258, 93]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [110, 93, 647, 138]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 93, 647, 115]<|/det|>• He must have forgotten about the invitation.
<|det|>text [111, 116, 468, 138]<|/det|>• We ought to strike this deal.
<|det|>text [60, 148, 520, 194]<|/det|>◆ Ought expresses moral obligation or desirability. For example,
<|det|>list [111, 195, 397, 240]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 195, 397, 218]<|/det|>• We ought to try hard.
<|det|>title [64, 237, 135, 259]<|/det|>Used
<|det|>list [65, 264, 575, 337]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [65, 264, 575, 313]<|/det|>It is used to indicate a discontinued habit. For example,
<|det|>text [116, 314, 424, 335]<|/det|>• She used to learn ballet.
<|det|>title [70, 355, 141, 377]<|/det|>Need
<|det|>list [74, 381, 856, 528]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [74, 381, 854, 429]<|/det|>It denotes necessity or obligation. It is commonly used in negatives and interrogatives. For example,
<|det|>text [118, 430, 463, 452]<|/det|>• He need not give up tennis.
<|det|>text [119, 452, 351, 474]<|/det|>- Need I help him?
<|det|>text [72, 483, 868, 531]<|/det|>- Need can also be used to denote requirement or desire. For example,
- I do not need your help.
<|det|>title [74, 550, 141, 570]<|/det|>Dare
<|det|>text [74, 575, 641, 599]<|/det|>Dare as modal auxiliary means 'be bold enough to'.
<|det|>text [74, 599, 774, 621]<|/det|>It is commonly used in interrogative and negative sentences.
<|det|>text [118, 622, 411, 643]<|/det|>• How dare she say this?
<|det|>text [74, 654, 771, 678]<|/det|>◆ Dare can also be used to indicate a challenge. For example,
<|det|>text [118, 678, 611, 699]<|/det|>• I dare you to eat all the food on the table.
<|det|>title [65, 736, 500, 758]<|/det|>A. Choose the correct alternatives.
<|det|>list [151, 768, 888, 891]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [151, 768, 674, 790]<|/det|>1. You ____ not lie. (should/can)
<|det|>text [150, 792, 868, 815]<|/det|>2. She ____ be ashamed of herself. (could/should)
<|det|>text [150, 817, 865, 840]<|/det|>3. We ____ to help those in distress. (need/ought)
<|det|>text [150, 842, 863, 864]<|/det|>4. ____ you please lend me this book? (will/shall)
<|det|>text [150, 866, 888, 890]<|/det|>5. I am glad she ____ come yesterday. (would/could)
<|det|>page_number [828, 930, 860, 948]<|/det|>83
<PAGE><|det|>text [208, 38, 920, 87]<|/det|>6. I come to meet you tomorrow, ma'am?
(May/can)
<|det|>text [209, 87, 938, 131]<|/det|>7. You ____ plan your future in advance. (shall/should)
<|det|>text [209, 133, 960, 155]<|/det|>8. I am late for office. I ____ take a cab. (may/must)
<|det|>text [209, 157, 935, 202]<|/det|>9. You could wait a while. You ____ not go to the doctor immediately. (need/ought)
<|det|>text [202, 203, 953, 251]<|/det|>10. We ____ buy this music compact disc. We have a copy at home. (might not/must not)
<|det|>text [202, 252, 945, 297]<|/det|>11. One ____ eat dinner too late. (should not/need not)
<|det|>text [202, 298, 912, 344]<|/det|>12. I don't think I ____ be able to reach on time. (should/ shall)
<|det|>text [202, 346, 832, 393]<|/det|>13. She was afraid that if she requested him again, he ____ refuse. (can/might)
<|det|>text [202, 394, 918, 438]<|/det|>14. If you are unruly, you ____ be kept back after school. (can/shall)
<|det|>text [128, 464, 685, 489]<|/det|>B. Fill in the blanks using appropriate modals.
<|det|>text [214, 497, 957, 565]<|/det|>1. ____ you please tell me the direction to the hotel?
2. You ____ pay in cash. They do not accept credit cards.
<|det|>text [214, 569, 860, 616]<|/det|>3. I ____ come home from the office today.
3. ____ I help you?
<|det|>text [214, 618, 705, 685]<|/det|>5. You ____ clean your room.
4. We ____ keep the lights and fans switched off when they are not in use.
<|det|>text [214, 688, 948, 737]<|/det|>7. We ____ improve the existing education system.
5. ____ you have a wonderful day!
<|det|>text [214, 738, 952, 784]<|/det|>9. I ____ go to see the movie. The reviews are good.
6. I ____ definitely be at the airport to receive you.
<|det|>text [206, 786, 931, 831]<|/det|>11. She ____ take her meals regularly.
7. 'You ____ reach home before eleven,' she said.
<|det|>text [205, 833, 662, 877]<|/det|>13. ____ we go for a walk?
8. The soldiers in the army ____ wear a uniform.
<|det|>page_number [152, 929, 182, 946]<|/det|>84
<PAGE><|det|>text [148, 39, 777, 63]<|/det|>15. There is one more point I ____ refer to.
<|det|>text [149, 64, 770, 89]<|/det|>16. You have worked hard. You ____ pass.
<|det|>text [150, 89, 664, 113]<|/det|>17. It is very cloudy. It ____ rain.
<|det|>text [150, 113, 890, 158]<|/det|>18. You ____ be properly dressed when you come to office.
<|det|>text [149, 160, 853, 186]<|/det|>19. ____ you like to come to the party tomorrow?
<|det|>text [148, 187, 620, 211]<|/det|>20. People ____ not tell lies.
<|det|>list [148, 39, 890, 211]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [146, 246, 876, 294]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the appropriate modals in accordance with the instructions given in the brackets.
<|det|>text [160, 304, 830, 328]<|/det|>1. I ____ attend the concert. (weak possibility)
<|det|>text [161, 329, 837, 375]<|/det|>2. ____ you pass on this message to my family? (polite request)
<|det|>text [161, 375, 846, 421]<|/det|>3. Do not worry. You ____ receive a scholarship.
(promise)
<|det|>text [161, 421, 856, 445]<|/det|>4. ____ I take your leave? (asking for permission)
<|det|>text [162, 445, 797, 468]<|/det|>5. You ____ apologise immediately. (threat)
<|det|>text [161, 468, 882, 490]<|/det|>6. The child ____ play the piano. (expressing ability)
<|det|>text [161, 490, 821, 535]<|/det|>7. The school buses ____ not be driven rashly.
(prohibition)
<|det|>text [161, 535, 828, 558]<|/det|>8. ____ you like to have a cup of tea? (request)
<|det|>text [161, 558, 830, 604]<|/det|>9. We ____ obey the laws of the land. (duty or obligation)
<|det|>text [148, 605, 672, 627]<|/det|>10. ____ this be true? (possibility)
<|det|>text [148, 627, 774, 673]<|/det|>11. He ____ to enjoy watching Hindi films.
(indicate past habit)
<|det|>text [148, 673, 770, 717]<|/det|>12. I ____ finish the work before he comes.
(determination)
<|det|>text [148, 718, 846, 764]<|/det|>13. The highway ____ be blocked due to a recent landslide. (possibility)
<|det|>text [148, 764, 850, 809]<|/det|>14. ____ you earn fame and fortune! (to express a wish)
<|det|>text [148, 810, 790, 833]<|/det|>15. She ____ speak eight languages. (ability)
<|det|>text [148, 833, 880, 881]<|/det|>16. You ____ complete your homework before going to play. (necessity)
<|det|>page_number [822, 930, 856, 948]<|/det|>85
<PAGE><|det|>text [195, 39, 966, 110]<|/det|>The following passage has not been edited. There are errors in the usage of modals. Write the incorrect word and the correct word in the space provided. Put x if there are no errors in a line.
<|det|>title [732, 123, 960, 144]<|/det|>Incorrect Correct
<|det|>text [194, 150, 670, 177]<|/det|>The label can be removed before cooking.
<|det|>text [195, 180, 633, 203]<|/det|>Then, the cookware could be rinsed in
<|det|>text [195, 206, 656, 230]<|/det|>warm soapy water. It is advisable to keep
<|det|>text [195, 234, 643, 258]<|/det|>to low heat. The cookware would never
<|det|>text [195, 261, 558, 286]<|/det|>be used on high heat. Very high
<|det|>text [195, 289, 591, 314]<|/det|>temperatures would cause sticking.
<|det|>text [195, 318, 563, 341]<|/det|>The handles used to also loosen.
<|det|>text [195, 346, 664, 371]<|/det|>However, one should not worry about the
<|det|>text [195, 374, 546, 400]<|/det|>maintenance as it is quite easy.
<|det|>text [195, 403, 667, 428]<|/det|>The vessel might be cleaned with a liquid
<|det|>text [195, 431, 677, 456]<|/det|>detergent. One cannot use sharp detergent.
<|det|>text [731, 123, 960, 144]<|/det|>Incorrect Correct
<|det|>page_number [142, 929, 177, 948]<|/det|>86
<PAGE><|det|>title [70, 37, 442, 73]<|/det|>Non-finite Verbs
<|det|>header [808, 46, 890, 83]<|/det|>11
<|det|>image [71, 149, 130, 196]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [136, 145, 798, 193]<|/det|>Read these sets of sentences. What do you notice about the highlighted words and the underlined words?
<|det|>list [73, 200, 410, 343]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [73, 200, 331, 223]<|/det|>• I want to go home.
<|det|>text [118, 224, 408, 247]<|/det|>Lathika wants to go home.
<|det|>text [118, 248, 373, 271]<|/det|>They want to go home.
<|det|>text [74, 273, 294, 295]<|/det|>• I am swimming.
<|det|>text [118, 296, 336, 319]<|/det|>Mansi is swimming.
<|det|>text [118, 320, 340, 343]<|/det|>They are swimming.
<|det|>image [77, 360, 135, 405]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [139, 358, 851, 450]<|/det|>In a sentence, the verb usually changes its form according to the person and number of the subject. However, in the above sentences, the underlined words remain the same even when the subject changes.
<|det|>text [75, 468, 856, 515]<|/det|>When the verb in the sentence indicates the tense and changes according to the subject, we call it the finite verb. A finite verb has a subject.
<|det|>text [75, 542, 825, 586]<|/det|>The verb in a sentence that has no subject, person or number is called a non-finite verb.
<|det|>text [75, 607, 865, 652]<|/det|>In these sentences, the highlighted verbs are finite verbs that change with the subject, while the underlined verbs are non-finite verbs.
<|det|>list [78, 654, 731, 722]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [78, 654, 570, 675]<|/det|>• I go to the market every day to fetch milk.
<|det|>text [78, 676, 615, 698]<|/det|>• He goes to the market every day to fetch milk.
<|det|>text [75, 698, 811, 743]<|/det|>There are three kinds of non-finite verbs—infinitives, participles and gerunds. Let us learn about them in detail.
<|det|>title [76, 760, 212, 780]<|/det|>Infinitives
<|det|>text [75, 790, 316, 810]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [75, 813, 485, 856]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [76, 813, 433, 835]<|/det|>• I want to buy some perfume.
<|det|>text [76, 836, 484, 857]<|/det|>- Indira wants to buy some clothes.
<|det|>text [72, 858, 741, 902]<|/det|>In these sentences, the verb want is the finite verb and to buy is the infinitive.
<|det|>page_number [818, 927, 851, 944]<|/det|>87
<PAGE><|det|>text [112, 28, 920, 74]<|/det|>Intuitives are also called the to-verb because to is usually a part of the infinitive form. Let us learn about some of the functions of infinitives.
<|det|>text [158, 76, 885, 98]<|/det|>Infinitives can function as the subject or the object of a sentence.
<|det|>list [160, 99, 526, 165]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 99, 482, 119]<|/det|>I want to bathe. (object)
<|det|>text [160, 121, 487, 144]<|/det|>• To delay is bad. (subject)
<|det|>text [115, 155, 525, 221]<|/det|>The infinitive can be the object of the verb and also modified by an adverb.
<|det|>list [160, 224, 490, 270]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 224, 490, 270]<|/det|>- Many men prefer to work slowly.
<|det|>text [115, 281, 520, 327]<|/det|>An infinitive can also qualify an adjective or a noun.
<|det|>list [160, 329, 480, 374]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 329, 480, 350]<|/det|>- Apples are sweet to eat.
<|det|>text [160, 352, 519, 374]<|/det|>• This is not the time to sleep.
<|det|>title [590, 125, 650, 150]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [735, 120, 784, 155]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [583, 162, 818, 183]<|/det|>Infinitives are useful when—
<|det|>list [583, 185, 934, 268]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [583, 185, 934, 206]<|/det|>• we wish to state an aim or purpose.
<|det|>text [618, 207, 852, 225]<|/det|>• He called to apologise,
<|det|>text [618, 227, 847, 246]<|/det|>• He wanted to buy milk.
<|det|>text [618, 248, 857, 268]<|/det|>• They agreed to help us.
<|det|>text [583, 281, 890, 300]<|/det|>- when we wish to indicate result.
<|det|>text [618, 302, 911, 322]<|/det|>• She lived to see the outcome.
<|det|>text [618, 324, 895, 344]<|/det|>• The news proved to be true.
<|det|>text [115, 385, 706, 409]<|/det|>- Infinitives can also be the object of a preposition.
<|det|>text [160, 410, 528, 430]<|/det|>• The teacher is about to start.
<|det|>list [115, 385, 528, 430]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [115, 443, 460, 464]<|/det|>Take a look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [117, 467, 480, 537]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 467, 341, 486]<|/det|>• To steal is a sin.
<|det|>text [117, 490, 468, 511]<|/det|>• To eat too much is harmful.
<|det|>text [114, 513, 942, 556]<|/det|>These sentences are grammatically correct, but sound strange. Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [117, 559, 486, 601]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 559, 358, 579]<|/det|>- It is a sin to steal.
<|det|>text [117, 582, 486, 602]<|/det|>• It is harmful to eat too much.
<|det|>text [115, 604, 922, 649]<|/det|>It is better to use it as the subject and the infinitive as the object in such sentences.
<|det|>text [115, 677, 905, 723]<|/det|>When the to+verb form is used in a sentence and functions as a noun, it is called the infinitive.
<|det|>text [114, 754, 951, 799]<|/det|>We usually use to with the infinitive. However it is not essential. When an infinitive is used without to it is called a bare infinitive.
<|det|>list [114, 801, 905, 897]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [114, 801, 903, 848]<|/det|>We do not use to when we use infinitives with auxiliary verbs like must, can, should, shall, will, may, might and do. For example,
<|det|>list [160, 850, 672, 896]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 850, 436, 871]<|/det|>- I must go home now.
<|det|>text [160, 872, 672, 896]<|/det|>• They might leave for Dehradun next week.
<|det|>page_number [142, 925, 172, 944]<|/det|>88
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 26, 876, 74]<|/det|>- We do not use to with the infinitive after verbs of perception like see, hear, watch, know and feel. For example,
<|det|>text [113, 76, 470, 98]<|/det|>• I saw Leena leave the house.
<|det|>text [113, 100, 472, 124]<|/det|>• I felt an ant crawl up my leg.
<|det|>list [113, 76, 472, 124]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [62, 135, 854, 182]<|/det|>We do not use to when we use infinitives after verbs like make, let, dare or need. For example,
<|det|>text [113, 183, 361, 205]<|/det|>• I made him speak.
<|det|>text [113, 207, 323, 229]<|/det|>- Let me try this.
<|det|>text [113, 230, 512, 253]<|/det|>• How dare you touch my books?
<|det|>text [113, 254, 547, 277]<|/det|>• You need not solve these problems.
<|det|>list [113, 183, 547, 277]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [94, 304, 154, 330]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [461, 293, 501, 330]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [90, 341, 851, 363]<|/det|>With verbs like need and dare, note that we use the infinitive with to in affirmative sentences.
<|det|>text [93, 366, 300, 383]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>text [93, 385, 517, 405]<|/det|>• He dared to dream, and achieved his ambition.
<|det|>text [93, 407, 383, 428]<|/det|>I need to visit the doctor soon.
<|det|>list [93, 385, 517, 428]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [65, 451, 102, 481]<|/det|>A.
<|det|>text [147, 464, 810, 512]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the infinitive forms of the verbs in the brackets, with or without to as the case may be.
<|det|>text [164, 520, 617, 542]<|/det|>1. Let us ____. (eat)
<|det|>text [164, 545, 727, 568]<|/det|>2. I am sorry ____ this. (learn)
<|det|>text [165, 571, 731, 594]<|/det|>3. I hope ____ the order. (win)
<|det|>text [165, 597, 753, 620]<|/det|>4. ____ was impossible. (return)
<|det|>text [165, 624, 753, 646]<|/det|>5. I will not let you ____. (leave)
<|det|>text [165, 650, 709, 672]<|/det|>6. Bid him ____ there. (stay)
<|det|>text [165, 676, 879, 698]<|/det|>7. He was quick ____ the matter. (understand)
<|det|>text [166, 702, 740, 724]<|/det|>8. You need not ____ now. (go)
<|det|>text [166, 727, 785, 749]<|/det|>9. I saw him ____ the phone. (take)
<|det|>text [154, 752, 885, 775]<|/det|>10. I felt something cold ____ my hand. (touch)
<|det|>text [154, 778, 844, 821]<|/det|>11. The police wanted ____ the premises.
(search)
<|det|>text [154, 827, 880, 850]<|/det|>12. ____ is a crime against humanity. (waste)
<|det|>text [155, 853, 765, 875]<|/det|>13. It was wrong ____ him. (scold)
<|det|>text [155, 878, 701, 901]<|/det|>14. I failed ____ you. (reach)
<|det|>text [155, 904, 600, 925]<|/det|>15. It is too late ____ now. (start)
<|det|>page_number [828, 928, 856, 944]<|/det|>89
<PAGE><|det|>text [127, 42, 472, 65]<|/det|>I look at these sentences.
<|det|>text [168, 77, 567, 100]<|/det|>It is very cold. I cannot go out now.
<|det|>text [168, 102, 560, 125]<|/det|>It is too cold for me to go out now.
<|det|>text [129, 126, 847, 148]<|/det|>This is a very small room. The exhibition cannot be held here.
<|det|>text [169, 150, 721, 171]<|/det|>This room is too small to hold the exhibition here.
<|det|>text [128, 174, 953, 245]<|/det|>Did you notice that in joining these sentences, one of the finite verbs was replaced by its infinitive form? The adverb too is often used along with the infinitive while joining sentences.
<|det|>text [130, 262, 535, 285]<|/det|>Now, look at these sets of sentences.
<|det|>text [133, 287, 607, 308]<|/det|>• I hope that I shall do well in the exams.
<|det|>text [174, 311, 519, 333]<|/det|>I hope to do well in the exams.
<|det|>text [133, 335, 664, 357]<|/det|>• We were surprised when we heard the news.
<|det|>text [174, 359, 577, 381]<|/det|>We were surprised to hear the news.
<|det|>text [135, 382, 856, 476]<|/det|>In the above pairs of sentences, we have dropped the underlined conjunction in the first sentence of each pair and replaced one of the finite verbs with the infinitive. This is sometimes done to make sentences compact.
<|det|>text [139, 493, 945, 542]<|/det|>B. Rewrite each of these sentences by replacing the highlighted verb with an infinitive. Make necessary changes.
<|det|>text [227, 551, 646, 573]<|/det|>1. I agreed that I would do the work.
<|det|>text [226, 574, 868, 596]<|/det|>2. Our country expects that its citizens will do their duty.
<|det|>text [226, 598, 788, 619]<|/det|>3. He has many sisters whom he must provide for.
<|det|>text [226, 620, 817, 642]<|/det|>4. As he had no money he could not buy vegetables.
<|det|>text [226, 643, 825, 665]<|/det|>5. We were delighted when we heard the good news.
<|det|>text [226, 666, 868, 710]<|/det|>6. The mayor was elated when he learnt that his city was declared the cleanest city.
<|det|>text [226, 711, 870, 733]<|/det|>7. Our landlord was the last man who left the apartment.
<|det|>text [229, 734, 708, 755]<|/det|>8. I was the last person who saw him here.
<|det|>text [229, 757, 735, 778]<|/det|>9. Mr Sen hopes that he will get the contract.
<|det|>text [218, 779, 951, 802]<|/det|>10. We were delighted when we reached Shimla by the toy train.
<|det|>text [149, 816, 688, 839]<|/det|>C. Join these sets of sentences using infinitives.
<|det|>text [217, 848, 672, 871]<|/det|>1. I heard of your new job. I was happy.
<|det|>text [217, 872, 635, 892]<|/det|>2. He has a plan. He will execute it.
<|det|>text [217, 894, 949, 916]<|/det|>3. Every company has a manager. He takes care of the business.
<|det|>page_number [182, 930, 212, 948]<|/det|>90
<PAGE><|det|>text [135, 32, 670, 55]<|/det|>4. He speaks the truth. He is not afraid of it.
<|det|>text [136, 56, 680, 80]<|/det|>5. All teams have coaches. They guide them.
<|det|>text [137, 81, 558, 102]<|/det|>6. I stayed at home. I wanted rest.
<|det|>text [137, 104, 803, 128]<|/det|>7. He went to Mumbai. His objective was getting a job.
<|det|>text [137, 129, 817, 152]<|/det|>8. He must study. It is the only way of achieving success.
<|det|>text [137, 153, 694, 175]<|/det|>9. He collects precious stones. It is his hobby.
<|det|>text [130, 176, 783, 199]<|/det|>10. Our teacher is very kind. She cannot scold anyone.
<|det|>list [130, 32, 817, 199]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [125, 219, 848, 266]<|/det|>Complete the following sentences with a ‘to-infinitive’ and any other necessary words.
<|det|>list [139, 274, 898, 510]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [141, 277, 895, 299]<|/det|>1. I think you will be surprised ____.
<|det|>text [141, 301, 895, 323]<|/det|>2. Miss Shyama Chona is certainly qualified ____.
<|det|>text [142, 325, 895, 347]<|/det|>3. Actually, I am not fully prepared ____.
<|det|>text [143, 349, 895, 371]<|/det|>4. We will be ready ____.
<|det|>text [143, 372, 895, 394]<|/det|>5. My friend is inclined ____.
<|det|>text [143, 395, 895, 418]<|/det|>6. Everyone will be disappointed ____.
<|det|>text [143, 419, 895, 441]<|/det|>7. He bought some flowers ____.
<|det|>text [143, 442, 895, 464]<|/det|>8. Remind me ____.
<|det|>text [143, 465, 895, 487]<|/det|>9. Leave early ____.
<|det|>text [137, 489, 895, 511]<|/det|>10. I would like you ____.
<|det|>title [64, 533, 206, 556]<|/det|>Participles
<|det|>text [64, 561, 879, 606]<|/det|>You have learnt about present participles (verb+-ing) and past participles 'verb+-ed/-d/-t/-en/-n).
<|det|>text [70, 623, 368, 643]<|/det|>vow, read these sentences.
<|det|>text [102, 645, 467, 666]<|/det|>The creaking noise disturbed us.
<|det|>text [64, 667, 497, 689]<|/det|>• The leaking pipe must be replaced.
<|det|>text [64, 690, 787, 711]<|/det|>- The quivering leaves of the willow trees rustled in the breeze.
<|det|>list [64, 667, 787, 711]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [62, 712, 790, 757]<|/det|>The highlighted words are present participles that have been used as adjectives.
<|det|>text [63, 773, 367, 794]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>text [63, 796, 571, 818]<|/det|>- The injured boy was taken to the hospital.
<|det|>text [63, 818, 492, 841]<|/det|>• The frightened girl shouted loudly.
<|det|>text [63, 842, 666, 864]<|/det|>• They rescued the passengers from the wrecked car.
<|det|>text [63, 865, 415, 886]<|/det|>- Nobody ate the burnt toast.
<|det|>list [63, 796, 666, 886]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [62, 887, 695, 932]<|/det|>The highlighted words are past participles that have been used as adjectives.
<|det|>image [699, 752, 748, 781]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [775, 750, 824, 781]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [728, 792, 856, 874]<|/det|>We may say that a participle is a verbal adjective.
<|det|>text [720, 884, 840, 884]<|/det|>\( \therefore m = \frac{3}{11} \)
<|det|>page_number [828, 924, 856, 941]<|/det|>91
<PAGE><|det|>text [137, 39, 845, 87]<|/det|>A participle is that form of the verb that can act as an adjective in a sentence.
<|det|>text [140, 112, 425, 135]<|/det|>Participles may be used—
<|det|>list [143, 137, 540, 209]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [143, 137, 470, 160]<|/det|>◆ as attributes. For example,
<|det|>text [185, 161, 506, 184]<|/det|>• The torn sheet flew away.
<|det|>text [185, 185, 538, 206]<|/det|>• A burnt child dreads the fire.
<|det|>text [142, 219, 625, 266]<|/det|>◆ in the predicate, modifying the subject or the object. For example,
<|det|>text [185, 268, 497, 290]<|/det|>• The boy looked terrified.
<|det|>text [185, 292, 458, 316]<|/det|>• He kept me standing.
<|det|>image [664, 121, 926, 328]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [143, 326, 643, 350]<|/det|>◆ to talk about an earlier action. For example,
<|det|>list [185, 351, 653, 397]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [185, 351, 543, 373]<|/det|>• Seeing the tiger, the man fled.
<|det|>text [185, 373, 653, 397]<|/det|>- Dejected by his failure, he left his home.
<|det|>text [143, 432, 920, 504]<|/det|>E. Complete these sentences by inserting the participles given in the brackets in the appropriate place. Rewrite making other necessary changes. Follow the example.
<|det|>list [231, 512, 885, 922]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [231, 512, 725, 560]<|/det|>1. Over the wall, the thief escaped. (jumping) lumping over the wall, the thief escaped.
<|det|>text [231, 561, 661, 584]<|/det|>2. The noise, the thieves fled. (hearing)
<|det|>text [231, 585, 671, 608]<|/det|>3. A baby is difficult to manage. (crying)
<|det|>text [231, 609, 778, 632]<|/det|>4. The men ran into the factory loudly. (screaming)
<|det|>text [231, 633, 885, 656]<|/det|>5. By the enemy, the soldier put up a brave front. (captured)
<|det|>text [231, 658, 571, 681]<|/det|>6. Slept, we felt fresh. (having)
<|det|>text [231, 682, 787, 705]<|/det|>7. By the door, the child waved goodbye. (standing)
<|det|>text [231, 707, 714, 730]<|/det|>8. The night cold, we stayed indoors. (being)
<|det|>text [231, 730, 834, 753]<|/det|>9. The poor children slept by the cinders. (smouldering)
<|det|>text [218, 755, 848, 778]<|/det|>10. By endless suffering, the man grew hopeless. (broken)
<|det|>text [218, 779, 907, 803]<|/det|>11. The waters of the stream sparkled in the sunlight. (gurgling)
<|det|>text [218, 804, 834, 827]<|/det|>12. Fed her little daughter, Rosy sat down to eat. (having)
<|det|>text [218, 828, 600, 851]<|/det|>13. The lodge has water. (running)
<|det|>text [218, 852, 714, 875]<|/det|>14. Goa boasts of a tourist business. (growing)
<|det|>text [218, 876, 841, 923]<|/det|>15. Bright and cheerful, the clown smiled at the audience. (appearing)
<|det|>page_number [178, 933, 207, 951]<|/det|>92
<PAGE><|det|>image [65, 45, 118, 90]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [144, 41, 867, 88]<|/det|>We can join pairs of sentences by using participles. Look at these pairs of sentences.
<|det|>text [67, 96, 722, 144]<|/det|>The girl noticed a car. The car was coming towards her. The girl noticed a car coming towards her.
<|det|>text [67, 144, 697, 192]<|/det|>- Roma opened the cupboard. Roma took out a shawl. Opening the cupboard, Roma took out a shawl.
<|det|>text [68, 192, 503, 215]<|/det|>• I saw an elephant. I ran for my life.
<|det|>text [116, 215, 517, 239]<|/det|>Seeing an elephant, I ran for my life.
<|det|>text [65, 247, 856, 320]<|/det|>In the above examples, both actions take place at the same time. When we join the sentences, one finite verb is removed and we use its present participle form instead.
<|det|>text [68, 333, 895, 405]<|/det|>But sometimes, the second action begins only after the first has been completed. In that case, we convert the finite verb denoting the first action into the participle.
<|det|>text [73, 411, 732, 435]<|/det|>• He had lost all his books. He went to his friend for help.
<|det|>text [121, 435, 725, 460]<|/det|>Having lost all his books, he went to his friend for help.
<|det|>title [152, 485, 651, 510]<|/det|>Join these pairs of sentences using participles.
<|det|>list [174, 517, 895, 758]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 517, 686, 541]<|/det|>1. He picked up his stick. He ran to the door.
<|det|>text [174, 542, 785, 565]<|/det|>2. He walked into an empty room. He looked around.
<|det|>text [175, 567, 727, 589]<|/det|>3. I walked into the library. I went to the counter.
<|det|>text [174, 591, 856, 614]<|/det|>4. I was mesmerised by the scene. I stood rooted to the spot.
<|det|>text [175, 616, 750, 638]<|/det|>5. I alighted from the train. I looked for my mother.
<|det|>text [174, 640, 894, 663]<|/det|>6. We saw some boys. They were quarrelling among themselves.
<|det|>text [174, 664, 783, 687]<|/det|>7. He was deceived by his employer. He lost all hope.
<|det|>text [174, 688, 690, 711]<|/det|>8. I heard a loud noise. I got up from my bed.
<|det|>text [174, 712, 690, 735]<|/det|>9. The man was injured. He was hospitalised.
<|det|>text [157, 736, 830, 760]<|/det|>10. A man was walking down the lane. He spotted a kitten.
<|det|>image [399, 770, 548, 932]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [834, 930, 865, 948]<|/det|>93

<PAGE><|det|>title [160, 32, 222, 60]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [159, 75, 550, 96]<|/det|>Look at these sentences. Do they seem correct?
<|det|>list [160, 97, 625, 160]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 97, 625, 116]<|/det|>• Walking through the forest, the surrounding was cold.
<|det|>text [160, 118, 580, 137]<|/det|>• Going down the road, horrifying sight was seen.
<|det|>text [160, 139, 795, 181]<|/det|>In each of these sentences, there is no noun or pronoun to which the highlighted particle refers.
<|det|>text [160, 183, 483, 200]<|/det|>These sentences should be written as—
<|det|>list [160, 203, 645, 247]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 203, 645, 223]<|/det|>• Walking through the forest, I found the surrounding cold.
<|det|>text [160, 225, 560, 247]<|/det|>• Going down the road, I saw a horrifying sight.
<|det|>title [146, 307, 251, 329]<|/det|>Gerunds
<|det|>text [145, 336, 900, 381]<|/det|>Look at these sentences. Notice how the -ing words have been used in these sentences.
<|det|>list [148, 389, 842, 485]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [148, 389, 841, 413]<|/det|>- Swimming is a good way to exercise. (subject of the sentence)
<|det|>text [149, 414, 604, 436]<|/det|>• I enjoy reading. (object of the sentence)
<|det|>text [149, 437, 788, 460]<|/det|>• He is fond of eating ice-cream. (object of the preposition)
<|det|>text [149, 460, 740, 484]<|/det|>• What I quite abhor is lying. (complement of the verb)
<|det|>text [145, 492, 935, 538]<|/det|>In these sentences, the -ing words function like nouns. However, all these words are derived from verbs.
<|det|>text [146, 566, 593, 614]<|/det|>When the -ing form of the verb is used as a noun in a sentence, we call it the gerund.
<|det|>image [516, 668, 560, 704]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [667, 564, 726, 588]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [664, 602, 892, 623]<|/det|>A gerund is a verbal noun.
<|det|>title [170, 677, 228, 703]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [166, 715, 614, 736]<|/det|>The -ing word can be used in different ways. For example,
<|det|>list [166, 737, 670, 800]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [166, 737, 455, 757]<|/det|>• Dancing is her hobby. (subject)
<|det|>text [166, 758, 487, 777]<|/det|>• The girl is dancing gracefully. (verb)
<|det|>text [166, 778, 670, 799]<|/det|>• Everybody gathered to see the dancing peacock. (adjective)
<|det|>page_number [180, 930, 212, 947]<|/det|>94
<PAGE><|det|>text [82, 45, 842, 94]<|/det|>5. Identify the highlighted words as participles or gerunds in these sentences.
<|det|>list [160, 98, 800, 702]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [167, 102, 625, 125]<|/det|>1. Loitering in the corridor is forbidden.
<|det|>text [166, 126, 750, 148]<|/det|>2. The thundering clouds soon came down as rains.
<|det|>text [166, 149, 797, 193]<|/det|>3. Rowing down the stream, we came across a beautiful waterfall.
<|det|>text [166, 195, 545, 243]<|/det|>4. Killing wild animals is a cruel sport.
<|det|>text [167, 244, 534, 289]<|/det|>5. We were tired of listening to his complaints.
<|det|>text [167, 290, 491, 336]<|/det|>6. Driving down the lane, I found a puppy.
<|det|>text [168, 337, 502, 359]<|/det|>7. My sister dislikes waiting.
<|det|>text [169, 359, 636, 383]<|/det|>8. Being frightened, she hid in the closet.
<|det|>text [169, 384, 550, 406]<|/det|>9. His behaviour was frustrating.
<|det|>text [160, 406, 578, 428]<|/det|>10. I love watching the stars at night.
<|det|>text [160, 428, 611, 449]<|/det|>11. The children slid on the frozen lake.
<|det|>text [160, 449, 776, 473]<|/det|>12. The boys, swimming in the pond, had a merry time.
<|det|>text [160, 473, 706, 496]<|/det|>13. Duped by a local thug, he lost all his savings.
<|det|>text [160, 496, 841, 541]<|/det|>14. Excusing herself from the conference, she went to answer the phone.
<|det|>text [160, 541, 610, 564]<|/det|>15. Sleeping is my cat's favourite hobby.
<|det|>text [160, 564, 668, 587]<|/det|>16. The speeding car crashed against the tree.
<|det|>text [160, 587, 475, 609]<|/det|>17. James loves swimming.
<|det|>text [160, 609, 806, 652]<|/det|>18. Camping along the banks of the Ganga is my idea of a vacation.
<|det|>text [160, 654, 498, 676]<|/det|>19. I love cooking for friends.
<|det|>text [159, 677, 751, 701]<|/det|>20. The gurgling stream provided a picturesque view.
<|det|>title [94, 726, 373, 748]<|/det|>:erunds or Infinitives?
<|det|>text [93, 754, 850, 825]<|/det|>, s both gerrunds and infinitives can act as nouns and verbs, they have the same uses. Thus, in many sentences either of them may be used. For example,
<|det|>text [126, 825, 433, 847]<|/det|>Teach me to sing. (infinitive)
<|det|>text [126, 847, 420, 869]<|/det|>Teach me singing. (gerund)
<|det|>text [126, 870, 469, 891]<|/det|>No one likes to wait. (infinitive)
<|det|>text [126, 892, 454, 915]<|/det|>No one likes waiting. (gerund)
<|det|>page_number [819, 927, 851, 946]<|/det|>95
<PAGE><|det|>title [116, 31, 490, 52]<|/det|>H. Circle the correct answer.
<|det|>list [189, 61, 958, 282]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [191, 63, 642, 87]<|/det|>1. I would like see/to see you again.
<|det|>text [191, 88, 642, 109]<|/det|>2. I saw him cross/to cross the road.
<|det|>text [191, 111, 642, 135]<|/det|>3. He likes swimming/to swimming.
<|det|>text [191, 135, 867, 158]<|/det|>4. He does nothing but watch/to watch TV all day long.
<|det|>text [191, 159, 745, 183]<|/det|>5. To learning/Learning French is not difficult.
<|det|>text [191, 185, 636, 207]<|/det|>6. Do not get off a run/running bus.
<|det|>text [191, 208, 881, 231]<|/det|>7. Finishing/Having finished their work, they went home.
<|det|>text [191, 231, 955, 256]<|/det|>8. After completing his homework, he went out to play/playing.
<|det|>text [191, 257, 684, 281]<|/det|>9. He is anxious to get/getting the news.
<|det|>image [104, 301, 151, 334]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [192, 306, 914, 356]<|/det|>Rewrite each of these sentences using a gerund in place of the infinitive.
<|det|>list [191, 367, 672, 577]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [193, 368, 470, 389]<|/det|>1. Teach me to cook.
<|det|>text [193, 391, 595, 414]<|/det|>2. To dance will require training.
<|det|>text [193, 416, 491, 436]<|/det|>3. To waste is not wise.
<|det|>text [193, 438, 672, 461]<|/det|>4. To sleep is Rajnish's favourite activity.
<|det|>text [193, 462, 612, 483]<|/det|>5. Children love to eat chocolates.
<|det|>text [193, 485, 521, 508]<|/det|>6. To save is a good habit.
<|det|>text [193, 509, 540, 530]<|/det|>7. I like to swim in the lake.
<|det|>text [193, 531, 603, 579]<|/det|>8. Farishta prefers to read, than to play outdoors.

<|det|>image [87, 611, 155, 647]<|/det|>
