<PAGE><|det|>title [62, 58, 89, 78]<|/det|>K.
<|det|>text [147, 54, 872, 145]<|/det|>A daughter has written a letter to her mother describing the journey that she and her younger brother undertook to see their uncle. Complete the letter using appropriate non-finites ('-ing', '-ed' and 'to' verb form).
<|det|>title [90, 180, 245, 202]<|/det|>Dear Mother
<|det|>text [90, 217, 701, 241]<|/det|>It would perhaps interest you a) ____ that
<|det|>text [90, 242, 884, 265]<|/det|>b) ____ by bus is a lot of fun. We keep c) ____
<|det|>text [90, 265, 794, 288]<|/det|>out of the window. It is good d) ____ such a lot of
<|det|>text [90, 288, 788, 311]<|/det|>greenery. Sheep and cows e) ____ in the field is a
<|det|>text [90, 311, 881, 336]<|/det|>common sight. I enjoy f) ____ the birds in flight. We hope
<|det|>text [90, 336, 648, 361]<|/det|>g) ____ our uncle's home in an hour.
<|det|>text [97, 375, 162, 395]<|/det|>Love
<|det|>text [97, 412, 170, 432]<|/det|>Kiran
<|det|>title [87, 489, 710, 513]<|/det|>L Pick out the non-finite verbs in the following text.
<|det|>title [463, 535, 531, 556]<|/det|>Kaffol
<|det|>text [390, 559, 604, 581]<|/det|>Your best protection
<|det|>text [387, 581, 609, 604]<|/det|>against heart trouble!
<|det|>text [105, 625, 767, 719]<|/det|>Make Kaffol part of your family's total health care plan, together with a balanced diet and regular exercise. Light and easy to digest, Kaffol can be used for cooking, frying, baking and in salad dressings to prepare all kinds of tasty dishes.
<|det|>image [793, 548, 868, 719]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [315, 739, 680, 763]<|/det|>Start your family on Kaffol, Today!
<|det|>text [388, 763, 610, 786]<|/det|>Good for your heart.
<|det|>page_number [838, 935, 868, 951]<|/det|>97
<PAGE><|det|>header [117, 35, 220, 130]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [772, 35, 955, 78]<|/det|>Escape!
<|det|>text [117, 154, 915, 203]<|/det|>What is your idea of happiness—a comfortable and cosy home or the big and unknown world outside?
<|det|>text [119, 215, 930, 286]<|/det|>I was then two years old, and was at the same time, the fattest and most naïve cat in existence. At that tender age, I took the sweetness and comforts of home life for granted.
<|det|>text [120, 298, 670, 440]<|/det|>How fortunate I was, indeed, to be placed with your aunt! That good woman adored me. I had, at the bottom of a wardrobe, a sleeping salon, with feather cushions and triple covers. My food was equally excellent; never just bread, or soup, but always meat, carefully chosen meat.
<|det|>text [122, 451, 641, 616]<|/det|>Well, in the midst of all this comfort, I had only one desire, one dream, and that was to slip out of the upper window and escape on to the roofs. Caresses annoyed me, the softness of my bed nauseated me, and I was so fat that it was disgusting even to myself. In short, I was bored the whole day long just with being happy.
<|det|>image [668, 270, 926, 610]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [122, 625, 942, 812]<|/det|>I must tell you that by stretching out my neck a bit, I had once seen the roof directly in front of my window. That day, four cats were playing with each other up there; their fur bristling, their tails high, they were romping around with every indication of joy on the blue roof slates baked by the sun. I had never before watched such an extraordinary spectacle. And from then on, I had a fixed belief; out there on that roof was true happiness, out there beyond the window which was always closed so carefully.
<|det|>text [122, 820, 922, 914]<|/det|>i resolved to flee. After all, there had to be other things in life besides a comfortable bed. Out there was the unknown, the ideal. And then one day, they forgot to close the kitchen window. I jumped out on to the small roof above it.
<|det|>page_number [150, 933, 182, 951]<|/det|>98
<PAGE><|det|>title [133, 51, 411, 73]<|/det|>Answer these questions.
<|det|>text [133, 82, 854, 129]<|/det|>1. In what ways was the life of the cat comfortable? (Mention any two ways.)
<|det|>text [132, 129, 844, 174]<|/det|>2. Why did the cat feel the need to leave the comforts of the house?
<|det|>text [132, 175, 865, 220]<|/det|>3. What had the cat seen when he stretched out his neck near his window?
<|det|>text [132, 222, 868, 268]<|/det|>4. Why do you think the window in the cat's room was always closed carefully?
<|det|>text [133, 269, 411, 290]<|/det|>5. 'I resolved to flee.'
<|det|>list [132, 82, 868, 290]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [200, 292, 842, 338]<|/det|>a. Who is the speaker of these words? Pick out any two adjectives that the speaker uses to describe himself.
<|det|>text [201, 339, 762, 362]<|/det|>b. Why did the speaker make such a resolution?
<|det|>text [201, 362, 848, 406]<|/det|>c. Did the speaker manage to flee? In 30 words, describe how he did it.
<|det|>list [200, 292, 848, 406]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [62, 442, 447, 463]<|/det|>B. Choose the correct answer.
<|det|>text [136, 471, 885, 495]<|/det|>1. The narrator describes the age of two as a tender age because
<|det|>text [201, 495, 803, 517]<|/det|>a. at this age, his faculties were not fully developed.
<|det|>text [201, 518, 722, 539]<|/det|>b. at this time, he led a very comfortable life.
<|det|>text [201, 540, 866, 561]<|/det|>c. he was naïve and had no idea about the world outside.
<|det|>list [201, 495, 866, 561]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [136, 565, 842, 588]<|/det|>2. The extraordinary spectacle that the narrator watched was
<|det|>text [201, 589, 667, 610]<|/det|>a. the blue roof slates baked by the sun.
<|det|>text [201, 610, 852, 633]<|/det|>b. four cats romping around with every indication of joy.
<|det|>text [201, 633, 835, 655]<|/det|>c. the open kitchen window with a small roof above it.
<|det|>list [201, 589, 852, 655]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [58, 684, 882, 732]<|/det|>C. Give the meanings of these words in the context in which they are used in the passage.
<|det|>text [136, 742, 270, 763]<|/det|>1. naïve
<|det|>text [136, 765, 273, 786]<|/det|>2. desire
<|det|>text [136, 788, 310, 810]<|/det|>3. spectacle
<|det|>text [136, 812, 299, 834]<|/det|>4. romping
<|det|>text [136, 835, 301, 855]<|/det|>5. resolved
<|det|>list [136, 742, 310, 855]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [824, 933, 856, 951]<|/det|>99
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 303, 136]<|/det|>12
<|det|>title [342, 48, 960, 128]<|/det|>Transitive and Intransitive Verbs
<|det|>image [119, 162, 177, 211]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [182, 189, 958, 214]<|/det|>We know that a sentence will always have a verb. A verb tells us-
<|det|>list [119, 216, 747, 285]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 216, 674, 239]<|/det|>• what a person or thing does—He repairs cars.
<|det|>text [120, 240, 677, 262]<|/det|>• what a person has—The girl has a pretty frock.
<|det|>text [120, 263, 747, 285]<|/det|>• what a person or thing is—The ostrich is a huge bird.
<|det|>text [119, 301, 424, 323]<|/det|>Now, look at this sentence.
<|det|>list [120, 325, 741, 370]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 325, 436, 346]<|/det|>• My mother cut the cake.
<|det|>text [120, 348, 741, 370]<|/det|>In this sentence, the cake receives the action of the verb.
<|det|>text [119, 397, 821, 443]<|/det|>The person or thing that receives the action of the verb is called the object.
<|det|>text [118, 480, 858, 525]<|/det|>When a verb has an object that receives the action of the verb, it is called a transitive verb.
<|det|>text [119, 562, 395, 583]<|/det|>Now, read this sentence.
<|det|>list [120, 585, 950, 671]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 585, 415, 607]<|/det|>• The child wept loudly.
<|det|>text [119, 607, 950, 629]<|/det|>In this sentence, there is no object. No one receives the action of the verb.
<|det|>text [118, 648, 767, 671]<|/det|>When a verb has no object, it is called an intransitive verb.
<|det|>image [120, 697, 182, 743]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [189, 709, 430, 730]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [120, 747, 474, 792]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 747, 446, 769]<|/det|>• My father gave me a fruit.
<|det|>text [120, 770, 474, 792]<|/det|>• My father gave a fruit to me.
<|det|>text [120, 810, 391, 833]<|/det|>Answer these questions.
<|det|>list [120, 838, 925, 910]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 838, 898, 861]<|/det|>• Who is the doer of the action? ____ (This is the subject.)
<|det|>text [121, 862, 864, 884]<|/det|>• What did Father give? ____ (This is the direct object.)
<|det|>text [121, 885, 923, 909]<|/det|>- To whom did Father give? ____ (This is the indirect object.)
<|det|>page_number [142, 933, 186, 950]<|/det|>100
<PAGE><|det|>text [70, 40, 899, 131]<|/det|>Most transitive verbs take a single object. But some transitive verbs like give, ask, promise, tell, etc. take two objects—a direct object and an indirect object. In the above sentences, the direct object is fruit, while the indirect object is me.
<|det|>text [73, 145, 821, 193]<|/det|>The person or thing that receives the action of the verb is called the direct object.
<|det|>text [73, 222, 821, 269]<|/det|>The person or thing to whom or on whom the action of the verb is done or who receives the direct object is called the indirect object.
<|det|>title [105, 305, 171, 331]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [472, 302, 521, 336]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 345, 867, 366]<|/det|>Do remember that most verbs can be used both transitively as well as intransitively. For example,
<|det|>text [102, 368, 368, 388]<|/det|>• The lawyer spoke the truth.
<|det|>text [102, 389, 351, 409]<|/det|>• The teacher spoke softly.
<|det|>list [102, 368, 368, 409]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 421, 848, 463]<|/det|>Sometime the subject and the object refer to the same person or thing. We then say that the verb has been used reflexively.
<|det|>text [102, 464, 304, 484]<|/det|>• The girl cut herself.
<|det|>text [101, 496, 521, 516]<|/det|>In such sentences, the object is sometimes omitted.
<|det|>text [102, 517, 323, 537]<|/det|>• The tyre burst (itself).
<|det|>list [101, 464, 521, 537]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [73, 575, 108, 598]<|/det|>A.
<|det|>text [160, 575, 895, 645]<|/det|>Underline the verbs in these sentences and state in each case whether the verb is used transitively or intransitively. If the verb is used transitively, point out the object/objects.
<|det|>list [174, 654, 577, 929]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [175, 654, 540, 676]<|/det|>1. The teacher gave me a book.
<|det|>text [175, 677, 576, 699]<|/det|>2. The children sang a lovely song.
<|det|>text [176, 700, 479, 722]<|/det|>3. The birds sang sweetly.
<|det|>text [176, 723, 512, 743]<|/det|>4. The men declared a truce.
<|det|>text [176, 745, 435, 766]<|/det|>5. He felled the trees.
<|det|>text [176, 768, 486, 790]<|/det|>6. The boy narrated a tale.
<|det|>text [176, 791, 384, 813]<|/det|>7. He fell asleep.
<|det|>text [175, 814, 553, 836]<|/det|>8. Can you spare a few minutes?
<|det|>text [175, 837, 414, 859]<|/det|>9. He spoke rudely.
<|det|>text [163, 860, 495, 882]<|/det|>10. The car halted suddenly.
<|det|>text [163, 883, 411, 905]<|/det|>11. I burnt my hand.
<|det|>text [162, 906, 582, 929]<|/det|>12. The fire raged through the forest.
<|det|>page_number [836, 922, 876, 940]<|/det|>101
<PAGE><|det|>list [189, 29, 675, 321]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [190, 30, 560, 55]<|/det|>13. They laughed at the joke.
<|det|>text [191, 56, 628, 79]<|/det|>14. The storm destroyed the house.
<|det|>text [191, 80, 674, 103]<|/det|>15. The farmer sold me some potatoes.
<|det|>text [191, 104, 636, 126]<|/det|>16. The boat floated down the river.
<|det|>text [191, 128, 475, 150]<|/det|>17. I moved the bowl.
<|det|>text [191, 152, 450, 175]<|/det|>18. The glass broke.
<|det|>text [191, 176, 528, 198]<|/det|>19. He taught me dancing.
<|det|>text [191, 198, 596, 221]<|/det|>20. The officer arrested the thief.
<|det|>text [191, 222, 636, 245]<|/det|>21. She sleeps in the next bedroom.
<|det|>text [191, 246, 653, 269]<|/det|>22. The dog chased the white mouse.
<|det|>text [191, 271, 468, 294]<|/det|>23. The door opened.
<|det|>text [191, 295, 550, 319]<|/det|>24. He promised me a book.
<|det|>image [725, 42, 964, 339]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [187, 346, 843, 373]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with appropriate objects for the verbs.
<|det|>list [201, 380, 867, 755]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [201, 380, 792, 404]<|/det|>1. My brother has polished his ____.
<|det|>text [201, 405, 720, 428]<|/det|>2. I brought ____ for my sister.
<|det|>text [201, 429, 825, 451]<|/det|>3. The boy threw a ____ at the window.
<|det|>text [201, 452, 821, 496]<|/det|>4. Frustrated with the official's call, Ethan kicked the ____.
<|det|>text [201, 498, 842, 523]<|/det|>5. The student guessed the ____ correctly.
<|det|>text [201, 524, 815, 546]<|/det|>6. Reema cleaned the ____ thoroughly.
<|det|>text [201, 547, 744, 569]<|/det|>7. I corrected the ____ yesterday.
<|det|>text [201, 570, 800, 591]<|/det|>8. Shabnam cooked ____ for all of us.
<|det|>text [201, 592, 815, 615]<|/det|>9. We staged ____ on our Parents' Day.
<|det|>text [194, 616, 631, 638]<|/det|>10. Mother helped ____.
<|det|>text [194, 639, 867, 661]<|/det|>11. He locked up all the ____ before leaving.
<|det|>text [194, 662, 833, 684]<|/det|>12. They pushed the ____ down the stairs.
<|det|>text [194, 685, 645, 706]<|/det|>13. She made ____ for us.
<|det|>text [194, 708, 825, 730]<|/det|>14. The farmer grows ____ in these fields.
<|det|>text [194, 731, 825, 753]<|/det|>15. We saw the ____ land on the runway.
<|det|>title [112, 766, 516, 791]<|/det|>Verbs of Incomplete Predication
<|det|>text [111, 792, 732, 816]<|/det|>We know that intransitive verbs do not require objects.
<|det|>text [112, 826, 388, 847]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [114, 848, 285, 916]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [114, 848, 331, 869]<|/det|>• The child cries.
<|det|>text [115, 872, 258, 890]<|/det|>- Men eat.
<|det|>text [115, 894, 283, 916]<|/det|>- Tigers roar.
<|det|>page_number [138, 934, 183, 952]<|/det|>102
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 32, 823, 81]<|/det|>he highlighted verbs in these sentences do not require any words to make complete sense.
<|det|>text [62, 102, 735, 149]<|/det|>An intransitive verb that makes complete sense on its own is called a verb of complete predication.
<|det|>text [61, 173, 862, 220]<|/det|>sometimes, an intransitive verb requires another word or words to make complete sense and to complete the predicate.
<|det|>text [61, 242, 882, 313]<|/det|>An intransitive verb that requires a word or phrase to complete its meaning is called a verb of incomplete predication. The word or words required to make the sense complete is called the complement of the verb.
<|det|>text [63, 337, 212, 359]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [64, 361, 346, 455]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [65, 361, 346, 384]<|/det|>• The boy appears sad.
<|det|>text [65, 385, 277, 406]<|/det|>• She is talented.
<|det|>text [65, 408, 310, 431]<|/det|>• She seems happy.
<|det|>text [65, 432, 446, 455]<|/det|>• The child continued laughing.
<|det|>text [61, 455, 823, 502]<|/det|>In the above examples, the complements tell us something about the subject. So, these are called subjective complements.
<|det|>text [61, 516, 867, 564]<|/det|>We know that transitive verbs have objects. Sometimes, just an object is not good enough. Something more is required to complete the predicate.
<|det|>text [63, 578, 310, 599]<|/det|>Look at this sentence.
<|det|>list [64, 601, 868, 670]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [64, 601, 499, 623]<|/det|>• The members elected Sam captain.
<|det|>text [62, 623, 868, 670]<|/det|>Here the word captain refers to Sam, the object of the verb. So captain is the objective complement.
<|det|>text [62, 694, 831, 765]<|/det|>C. Indicate whether the verbs in these sentences are transitive or intransitive. Name the object of each transitive verb and the complements of each verb of incomplete predication.
<|det|>list [146, 775, 538, 912]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [147, 775, 510, 798]<|/det|>1. The child has fallen asleep.
<|det|>text [146, 799, 510, 822]<|/det|>2. The count found him guilty.
<|det|>text [146, 823, 538, 845]<|/det|>3. The dog has gone quite crazy.
<|det|>text [146, 846, 515, 868]<|/det|>4. The boy watered the plants.
<|det|>text [146, 869, 478, 890]<|/det|>5. Pushpa speaks the truth.
<|det|>text [146, 891, 411, 913]<|/det|>6. They are Tibetans.
<|det|>page_number [822, 928, 866, 946]<|/det|>103
<PAGE><|det|>list [208, 37, 642, 365]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [210, 37, 585, 60]<|/det|>7. Rahul became the secretary.
<|det|>text [211, 62, 490, 85]<|/det|>8. She appears lonely.
<|det|>text [211, 85, 642, 107]<|/det|>9. The sky grew dark in the evening.
<|det|>text [198, 108, 516, 130]<|/det|>10. They opened the box.
<|det|>text [198, 131, 560, 152]<|/det|>11. He was elected chairman.
<|det|>text [198, 153, 570, 175]<|/det|>12. They set the prisoners free.
<|det|>text [198, 177, 513, 199]<|/det|>13. They looked relieved.
<|det|>text [198, 201, 575, 223]<|/det|>14. I have become an engineer.
<|det|>text [198, 224, 555, 244]<|/det|>15. The students blamed him.
<|det|>text [198, 246, 490, 268]<|/det|>16. The tea tastes bitter.
<|det|>text [198, 271, 550, 294]<|/det|>17. Rahim proved me wrong.
<|det|>text [198, 295, 520, 316]<|/det|>18. He became a minister.
<|det|>text [198, 318, 537, 340]<|/det|>19. The weather is pleasant.
<|det|>text [196, 340, 593, 365]<|/det|>20. We found grandfather asleep.
<|det|>image [312, 382, 778, 697]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [129, 934, 175, 952]<|/det|>104
<PAGE><|det|>title [65, 38, 606, 78]<|/det|>Active and Passive Voice
<|det|>page_number [816, 44, 886, 83]<|/det|>13
<|det|>image [62, 140, 118, 190]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [134, 148, 882, 174]<|/det|>Take a look at these sentences. How are these sentences different?
<|det|>text [131, 183, 316, 204]<|/det|>He wrote a letter.
<|det|>text [131, 206, 430, 230]<|/det|>A letter was written by him.
<|det|>image [65, 248, 123, 298]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [134, 245, 860, 293]<|/det|>In most sentences, the subject of the sentence is the doer of an action and the object of the sentence is the receiver of the action.
<|det|>text [140, 295, 284, 317]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [67, 318, 735, 365]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 318, 560, 341]<|/det|>- Cats eat fish. (cat—subject; fish—object)
<|det|>text [67, 341, 734, 365]<|/det|>• Rohan posted the letter. (Rohan—subject; letter—object)
<|det|>text [64, 383, 871, 454]<|/det|>The verb that expresses that the action is carried out by the subject is said to be in the active voice. The active voice is used when the agent or the doer is to be made prominent.
<|det|>text [65, 476, 852, 522]<|/det|>However, in some sentences, the subject receives the action of the verb. For example,
<|det|>list [67, 523, 825, 570]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 523, 672, 546]<|/det|>- Fish are eaten by cats. (fish—subject; cats—object)
<|det|>text [67, 546, 821, 570]<|/det|>• The letter was posted by Rohan. (letter—subject; Rohan—object)
<|det|>text [65, 593, 828, 640]<|/det|>The verb that expresses what is done to the subject is said to be in the passive voice.
<|det|>text [65, 669, 848, 716]<|/det|>Voice is that form of the verb which indicates whether the subject is the doer of the action or something is being done to it.
<|det|>text [65, 739, 848, 786]<|/det|>See how the following sentences in the active voice have been changed into the passive voice without changing their meanings.
<|det|>list [67, 787, 641, 880]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 787, 535, 809]<|/det|>• The captain called the players. (active)
<|det|>text [114, 810, 639, 832]<|/det|>The players were called by the captain. (passive)
<|det|>text [68, 833, 468, 855]<|/det|>• He is painting a portrait. (active)
<|det|>text [114, 855, 586, 877]<|/det|>A portrait is being painted by him. (passive)
<|det|>text [67, 891, 813, 914]<|/det|>Can you point out the differences in each of the pairs of sentences?
<|det|>page_number [816, 933, 856, 950]<|/det|>105
<PAGE><|det|>text [111, 29, 942, 80]<|/det|>Read these rules to be kept in mind when changing the voice of the verb from active to passive.
<|det|>list [112, 80, 816, 244]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [112, 80, 812, 148]<|/det|>- The positions of the subject and the object of the verb are interchanged. The subject of the verb in the active voice is made the object of the verb in the passive voice.
<|det|>text [112, 149, 816, 194]<|/det|>- In the passive voice, the preposition by is added before the object.
<|det|>text [112, 196, 886, 244]<|/det|>- The number of the verb is changed according to the number and person of the subject.
<|det|>text [111, 258, 911, 306]<|/det|>Study the table to understand how the verb is changed into the passive voice form in different tenses.
<|det|>table [115, 330, 952, 858]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Tense</td><td>Active voice</td><td>Passive voice</td></tr><tr><td>Simple present</td><td>He paints a wall.</td><td>A wall is painted by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Present continuous</td><td>He is painting a wall.</td><td>A wall is being painted by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Present perfect</td><td>He has painted a wall.</td><td>A wall has been painted by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Simple past</td><td>He painted a wall.</td><td>A wall was painted by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Past continuous</td><td>He was painting a wall.</td><td>A wall was being painted by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Past perfect</td><td>He had painted a wall.</td><td>A wall had been painted by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Simple future</td><td>He will paint a wall.</td><td>A wall will be painted by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Future continuous</td><td>He will be painting a wall.</td><td>—</td></tr><tr><td>Future perfect</td><td>He will have painted a wall.</td><td>A wall will have been painted by him.</td></tr></table>
<|det|>page_number [137, 925, 180, 946]<|/det|>106
<PAGE><|det|>title [115, 32, 872, 81]<|/det|>Underline the verbs in these sentences and state whether they are in the active or in the passive voice.
<|det|>list [116, 84, 812, 660]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 86, 633, 110]<|/det|>1. The farmer ploughed his land regularly.
<|det|>text [130, 111, 648, 134]<|/det|>2. The storm had caused extensive damage.
<|det|>text [130, 134, 560, 157]<|/det|>3. The ball was gifted by my coach.
<|det|>text [130, 157, 491, 179]<|/det|>4. He drank the glass of milk.
<|det|>text [130, 179, 643, 203]<|/det|>5. I dug the pit in the darkness of the night.
<|det|>text [130, 203, 699, 225]<|/det|>6. The teacher opened the door of the cupboard.
<|det|>text [130, 225, 545, 248]<|/det|>7. The mission was accomplished.
<|det|>text [130, 248, 808, 272]<|/det|>8. A moment later, the door was flung open by the prince.
<|det|>text [130, 272, 699, 295]<|/det|>9. The chocolate cake was baked by my mother.
<|det|>text [120, 295, 713, 318]<|/det|>10. The hunter continued to step cautiously ahead.
<|det|>text [120, 318, 648, 341]<|/det|>11. I picked up the heavy box from the table.
<|det|>text [120, 341, 503, 364]<|/det|>12. Mowgli laughed at the joke.
<|det|>text [120, 364, 800, 388]<|/det|>13. The girl was chewing the same piece of gum for hours.
<|det|>text [120, 388, 570, 411]<|/det|>14. I unfastened the strings of the bag.
<|det|>text [120, 411, 527, 432]<|/det|>15. The servant obeyed his orders.
<|det|>text [120, 432, 680, 455]<|/det|>16. The prescription will be written by Dr Mitra.
<|det|>text [120, 455, 580, 478]<|/det|>17. The squeaky mouse ate the cheese.
<|det|>text [120, 478, 737, 501]<|/det|>18. The crowd lined the road and cheered the leader.
<|det|>text [120, 501, 364, 523]<|/det|>19. He lit a candle.
<|det|>text [120, 524, 535, 547]<|/det|>20. He picked up the ring furtively.
<|det|>text [120, 547, 548, 571]<|/det|>21. The letter was mailed by Pragya.
<|det|>text [120, 571, 578, 593]<|/det|>22. The book was bought by my father.
<|det|>text [120, 593, 498, 616]<|/det|>23. They called off the meeting.
<|det|>text [120, 616, 533, 638]<|/det|>24. Someone cleaned the window.
<|det|>text [120, 638, 588, 661]<|/det|>25. English is spoken all over the world.
<|det|>title [73, 681, 130, 707]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [92, 716, 741, 800]<|/det|>- If the active verb has two objects—one direct and the other indirect—either of them becomes the subject of the passive verb, and the other remains as an object of the verb in the passive voice. The object that comes after the passive verb is called the retained object. For example,
<|det|>list [117, 801, 603, 925]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 801, 490, 820]<|/det|>• My father gave me a cycle. (active voice)
<|det|>text [117, 822, 555, 841]<|/det|>• I was given a cycle by my father. (passive voice)
<|det|>text [117, 843, 597, 862]<|/det|>• A cycle was given to me by my father. (passive voice)
<|det|>text [92, 864, 607, 883]<|/det|>• Active verbs followed by a preposition retain the preposition.
<|det|>text [117, 885, 438, 903]<|/det|>• The students laughed at the clown.
<|det|>text [117, 905, 498, 925]<|/det|>• The clown was laughed at by the students.
<|det|>text [657, 755, 715, 780]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [760, 747, 812, 780]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [655, 791, 892, 832]<|/det|>Do, does and did are not used in the passive voice.
<|det|>page_number [805, 930, 853, 950]<|/det|>107
<PAGE><|det|>header [110, 36, 160, 84]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [174, 35, 890, 84]<|/det|>Study this table that shows the changes in imperative sentences where the subject is omitted.
<|det|>table [111, 98, 952, 264]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Active voice</td><td>Passive voice</td></tr><tr><td>Take it home.</td><td>Let it be taken home.</td></tr><tr><td>Do it right now.</td><td>Let it be done right now.</td></tr><tr><td>Open the door.</td><td>Let the door be opened.</td></tr><tr><td>Do not heat the food.</td><td>Let not the food be heated.</td></tr></table>
<|det|>text [112, 276, 837, 322]<|/det|>- The imperative sentence in the passive voice takes this form—Let + object + be + past participle
<|det|>text [113, 324, 758, 370]<|/det|>- If the sentence is in the negative, then the structure is—Let not + object + be + past participle
<|det|>list [112, 276, 837, 370]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [112, 385, 785, 433]<|/det|>Study this table to understand how verbs in the active voice in interrogative sentences are changed into the passive voice.
<|det|>table [115, 445, 943, 715]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Active voice</td><td>Passive voice</td></tr><tr><td>Did Suman invite you?</td><td>Were you invited by Suman?</td></tr><tr><td>Do the children like chocolates?</td><td>Are chocolates liked by the children?</td></tr><tr><td>Have you finished the work?</td><td>Has the work been finished by you?</td></tr><tr><td>Can anybody cure this disease?</td><td>Can this disease be cured?(Anybody is not used.)</td></tr><tr><td>Who hit the ball?</td><td>By whom was the ball hit?</td></tr><tr><td>When will they announce the date?</td><td>When will the date be announced?(They is not used.)</td></tr></table>
<|det|>title [115, 741, 586, 810]<|/det|>B. Change the verbs in these sentences from the active to the passive voice. Make changes as required.
<|det|>text [192, 819, 478, 841]<|/det|>1. Children love cakes.
<|det|>text [192, 843, 425, 865]<|/det|>2. Open the door.
<|det|>text [192, 866, 697, 889]<|/det|>3. Gargi made special biscuits for everyone.
<|det|>text [192, 890, 679, 913]<|/det|>4. Suresh picked up the ball and kicked it.
<|det|>list [192, 819, 697, 913]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [631, 748, 687, 781]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [657, 791, 892, 833]<|/det|>Do, does and did are not used in the passive voice.
<|det|>page_number [145, 927, 187, 948]<|/det|>108
<PAGE><|det|>list [136, 29, 605, 411]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [136, 29, 521, 54]<|/det|>5. My mother loves her pet dog.
<|det|>text [136, 56, 571, 78]<|/det|>6. Have you completed the painting?
<|det|>text [136, 79, 603, 100]<|/det|>7. We elected Malini our class monitor.
<|det|>text [136, 102, 495, 126]<|/det|>8. Santa Claus gave me a gift.
<|det|>text [136, 127, 606, 148]<|/det|>9. Everyone loves ice creams in summer.
<|det|>text [130, 149, 543, 172]<|/det|>10. When will they shift the office?
<|det|>text [130, 173, 372, 195]<|/det|>11. They knew this.
<|det|>text [130, 197, 457, 221]<|/det|>12. Maya kept Rita waiting.
<|det|>text [130, 222, 563, 244]<|/det|>13. One should keep one's promises.
<|det|>text [130, 245, 416, 266]<|/det|>14. Who has done this?
<|det|>text [130, 268, 512, 291]<|/det|>15. Your uncle taught me maths.
<|det|>text [130, 292, 588, 315]<|/det|>16. The masons are building the house.
<|det|>text [130, 316, 530, 339]<|/det|>17. Somebody has stolen my ring.
<|det|>text [130, 340, 519, 362]<|/det|>18. They are watching us closely.
<|det|>text [130, 363, 543, 385]<|/det|>19. What does this packet contain?
<|det|>text [130, 386, 723, 410]<|/det|>20. The gardener tends to the flowers in my garden.
<|det|>image [607, 55, 964, 301]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [65, 420, 823, 468]<|/det|>1 Let us look at the instances when the use of the passive voice is preferred.
<|det|>text [67, 483, 424, 505]<|/det|>We use the passive voice when—
<|det|>list [68, 506, 871, 688]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [68, 506, 856, 597]<|/det|>the person or thing acted upon is to be made prominent. The passive voice is thus preferred when the active form would involve the use of a vague pronoun or noun such as somebody, they, people, anybody, as the subject. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 598, 774, 620]<|/det|>• My book has been stolen. (Someone has stolen my book.)
<|det|>text [103, 621, 722, 643]<|/det|>• I was asked my identity. (They asked me my identity.)
<|det|>text [103, 643, 871, 687]<|/det|>- I have been invited to the conference. (Someone has invited me to the conference.)
<|det|>text [68, 700, 827, 744]<|/det|>◆ the doer is not known or when it is not intended to name the doer. For example,
<|det|>text [104, 746, 446, 766]<|/det|>• The victim was carried out.
<|det|>text [68, 780, 875, 825]<|/det|>◆ we wish to focus on the person or the thing acted upon rather than the doer or the agent. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 826, 590, 850]<|/det|>• The building was destroyed by lightning.
<|det|>text [68, 860, 810, 883]<|/det|>When the subject in the active voice is implied from the context.
<|det|>list [103, 885, 620, 931]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [103, 885, 620, 908]<|/det|>• He was fired from his job (by his employer).
<|det|>text [103, 909, 617, 931]<|/det|>• The city has been captured (by the enemy).
<|det|>page_number [808, 929, 851, 947]<|/det|>109
<PAGE><|det|>title [141, 45, 205, 72]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [137, 84, 923, 126]<|/det|>Since the object of a verb in the active voice becomes the subject of the passive form, it means that only transitive verbs can be used in the passive, because an intransitive verb has no object.
<|det|>text [115, 154, 905, 201]<|/det|>See how sentences in the passive voice can be changed into the active voice.
<|det|>text [117, 215, 796, 239]<|/det|>Passive: The work will be finished by the plumber in a week.
<|det|>text [119, 240, 705, 262]<|/det|>Active: The plumber will finish the work in a week.
<|det|>text [117, 277, 713, 301]<|/det|>Passive: By whom was this major task accomplished?
<|det|>text [119, 302, 618, 325]<|/det|>Active: Who accomplished this major task?
<|det|>text [117, 340, 668, 364]<|/det|>Passive: I have been asked to attend this meeting.
<|det|>text [119, 365, 727, 389]<|/det|>Active: Someone has asked me to attend this meeting.
<|det|>title [115, 416, 860, 464]<|/det|>C. Change the verbs in these sentences into active voice. Make changes as required.
<|det|>text [205, 475, 747, 499]<|/det|>1. The procession was led by a noted politician.
<|det|>text [206, 500, 529, 522]<|/det|>2. Jerry is troubled by Tom.
<|det|>text [206, 523, 573, 545]<|/det|>3. Let the painting be unveiled.
<|det|>text [206, 546, 721, 568]<|/det|>4. A cup of coffee was ordered by my mother.
<|det|>text [207, 569, 721, 591]<|/det|>5. The factory was inaugurated by Mr Gomes.
<|det|>text [207, 592, 646, 614]<|/det|>6. The ball was kicked hard by Dorian.
<|det|>text [207, 615, 916, 638]<|/det|>7. Mr Sultan was elected captain by the members of the club.
<|det|>text [207, 639, 671, 661]<|/det|>8. Can the lead pieces be moved by you?
<|det|>text [207, 662, 663, 684]<|/det|>9. Nothing can be done by anyone now.
<|det|>text [200, 685, 666, 707]<|/det|>10. A large box was opened by my friend.
<|det|>text [200, 709, 596, 731]<|/det|>11. A large crowd was seen by me.
<|det|>text [200, 732, 580, 775]<|/det|>12. A very comfortable night was spent by me at the hotel.
<|det|>text [200, 777, 589, 823]<|/det|>13. A huge bag was carried by the porter.
<|det|>text [200, 824, 556, 890]<|/det|>14. The streets were crowded by anxious supporters of the national team.
<|det|>text [199, 893, 596, 940]<|/det|>15. The tree was knocked down by a speeding vehicle.
<|det|>image [639, 720, 941, 925]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [140, 930, 186, 950]<|/det|>110
<PAGE><|det|>text [130, 40, 845, 160]<|/det|>Given below are six pairs of jumbled up sentences. The verb in one sentence in each pair is in active voice, while the other is in passive voice. Rearrange them to form meaningful sentences and write down if they are in active or passive voice. Follow the example.
<|det|>text [135, 168, 650, 240]<|/det|>1. the bone / chewed / dog / the
The dog chewed the bone. (active voice)
was chewed / by / the bone / dog/ the
<|det|>text [134, 242, 848, 310]<|/det|>The bone was chewed by the dog. (passive voice)
2. were cheered / of the debate / the winners / by the children the winners / cheered / the children / of the debate
<|det|>text [137, 312, 600, 358]<|/det|>3. by grandpa / was made / a sandwich
a sandwich / made / grandpa
<|det|>text [138, 359, 655, 404]<|/det|>4. Ritika / the flowers / is watering by Ritika / are being watered / the flowers
<|det|>text [139, 405, 781, 450]<|/det|>5. helped the old lady/ Koel / cross the road to cross the road / by Koel / the old lady / was helped
<|det|>text [139, 451, 572, 499]<|/det|>6. a pizza / Abhinav / bought by Abhinav / was bought / a pizza
<|det|>text [135, 535, 586, 559]<|/det|>Rewrite this passage in the passive voice.
<|det|>text [134, 572, 875, 712]<|/det|>Amit opened the door, switched on the lights and turned on the TV. He took out his wallet and threw it on the bed. Someone had broken the flower vase. Who could have done that? A strange sense of fear plagued him. Was it a haunted room? The sound of a creaking door nearby startled him. Everyone had warned him not to spend the night there.
<|det|>page_number [808, 930, 851, 950]<|/det|>111
<PAGE><|det|>image [39, 0, 287, 136]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [606, 29, 944, 71]<|/det|>Notice Writing
<|det|>image [119, 144, 177, 191]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [183, 141, 921, 213]<|/det|>A notice is a brief written communication. It is written to make an announcement or to display information to a specific group of people. A notice should be presented within a box.
<|det|>text [117, 226, 471, 251]<|/det|>Look at this sample of a notice.
<|det|>title [489, 265, 567, 285]<|/det|>Notice
<|det|>title [472, 289, 593, 309]<|/det|>MV School
<|det|>title [435, 311, 620, 332]<|/det|>Trek to Rishikesh
<|det|>text [135, 351, 286, 373]<|/det|>15 July 20XX
<|det|>text [132, 378, 877, 472]<|/det|>The Adventure Club of the school is organising a three-day trek to Rishikesh in August, for students of Classes 6, 7 and 8. The total expenditure per student would be Rs 2000/-, which will include bus fare, food and lodging.
<|det|>text [132, 475, 856, 523]<|/det|>Those interested, kindly submit your names to the undersigned by 28 July 20XX.
<|det|>text [131, 538, 281, 561]<|/det|>Vinod Gupta
<|det|>text [134, 563, 242, 584]<|/det|>Secretary
<|det|>text [134, 585, 312, 605]<|/det|>Adventure Club
<|det|>image [115, 636, 177, 682]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [189, 636, 925, 682]<|/det|>The format in which a notice is drafted is very important. A notice must include—
<|det|>list [114, 685, 611, 802]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [115, 685, 297, 709]<|/det|>• the heading
<|det|>text [115, 709, 532, 729]<|/det|>• the date when the notice is issued
<|det|>text [115, 731, 611, 800]<|/det|>- the topic of the notice placed in a central position in line with the name of the institution
<|det|>text [111, 803, 598, 825]<|/det|>The content of the notice should not exceed
<|det|>text [631, 680, 696, 706]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [770, 675, 824, 711]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [631, 720, 905, 805]<|/det|>The people who are being addressed in the notice may be mentioned below the heading, towards the left margin.
<|det|>text [110, 827, 920, 922]<|/det|>50–55 words. It must contain the details of the event—when, where and for how long, or the last date for registration/response. Last, but not the least, there must be the name of the organisation or person issuing the notice.
<|det|>page_number [135, 937, 180, 958]<|/det|>112
<PAGE><|det|>text [62, 39, 826, 87]<|/det|>Now, write notices of about 50–55 words for each of these topics or situations. You can use the guidelines provided.
<|det|>text [138, 94, 867, 186]<|/det|>The students of Class 7 will be taken on a trip to the Industrial and Technological Museum on 7 December. Draft a notice informing the students that they have to bring Rs 50 for the entrance fee, bus fare and lunch.
<|det|>text [141, 188, 874, 282]<|/det|>A magic show is being organised in the school in order to raise money for the disaster-stricken people of Uttarakhand. Formulate a notice informing the students of the date and venue and calling for volunteers to manage the audience.
<|det|>text [62, 282, 867, 374]<|/det|>3. Selections for the school football team will take place on Monday, 14 August. As captain of the team, formulate a notice informing the students about the selections which will take place after school hours.
<|det|>text [70, 374, 868, 464]<|/det|>1. A fancy dress competition is being organised by the school on Children's Day. This will give an opportunity to showcase the hidden talents of the students. As a student's coordinator, write out a notice for the same.
<|det|>text [68, 465, 862, 580]<|/det|>5. As the editor of your school magazine, write a notice announcing an inter-class poetry writing competition on 28 September, at 3 p.m. in the library. Invite names of those interested in participating and announce that the best poems will be published in the annual edition.
<|det|>page_number [816, 924, 860, 943]<|/det|>113
