<PAGE><|det|>header [115, 28, 187, 70]<|/det|>14
<|det|>header [395, 28, 959, 66]<|/det|>Subject-Verb Agreement
<|det|>image [115, 139, 173, 183]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 159, 925, 231]<|/det|>As a rule, the verb in a sentence always agrees with the subject in number and person. If the subject is singular, the verb is singular. If the subject is plural, the verb is also plural. For example,
<|det|>list [117, 233, 346, 280]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 233, 331, 255]<|/det|>• The baby cries.
<|det|>text [117, 257, 286, 280]<|/det|>- Babies cry.
<|det|>image [115, 295, 174, 345]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [182, 293, 954, 365]<|/det|>Sometimes, it is difficult to tell whether the subject is singular or plural. Here are a few hints that will help you find out if the subject is singular or plural.
<|det|>list [113, 366, 954, 435]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 366, 954, 412]<|/det|>- Two or more singular subjects connected by and usually take a verb in the plural. For example,
<|det|>text [160, 414, 474, 435]<|/det|>• Rahul and Ravi are here.
<|det|>list [113, 452, 938, 522]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 452, 938, 498]<|/det|>- If two singular nouns refer to the same person or thing, the verb must be singular. For example,
<|det|>text [159, 499, 563, 522]<|/det|>• My friend and neighbour is here.
<|det|>list [113, 537, 938, 612]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 537, 868, 584]<|/det|>- If two subjects together express one idea, the verb may be in the singular. For example,
<|det|>text [157, 585, 933, 608]<|/det|>- The long and short of the matter is that she has decided to return.
<|det|>text [107, 624, 834, 671]<|/det|>- If the subject is preceded by each or every, the verb is usually singular. For example,
<|det|>list [149, 672, 841, 717]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 672, 841, 695]<|/det|>• Every man, woman and child was present at the gathering.
<|det|>text [150, 696, 541, 717]<|/det|>• Each child wants an ice cream.
<|det|>text [106, 734, 901, 781]<|/det|>- Two or more singular subjects connected by or, nor, neither...nor, either...or take a verb in the singular. For example,
<|det|>list [149, 782, 558, 805]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 782, 549, 805]<|/det|>Neither Rita nor I was prepared.
<|det|>text [106, 821, 911, 868]<|/det|>- When the subjects joined by or, nor take different persons, the verb agrees in person with the one nearest to it. For example,
<|det|>list [149, 869, 737, 913]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 869, 491, 890]<|/det|>Either he or I am to blame.
<|det|>text [150, 891, 737, 913]<|/det|>Neither Amit nor his friends have seen that movie.
<|det|>page_number [120, 932, 172, 952]<|/det|>114
<PAGE><|det|>text [70, 22, 871, 96]<|/det|>- When the subjects joined by or, nor are of different numbers, the verb must be plural, and the plural subject must be placed next to the verb. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 97, 605, 121]<|/det|>- Neither Susan nor her parents were ready.
<|det|>text [70, 135, 844, 183]<|/det|>- When subjects differing in number or person or both are connected by and, the verb must always be in the plural. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 183, 471, 207]<|/det|>• She and I are leaving the job.
<|det|>text [70, 221, 867, 293]<|/det|>A collective noun takes a singular verb when the collection is thought of as a whole. A plural verb is used when the collection is thought of separate individuals. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 294, 667, 316]<|/det|>• A number of members of the team were absent.
<|det|>text [110, 317, 545, 340]<|/det|>• The jury has agreed to our proposal.
<|det|>text [70, 355, 792, 403]<|/det|>- Nouns which are plural in form but singular in meaning, take a singular verb. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 404, 337, 425]<|/det|>• The news is true.
<|det|>text [70, 440, 803, 488]<|/det|>- Words joined to a singular subject by along with, together with, as well as take a singular verb.
<|det|>text [110, 489, 684, 513]<|/det|>• The general, along with his regiment, has arrived.
<|det|>text [70, 526, 770, 551]<|/det|>- Class names such as furniture, stationery, take a singular verb.
<|det|>text [110, 551, 492, 575]<|/det|>• The furniture is of good quality.
<|det|>text [70, 588, 802, 635]<|/det|>- Words in which two components are always thought of together, as in, scissors, spectacles, shoes, trousers, take a singular verb.
<|det|>text [110, 636, 429, 659]<|/det|>• The pair of trousers is his.
<|det|>text [70, 672, 830, 719]<|/det|>- However, when we omit the words a pair of, such words then take a plural verb.
<|det|>text [110, 721, 350, 743]<|/det|>• My shoes are wet.
<|det|>text [70, 758, 815, 829]<|/det|>- If two or more singular subjects are preceded by either, either of, neither of, each, each of, every one of, many a, none, none of, nobody or somebody, the verb is in the singular.
<|det|>text [110, 830, 704, 853]<|/det|>• Every one of us is equal in the eyes of the Almighty.
<|det|>text [110, 853, 490, 876]<|/det|>• Somebody has eaten the apple.
<|det|>text [110, 876, 545, 901]<|/det|>• Each of the girls has performed well.
<|det|>page_number [808, 930, 850, 948]<|/det|>115
<PAGE><|det|>text [115, 20, 891, 69]<|/det|>- When a plural number applies to distances, heights, weights, it is taken as a whole and is therefore treated as a singular.
<|det|>text [167, 73, 611, 96]<|/det|>• Ten million rupees is a lot of money.
<|det|>text [167, 98, 720, 122]<|/det|>• Ten kilos of apples costs three hundred rupees.
<|det|>list [167, 73, 720, 122]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [115, 131, 915, 179]<|/det|>The quantifier many refers to numbers and is plural; much refers to amounts and is thus singular.
<|det|>text [167, 182, 558, 205]<|/det|>- Many of the peaches were ripe.
<|det|>text [167, 207, 635, 231]<|/det|>• Much of the rice was saved from pests.
<|det|>list [167, 182, 635, 231]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [115, 240, 951, 290]<|/det|>- Fractions take a plural verb if the reference is to number, but they take a singular verb if the reference is to amount or quantity.
<|det|>text [167, 292, 492, 316]<|/det|>• Some of the girls are sick.
<|det|>text [167, 318, 538, 341]<|/det|>• Some of the milk has curdled.
<|det|>list [167, 292, 538, 341]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [115, 361, 943, 409]<|/det|>A. Vineet and Nidhi visit a restaurant. Complete the conversation by choosing the correct form of the verb.
<|det|>text [191, 424, 951, 472]<|/det|>Nidhi: This table beside the window ____ (offer/offers) a good view.
<|det|>text [191, 475, 901, 522]<|/det|>Vineet: Yes, the view from here—of the mountains, clouds and sky—____ (is/are) beautiful, isn't it?
<|det|>text [191, 526, 912, 573]<|/det|>Nidhi: The music and the lighting ____ (is/are) both perfect. Here's the menu.
<|det|>text [191, 578, 940, 625]<|/det|>Vineet: Every item listed here ____ (sound/sounds) so French, but none of them ____ (is/are) expensive.
<|det|>text [191, 629, 901, 674]<|/det|>Nidhi: Can you see if the house ____ (serve/serves) Chinese?
<|det|>text [191, 679, 948, 704]<|/det|>Vineet: No, they ____ (serve/serves) only Tibetan and Thai.
<|det|>text [191, 707, 750, 730]<|/det|>Nidhi: Either of them ____ (are/is) fine.
<|det|>title [115, 759, 895, 783]<|/det|>B. Fill in the blanks choosing the correct verbs from the brackets.
<|det|>text [191, 791, 809, 815]<|/det|>1. Slow and steady ____ the race. (win/wins)
<|det|>text [191, 816, 901, 839]<|/det|>2. My spectacles ____ missing from my bag. (are/is)
<|det|>text [191, 840, 714, 863]<|/det|>3. Half of hundred ____ fifty. (are/is)
<|det|>text [191, 864, 895, 887]<|/det|>4. Many a candidate ____ failed the test. (has/have)
<|det|>text [191, 888, 938, 911]<|/det|>5. The pupils of this class ____ very committed. (is/are)
<|det|>page_number [142, 927, 187, 948]<|/det|>116
<PAGE><|det|>text [135, 45, 815, 91]<|/det|>6. The crockery in this shop ____ quite expensive.
(are/is)
<|det|>text [136, 92, 752, 115]<|/det|>7. A fleet of ships ____ docked here. (is/are)
<|det|>text [137, 116, 842, 139]<|/det|>8. Notices ____ been sent to all involved. (has/have)
<|det|>text [137, 140, 867, 162]<|/det|>9. Neither ____ he educated, nor a gentleman. (are/is)
<|det|>text [130, 163, 825, 186]<|/det|>10. A herd of cows ____ grazing in the field. (is/are)
<|det|>text [130, 187, 833, 209]<|/det|>11. Much of the truth ____ not disclosed. (were/was)
<|det|>text [130, 210, 686, 232]<|/det|>12. Not a drum ____ heard. (was/were)
<|det|>text [130, 233, 839, 279]<|/det|>13. Seventy-five thousand rupees ____ a large sum of money. (is/are)
<|det|>text [130, 280, 867, 303]<|/det|>14. The Government ____ taken its decision. (have/has)
<|det|>text [130, 305, 815, 327]<|/det|>15. Each of the criminals ____ arrested. (was/were)
<|det|>text [130, 329, 780, 374]<|/det|>16. Neither Harish nor his sister ____ been here.
(have/has)
<|det|>text [130, 376, 794, 422]<|/det|>17. The gardener or his sons ____ care of the land. (take/takes)
<|det|>text [130, 423, 833, 469]<|/det|>18. The father, as well as his sons, ____ hardworking.
(is/are)
<|det|>list [130, 95, 867, 469]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [130, 486, 793, 533]<|/det|>In each of these sentences, supply a verb in agreement with the subject.
<|det|>text [147, 541, 641, 565]<|/det|>1. Suman and I ____ great friends.
<|det|>text [146, 567, 650, 588]<|/det|>2. Bread and butter ____ nutrition.
<|det|>text [146, 590, 567, 613]<|/det|>3. Four and four ____ eight.
<|det|>text [146, 614, 800, 636]<|/det|>4. Mathematics ____ a difficult subject to master.
<|det|>text [146, 639, 678, 662]<|/det|>5. Each of the boys ____ given a plan.
<|det|>text [146, 664, 655, 686]<|/det|>6. Every one of the motels ____ full.
<|det|>text [146, 688, 657, 711]<|/det|>7. Neither he nor they ____ correct.
<|det|>text [146, 712, 721, 735]<|/det|>8. My friend, as well as I, ____ felicitated.
<|det|>text [146, 737, 580, 783]<|/det|>9. A number of vehicles ____ been impounded by the police.
<|det|>text [137, 785, 597, 828]<|/det|>10. The committee ____ passed the resolution.
<|det|>text [137, 832, 534, 877]<|/det|>11. Ten miles ____ a long distance.
<|det|>text [137, 880, 601, 902]<|/det|>12. Oil and water ____ not mix.
<|det|>list [137, 541, 800, 902]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [616, 716, 882, 888]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [814, 927, 856, 948]<|/det|>117
<PAGE><|det|>text [184, 36, 646, 59]<|/det|>13. His means ____ meagre.
<|det|>text [185, 61, 838, 84]<|/det|>14. The historian and philosopher ____ dead.
<|det|>text [185, 87, 785, 110]<|/det|>15. No one ____ explained this properly.
<|det|>list [184, 36, 838, 110]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [104, 128, 136, 150]<|/det|>D.
<|det|>text [180, 127, 942, 199]<|/det|>This paragraph contains some errors in subject-verb agreement. Find and correct these errors. Remember the tense of the verb should not change.
<|det|>text [180, 209, 957, 570]<|/det|>It are one thing to visit a zoo as an ordinary member of the public but quite another to live in the middle of it. This, at times, are a mixed blessing. It certainly enable you to rush out at any hour of the day or night to observe your charges, but it also mean that you is on duty twenty-four hours a day, and you finds that a cosy little dinner party disintegrate because some animal have broken its leg, or because the heaters in the reptile house has failed, or for any of a dozen reasons. Winter, of course, are your slack period, and sometimes days on end passes without a single visitor in the grounds and you begins to feel that the zoo are really your own private one. The pleasantness of this sensation are more than slightly marred by the alarm with which you views the mounting of your bills and compares them to the lack of gate-money. But in the season the days is so full and the visitors so numerous that your badly seems to notice the passing of time, and you forgets your overdraft.
<|det|>image [195, 580, 952, 927]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 922, 175, 944]<|/det|>118
<PAGE><|det|>header [54, 35, 348, 74]<|/det|>prepositions
<|det|>header [782, 35, 870, 78]<|/det|>15
<|det|>header [875, 35, 967, 130]<|/det|>15
<|det|>image [58, 140, 114, 190]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [126, 140, 870, 209]<|/det|>You have learnt that a preposition is a word that shows the relationship between a noun or a pronoun and some other word in a sentence.
<|det|>text [55, 226, 845, 295]<|/det|>Let us take a look at a few examples to understand this better. The highlighted prepositions in the sentences given below show the relation between the underlined words.
<|det|>list [59, 297, 452, 369]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [60, 297, 361, 321]<|/det|>• The baby is on the bed.
<|det|>text [60, 322, 353, 345]<|/det|>• I am scared of spiders.
<|det|>text [60, 345, 451, 369]<|/det|>• The diver jumped into the pool.
<|det|>text [58, 383, 862, 431]<|/det|>Did you notice that in each of the three sentences, the preposition comes before a noun? In each case, the noun is the object of that preposition.
<|det|>text [59, 445, 874, 493]<|/det|>You have also learnt that prepositions may be used to express a number of relationships.
<|det|>text [60, 508, 331, 528]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [61, 530, 778, 600]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [62, 530, 429, 553]<|/det|>- School reopened on Monday.
<|det|>text [62, 553, 593, 575]<|/det|>• We were on holiday for a month before that.
<|det|>text [62, 576, 776, 599]<|/det|>• Getting to school by 8 a.m. on that first Monday was not easy.
<|det|>text [59, 612, 862, 660]<|/det|>The highlighted prepositions in these sentences have been used to denote time so they are known as prepositions of time.
<|det|>text [59, 674, 825, 721]<|/det|>Some prepositions that are commonly used to denote time are before, after, during, from, since, for, within, by, till, at, in and on.
<|det|>text [59, 736, 361, 757]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [61, 758, 668, 828]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 758, 599, 780]<|/det|>• The students are waiting in the assembly hall.
<|det|>text [61, 782, 668, 804]<|/det|>• A few among the younger students are very excited.
<|det|>text [61, 805, 643, 827]<|/det|>• The prefects are standing in two rows at the back.
<|det|>text [58, 846, 813, 894]<|/det|>The highlighted prepositions in these sentences have been used to indicate position so they are called prepositions of position or place.
<|det|>page_number [814, 927, 856, 946]<|/det|>119
<PAGE><|det|>text [105, 13, 568, 107]<|/det|>Some prepositions that are commonly used to denote position are at, on, in, beside, behind, beyond, above, over, under, below, near, among and between.
<|det|>text [107, 121, 453, 143]<|/det|>Take a look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [109, 145, 565, 240]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 145, 565, 191]<|/det|>- As soon as the bell rang, the students ran to the playground.
<|det|>text [110, 192, 854, 216]<|/det|>- The teachers asked the students to walk down the stairs quietly.
<|det|>text [110, 217, 744, 240]<|/det|>• The students could not wait to get out of the building.
<|det|>image [604, 24, 675, 58]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [595, 65, 663, 94]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [595, 105, 928, 147]<|/det|>The words at, on and in can be used both as prepositions of time and
<|det|>text [595, 148, 800, 128]<|/det|>prepositions of position.
<|det|>text [106, 262, 953, 335]<|/det|>The highlighted prepositions in the above sentences have been used to indicate direction related to movement so they are known as prepositions of motion.
<|det|>text [108, 360, 571, 477]<|/det|>Some prepositions that are commonly used to denote direction or movement are to, towards, out of, into, at, over, across, up, down, around, along, against, through, from, off and for.
<|det|>text [110, 492, 548, 516]<|/det|>Prepositions can also be classified as—
<|det|>list [111, 516, 664, 682]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 516, 664, 561]<|/det|>◆ simple prepositions that only consist of one word like most of the ones listed above.
<|det|>text [111, 562, 545, 682]<|/det|>◆ compound or phrase prepositions that are made up of more than one word. For example, in place of, away from, in case of, by means of, because of, in front of.
<|det|>image [584, 365, 671, 404]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [593, 412, 901, 455]<|/det|>The word at can also be used as a preposition of motion. For example,
<|det|>text [614, 455, 864, 495]<|/det|>- The boy threw a stone at the dog.
<|det|>image [594, 577, 661, 610]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [593, 619, 938, 661]<|/det|>Phrase prepositions always end with a simple preposition.
<|det|>image [104, 699, 153, 731]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [180, 696, 667, 723]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with suitable prepositions.
<|det|>list [180, 731, 934, 900]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [181, 731, 641, 754]<|/det|>1. You must leave ____ sunset.
<|det|>text [180, 755, 672, 777]<|/det|>2. The child hid ____ the curtains.
<|det|>text [180, 778, 791, 800]<|/det|>3. Our class teacher will return ____ a week.
<|det|>text [180, 801, 845, 825]<|/det|>4. I jumped ____ the lake ____ the others.
<|det|>text [180, 826, 898, 849]<|/det|>5. I shall return ____ the fifth ____ the month.
<|det|>text [180, 850, 892, 874]<|/det|>6. The event proved to be an eye-opener ____ many.
<|det|>text [180, 875, 931, 899]<|/det|>7. I think you should leave ____ completing your work.
<|det|>page_number [135, 923, 180, 943]<|/det|>120
<PAGE><|det|>list [157, 33, 856, 345]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [157, 33, 856, 58]<|/det|>8. Keep the biscuits ____ the shelf ____ the sink.
<|det|>text [158, 58, 821, 102]<|/det|>9. He stood ____ the principal ____ his head bowed.
<|det|>text [149, 107, 805, 152]<|/det|>10. I arrived ____ time ____ the inauguration ceremony.
<|det|>text [149, 154, 765, 199]<|/det|>11. You will find him sitting quietly ____ a chair ____ the fireside.
<|det|>text [149, 202, 800, 250]<|/det|>12. Five years ____ I was born, this house was built ____ my father.
<|det|>text [150, 251, 785, 297]<|/det|>13. He has come ____ London and will be staying ____ this country ____ a month.
<|det|>text [150, 298, 862, 345]<|/det|>14. The American tourists plan to walk ____ a village ____ the hills. They will stay there ____ a week.
<|det|>image [80, 362, 135, 408]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [149, 362, 748, 408]<|/det|>Look at these sentences showing the correct use of some prepositions that are often confused.
<|det|>text [81, 409, 793, 431]<|/det|>- Beside means by the side of while besides means in addition to.
<|det|>list [123, 433, 590, 477]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [123, 433, 630, 455]<|/det|>• The cat slept on the bed beside the little boy.
<|det|>text [124, 456, 567, 478]<|/det|>• Besides this cat, he has two other pets.
<|det|>text [81, 489, 806, 535]<|/det|>Of may be used to indicate origin, cause, material, etc. However, off usually means from, away or not on.
<|det|>list [123, 536, 463, 580]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [124, 536, 429, 556]<|/det|>• The dress is made of silk.
<|det|>text [125, 558, 463, 580]<|/det|>• The toddler fell off the chair.
<|det|>text [81, 591, 535, 684]<|/det|>Since is used to indicate a point of time in the past when an action started while from can be used to indicate an action in the past, present or future.
<|det|>list [123, 685, 528, 775]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [124, 685, 528, 707]<|/det|>• The child has been ill since Friday.
<|det|>text [125, 708, 520, 730]<|/det|>• My son begins school from today.
<|det|>text [125, 731, 495, 775]<|/det|>• He will begin to repay the loan from September 2015.
<|det|>text [81, 787, 534, 857]<|/det|>Between is used when speaking of two things or people while among refers to more than two people or things.
<|det|>list [123, 858, 710, 903]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [124, 858, 660, 880]<|/det|>• Meghna is standing between Mohana and Srija.
<|det|>text [125, 880, 710, 903]<|/det|>• The cadets distributed the fruits among the children.
<|det|>text [572, 603, 631, 631]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [704, 599, 755, 636]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [567, 643, 857, 701]<|/det|>The word from can indicate source or origin as well as reason and motion.
<|det|>list [568, 704, 848, 803]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [568, 704, 816, 723]<|/det|>• We get heat from the sun.
<|det|>text [569, 724, 821, 763]<|/det|>• The poor man suffers from asthma terribly.
<|det|>text [569, 764, 847, 803]<|/det|>• We walked from the market to our house.
<|det|>page_number [799, 933, 843, 953]<|/det|>121
<PAGE><|det|>text [120, 32, 942, 81]<|/det|>- With relates to the instrument used to do something while by refers to the agent or the doer.
<|det|>text [166, 80, 918, 105]<|/det|>• I prefer to write with a fountain pen rather than a ballpoint pen.
<|det|>text [166, 105, 730, 127]<|/det|>• This chart was drawn by me with water colours.
<|det|>list [166, 80, 918, 127]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 143, 936, 191]<|/det|>B. Now, complete these passages choosing appropriate prepositions from the ones given in the brackets.
<|det|>text [195, 200, 942, 295]<|/det|>1. I would like to sit ____ my mother during the show. My mother said that she had no idea as to who ____ me would accompany her that day. I spotted the tickets for the show on a table ____ the window. (beside, besides)
<|det|>text [194, 297, 942, 367]<|/det|>2. The island nation of Sri Lanka is situated ____ the coast ____ India. The people ____ Sri Lanka are very similar to Indians in their physical appearance. (of, off)
<|det|>text [194, 368, 938, 461]<|/det|>3. I have been ill ____ Monday so I am not sure whether our principal has joined work. ____ tomorrow, I should be able to assist you. I know that our clerk has been assisting you ____ last year. (from, since)
<|det|>text [194, 462, 938, 575]<|/det|>4. The Smith brothers are quarrelling ____ themselves. I was surprised to see this because there have never been any arguments ____ my brother and me. The problem will be solved if the brothers agree to share the responsibilities ____ themselves equally. (among, between)
<|det|>text [195, 577, 920, 669]<|/det|>5. There was once a talented artist who learnt to paint ____ his left foot because he could not do anything ____ his hands. He was encouraged ____ his mother. (by, with)
<|det|>list [194, 200, 942, 669]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 685, 931, 757]<|/det|>C. Fill in the blanks choosing prepositions from the box given below. Put x where no preposition is required. You may need to use some prepositions more than once.
<|det|>text [208, 772, 914, 860]<|/det|>since from between among on upon
beside besides in into of off
over at with for by behind
<|det|>text [195, 875, 720, 898]<|/det|>1. The child has been ill ____ Monday.
<|det|>text [195, 899, 802, 922]<|/det|>2. He has been my teacher ____ I was a child.
<|det|>page_number [145, 932, 192, 950]<|/det|>122
<PAGE><|det|>list [136, 35, 836, 601]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [136, 35, 800, 61]<|/det|>3. The cat jumped ____ the fat mouse and killed it.
<|det|>text [136, 61, 823, 86]<|/det|>4. You will have to choose ____ the two participants.
<|det|>text [137, 86, 821, 132]<|/det|>5. He will start his journey ____ five ____ the morning.
<|det|>text [137, 133, 830, 178]<|/det|>6. I wonder how ____ Tom was chosen ____ the task.
<|det|>text [137, 180, 735, 229]<|/det|>7. The leopard jumped ____ the tree and ran ____ its cage.
<|det|>text [137, 229, 846, 274]<|/det|>8. Remember to distribute the sweets ____ the children ____ the class.
<|det|>text [137, 276, 842, 323]<|/det|>9. I asked him to switch ____ the lights ____ all the empty rooms.
<|det|>text [127, 324, 858, 367]<|/det|>10. I asked him to put the box ____ the table ____ the window.
<|det|>text [128, 369, 767, 414]<|/det|>11. I will go and sit ____ my grandmother who is ____ the next room.
<|det|>text [128, 416, 826, 462]<|/det|>12. He has been ____ town ____ yesterday and will deliver his first lecture ____ today.
<|det|>text [128, 463, 860, 509]<|/det|>13. The bowl is made ____ silver and will be ____ display ____ the museum ____ Monday.
<|det|>text [128, 509, 815, 554]<|/det|>14. The sum ____ money was too small to be divided ____ thirty men.
<|det|>text [128, 555, 811, 602]<|/det|>15. ____ famous actors, ____ many sportsmen will also participate ____ the opening ceremony.
<|det|>text [125, 618, 836, 688]<|/det|>In this paragraph, some prepositions have been incorrectly used. Rewrite the paragraph using the correct prepositions. You may also omit prepositions when they are not required.
<|det|>text [125, 698, 854, 927]<|/det|>Close by the nursery is a garden that is tended to by an old man. Besides the garden, there is a church. In Sundays, many people come to the church. For the service, some of the people enter the garden. On one corner of the garden, there is a small pond for many water lilies. The garden is open to the public from four hours at the morning. The old gardener lives in a cottage after the church. The priests for the church are very fond off him. On yesterday, the priests were shocked to find that his cottage was empty. A bunch of keys was in the table around the window. His coat was hanging on a hook at the wall.
<|det|>page_number [793, 932, 836, 952]<|/det|>123
<PAGE><|det|>header [98, 33, 201, 130]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [591, 38, 951, 78]<|/det|>The Shoe Story
<|det|>text [111, 159, 951, 278]<|/det|>Necessity forced our ancestors to invent a method of protecting their feet from nature's fury. Early footwear was made by layering leaves together and later from leather. Then came the sandal, which was a simple piece of plaited grass or leather strapped to the feet. Eventually, sandal-making became a recognised art in Egyptian history.
<|det|>text [112, 292, 940, 361]<|/det|>There have also been ancient hieroglyphics found in caves depicting men wearing shoes. Records of the Egyptians and Chinese as well as other early civilisations have recorded references to shoes.
<|det|>text [112, 375, 935, 446]<|/det|>The Egyptians favoured beautiful and artistic sandals. The Greeks stressed on design and beauty, while the Romans created a military sandal that enabled their armies to travel throughout the empire.
<|det|>text [112, 459, 933, 553]<|/det|>In many ancient cultures, shoes indicated social status. A Roman officer's rank was noted by the height of the shoe; the higher the shoe, the higher the rank. A Pharaoh's sandal had a long, peaked toe to denote a king, prince or priest.
<|det|>text [112, 566, 945, 753]<|/det|>During the mid 1600s, the Sun King Louis XIV created a new fad of platform shoes, simply because he had the royal power to do so. The platform shoes soon became the symbol of nobility. Also during this time, women in Florence were wearing platform shoes known as chopines. The chopines were worn to increase a woman's height. They were often 24 inches off the ground and ladies had to be escorted through the streets. Some historians believe the modern day high heel originates from the chopine.
<|det|>text [111, 766, 911, 883]<|/det|>The first Oxford was worn by Oxford University students in the seventeenth century. By the eighteenth century, satin pumps with high heels and pointed toes became fashionable for women. Toes became rounded and heels became lower so that by end of the century, low cut slippers were in style.
<|det|>page_number [135, 925, 180, 944]<|/det|>124
<PAGE><|det|>text [54, 33, 808, 85]<|/det|>the history of shoes and what they stand for is preserved and put on display in countless museums around the world.
<|det|>title [131, 110, 378, 133]<|/det|>Answer the questions.
<|det|>list [129, 139, 870, 260]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 141, 865, 187]<|/det|>1. How did the shoe story begin and how were the early shoes made?
<|det|>text [131, 189, 706, 213]<|/det|>2. When did sandal-making first get recognition?
<|det|>text [132, 213, 743, 235]<|/det|>3. Where can we find recorded references to shoes?
<|det|>text [131, 235, 865, 259]<|/det|>4. Mention with an example how shoes indicated social status.
<|det|>title [131, 290, 650, 314]<|/det|>Say whether these statements are true or false.
<|det|>list [131, 322, 631, 416]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [134, 323, 565, 348]<|/det|>1. The Egyptians liked artistic shoes.
<|det|>text [134, 348, 516, 371]<|/det|>2. The Greeks preferred design.
<|det|>text [134, 371, 582, 394]<|/det|>3. The Romans wore military sandals.
<|det|>text [134, 394, 624, 416]<|/det|>4. A Pharaoh's sandal had flat broad toes.
<|det|>title [131, 447, 661, 471]<|/det|>C. Find the meanings of these words from the text.
<|det|>list [131, 479, 370, 549]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [134, 481, 297, 503]<|/det|>1. a sandal
<|det|>text [134, 504, 365, 526]<|/det|>2. platform shoes
<|det|>text [134, 527, 370, 549]<|/det|>3. the first Oxford
<|det|>title [131, 580, 776, 604]<|/det|>D. Find a suitable word from the text for the given meanings.
<|det|>list [131, 613, 705, 728]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 613, 705, 637]<|/det|>1. folding three or more bunches of hair together
<|det|>text [131, 638, 378, 660]<|/det|>2. very great anger
<|det|>text [131, 660, 534, 684]<|/det|>3. to be allowed to do something
<|det|>text [131, 684, 341, 707]<|/det|>4. a pointed tip
<|det|>text [131, 707, 278, 728]<|/det|>5. guided
<|det|>page_number [814, 923, 856, 941]<|/det|>125
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 322, 142]<|/det|>16
<|det|>header [671, 44, 948, 88]<|/det|>Conjunctio
<|det|>image [113, 149, 177, 196]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [191, 179, 544, 204]<|/det|>Read the following sentences.
<|det|>list [116, 204, 714, 274]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [116, 204, 714, 228]<|/det|>• I was late but I still managed to finish the work.
<|det|>text [116, 226, 625, 251]<|/det|>• Gita bought a pen and I bought a book.
<|det|>text [116, 249, 565, 274]<|/det|>• I will call you when I reach home.
<|det|>image [114, 284, 177, 331]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [194, 297, 663, 323]<|/det|>The highlighted words are conjunctions.
<|det|>text [111, 345, 821, 388]<|/det|>A conjunction is a word used to join words, groups of words or sentences.
<|det|>text [112, 412, 544, 437]<|/det|>There are two types of conjunctions.
<|det|>list [111, 441, 901, 510]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 441, 476, 465]<|/det|>◆ coordinating conjunctions
<|det|>text [526, 444, 901, 468]<|/det|>◆ subordinating conjunctions
<|det|>title [112, 474, 483, 498]<|/det|>Coordinating Conjunctions
<|det|>text [111, 503, 378, 523]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [111, 527, 741, 707]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [112, 527, 741, 549]<|/det|>- I went to the market and bought a pair of trousers.
<|det|>text [113, 550, 652, 571]<|/det|>• He, as well as his father, has left for Delhi.
<|det|>text [113, 572, 567, 595]<|/det|>• I was tired, still I went to the party.
<|det|>text [113, 595, 724, 619]<|/det|>• It rained heavily, but there was no water-logging.
<|det|>text [113, 619, 623, 641]<|/det|>• The man is very rich, yet he is unhappy.
<|det|>text [113, 641, 690, 664]<|/det|>- Either visit your aunt or call her up right now.
<|det|>text [113, 664, 688, 687]<|/det|>• Neither is he a loyal person nor is he truthful.
<|det|>text [113, 687, 633, 709]<|/det|>• He will do well in life for he is focussed.
<|det|>image [765, 558, 952, 705]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 722, 804, 749]<|/det|>These highlighted conjunctions help to join two statements.
<|det|>text [106, 763, 951, 814]<|/det|>A conjunction that joins two independent statements or two statements of equal importance is called a coordinating conjunction.
<|det|>text [106, 828, 915, 902]<|/det|>Most commonly used coordinating conjunctions are and, but, for, or, either...or, neither...nor, yet, and still. Either...or, neither...nor, not only...but also, both...and, no less...than are used in pairs.
<|det|>page_number [132, 924, 181, 943]<|/det|>126
<PAGE><|det|>title [90, 38, 152, 64]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [459, 32, 511, 70]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [84, 78, 680, 100]<|/det|>We should choose a conjunction carefully, else the meaning will change.
<|det|>text [85, 100, 283, 118]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [85, 121, 577, 184]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 121, 576, 141]<|/det|>Kate gave me textbooks. Kate gave me compact discs.
<|det|>text [121, 142, 492, 162]<|/det|>Kate gave me textbooks and compact discs.
<|det|>text [121, 163, 537, 183]<|/det|>Kate gave me both textbooks and compact discs.
<|det|>text [84, 185, 862, 206]<|/det|>The meaning here is that not only did Kate give textbooks but also provided the compact discs.
<|det|>text [85, 207, 652, 227]<|/det|>By using the conjunction and, the sense is not conveyed completely.
<|det|>list [85, 228, 408, 313]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 228, 377, 248]<|/det|>• I was tired. I went to the party.
<|det|>text [121, 249, 408, 269]<|/det|>I was tired and I went to the party.
<|det|>text [121, 270, 397, 291]<|/det|>I was tired so I went to the party.
<|det|>text [121, 292, 408, 313]<|/det|>I was tired, yet I went to the party.
<|det|>text [85, 315, 856, 337]<|/det|>By using the coordinating conjunctions so and and, the idea has not been conveyed correctly.
<|det|>title [78, 375, 108, 394]<|/det|>A.
<|det|>text [143, 374, 809, 445]<|/det|>Match the columns and rewrite the two sets of sentences by joining them with the conjunctions and/but/or. Make other necessary changes.
<|det|>list [159, 462, 877, 901]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 462, 512, 486]<|/det|>1. Anusha won the competition.
<|det|>text [160, 522, 487, 546]<|/det|>2. Rita wanted to buy a book.
<|det|>text [160, 559, 442, 602]<|/det|>3. Seema could go to the market.
<|det|>text [160, 616, 480, 661]<|/det|>4. The truck carrying aid has overturned.
<|det|>text [160, 676, 470, 720]<|/det|>5. You can take a bus to the station.
<|det|>text [160, 735, 512, 780]<|/det|>6. Janaki will accompany me to the hostel.
<|det|>text [160, 794, 512, 841]<|/det|>7. I have decided not to participate in the competition.
<|det|>text [160, 853, 521, 898]<|/det|>8. The football team lost the first match.
<|det|>text [536, 462, 821, 508]<|/det|>a. Radha could go to the market.
<|det|>text [536, 522, 836, 546]<|/det|>b. I will certainly attend it.
<|det|>text [536, 558, 821, 580]<|/det|>c. All aid material is lost.
<|det|>text [536, 618, 856, 662]<|/det|>d. You can take a taxi to the station.
<|det|>text [536, 677, 822, 698]<|/det|>e. She also won the race.
<|det|>text [536, 734, 816, 782]<|/det|>f. The team has another chance to qualify.
<|det|>text [536, 794, 802, 841]<|/det|>g. She did not have the money.
<|det|>text [536, 853, 866, 876]<|/det|>h. Janaki will leave after that.
<|det|>page_number [816, 925, 865, 946]<|/det|>127
<PAGE><|det|>title [111, 45, 912, 115]<|/det|>B. Fill in the blanks with appropriate conjunctions. Choose from the words given in the box. (You may use a conjunction more than once. You may not need to use a few.)
<|det|>text [197, 135, 920, 205]<|/det|>and or but still yet so for nevertheless otherwise either...or neither...nor not only...but also both...and not one less...than as well as
<|det|>text [200, 230, 898, 280]<|/det|>1. ____ is he good at his work ____ does he wish to learn it.
<|det|>text [200, 288, 920, 313]<|/det|>2. Mary was late, ____ she finished the paper on time.
<|det|>text [200, 318, 835, 342]<|/det|>3. ____ take a bath ____ go for a swim.
<|det|>text [200, 346, 870, 368]<|/det|>4. She is ____ industrious ____ motivated.
<|det|>text [200, 374, 854, 398]<|/det|>5. Gita ____ her sister has received a scholarship.
<|det|>text [200, 402, 890, 426]<|/det|>6. Leave immediately ____ you will miss your flight.
<|det|>text [200, 430, 813, 479]<|/det|>7. The rescue workers were exhausted by the heat, ____ they refused to cease work for a while.
<|det|>text [200, 485, 885, 536]<|/det|>8. The recent floods caused extensive damage to the village, ____ the school on the hillock was spared.
<|det|>text [200, 540, 828, 590]<|/det|>9. ____ do I want to learn French ____ German.
<|det|>text [192, 595, 876, 644]<|/det|>10. He did not put in adequate effort, ____ he failed the test.
<|det|>text [120, 659, 862, 684]<|/det|>C. Join these pairs of sentences using coordinating conjunctions.
<|det|>text [208, 690, 521, 713]<|/det|>1. He is poor. He is happy.
<|det|>text [208, 714, 568, 736]<|/det|>2. It is raining. I have to go out.
<|det|>text [208, 736, 590, 757]<|/det|>3. Run fast. You will miss the bus.
<|det|>text [208, 759, 625, 781]<|/det|>4. I was angry. I did not utter a word.
<|det|>text [208, 782, 669, 804]<|/det|>5. The food was tasty. It was inexpensive.
<|det|>text [208, 805, 770, 827]<|/det|>6. I do not know the details. I will find out for you.
<|det|>text [208, 828, 795, 850]<|/det|>7. Kushal bought a pen. He also bought a calculator.
<|det|>text [208, 851, 752, 873]<|/det|>8. Ravi was punished. Sammy was punished too.
<|det|>text [208, 873, 839, 896]<|/det|>9. He is committed to his work. His employer loves him.
<|det|>text [196, 897, 868, 940]<|/det|>10. Raju did not leave the office on time. He missed the last train.
<|det|>page_number [145, 937, 192, 956]<|/det|>128
<PAGE><|det|>title [54, 38, 420, 63]<|/det|>subordinating Conjunctions
<|det|>text [54, 69, 304, 90]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [55, 91, 722, 181]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [55, 91, 455, 111]<|/det|>• I returned after the sun had set.
<|det|>text [55, 113, 633, 136]<|/det|>- Do not leave the premises till the guards arrive.
<|det|>text [55, 137, 721, 160]<|/det|>• The fire brigade reached the spot as soon as they could.
<|det|>text [55, 160, 541, 181]<|/det|>• I will inform you whenever I meet him.
<|det|>text [53, 197, 855, 268]<|/det|>The highlighted parts of these sentences do not convey complete sense. It is only when these parts are added to the rest of the sentence with conjunctions (the underlined words) that they make sense.
<|det|>text [54, 290, 890, 338]<|/det|>A conjunction that joins two statements, one of which is dependent on the other, is called a subordinating conjunction.
<|det|>text [54, 361, 856, 407]<|/det|>Subordinating conjunctions express contrast between ideas or show the cause and effect relationship between ideas. For example,
<|det|>list [56, 407, 851, 568]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [56, 407, 770, 429]<|/det|>• I left for the studio when the rain stopped. (cause and effect)
<|det|>text [56, 430, 851, 452]<|/det|>• I dislike him because he does not keep his word. (cause and effect)
<|det|>text [56, 453, 724, 476]<|/det|>• I could not visit you as I had fallen ill. (cause and effect)
<|det|>text [56, 477, 851, 521]<|/det|>- The train was so crowded that we could not reach our seats. (cause and effect)
<|det|>text [56, 522, 825, 544]<|/det|>• She has not lost her will though she has suffered much. (contrast)
<|det|>text [56, 545, 675, 568]<|/det|>• Although he is poor, he is an honest man. (contrast)
<|det|>text [50, 591, 836, 638]<|/det|>D. Rewrite these sentences using the conjunctions given in the brackets. The meaning should not change. One is done for you.
<|det|>list [131, 647, 768, 925]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 647, 768, 694]<|/det|>1. I shall do the work only if you allow me to. (unless) I shall not do the work unless you allow me to.
<|det|>text [128, 695, 558, 715]<|/det|>2. We eat in order to survive. (that)
<|det|>text [128, 717, 640, 740]<|/det|>3. On realising my mistake, I felt sorry. (as)
<|det|>text [128, 741, 621, 763]<|/det|>4. Being ill, he stayed at home. (because)
<|det|>text [128, 764, 642, 786]<|/det|>5. He wins respect everywhere. (wherever)
<|det|>text [127, 787, 761, 809]<|/det|>6. Without studying hard, I will not succeed. (unless)
<|det|>text [127, 810, 745, 833]<|/det|>7. The villagers kept awake fearing another raid. (as)
<|det|>text [126, 834, 831, 877]<|/det|>8. No policeman being present, the thief escaped with ease. (since)
<|det|>text [125, 879, 788, 924]<|/det|>9. The heavy rains resulted in the crops being destroyed. (so...that)
<|det|>page_number [814, 927, 856, 946]<|/det|>129
<PAGE><|det|>text [189, 26, 912, 75]<|/det|>10. He is practising hard in order to be selected in the school cricket team. (so that)
<|det|>text [189, 75, 902, 121]<|/det|>11. As soon as she heard the news, she ran out of the house. (when)
<|det|>text [189, 121, 953, 145]<|/det|>12. When they reached the theatre the show had started. (before)
<|det|>list [189, 26, 953, 145]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 162, 912, 188]<|/det|>E. Join these sentences using suitable subordinating conjunctions.
<|det|>text [200, 196, 930, 219]<|/det|>1. His performance was poor. We were all very disappointed.
<|det|>text [200, 220, 928, 242]<|/det|>2. I am taking a taxi. I do not want to be late for the function.
<|det|>text [200, 243, 714, 266]<|/det|>3. He must do well. I will give him a prize.
<|det|>text [200, 266, 565, 288]<|/det|>4. There is life. There is hope.
<|det|>text [200, 289, 781, 311]<|/det|>5. The teacher found his bag. He had left it there.
<|det|>text [200, 313, 796, 335]<|/det|>6. I cannot spare my notes. I have a test tomorrow.
<|det|>text [200, 337, 639, 358]<|/det|>7. Trust is once lost. It is lost forever.
<|det|>text [200, 360, 888, 383]<|/det|>8. I got a phone call. I left for my home town immediately.
<|det|>text [200, 384, 942, 429]<|/det|>9. We did not invite him to the party. We did not know he was in the city.
<|det|>text [188, 430, 948, 453]<|/det|>10. I prefer to visit this library. It has a better collection of books.
<|det|>list [188, 196, 948, 453]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [104, 475, 156, 520]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [165, 475, 946, 522]<|/det|>These conjunctions convey their full sense only when they are used in pairs.
<|det|>text [140, 525, 275, 544]<|/det|>either...or
<|det|>text [157, 547, 740, 569]<|/det|>- You must either apologise or accept punishment.
<|det|>text [157, 570, 774, 593]<|/det|>• She will either visit you today or call you tomorrow.
<|det|>list [157, 547, 774, 593]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [107, 606, 300, 626]<|/det|>neither...nor
<|det|>text [157, 628, 515, 648]<|/det|>Neither he nor I can do this.
<|det|>text [157, 651, 619, 674]<|/det|>Neither Rekha nor Rama was present.
<|det|>list [157, 628, 619, 674]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [107, 686, 275, 707]<|/det|>both...and
<|det|>text [157, 709, 648, 731]<|/det|>• The athlete was both fined and expelled.
<|det|>text [157, 732, 771, 756]<|/det|>- The trainer was both happy and relieved to see him.
<|det|>list [157, 686, 771, 756]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [107, 767, 245, 788]<|/det|>so...that
<|det|>text [157, 790, 892, 812]<|/det|>• He worked so intently that he completed the task before time.
<|det|>text [157, 813, 640, 834]<|/det|>• He ran so fast that he was out of breath.
<|det|>list [157, 790, 892, 834]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [104, 848, 341, 869]<|/det|>no sooner...than
<|det|>text [157, 871, 928, 894]<|/det|>• No sooner had I entered the room than I heard a loud explosion.
<|det|>text [157, 895, 930, 920]<|/det|>- No sooner had the child entered the room than he started crying.
<|det|>list [157, 871, 930, 920]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 928, 168, 948]<|/det|>130
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 35, 329, 59]<|/det|>not only...but also
<|det|>list [111, 60, 826, 152]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 60, 816, 106]<|/det|>- The hailstorm not only destroyed the standing crops but also damaged the fruit trees.
<|det|>text [111, 106, 829, 152]<|/det|>- My teacher not only helped me but also promised to lend me some books.
<|det|>text [65, 170, 192, 187]<|/det|>as...so
<|det|>text [111, 189, 504, 211]<|/det|>• As the morning is, so is the day.
<|det|>text [111, 213, 489, 237]<|/det|>• As you sow, so shall you reap.
<|det|>text [65, 258, 790, 285]<|/det|>F. Correct these sentences. Focus on the use of conjunctions.
<|det|>list [160, 293, 870, 598]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [161, 293, 612, 317]<|/det|>1. I will leave while my mother arrives.
<|det|>text [161, 318, 729, 340]<|/det|>2. He no less his sister is responsible for the mess.
<|det|>text [161, 341, 868, 365]<|/det|>3. The earthquake was severe and the village was not affected.
<|det|>text [161, 366, 756, 387]<|/det|>4. The weather was fine as well as the sea was calm.
<|det|>text [161, 389, 714, 411]<|/det|>5. It appears since his mother is in a foul temper.
<|det|>text [161, 412, 798, 435]<|/det|>6. He checked my notes nevertheless I finished cooking.
<|det|>text [161, 436, 776, 457]<|/det|>7. No sooner did I receive the news, I left for Mumbai.
<|det|>text [161, 459, 679, 481]<|/det|>8. I shall accompany you unless you desire it.
<|det|>text [161, 482, 682, 503]<|/det|>9. I shall complete the task where I have time.
<|det|>text [148, 505, 857, 550]<|/det|>10. The management wanted to know though he would accept the proposal.
<|det|>text [148, 551, 792, 573]<|/det|>11. Not only had Jatin boarded the bus but also it started.
<|det|>text [148, 574, 679, 597]<|/det|>12. Both Randhir and Raj has broken the plate.
<|det|>page_number [808, 933, 850, 952]<|/det|>131
<PAGE><|det|>header [98, 33, 238, 130]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [385, 35, 948, 81]<|/det|>Explaining the Itinerary
<|det|>text [98, 158, 920, 230]<|/det|>Sports Day, Ayesha is working as a volunteer at the enquiry desk. A parent comes up to ask her about the forthcoming events. Read their conversation.
<|det|>text [98, 245, 874, 316]<|/det|>Parent: Excuse me! I think I'm a bit late. I must have missed the opening ceremony, but can I enter the grounds and be a spectator for the rest of the events?
<|det|>text [98, 317, 907, 409]<|/det|>Ayesha: Of course, you can go in, sir. You're not that late. The chief guest has just finished his speech. The march past will begin in a while and all the other competitions will begin after that.
<|det|>text [98, 410, 870, 433]<|/det|>Parent: Could you tell me what will be the itinerary for the day?
<|det|>text [98, 434, 920, 619]<|/det|>Ayesha: Well, there'll be a lot of races. The 100m and 200m races will occur shortly after the march past. Then, there will be others like the sack race, obstacle race, three-legged race and so on. Since our field is quite big, three separate sets of tracks have been drawn. The races have been split among them. There is a huge board beside each set of tracks, listing the races to be held there. You can decide which races you want to watch and move about.
<|det|>text [98, 620, 922, 667]<|/det|>Parent: What about the high jump and the long jump? My daughter is participating in those events. I specially want to see those.
<|det|>text [98, 667, 901, 757]<|/det|>Ayesha: The sand pit is at the far end of the field. The high jump competition will begin at 10:30 a.m., followed by the long jump. And the javelin and discus throwing competitions will take place near the fountain from 11:30 a.m.
<|det|>text [98, 758, 871, 802]<|/det|>Parent: Good. Is there anything else that you would recommend I see?
<|det|>text [98, 804, 931, 851]<|/det|>Ayesha: Well, sir, in the hall down this corridor, there is an exhibition you could see.
<|det|>text [98, 852, 574, 876]<|/det|>Parent: What exhibition might that be?
<|det|>page_number [120, 937, 165, 958]<|/det|>132
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 27, 862, 75]<|/det|>Ayesha: Some of the exemplary art work done this term by each class is on display. Your daughter's painting could be there.
<|det|>text [64, 75, 453, 96]<|/det|>Parent: I'll definitely take a look.
<|det|>text [65, 97, 868, 186]<|/det|>Ayesha: And sir, a refreshments tent has been set up near the swings. You will get tea or coffee and snacks there. And would you like to sign up for the parents' relay race? It's towards the end of the programme and great fun.
<|det|>text [65, 187, 862, 256]<|/det|>Parent: Oh, thank you for reminding me. My daughter wanted me to participate in that race. She promised to cheer hard for me. Should I register with you?
<|det|>text [65, 257, 847, 303]<|/det|>Ayesha: Yes. Just write your name in this book and I'll give you your number. The rules will be explained to you before the race.
<|det|>text [65, 304, 528, 326]<|/det|>Parent: And when is it all likely to end?
<|det|>text [65, 327, 868, 370]<|/det|>Ayesha: The prize-giving ceremony is scheduled at 3:30 p.m. By then, all the events should be over.
<|det|>text [65, 373, 607, 397]<|/det|>Parent: Thank you very much for all your help.
<|det|>text [65, 397, 855, 464]<|/det|>Ayesha: It was my pleasure, sir. You had better take your seat now in the spectator's enclosure. The girls are all queuing up for the march past.
<|det|>text [65, 466, 613, 490]<|/det|>Parent: Very well. I'd better be going then. Bye.
<|det|>text [65, 490, 306, 513]<|/det|>Ayesha: Bye bye, sir.
<|det|>text [64, 543, 368, 566]<|/det|>Now, enact these situations.
<|det|>text [65, 577, 856, 671]<|/det|>1. Rajeev and Sumathi, the head boy and girl have gone to invite the district school inspector to their school's Annual Day Function. Work in groups of three and enact how the two students inform the inspector about the itinerary for that day.
<|det|>text [61, 672, 856, 768]<|/det|>2. Jayram wants to buy tickets for himself and his sister to see a magic show. Work in pairs and enact the conversation between Jayram and the ticket seller where he finds out about the show and the details of its programme.
<|det|>page_number [799, 927, 846, 946]<|/det|>133
