<PAGE><|det|>header [90, 24, 168, 65]<|/det|>17
<|det|>header [647, 20, 959, 58]<|/det|>Interjections
<|det|>image [99, 136, 160, 183]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 147, 428, 168]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [99, 186, 444, 328]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 186, 421, 209]<|/det|>• Ah! I damaged the vase.
<|det|>text [100, 211, 436, 231]<|/det|>• Hello! What's the matter?
<|det|>text [100, 234, 327, 256]<|/det|>• Hush! Be quiet.
<|det|>text [100, 258, 532, 279]<|/det|>• Hurrah! We won the tournament.
<|det|>text [100, 281, 456, 304]<|/det|>• Oh! How did that happen?
<|det|>text [100, 305, 603, 328]<|/det|>- Alas! There is little hope of her winning.
<|det|>image [695, 135, 957, 337]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [99, 343, 160, 395]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 343, 890, 368]<|/det|>The highlighted words—Ah!, Hello!, Hush!, Hurrah!, Oh! and
<|det|>text [174, 369, 836, 391]<|/det|>Alas!—express sudden feelings or emotions. They have no
<|det|>text [99, 392, 911, 439]<|/det|>grammatical relation with any of the other words of the sentence. Such words are called interjections.
<|det|>text [98, 467, 948, 515]<|/det|>An interjection is a word or a group of words that expresses some sudden feeling or emotion of the speaker.
<|det|>text [98, 548, 801, 595]<|/det|>The interjection is placed before a sentence begins. A mark of exclamation (!) follows an interjection.
<|det|>text [98, 596, 813, 617]<|/det|>Some groups of words or phrases that act as interjections are—
<|det|>text [98, 618, 935, 662]<|/det|>Dear me!, Hold on!, Well done!, Good grief!, Good gracious! and Good heavens!.
<|det|>text [98, 679, 926, 725]<|/det|>Study this list to learn which interjections can be used to express various emotions.
<|det|>list [99, 741, 783, 902]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 741, 375, 763]<|/det|>• Joy: Hurrah! Ha ha!
<|det|>text [100, 764, 604, 786]<|/det|>• Surprise: Ha! What! Oh! Good heavens!
<|det|>text [100, 787, 783, 809]<|/det|>- Drawing attention: Listen! Behold! Look! Hello! Ho! Hi!
<|det|>text [100, 811, 565, 833]<|/det|>• Making/becoming silent: Hush! Shh!
<|det|>text [100, 835, 517, 856]<|/det|>• Appreciation: Bravo! Well done!
<|det|>text [100, 858, 520, 880]<|/det|>• Contempt: Pooh! What a shame!
<|det|>text [100, 882, 402, 902]<|/det|>• Sorrow: Alas! Ah! Oh!
<|det|>page_number [127, 925, 172, 944]<|/det|>134
<PAGE><|det|>text [115, 13, 695, 37]<|/det|>Indicate the emotions expressed in these sentences.
<|det|>list [128, 46, 836, 305]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 46, 657, 69]<|/det|>1. Well done! That was really commendable.
<|det|>text [130, 71, 523, 93]<|/det|>2. Oh! What a horrible accident.
<|det|>text [130, 95, 432, 115]<|/det|>3. What! It can't be true.
<|det|>text [130, 118, 523, 139]<|/det|>4. Hush! Tabla rehearsals are on.
<|det|>text [130, 141, 525, 163]<|/det|>5. Ah! What a stupid thing to do.
<|det|>text [130, 165, 550, 187]<|/det|>6. Alas! What a loss to our country.
<|det|>text [130, 189, 536, 209]<|/det|>7. Well done! You deserve a treat.
<|det|>text [130, 211, 692, 234]<|/det|>8. Bravo! That was a brilliant effort on your part.
<|det|>text [130, 235, 677, 257]<|/det|>9. Dear me! What have you done to your hair?
<|det|>text [117, 259, 830, 303]<|/det|>10. What a shame! You did not have the courage to speak the truth.
<|det|>image [658, 70, 860, 223]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 329, 843, 378]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the suitable interjections selected from the words in the brackets.
<|det|>list [128, 396, 836, 905]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 396, 742, 420]<|/det|>1. ____! We won the tournament again.
<|det|>text [192, 427, 348, 448]<|/det|>(Hurrah/Alas)
<|det|>text [128, 456, 792, 509]<|/det|>2. ____! There is a tiger behind the clump of bamboos. (Look/Oh)
<|det|>text [128, 516, 836, 569]<|/det|>3. ____! Are you calling from the police station? (Oh/Hello)
<|det|>text [128, 577, 830, 601]<|/det|>4. ____! She won the first prize. (Hurrah/Bravo)
<|det|>text [129, 608, 790, 661]<|/det|>5. ____! You risked your life to save the lady.
(Oh/Bravo)
<|det|>text [128, 668, 788, 722]<|/det|>6. ____! The kitten is trying to climb the huge banyan tree. (Hurrah/Look)
<|det|>text [128, 728, 750, 782]<|/det|>7. ____! They're writing an exam in that classroom. (Oh/Hush)
<|det|>text [128, 789, 812, 814]<|/det|>8. ____! What a beautiful painting. (Oh/Alas)
<|det|>text [128, 820, 824, 844]<|/det|>9. ____! That's not a very good sign. (Bravo/Ah)
<|det|>text [117, 850, 805, 905]<|/det|>10. ____! This wound will take a while to heal.
(Dear me/Hold on)
<|det|>page_number [805, 927, 850, 948]<|/det|>135
<PAGE><|det|>header [117, 38, 300, 138]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [592, 38, 940, 80]<|/det|>Report Writing
<|det|>image [120, 164, 182, 208]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [119, 189, 940, 261]<|/det|>In this chapter, we are going to discuss how to write a newspaper report covering an event. A report is a brief, factual piece of writing which has no scope for expressing personal opinions or emotions.
<|det|>text [120, 275, 592, 300]<|/det|>Look at this sample of a newspaper report.
<|det|>title [375, 341, 680, 369]<|/det|>Pulse Polio Campaign
<|det|>text [168, 382, 421, 429]<|/det|>by Sudeep Das
Kolkata, 14 May, 2013
<|det|>text [168, 443, 512, 653]<|/det|>A pulse polio campaign was organised in a village of Burdwan District, called Govardhanpur, in West Bengal. It was organised by the World Health Organisation (WHO), a wing of the United Nations. There were roughly four hundred children present at the camp.
<|det|>text [168, 666, 512, 807]<|/det|>A number of tents had been set up in the camp. In each tent, there was a team of two doctors, and a nurse aided by two people who were handling the supplies. The
<|det|>text [542, 382, 898, 550]<|/det|>helpers were volunteers from among the villagers. There were only two hundred and fifty polio units in stock, however, and the additional units were brought in by a helicopter in the evening.
<|det|>text [542, 563, 902, 807]<|/det|>The villagers have appreciated this campaign and are very happy to see the genuine concern of WHO. One of them, Mr Ashish Banerjee, commented, 'This campaign is a boon for poor villagers like us. Polio is a crippling disease and our children are lucky that they are being immunised against it.'
<|det|>page_number [143, 930, 189, 950]<|/det|>136
<PAGE><|det|>header [66, 28, 120, 74]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [132, 33, 851, 61]<|/det|>Look at these guidelines to be kept in mind for writing a report.
<|det|>list [60, 75, 882, 435]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [60, 75, 503, 100]<|/det|>• A newspaper report must be factual.
<|det|>text [60, 100, 797, 123]<|/det|>- It should be short and precise (not more than 100–150 words).
<|det|>text [60, 123, 810, 148]<|/det|>- All the relevant points should be narrated in a proper sequence.
<|det|>text [60, 148, 510, 171]<|/det|>• Nothing should be left to guesswork.
<|det|>text [60, 171, 651, 195]<|/det|>• An account by an eyewitness should be included.
<|det|>text [60, 195, 882, 239]<|/det|>- Start a report directly with the topic. It does not provide any scope for an elaborate introduction.
<|det|>text [60, 241, 380, 265]<|/det|>• Write in indirect speech.
<|det|>text [60, 266, 354, 289]<|/det|>• Use the passive voice.
<|det|>text [60, 289, 879, 336]<|/det|>- Eliminate the use of the personal pronoun, 'I'. If necessary, the phrase 'the reporter' should be used.
<|det|>text [60, 337, 839, 362]<|/det|>• No personal opinion should be given unless specifically asked for.
<|det|>text [60, 362, 797, 409]<|/det|>- The language used in a report should be matter of fact and not ornamented with similes or metaphors.
<|det|>text [60, 409, 670, 435]<|/det|>• Always keep in mind the reader you are writing for.
<|det|>title [60, 457, 281, 480]<|/det|>Format of a report
<|det|>list [60, 481, 884, 861]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [60, 481, 862, 553]<|/det|>- A short heading in the centre. The heading should not be so creative or far fetched that it has no connection with the report. It is best to convert the question into the heading.
<|det|>text [60, 553, 812, 599]<|/det|>- The heading may be followed by the name of the person who is writing the report.
<|det|>text [60, 599, 882, 648]<|/det|>- The report must begin with the name of the place where the event has occurred followed by the date of reporting.
<|det|>text [60, 648, 427, 669]<|/det|>• Word limit: 100–150 words.
<|det|>text [60, 671, 885, 767]<|/det|>- A report is a short essay and is normally marked out of 20. The mark division is 15 for the content and 5 for the format. The format includes the heading, designation of the reporter, name of place, date and the use of paragraphs.
<|det|>text [60, 767, 887, 861]<|/det|>- In a report writing question, some guidelines will always be given. It is imperative to touch upon all the points and to maintain the sequence of events. Any digression from these rules may result in loss of marks for the format.
<|det|>page_number [828, 937, 868, 956]<|/det|>137
<PAGE><|det|>text [180, 24, 936, 75]<|/det|>Now, write newspaper reports of about 150 words for each of the following topics or situations. You can use the guidelines provided.
<|det|>text [183, 83, 936, 228]<|/det|>1. Topic: The devastating cyclone
Guidelines: name of the cyclone...date...time...areas affected...people marooned...eyewitness account...
immediate and subsequent rescue work...evacuation...
chances of widespread disease and sickness...response of the whole world...sympathy and help for the suffering
<|det|>text [180, 228, 911, 345]<|/det|>2. Topic: The inter-school soccer match held in your school Guidelines: date...time...venue...schools participating...objective...a festive mood...rules of the game...a brief description...winning team...prize distribution...comments from a spectator
<|det|>text [182, 346, 925, 532]<|/det|>3. Situation: A famous actor has come to your school to conduct auditions for his forthcoming production. On popular demand he has even addressed the students of the school-leaving batch Guidelines: date...venue...number of days that he visited...number of students who turned up for the auditions...arrangements for the same...brief description of the auditions...results...a brief on the actor's address... impact on students
<|det|>text [182, 533, 905, 604]<|/det|>4. Situation: As the head boy/girl, prepare a report on the pet show recently organised by the nursery department of your school.
<|det|>text [248, 605, 845, 699]<|/det|>Guidelines: date...venue...time...aim and objective... number of pets...breed, species...classes that visited... schools invited...brief description of the programme... security arrangements...impact on students
<|det|>text [182, 700, 938, 871]<|/det|>5. Situation: As an eyewitness, prepare a report on the one-act theatre festival held among the schools in your city, which was organised by the British Council, in collaboration with the Central School of Speech and Drama, London. Guidelines: date...venue...time...organisers...schools participating... sponsors...rules...judges...brief description of the programme ...results...audience response
<|det|>page_number [127, 940, 175, 960]<|/det|>138
<PAGE><|det|>title [133, 26, 842, 53]<|/det|>Write reports of about 150 words on these topics or situations.
<|det|>text [139, 65, 867, 154]<|/det|>1. You are Akshit, secretary of the Science Club in your school. There was a science quiz held in your school recently and many other schools participated. Write are report on the event.
<|det|>text [138, 160, 868, 228]<|/det|>2. You are a newspaper reporter and have just witnessed a hit-and-run road accident. Take the account of the eyewitnesses and the people involved and write a report on the accident.
<|det|>text [140, 230, 842, 301]<|/det|>3. There has been a sensational kidnapping of the CEO of an MNC. Find out details about it from the family, the police and the eyewitnesses, and write a report.
<|det|>text [140, 302, 872, 350]<|/det|>4. There has been a Diwali celebration in your society recently. Write a report on it for your society magazine.
<|det|>text [142, 351, 830, 418]<|/det|>5. You are Ragini/Rahul, a reporter with a newspaper. Write a report on the increasing crime rate in your city and the probable causes for it.
<|det|>text [143, 420, 833, 492]<|/det|>6. You are Pratibha, a reporter with The Indian Times. Write a report on the polio camps held in the city and their importance in India's progress as a healthy nation.
<|det|>page_number [824, 933, 868, 950]<|/det|>139
<PAGE><|det|>image [154, 0, 307, 136]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [640, 40, 936, 72]<|/det|>Punctuation
<|det|>image [117, 152, 177, 196]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [182, 160, 933, 185]<|/det|>Read the sentences. Notice the punctuation marks at the end of each.
<|det|>list [114, 203, 432, 272]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [114, 203, 429, 225]<|/det|>• We are going to the zoo.
<|det|>text [114, 226, 432, 249]<|/det|>• We are going to the zoo!
<|det|>text [114, 250, 432, 272]<|/det|>• We are going to the zoo?
<|det|>image [111, 291, 174, 338]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [182, 289, 905, 335]<|/det|>The punctuation marks in the three sentences above suggest three different ideas.
<|det|>text [108, 356, 902, 472]<|/det|>You must have noticed that when we speak, there are times when our voice is raised and sometimes it is lowered. Then, we pause and our pauses are not of the same duration. Some pauses are short, while some are long. Marks of punctuation in writing carry out the same function as pauses and stresses in speech.
<|det|>text [110, 483, 643, 506]<|/det|>The most commonly used punctuation marks are:
<|det|>list [111, 508, 610, 624]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 508, 277, 529]<|/det|>- full stop (.)
<|det|>text [111, 531, 270, 553]<|/det|>- comma (.)
<|det|>text [111, 555, 420, 576]<|/det|>• mark of interrogation (?)
<|det|>text [111, 578, 411, 599]<|/det|>• mark of exclamation (!)
<|det|>text [111, 600, 609, 623]<|/det|>• quotation marks or inverted commas (“...)
<|det|>title [110, 636, 225, 660]<|/det|>Full Stop
<|det|>text [109, 665, 811, 689]<|/det|>The full stop, or period, represents the longest pause. It is used—
<|det|>list [109, 691, 548, 761]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [109, 691, 548, 737]<|/det|>◆ to mark the end of an assertive or an imperative sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [150, 738, 438, 761]<|/det|>• I will go to the market.
<|det|>title [109, 774, 212, 794]<|/det|>Comma
<|det|>text [108, 806, 375, 827]<|/det|>The comma is used to—
<|det|>text [108, 829, 569, 877]<|/det|>- set off a noun of address. For example,
  - Rahul, come here.
<|det|>image [660, 703, 928, 880]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [129, 937, 171, 956]<|/det|>140
<PAGE><|det|>text [85, 16, 586, 63]<|/det|>- separate each pair of words of the same class or kind. For example,
<|det|>list [89, 64, 580, 136]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [90, 64, 580, 110]<|/det|>- Rich and poor, young and old, men and women, took part in the celebrations.
<|det|>text [46, 121, 589, 168]<|/det|>- separate parts of a date from the other words following the date. For example,
<|det|>text [90, 169, 562, 192]<|/det|>• On 12 May 2009, we left for Mumbai.
<|det|>text [46, 204, 535, 230]<|/det|>- separate words in a series. For example,
<|det|>text [89, 230, 697, 254]<|/det|>• We bought some pencils, pens, erasers and books.
<|det|>text [46, 265, 699, 290]<|/det|>- separate a statement from a question tag. For example,
<|det|>text [90, 290, 475, 313]<|/det|>• You didn't meet him, did you?
<|det|>text [46, 324, 790, 371]<|/det|>- show a brief pause that is needed to give clarity to our writing. For example,
<|det|>text [90, 373, 645, 398]<|/det|>• It is, as far as I have understood, a novel plan.
<|det|>text [46, 407, 822, 455]<|/det|>- separate expressions like yes, no, oh and well from the rest of the sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [91, 456, 565, 479]<|/det|>• Yes, you will be in charge of the team.
<|det|>text [92, 479, 369, 501]<|/det|>• Well, I think so too.
<|det|>text [46, 515, 565, 539]<|/det|>- separate parts of an address. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 539, 412, 563]<|/det|>• 4, Chowringhee Square
<|det|>text [46, 574, 779, 621]<|/det|>- separate the words spoken by the speaker from the rest of the sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 622, 477, 645]<|/det|>• She said, 'I want to go home.'
<|det|>text [46, 657, 782, 706]<|/det|>- separate words, phrases or clauses inserted into the body of a sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 706, 558, 730]<|/det|>• Health is, after all, of prime concern.
<|det|>title [46, 748, 258, 772]<|/det|>Capital Letters
<|det|>text [45, 777, 834, 826]<|/det|>You have also learnt about the importance of capital letters in writing. There are rules for using capital letters as well.
<|det|>title [46, 841, 352, 865]<|/det|>A capital letter is used—
<|det|>text [48, 867, 485, 891]<|/det|>♦ to begin a sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [102, 892, 387, 916]<|/det|>• He is a good person.
<|det|>page_number [824, 943, 868, 960]<|/det|>141
<PAGE><|det|>text [114, 38, 667, 63]<|/det|>◆ to begin each fresh line of poetry. For example,
<|det|>text [158, 61, 777, 156]<|/det|>- Where the voice of the wind calls our wandering feet, Through echoing forest and echoing street, With lutes in our hands ever-singing we roam, All men are our kindred, the world is our home.
<|det|>text [114, 165, 901, 216]<|/det|>- for all proper nouns and adjectives formed from them. For example, May, Friday, Delhi, Japan, British, French and Wendy.
<|det|>text [114, 226, 742, 252]<|/det|>◆ to write the pronoun I and the interjections O and Oh.
<|det|>text [114, 268, 901, 293]<|/det|>A. Insert commas, full stops and capital letters wherever necessary.
<|det|>list [194, 304, 922, 795]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [195, 304, 717, 328]<|/det|>1. madam I shall be happy to be of assistance
<|det|>text [195, 329, 807, 351]<|/det|>2. i need an intelligent sincere and committed scholar
<|det|>text [195, 352, 922, 400]<|/det|>3. the statue of liberty is a world famous monument it was gifted to the united states of america by the people of france
<|det|>text [195, 401, 864, 444]<|/det|>4. the captain when he was dying expressed his wish to be reborn as an indian
<|det|>text [195, 446, 836, 490]<|/det|>5. the violin the cello the trumpet and the clarinet are all musical instruments
<|det|>text [195, 493, 848, 539]<|/det|>6. one day we were walking towards the market when we spotted miss jones taking her dog for a walk
<|det|>text [195, 540, 744, 562]<|/det|>7. i was born in kolkata but i grew up in chennai
<|det|>text [195, 563, 874, 609]<|/det|>8. the germans the french the italians and the dutch do have something in common
<|det|>text [194, 610, 914, 656]<|/det|>9. large crowds gathered outside buckingham palace hoping to catch a glimpse of queen elizabeth
<|det|>text [185, 657, 876, 702]<|/det|>10. mother I want to talk to you for a minute I need to discuss the salient features of the indian constitution with you
<|det|>text [185, 703, 860, 749]<|/det|>11. when the sun rose when the mist cleared when the birds began to sing I came out of my tent
<|det|>text [184, 750, 920, 793]<|/det|>12. there is an interesting chapter in our book about the habits of certain tribes in south africa
<|det|>title [106, 804, 383, 827]<|/det|>Mark of Interrogation
<|det|>text [105, 832, 880, 878]<|/det|>The mark of interrogation or the question mark is used at the end of an interrogative sentence. For example,
<|det|>list [107, 879, 548, 925]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [107, 879, 548, 901]<|/det|>- Would you like to have some coffee?
<|det|>text [107, 902, 383, 924]<|/det|>• Can I visit you today?
<|det|>page_number [130, 941, 175, 960]<|/det|>142
<PAGE><|det|>title [70, 26, 340, 51]<|/det|>Mark of Exclamation
<|det|>text [68, 56, 878, 103]<|/det|>The mark of exclamation is placed at the end of an exclamatory sentence or after an interjection. For example,
<|det|>list [70, 103, 484, 147]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [70, 103, 275, 123]<|/det|>• What a relief!
<|det|>text [70, 125, 484, 147]<|/det|>• Hurrah! We have won the match.
<|det|>title [65, 165, 882, 191]<|/det|>5. Punctuate the following and insert capital letters wherever required.
<|det|>list [169, 200, 581, 576]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [169, 200, 403, 223]<|/det|>1. oh I am so sorry
<|det|>text [169, 224, 510, 247]<|/det|>2. alas our great king is dead
<|det|>text [170, 248, 570, 271]<|/det|>3. do you have a copy of the bible
<|det|>text [170, 272, 580, 293]<|/det|>4. what bad luck it had to rain now
<|det|>text [170, 295, 487, 341]<|/det|>5. bravo you have done an excellent job
<|det|>text [170, 342, 480, 389]<|/det|>6. why must you leave for chandigarh right away
<|det|>text [170, 389, 657, 413]<|/det|>7. do you think she of all people will agree
<|det|>text [170, 413, 667, 436]<|/det|>8. if you are willing we will accept the deal
<|det|>text [170, 436, 797, 459]<|/det|>9. hurrah we have won the first prize in the tournament
<|det|>text [160, 460, 835, 482]<|/det|>10. will it not hurt your parents if you behave in this manner
<|det|>text [160, 483, 838, 526]<|/det|>11. o dear you are bleeding profusely I think we need to call dr sen
<|det|>text [160, 529, 874, 573]<|/det|>12. would it be possible for you to lend me your geography text book
<|det|>title [70, 584, 550, 608]<|/det|>Inverted Commas or Quotation Marks
<|det|>title [70, 613, 397, 635]<|/det|>Quotation marks or inverted
<|det|>text [70, 637, 470, 684]<|/det|>commas are used to mark the exact words of a speaker. For example,
<|det|>list [74, 684, 457, 800]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [74, 684, 411, 706]<|/det|>• The lady said, 'I am tired.'
<|det|>text [74, 707, 457, 800]<|/det|>- Warren Buffet said, 'Someone's sitting in the shade today because someone planted a tree a long time ago.'
<|det|>image [508, 642, 551, 675]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [512, 650, 569, 673]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [668, 642, 712, 675]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [508, 688, 874, 771]<|/det|>Inverted commas are also sometimes used when we are mentioning the name of a book or an article in a sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [509, 772, 860, 790]<|/det|>I read 'Alice in Wonderland' as a child.
<|det|>text [157, 815, 868, 863]<|/det|>Insert full stops, commas, inverted commas, interrogation marks, exclamation marks and use capital letters where required.
<|det|>text [157, 869, 791, 892]<|/det|>1. why did you purchase so many mangoes I asked him
<|det|>text [157, 893, 825, 938]<|/det|>2. who is the man at the door asked rama leaving his chair putting on his coat he mowed towards the door
<|det|>list [157, 869, 825, 938]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [824, 927, 865, 946]<|/det|>143
<PAGE><|det|>text [205, 24, 836, 79]<|/det|>3. the sun has set the sky has cleared and a soft wind is blowing said the hermit
<|det|>text [205, 80, 643, 103]<|/det|>4. he said to me please take your seat
<|det|>text [205, 103, 926, 150]<|/det|>5. my father said you better be careful about what you say you are too short-tempered
<|det|>text [205, 150, 930, 198]<|/det|>6. the teacher said there is an interesting piece in our text book titled hardy and ramanujan by c p snow
<|det|>text [205, 198, 790, 221]<|/det|>7. she said I am feeling ill I will not go school today
<|det|>text [205, 221, 879, 246]<|/det|>8. well this is indeed a pleasant surprise she said cheerfully
<|det|>text [205, 246, 847, 271]<|/det|>9. he answered oh yes he was a great english playwright
<|det|>text [196, 271, 702, 295]<|/det|>10. mother said william do stop complaining
<|det|>list [196, 24, 930, 295]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [100, 313, 936, 338]<|/det|>D. Punctuate these passages and use capital letters wherever required.
<|det|>text [196, 345, 601, 369]<|/det|>1. my god he said as I drew him in
<|det|>text [260, 369, 550, 393]<|/det|>what has happened I asked
<|det|>text [260, 393, 575, 416]<|/det|>what hasn't they wiped us out
<|det|>text [260, 416, 506, 438]<|/det|>drink some water I said
<|det|>text [260, 438, 884, 462]<|/det|>he drank it he then sat down before the table put his head
<|det|>text [260, 462, 904, 486]<|/det|>on his arms and began to sob like a little boy while I with a
<|det|>text [260, 486, 907, 510]<|/det|>curious forgetfulness of my own recent despair stood beside
<|det|>text [260, 510, 428, 533]<|/det|>him wondering
<|det|>text [196, 533, 628, 556]<|/det|>2. look said lucky suddenly what's that
<|det|>text [260, 556, 926, 579]<|/det|>a stream shouted the others and tired as they were they raced
<|det|>text [260, 579, 446, 599]<|/det|>to the fresh water
<|det|>text [260, 600, 911, 624]<|/det|>now said edmund when they had drunk their fill what about
<|det|>text [260, 624, 450, 644]<|/det|>those sandwiches
<|det|>text [260, 646, 913, 670]<|/det|>oh hadn't we better save them said susan we may need them
<|det|>text [260, 670, 446, 690]<|/det|>far worse later on
<|det|>text [260, 691, 884, 716]<|/det|>i do wish said lucy now that we're not thirsty we could get
<|det|>text [260, 716, 455, 737]<|/det|>some food as well
<|det|>text [260, 738, 881, 763]<|/det|>well then said peter let's have those sandwiches what's the
<|det|>text [260, 763, 620, 787]<|/det|>use of saving them till they go bad
<|det|>image [425, 792, 610, 937]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [138, 930, 183, 948]<|/det|>144
<PAGE><|det|>header [62, 17, 645, 63]<|/det|>direct and Indirect Speech
<|det|>header [793, 14, 961, 104]<|/det|>19
<|det|>image [58, 133, 118, 183]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 141, 441, 163]<|/det|>Read these sets of sentences.
<|det|>text [98, 173, 430, 198]<|/det|>She said, 'I am going to Paris.'
<|det|>text [98, 198, 532, 223]<|/det|>She told me that she was going to Paris.
<|det|>image [60, 240, 118, 290]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 237, 869, 261]<|/det|>In the first sentence, the exact words of the speaker are reproduced.
<|det|>text [130, 261, 664, 285]<|/det|>The entire speech is put within inverted commas.
<|det|>text [57, 299, 860, 349]<|/det|>When a sentence reports the exact words of the speaker, it is said to be in direct speech.
<|det|>text [57, 366, 831, 415]<|/det|>In the second sentence, the words of the speaker are reproduced in the past tense and are not within inverted commas.
<|det|>text [57, 428, 842, 475]<|/det|>In indirect speech, we report what the speaker has said without quoting his or her exact words.
<|det|>text [53, 493, 868, 613]<|/det|>In a sentence containing direct speech, there is a verb of speaking. In the above example, the verb is said. The verb said is called the reporting verb. Usually, a comma or a colon separates the words of the speaker from the reporting verb. The first word in the quoted section will always begin with a capital letter.
<|det|>title [80, 633, 145, 660]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [68, 674, 617, 696]<|/det|>Two of the most commonly used reporting verbs are said and told.
<|det|>text [68, 696, 620, 717]<|/det|>When we need to mention to whom something is said, we use told.
<|det|>text [68, 717, 838, 759]<|/det|>When we either do not know or do not want to mention to whom something has been said, we use said. For example,
<|det|>text [68, 761, 354, 782]<|/det|>Rahul said, 'I am very tired.'
<|det|>text [114, 783, 400, 804]<|/det|>Rahul said that he was very tired.
<|det|>text [114, 805, 401, 826]<|/det|>Rahul said to me, 'I am very tired.'
<|det|>text [114, 827, 424, 848]<|/det|>Rahul told me that he was very tired.
<|det|>text [68, 862, 816, 906]<|/det|>Some other words that can be used in place of said are add, complain, promise, remind, remark and point out.
<|det|>page_number [808, 945, 851, 965]<|/det|>145
<PAGE><|det|>text [115, 35, 735, 61]<|/det|>A sentence in direct speech can thus be divided into two—
<|det|>text [116, 60, 802, 86]<|/det|>● the main part of the sentence that contains the reporting verb
<|det|>text [116, 86, 780, 109]<|/det|>● the quoted section which is the object of the reporting verb
<|det|>list [116, 60, 802, 109]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [115, 121, 926, 171]<|/det|>When we are asked to change direct speech into indirect speech, we have to make certain changes.
<|det|>text [117, 186, 646, 209]<|/det|>Now, the changes will depend on various things—
<|det|>text [117, 209, 905, 257]<|/det|>- The manner in which the speaker utters the words—is he merely making a statement or is he giving an order or is he perhaps making a request?
<|det|>text [117, 258, 506, 281]<|/det|>Is the speaker asking a question?
<|det|>text [117, 281, 625, 304]<|/det|>Is the speaker happy, sad, excited or furious?
<|det|>text [117, 304, 914, 374]<|/det|>- We will also notice that when we are changing from direct to indirect speech, the main change takes place in that part of the sentence that contains what has been said.
<|det|>list [117, 209, 914, 374]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [116, 390, 895, 437]<|/det|>Now, take a look at the following sentences in direct speech. Also notice the changes made while transforming them into indirect speech.
<|det|>text [118, 438, 660, 460]<|/det|>• The teacher said to the naughty child, 'Be quiet.'
<|det|>text [150, 461, 590, 482]<|/det|>The teacher ordered the child to be quiet.
<|det|>list [118, 438, 660, 482]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [115, 483, 918, 530]<|/det|>The reporting verb ordered has replaced said. We have taken into account what the speaker is saying and how he is saying it.
<|det|>text [118, 530, 790, 552]<|/det|>- Our coach said to us, 'Drink fluids during practice sessions.'
<|det|>text [150, 553, 795, 575]<|/det|>Our coach advised us to drink fluids during practice sessions.
<|det|>text [116, 576, 888, 600]<|/det|>The word advised is appropriate because that is what the coach is doing.
<|det|>text [118, 600, 795, 666]<|/det|>- My mother said, 'My car has been giving me a lot of trouble.' My mother complained that her car had been giving her a lot of trouble.
<|det|>text [116, 669, 687, 692]<|/det|>The word complained reflects the mood of the speaker.
<|det|>text [114, 706, 914, 776]<|/det|>So, when we change from direct speech to indirect speech, we do need to take into account not only what is being said but how it is being said. The mood and sentiment of the speaker will play a crucial role.
<|det|>text [114, 790, 864, 837]<|/det|>Here are some rules that should be followed while changing sentences from direct into indirect, or reported speech.
<|det|>text [114, 838, 914, 884]<|/det|>- If the reporting verb is in the present tense or the future tense, the tense of the verb in the reported speech remains unchanged. For example,
<|det|>text [150, 885, 590, 930]<|/det|>- The lady says, 'I am a good cook.'
The lady says that she is a good cook.
<|det|>page_number [135, 937, 179, 956]<|/det|>146
<PAGE><|det|>text [107, 14, 851, 64]<|/det|>- The trainer will say, 'The children are ready for the tournament.'
The trainer will say that the children are ready for the tournament.
<|det|>text [70, 77, 854, 129]<|/det|>study this table to understand how tenses change when we convert direct speech into indirect speech.
<|det|>title [182, 140, 335, 163]<|/det|>Direct speech
<|det|>title [556, 141, 732, 165]<|/det|>Indirect speech
<|det|>title [82, 175, 252, 198]<|/det|>simple present
<|det|>text [80, 198, 425, 221]<|/det|>The man said, 'I like flying kites.'
<|det|>title [80, 231, 297, 253]<|/det|>present continuous
<|det|>text [80, 254, 401, 301]<|/det|>Rita said, 'My aunt is cooking dinner.'
<|det|>title [80, 311, 255, 334]<|/det|>present perfect
<|det|>text [80, 334, 424, 356]<|/det|>Rana said, 'I have taken a bath.'
<|det|>title [80, 368, 212, 390]<|/det|>simple past
<|det|>text [79, 391, 404, 439]<|/det|>He said, 'I bought a watch for my mother.'
<|det|>title [80, 449, 218, 471]<|/det|>past perfect
<|det|>text [79, 471, 395, 517]<|/det|>Ronny said, 'Rahul had done this before.'
<|det|>title [536, 175, 732, 200]<|/det|>Indirect speech
<|det|>text [448, 175, 571, 198]<|/det|>simple past
<|det|>text [448, 199, 848, 223]<|/det|>The man said that he liked flying kites.
<|det|>text [448, 233, 619, 254]<|/det|>past continuous
<|det|>text [448, 256, 821, 300]<|/det|>Rita said that her aunt was cooking dinner.
<|det|>text [448, 313, 576, 335]<|/det|>past perfect
<|det|>text [448, 336, 820, 358]<|/det|>Rana said that he had taken a bath.
<|det|>title [448, 370, 574, 391]<|/det|>past perfect
<|det|>text [448, 392, 824, 437]<|/det|>He said that he had bought a watch for his mother.
<|det|>title [448, 450, 574, 471]<|/det|>past perfect
<|det|>text [448, 472, 834, 517]<|/det|>Ronny said that Rahul had done that before.
<|det|>text [65, 540, 547, 563]<|/det|>Here are some exceptions to the above rules.
<|det|>text [67, 564, 432, 660]<|/det|>If the reported speech expresses a universal truth or an established/habitual fact, its tenses remain unchanged.
<|det|>list [98, 660, 430, 753]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 660, 430, 704]<|/det|>• The teacher told the class, 'The sun is a star.'
<|det|>text [144, 707, 427, 753]<|/det|>The teacher told the class that the sun is a star.
<|det|>title [508, 584, 564, 610]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [628, 580, 680, 616]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [497, 623, 834, 644]<|/det|>Modal verb forms also change sometimes.
<|det|>list [498, 645, 825, 728]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [498, 645, 714, 663]<|/det|>- must becomes had to
<|det|>text [498, 666, 736, 685]<|/det|>- will/shall become would
<|det|>text [498, 687, 821, 706]<|/det|>- shall sometimes changes to should
<|det|>text [498, 708, 694, 726]<|/det|>- can becomes could
<|det|>text [60, 765, 797, 815]<|/det|>- If the reported speech describes a state of affairs which still exists when the speech is reported, its tenses remain unchanged.
<|det|>text [98, 816, 736, 864]<|/det|>- The President said, 'The people of this country are very intelligent.'
<|det|>text [140, 864, 763, 914]<|/det|>The President said that the people of this country are very intelligent.
<|det|>page_number [799, 932, 840, 950]<|/det|>147
<PAGE><|det|>text [112, 25, 827, 81]<|/det|>If the reported speech contains a time clause, its tense remains unchanged.
<|det|>text [155, 78, 825, 126]<|/det|>She said, 'When I was an actor, I lived in Mumbai.'
She said that when she was an actor, she lived in Mumbai.
<|det|>text [112, 135, 926, 185]<|/det|>- Sometimes the gender of the person speaking remains unclear. In that case, we usually use the masculine gender.
<|det|>text [155, 184, 825, 232]<|/det|>- My teacher said to me, 'I will speak to you on Monday.' My teacher told me that he would speak to me on Monday.
<|det|>text [112, 250, 670, 273]<|/det|>A. Change these sentences into indirect speech.
<|det|>text [194, 279, 874, 303]<|/det|>1. She said to Rita, 'When I was in Delhi, I often met Mini.'
<|det|>text [195, 306, 637, 329]<|/det|>2. I said, 'Children are very energetic.'
<|det|>text [195, 328, 854, 373]<|/det|>3. The pilot said to the passengers, 'The plane will land in Kolkata.'
<|det|>text [195, 374, 923, 420]<|/det|>4. The speaker said, 'Young students need to devote some time to social work.'
<|det|>text [196, 422, 668, 446]<|/det|>5. The lawyer said, 'It is a hopeless case.'
<|det|>text [196, 446, 868, 469]<|/det|>6. The teacher announced, 'There will be no classes today.'
<|det|>text [197, 470, 684, 492]<|/det|>7. Sarah said, 'Gita had worked efficiently.'
<|det|>text [196, 492, 921, 538]<|/det|>8. The director said to the students, 'You are very dedicated, so you will do well in life.'
<|det|>text [196, 539, 824, 562]<|/det|>9. Soma said to me, 'I was very ill, but I am better now.'
<|det|>text [190, 563, 726, 585]<|/det|>10. The passenger said, 'I must buy some water.'
<|det|>text [190, 585, 882, 608]<|/det|>11. He said, 'My wife wants to study law and join a law firm.'
<|det|>text [190, 608, 884, 631]<|/det|>12. The police officer said, 'The accident took place at dawn.'
<|det|>text [115, 645, 728, 669]<|/det|>Statements: From Direct Speech to Indirect Speech
<|det|>text [114, 674, 856, 720]<|/det|>Pronouns and possessive adjectives in the direct speech are changed, wherever necessary. For example,
<|det|>text [114, 720, 870, 767]<|/det|>- Pronouns and possessive adjectives of the first person are changed according to the subject of the reporting verb.
<|det|>text [160, 767, 650, 789]<|/det|>• He said, 'I want to give my sister a present.'
<|det|>text [160, 790, 723, 813]<|/det|>• He said that he wanted to give his sister a present.
<|det|>text [114, 823, 921, 871]<|/det|>- Pronouns and possessive adjectives of the second person are changed according to the object of the reporting verb. For example,
<|det|>text [160, 871, 714, 893]<|/det|>• Mahi said to me, 'You have not done your work.'
<|det|>text [160, 893, 657, 916]<|/det|>• Mahi told me that I had not done my work.
<|det|>page_number [138, 930, 180, 947]<|/det|>148
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 46, 790, 93]<|/det|>- Pronouns and possessive adjectives in the third person remain unchanged. For example,
<|det|>list [108, 94, 705, 142]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 94, 667, 116]<|/det|>• She said, 'He wants to give his sister a present.'
<|det|>text [109, 117, 705, 141]<|/det|>• She said that he wanted to give his sister a present.
<|det|>text [65, 148, 841, 196]<|/det|>- If the person addressed reports the speech himself/herself, then the second person is changed into first person. For example,
<|det|>list [109, 197, 520, 245]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 197, 528, 219]<|/det|>• She said to me, 'You appear tired.'
<|det|>text [110, 220, 519, 244]<|/det|>• She told me that I appeared tired.
<|det|>text [65, 253, 806, 300]<|/det|>The nominative of address in direct speech becomes the person spoken to in indirect speech. For example,
<|det|>list [110, 301, 925, 348]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 301, 546, 323]<|/det|>• She said, 'Sam, I am going to sleep.'
<|det|>text [110, 324, 604, 347]<|/det|>• She told Sam that she was going to sleep.
<|det|>text [65, 356, 868, 401]<|/det|>- Certain words expressing nearness are changed into words expressing distance.
<|det|>list [111, 404, 320, 667]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 404, 297, 424]<|/det|>- now → then
<|det|>text [111, 427, 285, 446]<|/det|>- this → that
<|det|>text [111, 449, 320, 469]<|/det|>• these → those
<|det|>text [111, 472, 310, 491]<|/det|>- here → there
<|det|>text [111, 495, 275, 514]<|/det|>- thus → so
<|det|>text [111, 517, 316, 539]<|/det|>- ago → before
<|det|>text [111, 541, 351, 562]<|/det|>• today → that day
<|det|>text [111, 564, 443, 585]<|/det|>• tomorrow → the next day
<|det|>text [111, 586, 646, 608]<|/det|>• yesterday → the day before; the previous day
<|det|>text [111, 609, 677, 631]<|/det|>• last night → the night before; the previous night
<|det|>text [111, 631, 844, 676]<|/det|>- last week/month/year, etc. → the week/month/year, etc. before; the previous week/month/year, etc.
<|det|>title [65, 696, 843, 742]<|/det|>B. Change these sentences into indirect speech. Remember to use appropriate reporting verbs.
<|det|>list [151, 749, 877, 909]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [151, 749, 847, 797]<|/det|>1. The budding cricketer said to his coach, 'I shall improve if you guide me.'
<|det|>text [150, 797, 807, 819]<|/det|>2. I said to my friend, 'You are not working hard enough.'
<|det|>text [150, 820, 837, 864]<|/det|>3. The lady said to her son, 'You are very impatient and lose your temper for trivial reasons.'
<|det|>text [150, 865, 646, 886]<|/det|>4. I said, 'He can be trusted to do this job.'
<|det|>text [150, 887, 876, 909]<|/det|>5. You said to me, 'Sandeep should not have criticised his sister.'
<|det|>page_number [816, 930, 860, 949]<|/det|>149
<PAGE><|det|>list [194, 43, 900, 295]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [194, 43, 785, 65]<|/det|>6. He said, 'I shall talk to you about this next month.'
<|det|>text [195, 66, 900, 110]<|/det|>7. Jiya said to me, 'I shall return from London before you leave for Chennai.'
<|det|>text [195, 111, 852, 156]<|/det|>8. I said to the mason, 'You are taking too long to finish the work. You promised to complete it in a month.'
<|det|>text [195, 157, 832, 201]<|/det|>9. Dr David said to me, 'You have injured your hamstring muscle. You need to take rest till next week.'
<|det|>text [187, 203, 774, 226]<|/det|>10. He said to me, 'You can go swimming tomorrow.'
<|det|>text [187, 226, 862, 272]<|/det|>11. Rahul told me, 'I met our maths teacher yesterday and he promised to visit us today.'
<|det|>text [187, 272, 777, 295]<|/det|>12. I said, 'We shifted to this apartment a month ago.'
<|det|>title [111, 309, 708, 333]<|/det|>Questions: From Direct Speech to Indirect Speech
<|det|>text [110, 338, 877, 384]<|/det|>Now, let us see how we should change questions from direct to indirect speech.
<|det|>list [111, 385, 877, 543]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 385, 740, 408]<|/det|>◆ The reporting verb must be changed to asked, enquired.
<|det|>text [111, 408, 875, 430]<|/det|>- Words like demanded, wondered, wanted to know, may also be used.
<|det|>text [112, 431, 714, 453]<|/det|>The question mark is removed. A statement is framed.
<|det|>text [112, 454, 860, 499]<|/det|>- No conjunction is used if the question begins with words like who, whom, whose, which, what, why. For example,
<|det|>text [158, 499, 652, 545]<|/det|>- Rita said to me, 'What do you wish to buy?' Rita asked me what I wished to buy.
<|det|>text [111, 553, 911, 599]<|/det|>- When the question is not introduced by a question word, the reporting verb is followed by whether or if. For example,
<|det|>text [158, 599, 560, 643]<|/det|>- I asked him, 'Will you be late?'
I asked him whether he would be late.
<|det|>text [111, 654, 882, 723]<|/det|>- Do remember that we need to keep in mind the tone of the question and the sentiment of the speaker when we change from direct to indirect speech. For example,
<|det|>text [158, 723, 730, 768]<|/det|>- My friend said, 'Would you like to go for a movie?' I said, 'No.'
<|det|>text [190, 769, 821, 816]<|/det|>My friend asked me whether I would like to go for a movie. I replied that I would not.
<|det|>title [111, 838, 639, 862]<|/det|>C. Change the following into indirect speech.
<|det|>text [186, 871, 777, 894]<|/det|>1. The stranger said to me, 'Have you lost your way?'
<|det|>text [185, 895, 821, 917]<|/det|>2. I said to Rima, 'Would you like to have an ice cream?'
<|det|>page_number [132, 930, 177, 950]<|/det|>150
<PAGE><|det|>text [148, 44, 767, 68]<|/det|>3. She said to me, 'What would you like to eat, dear?'
<|det|>text [148, 69, 821, 113]<|/det|>4. She said to me, 'How much money should I take for the vacation?'
<|det|>text [150, 114, 877, 160]<|/det|>5. She said to the plumber, 'Can you fix it in a day?' The plumber said, 'No, I cannot start work before next week.'
<|det|>text [151, 161, 848, 185]<|/det|>6. I said to the man, 'Could you direct me to the post office?'
<|det|>text [152, 186, 871, 209]<|/det|>7. I said to my teacher, 'Can you explain this to me tomorrow?'
<|det|>text [152, 210, 873, 255]<|/det|>8. Gora said to his mother, 'Why can't we go to the zoo today?'  
9. She said to her friend, 'Will you help me pack my bags?'
<|det|>list [148, 44, 877, 255]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [215, 256, 553, 279]<|/det|>Her friend said, 'Yes, certainly.'
<|det|>text [148, 280, 830, 324]<|/det|>10. She said to me, 'Who are you and what is it that you want from me?'
<|det|>text [148, 326, 740, 349]<|/det|>11. He said to me, 'Can you meet me at the library?'
<|det|>text [148, 350, 874, 397]<|/det|>12. I said to the porter, 'How much will you take for carrying my bags to the platform?'
<|det|>list [148, 280, 874, 397]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [70, 418, 708, 443]<|/det|>Imperatives: From Direct Speech to Indirect Speech
<|det|>text [70, 449, 816, 495]<|/det|>Let us now take a look at the rules for changing commands, requests, advice, etc. into indirect speech.
<|det|>text [71, 495, 867, 609]<|/det|>The reporting verb will have to be changed to ordered, requested, or advised. Other words that may be used are asked, invited, encouraged, reminded, urged, warned, recommended, etc. The reporting verb must be chosen according to who said what and in what way. The verb that is to be reported is changed into an infinitive. For example,
<|det|>text [117, 609, 778, 655]<|/det|>- I said to my friend, 'Could you make me a cup of coffee?' I requested my friend to make me a cup of coffee.
<|det|>text [117, 655, 815, 699]<|/det|>- The teacher said to the pupils, 'You must complete the work right now.'
<|det|>list [117, 609, 815, 699]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [157, 700, 852, 723]<|/det|>The teacher ordered the pupils to complete their work right then.
<|det|>title [70, 738, 848, 763]<|/det|>Exclamatory Sentences: From Direct Speech to Indirect Speech
<|det|>text [70, 769, 862, 814]<|/det|>Let us now take a look at the rules for changing exclamations and wishes into indirect speech.
<|det|>text [71, 814, 735, 858]<|/det|>The reporting verb is changed into wished, prayed, cried, exclaimed, etc.
<|det|>text [71, 858, 875, 926]<|/det|>Words such as hurrah, alas, oh, etc., are omitted. However, the feeling expressed must be conveyed appropriately by using expressions such as exclaimed with joy.
<|det|>list [71, 814, 875, 926]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [808, 932, 848, 950]<|/det|>151
<PAGE><|det|>text [107, 48, 666, 73]<|/det|>The sign of exclamation is omitted. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 73, 577, 95]<|/det|>- 'Alas! The elephant is dead,' he said.
<|det|>text [192, 95, 700, 120]<|/det|>He exclaimed sadly that the elephant was dead.
<|det|>title [106, 135, 636, 161]<|/det|>D. Change the following into indirect speech.
<|det|>list [194, 167, 911, 562]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [195, 167, 755, 191]<|/det|>1. I said to Rima, 'Help me in cleaning this room.'
<|det|>text [194, 190, 895, 215]<|/det|>2. The teacher said to the pupils, 'Do not go out into the rain.'
<|det|>text [195, 215, 780, 238]<|/det|>3. She said to me, 'Please repeat what you just said.'
<|det|>text [194, 237, 898, 282]<|/det|>4. The director said to the actor, 'Be careful when you perform the stunts.'
<|det|>text [194, 282, 911, 308]<|/det|>5. The priest said to me, 'May the Almighty grant you a long life!'
<|det|>text [195, 308, 660, 331]<|/det|>6. 'How pretty I look!' said the young girl.
<|det|>text [195, 330, 906, 354]<|/det|>7. My father said to me, 'At this rate, you will lose all your money.'
<|det|>text [195, 354, 852, 398]<|/det|>8. Her brother said to her, 'You should not speak ill of your friends.'
<|det|>text [195, 399, 866, 446]<|/det|>9. He said to his sister, 'Do not rush through this or you may commit grave errors.'
<|det|>text [182, 446, 750, 468]<|/det|>10. 'Please don't misunderstand me,' he said to me.
<|det|>text [182, 468, 885, 512]<|/det|>11. The mother said, 'Children, do not be so nervous about the exams.'
<|det|>text [182, 512, 890, 561]<|/det|>12. 'You have done very well! Keep up the good work,' said the teacher to his pupil.
<|det|>title [110, 576, 562, 600]<|/det|>From Indirect Speech to Direct Speech
<|det|>text [109, 604, 801, 650]<|/det|>Let us now study how to change sentences in indirect speech into direct speech.
<|det|>list [109, 650, 918, 925]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 650, 905, 696]<|/det|>We know that indirect speech is a means of reporting what somebody has said without using the actual words of the speaker.
<|det|>text [110, 696, 874, 741]<|/det|>The best way to change from indirect to direct speech is to mentally identify the two parts of the sentence.
<|det|>text [110, 741, 890, 787]<|/det|>The main part will contain a reporting verb like, said, tell, suggested, requested, etc.
<|det|>text [110, 787, 918, 833]<|/det|>The other part will contain what is being reported. This part will be the quoted section in direct speech.
<|det|>text [110, 833, 800, 878]<|/det|>The part that will become the quote, once we have made the transformation, is the object of the reporting verb.
<|det|>text [150, 878, 858, 924]<|/det|>- He told me that my letter had reached him the previous week. He said to me, 'Your letter reached me last week.'
<|det|>page_number [124, 932, 171, 952]<|/det|>152
<PAGE><|det|>text [120, 48, 760, 71]<|/det|>The teacher asked the boy whether he had read that book.
<|det|>text [122, 72, 721, 96]<|/det|>The teacher said to the boy, 'Have you read this book?'
<|det|>text [90, 97, 714, 120]<|/det|>- His mother urged him to pursue a career in medicine.
<|det|>text [126, 120, 844, 144]<|/det|>His mother said to him, 'You should pursue a career in medicine.'
<|det|>text [90, 145, 575, 167]<|/det|>• He exclaimed that it was a violent storm.
<|det|>text [126, 168, 517, 190]<|/det|>'What a violent storm it is!' said he.
<|det|>title [120, 204, 393, 230]<|/det|>Change to direct speech.
<|det|>list [130, 234, 856, 591]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 238, 714, 263]<|/det|>1. He proposed to us that we should go to the zoo.
<|det|>text [134, 263, 850, 288]<|/det|>2. The teacher advised his students to work hard and consistently.
<|det|>text [135, 288, 597, 311]<|/det|>3. He begged his mother to forgive him.
<|det|>text [134, 311, 841, 355]<|/det|>4. The man wanted to know how long it would take to reach the station.
<|det|>text [135, 358, 631, 382]<|/det|>5. The manager said that he was busy then.
<|det|>text [137, 382, 691, 404]<|/det|>6. He said that he had met them a month before.
<|det|>text [137, 405, 650, 428]<|/det|>7. Salim admitted that he had acted foolishly.
<|det|>text [137, 428, 792, 474]<|/det|>8. He asked his students whether they had understood the topic.
<|det|>text [137, 474, 805, 520]<|/det|>9. He informed me that the shop had been closed since the previous month.
<|det|>text [127, 521, 771, 543]<|/det|>10. The teacher said that the earth rotates on its own axis.
<|det|>text [128, 544, 756, 568]<|/det|>11. He reminded me that I had to visit my aunt that day.
<|det|>text [128, 568, 851, 591]<|/det|>12. She advised her brother not to worry as she was there to help.
<|det|>image [296, 608, 631, 808]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [787, 939, 835, 961]<|/det|>153

<PAGE><|det|>image [0, 0, 255, 142]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [556, 35, 944, 68]<|/det|>Rain in Summer
<|det|>text [90, 150, 383, 171]<|/det|>How beautiful is the rain!
<|det|>text [90, 174, 357, 195]<|/det|>After the dust and heat,
<|det|>text [90, 197, 406, 220]<|/det|>In the broad and fiery street,
<|det|>text [90, 221, 312, 242]<|/det|>In the narrow lane,
<|det|>text [90, 243, 383, 264]<|/det|>How beautiful is the rain!
<|det|>text [90, 266, 437, 289]<|/det|>How it clatters along the roofs,
<|det|>text [90, 290, 359, 311]<|/det|>Like the tramp of hoofs;
<|det|>text [90, 312, 451, 335]<|/det|>How it gushes and struggles out
<|det|>text [90, 336, 543, 358]<|/det|>From the throat of the overflowing spout!
<|det|>text [89, 368, 373, 391]<|/det|>Across the window-pane
<|det|>text [90, 392, 309, 414]<|/det|>It pours and pours;
<|det|>text [90, 415, 316, 436]<|/det|>And swift and wide,
<|det|>text [90, 438, 310, 460]<|/det|>With a muddy tide,
<|det|>text [90, 462, 461, 484]<|/det|>Like a river down the gutter roars
<|det|>text [90, 485, 401, 505]<|/det|>The rain, the welcome rain!
<|det|>image [584, 160, 895, 398]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [114, 541, 300, 668]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [336, 516, 752, 538]<|/det|>The sick man from his chamber looks
<|det|>text [337, 540, 583, 561]<|/det|>At the twisted brooks;
<|det|>text [338, 563, 564, 583]<|/det|>He can feel the cool
<|det|>text [338, 586, 616, 608]<|/det|>Breath of each little pool;
<|det|>text [338, 610, 528, 629]<|/det|>His fevered brain
<|det|>text [338, 633, 545, 655]<|/det|>Grows calm again,
<|det|>text [338, 656, 767, 678]<|/det|>And he breathes a blessing on the rain.
<|det|>text [87, 688, 427, 712]<|/det|>From the neighbouring school
<|det|>text [89, 714, 267, 736]<|/det|>Come the boys,
<|det|>text [89, 737, 478, 757]<|/det|>With more than their wonted noise
<|det|>text [89, 760, 282, 781]<|/det|>And commotion;
<|det|>text [89, 784, 370, 804]<|/det|>And down the wet streets
<|det|>text [89, 807, 336, 829]<|/det|>Sail their mimic fleets,
<|det|>text [89, 831, 356, 853]<|/det|>Till the treacherous pool
<|det|>text [89, 855, 388, 877]<|/det|>Engulfs them in its whirling
<|det|>text [88, 879, 322, 899]<|/det|>And turbulent ocean.
<|det|>image [572, 687, 931, 888]<|/det|>
<|det|>image_caption [718, 900, 934, 925]<|/det|>(H W Longfellow)
<|det|>page_number [111, 935, 146, 953]<|/det|>154
<PAGE><|det|>title [122, 24, 388, 51]<|/det|>Answer these questions.
<|det|>list [121, 59, 834, 180]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [122, 60, 768, 84]<|/det|>1. How have the streets been described before the rains?
<|det|>text [122, 85, 710, 109]<|/det|>2. From where does the rain gush and struggle out?
<|det|>text [123, 109, 599, 130]<|/det|>3. Why does the sick man bless the rain?
<|det|>text [122, 131, 829, 180]<|/det|>4. Why do the boys make more than their usual noise when it rains? What do they do?
<|det|>title [122, 200, 785, 248]<|/det|>Mark these statements as true or false. Rewrite the incorrect sentences correctly.
<|det|>list [121, 258, 856, 405]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [122, 259, 806, 283]<|/det|>1. The rains are welcome after the dust and heat of summer.
<|det|>text [122, 285, 625, 308]<|/det|>2. The rains gently trickle down the gutters.
<|det|>text [122, 309, 853, 352]<|/det|>3. The raindrops clatter on the rooftops like the stamping of cow hooves.
<|det|>text [123, 356, 828, 404]<|/det|>4. The pools created by the rains engulf the little boats floated by the schoolboys.
<|det|>title [127, 430, 562, 453]<|/det|>Find words from the poem which mean:
<|det|>list [122, 464, 570, 534]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [123, 464, 567, 488]<|/det|>1. stomp 2. nearby
<|det|>text [126, 489, 569, 511]<|/det|>3. curling 4. chaotic
<|det|>text [126, 512, 301, 534]<|/det|>5. deceptive
<|det|>title [73, 559, 811, 630]<|/det|>D. Make sentences with the following words. You may look up the dictionary if you are not sure of any of the meanings. Do not change the form of the words.
<|det|>list [123, 640, 301, 780]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [126, 641, 260, 663]<|/det|>1. gutter
<|det|>text [126, 665, 280, 684]<|/det|>2. fevered
<|det|>text [126, 688, 282, 707]<|/det|>3. wonted
<|det|>text [126, 712, 267, 731]<|/det|>4. mimic
<|det|>text [126, 735, 277, 757]<|/det|>5. engulfs
<|det|>text [126, 759, 299, 778]<|/det|>6. turbulent
<|det|>image [375, 634, 770, 945]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [793, 941, 834, 958]<|/det|>155
<PAGE><|det|>header [101, 38, 168, 80]<|/det|>20
<|det|>header [295, 47, 930, 80]<|/det|>Transformation of Sentences
<|det|>image [108, 162, 164, 208]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 175, 697, 197]<|/det|>You know that there are four kinds of sentences.
<|det|>list [108, 212, 388, 302]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 212, 348, 232]<|/det|>◆ assertive sentence
<|det|>text [109, 235, 387, 256]<|/det|>◆ exclamatory sentence
<|det|>text [109, 259, 370, 279]<|/det|>◆ imperative sentence
<|det|>text [109, 282, 388, 303]<|/det|>◆ interrogative sentence
<|det|>text [108, 318, 615, 339]<|/det|>And, there are two kinds of assertive sentences.
<|det|>list [109, 342, 348, 386]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 342, 370, 362]<|/det|>◆ affirmative sentence
<|det|>text [110, 366, 348, 386]<|/det|>◆ negative sentence
<|det|>text [110, 402, 499, 422]<|/det|>Now, take a look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [113, 425, 450, 514]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 425, 435, 445]<|/det|>• How beautiful is this rose!
<|det|>text [150, 448, 449, 469]<|/det|>This is a very beautiful rose.
<|det|>text [113, 470, 378, 492]<|/det|>• What a great victory!
<|det|>text [151, 494, 378, 515]<|/det|>It was a great victory.
<|det|>text [110, 528, 915, 571]<|/det|>The first sentence in each pair is an exclamatory sentence and the second is an assertive sentence. But, both the sentences convey the same idea.
<|det|>text [110, 573, 885, 618]<|/det|>So, we see that it is possible to change an exclamatory sentence into an assertive sentence.
<|det|>text [110, 634, 825, 680]<|/det|>It is also possible to change an affirmative sentence into a negative sentence without altering the meaning. For example,
<|det|>list [113, 681, 524, 771]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 681, 478, 702]<|/det|>• I was doubtful of your success.
<|det|>text [150, 704, 474, 725]<|/det|>I was not sure of your success.
<|det|>text [113, 727, 523, 747]<|/det|>• Where there is smoke, there is fire.
<|det|>text [150, 749, 480, 770]<|/det|>There is no smoke without fire.
<|det|>text [110, 786, 575, 856]<|/det|>We can also change an interrogative sentence into an assertive sentence without changing the meaning. For example,
<|det|>text [113, 857, 345, 877]<|/det|>• What if I am late?
<|det|>text [150, 880, 466, 901]<|/det|>It does not matter if I am late.
<|det|>image [607, 689, 925, 873]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [130, 927, 177, 948]<|/det|>156
<PAGE><|det|>text [110, 48, 674, 71]<|/det|>Is there any sense in waiting for him for three hours?
<|det|>text [110, 72, 667, 93]<|/det|>There is no sense in waiting for him for three hours.
<|det|>title [68, 116, 553, 145]<|/det|>change each of these sentences as directed.
<|det|>list [78, 150, 877, 658]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [78, 150, 752, 175]<|/det|>1. Who can forget Mahatma Gandhi? (change to assertive)
<|det|>text [79, 175, 701, 198]<|/det|>2. I wish I were young again. (change to exclamatory)
<|det|>text [79, 198, 805, 221]<|/det|>3. Why waste time in this futile operation? (change to assertive)
<|det|>text [80, 221, 674, 244]<|/det|>4. How wet and cold you are! (change to assertive)
<|det|>text [80, 244, 540, 268]<|/det|>5. It was possible. (change to negative)
<|det|>text [80, 268, 677, 291]<|/det|>6. Was he not rude to say this? (change to assertive)
<|det|>text [80, 291, 785, 336]<|/det|>7. There was no one present who did not applaud. (change to affirmative)
<|det|>text [80, 337, 708, 361]<|/det|>8. He is too weak to move about. (change to negative)
<|det|>text [80, 361, 867, 384]<|/det|>9. Is this the kind of behaviour expected of you? (change to assertive)
<|det|>text [78, 384, 860, 408]<|/det|>10. Everybody will admit that this is a good idea. (change to negative)
<|det|>text [79, 408, 747, 431]<|/det|>11. I long for a good book to read. (change to exclamatory)
<|det|>text [79, 431, 673, 453]<|/det|>12. This can never happen. (change to interrogative)
<|det|>text [80, 453, 675, 475]<|/det|>13. How can I put up with this? (change to assertive)
<|det|>text [80, 476, 555, 498]<|/det|>14. Well done, Ray! (change to assertive)
<|det|>text [80, 499, 773, 522]<|/det|>15. I am yearning for a short holiday. (change to exclamatory)
<|det|>text [80, 522, 636, 544]<|/det|>16. Shall I ever forgive him? (change to assertive)
<|det|>text [80, 544, 874, 568]<|/det|>17. None will deny that he is a dignified person. (change to affirmative)
<|det|>text [80, 568, 805, 591]<|/det|>18. As soon as he arrived, he began to argue. (change to negative)
<|det|>text [80, 591, 756, 613]<|/det|>19. We are not here to waste time. (change to interrogative)
<|det|>text [78, 613, 765, 658]<|/det|>20. You will never forget this wonderful boat ride. (change to interrogative)
<|det|>image [262, 675, 681, 890]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [820, 937, 862, 956]<|/det|>157
<PAGE><|det|>title [111, 38, 177, 80]<|/det|>21
<|det|>title [536, 45, 926, 78]<|/det|>Kinds of Phrases
<|det|>image [111, 164, 168, 211]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 161, 914, 207]<|/det|>You have learnt that a group of words that makes complete sense is called a sentence.
<|det|>text [110, 225, 720, 251]<|/det|>Which of these groups of words are complete sentences?
<|det|>list [111, 259, 692, 427]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 260, 687, 285]<|/det|>• The boy in the blue coat is the captain of the team.
<|det|>text [113, 286, 289, 306]<|/det|>• I hit the ball.
<|det|>text [113, 310, 323, 331]<|/det|>• in the blue coat
<|det|>text [113, 334, 582, 358]<|/det|>• The children are playing in the backyard.
<|det|>text [113, 359, 318, 381]<|/det|>• in the backyard
<|det|>text [113, 383, 532, 406]<|/det|>• In a flash, the frog caught the insect.
<|det|>text [113, 408, 252, 429]<|/det|>- in a flash
<|det|>image [626, 281, 916, 428]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [109, 446, 885, 493]<|/det|>Did you notice that the groups of words which sound incomplete do not have a verb?
<|det|>text [109, 512, 875, 560]<|/det|>A phrase is group of words that makes complete sense only when it is a part of a sentence. A phrase does not contain a finite verb.
<|det|>text [111, 603, 654, 627]<|/det|>A. Use these phrases in sentences of your own.
<|det|>list [194, 637, 480, 866]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [195, 637, 444, 659]<|/det|>1. on the blackboard
<|det|>text [195, 661, 463, 684]<|/det|>2. through the window
<|det|>text [195, 685, 406, 707]<|/det|>3. some time ago
<|det|>text [195, 708, 400, 729]<|/det|>4. the open door
<|det|>text [195, 730, 414, 751]<|/det|>5. for her children
<|det|>text [195, 754, 429, 775]<|/det|>6. towards the door
<|det|>text [195, 778, 369, 800]<|/det|>7. not so easy
<|det|>text [195, 801, 368, 823]<|/det|>8. to the shop
<|det|>text [195, 824, 479, 847]<|/det|>9. in the swimming pool
<|det|>text [183, 847, 399, 868]<|/det|>10. in the shadow
<|det|>image [576, 681, 801, 852]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [127, 924, 171, 943]<|/det|>158
<PAGE><|det|>text [120, 25, 805, 79]<|/det|>Mark √ if you think the highlighted group of words in each of these sentences is a phrase.
<|det|>list [130, 84, 856, 478]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 87, 657, 113]<|/det|>1. We have purchased a house in this village.
<|det|>text [136, 114, 414, 137]<|/det|>2. He did it in a hurry.
<|det|>text [137, 138, 490, 162]<|/det|>3. That was an act of bravery.
<|det|>text [137, 162, 464, 186]<|/det|>4. Playing golf is his hobby.
<|det|>text [137, 186, 580, 211]<|/det|>5. If you wish, I shall accompany you.
<|det|>text [137, 211, 532, 235]<|/det|>6. Be careful! Drive with caution.
<|det|>text [137, 235, 578, 258]<|/det|>7. The bus will reach in a short while.
<|det|>text [137, 259, 624, 284]<|/det|>8. The crowd attacked the office violently.
<|det|>text [137, 284, 612, 308]<|/det|>9. The soldiers fought in a brave manner.
<|det|>text [130, 308, 647, 333]<|/det|>10. My little brother loves listening to stories.
<|det|>text [130, 334, 625, 357]<|/det|>11. You must carry garments made of wool.
<|det|>text [130, 357, 767, 382]<|/det|>12. Keep the food in the fridge as it is quite warm today.
<|det|>text [130, 382, 835, 425]<|/det|>13. The child standing near the gate is wearing a coat made of silk.
<|det|>text [130, 428, 851, 453]<|/det|>14. The chair that was placed by the window is an antique piece.
<|det|>text [130, 453, 846, 477]<|/det|>15. We need a basketball coach with some years of experience.
<|det|>image [616, 111, 857, 328]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [72, 499, 508, 524]<|/det|>There are three kinds of phrases. They are:
<|det|>list [73, 526, 281, 595]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [73, 526, 245, 548]<|/det|>◆ noun phrase
<|det|>text [73, 548, 280, 571]<|/det|>◆ adjective phrase
<|det|>text [73, 571, 260, 595]<|/det|>◆ adverb phrase
<|det|>title [74, 610, 225, 631]<|/det|>Noun Phrase
<|det|>text [74, 641, 300, 661]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [74, 663, 562, 733]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [74, 663, 562, 686]<|/det|>• To win the competition is my dearest wish.
<|det|>text [74, 687, 325, 710]<|/det|>• I tried to get it right.
<|det|>text [74, 710, 458, 733]<|/det|>- Your warning him was necessary.
<|det|>text [72, 748, 848, 796]<|/det|>Sometimes, we need a group of words to speak or write about a person or a thing.
<|det|>text [72, 813, 502, 884]<|/det|>When a group of words does the work of a noun in a sentence, we call it the noun phrase.
<|det|>image [616, 111, 857, 325]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [616, 113, 857, 325]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [616, 113, 857, 325]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [616, 113, 856, 325]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [616, 113, 856, 325]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [616, 113, 856, 326]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [616, 113, 856, 326]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [616, 113, 856, 326]<|/det|>
