<PAGE><|det|>image [0, 0, 236, 117]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [463, 35, 936, 114]<|/det|>On the Grasshopper and Cricket
<|det|>image [8, 156, 406, 501]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [400, 150, 766, 173]<|/det|>The poetry of earth is never dead:
<|det|>text [411, 174, 767, 194]<|/det|>When all the birds are faint with
<|det|>text [445, 197, 574, 219]<|/det|>the hot sun,
<|det|>text [411, 219, 878, 241]<|/det|>And hide in cooling trees, a voice will run
<|det|>text [230, 244, 776, 266]<|/det|>From hedge to hedge about the new-mown mead;
<|det|>text [194, 267, 672, 289]<|/det|>That is the Grasshopper's—he takes the lead
<|det|>text [182, 290, 601, 311]<|/det|>In summer luxury,—he has never done
<|det|>text [178, 312, 662, 334]<|/det|>With his delights; for when tired out with fun
<|det|>text [179, 336, 668, 357]<|/det|>He rests at ease beneath some pleasant weed.
<|det|>text [178, 358, 565, 380]<|/det|>The poetry of earth is ceasing never:
<|det|>text [185, 381, 627, 402]<|/det|>On a lone winter evening, when the frost
<|det|>text [238, 403, 750, 425]<|/det|>Has wrought a silence, from the stove there shrills
<|det|>text [377, 426, 880, 448]<|/det|>The Cricket's song, in warmth increasing ever,
<|det|>text [415, 449, 868, 470]<|/det|>And seems to one in drowsiness half lost,
<|det|>text [415, 471, 911, 494]<|/det|>The Grasshopper's among some grassy hills.
<|det|>text [765, 501, 920, 524]<|/det|>(John Keats)
<|det|>image [71, 533, 152, 590]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [172, 536, 440, 558]<|/det|>Complete the sentences.
<|det|>list [174, 566, 888, 704]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 566, 888, 611]<|/det|>1. ____ tells us that the ‘poetry of the earth’ is not dead in summer.
<|det|>text [172, 614, 794, 636]<|/det|>2. The birds avoid the heat by ____.
<|det|>text [172, 637, 918, 658]<|/det|>3. The grasshopper rests under the weed after ____.
<|det|>text [172, 660, 870, 681]<|/det|>4. In winter the silence is broken by ____.
<|det|>text [172, 682, 813, 704]<|/det|>5. The cricket's song seems like ____.
<|det|>image [71, 725, 152, 784]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [170, 729, 467, 749]<|/det|>Choose the correct answer.
<|det|>text [172, 760, 492, 781]<|/det|>1. In the hot sun, the birds
<|det|>text [235, 784, 451, 806]<|/det|>a. chirp in delight.
<|det|>text [599, 783, 750, 804]<|/det|>b. feel faint.
<|det|>text [235, 808, 386, 830]<|/det|>c. fly about.
<|det|>text [168, 844, 875, 868]<|/det|>2. The grasshopper's voice can be heard in the meadow from
<|det|>text [235, 870, 454, 891]<|/det|>a. hedge to hedge.
<|det|>text [599, 868, 792, 890]<|/det|>b. pillar to post.
<|det|>text [235, 893, 408, 911]<|/det|>c. tree to tree.
<|det|>page_number [111, 924, 159, 944]<|/det|>174
<PAGE><|det|>text [122, 8, 554, 35]<|/det|>3. In winter, the cricket finds shelter
<|det|>text [189, 36, 384, 59]<|/det|>a. in the pantry.
<|det|>text [559, 33, 819, 58]<|/det|>b. among grassy hills.
<|det|>text [190, 59, 377, 83]<|/det|>c. by the stove.
<|det|>text [122, 99, 400, 123]<|/det|>4. In winter, one feels
<|det|>text [190, 125, 324, 148]<|/det|>a. drowsy.
<|det|>text [559, 121, 753, 147]<|/det|>b. like dancing.
<|det|>text [191, 149, 297, 171]<|/det|>c. cold.
<|det|>text [122, 188, 597, 213]<|/det|>5. The cricket's song reminds one of the
<|det|>text [191, 214, 478, 236]<|/det|>a. butterflies in summer.
<|det|>text [560, 214, 862, 238]<|/det|>b. grasshopper in summer.
<|det|>text [191, 238, 508, 262]<|/det|>c. birds singing in summer.
<|det|>title [122, 296, 801, 323]<|/det|>Find suitable words or phrases from the poem which mean—
<|det|>text [125, 333, 255, 358]<|/det|>1. ending
<|det|>text [441, 333, 630, 356]<|/det|>2. enjoyment
<|det|>text [126, 358, 306, 380]<|/det|>3. to be ahead
<|det|>text [441, 358, 684, 380]<|/det|>4. caused, created
<|det|>text [127, 383, 348, 405]<|/det|>5. frozen moisture
<|det|>image [288, 457, 716, 713]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [793, 943, 834, 960]<|/det|>175
<PAGE><|det|>header [106, 35, 177, 78]<|/det|>24
<|det|>title [397, 44, 912, 126]<|/det|>Simple, Compound and Complex Sentences
<|det|>image [108, 151, 163, 201]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [178, 151, 856, 198]<|/det|>You have already learnt that a sentence is a group of words that makes absolute sense.
<|det|>text [107, 212, 908, 258]<|/det|>Sentences can be categorised into three types, based on their grammatical structure.
<|det|>list [109, 262, 370, 330]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 262, 327, 283]<|/det|>◆ simple sentence
<|det|>text [110, 285, 370, 306]<|/det|>◆ compound sentence
<|det|>text [110, 308, 348, 330]<|/det|>◆ complex sentence
<|det|>title [109, 346, 314, 370]<|/det|>Simple Sentence
<|det|>text [109, 377, 341, 396]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [110, 399, 408, 444]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 399, 408, 421]<|/det|>• He lives in this building.
<|det|>text [110, 422, 366, 444]<|/det|>• Sammy likes sweets.
<|det|>text [107, 457, 878, 527]<|/det|>Each of these sentences is a clause. We can say so because each has a subject and a verb. And these sentences can also be divided into subject and predicate.
<|det|>text [107, 557, 895, 604]<|/det|>A sentence having only one clause and therefore, only a single finite verb, is called a simple sentence.
<|det|>title [108, 637, 357, 660]<|/det|>Compound Sentence
<|det|>text [108, 668, 336, 688]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [109, 690, 682, 738]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 690, 682, 713]<|/det|>• The rain arrived and the peacocks began to dance.
<|det|>text [110, 714, 523, 738]<|/det|>• I was disappointed but I kept quiet.
<|det|>text [106, 751, 888, 822]<|/det|>The highlighted parts in the above sentences are independent clauses that can stand on their own. These independent clauses have been joined by coordinating conjunctions like and and but.
<|det|>text [106, 847, 845, 895]<|/det|>A sentence that contains two independent clauses that are joined by a coordinating conjunction is called a compound sentence.
<|det|>page_number [128, 927, 172, 946]<|/det|>176
<PAGE><|det|>title [98, 44, 161, 69]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [92, 83, 856, 125]<|/det|>If a compound sentence is made up of two clauses and if the subject of the second clause is the same as the subject of the first, the second subject may be omitted.
<|det|>text [119, 127, 488, 145]<|/det|>• Monica reached home and cooked dinner.
<|det|>text [94, 161, 828, 203]<|/det|>Since both the clauses are of the same rank, we refer to them as coordinate clauses. Each clause can be divided into subject and predicate and has a finite verb.
<|det|>title [80, 233, 314, 258]<|/det|>Complex Sentence
<|det|>text [80, 264, 830, 311]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences. Note that in each sentence, the underlined clause depends on the highlighted clause to complete its meaning.
<|det|>list [85, 312, 562, 357]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 312, 516, 333]<|/det|>• This is the girl who is my neighbour.
<|det|>text [85, 334, 562, 357]<|/det|>• I left the house when my mother arrived.
<|det|>text [84, 373, 788, 395]<|/det|>These sentences comprise a main clause and a subordinate clause.
<|det|>text [84, 397, 844, 443]<|/det|>The subordinate clause in each case is dependent on the main clause to make its meaning clear.
<|det|>text [84, 464, 838, 510]<|/det|>A sentence having one main clause and at least one subordinate clause is called a complex sentence.
<|det|>title [116, 545, 182, 570]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [111, 583, 838, 624]<|/det|>A complex sentence can have many subordinate clauses. But there must be at least one main clause that is absolutely independent and makes complete sense. For example,
<|det|>text [111, 625, 838, 666]<|/det|>- (As the sun set) we walked towards our tent (which had been pitched near a river) (that was almost overflowing).
<|det|>text [111, 679, 838, 740]<|/det|>The highlighted part is the main clause. There are three subordinate clauses in this sentence. They have been put within brackets. The main clause, as well as each of the subordinate clauses, has a finite verb.
<|det|>image [78, 775, 141, 811]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [172, 779, 858, 847]<|/det|>State whether these sentences are simple, complex or compound. In case of a complex sentence, point out its main clause and subordinate clause/clauses.
<|det|>list [174, 860, 861, 927]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [175, 860, 770, 881]<|/det|>1. Although Porus was defeated, he never lost courage.
<|det|>text [174, 882, 861, 927]<|/det|>2. This violin, bought from an antique shop, belonged to a man living in a small town in the hills.
<|det|>page_number [808, 930, 851, 948]<|/det|>177
<PAGE><|det|>list [194, 36, 911, 642]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [195, 36, 783, 63]<|/det|>3. I like playing football but I am not fond of cricket.
<|det|>text [195, 61, 871, 107]<|/det|>4. Many people live in small huts by the river flowing down from the mountains in the north.
<|det|>text [195, 107, 825, 133]<|/det|>5. If you hope to succeed, you should put in more effort.
<|det|>text [195, 131, 907, 179]<|/det|>6. The Town Hall was destroyed by the last earthquake, but the War Memorial was not damaged.
<|det|>text [195, 177, 875, 226]<|/det|>7. Tired of waiting, we started walking in the direction of the station, hoping to catch the last train to the city.
<|det|>text [195, 225, 777, 251]<|/det|>8. You must not go to work today as you are unwell.
<|det|>text [195, 248, 916, 297]<|/det|>9. We were all delighted by his achievements, yet we could not reveal our true feelings.
<|det|>text [184, 297, 701, 321]<|/det|>10. I must leave now, else I shall miss the train.
<|det|>text [184, 320, 819, 367]<|/det|>11. The prince wandered through the forests of the region searching for the mythical animal.
<|det|>text [184, 365, 910, 391]<|/det|>12. Neither the colour nor the design of this dress appeals to me.
<|det|>text [184, 389, 857, 435]<|/det|>13. Yesterday, I met Rita who is now in Delhi where she lives with her brother who is an architect.
<|det|>text [184, 434, 838, 459]<|/det|>14. I have always wondered how he manages to do so well.
<|det|>text [184, 458, 840, 482]<|/det|>15. When I walked through that door, I was feeling nervous.
<|det|>text [184, 481, 811, 528]<|/det|>16. As soon as the teacher walked into the classroom, the students stopped shouting.
<|det|>text [184, 527, 848, 573]<|/det|>17. The book was published in the month of May prior to his departure for a country on the west coast of Africa.
<|det|>text [184, 572, 871, 595]<|/det|>18. I have visited many continents, but I have not toured India.
<|det|>text [184, 595, 794, 618]<|/det|>19. A great commotion at the station drew my attention.
<|det|>text [184, 618, 886, 641]<|/det|>20. Neither he nor I was prepared for the sudden turn of events.
<|det|>image [348, 675, 637, 920]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 927, 175, 949]<|/det|>178
<PAGE><|det|>title [72, 28, 543, 73]<|/det|>Making Introductions
<|det|>header [678, 0, 944, 123]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>text [71, 135, 777, 184]<|/det|>A class teacher introduces an ex-student who is also a celebrity to the class. Read the conversation to find out more.
<|det|>text [71, 198, 825, 293]<|/det|>teacher: My dear students, as you can see, we have a special guest with us today. But, before I tell you more about her, let me ask. Have any of you heard of the book The Spectacular Saga of Suhasini Sangam?
<|det|>text [74, 293, 367, 319]<|/det|>(A student raises her hand.)
<|det|>text [74, 319, 346, 341]<|/det|>Teacher: Yes, Ragini?
<|det|>text [74, 341, 835, 385]<|/det|>Ragini: I got the book as a present on my birthday last year, ma'am. I really enjoyed reading it.
<|det|>text [74, 385, 830, 430]<|/det|>Teacher: And do you remember the name of the author of the book? Of course. Radhika Balakrishnan.
<|det|>text [74, 431, 848, 593]<|/det|>Teacher: Very good, Ragini. Well, students, our special guest today is the very same Radhika Balakrishnan! She has come here especially to meet all of you and to read for you the excerpts from her new book Suhasini and the Swaying Swings of Septimus. But before she does that, let me tell you a little bit about our guest. Ragini, since you've read the book, do you know anything about the author?
<|det|>text [74, 594, 830, 711]<|/det|>Ragini: I don't know a lot, ma'am, only that Ms Balakrishnan is a highly acclaimed children's author and that she won the Commonwealth Children's Fiction Award and the Amanda Burkley Writer's Prize for The Spectacular Saga of Suhasini Sangam.
<|det|>text [70, 712, 851, 904]<|/det|>Teacher: Very true. But the stories of Suhasini Sangam are not the only books written by Radhika. She has been writing for children ever since she graduated from college. But, let me start from the beginning. Radhika was a student at our school and I am proud to say one of my own students. She was a very bright student, and she went on to study English literature at one of the premier colleges in India. After finishing her graduation, she studied for a post graduate degree at Oxford. But unlike
<|det|>page_number [793, 927, 840, 950]<|/det|>179
<PAGE><|det|>text [245, 32, 911, 593]<|/det|>many who choose to stay on in a foreign country, Radhika decided to come back to India. She had already started to write for children by then and she published short stories in some of India's leading daily newspapers. Some of her well-known short stories are on the adventures of Ashu, the Ant, and on Homi, the Hawk. These were not just pieces of fiction, but were educationally motivated as well. Later on, these stories were all published into separate books which you will find in our library. After returning to India, Radhika worked for a few years for the Times of India. But soon, she gave up her job to devote herself completely to writing books for children. Then she went on to publish The Spectacular Saga of Suhasini Sangam which became a great hit. And she won the awards that Ragini just mentioned. Today, however, Radhika is not just a writer but also a child rights' activist. She is associated with various NGOs for children and is also involved in setting up schools in rural India which provide free education in the English medium to those children who cannot afford to go to private schools. Next month, a new sequel to the Suhasini stories called Suhasini and the Swaying Swings of Septimus is going to be brought out. But as a special treat to the students of the school, she will read out portions of the book. Now, let us all proceed to the auditorium where the reading will take place.
<|det|>title [108, 609, 607, 631]<|/det|>Now, work in pairs and enact these situations.
<|det|>list [114, 637, 899, 753]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [115, 637, 856, 682]<|/det|>1. Robin introduces his uncle to his friends. His uncle has recently returned from an expedition to Mt Everest.
<|det|>text [114, 683, 900, 753]<|/det|>2. Sonam tells her classmate about an event she has recently attended where a celebrity was the chief guest. Enact how she describes the celebrity.
<|det|>page_number [127, 928, 168, 946]<|/det|>180
<PAGE><|det|>header [78, 14, 556, 58]<|/det|>synthesis of Sentences
<|det|>page_number [785, 14, 855, 56]<|/det|>25
<|det|>image [79, 133, 130, 181]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 130, 840, 179]<|/det|>Based on the structure of sentences, we can divide them into three kinds. Look at these examples of the three types of sentences.
<|det|>list [78, 188, 744, 263]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [78, 188, 388, 213]<|/det|>. I know him well. (simple)
<|det|>text [78, 214, 744, 239]<|/det|>. I want to work, but I do not want to work here. (compound)
<|det|>text [78, 239, 558, 263]<|/det|>• I like the dress that you bought. (complex)
<|det|>text [78, 277, 845, 325]<|/det|>Did you know that it is possible to join two or more simple sentences and form compound or complex sentences?
<|det|>text [78, 361, 769, 409]<|/det|>synthesis means combining two or more simple sentences to form compound or complex sentences.
<|det|>text [78, 450, 811, 568]<|/det|>You have learnt that compound sentences are made up of two independent clauses that are joined with the help of conjunctions. We can join two or more simple sentences to form compound sentences. Let us learn the ways of combining simple sentences into compound sentences.
<|det|>list [78, 580, 787, 678]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [78, 580, 785, 628]<|/det|>- We can combine two statements having similar ideas using and, both...and, as well as and not only...but also. For example,
<|det|>list [120, 629, 555, 724]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 629, 555, 676]<|/det|>- Pawan got up. He went to the market.
Pawan got up and went to the market.
<|det|>text [121, 677, 537, 724]<|/det|>• We learn English. We learn Sanskrit. We learn English as well as Sanskrit.
<|det|>text [78, 735, 834, 784]<|/det|>We can combine two statements that express contrasting ideas using but, yet, whereas and nevertheless. For example,
<|det|>list [119, 785, 461, 880]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 785, 461, 854]<|/det|>- He is rich. He is unhappy.
He is rich, but he is unhappy.
He is sick. He is cheerful.
<|det|>text [162, 856, 459, 880]<|/det|>He is sick, yet he is cheerful.
<|det|>page_number [787, 935, 829, 956]<|/det|>181
<PAGE><|det|>text [96, 38, 900, 107]<|/det|>We can combine two statements that express a choice between two alternatives using or, either...or, neither...nor and otherwise. For example,
<|det|>list [140, 109, 622, 202]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 109, 560, 154]<|/det|>- Are you at home? Are you outside? Are you at home or outside?
<|det|>text [140, 157, 622, 202]<|/det|>• We can make pizza. We can make pasta.
We can either make pizza or pasta.
<|det|>text [140, 204, 540, 247]<|/det|>- He is not a cheat. He is not a liar. He is neither a cheat nor a liar.
<|det|>text [98, 261, 862, 308]<|/det|>We can combine two statements that express cause and effect using for, so and therefore. For example,
<|det|>list [141, 310, 567, 400]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [141, 310, 567, 356]<|/det|>• He was sick. He went home early. He was sick so he went home early.
<|det|>text [142, 357, 567, 400]<|/det|>- I was late. I missed the bus.
I was late therefore I missed the bus.
<|det|>text [100, 436, 850, 486]<|/det|>A. Using suitable conjunctions, combine these simple sentences to form compound sentences.
<|det|>list [189, 495, 848, 880]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [190, 495, 491, 517]<|/det|>1. Is this true? Is this false?
<|det|>text [190, 519, 542, 541]<|/det|>2. He was angry. He kept quiet.
<|det|>text [190, 542, 567, 564]<|/det|>3. He was tired. He kept working.
<|det|>text [190, 565, 582, 586]<|/det|>4. He was guilty. He was punished.
<|det|>text [190, 587, 649, 608]<|/det|>5. He lost his suitcase. He lost his money.
<|det|>text [190, 609, 735, 631]<|/det|>6. Will you watch a movie? Will you read a book?
<|det|>text [190, 632, 679, 653]<|/det|>7. He was obstinate. His friends adored him.
<|det|>text [190, 654, 702, 676]<|/det|>8. He cannot attend the meeting. He is unwell.
<|det|>text [190, 678, 711, 700]<|/det|>9. It rained heavily. There was no water-logging.
<|det|>text [180, 700, 739, 722]<|/det|>10. She was very kind to us. We did not mind at all.
<|det|>text [180, 723, 740, 745]<|/det|>11. He read the book. He remembered every detail.
<|det|>text [180, 746, 733, 768]<|/det|>12. You must speak the truth. You will be punished.
<|det|>text [180, 769, 780, 790]<|/det|>13. His father is not a teacher. His father is not an artist.
<|det|>text [180, 791, 804, 813]<|/det|>14. He was a good student. His parents took pride in him.
<|det|>text [180, 814, 845, 837]<|/det|>15. She will go to school by car. She will go to school by bus.
<|det|>text [180, 838, 831, 881]<|/det|>16. Our neighbours must be asleep. There is no light in their house.
<|det|>page_number [120, 928, 168, 950]<|/det|>182
<PAGE><|det|>header [60, 21, 114, 73]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [120, 20, 842, 116]<|/det|>You have learnt that complex sentences are made up of one main clause and one or more subordinate clauses. We can join two or more simple sentences using noun clauses, adjective clauses or adverb clauses and form complex sentences.
<|det|>text [58, 127, 796, 175]<|/det|>We can join two or more simple sentences using a noun clause. For example,
<|det|>list [93, 176, 816, 270]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 176, 625, 198]<|/det|>• He is a talented artist. Everybody knows this.
<|det|>text [134, 199, 625, 221]<|/det|>Everybody knows that he is a talented artist.
<|det|>text [93, 223, 816, 245]<|/det|>• We lost the match. It was the consequence of his carelessness.
<|det|>text [134, 246, 650, 269]<|/det|>Because of his carelessness, we lost the match.
<|det|>text [58, 281, 830, 354]<|/det|>We can join two or more simple sentences with an adjective clause using relative pronouns like who, which or that and adverbs like where, when and why. For example,
<|det|>list [93, 355, 672, 448]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 355, 575, 378]<|/det|>• I met a girl. She works at the emporium.
<|det|>text [134, 379, 580, 401]<|/det|>I met a girl who works at the emporium.
<|det|>text [93, 402, 661, 424]<|/det|>- This is the house. The theft was committed here.
<|det|>text [134, 425, 675, 447]<|/det|>This is the house where the theft was committed.
<|det|>text [58, 460, 801, 531]<|/det|>We can join two or more simple sentences with an adverb clause using conjunctions like when, as, that, since, though, although, whether, whereas, unless and if. For example,
<|det|>list [93, 531, 752, 626]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 531, 752, 554]<|/det|>• He worked hard. His objective was to win a scholarship.
<|det|>text [134, 555, 671, 578]<|/det|>He worked hard that he might win a scholarship.
<|det|>text [93, 578, 483, 600]<|/det|>• He began late. He finished first.
<|det|>text [134, 601, 578, 625]<|/det|>Although he began late, he finished first.
<|det|>image [50, 640, 87, 673]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [120, 644, 655, 668]<|/det|>Join these sentences to form complex sentences.
<|det|>list [120, 678, 856, 915]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 678, 696, 701]<|/det|>1. The boy is my friend. He lives in that mansion.
<|det|>text [121, 703, 609, 725]<|/det|>2. The men escaped. They were convicts.
<|det|>text [121, 727, 562, 747]<|/det|>3. He is ill. He cannot attend school.
<|det|>text [120, 749, 854, 772]<|/det|>4. The soldiers fought very bravely. They pushed back the enemy.
<|det|>text [121, 773, 803, 795]<|/det|>5. Harish has not come for work. Do you know the reason?
<|det|>text [121, 797, 783, 820]<|/det|>6. He finished writing the essay. He sat back and relaxed.
<|det|>text [121, 821, 720, 843]<|/det|>7. The boy was flying a kite. His sister cheered him.
<|det|>text [120, 845, 856, 888]<|/det|>8. The teacher spoke in a very low voice. The students could not hear him.
<|det|>text [120, 891, 845, 915]<|/det|>9. The thief saw the policemen approaching. He fled the scene.
<|det|>page_number [793, 933, 834, 952]<|/det|>183
<PAGE><|det|>list [164, 42, 896, 321]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [164, 42, 896, 65]<|/det|>10. I visited the spot. I had been there before. I thought so then.
<|det|>text [165, 66, 892, 111]<|/det|>11. I heard the news. I rushed to the hospital. I found my father waiting there.
<|det|>text [164, 111, 906, 136]<|/det|>12. He would win the swimming competition. That was his wish.
<|det|>text [165, 137, 555, 159]<|/det|>13. I jumped up. I was surprised.
<|det|>text [165, 159, 769, 181]<|/det|>14. He was fined. He had kept the book for too long.
<|det|>text [166, 182, 656, 204]<|/det|>15. You must work hard. You will not pass.
<|det|>text [166, 205, 696, 226]<|/det|>16. I went to the market. The shop was closed.
<|det|>text [166, 228, 746, 251]<|/det|>17. It might rain. The marriage party will be ruined.
<|det|>text [166, 251, 846, 273]<|/det|>18. You refused to do the work. Can you tell me the reason?
<|det|>text [166, 275, 709, 297]<|/det|>19. The thief was caught. He had stolen the car.
<|det|>text [165, 298, 589, 320]<|/det|>20. You finish your work. I will wait.
<|det|>image [316, 344, 681, 684]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [111, 928, 152, 946]<|/det|>184
<PAGE><|det|>title [62, 44, 461, 83]<|/det|>Narrative Writing
<|det|>header [695, 0, 962, 142]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>text [60, 149, 867, 245]<|/det|>There are different ways of going about writing a story, and there are no set guidelines that can be followed to ensure a good short story. However, one of the first things to remember is that for your story to be a success, you have to hold the reader's attention.
<|det|>text [61, 261, 768, 285]<|/det|>Here are some points to keep in mind when writing a short story.
<|det|>text [62, 286, 868, 472]<|/det|>- Decide on a situation—this could be a problem that has to be resolved by the main character or a set of characters. This situation can depend on the title or theme that you are given. The plot is the most important aspect of a short story. Decide the main events and try to think of a twist at the end. Your story must have a beginning, a middle and a satisfactory ending. Everything could end unhappily for all the characters, but at least the reader must know what happened to the people in the story.
<|det|>text [62, 484, 862, 601]<|/det|>- Choose a narrative point of view. You can write your story in the first person (as if you were one of the characters), or in the third person (as a detached narrator who presents the thoughts and observations of several characters). If you write in the first person, be consistent about the use of the pronoun 'I'.
<|det|>text [58, 614, 844, 660]<|/det|>- Create a list of characters. Try to give them individual characteristics. Focus more on the main character.
<|det|>text [58, 675, 836, 795]<|/det|>- Establish believable characters and settings, with vivid descriptions and dialogues, to create a story that your readers will care about. Remember that you have to work within a limited number of words; you cannot go on for pages, so you must use language effectively. Appeal to your readers' five senses to make your settings more real.
<|det|>text [53, 807, 851, 904]<|/det|>- Never underestimate the power of dialogue in revealing the character of a person or the complexity of a situation, but in a short story, there is no place for long speeches. Every word you put into the mouth of your characters must contribute to revealing your theme.
<|det|>page_number [799, 937, 843, 956]<|/det|>185
<PAGE><|det|>title [80, 38, 127, 64]<|/det|>A.
<|det|>title [168, 41, 816, 65]<|/det|>Write original short stories based on the given instructions.
<|det|>list [169, 74, 870, 258]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [170, 75, 852, 163]<|/det|>1. Begin with—'It was a stormy night. The rain was pouring down heavily and the wind was so strong that many trees were uprooted. I was returning home after visiting Vivek when...'
<|det|>text [169, 166, 740, 190]<|/det|>2. End with—'I shall always cherish that memory.'
<|det|>text [169, 190, 871, 235]<|/det|>3. Begin with—'The water was cold, I took a deep breath and jumped...'
<|det|>text [168, 235, 855, 259]<|/det|>4. End with—'...where there is a will, there is always a way.'
<|det|>title [100, 290, 708, 315]<|/det|>B. Write original short stories using these guidelines.
<|det|>list [169, 324, 822, 644]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [170, 324, 817, 370]<|/det|>1. in which a briefcase, a camera and a policeman play a significant part
<|det|>text [169, 370, 817, 394]<|/det|>2. in which one of the following plays a significant part—
<|det|>list [241, 395, 468, 460]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [241, 395, 384, 416]<|/det|>- a birthday
<|det|>text [241, 417, 467, 438]<|/det|>• a family argument
<|det|>text [241, 440, 403, 460]<|/det|>- a power cut
<|det|>text [170, 462, 840, 506]<|/det|>3. You are being followed; you manage to get away or were caught by your pursuers.
<|det|>text [169, 507, 895, 597]<|/det|>4. 'When the door of happiness closes, another opens; but often we look so long at the closed door that we do not see the one which has opened for us.'—Helen Keller. Write a short story using the above quotation as its theme.
<|det|>text [169, 598, 886, 644]<|/det|>5. to bring out clearly the meaning of this proverb—'Honesty is the best policy.'
<|det|>page_number [120, 927, 161, 946]<|/det|>186
<PAGE><|det|>header [45, 39, 301, 82]<|/det|>vocabulary
<|det|>header [679, 0, 955, 134]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>title [261, 133, 641, 165]<|/det|>Words Easily Confused
<|det|>text [55, 178, 834, 226]<|/det|>Read these sentences to understand the difference in meaning between these words that are often confused with each other.
<|det|>list [63, 235, 805, 707]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [63, 235, 796, 261]<|/det|>• adoptive: Mr and Mrs Chaki are the adoptive parents of Rakesh.
<|det|>text [64, 263, 760, 289]<|/det|>- adopted: Rakesh was adopted at birth by Mr and Mrs Chaki.
<|det|>text [64, 300, 785, 325]<|/det|>• adverse: The flight took off despite adverse weather conditions.
<|det|>text [65, 328, 767, 352]<|/det|>• averse: Mahatma Gandhi was averse to all forms of violence.
<|det|>text [65, 365, 706, 390]<|/det|>• advice: You should listen to the advice of your teachers.
<|det|>text [66, 392, 686, 417]<|/det|>• advise: I advise you to practise these maths problems.
<|det|>text [66, 428, 844, 478]<|/det|>- allusion: I saw a movie recently that had a clear allusion to the story of the great Indian epic, The Mahabharata.
<|det|>text [67, 480, 841, 530]<|/det|>- illusion: It looked as though the magician was sawing the woman in half, but it was actually just a clever illusion.
<|det|>text [67, 543, 777, 566]<|/det|>• altar: The Aztecs used to offer terrible sacrifices on their altars.
<|det|>text [67, 569, 825, 592]<|/det|>• alter: I cannot wear this dress unless I alter it, it is too short for me.
<|det|>text [67, 605, 767, 654]<|/det|>- award: The children who receive awards for bravery from the President are truly admirable.
<|det|>text [67, 657, 815, 707]<|/det|>- reward: Nita has lost her pet dog and is offering a huge reward to anyone who can find him.
<|det|>text [65, 720, 816, 770]<|/det|>- canvas: The artist destroyed the canvas because he was not happy with the painting he had made.
<|det|>text [64, 773, 775, 821]<|/det|>- canvass: Politicians have to canvass for many votes before the elections.
<|det|>text [63, 836, 816, 861]<|/det|>- defuse: It takes a lot of knowledge and courage to defuse a bomb.
<|det|>text [64, 863, 808, 911]<|/det|>- diffuse: The rich smell of chocolate began to diffuse through the room as the cake was baked in the oven.
<|det|>page_number [787, 927, 829, 946]<|/det|>187
<PAGE><|det|>text [133, 26, 862, 128]<|/det|>- draft: Each class had to submit a draft of their script to the judges who would select the best play for the inter-school competition.
<|det|>text [133, 129, 862, 180]<|/det|>- draught: There was a cold draught coming through a crack in the window all night, and in the morning I had caught a cold.
<|det|>text [133, 195, 808, 247]<|/det|>- imply: The speaker in the debate attempted to imply that the opposition was lying.
<|det|>text [133, 248, 905, 298]<|/det|>- infer: We could infer that our class teacher was annoyed by the cool tone of her voice.
<|det|>text [133, 315, 898, 365]<|/det|>- incidence: There has been an increasing incidence of crimes in that part of town over the last three months.
<|det|>text [133, 368, 878, 422]<|/det|>- incidents: The minister's speech was disrupted by three separate incidents in which the members of the Opposition started shouting.
<|det|>text [133, 437, 867, 488]<|/det|>- incite: The naughty girl tried to incite her classmates to be rowdy, but they refused to listen to her.
<|det|>text [133, 491, 890, 543]<|/det|>- insight: I read a fascinating book that gave me an insight into life in my grandparents' time.
<|det|>text [133, 558, 870, 608]<|/det|>- insure: You should insure your car against theft with an insurance policy.
<|det|>text [133, 610, 893, 658]<|/det|>- ensure: Including air bags in cars will ensure that you are protected if your car crashes.
<|det|>text [133, 675, 838, 722]<|/det|>- precede: The paragraph you are looking for is in the preceding chapter of that book.
<|det|>text [133, 726, 815, 775]<|/det|>- proceed: After you solve all the sums in the section, you can proceed to the next chapter.
<|det|>text [133, 792, 838, 840]<|/det|>- regretful: Ronita was regretful when her parents explained the consequences of her misdeeds to her.
<|det|>text [133, 844, 888, 890]<|/det|>- regrettable: It is regrettable that Aishwarya does not learn from her mistakes.
<|det|>page_number [133, 924, 177, 944]<|/det|>188
<PAGE><|det|>title [198, 44, 731, 78]<|/det|>Words Followed by Prepositions
<|det|>title [73, 98, 147, 117]<|/det|>nouns
<|det|>text [89, 117, 741, 143]<|/det|>• The following nouns take the preposition for after them—
<|det|>table [126, 147, 740, 453]<|/det|><table><tr><td>affection</td><td>craving</td><td>prediction</td></tr><tr><td>aptitude</td><td>cure</td><td>preference</td></tr><tr><td>anxiety</td><td>desire</td><td>payment</td></tr><tr><td>apology</td><td>excuse</td><td>penalty</td></tr><tr><td>appetite</td><td>fondness</td><td>pity</td></tr><tr><td>bid</td><td>freedom</td><td>pretext</td></tr><tr><td>blame</td><td>hatred</td><td>regard</td></tr><tr><td>candidate</td><td>longing</td><td>remorse</td></tr><tr><td>capacity</td><td>love</td><td>search</td></tr><tr><td>cause</td><td>opportunity</td><td>sympathy</td></tr><tr><td>compassion</td><td>pity</td><td>surety</td></tr><tr><td>compensation</td><td>passion</td><td>tolerance</td></tr><tr><td>concern</td><td>partiality</td><td>zeal</td></tr></table>
<|det|>text [98, 467, 761, 492]<|/det|>- The following nouns take the preposition from after them—
<|det|>table [137, 498, 745, 571]<|/det|><table><tr><td>abstinence</td><td>deliverance</td><td>escape</td></tr><tr><td>cessation</td><td>descent</td><td>exemption</td></tr><tr><td>freedom</td><td>digression</td><td>respite</td></tr></table>
<|det|>text [100, 581, 731, 606]<|/det|>- The following nouns take the preposition of after them—
<|det|>table [137, 621, 755, 783]<|/det|><table><tr><td>assurance</td><td>distrust</td><td>observance</td></tr><tr><td>abhorrence</td><td>experience</td><td>proof</td></tr><tr><td>care</td><td>fear</td><td>result</td></tr><tr><td>chance</td><td>grasp</td><td>slave</td></tr><tr><td>charge</td><td>lack</td><td>use</td></tr><tr><td>control</td><td>need</td><td>want</td></tr><tr><td>danger</td><td>neglect</td><td>victim</td></tr></table>
<|det|>text [98, 797, 731, 821]<|/det|>- The following nouns take the preposition to after them—

<|det|>text [128, 275, 812, 300]<|/det|>• The following nouns take the preposition with after them—

<|det|>table [152, 210, 812, 628]<|/det|><table><tr><td>accused</td><td>defrauded</td><td>innocent</td></tr><tr><td>acquitted</td><td>deprived</td><td>irrespective</td></tr><tr><td>afraid</td><td>desirous</td><td>made</td></tr><tr><td>apprehensive</td><td>destitute</td><td>negligent</td></tr><tr><td>apprised</td><td>devoid</td><td>productive</td></tr><tr><td>assured</td><td>diffident</td><td>proud</td></tr><tr><td>aware</td><td>distrustful</td><td>regardless</td></tr><tr><td>bereft</td><td>dull</td><td>sanguine</td></tr><tr><td>bought</td><td>easy</td><td>sensible</td></tr><tr><td>capable</td><td>envious</td><td>sick</td></tr><tr><td>cautious</td><td>fearful</td><td>slow</td></tr><tr><td>certain</td><td>fond</td><td>subservience</td></tr><tr><td>characteristic</td><td>greedy</td><td>sure</td></tr><tr><td>composed</td><td>guilty</td><td>suspicious</td></tr><tr><td>confident</td><td>heedless</td><td>tolerant</td></tr><tr><td>conscious</td><td>hopeful</td><td>vain</td></tr><tr><td>convicted</td><td>ignorant</td><td>weary</td></tr><tr><td>convinced</td><td>informed</td><td>worthy</td></tr></table>

<PAGE><|det|>title [283, 44, 685, 76]<|/det|>Idiomatic Expressions
<|det|>text [53, 98, 787, 166]<|/det|>An idiomatic expression is a group of words which when taken together imply a meaning that is different from the dictionary definitions of the individual words.
<|det|>table [58, 196, 918, 901]<|/det|><table><tr><td>apple of the eye</td><td>favourite</td></tr><tr><td>apple pie order</td><td>neat and tidy</td></tr><tr><td>at sea</td><td>unable to decide how to act</td></tr><tr><td>axe to grind</td><td>have a grievance against someone</td></tr><tr><td>back to square one</td><td>start from the beginning again</td></tr><tr><td>be in the same boat</td><td>face the same situation</td></tr><tr><td>be on cloud nine</td><td>very happy</td></tr><tr><td>blue-eyed boy</td><td>favourite</td></tr><tr><td>call a spade a spade</td><td>speak frankly without worrying about another person&#x27;s feelings</td></tr><tr><td>carry the day</td><td>win a battle</td></tr><tr><td>cast away</td><td>wrecked</td></tr><tr><td>deer in the headlights</td><td>caught offguard and need to make a decision but unable to do so</td></tr><tr><td>eat humble pie</td><td>apologise and show regret</td></tr><tr><td>get cold feet</td><td>feel anxious or apprehensive about something</td></tr><tr><td>green-eyed monster</td><td>jealousy</td></tr><tr><td>hold the purse strings</td><td>control the spending of money</td></tr><tr><td>in black and white</td><td>in writing</td></tr><tr><td>in the nick of time</td><td>at the last moment</td></tr><tr><td>once in a blue moon</td><td>very rarely</td></tr><tr><td>out of the blue</td><td>unexpectedly</td></tr></table>
<|det|>page_number [848, 933, 892, 950]<|/det|>195

<PAGE><|det|>title [396, 69, 592, 106]<|/det|>Preface
<|det|>text [82, 171, 885, 386]<|/det|>At its core, grammar is the set of rules in a language that changes the form of words and joins them into sentences. Grammar is now seen, not simply as theory, but as an instrument that enables language use. The Revised Cornerstone: Grammar and Composition Skills for 1-8 helps students to learn the nuances of a language which is not their mother tongue, and the rules of which therefore need to be reiterated for better retention. This series presents grammatical facts lucidly and informally, leads the student to assimilate them thoroughly, and more importantly, to apply them to communication in everyday life. In addition, this book provides young learners with ample opportunities to expand their dexterity in language use through the comprehension, composition and vocabulary components.
<|det|>text [82, 394, 885, 542]<|/det|>Each concept of grammar is introduced through detailed explanations and exercises which will help the students to grasp its functions. To make the learning of grammar an enjoyable process, we have introduced concepts through comic strips, stories and poems. This also acknowledges that grammar is not just a subject, the rules of which must merely be committed to memory, but also that which enhances language usage. The use of different stories, poems and informative pieces in exercises also ensures that children learn grammar in actual contexts of use.
<|det|>text [81, 549, 884, 812]<|/det|>The grammar chapters in these wonderfully illustrated books are interspersed with sections on vocabulary, composition, comprehension and conversation. The sections dealing with composition are designed to develop creative writing skills. To begin with, the learners are not only given the themes or subjects, but are also given suggestions along the lines of which the composition can be drafted. The comprehension passages test the literal and evaluative comprehension of the learners through an array of texts, fiction and non-fiction. The conversation sections begin with an example of a dialogue or a conversation on topics that are appropriate to the level of the class. Thereafter, topics are suggested around which further conversation practice can be carried out. This also ensures collaborative learning and group work. The vocabulary pages help students to extend their active and passive vocabulary by introducing them to new words through exercises and games. They are also encouraged to develop reference skills.
<|det|>text [80, 818, 884, 884]<|/det|>The teaching notes in the primary classes provide guidelines to both teachers and parents on how to introduce or revise concepts in a meaningful and interesting way and to carry out the activities that are given.
<|det|>text [80, 899, 286, 920]<|/det|>Rupdeha Sengupta
<|det|>text [83, 923, 217, 943]<|/det|>Nupur Ghosh

<PAGE><|det|>title [53, 52, 375, 85]<|/det|>The Sentence
<|det|>header [728, 0, 999, 123]<|/det|>1
<|det|>image [50, 142, 108, 188]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 141, 856, 188]<|/det|>You have learnt that a sentence is a group of words that makes complete sense. A sentence
<|det|>text [50, 190, 526, 278]<|/det|>always has at least one finite verb. A sentence starts with a capital letter and ends with a full stop, a question mark or an exclamation mark.
<|det|>title [51, 295, 350, 319]<|/det|>Subject and Predicate
<|det|>text [50, 325, 490, 371]<|/det|>A sentence consists of two parts—the subject and the predicate.
<|det|>image [564, 188, 825, 366]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [50, 385, 911, 430]<|/det|>In a sentence, the subject may be the person or thing that does the action or the one on whom the action is performed. For example,
<|det|>list [53, 431, 528, 636]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [53, 431, 490, 453]<|/det|>• The swimmer dived into the pool.
<|det|>text [54, 454, 440, 475]<|/det|>• The spacecraft was launched.
<|det|>text [51, 476, 526, 633]<|/det|>In the first sentence, the subject is the swimmer who does the action; and in the second sentence, the action is performed on the subject, that is, the spacecraft. The rest of the sentence, excluding the subject, is known as the predicate.
<|det|>image [565, 440, 825, 520]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [567, 525, 631, 548]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [718, 522, 776, 556]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [565, 560, 911, 685]<|/det|>To identify the subject of the sentence, we usually ask the question who or what before the verb. In the given examples, we can identify the subject by asking—
<|det|>list [567, 686, 911, 725]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [567, 686, 825, 705]<|/det|>• Who dived into the pool?
<|det|>text [567, 706, 789, 724]<|/det|>• What was launched?
<|det|>text [50, 661, 885, 708]<|/det|>The subject of the sentence is the part which denotes the person or the thing we are talking about.
<|det|>text [50, 740, 914, 788]<|/det|>The predicate is that part of the sentence which tells us about the subject. The predicate will always include the verb.
<|det|>text [50, 809, 911, 856]<|/det|>The subject usually comes at the beginning of a sentence and is followed by the predicate. However, there are exceptions.
<|det|>text [51, 864, 805, 888]<|/det|>◆ In lines of poetry, the subject might come after the predicate.
<|det|>text [98, 888, 414, 908]<|/det|>• Out of the sea came he!
<|det|>page_number [868, 920, 887, 938]<|/det|>1
<PAGE><|det|>text [106, 40, 740, 63]<|/det|>◆ In questions, the subject comes after the helping verb.
<|det|>text [146, 64, 421, 87]<|/det|>- Can I be of any help?
<|det|>text [106, 95, 898, 118]<|/det|>◆ In imperative sentences, the subject is often implied or understood.
<|det|>text [146, 120, 702, 142]<|/det|>- (You) Go home as soon as you finish this work.
<|det|>list [106, 40, 898, 142]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 158, 538, 319]<|/det|>The subject of a sentence may be one or several words. The word that is most important in such a phrase is then called the subject-word. Look at these sentences where the subjects have been highlighted and the subject-word underlined in each case.
<|det|>text [107, 322, 375, 342]<|/det|>• The weather was hot.
<|det|>text [107, 345, 540, 389]<|/det|>- The hot weather caused the death of many birds.
<|det|>text [107, 391, 676, 414]<|/det|>- The weather, being sultry, spoilt our picnic plans.
<|det|>list [107, 322, 676, 414]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 414, 851, 504]<|/det|>In the second and third sentences above, the subject-words have been qualified by an adjective (hot) and a group of words that acts as an adjective (being sultry), respectively. The is also considered to be an attribute.
<|det|>title [559, 159, 636, 183]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [725, 153, 787, 190]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [591, 198, 928, 240]<|/det|>The subject-word is always a noun, or a group of words that acts as a noun.
<|det|>text [592, 241, 704, 259]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [592, 261, 926, 301]<|/det|>- To err is human. (infinitive acting as noun)
<|det|>text [592, 303, 880, 345]<|/det|>- Walking is excellent exercise. (gerund acting as noun)
<|det|>list [591, 198, 928, 345]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 530, 851, 578]<|/det|>The adjective or the adjective phrase that qualifies the subject-word in a sentence is called the attribute.
<|det|>text [105, 618, 918, 709]<|/det|>A. Look at these excerpts from some famous poems. Identify the subject and the subject-word as well as its attribute in each sentence. Underline the predicates. Put a (/) where the subject has been omitted.
<|det|>text [154, 717, 577, 808]<|/det|>1. A crumb of cake would be a feast And last you seven days at least. A flea would be a frightening beast. If you were one inch tall.
<|det|>text [722, 785, 928, 807]<|/det|>(Shel Silverstein)
<|det|>text [154, 811, 558, 854]<|/det|>2. Out of the huts of history's shame I rise.
<|det|>text [191, 857, 577, 900]<|/det|>Up from a past that's rooted in pain I rise.
<|det|>text [727, 877, 926, 901]<|/det|>(Maya Angelou)
<|det|>page_number [142, 933, 161, 950]<|/det|>2
<PAGE><|det|>text [103, 54, 674, 98]<|/det|>3. Do not go gentle into that good night. Old age should burn and rave at close of day.
<|det|>text [140, 98, 614, 121]<|/det|>Rage, rage against the dying of the light.
<|det|>text [724, 98, 914, 121]<|/det|>(Dylan Thomas)
<|det|>text [103, 121, 588, 166]<|/det|>4. Where now is the horse and the rider? Where is the horn that was blowing?
<|det|>text [744, 143, 914, 166]<|/det|>(J R R Tolkien)
<|det|>image [53, 180, 108, 224]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 179, 516, 245]<|/det|>Like the subject, the predicate of a sentence is usually made up of more than one word.
<|det|>text [53, 262, 526, 350]<|/det|>Sometimes, the verb in a sentence may be qualified by an adverb or a group of words that works as an adverb. Look at these sentences where the verb has been
<|det|>text [53, 352, 903, 378]<|/det|>underlined and the qualifying word or group of words has been highlighted.
<|det|>text [55, 378, 258, 396]<|/det|>• Ravi runs fast.
<|det|>text [55, 398, 320, 421]<|/det|>• We ran all the way.
<|det|>list [55, 378, 320, 421]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [53, 433, 816, 478]<|/det|>An adverb or an adverb phrase that qualifies the verb is called the adverbial qualification.
<|det|>text [52, 491, 893, 560]<|/det|>You have learnt that a verb in a sentence may be used transitively or intransitively. When the verb is used transitively, it may take one or more objects.
<|det|>image [475, 570, 526, 609]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [72, 612, 907, 655]<|/det|>When a verb requires an object to receive its action, we say the verb is used transitively. A verb whose meaning is complete without an object is said to be used intransitively. For example,
<|det|>text [73, 655, 470, 674]<|/det|>• The little dog is chasing a rat. (transitive)
<|det|>text [73, 675, 605, 695]<|/det|>- The rat is running away as fast as possible. (intransitive)
<|det|>list [73, 655, 605, 695]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [51, 722, 629, 768]<|/det|>The person or thing that receives the action of the verb is called the object of the verb.
<|det|>text [51, 786, 336, 807]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>text [53, 809, 370, 831]<|/det|>• Sarita is reading a book.
<|det|>text [53, 832, 472, 853]<|/det|>• I love peanut butter sandwiches.
<|det|>text [53, 854, 575, 876]<|/det|>• Tara met her childhood friends after ages.
<|det|>list [53, 809, 575, 876]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [50, 877, 886, 923]<|/det|>In the above sentences, the verb has only one object—the direct object, which we identify by asking the question who/what after the verb.
<|det|>image [691, 737, 910, 860]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [864, 925, 882, 943]<|/det|>3
<PAGE><|det|>text [93, 40, 417, 60]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [93, 63, 697, 130]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 63, 576, 85]<|/det|>- Manisha is making tea for her guests.
<|det|>text [93, 86, 696, 108]<|/det|>- Akshay gave a beautiful pendant to his mother.
<|det|>text [93, 109, 696, 130]<|/det|>• The children received a letter from their father.
<|det|>text [90, 131, 938, 201]<|/det|>In the above sentences, the verbs take two objects—direct and indirect. The indirect object is identified by asking the question to/for/from + whom/what after the verb.
<|det|>text [89, 217, 957, 266]<|/det|>Intransitive verbs do not have objects, but sometimes they require a word or a group of words to complete their sense. Look at these examples.
<|det|>list [93, 268, 538, 315]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 268, 331, 289]<|/det|>- Mira is a doctor.
<|det|>text [93, 291, 538, 315]<|/det|>- Romit seems to be a studious boy.
<|det|>text [89, 315, 943, 387]<|/det|>The highlighted words in the above sentences are required to make the sentences complete and meaningful. They tell us something more about the subjects of the verbs.
<|det|>text [89, 402, 926, 472]<|/det|>A word or a group of words that is required to complete the sense of a verb, and that tells us something about the subject of the verb is called the subjective complement.
<|det|>text [89, 490, 926, 538]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences. Note that in the absence of the highlighted words, the sentences would sound incomplete.
<|det|>list [93, 539, 417, 584]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 539, 420, 561]<|/det|>- I appointed him captain.
<|det|>text [93, 562, 412, 584]<|/det|>• They proved me wrong.
<|det|>text [89, 584, 951, 631]<|/det|>Here, the words captain and wrong tell us something about the object of the verbs, namely him and me.
<|det|>text [88, 647, 945, 718]<|/det|>A word or a group of words that is required to complete the sense of the predicate and that tells us more about the object of the verb is called the objective complement.
<|det|>text [90, 746, 925, 840]<|/det|>Separate the subject and the predicate in these sentences. State whether the highlighted words and phrases act as attributes (A), adverbial qualifications (AQ), direct objects (O), indirect objects (I), subjective complements (SC) or objective complements (OC).
<|det|>list [139, 844, 524, 892]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 844, 503, 868]<|/det|>1. The capital seems worried.
<|det|>text [140, 868, 523, 892]<|/det|>2. The general kept us standing.
<|det|>page_number [126, 940, 143, 956]<|/det|>4
<PAGE><|det|>list [111, 40, 567, 450]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 40, 438, 62]<|/det|>3. My father is a journalist.
<|det|>text [113, 63, 406, 83]<|/det|>4. He became a doctor.
<|det|>text [113, 85, 435, 105]<|/det|>5. I found it in the drawer.
<|det|>text [113, 107, 468, 129]<|/det|>6. Susan gave me chocolates.
<|det|>text [113, 131, 500, 153]<|/det|>7. We saw the car approaching.
<|det|>text [113, 154, 506, 175]<|/det|>8. They appointed him director.
<|det|>text [113, 176, 557, 197]<|/det|>9. In the morning, the sky was clear.
<|det|>text [102, 198, 517, 219]<|/det|>10. The soldiers marched forward.
<|det|>text [102, 221, 512, 242]<|/det|>11. Would you like an ice cream?
<|det|>text [102, 244, 555, 266]<|/det|>12. The little children look energetic.
<|det|>text [102, 267, 743, 289]<|/det|>13. Rahim, a well-known artist, teaches her painting.
<|det|>text [102, 290, 562, 312]<|/det|>14. The judge pronounced him guilty.
<|det|>text [102, 313, 613, 334]<|/det|>15. I bought a new dress from the market.
<|det|>text [102, 336, 627, 357]<|/det|>16. This certainly makes the matter serious.
<|det|>text [102, 358, 692, 380]<|/det|>17. The owner of the car spoke in a gentle voice.
<|det|>text [102, 382, 741, 403]<|/det|>18. The man carrying a large bag entered the station.
<|det|>text [102, 404, 795, 425]<|/det|>19. The money from the bank was hidden under the bed.
<|det|>text [102, 427, 868, 449]<|/det|>20. The king, dressed in regal splendour, walked to the temple.
<|det|>title [62, 463, 325, 484]<|/det|>Kinds of Sentences
<|det|>image [64, 495, 118, 535]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 492, 916, 538]<|/det|>You have learnt that a sentence can be a statement, a question, a request, a command, a denial or a response.
<|det|>text [63, 554, 327, 575]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [65, 577, 432, 646]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [65, 577, 290, 599]<|/det|>• I like mangoes.
<|det|>text [65, 601, 294, 623]<|/det|>- It is not raining.
<|det|>text [65, 623, 431, 646]<|/det|>- Our pet dog is very playful.
<|det|>text [63, 647, 492, 669]<|/det|>All these sentences state something.
<|det|>text [61, 690, 434, 759]<|/det|>An assertive sentence makes a statement. It is also known as a declarative sentence.
<|det|>image [691, 522, 924, 660]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [571, 681, 641, 707]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [569, 720, 934, 765]<|/det|>An assertive sentence begins with a capital letter and ends with a full stop (.)
<|det|>text [61, 785, 951, 808]<|/det|>Assertive sentences are of two kinds—affirmative and negative sentences.
<|det|>text [63, 810, 357, 830]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [65, 833, 468, 880]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [65, 833, 320, 854]<|/det|>• The knife is blunt.
<|det|>text [65, 857, 372, 880]<|/det|>• The knife is not sharp.
<|det|>text [61, 880, 948, 928]<|/det|>The two sentences convey the same meaning, but one does so negatively, while the other states it positively.
<|det|>page_number [907, 930, 924, 946]<|/det|>5
<PAGE><|det|>text [100, 46, 928, 115]<|/det|>A sentence that tells us that something exists or something is happening is called an affirmative sentence. An affirmative sentence is also known as a positive sentence.
<|det|>text [99, 142, 597, 212]<|/det|>A sentence that states that something does not exist or that something is not happening is called a negative sentence.
<|det|>text [100, 232, 553, 255]<|/det|>Read these sentences that ask questions.
<|det|>list [101, 256, 521, 301]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 256, 403, 277]<|/det|>• Where is my umbrella?
<|det|>text [101, 279, 518, 301]<|/det|>• Has the gardener sown the seeds?
<|det|>title [98, 317, 565, 341]<|/det|>An interrogative sentence asks questions.
<|det|>text [97, 353, 921, 401]<|/det|>There are two kinds of interrogative sentences—yes or no questions and wh- questions.
<|det|>title [99, 416, 346, 435]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [101, 439, 470, 484]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 439, 312, 460]<|/det|>- Can you swim?
<|det|>text [101, 462, 470, 484]<|/det|>- Does your brother play tennis?
<|det|>text [591, 395, 655, 420]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [584, 430, 938, 471]<|/det|>An interrogative sentence always ends with a question mark (?).
<|det|>text [97, 501, 908, 547]<|/det|>When we ask these questions, we expect the answer to be yes or no, so they are called yes or no questions.
<|det|>text [97, 562, 935, 631]<|/det|>We use helping verbs like be, have, do, can, should, may or will to ask yes or no questions. In such questions, the helping verb comes before the subject and the main verb comes after the subject.
<|det|>text [99, 633, 251, 655]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [101, 656, 452, 702]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 656, 452, 678]<|/det|>- Can you read out this story?
<|det|>text [101, 679, 411, 702]<|/det|>• Are the Guptas coming?
<|det|>text [99, 717, 405, 738]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [101, 740, 530, 786]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 740, 464, 763]<|/det|>• Where did you go yesterday?
<|det|>text [101, 764, 530, 786]<|/det|>• What have you done to my phone?
<|det|>text [97, 802, 915, 850]<|/det|>Questions that begin with why, what, when, which, who, where, whom and how are called wh- questions.
<|det|>text [97, 864, 892, 909]<|/det|>Like yes or no questions, in wh- questions too, the helping verb comes before the subject and the main verb comes after the subject.
<|det|>page_number [136, 932, 153, 948]<|/det|>6
<PAGE><|det|>title [86, 69, 148, 94]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [80, 106, 710, 126]<|/det|>Sometimes, what, who or which may act as the subject of the sentence.
<|det|>text [82, 127, 197, 145]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [82, 146, 367, 165]<|/det|>• Who committed this mistake?
<|det|>text [82, 166, 304, 186]<|/det|>• Who left the lights on?
<|det|>list [82, 146, 367, 186]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [60, 209, 377, 230]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>text [61, 232, 300, 252]<|/det|>- Go away. (order)
<|det|>text [61, 254, 504, 275]<|/det|>- Please close.the window. (request)
<|det|>text [61, 277, 333, 299]<|/det|>• Study well. (advice)
<|det|>list [61, 232, 504, 299]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [55, 322, 525, 390]<|/det|>A sentence that gives a command or an order, makes a request or gives advice is called an imperative sentence.
<|det|>text [55, 413, 525, 501]<|/det|>In an imperative sentence, the subject is usually left out. The subject is understood to be you in most sentences. For example,
<|det|>text [61, 503, 404, 525]<|/det|>• (You) Apologise right now.
<|det|>text [61, 526, 275, 547]<|/det|>- (You) Go away.
<|det|>list [61, 503, 404, 547]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [55, 562, 375, 583]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>text [61, 584, 372, 607]<|/det|>• What a lovely painting!
<|det|>text [61, 607, 251, 626]<|/det|>• What a miss!
<|det|>list [61, 584, 372, 626]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [53, 647, 545, 694]<|/det|>A sentence that expresses strong feeling is called an exclamatory sentence.
<|det|>text [578, 325, 640, 351]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [714, 320, 764, 358]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [572, 363, 890, 422]<|/det|>Not all imperative sentences are necessarily commands. They can be social expressions. For example,
<|det|>text [576, 425, 720, 443]<|/det|>- Get well soon.
<|det|>text [576, 447, 745, 466]<|/det|>- Have a nice day.
<|det|>list [576, 425, 745, 466]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [651, 583, 715, 608]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [752, 580, 812, 616]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [646, 621, 871, 682]<|/det|>An exclamatory sentence always ends with an exclamation mark (!).
<|det|>image [37, 721, 88, 748]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 721, 892, 767]<|/det|>Rearrange the words and phrases to form meaningful sentences as directed in the brackets.
<|det|>text [106, 772, 797, 796]<|/det|>1. young / worried / the / man / how / looks (exclamatory)
<|det|>text [105, 796, 867, 843]<|/det|>2. how many / are there / do you know / in our lives / sources of stress (interrogative)
<|det|>list [105, 772, 867, 843]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [867, 923, 887, 940]<|/det|>7
<PAGE><|det|>list [147, 33, 951, 252]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [147, 33, 885, 80]<|/det|>3. are four / of stress / sources / our lives / there / in / common (assertive)
<|det|>text [146, 82, 951, 105]<|/det|>4. pressure / external / the / or internal / can be / first one (assertive)
<|det|>text [146, 107, 907, 152]<|/det|>5. cause / stress / can / goals and expectations / internal / unreal (interrogative)
<|det|>text [145, 153, 895, 200]<|/det|>6. due to / did you know / factors / takes place / that / outside / external stress (interrogative)
<|det|>text [145, 202, 785, 224]<|/det|>7. of / is / another / anxiety / source / stress (assertive)
<|det|>text [145, 226, 938, 251]<|/det|>8. panic / goes wrong / when / do not / any little thing (imperative)
<|det|>title [94, 270, 489, 291]<|/det|>Transformation of Sentences
<|det|>image [94, 302, 160, 350]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [168, 300, 951, 371]<|/det|>You have learnt that it is possible to change the form of a sentence without entirely changing the meaning. Look at these pairs of sentences that convey the same meaning.
<|det|>list [174, 373, 770, 512]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 373, 655, 418]<|/det|>- How sweet the song is! (exclamatory)
The song is very sweet. (assertive)
<|det|>text [174, 419, 769, 464]<|/det|>- Aren't elephants very intelligent? (interrogative)
Elephants are very intelligent. (assertive)
<|det|>text [174, 465, 688, 511]<|/det|>- He did the work. (affirmative)
He did not fail to do the work. (negative)
<|det|>image [65, 535, 136, 580]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [139, 535, 571, 558]<|/det|>Change these sentences as directed.
<|det|>list [150, 562, 831, 911]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [157, 565, 552, 587]<|/det|>1. I remained quiet. (to negative)
<|det|>text [156, 589, 570, 611]<|/det|>2. What a curt reply! (to assertive)
<|det|>text [156, 612, 628, 635]<|/det|>3. I will not reach early. (to affirmative)
<|det|>text [156, 636, 645, 658]<|/det|>4. No man is immortal. (to interrogative)
<|det|>text [156, 659, 649, 681]<|/det|>5. It is a very cold night. (to exclamatory)
<|det|>text [156, 682, 649, 703]<|/det|>6. Who does not know this? (to assertive)
<|det|>text [156, 704, 598, 726]<|/det|>7. It is a good idea. (to interrogative)
<|det|>text [156, 727, 694, 749]<|/det|>8. He rejected all our proposals. (to negative)
<|det|>text [156, 750, 717, 772]<|/det|>9. To think we should meet again! (to assertive)
<|det|>text [148, 773, 691, 795]<|/det|>10. You should not break rules. (to imperative)
<|det|>text [148, 796, 694, 818]<|/det|>11. I wish I had come sooner. (to exclamatory)
<|det|>text [148, 819, 770, 841]<|/det|>12. Why worry about such small issues? (to assertive)
<|det|>text [148, 842, 825, 864]<|/det|>13. That was not the correct thing to say. (to interrogative)
<|det|>text [148, 865, 830, 887]<|/det|>14. As soon as he saw the policeman, he ran. (to negative)
<|det|>text [148, 888, 801, 910]<|/det|>15. Was it for this that we worked so hard? (to assertive)
<|det|>page_number [129, 937, 147, 956]<|/det|>8
<PAGE><|det|>text [102, 58, 842, 79]<|/det|>16. You are instructed to sit down immediately. (to imperative)
<|det|>text [102, 80, 857, 102]<|/det|>17. There was no one present who did not reply. (to affirmative)
<|det|>text [102, 102, 829, 145]<|/det|>18. In the opposition too, the orthodox fought against the bill. (to negative)
<|det|>text [102, 146, 805, 188]<|/det|>19. Was there ever anything so exquisitely beautiful before? (to assertive)
<|det|>text [101, 190, 722, 235]<|/det|>20. I shall never forget those glorious days in school.
(to interrogative)
<|det|>text [101, 236, 911, 280]<|/det|>21. You must solve all the sums in this exercise before turning on the television. (to negative)
<|det|>text [101, 281, 821, 302]<|/det|>22. You must not run or shout in the corridor. (to imperative)
<|det|>text [101, 304, 797, 325]<|/det|>23. This carpet is an exquisite work of art. (to exclamatory)
<|det|>text [101, 326, 830, 347]<|/det|>24. Who thought it would rain so heavily today? (to assertive)
<|det|>text [101, 348, 876, 370]<|/det|>25. The minister whispered in the emperor's ear. (to interrogative)
<|det|>image [189, 426, 773, 888]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [864, 920, 882, 937]<|/det|>9
<PAGE><|det|>image [19, 0, 303, 142]<|/det|>
<|det|>header [560, 40, 978, 80]<|/det|>Wedding Dinners
<|det|>text [91, 147, 948, 218]<|/det|>What are the things that amuse you at wedding dinners? What are the things that put you off on such occasions? Read how the author feels about wedding dinners.
<|det|>text [90, 232, 974, 444]<|/det|>And what a change! Have you noticed something about wedding dinners—buffet, of course—these days? They are a strangely quiet affair. The orderly queue of the guests leading up to the tables where the food has been neatly laid out, the silent, efficient service by the catering staff, the murmur of 'Yes, please' or 'No, thank you' in the way of response, and the ever-so-soft conversation thereafter to the equally soft accompaniment of crockery coming into delicate contact with cutlery. A clinical setting almost—the spotless white gloves worn by the catering staff contributing their special touch. For scalpel, read serving spoon.
<|det|>text [90, 457, 971, 738]<|/det|>Compare this with the bedlam at wedding dinner-time at an earlier point in history. The maddening, yet so delightful row that went on as one lot of diners finished eating and the next lot moved in. The full-throated cries of 'Now, bring the daal', 'What happened to the fried brinjal?' and 'Serve the mutton curry and pulao the second time around', punctuated with the personal entreaties by the hosts. These made a world of difference to the meals. For the people who served you were not professionals, jaded and tired of routine. Those who supervised your meal in those dear old days were almost always relatives and family friends. They knew your likes and dislikes, perhaps even a fair bit about your health problems. 'Okay, forget the mutton, but do have another piece of fish.' You can't possibly expect the caterer's men to know that red meat does you no good.
<|det|>title [147, 761, 661, 785]<|/det|>Answer these questions in about 75 words.
<|det|>list [146, 790, 940, 928]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [146, 790, 944, 836]<|/det|>1. What is the one 'change' that the author notices about wedding dinners these days?
<|det|>text [145, 837, 888, 882]<|/det|>2. Quote a sentence to show that the author enjoyed wedding dinners in earlier times.
<|det|>text [145, 884, 937, 928]<|/det|>3. Mention three things that the author says about the people who served us earlier.
<|det|>page_number [127, 936, 156, 954]<|/det|>10
<PAGE><|det|>text [62, 57, 628, 79]<|/det|>B. Find words from the passage which mean—
<|det|>text [113, 84, 201, 104]<|/det|>1. line
<|det|>text [422, 85, 567, 104]<|/det|>2. scientific
<|det|>text [726, 86, 874, 104]<|/det|>3. confusion
<|det|>text [111, 107, 341, 129]<|/det|>4. earnest requests
<|det|>text [424, 107, 640, 127]<|/det|>5. tired and bored
<|det|>text [726, 108, 917, 127]<|/det|>6. watched over
<|det|>text [62, 150, 914, 196]<|/det|>C. In 60 words, state the difference between wedding dinners in these days and those that took place earlier. Give a title to your summary.
<|det|>image [175, 254, 831, 611]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [856, 919, 886, 937]<|/det|>11
<PAGE><|det|>image [26, 0, 312, 142]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [808, 42, 962, 80]<|/det|>Nouns
<|det|>text [100, 149, 923, 196]<|/det|>A noun is the name of a person, a place, an animal, a feeling or an idea.
<|det|>title [103, 214, 296, 236]<|/det|>Kinds of Noun
<|det|>text [102, 244, 555, 266]<|/det|>Nouns can be classified into five kinds.
<|det|>text [100, 293, 554, 390]<|/det|>The name of a particular person, place, monument or building is called a proper noun. For example, Florence Nightingale, Mumbai, Howrah Bridge.
<|det|>title [612, 277, 678, 304]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [768, 270, 824, 310]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [606, 318, 948, 380]<|/det|>Proper nouns always begin with a capital letter. We do not use a, an or the before proper nouns.
<|det|>text [100, 421, 935, 469]<|/det|>The name of a person, place, or building in general is called a common noun. For example, man, river, monument.
<|det|>text [99, 501, 871, 550]<|/det|>The name used for a collection of people, animals, birds or objects is called a collective noun. For example, flock, herd, family.
<|det|>text [99, 580, 893, 629]<|/det|>The name of an idea, a concept, a state of being or a feeling is called an abstract noun. For example, beauty, hatred, trust.
<|det|>text [99, 659, 915, 708]<|/det|>The name of the matter or substance of which a thing is made is called a material noun. For example, silver, wood, paper.
<|det|>text [99, 742, 842, 790]<|/det|>A. Underline the nouns in these sentences and put them in the appropriate columns in the table provided on the next page.

Quietness and value—the ____ applied to both. With that ____ on his watch Jim might be properly anxious about the ____ in any company. Grand as the gold ____ was, he sometimes

<|det|>title [54, 139, 156, 160]<|/det|>Gender
<|det|>image [54, 168, 108, 210]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 178, 667, 202]<|/det|>There are four genders in the English language.
<|det|>text [51, 220, 532, 287]<|/det|>Nouns are said to be in the masculine gender if they refer to males. For example, grandfather, uncle, tiger, peacock.
<|det|>text [51, 310, 554, 377]<|/det|>Nouns are said to be in the feminine gender if they refer to females. For example, grandmother, aunt, tigress, peahen.
<|det|>image [584, 220, 648, 264]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [590, 264, 892, 361]<|/det|>Words of the feminine gender like actress or poetess are used less often these days. Instead, the usage of nouns of the common gender is preferred.
<|det|>text [65, 398, 898, 443]<|/det|>Nouns of the common gender may be of the masculine or the feminine gender. For example, parent, leader, cousin, child, doctor.
<|det|>title [80, 480, 141, 503]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [70, 516, 350, 556]<|/det|>Nouns of the neuter gender are usually non-living things.
<|det|>text [428, 474, 848, 562]<|/det|>Nouns of the neuter gender are neither of the masculine gender nor the feminine gender. For example, table, book, torch, pen.
<|det|>image [187, 474, 248, 512]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [50, 588, 908, 634]<|/det|>D. Rewrite the passage changing all nouns in the masculine gender into the feminine and vice-versa. Make other necessary changes.
<|det|>text [96, 649, 910, 848]<|/det|>The boy walked towards the market. He entered a pet shop. He asked the owner, 'Sir, do you sell ducks?' The owner showed him a few. The boy also wanted to buy a colt. However, the shop only sold small animals. The boy then decided to visit his aunt who had a large farm. She owned a bullock, two horses and had even got a doe recently. She was a noted authoress and had married a professor. She was also an advisor to a viscount who had settled in Almora many years ago. The gentleman was loved by all. His aunt trusted him implicitly and made him the executor of her will.
<|det|>page_number [851, 909, 882, 927]<|/det|>15
<PAGE><|det|>header [108, 34, 224, 54]<|/det|>Number
<|det|>image [108, 66, 177, 113]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 77, 963, 105]<|/det|>In English, we say a noun may be either singular or plural in numbe.
<|det|>text [108, 129, 845, 178]<|/det|>The singular form of a noun denotes one person, place or thing. For example, chair, torch, knife.
<|det|>text [108, 204, 951, 253]<|/det|>The plural form of a noun denotes more than one person, place or thing. For example, chairs, torches, knives.
<|det|>title [130, 299, 195, 325]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [515, 293, 574, 333]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [126, 341, 636, 361]<|/det|>Nouns can also be classified as countable or uncountable.
<|det|>list [122, 363, 955, 490]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [122, 363, 955, 424]<|/det|>- A countable noun denotes that which can be counted. For example, we can say, one girl, ten boys, two apples. We usually add one of the articles (a, an or the) or a number before countable nouns.
<|det|>text [122, 426, 951, 490]<|/det|>- An uncountable noun refers to things that cannot be counted. For example, news, tea, salt, glass. We do not use a or an with uncountable nouns. However, we can say, a cup of tea or a pinch of salt.
<|det|>title [108, 530, 766, 553]<|/det|>E. Circle the correct words to complete these sentences.
<|det|>list [169, 559, 951, 888]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [171, 560, 514, 581]<|/det|>1. Politics is/are in his blood.
<|det|>text [170, 584, 585, 605]<|/det|>2. My trousers/trouser are too tight.
<|det|>text [170, 607, 643, 629]<|/det|>3. I bought three dozens/dozen mangoes.
<|det|>text [170, 630, 612, 652]<|/det|>4. She gave an alm/alms to the beggar.
<|det|>text [171, 653, 615, 674]<|/det|>5. I have a new pair of scissors/scissor.
<|det|>text [170, 675, 858, 698]<|/det|>6. He has learnt the English alphabet/alphabets very quickly.
<|det|>text [171, 699, 800, 721]<|/det|>7. Some staff/staffs from the office will accompany you.
<|det|>text [170, 722, 883, 744]<|/det|>8. I need a lot of information/informations regarding this tribe.
<|det|>text [170, 745, 951, 790]<|/det|>9. Many leading member/members of the opposition have/has tried to justify this.
<|det|>text [160, 791, 834, 833]<|/det|>10. We must buy appropriate clothing/clothings for our high altitude trek.
<|det|>text [160, 835, 708, 856]<|/det|>11. I have an important piece of new/news for you.
<|det|>text [160, 857, 709, 877]<|/det|>12. We are buying some furnitures/furniture today.
<|det|>text [160, 878, 680, 898]<|/det|>13. Would you like to have some coffee/coffees.

<|det|>text [125, 284, 878, 305]<|/det|>4. We grow different kinds of ____ in our orchard.
<|det|>text [126, 306, 837, 326]<|/det|>5. He has shown remarkable ____ in his work.
