<PAGE><|det|>header [19, 15, 260, 134]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [517, 31, 978, 69]<|/det|>Paragraph Writing
<|det|>text [65, 135, 960, 184]<|/det|>A paragraph is a piece of writing that consists of several sentences that present and develop one main idea, theme or subject.
<|det|>text [80, 199, 625, 223]<|/det|>The elements of a paragraph are listed below.
<|det|>list [81, 222, 978, 460]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [81, 222, 978, 290]<|/det|>- Topic sentence: This is the first sentence of the paragraph. It introduces the basic idea of the paragraph and motivates the reader to want to read more.
<|det|>text [81, 292, 940, 388]<|/det|>- Supporting sentences: These form the body of the paragraph. They prove, explain, develop or support the topic sentence. They give more information about the main idea through examples and strong, convincing points.
<|det|>text [81, 388, 963, 459]<|/det|>- Closing sentence: This restates the topic sentence in different words. It is the last sentence of the paragraph and clearly indicates the end of the paragraph.
<|det|>text [79, 475, 962, 520]<|/det|>Now, write a paragraph of about 100 words for each of these situations/clues.
<|det|>text [80, 532, 952, 626]<|/det|>1. From the sentences given below, identify the topic sentence, first supporting sentence, second supporting sentence, third supporting sentence and the closing sentence. Then, expand and rewrite them as a cohesive paragraph.
<|det|>list [119, 627, 717, 744]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 627, 531, 648]<|/det|>• Let me tell you more about her.
<|det|>text [120, 651, 455, 672]<|/det|>• She lives near my house.
<|det|>text [120, 673, 717, 695]<|/det|>• I think she is the greatest friend I have ever had.
<|det|>text [121, 698, 457, 720]<|/det|>• My best friend is Gayatri.
<|det|>text [120, 722, 506, 743]<|/det|>• We always have fun together.
<|det|>text [77, 744, 669, 768]<|/det|>2. Write a paragraph with the help of these clues.
<|det|>text [114, 769, 945, 840]<|/det|>museum...deserted...burglars...look into...Egyptian section...eyes of the Sphinx...glowed...in the dark...sound of siren...sudden crash...
a narrow escape...investigation...nothing found missing
<|det|>page_number [109, 931, 140, 949]<|/det|>18
<PAGE><|det|>text [57, 54, 840, 78]<|/det|>3. Study the word web and then write it in the form of a paragraph.
<|det|>image [107, 116, 955, 380]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [46, 411, 862, 456]<|/det|>4. Imagine you are inside a whale's stomach. Write a paragraph titled 'Inside a Whale', elaborating the following points.
<|det|>list [89, 457, 731, 544]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [90, 457, 420, 478]<|/det|>• how did you reach there
<|det|>text [90, 480, 437, 501]<|/det|>• description of the location
<|det|>text [90, 503, 282, 525]<|/det|>- your feelings
<|det|>text [89, 525, 731, 546]<|/det|>• what happened next...your rescue/escape (optional)
<|det|>text [46, 546, 848, 590]<|/det|>5. Write short paragraphs agreeing or disagreeing with each of these statements.
<|det|>list [85, 591, 631, 682]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 591, 631, 613]<|/det|>a. Teenagers ought to be given more freedom.
<|det|>text [86, 614, 486, 636]<|/det|>b. Money cannot buy everything.
<|det|>text [86, 637, 537, 658]<|/det|>c. Today's media is a responsible one.
<|det|>text [86, 659, 487, 681]<|/det|>d. Technology is killing creativity.
<|det|>text [85, 681, 845, 704]<|/det|>You may have a classroom discussion prior to the writing activity.
<|det|>text [44, 704, 898, 751]<|/det|>6. Write a paragraph beginning with, 'I jumped. The water was cold and freezing...'
<|det|>page_number [848, 917, 880, 935]<|/det|>19
<PAGE><|det|>title [102, 46, 143, 83]<|/det|>3
<|det|>header [730, 45, 969, 76]<|/det|>Pronouns
<|det|>image [100, 150, 160, 196]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 150, 699, 194]<|/det|>A pronoun is a word that is used instead of a noun. Look at these sentences.
<|det|>text [100, 197, 704, 288]<|/det|>- Jaideep is a very hardworking boy. He is good at studies as well as in sports. His brothers admire him. Both of them would like to follow in his footsteps.
<|det|>text [95, 288, 726, 334]<|/det|>Here, the words he and him take the place of Jaideep, while them is used instead of his brothers.
<|det|>image [749, 156, 960, 386]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [96, 356, 616, 402]<|/det|>The noun which is replaced by a pronoun is known as its antecedent.
<|det|>text [95, 432, 874, 456]<|/det|>Pronouns are of different kinds. Let us revise what you have learnt.
<|det|>list [98, 458, 312, 569]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 458, 245, 480]<|/det|>▶ personal
<|det|>text [98, 481, 307, 501]<|/det|>◆ demonstrative
<|det|>text [98, 503, 244, 523]<|/det|>◆ reflexive
<|det|>text [98, 526, 271, 546]<|/det|>◆ distributive
<|det|>text [98, 549, 254, 569]<|/det|>◆ indefinite
<|det|>list [531, 458, 721, 569]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [531, 458, 697, 479]<|/det|>◆ possessive
<|det|>text [532, 481, 720, 502]<|/det|>◆ interrogative
<|det|>text [532, 504, 720, 525]<|/det|>◆ emphasising
<|det|>text [532, 527, 692, 548]<|/det|>reciprocal
<|det|>text [532, 549, 666, 568]<|/det|>relative
<|det|>text [95, 602, 641, 697]<|/det|>The pronouns I, me, she, her, he, him, you, we, us, it, they and them come in the place of people, animals or things. So they are called personal pronouns.
<|det|>image [735, 156, 960, 386]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [709, 552, 958, 678]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [95, 725, 252, 748]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [97, 748, 951, 797]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [97, 748, 951, 794]<|/det|>- These boys are lazy. Their teacher has given them a lot of homework, but they are not interested in completing it.
<|det|>text [94, 795, 902, 842]<|/det|>In the above sentences, the pronoun it replaces the noun homework while these boys is the antecedent for the pronouns they and them.
<|det|>text [94, 851, 931, 900]<|/det|>Note that we use they when the pronoun is the subject of the verb and them when it acts as the object.
<|det|>page_number [126, 932, 159, 949]<|/det|>20
<PAGE><|det|>text [48, 65, 842, 111]<|/det|>The pronouns mine, ours, his, hers, its, theirs and yours are used to show possession. So they are called possessive pronouns.
<|det|>title [49, 125, 206, 147]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [49, 148, 720, 191]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [50, 148, 720, 169]<|/det|>• Jayshree has a small zoo in her house. That cat is hers.
<|det|>text [51, 170, 447, 191]<|/det|>• The decision is yours to make.
<|det|>title [48, 209, 851, 233]<|/det|>A. Fill in the blanks with suitable personal or possessive pronouns.
<|det|>list [113, 243, 896, 692]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 243, 565, 265]<|/det|>1. I am taller than ____ is.
<|det|>text [113, 266, 701, 288]<|/det|>2. He is as lazy as that brother of ____.
<|det|>text [113, 289, 705, 311]<|/det|>3. They are far better players than ____.
<|det|>text [113, 312, 692, 333]<|/det|>4. My sister's hair is longer than ____.
<|det|>text [113, 334, 650, 375]<|/det|>5. None as blind as ____ that will not see.
<|det|>text [113, 378, 704, 423]<|/det|>6. Rohit has found a diary of ____ that ____ had thought was lost.
<|det|>text [113, 424, 631, 466]<|/det|>7. I am sure that nobody will help you but ____.
<|det|>text [113, 468, 831, 489]<|/det|>8. She knows her responsibilities as well as ____.
<|det|>text [113, 490, 895, 533]<|/det|>9. My father is furious with ____. I have lost a favourite book of ____.
<|det|>text [100, 535, 836, 601]<|/det|>10. "Who did this?" Mother asked.
'It was ____. But the idea was ____,"
replied Akash.
<|det|>text [99, 602, 903, 647]<|/det|>11. Reeta is very happy; the essay that won the competition is ____ ____ ____ is a wonderful piece of writing.
<|det|>text [99, 647, 890, 691]<|/det|>12. Mr Rathore is a nominee for the elections. Between you and ____, ____ is a truly deserving candidate.
<|det|>image [50, 700, 108, 743]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [119, 710, 376, 731]<|/det|>Look at this sentence.
<|det|>text [73, 746, 460, 793]<|/det|>• This is my book and those are Rajeev's pens.
<|det|>text [47, 812, 499, 879]<|/det|>Pronouns that are used to point out the objects to which they refer are called demonstrative pronouns.
<|det|>title [572, 715, 640, 743]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [711, 711, 764, 745]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [568, 754, 877, 875]<|/det|>We use this and these to indicate objects that are nearby while that and those are used for objects that are far away. A less commonly used demonstrative pronoun is yonder.
<|det|>page_number [848, 920, 877, 938]<|/det|>21
<PAGE><|det|>text [100, 45, 428, 66]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [101, 67, 510, 136]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 67, 425, 89]<|/det|>• Whom did you call up?
<|det|>text [101, 90, 509, 113]<|/det|>• Which of these boxes is yours?
<|det|>text [101, 113, 495, 136]<|/det|>• What is the name of that girl?
<|det|>text [99, 153, 526, 201]<|/det|>Pronouns that help to ask questions are called interrogative pronouns.
<|det|>image [631, 46, 936, 212]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [98, 235, 892, 283]<|/det|>B. Fill in the blanks with suitable demonstrative or interrogative pronouns.
<|det|>text [153, 296, 938, 342]<|/det|>At dawn, we started to climb the mountain road. '____ is an ancient road. It leads to a monastery and a temple.
<|det|>text [153, 342, 940, 391]<|/det|>____ were built about two hundred years ago,' said my guide Kabu, pointing to some low huts by the roadside.
<|det|>text [153, 405, 933, 450]<|/det|>On either side of the narrow track grew many unfamiliar flowers. '____ are these flowers called?' I asked.
<|det|>text [153, 466, 960, 514]<|/det|>____ are orchids and ____ in the distance are rhododendrons,' said a local youth who had joined us.
<|det|>text [153, 527, 898, 575]<|/det|>____ is proving to be an even better trek than I had expected,' I said to my friend.
<|det|>text [152, 589, 972, 636]<|/det|>'Yes, indeed,' he replied. '____ is that fellow racing up at a great pace while we're panting behind him?'
<|det|>text [152, 650, 898, 674]<|/det|>I looked at the man in front wondering at his incredible speed.
<|det|>image [58, 696, 353, 897]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [378, 698, 812, 721]<|/det|>Suddenly, there was a sudden shout.
<|det|>text [377, 727, 960, 841]<|/det|>'Look at ____,' said my guide pointing at a distant peak. '____ lies Mount Everest. In our country, we also call it Sagarmatha. ____ of you would like to try to climb that peak?'
<|det|>text [377, 856, 970, 902]<|/det|>We looked at each other and shook our heads. ____ seemed like an impossible dream.
<|det|>page_number [127, 933, 161, 951]<|/det|>22
<PAGE><|det|>header [52, 48, 108, 93]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [129, 56, 416, 76]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [53, 94, 556, 163]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [53, 94, 463, 116]<|/det|>• I poured myself a cup of coffee.
<|det|>text [86, 117, 556, 139]<|/det|>I, myself am to blame for the confusion.
<|det|>text [53, 141, 433, 163]<|/det|>• Priya loveshoreing to herself.
<|det|>text [85, 164, 744, 185]<|/det|>She herself does not seem too worried about her results.
<|det|>text [48, 186, 914, 257]<|/det|>In the first sentence of each pair, the highlighted pronoun (myself, herself) receives the action of the subject (I, Priya). In the second sentence of each pair, the highlighted pronoun is used for emphasis.
<|det|>text [48, 278, 871, 325]<|/det|>A pronoun that indicates that the action of the subject reflects, or turns back, on the subject itself is called a reflexive pronoun.
<|det|>text [48, 358, 893, 404]<|/det|>A pronoun that is used for the sake of emphasis is called an emphasising pronoun.
<|det|>text [50, 435, 395, 455]<|/det|>Now, look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [51, 458, 601, 544]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [51, 458, 449, 479]<|/det|>• Each of the boxes was opened.
<|det|>text [51, 480, 480, 501]<|/det|>• I haven't read either of the books.
<|det|>text [51, 503, 601, 544]<|/det|>- Neither of them will admit to having broken the vase.
<|det|>text [658, 428, 726, 454]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [761, 425, 807, 458]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [655, 467, 882, 526]<|/det|>Distributive pronouns are always followed by the verb in the singular.
<|det|>text [47, 548, 855, 596]<|/det|>In each of the sentences, the highlighted pronoun indicates people or things individually, rather than as a group.
<|det|>text [46, 620, 875, 688]<|/det|>Pronouns that show that people or things are taken singly or in separate groups are called distributive pronouns. They are always singular and are, therefore, followed by the verb in the singular.
<|det|>text [48, 708, 304, 729]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [49, 731, 520, 797]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [50, 731, 492, 752]<|/det|>• One must preserve one's traditions.
<|det|>text [50, 754, 519, 797]<|/det|>- Many had proposed the idea, but few voted in its favour later.
<|det|>text [45, 807, 512, 900]<|/det|>In the above sentences, the highlighted pronouns refer to nouns that are understood or implied rather than stated in particular.
<|det|>text [571, 732, 948, 842]<|/det|>Note. Remember, when using the pronoun one, you must be consistent. We cannot say—One tries to help his parents.
<|det|>page_number [848, 928, 882, 946]<|/det|>23
<PAGE><|det|>text [100, 50, 899, 121]<|/det|>Pronouns that do not refer to any person in particular are called indefinite pronouns. Some other indefinite pronouns are several, all, both, anyone, everyone, someone, everybody and nobody.
<|det|>text [101, 133, 452, 154]<|/det|>Now, look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [101, 155, 720, 201]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 155, 677, 176]<|/det|>- The two sisters always try to help one another.
<|det|>text [101, 178, 720, 200]<|/det|>• They waved goodbye to each other as they parted.
<|det|>text [98, 209, 951, 255]<|/det|>The highlighted pronouns indicate that two or more people carry out an action and receive its effect at the same time.
<|det|>text [99, 268, 892, 291]<|/det|>Pronouns that denote mutual action are called reciprocal pronouns.
<|det|>image [65, 315, 136, 351]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [143, 318, 856, 339]<|/det|>Correct the errors in the use of pronouns in these sentences.
<|det|>text [154, 339, 966, 388]<|/det|>Identify whether the pronouns that you have replaced are reflexive, emphasising, distributive, reciprocal or indefinite.
<|det|>list [160, 394, 964, 742]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [167, 398, 630, 420]<|/det|>1. I did not buy neither of the two pens.
<|det|>text [166, 421, 523, 442]<|/det|>2. I filled up the forms herself.
<|det|>text [166, 444, 671, 465]<|/det|>3. One must be careful about what he says.
<|det|>text [165, 465, 961, 488]<|/det|>4. 'You will only do well if you help one,' the coach told the team.
<|det|>text [166, 489, 705, 511]<|/det|>5. Jaya himself invited her friends for the party.
<|det|>text [166, 512, 646, 534]<|/det|>6. Some of the boys was given a balloon.
<|det|>text [166, 535, 709, 557]<|/det|>7. Raj didn't tell someone that he needed help.
<|det|>text [166, 558, 776, 579]<|/det|>8. Every of the students in the class passed the exam.
<|det|>text [166, 580, 743, 602]<|/det|>9. Any of the company's business is done in India.
<|det|>text [156, 603, 699, 625]<|/det|>10. I had to hold on to the chair to steady itself.
<|det|>text [156, 626, 785, 647]<|/det|>11. None doesn't know what to say in such a situation.
<|det|>text [156, 648, 961, 671]<|/det|>12. Although he is an Indian, either of his companies is set in India.
<|det|>text [156, 672, 702, 694]<|/det|>13. The criminals gave himself up to the police.
<|det|>text [156, 695, 898, 741]<|/det|>14. Sam and his brother have stopped talking to one other after a silly fight.
<|det|>image [100, 744, 164, 791]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [178, 756, 456, 777]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [99, 793, 770, 865]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [99, 793, 770, 818]<|/det|>- The book which you had suggested was very interesting.
<|det|>text [99, 819, 730, 841]<|/det|>• He is a boy who always completes his work on time.
<|det|>text [99, 842, 663, 865]<|/det|>- This is the mansion that was sold by my uncle.
<|det|>text [97, 866, 869, 913]<|/det|>In each of these sentences, the highlighted pronoun relates a noun (mansion, boy, book) to the underlined words that follow.
<|det|>page_number [126, 927, 161, 946]<|/det|>24
<PAGE><|det|>text [60, 68, 516, 132]<|/det|>A pronoun that relates a noun that comes before it to a group of words that follow is called a relative pronoun.
<|det|>text [58, 158, 541, 246]<|/det|>The most frequently used relative pronouns are who, whose, whom, which and that. Let us learn more about the uses of each.
<|det|>title [589, 64, 655, 88]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [728, 60, 780, 94]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [585, 100, 905, 176]<|/det|>A relative pronoun should always be placed close to its antecedent. We say—The boy who is in the train is my brother.
<|det|>text [585, 178, 860, 214]<|/det|>We do not say—The boy is my brother who is in the train.
<|det|>text [58, 255, 842, 299]<|/det|>◆ The relative pronouns who and whom are used only for people, irrespective of gender or number. Look at these sentences.
<|det|>text [98, 300, 903, 342]<|/det|>- Students who forget to bring their notebooks will not be allowed to sit for the test.
<|det|>text [98, 344, 854, 366]<|/det|>- The teacher whom you were supposed to meet has left already.
<|det|>list [98, 300, 903, 366]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [56, 367, 916, 412]<|/det|>Note that who is used when the relative pronoun functions as the subject, whereas whom is used when the pronoun is the object.
<|det|>text [58, 422, 571, 468]<|/det|>◆ We use whose to refer to both people and non-living things. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 468, 560, 510]<|/det|>- The bicycle whose lamp was missing belongs to Ratul.
<|det|>text [98, 511, 497, 554]<|/det|>- The teacher scolded the students whose results were poor.
<|det|>list [98, 468, 560, 554]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [56, 565, 556, 610]<|/det|>◆ We can use which for non-living things and animals. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 611, 570, 654]<|/det|>- The sparrows which lost their home in the storm are building a new nest.
<|det|>list [56, 565, 570, 654]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [56, 655, 552, 699]<|/det|>◆ The relative pronoun that usually refers to non-living things. For example,
<|det|>text [96, 699, 901, 742]<|/det|>- We were the first to find the clues that would lead us to the hidden treasure.
<|det|>list [96, 699, 901, 742]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [58, 767, 812, 813]<|/det|>D. There are some errors in the use of relative pronouns in the following sentences. Correct the errors.
<|det|>text [105, 823, 865, 845]<|/det|>1. She is the girl that was selected to play the protagonist's role.
<|det|>text [105, 846, 869, 868]<|/det|>2. Buy strawberry ice cream. It is the only flavour what he likes.
<|det|>text [105, 868, 722, 891]<|/det|>3. That is the boy to who the judges gave the prize.
<|det|>list [105, 823, 869, 891]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [860, 910, 892, 927]<|/det|>25
<PAGE><|det|>list [162, 32, 781, 242]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [162, 32, 756, 56]<|/det|>4. This is the puppy who we rescued from the street.
<|det|>text [162, 56, 778, 78]<|/det|>5. The car which headlights were on has broken down.
<|det|>text [162, 78, 690, 121]<|/det|>6. These are the mangoes that we bought from the market.
<|det|>text [162, 122, 675, 145]<|/det|>7. This is the road what leads to the hospital.
<|det|>text [162, 146, 583, 191]<|/det|>8. The forms which I had to fill up yesterday were very complicated.
<|det|>text [162, 191, 562, 234]<|/det|>9. This is the boy which father is a mountaineer.
<|det|>text [150, 236, 624, 282]<|/det|>10. She collected all the items which she needed for the experiment.
<|det|>image [654, 90, 971, 297]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [58, 315, 130, 355]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [145, 316, 960, 363]<|/det|>Join each of these sets of sentences with a relative pronoun. One has been done for you.
<|det|>list [159, 371, 951, 821]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 372, 948, 439]<|/det|>1. Mr Roy is an architect. His designs have won him many awards. Mr Roy is an architect whose designs have won him many awards.
<|det|>text [160, 441, 907, 485]<|/det|>2. The medicines have been withdrawn by the medical council. The medicines were used to treat bacterial infection.
<|det|>text [160, 487, 851, 510]<|/det|>3. I stopped the ambulance. It was speeding down the road.
<|det|>text [160, 511, 796, 554]<|/det|>4. The diary was preserved by the family. It belonged to Grandfather.
<|det|>text [160, 557, 674, 625]<|/det|>5. The hurricane is heading towards the next town. The hurricane has caused extensive damage in our town.
<|det|>text [159, 627, 681, 670]<|/det|>6. The writer will visit us next week. I admire the writer.
<|det|>text [159, 674, 649, 720]<|/det|>7. We received a good offer for the house. We accepted it.
<|det|>text [158, 722, 655, 769]<|/det|>8. We went to a hotel. It was recommended by you.
<|det|>text [157, 770, 745, 793]<|/det|>9. This lunch box is broken. It belongs to my sister.
<|det|>text [148, 794, 921, 818]<|/det|>10. He took me to see the dilapidated building. He is repairing it.
<|det|>image [654, 536, 964, 687]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [117, 924, 151, 942]<|/det|>26
<PAGE><|det|>title [47, 59, 367, 93]<|/det|>An Invitation
<|det|>header [722, 14, 999, 152]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>image [46, 153, 108, 198]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [115, 149, 870, 214]<|/det|>Read this conversation where two students have come to invite a celebrity to a cultural programme being organised by their students' club.
<|det|>title [44, 230, 186, 252]<|/det|>First student:
<|det|>text [44, 300, 147, 320]<|/det|>Servant:
<|det|>text [44, 344, 241, 364]<|/det|>Second student:
<|det|>text [44, 368, 264, 389]<|/det|>Mr Venkateshwar:
<|det|>text [44, 391, 264, 412]<|/det|>(entering the room):
<|det|>text [44, 440, 264, 461]<|/det|>First student:
<|det|>text [44, 487, 204, 508]<|/det|>First student:
<|det|>text [44, 533, 204, 554]<|/det|>First student:
<|det|>text [44, 565, 264, 586]<|/det|>Mr Venkateshwar:
<|det|>text [44, 612, 240, 632]<|/det|>Second student:
<|det|>text [44, 635, 264, 655]<|/det|>Mr Venkateshwar:
<|det|>text [44, 657, 204, 676]<|/det|>First student:
<|det|>text [320, 230, 905, 321]<|/det|>May we speak to Mr Venkateshwar for a few minutes? We had spoken to him on the telephone earlier, and he had asked us to come this evening. Come in and wait here, please. Who should I say has come?
<|det|>text [320, 323, 842, 365]<|/det|>Please tell him that we are the boys from the Peerless Park Students' Club.
<|det|>text [320, 409, 911, 496]<|/det|>Hello! I've just returned from a full day at the film studio and it's been a very long and tiresome day. So, do be quick and tell me what you would like me to do?
<|det|>text [320, 497, 905, 561]<|/det|>We are awfully sorry to have bothered you, sir, but we'll only take a few minutes of your valuable time.
<|det|>text [320, 563, 854, 608]<|/det|>That's alright. But first, would you like to have something to drink?
<|det|>text [320, 608, 883, 673]<|/det|>Thank you, sir, but we've just had tea and come. Okay. So let's hear what you have to say. Well, sir, next Sunday, 3 September, is our
<|det|>text [320, 673, 885, 809]<|/det|>club's Founder's Day. This year, it's going to be six years old. For the last five years, we have been organising different cultural programmes. This year we've thought of organising a film show. We've decided to show the film Beehive, directed by you.
<|det|>text [320, 810, 530, 831]<|/det|>Have you seen it?
<|det|>page_number [848, 911, 880, 929]<|/det|>27
<PAGE><|det|>title [95, 42, 260, 63]<|/det|>First student:
<|det|>text [95, 111, 320, 133]<|/det|>Mr Venkateshwar:
<|det|>text [95, 134, 312, 155]<|/det|>Second student:
<|det|>text [95, 345, 317, 366]<|/det|>Mr Venkateshwar:
<|det|>text [95, 368, 300, 389]<|/det|>Second student:
<|det|>text [95, 391, 317, 412]<|/det|>Mr Venkateshwar:
<|det|>text [95, 414, 320, 435]<|/det|>First student:
<|det|>text [375, 39, 964, 131]<|/det|>No, sir, we haven't had the privilege, but we know that it won the National Award as the Best Regional Film this year. So, we are all looking forward to seeing it.
<|det|>text [375, 132, 884, 154]<|/det|>That's interesting, what else do you know?
<|det|>text [375, 155, 911, 244]<|/det|>We also know that you have been honoured for two of your documentary films—one got the Golden Lotus in 2005 and the other an international award.
<|det|>text [375, 246, 907, 293]<|/det|>I must say that you've done your homework quite well!
<|det|>text [375, 294, 984, 362]<|/det|>Here's the invitation card, sir. We would be highly honoured if you could kindly be our chief guest and inaugurate the function.
<|det|>text [375, 364, 672, 384]<|/det|>When should I be there?
<|det|>text [375, 386, 907, 409]<|/det|>We'll be here sharp at 6 p.m. to pick you up.
<|det|>text [375, 410, 972, 454]<|/det|>All right, but I have another important meeting at 7.30 p.m. the same day.
<|det|>text [375, 455, 960, 499]<|/det|>That shouldn't be a problem. You should be free by that time.
<|det|>text [375, 500, 984, 546]<|/det|>It was a pleasure talking to you, sir. Thank you and good night!
<|det|>text [375, 547, 524, 569]<|/det|>Good night!
<|det|>title [95, 608, 658, 631]<|/det|>Now, get into pairs and enact these situations.
<|det|>text [95, 642, 987, 711]<|/det|>1. Your school wishes to appoint a well-known footballer to coach the school's football team. You, as the captain of the school's football team. have been asked to convey the message.
<|det|>text [93, 712, 987, 779]<|/det|>2. Your school is organising an inter-school theatre festival. You have been asked to invite a well-known theatre personality to inaugurate the event.
<|det|>page_number [120, 928, 154, 946]<|/det|>28
<PAGE><|det|>title [56, 65, 304, 103]<|/det|>Adjectives
<|det|>header [729, 14, 999, 152]<|/det|>4
<|det|>image [55, 155, 118, 201]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 152, 834, 198]<|/det|>You have learnt that an adjective is a word used to qualify or describe a noun or a pronoun.
<|det|>text [52, 212, 869, 258]<|/det|>Can you say which of the highlighted words below have been used as adjectives? Try to identify the different types of adjectives used.
<|det|>text [52, 270, 896, 404]<|/det|>Kolkata is my city. Kolkata is a big city. It stands on the left bank of the beautiful river Hooghly. This river is a tributary of the Ganges. The river usually has enough water throughout the year. Kolkata has two railway stations. There are many monuments in the city. Each monument is quite stunning. Which monument is my favourite? The Victoria Memorial, of course!
<|det|>text [52, 418, 866, 485]<|/det|>All the highlighted words above have been used to describe or qualify nouns. Let us revise what you have learnt about the different kinds of adjectives.
<|det|>text [52, 500, 860, 546]<|/det|>Adjectives of quality describe the kind or quality of a person or thing. For example, big city, beautiful river.
<|det|>text [52, 575, 911, 621]<|/det|>Adjectives of quantity indicate how much of a thing is meant. For example, little rain, enough water.
<|det|>text [52, 653, 911, 698]<|/det|>Adjectives of number indicate how many of a thing is meant. For example, two stations, six fairies.
<|det|>title [80, 739, 145, 765]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [73, 776, 418, 796]<|/det|>Adjectives of number are of three kinds.
<|det|>text [98, 797, 757, 838]<|/det|>- Definite numeral adjectives state the exact number of people or things. They are of two kinds.
<|det|>text [128, 838, 698, 879]<|/det|>- Cardinals tell us the number of people or things. For example, thirty years, twelve days.
<|det|>list [98, 797, 757, 879]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [854, 911, 885, 927]<|/det|>29
<PAGE><|det|>text [175, 48, 815, 89]<|/det|>Ordinals tell us the order of people or things in a series. For example, second example, sixth month of the year.
<|det|>text [140, 90, 854, 131]<|/det|>- Indefinite numeral adjectives do not indicate an exact number. For example, few children, many people.
<|det|>text [140, 131, 854, 172]<|/det|>- Distributive numeral adjectives refer to each one of a number. For example, every child, each person.
<|det|>list [140, 90, 854, 172]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [89, 212, 877, 260]<|/det|>Demonstrative adjectives indicate which person or thing is meant. For example, this river, those girls.
<|det|>title [111, 310, 182, 336]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [425, 303, 471, 341]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [516, 303, 561, 341]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 349, 957, 433]<|/det|>The words this, that, these and those can be used both as adjectives and as pronouns. It is important to distinguish between the two functions. The easiest way to determine the part of speech is to examine whether the word qualifies a noun or points to a noun. Demonstrative adjectives will always be followed by a noun. For example,
<|det|>list [134, 434, 758, 476]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [134, 434, 728, 454]<|/det|>• This is a charming old city. (pronoun, pointing to the noun, city)
<|det|>text [134, 455, 757, 475]<|/det|>• This book is very interesting. (adjective, qualifying the noun, book)
<|det|>text [88, 521, 978, 569]<|/det|>Interrogative adjectives are used with nouns to ask questions. For example which city, whose pen.
<|det|>text [87, 620, 978, 668]<|/det|>Possessive adjectives show belonging or possession. For example, my city, their house.
<|det|>title [111, 720, 182, 746]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [506, 714, 556, 751]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 759, 945, 802]<|/det|>It is important to understand the difference between possessive adjectives and possessive pronouns. Look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [105, 804, 958, 890]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 804, 697, 824]<|/det|>• My aunt wrote me a letter. This aunt of mine lives in Canada.
<|det|>text [105, 825, 958, 889]<|/det|>- Jasbeer spoke to a friend of his about this problem. His friend has promised to help. The underlined words have been used as adjectives because they qualify the nouns that follow them. The highlighted words are possessive pronouns.

<|det|>title [105, 59, 862, 105]<|/det|>Underline the adjectives in these sentences and put them in the appropriate columns in the table provided.

<|det|>text [121, 113, 911, 156]<|/det|>1. The teacher could sense that there was something fishy going on in the back bench.
<|det|>text [120, 162, 898, 185]<|/det|>2. Ront prepared a beautiful chart on the benefits of afforestation.
<|det|>text [121, 189, 797, 211]<|/det|>3. The incessant rains in Jaipur brought life to a standstill.
<|det|>text [120, 216, 796, 239]<|/det|>4. Those sweets in the third row are made from dry fruits.
<|det|>text [121, 243, 838, 286]<|/det|>5. The salted peanuts and the tangy chips were a treat for my blunted taste buds.
<|det|>text [120, 292, 794, 316]<|/det|>6. The naughty black cat was playing happily with owlet.
<|det|>text [121, 320, 501, 342]<|/det|>7. Which fruit is your favourite?
<|det|>text [120, 346, 815, 370]<|/det|>8. I visited their house while I was passing through the city.
<|det|>text [121, 374, 733, 397]<|/det|>9. I have bought enough supplies to last me a week.
<|det|>text [108, 400, 886, 444]<|/det|>10. Every household in my locality has contributed generously for the cause.
<|det|>text [108, 449, 922, 495]<|/det|>11. Those tourists booked tickets for an hour-long scenic cruise along the Mandovi River aboard a cruise ship.
<|det|>text [109, 498, 714, 521]<|/det|>12. John handled the dicey situation with great tact.
<|det|>text [109, 524, 721, 547]<|/det|>13. My dress for the function will be a red sherwani.
<|det|>text [109, 551, 461, 574]<|/det|>14. Which shirt should I buy?

<PAGE><|det|>title [65, 38, 950, 85]<|/det|>3. In this passage, circle the adjectives and underline the nouns they describe.
<|det|>text [141, 96, 948, 400]<|/det|>We entered through the open gate into a large, square courtyard. No less than fifty horses were lined up for the rajah's annual parade. The saddle seats of these splendid animals were covered with silken cloth. The enormous mansion had four minarets and broad balconies. These were now bedecked with jasmine garlands. Twinkling bulbs of many hues added to the charm of the surroundings. Members of the household—aged grandmas, tiny toddlers and rugged men—were all going to participate in the day's events. The rajah in his oriental attire and ancestral jewellery stepped into the courtyard at 9 a.m. His loyal retainers rushed to greet him. A large retinue of servants stood to attention. However, the members of the household did not know that they would soon become hapless victims of a British conspiracy that a conniving relative had ensured.
<|det|>image [58, 415, 143, 455]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [142, 415, 960, 486]<|/det|>Rewrite these sentences with the adjective forms of the highlighted words. Make necessary changes, add words if required, but do not alter the meaning. One has been done for you.

<|det|>text [160, 496, 600, 519]<|/det|>1. I was surprised by his foolishness.
<|det|>text [180, 523, 627, 546]<|/det|>His foolish behaviour surprised me.
<|det|>text [160, 551, 492, 573]<|/det|>2. He behaved like a child.

<|det|>text [160, 606, 680, 629]<|/det|>4. London is a city with a large population.

<|det|>text [160, 688, 645, 732]<|/det|>7. You have the freedom to do what you wish.
