<PAGE><|det|>text [158, 54, 960, 288]<|/det|>The ____ town I visited was Agra where I met ____ college friends of yours. They were touring ____ part of the country. They wanted me to give them ____ advice on the mode of transport. I told them that they could take any of the roads to Jaipur. You see, ____ trains to Jaipur had been cancelled that day. The ____ member of the group wanted to know the name of the ____ town where they could halt for the night on the way to Jaipur. It was getting late. So I asked them to leave without ____ delay. I really liked them a lot. ____ people are so friendly these days.
<|det|>text [158, 299, 951, 346]<|/det|>I am really tired now. I could do with ____ sleep. Hope to meet you soon.
<|det|>text [159, 361, 285, 407]<|/det|>With love
Rekha
<|det|>page_number [114, 927, 143, 944]<|/det|>34
<PAGE><|det|>title [65, 54, 395, 93]<|/det|>Essay Writing
<|det|>image [735, 0, 979, 150]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [62, 162, 118, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 159, 928, 202]<|/det|>An essay is a foray into the imagination; it is a work expressing our opinions, thoughts and understanding of a topic or a theme. Like all
<|det|>text [62, 204, 911, 252]<|/det|>works of composition, an essay, too, has a format. The usual format of an essay is that it is divided into paragraphs.
<|det|>text [61, 265, 889, 379]<|/det|>The opening paragraph of an essay is normally called the introduction. The last or final paragraph is called the conclusion. In between the introduction and the conclusion, there may be one or more paragraphs that constitute the body of the composition. These paragraphs help to develop the thoughts and ideas to be expressed in the composition.
<|det|>text [62, 393, 697, 415]<|/det|>Let us recapitulate the qualities of a good composition.
<|det|>list [63, 416, 911, 594]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [63, 416, 885, 457]<|/det|>- An essay must have unity of thought, that is, it should focus on one definite theme.
<|det|>text [63, 460, 825, 482]<|/det|>- It should follow an order or sequence and have a logical flow.
<|det|>text [63, 483, 842, 505]<|/det|>- The length of an essay ought to be around three hundred words.
<|det|>text [64, 506, 708, 528]<|/det|>- The composition should be written in a simple style.
<|det|>text [64, 528, 914, 594]<|/det|>- The composition must reveal the personal feelings and opinions of the writer. Without the personal touch, a composition becomes devoid of individuality.
<|det|>title [94, 617, 151, 640]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [476, 608, 532, 645]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [82, 653, 901, 694]<|/det|>In order to write a good essay, it helps to spend a few moments writing down the questions that arise in your mind after seeing the topic, and trying to answer them.
<|det|>text [82, 706, 878, 748]<|/det|>Start the composition with the help of a creative introduction. You may use one of the various methods given below—
<|det|>list [85, 749, 406, 848]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 749, 394, 768]<|/det|>• by setting the scene for the story
<|det|>text [85, 769, 376, 789]<|/det|>• by plunging straight into action
<|det|>text [85, 790, 403, 809]<|/det|>• by an interesting or unusual detail
<|det|>text [85, 810, 227, 829]<|/det|>- by a dialogue
<|det|>text [82, 830, 907, 891]<|/det|>Make sure you end the essay with a meaningful conclusion. Remember to round off your narration and avoid ending your composition with sentences such as 'In conclusion…', 'It can be seen that…', 'Summing up, I'd like to state…'.
<|det|>page_number [864, 924, 898, 943]<|/det|>35
<PAGE><|det|>image [58, 45, 125, 95]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [141, 39, 948, 84]<|/det|>Write essays of about 300 words on any three topics from the ones listed below.
<|det|>list [141, 92, 627, 250]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [142, 92, 535, 115]<|/det|>1. Your City on a Festive Evening
<|det|>text [141, 115, 381, 134]<|/det|>2. Recess at School
<|det|>text [141, 137, 480, 157]<|/det|>3. The India of Your Dreams
<|det|>text [141, 159, 577, 180]<|/det|>4. Your Favourite Fictional Character
<|det|>text [141, 182, 625, 203]<|/det|>5. Your First Outing Without Your Parents
<|det|>text [141, 205, 456, 226]<|/det|>6. A Terrifying Experience
<|det|>text [141, 228, 555, 251]<|/det|>7. A Day in the Life of a Stray Dog
<|det|>image [58, 264, 125, 328]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [141, 267, 785, 290]<|/det|>Write an essay of about 300 words on these situations.
<|det|>list [141, 295, 966, 435]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [141, 295, 958, 365]<|/det|>1. Imagine you are an alien who has just arrived on earth from another planet. You are shocked by the amount of waste you see. Write a description of the specific scenes that shock you.
<|det|>text [140, 365, 927, 412]<|/det|>2. Imagine that you have been granted three wishes. What would you wish for and why? Justify the order of priority.
<|det|>text [140, 412, 967, 435]<|/det|>3. What you would do to make the world a happier place to live in?
<|det|>page_number [117, 935, 147, 951]<|/det|>36
<PAGE><|det|>title [65, 58, 265, 90]<|/det|>Adverbs
<|det|>header [735, 8, 999, 145]<|/det|>5
<|det|>image [64, 168, 118, 211]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 165, 856, 209]<|/det|>We know that an adverb is a word that qualifies, or modifies, a verb, an adjective or another adverb. For example,
<|det|>list [64, 211, 770, 278]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [65, 211, 521, 233]<|/det|>• He ran fast. (modifies the verb, ran)
<|det|>text [65, 234, 769, 256]<|/det|>- The apple was very sweet. (modifies the adjective, sweet)
<|det|>text [65, 257, 740, 278]<|/det|>• He walked rather slowly. (modifies the adverb, slowly)
<|det|>title [64, 295, 281, 316]<|/det|>Kinds of Adverb
<|det|>text [63, 345, 816, 369]<|/det|>Adverbs of manner tell us how an action occurred. For example,
<|det|>text [65, 370, 331, 392]<|/det|>• He laughed loudly.
<|det|>text [63, 435, 809, 459]<|/det|>Adverbs of place tell us where an action occurred. For example,
<|det|>text [65, 460, 245, 480]<|/det|>- I lived here.
<|det|>text [63, 519, 790, 541]<|/det|>Adverbs of time tell us when an action occurred. For example,
<|det|>text [65, 542, 383, 563]<|/det|>• I have done this before.
<|det|>text [63, 602, 871, 625]<|/det|>Adverbs of frequency tell us how often an action occurs. For example,
<|det|>text [65, 626, 312, 647]<|/det|>• I fell down twice.
<|det|>text [63, 687, 885, 734]<|/det|>Adverbs of degree tell us the degree or extent of an action or a quality. For example,
<|det|>text [65, 735, 285, 756]<|/det|>• I was very sick.
<|det|>text [63, 798, 816, 845]<|/det|>Adverbs of reason state a reason; they answer the question why? for example,
<|det|>text [65, 845, 714, 867]<|/det|>• He received a call from his father. Therefore, he left.
<|det|>page_number [868, 910, 901, 929]<|/det|>37
<PAGE><|det|>text [82, 42, 972, 90]<|/det|>Adverbs that are used in asking questions are called interrogative adverbs. For example,
<|det|>list [82, 90, 845, 180]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [82, 90, 680, 111]<|/det|>• Where is Mira? (interrogative adverb of place)
<|det|>text [82, 112, 792, 134]<|/det|>- When did the train leave? (interrogative adverb of time)
<|det|>text [82, 135, 725, 157]<|/det|>• Why did you cry? (interrogative adverb of reason)
<|det|>text [82, 157, 844, 180]<|/det|>- How did you manage this? (interrogative adverb of manner)
<|det|>text [73, 207, 957, 255]<|/det|>A: Choose adverbs from the box to complete the passage given below. Identify the kind of adverb you have used in each case.
<|det|>text [85, 270, 968, 322]<|/det|>usually concernedly early anywhere invariably hardly
around enormously too so violently why very
<|det|>text [85, 323, 968, 644]<|/det|>My family ____ needs an excuse to travel. We're ____ ready to travel ____. I ____ get ____ excited before a trip that I can ____ sleep in anticipation of the event. ____, if we travel by car, we try to set off ____ so that we can reach our destination ____ quickly. ____, I was looking at the pictures of our trip to Goa. It rained ____, ____ of that trip. Although we couldn't go swimming, we enjoyed ourselves ____. ____ couldn't we go swimming? The waves were ____ high. I remember the sea pounding ____ against the beach. ____, we just relaxed and drove ____ in the rain.
<|det|>title [70, 666, 378, 688]<|/det|>Formation of Adverbs
<|det|>text [154, 696, 856, 720]<|/det|>Adverbs are formed from adjectives in the following ways:
<|det|>image [70, 723, 138, 770]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 720, 848, 768]<|/det|>Most adjectives add -ly to form adverbs. For example, rude—rudely, quick—quickly.
<|det|>text [69, 768, 898, 838]<|/det|>In an adjective ending in -y preceded by a consonant, the -y is changed into -i and -ly is added. For example, happy—happily, angry—angrily.
<|det|>text [68, 838, 925, 886]<|/det|>When the adjective ends in -e, the -e is dropped and -ly is added. For example, true—truly, double—doubly.
<|det|>list [68, 768, 925, 886]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [102, 929, 131, 945]<|/det|>38
<PAGE><|det|>title [127, 63, 887, 109]<|/det|>Rewrite these sentences using adverbs in place of the highlighted adjectives. The meaning of the sentences should not change.
<|det|>list [140, 113, 930, 335]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [141, 114, 421, 133]<|/det|>1. I am a careful driver.
<|det|>text [141, 136, 488, 155]<|/det|>2. Suman is not a fast runner.
<|det|>text [141, 157, 483, 177]<|/det|>3. Gargi is a brilliant dancer.
<|det|>text [141, 179, 497, 199]<|/det|>4. He is quick to take offence.
<|det|>text [141, 201, 715, 221]<|/det|>5. Sita's behaviour is more courteous than Mira's.
<|det|>text [141, 223, 748, 244]<|/det|>6. Of the two, Harish is the more articulate speaker.
<|det|>text [141, 245, 835, 266]<|/det|>7. She has a very good pronunciation of the English vowels.
<|det|>text [140, 267, 884, 288]<|/det|>8. Mr Sen approaches the subject in a far less scientific manner.
<|det|>text [140, 289, 927, 310]<|/det|>9. There has been a rapid increase in the number of train accidents.
<|det|>text [129, 311, 928, 333]<|/det|>10. Last night's performance left a considerable impression upon me.
<|det|>title [100, 373, 166, 397]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>list [123, 409, 867, 493]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [123, 409, 862, 449]<|/det|>- Not all words ending in -ly are adverbs. Adjectives can also end in -ly. For example, friendly, lively, lonely or cowardly.
<|det|>text [123, 449, 867, 468]<|/det|>• Some words can be used as adverbs as well as adjectives. Look at these examples.
<|det|>title [230, 482, 321, 501]<|/det|>Adjective
<|det|>text [110, 509, 295, 528]<|/det|>She is an early riser.
<|det|>image [321, 508, 523, 528]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [458, 509, 735, 528]<|/det|>She started for the airport early.
<|det|>text [110, 536, 307, 555]<|/det|>This is a very hard nut.
<|det|>text [458, 535, 680, 555]<|/det|>He works hard every day.
<|det|>text [110, 560, 309, 579]<|/det|>This is a straight road.
<|det|>text [458, 559, 698, 578]<|/det|>Go straight down that road.
<|det|>image [39, 624, 138, 666]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [125, 627, 823, 671]<|/det|>State whether the last word in each of these sentences is an adjective or an adverb.
<|det|>list [139, 677, 648, 878]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 677, 538, 699]<|/det|>1. The fire burnt the grass quickly.
<|det|>text [140, 700, 521, 721]<|/det|>2. Heat turned the paper brown.
<|det|>text [140, 722, 491, 742]<|/det|>3. He drove his workers hard.
<|det|>text [140, 744, 506, 765]<|/det|>4. The man gave them enough.
<|det|>text [140, 766, 394, 787]<|/det|>5. I find this unlikely.
<|det|>text [140, 788, 597, 809]<|/det|>6. I have made his acquaintance lately.
<|det|>text [140, 810, 406, 831]<|/det|>7. Pull the string hard.
<|det|>text [140, 832, 525, 852]<|/det|>8. We shall leave for school late.
<|det|>text [140, 854, 653, 876]<|/det|>9. The spectators shall leave the hall empty.
<|det|>text [127, 877, 582, 898]<|/det|>10. Her drawings are always very neat.
<|det|>image [658, 685, 932, 848]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [876, 911, 907, 929]<|/det|>39
<PAGE><|det|>title [81, 40, 408, 63]<|/det|>Comparison of Adverbs
<|det|>image [82, 70, 147, 116]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [157, 68, 948, 136]<|/det|>Adverbs, like adjectives, can be used to make comparisons. The degrees of comparison are positive, comparative and superlative. For example,
<|det|>list [160, 137, 860, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 137, 567, 159]<|/det|>• Rahim writes neatly. (positive)
<|det|>text [160, 159, 858, 181]<|/det|>- His brother writes more neatly than him. (comparative)
<|det|>text [160, 182, 761, 205]<|/det|>- Their sister writes the most neatly. (superlative)
<|det|>text [78, 215, 966, 284]<|/det|>We form the comparative and the superlative forms by adding -er and -est, respectively, to the positive degree of most adverbs. For example, fast—faster—fastest, hard—harder—hardest.
<|det|>text [78, 285, 942, 352]<|/det|>We also form the comparatives and the superlatives by adding more and most, respectively, to the positive degree of many adverbs. For example, happily—more happily—most happily.
<|det|>text [78, 353, 830, 422]<|/det|>Some adverbs form the comparatives and the superlatives in an irregular way. For example, little—less—least, much—more—most.
<|det|>title [80, 447, 750, 470]<|/det|>D. Circle the correct word to complete each sentence.
<|det|>list [143, 476, 956, 749]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [144, 476, 470, 498]<|/det|>1. She acted wisely/wiser.
<|det|>text [143, 499, 660, 520]<|/det|>2. Harish ran faster/fastest than the others.
<|det|>text [143, 522, 750, 544]<|/det|>3. You should worry less/least about these matters.
<|det|>text [143, 545, 750, 568]<|/det|>4. Rama is studying hard/more hard for his exams.
<|det|>text [143, 568, 824, 589]<|/det|>5. And last, but not the less/least, was the vote of thanks.
<|det|>text [143, 590, 922, 613]<|/det|>6. Companies often pay their employees very badly/more badly.
<|det|>text [143, 614, 955, 636]<|/det|>7. The hummingbird flies more swiftly/most swiftly than the robin.
<|det|>text [143, 637, 891, 660]<|/det|>8. I gave him directions. He found the road easily/most easily.
<|det|>text [143, 660, 901, 703]<|/det|>9. She tried to speak more slowly/most slowly so that the child could understand.
<|det|>text [130, 706, 878, 753]<|/det|>10. The maths paper worries me the most/more. The rest I can manage to do well/good in.
<|det|>title [70, 781, 345, 804]<|/det|>Position of Adverbs
<|det|>text [145, 811, 945, 857]<|/det|>◆ Adverbs of manner, time and place are placed after transitive verbs or after the object if the verb is transitive. For example,
<|det|>list [194, 859, 768, 929]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [195, 859, 690, 881]<|/det|>• Arun sang loudly. (adverb of manner)
<|det|>text [195, 883, 767, 906]<|/det|>• Cross the road carefully. (adverb of manner)
<|det|>text [195, 907, 679, 929]<|/det|>• Put the book there. (adverb of place)
<|det|>page_number [96, 930, 131, 947]<|/det|>40
<PAGE><|det|>list [87, 42, 628, 88]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [87, 42, 540, 64]<|/det|>• They started early. (adverb of time)
<|det|>text [87, 66, 627, 88]<|/det|>- I wrote a poem yesterday. (adverb of time)
<|det|>text [50, 98, 907, 144]<|/det|>◆ Adverbs of frequency and degree are placed between the subject and the verb if the sentence contains only one verb. For example,
<|det|>text [87, 145, 873, 169]<|/det|>• He always meets people in the afternoon. (adverb of frequency)
<|det|>text [50, 178, 878, 225]<|/det|>◆ Adverbs are also placed after the first auxiliary or helping verb if the verb consists of more than one word. For example,
<|det|>text [88, 226, 457, 249]<|/det|>• The guests have just arrived.
<|det|>text [50, 259, 890, 305]<|/det|>◆ Adverbs are placed after the forms of the verb, be (am, is, was, were). For example,
<|det|>text [89, 306, 340, 326]<|/det|>• He is seldom late.
<|det|>text [50, 339, 872, 384]<|/det|>◆ When an adverb modifies an adjective or another adverb, it usually comes before the word it modifies. For example,
<|det|>text [89, 386, 396, 409]<|/det|>- It is a rather sultry day.
<|det|>title [73, 436, 141, 461]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [468, 431, 521, 466]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [70, 474, 875, 514]<|/det|>The position of the adverb needs careful handling, as the position alters the implication of the sentence. For example,
<|det|>list [97, 516, 450, 556]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [97, 516, 449, 536]<|/det|>• David only reached Pune yesterday.
<|det|>text [97, 537, 450, 556]<|/det|>• Only David reached Pune yesterday.
<|det|>image [26, 594, 108, 634]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 598, 890, 621]<|/det|>Insert the adverbs mentioned in the brackets in the correct places.
<|det|>list [117, 628, 470, 928]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 628, 470, 651]<|/det|>1. The bus isn't late. (usually)
<|det|>text [116, 652, 410, 674]<|/det|>2. Is Rita here? (already)
<|det|>text [116, 675, 472, 697]<|/det|>3. He makes an error. (rarely)
<|det|>text [116, 698, 445, 720]<|/det|>4. Kapil is very weak. (still)
<|det|>text [116, 722, 454, 743]<|/det|>5. I wrote a note. (carefully)
<|det|>text [116, 745, 493, 766]<|/det|>6. I have been told that. (often)
<|det|>text [116, 768, 570, 790]<|/det|>7. Nothing happens by chance. (ever)
<|det|>text [116, 791, 586, 813]<|/det|>8. They stay in the same hotel. (always)
<|det|>text [115, 814, 810, 837]<|/det|>9. The prisoner escaped from the dungeon cleverly. (quite)
<|det|>text [105, 838, 686, 860]<|/det|>10. It is true that Mary has started a school? (just)
<|det|>text [105, 861, 767, 883]<|/det|>11. She has acted on the school stage. (previously, well)
<|det|>text [105, 884, 830, 929]<|/det|>12. She had reached the station when the mobile phone rang. (almost)
<|det|>image [654, 648, 912, 813]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [859, 930, 890, 947]<|/det|>41
<PAGE><|det|>header [107, 41, 160, 82]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [182, 37, 900, 80]<|/det|>Let us look at the use of some common adverbs that are often confused.
<|det|>text [104, 103, 544, 170]<|/det|>◆ The adverbs very and too are used with an adjective or another adverb.
<|det|>text [146, 173, 507, 195]<|/det|>• He did the work very nicely.
<|det|>text [146, 196, 463, 240]<|/det|>• She is too lazy to finish the work.
<|det|>list [146, 173, 507, 240]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [104, 253, 553, 301]<|/det|>◆ The adverbs fairly and rather both mean ‘moderately’. But fairly is
<|det|>text [104, 301, 945, 345]<|/det|>chiefly used with favourable adjectives and adverbs, while rather is used before unfavourable ones.
<|det|>text [146, 348, 540, 371]<|/det|>• The weather is fairly pleasant.
<|det|>text [146, 372, 576, 393]<|/det|>• I received some rather bad news.
<|det|>list [146, 348, 576, 393]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [104, 405, 965, 451]<|/det|>◆ We use much to qualify an adjective or an adverb in the comparative degree while very is used along with the positive.
<|det|>text [146, 453, 460, 474]<|/det|>• He is much better now.
<|det|>text [146, 475, 460, 498]<|/det|>• He walked very slowly.
<|det|>list [146, 453, 460, 498]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [73, 520, 138, 565]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [150, 524, 905, 594]<|/det|>Rewrite the paragraphs correcting the use of adverbs wherever required. You may have to omit words or even change their positions.
<|det|>text [154, 600, 940, 669]<|/det|>1. He was much sorry for his behaviour. He seldom or ever told a lie. He was very much surprised by his own conduct. He had behaved fairly badly. Just not like him.
<|det|>text [152, 670, 953, 761]<|/det|>2. I am too tired, but not very tired to walk to the market. Do not worry, I shall walk so slowly. The weather is surely pleasant. It is not too humid today. I surely will manage. Remember that I go to the market alone always.
<|det|>text [152, 762, 937, 810]<|/det|>3. Don't you think she talks very much? She is a rather clever girl, but fairly talkative.
<|det|>list [152, 600, 953, 810]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [127, 933, 161, 950]<|/det|>42
<PAGE><|det|>title [45, 48, 422, 131]<|/det|>The Sri Krishna
Eating House
<|det|>header [720, 0, 999, 144]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>text [43, 155, 856, 222]<|/det|>For generations, the simple food of roadside restaurants has satisfied the rich and the poor alike. Read about a unique restaurant that was affordable even to the downtrodden.
<|det|>text [43, 238, 885, 308]<|/det|>Hari had been so tired and weak and anxious that first night that he had not really been aware of the place in which he found himself. He only saw it for the first time when he woke up the next morning.
<|det|>text [43, 321, 903, 502]<|/det|>The Sri Krishna Eating House was the meanest and shabbiest restaurant Hari had ever seen; even in Thul', there were cafés that were more pleasant; usually wooden shacks built in the shade of a mango or frangipani tree, with a handful of marigolds and hibiscus crammed into an old ink bottle for a vase, coloured bottles of aerated drinks attractively arranged on the shelves, and possibly a bright picture of a god or goddess on the wall with a tinsel garland around the frame and heavily scented joss sticks burning before it.
<|det|>text [42, 516, 912, 719]<|/det|>But the Sri Krishna Eating House did not have even so much as a coloured picture of Krishna glued to the wall. Or perhaps, there had been one and it had disappeared under the layers of grime and soot with which the walls were coated. The ceiling was thick with cobwebs that trapped the soot and made a kind of furry blanket over one's head. The floor and the wooden tables were all black, too, since they all got an even share of soot from the open stoves in the back room where the lentils were cooked all day in a huge aluminium pan and the chapattis were rolled by hand and baked.
<|det|>text [41, 733, 875, 828]<|/det|>It was certainly the cheapest restaurant anyone could possibly find in Bombay (now Mumbai)—even a beggar could afford to buy himself a meal here, and the usual customers were beggars and coolies who had stopped in between carrying their loads—sacks of coal and cylinders
<|det|>page_footnote [43, 871, 725, 892]<|/det|>* a small fishing village in Maharashtra, on the western coast of India
<|det|>page_number [854, 919, 887, 937]<|/det|>43
<PAGE><|det|>text [104, 35, 980, 199]<|/det|>of gas—and cart-pullers who dragged goods through the city on long wooden hand-drawn carts. These people seemed to have no fixed working hours—before daylight, there were some waiting for a meal—who were given the leftovers of the night before, and the last came in after midnight, when the whole city seemed to collapse into exhausted, disturbed sleep. So, of course, the owner had no time to sweep or clean his restaurant or the money to decorate it with pictures and flowers.
<|det|>title [105, 216, 516, 239]<|/det|>A. Now, answer these questions.
<|det|>list [160, 245, 970, 386]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [161, 245, 818, 269]<|/det|>1. Describe the pleasant, nicely organised cafés of Thul.
<|det|>text [160, 269, 965, 311]<|/det|>2. In what ways was the Sri Krishna Eating House different from the restaurants in Thul?
<|det|>text [160, 314, 972, 338]<|/det|>3. What kind of people came to eat at the Sri Krishna Eating House?
<|det|>text [160, 339, 758, 361]<|/det|>4. Why did they come to this particular restaurant?
<|det|>text [160, 362, 937, 385]<|/det|>5. Why was the restaurant in such a shabby, worn-out condition?
<|det|>title [105, 404, 670, 427]<|/det|>B. Give meanings of these words and phrases.
<|det|>list [159, 433, 952, 540]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 433, 301, 454]<|/det|>1. meanest
<|det|>text [468, 433, 617, 454]<|/det|>2. crammed
<|det|>text [720, 433, 951, 455]<|/det|>3. grime and soot
<|det|>text [160, 456, 369, 477]<|/det|>4. aerated drinks
<|det|>text [468, 456, 595, 477]<|/det|>5. scented
<|det|>text [720, 456, 937, 478]<|/det|>6. tinsel garland
<|det|>title [104, 495, 938, 542]<|/det|>C. Fill in the blanks in these sentences with words from the passage. You may have to change the form of the words in some cases.
<|det|>list [157, 548, 965, 873]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [157, 548, 965, 593]<|/det|>1. These utensils are ____ with a special chemical to make them non-stick.
<|det|>text [156, 595, 944, 641]<|/det|>2. The performance of the actors was so good that our eyes were ____ to the stage.
<|det|>text [156, 642, 911, 686]<|/det|>3. The groom put a ____ of pink roses and white chrysanthemums around the neck of the bride.
<|det|>text [156, 687, 717, 733]<|/det|>4. The room had been prettily decorated with ____ and paper streamers for the birthday party.
<|det|>text [156, 734, 888, 779]<|/det|>5. After the long trek, we were so ____ that we crashed into our beds and soon fell asleep.
<|det|>text [156, 780, 951, 826]<|/det|>6. The children were ____ after hearing the news of the kidnapping.
<|det|>text [156, 826, 965, 872]<|/det|>7. The date for submitting the project was nearing and we couldn't ____ to lose any time.
<|det|>page_number [135, 932, 168, 948]<|/det|>44
<PAGE><|det|>title [46, 59, 354, 90]<|/det|>Determiners
<|det|>header [714, 8, 999, 142]<|/det|>6
<|det|>image [44, 160, 99, 201]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 169, 804, 193]<|/det|>Read these sentences. Can you say why they sound funny?
<|det|>text [46, 201, 217, 222]<|/det|>• This is pen.
<|det|>text [46, 223, 320, 244]<|/det|>- I just had sandwich.
<|det|>text [46, 246, 387, 267]<|/det|>• Boy in red shirt is brother.
<|det|>text [46, 269, 429, 290]<|/det|>• Mangoes in basket are sweet.
<|det|>list [46, 201, 429, 290]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [43, 304, 905, 351]<|/det|>The above sentences have been rewritten correctly. Underline the words that have been added to complete the sentences.
<|det|>text [46, 358, 265, 380]<|/det|>• This is my pen.
<|det|>text [46, 381, 343, 402]<|/det|>• I just had a sandwich.
<|det|>text [46, 403, 523, 425]<|/det|>• The boy in the red shirt is my brother.
<|det|>text [46, 426, 524, 448]<|/det|>• The mangoes in this basket are sweet.
<|det|>list [46, 358, 524, 448]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [47, 457, 102, 502]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 456, 895, 522]<|/det|>The words you have just underlined come before nouns and help to identify or specify which person or thing is being referred to. These words are called determiners.
<|det|>text [44, 533, 908, 580]<|/det|>Determiners are used before nouns to indicate which particular person or thing we are referring to. They do not describe nouns, but point them out.
<|det|>text [46, 598, 558, 619]<|/det|>There are six different kinds of determiners.
<|det|>text [46, 622, 197, 642]<|/det|>◆ articles
<|det|>text [46, 645, 245, 666]<|/det|>◆ possessives
<|det|>text [46, 667, 237, 689]<|/det|>◆ quantifiers
<|det|>list [46, 622, 245, 689]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [510, 622, 732, 641]<|/det|>◆ demonstratives
<|det|>text [510, 644, 714, 665]<|/det|>◆ interrogatives
<|det|>text [510, 666, 698, 686]<|/det|>◆ distributives
<|det|>list [510, 622, 732, 686]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [47, 703, 158, 723]<|/det|>Articles
<|det|>text [46, 731, 733, 753]<|/det|>You have learnt that there are three articles—a, an and the.
<|det|>text [44, 768, 858, 816]<|/det|>The words a and an are called indefinite articles because they do not point to any special person or thing.
<|det|>text [44, 845, 830, 894]<|/det|>The word the is known as the definite article because it points to a specific person or thing.
<|det|>page_number [855, 918, 887, 935]<|/det|>45
<PAGE><|det|>text [87, 44, 441, 65]<|/det|>The definite article is used—
<|det|>text [88, 67, 646, 89]<|/det|>◆ to make a specific reference. For example,
<|det|>text [130, 90, 586, 111]<|/det|>• There's the shop I was looking for.
<|det|>text [88, 122, 993, 168]<|/det|>◆ when we are talking to someone who knows which thing or person we are referring to. For example,
<|det|>text [130, 168, 504, 190]<|/det|>• There is water in the fridge.
<|det|>text [130, 191, 442, 211]<|/det|>• Turn the television on.
<|det|>text [88, 224, 374, 247]<|/det|>◆ to generalise a class
<|det|>text [128, 248, 593, 270]<|/det|>• The tiger is an endangered species.
<|det|>text [85, 280, 988, 327]<|/det|>◆ before an adjective, when it is used as a noun indicating nationality or a class. For example,
<|det|>text [128, 328, 448, 349]<|/det|>• The rich can be selfish.
<|det|>text [128, 350, 835, 371]<|/det|>• The Swiss are famous for their watches and chocolates.
<|det|>text [85, 384, 950, 429]<|/det|>◆ before the names of rivers, seas, oceans or mountains. For example, the Alps, the Indian Ocean.
<|det|>text [84, 440, 938, 486]<|/det|>◆ before the names of parts of the body when these are referred to in an impersonal way. For example,
<|det|>text [128, 487, 456, 507]<|/det|>• He was hit on the head.
<|det|>text [81, 523, 990, 590]<|/det|>The indefinite articles are used with singular countable nouns. The article a is used before words starting with a consonant sound while an is used before words beginning with a vowel sound. The indefinite articles are also used—
<|det|>text [81, 593, 501, 636]<|/det|>◆ to refer to something mentioned for the first time. For example,
<|det|>list [119, 638, 501, 709]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 638, 501, 680]<|/det|>- I bought a newspaper at the station.
<|det|>text [120, 684, 474, 707]<|/det|>- It was an interesting idea.
<|det|>text [80, 718, 463, 787]<|/det|>◆ to refer to something you do not want to be specific about. For example,
<|det|>text [119, 788, 440, 810]<|/det|>• A girl came to see you.
<|det|>text [75, 822, 492, 867]<|/det|>◆ to refer to a person's profession.
For example,
<|det|>text [116, 869, 378, 890]<|/det|>• He is an engineer.
<|det|>title [567, 618, 632, 642]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [747, 608, 794, 642]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [555, 655, 960, 716]<|/det|>If there is an adjective before a singular countable noun, we place the article in front of the adjective. For example,
<|det|>list [556, 717, 774, 797]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [556, 717, 774, 736]<|/det|>• a monthly magazine
<|det|>text [556, 738, 771, 757]<|/det|>• an African elephant
<|det|>text [556, 759, 737, 778]<|/det|>• a united country
<|det|>text [556, 780, 765, 796]<|/det|>• an honourable task
<|det|>text [554, 800, 960, 884]<|/det|>Note that we use an before honourable since it begins with a vowel sound (o), while united begins with a consonant sound (you) and is thus preceded by a.
<|det|>page_number [99, 925, 131, 941]<|/det|>46
<PAGE><|det|>list [59, 53, 711, 100]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [60, 53, 411, 76]<|/det|>◆ in definitions. For example,
<|det|>text [106, 77, 450, 99]<|/det|>• An opossum is a marsupial.
<|det|>text [59, 113, 710, 135]<|/det|>It is important to remember that we do not use articles—
<|det|>list [60, 136, 542, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [60, 136, 542, 158]<|/det|>◆ with uncountable nouns. For example,
<|det|>text [106, 158, 471, 179]<|/det|>• I always welcome good news.
<|det|>text [60, 190, 465, 213]<|/det|>◆ with plural nouns. For example,
<|det|>text [106, 213, 477, 235]<|/det|>- Teachers enjoy long vacations.
<|det|>text [59, 245, 570, 269]<|/det|>◆ with the names of holidays. For example,
<|det|>text [106, 269, 538, 290]<|/det|>• I will meet my uncle during Easter.
<|det|>text [59, 302, 405, 323]<|/det|>◆ with the names of months.
<|det|>text [105, 325, 250, 346]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [105, 347, 532, 368]<|/det|>• Last September, he was in London.
<|det|>text [59, 380, 438, 425]<|/det|>◆ with the names of the days of the week. For example,
<|det|>text [105, 426, 441, 448]<|/det|>• We will go out on Sunday.
<|det|>text [59, 459, 408, 504]<|/det|>◆ with the names of seasons.
For example,
<|det|>text [105, 505, 521, 546]<|/det|>- Siberian cranes visit the sanctuary in winter.
<|det|>text [58, 559, 538, 602]<|/det|>◆ when we speak of the time of the day or with the names of meals.
<|det|>text [105, 604, 245, 625]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [105, 626, 408, 646]<|/det|>• I should finish by noon.
<|det|>text [105, 647, 492, 668]<|/det|>• What shall we have for dinner?
<|det|>text [58, 681, 502, 791]<|/det|>◆ with words such as ‘hospital’ ‘university’ or ‘school’, when they are used for their intended purpose, that is, medical, educational, etc. For example,
<|det|>text [102, 792, 521, 835]<|/det|>- He'll have to stay in hospital for a while.
<|det|>title [577, 336, 640, 360]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [717, 324, 771, 360]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [590, 372, 900, 432]<|/det|>We use the with a plural or uncountable noun, when we are talking of something specific.
<|det|>text [614, 433, 732, 451]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [614, 453, 876, 491]<|/det|>- Flowers make a room look bright.
<|det|>text [614, 492, 880, 531]<|/det|>• The flowers were delivered this morning.
<|det|>text [590, 535, 866, 594]<|/det|>◆ We use the if we know which season we are referring to.
For example,
<|det|>text [614, 596, 884, 635]<|/det|>• In the summer of 1999, we were all down with measles.
<|det|>text [590, 639, 870, 698]<|/det|>We use a, an or the when we refer to the time of a particular day. For example,
<|det|>text [614, 699, 880, 738]<|/det|>- When I woke up in the morning, I saw grey clouds.
<|det|>text [590, 741, 892, 821]<|/det|>We use articles when we refer to them as a particular place or buildings. For example,
<|det|>text [614, 822, 866, 841]<|/det|>- I went to the hospital
<|det|>text [644, 842, 866, 841]<|/det|>yesterday to see my aunt.
<|det|>page_number [848, 911, 882, 927]<|/det|>47
<PAGE><|det|>image [90, 36, 147, 66]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [150, 38, 790, 60]<|/det|>Circle the correct phrase to complete each sentence.
<|det|>list [160, 66, 974, 297]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [161, 66, 880, 89]<|/det|>1. The snow leopard/A snow leopard is close to extinction.
<|det|>text [161, 90, 757, 113]<|/det|>2. The novel/A novel is a popular form of fiction.
<|det|>text [161, 114, 725, 136]<|/det|>3. He lives near the church/church by the bay.
<|det|>text [161, 137, 763, 158]<|/det|>4. A fire broke out at school/a school in Chennai.
<|det|>text [161, 160, 854, 183]<|/det|>5. They had to spend night/the night at the Delhi airport.
<|det|>text [161, 184, 974, 207]<|/det|>6. Writing the letter/a letter is sometimes better than telephoning.
<|det|>text [161, 208, 847, 229]<|/det|>7. The aeroplane/An aeroplane has revolutionised travel.
<|det|>text [161, 230, 901, 252]<|/det|>8. The schools/Schools are using computers more and more.
<|det|>text [161, 254, 960, 297]<|/det|>9. Giving the card/a card can be a beautiful way to show that you care.
<|det|>text [150, 300, 847, 347]<|/det|>10. The magazines/Magazines all over Asia published the photograph.
<|det|>text [150, 348, 940, 391]<|/det|>11. Today the fridge/a fridge is an essential commodity in every household.
<|det|>text [150, 394, 960, 438]<|/det|>12. Can I drive you to the university/university? I am going in that direction.
<|det|>image [80, 445, 147, 470]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 445, 978, 492]<|/det|>Read the paragraph. Some articles have been omitted from it. Fill in the missing articles and rewrite the paragraph.
<|det|>text [150, 497, 985, 766]<|/det|>I left place eager to learn. Everything had name, and each name gave birth to new thought. As we returned to house, every object I touched seemed to quiver with life. I entered house. I remembered broken doll. It was gift from Miss Sullivan. I felt my way to hearth and picked up pieces. My eyes filled with tears. I was filled with sense of regret. Incident proved to be turning point in my life. Not only had I learnt importance of words, I had also understood what nice teacher I had. I had been given opportunity to explore wide world of language. Children who hear acquire language without any particular effort. Deaf child is at disadvantage. The process of learning language is slow one. The deaf child does not learn in month. It was long time before I had something appropriate to say at right time.
<|det|>title [96, 793, 320, 813]<|/det|>Demonstratives
<|det|>image [98, 824, 160, 866]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 822, 851, 867]<|/det|>Let us revise what you have learnt about the use of the four demonstratives—this, that, these and those.
<|det|>text [94, 888, 801, 911]<|/det|>Demonstrative determiners help to point to specific nouns.
<|det|>page_number [127, 929, 159, 946]<|/det|>48
<PAGE><|det|>text [45, 56, 892, 125]<|/det|>We use this and these to refer to people or objects that are nearby while that and those are used in the context of people or objects that are at a distance. For example,
<|det|>list [48, 132, 536, 200]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [48, 132, 495, 153]<|/det|>• This book is heavier than that one.
<|det|>text [48, 155, 536, 197]<|/det|>• These mangoes are sweeter than those ones.
<|det|>text [45, 209, 502, 300]<|/det|>As you can see in the above sentences, we use this and that before singular nouns while these and those are used before plural nouns.
<|det|>title [45, 315, 210, 335]<|/det|>Possessives
<|det|>text [47, 344, 310, 364]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [48, 366, 474, 412]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [48, 366, 474, 388]<|/det|>• I gave my sandwich to his brother.
<|det|>text [49, 388, 370, 412]<|/det|>- Put my book in her bag.
<|det|>text [45, 429, 830, 475]<|/det|>The highlighted adjectives show possession or belonging and are possessive determiners.
<|det|>text [47, 509, 915, 577]<|/det|>The possessive determiners are my, our, your, his, her, its and their. These can be used before singular and plural nouns regardless of whether they are countable or uncountable.
<|det|>title [49, 592, 248, 614]<|/det|>Interrogatives
<|det|>text [49, 621, 310, 641]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [50, 643, 430, 689]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [51, 643, 381, 665]<|/det|>• Which sari should I buy?
<|det|>text [51, 665, 429, 688]<|/det|>• What book are they reading?
<|det|>text [47, 714, 865, 781]<|/det|>The highlighted adjectives are used in interrogative sentences to gather information about the noun in question and are called interrogative determiners.
<|det|>text [47, 818, 794, 866]<|/det|>The words what, which, whose, how much and how many are interrogatives and they can be used before any noun.
<|det|>page_number [856, 918, 889, 934]<|/det|>49
<PAGE><|det|>title [105, 44, 265, 66]<|/det|>Quantifiers
<|det|>text [104, 73, 365, 94]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [105, 97, 592, 144]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 97, 500, 119]<|/det|>• Can you lend me some sugar?
<|det|>text [105, 120, 591, 144]<|/det|>• Few people turned up for the meeting.
<|det|>text [101, 169, 958, 217]<|/det|>The highlighted words provide information about the quantity of a noun and are called quantifiers.
<|det|>text [100, 253, 972, 325]<|/det|>Some common quantifiers are any, much, many, several, little, some and few. These can be used to suggest number as well as quantity and are accordingly used with countable or uncountable nouns.
<|det|>text [101, 339, 931, 386]<|/det|>We use many, several and few with plural countable nouns since they suggest number. For example, many lions, several bushes.
<|det|>text [101, 399, 922, 448]<|/det|>The quantifiers much and little refer to quantity and can only be used before uncountable nouns. For example, much work, little hope.
<|det|>text [101, 461, 855, 530]<|/det|>The words some and any can be used before countable as well as uncountable nouns. However, we generally use some in affirmative sentences and any in negative sentences.
<|det|>list [105, 532, 545, 621]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 532, 545, 574]<|/det|>- Some houses are being renovated in that area.
<|det|>text [105, 577, 543, 620]<|/det|>- I don't have any money to spare at the moment.
<|det|>title [104, 637, 285, 658]<|/det|>Distributives
<|det|>text [103, 666, 361, 687]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [105, 689, 645, 803]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 689, 512, 711]<|/det|>• Neither boy deserves this award.
<|det|>text [105, 713, 517, 755]<|/det|>- You can take either road to reach his house.
<|det|>text [105, 758, 557, 780]<|/det|>• Every student must attend assembly.
<|det|>text [105, 781, 695, 803]<|/det|>• She has solved each sample paper at least twice.
<|det|>text [580, 545, 648, 568]<|/det|>Note.
<|det|>text [580, 583, 960, 644]<|/det|>We also use any when we wish to suggest that we are not looking for something specific. For example,
<|det|>text [581, 645, 956, 666]<|/det|>• We can watch any movie that you like.
<|det|>text [98, 830, 948, 878]<|/det|>The highlighted words point out that the people or things mentioned are to be considered separately and are thus called distributives.
<|det|>page_number [129, 929, 161, 946]<|/det|>50
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 55, 796, 96]<|/det|>C. Choose appropriate determiners from the box to complete the sentences.
<|det|>text [182, 121, 249, 139]<|/det|>some
<|det|>text [182, 147, 264, 166]<|/det|>several
<|det|>text [349, 121, 410, 139]<|/det|>each
<|det|>text [321, 149, 390, 170]<|/det|>many
<|det|>text [468, 121, 526, 139]<|/det|>their
<|det|>text [468, 148, 525, 168]<|/det|>both
<|det|>text [599, 121, 674, 139]<|/det|>either
<|det|>text [614, 149, 656, 169]<|/det|>my
<|det|>text [743, 121, 834, 139]<|/det|>neither
<|det|>text [765, 149, 808, 167]<|/det|>this
<|det|>text [103, 190, 854, 211]<|/det|>1. I will write ____ name on ____ book.
<|det|>text [101, 216, 790, 238]<|/det|>2. The children are walking with ____ parents.
<|det|>text [102, 242, 729, 263]<|/det|>3. ____ trees were uprooted in the storm.
<|det|>text [102, 267, 720, 289]<|/det|>4. ____ team won the game. It was a tie.
<|det|>text [102, 293, 635, 314]<|/det|>5. You can wear it on ____ hand.
<|det|>text [102, 318, 842, 340]<|/det|>6. ____ animals are in danger of becoming extinct.
<|det|>text [102, 343, 859, 365]<|/det|>7. ____ sisters lived in the same town after marriage.
<|det|>text [102, 369, 842, 413]<|/det|>8. The gardener put ____ soil into ____ flower pot.
<|det|>text [55, 429, 893, 474]<|/det|>D: How many determiners does each sentence have? Identify them as well as the nouns they help to indicate.
<|det|>text [117, 483, 424, 506]<|/det|>1. I did not buy any food.
<|det|>text [117, 509, 421, 529]<|/det|>2. The French are artistic.
<|det|>text [117, 534, 570, 556]<|/det|>3. Have you seen many tourists today?
<|det|>text [116, 559, 679, 581]<|/det|>4. Many horses were disqualified from the race.
<|det|>text [117, 585, 451, 606]<|/det|>5. I did not put much water.
<|det|>text [117, 610, 529, 632]<|/det|>6. Call all your friends to the party.
<|det|>text [117, 635, 497, 656]<|/det|>7. I waited an hour for the train.
<|det|>text [117, 661, 567, 683]<|/det|>8. Those men were arrested yesterday.
<|det|>text [117, 686, 457, 708]<|/det|>9. This animal looks healthy.
<|det|>text [105, 711, 472, 732]<|/det|>10. All the milk has been used.
<|det|>text [105, 736, 570, 758]<|/det|>11. We couldn't decide on either book.
<|det|>text [105, 762, 645, 785]<|/det|>12. These last few days have been exhausting.
<|det|>text [105, 788, 640, 810]<|/det|>13. The yellow roses in the garden have died.
<|det|>text [104, 813, 862, 837]<|/det|>14. He is the architect for each project planned by the company.
<|det|>text [104, 840, 790, 862]<|/det|>15. They gave a chocolate to every student on the last day.
<|det|>text [104, 865, 830, 909]<|/det|>16. The elephant is a majestic creature that inhabits the jungles of India.
<|det|>page_number [857, 918, 887, 935]<|/det|>51
<PAGE><|det|>image [58, 42, 117, 78]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [145, 44, 933, 91]<|/det|>There is a determiner missing in each line of this passage. Mark the place with (/) and write the missing word in the given space.
<|det|>text [145, 96, 785, 141]<|/det|>Great was the grief amongst village schoolboys when Tom drove off to meet the coach on way to school.
<|det|>text [145, 143, 725, 211]<|/det|>Each of them had given him little present of best that he had, and his small private box was full of kinds of miscellaneous boys' wealth.
<|det|>text [145, 213, 793, 258]<|/det|>Poor Jacob, in floods of tears, had offered lame pet hedgehog but Tom had been obliged to refuse gift.
<|det|>text [145, 259, 793, 329]<|/det|>Tom had given them great tea under the big elm in playground, for which Madam Brown had supplied biggest cake ever seen in the village.
<|det|>text [145, 330, 585, 352]<|/det|>Tom was really sorry to leave boys,
<|det|>text [145, 353, 603, 373]<|/det|>but sorrow was not unmixed with the
<|det|>text [145, 375, 709, 398]<|/det|>pride and excitement of taking new step in life.
<|det|>text [145, 399, 720, 421]<|/det|>And feeling carried him through his first parting
<|det|>text [145, 422, 760, 444]<|/det|>from mother better than could have been expected.
<|det|>image [65, 475, 117, 515]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [145, 479, 879, 503]<|/det|>Complete the following dialogue using suitable determiners.
<|det|>text [145, 504, 667, 526]<|/det|>(A word or a group of words may be used.)
<|det|>text [145, 530, 945, 576]<|/det|>Rajiv: I have lost ____ pen. ____ pen is in that box. May I use it?
<|det|>text [145, 577, 978, 622]<|/det|>Vineet: Of course. I have ____ pens. I have ____ pen in my pocket too.
<|det|>text [145, 623, 858, 691]<|/det|>Rajiv: I have recently purchased ____ pens, but unfortunately I have left them at home. By the way ____ pen writes very well.
<|det|>text [145, 692, 978, 757]<|/det|>Vineet: I don't blame you for having forgotten to bring ____ pencil box. Aren't you shifting to ____ apartment near the lakes?
<|det|>text [145, 760, 945, 806]<|/det|>Rajiv: ____ neighbours and ____ are helping us do ____ packing.
<|det|>text [145, 807, 972, 853]<|/det|>Vineet: If you had shifted during ____ vacations, I could have helped.
<|det|>page_number [124, 932, 156, 949]<|/det|>52
<PAGE><|det|>title [70, 58, 475, 96]<|/det|>Letter Writing—I
<|det|>header [728, 18, 999, 144]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>image [70, 160, 135, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [142, 156, 900, 201]<|/det|>Letters are an important means of communication, both for official and personal purposes. We write letters to describe an event, to
<|det|>text [68, 203, 877, 270]<|/det|>provide information, to make a request or turn it down. Through letters, we convey emotions or share ideas or events. Letters are of two types—formal and informal.
<|det|>text [67, 282, 915, 395]<|/det|>Informal letters are those that are written for unofficial purposes, while formal letters are written to institutions or officials. When writing any letter, you must keep the format in mind. You have learnt what the parts of an informal and formal letter are. Let us revise these formats along with the marks that will be allotted to each of these parts in the exam—
<|det|>title [120, 413, 428, 435]<|/det|>Layout of an informal letter
<|det|>list [89, 452, 411, 571]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [90, 452, 408, 473]<|/det|>• sender's address: 1 mark
<|det|>text [91, 476, 283, 495]<|/det|>• date: ½ mark
<|det|>text [91, 498, 345, 517]<|/det|>- salutation: ½ mark
<|det|>text [91, 520, 370, 540]<|/det|>- subscription: ½ mark
<|det|>text [91, 542, 334, 564]<|/det|>• signature: ½ mark
<|det|>title [575, 415, 854, 438]<|/det|>Layout of a formal letter
<|det|>list [527, 451, 901, 585]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [527, 451, 901, 471]<|/det|>- writer's address, date: 1 mark
<|det|>text [528, 474, 886, 514]<|/det|>- name & address of receiver: ½ mark
<|det|>text [528, 518, 898, 539]<|/det|>- subject & salutation: ½ mark
<|det|>text [528, 541, 808, 561]<|/det|>- subscription: ½ mark
<|det|>text [528, 563, 771, 584]<|/det|>• signature: ½ mark
<|det|>title [94, 622, 158, 646]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [474, 617, 526, 650]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [89, 659, 882, 720]<|/det|>There will usually be two marks allotted to the content of the letter, where you will be marked on whether you have covered all the points mentioned in the question. Another five marks are for language and style, in other words, the substance of the letter.
<|det|>title [66, 755, 300, 775]<|/det|>Points to remember
<|det|>list [67, 793, 634, 882]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 793, 531, 813]<|/det|>- Do not use abbreviations in the letter.
<|det|>text [67, 816, 631, 837]<|/det|>• Write the month. If June, write June and not 6.
<|det|>text [67, 839, 603, 860]<|/det|>- Do not address your friend as ‘Dear Friend’.
<|det|>text [67, 862, 461, 882]<|/det|>• Never write ‘Respected Father’.
<|det|>page_number [859, 911, 892, 929]<|/det|>53
