<PAGE><|det|>list [98, 28, 966, 266]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [99, 28, 936, 78]<|/det|>- Opening lines like 'How are you? I hope this letter finds you in the pink of health' are old-fashioned and should not be used.
<|det|>text [99, 78, 912, 125]<|/det|>- The first word of the subscription must begin with a capital letter. For example, Yours lovingly.
<|det|>text [99, 125, 972, 193]<|/det|>- The writer of an informal letter must use only his/her first name to sign, whereas in a formal letter if the signature is not legible, the full name must be printed below.
<|det|>text [99, 194, 972, 266]<|/det|>- Be careful about the spacing in the body of the letter. The paragraphs must all be in line. The placement of the heading and subscription too should correspond.
<|det|>title [130, 304, 188, 328]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [115, 341, 960, 425]<|/det|>Do not mix the formats. If you select the classical or traditional format (the indented style), continue writing it. Do not write the heading using the classical format and the left-aligned format for the subscription and the signature. The same goes for indentation. If the heading is indented, then the subscription must be indented too.
<|det|>title [140, 473, 669, 496]<|/det|>A Now, write informal letters on these topics.
<|det|>list [149, 501, 965, 895]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 501, 965, 590]<|/det|>1. Your friend wishes to join your school's drama club. Write to him telling him what is expected of him in this club and that you would be willing to recommend him to the teacher in charge of the club.
<|det|>text [149, 593, 951, 682]<|/det|>2. You celebrated your birthday recently by going to an orphanage. Write a letter to your friend describing how you celebrated your birthday there and how this event has changed the course of your life.
<|det|>text [149, 685, 935, 754]<|/det|>3. A friend of yours has just won an All-India Essay Writing Competition. Write a letter congratulating him/her and indicate what you liked best about the essay.
<|det|>text [148, 755, 877, 800]<|/det|>4. You are in a boarding school. Write a letter to your mother explaining to her your poor performance in your last exam.
<|det|>text [148, 801, 968, 895]<|/det|>5. You have been selected for the bravery award for children for the year 2010. Write a letter to your friend expressing your feelings of joy and excitement and give him a brief account of the brave act for which you are going to be awarded.
<|det|>page_number [114, 927, 146, 946]<|/det|>54
<PAGE><|det|>text [94, 48, 904, 94]<|/det|>6. Write a letter to your cousin thanking her for the information that she has sent you for a project you are working on.
<|det|>text [94, 94, 895, 139]<|/det|>7. You want to go on an educational excursion from school. Write a letter to your father requesting him for money for the same.
<|det|>list [94, 48, 904, 139]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [96, 155, 588, 178]<|/det|>Now, write formal letters on these topics.
<|det|>text [95, 183, 867, 249]<|/det|>1. Write a letter to your principal telling him about the ‘ragging’ that is going on in your school. Suggest ways in which this can be stopped.
<|det|>text [94, 251, 876, 340]<|/det|>2. You, along with your classmates, have been a victim of food poisoning. Write a letter to the manager of the school catering service, complaining about the poor quality of food and services at the cafeteria that has resulted in these cases of food poisoning.
<|det|>text [94, 341, 862, 406]<|/det|>3. Write a letter to a famous personality asking him to inaugurate the new school auditorium, giving him details of the venue, place and time.
<|det|>text [94, 408, 882, 475]<|/det|>4. Write a letter to the director of a television channel complaining about the quality of the programmes. Suggest ways to improve the quality and presentation of the programmes.
<|det|>text [95, 476, 907, 629]<|/det|>5. Imagine that you bought a radio from a well-known shop, and that it broke down soon after you purchased it. You took it back to be repaired and it worked satisfactorily for a few days before breaking down again. On your next visit to the shop, the assistant was impatient and rude and said that he could not help you. Write a letter to the manager of the shop complaining about the product and the unhelpful shop assistant.
<|det|>text [94, 630, 880, 674]<|/det|>6. You have lost your purse at a shopping mall. Write a letter to the manager of the mall requesting him to help you.
<|det|>text [95, 675, 898, 808]<|/det|>7. While on a holiday recently, you and your family spent a week at a hotel. However, the poor facilities and the rude behaviour of the hotel staff made your stay very unpleasant. Write a letter of complaint to the manager of the hotel, expressing your feelings of annoyance, and suggesting what could be done to improve the services provided by the hotel.
<|det|>page_number [845, 914, 879, 931]<|/det|>55
<PAGE><|det|>title [92, 38, 147, 78]<|/det|>7
<|det|>title [619, 35, 980, 74]<|/det|>Simple Tenses
<|det|>image [89, 142, 153, 186]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 141, 892, 188]<|/det|>You have learnt that the tense of a verb in a sentence denotes the time when the action is taking place.
<|det|>text [85, 200, 972, 246]<|/det|>Look at the highlighted verbs in this passage. Can you say which tense is indicated by each?
<|det|>text [85, 260, 925, 285]<|/det|>Two girls were standing at their front gate one sunny afternoon in July.
<|det|>text [85, 289, 655, 359]<|/det|>'The car ought to be here by now,' said Nora. 'I hope it hasn't had a puncture or anything. I'm longing to see Mike and Jack.'
<|det|>text [85, 364, 651, 435]<|/det|>‘So am I,’ said Peggy, her sister. ‘I wonder if Paul will be with them. Is he going to spend his holidays with us or go back to Baronia?’
<|det|>text [85, 439, 608, 463]<|/det|>Paul was the little Prince of Baronia, a great
<|det|>text [84, 463, 956, 508]<|/det|>friend of Nora, Peggy, Mike and Jack. He went to the same school as the boys, and had had plenty of adventures with them.
<|det|>image [732, 301, 986, 455]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [83, 513, 962, 583]<|/det|>'I expect he will spend a few days with us first,' said Nora, swinging on the gate. 'Then he'll have to go back to Baronia to see his parents and all his many brothers and sisters!'
<|det|>text [81, 588, 961, 638]<|/det|>'It's a silly idea, our school breaking up two whole days before the boys,' said Peggy. 'We go back earlier too—that's even more of a nuisance!'
<|det|>image [80, 645, 131, 690]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 649, 931, 694]<|/det|>We say that there are three tenses of a verb—present, past and future—time now, time then and time to come. These tenses are
<|det|>text [80, 696, 890, 745]<|/det|>further subdivided. Let us now look at simple tenses—simple present, simple past and simple future.
<|det|>title [79, 761, 382, 785]<|/det|>Simple Present Tense
<|det|>text [78, 793, 497, 815]<|/det|>We use the simple present tense—
<|det|>list [78, 817, 605, 886]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [78, 817, 580, 839]<|/det|>◆ to state a universal truth. For example,
<|det|>text [116, 840, 605, 862]<|/det|>- The moon is the earth's only satellite.
<|det|>text [116, 864, 575, 885]<|/det|>• The earth revolves around the sun.
<|det|>page_number [107, 923, 140, 938]<|/det|>56
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 60, 885, 107]<|/det|>◆ to describe something that has been happening for some time and is still continuing. For example,
<|det|>list [113, 107, 490, 150]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 107, 465, 125]<|/det|>• The Mehtas live in Mumbai.
<|det|>text [113, 128, 316, 148]<|/det|>- She is a singer.
<|det|>text [65, 157, 888, 181]<|/det|>◆ to refer to something that happens regularly or habitually. For example,
<|det|>list [113, 181, 674, 222]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 181, 674, 202]<|/det|>• My father reads the newspaper every morning.
<|det|>text [113, 203, 326, 221]<|/det|>• He swims a lot.
<|det|>text [65, 232, 888, 255]<|/det|>◆ in proverbs that are considered to be permanent truths. For example,
<|det|>list [113, 256, 521, 296]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 256, 521, 277]<|/det|>• A thing of beauty is a joy forever.
<|det|>text [113, 278, 443, 296]<|/det|>- Fortune favours the brave.
<|det|>title [67, 315, 312, 338]<|/det|>Simple Past Tense
<|det|>text [67, 343, 430, 365]<|/det|>We use the simple past tense—
<|det|>list [68, 366, 510, 472]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [68, 366, 510, 431]<|/det|>◆ when we refer to an action that has already been completed before the time of speaking. For example,
<|det|>text [113, 432, 378, 452]<|/det|>- It snowed yesterday.
<|det|>text [113, 453, 446, 472]<|/det|>• She cooked last afternoon.
<|det|>text [571, 345, 636, 368]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [717, 338, 768, 372]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [567, 381, 895, 459]<|/det|>Adverbs or adverb phrases like yesterday, a week ago, four months ago, are usually added to sentences describing past action.
<|det|>list [68, 484, 765, 535]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [68, 484, 765, 506]<|/det|>◆ to indicate that something was done in the past as a habit.
<|det|>text [113, 507, 262, 528]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [113, 528, 578, 572]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 528, 578, 550]<|/det|>• My mother always enjoyed gardening.
<|det|>text [113, 550, 517, 571]<|/det|>• As a young girl, I exercised a lot.
<|det|>text [68, 581, 868, 624]<|/det|>◆ to express an action that occurred over a period of time in the past. For example,
<|det|>list [113, 624, 586, 647]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 624, 586, 647]<|/det|>• I lived in New Delhi till the age of five.
<|det|>title [67, 663, 341, 685]<|/det|>Simple Future Tense
<|det|>text [66, 691, 448, 712]<|/det|>We use the simple future tense—
<|det|>list [67, 713, 905, 802]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 713, 905, 755]<|/det|>◆ to say what we think or know for certain will happen in the future. For example,
<|det|>text [113, 757, 543, 778]<|/det|>• I shall repair my bicycle tomorrow.
<|det|>text [113, 779, 502, 801]<|/det|>• Malti will visit us next Monday.
<|det|>text [66, 812, 789, 835]<|/det|>◆ to make a promise or to offer to do something. For example,
<|det|>list [113, 835, 574, 877]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 835, 574, 856]<|/det|>• We'll complete this project tomorrow.
<|det|>text [113, 857, 562, 877]<|/det|>- You look feverish. I'll call the doctor.
<|det|>page_number [856, 902, 886, 920]<|/det|>57
<PAGE><|det|>title [105, 53, 172, 77]<|/det|>Note.
<|det|>list [125, 88, 948, 187]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [125, 88, 948, 146]<|/det|>- Shall is traditionally used with the first person singular (!) or plural (we), but the use of shall is no longer common in spoken English. Increasingly, shall is replaced by will or contractions are used. Thus we say, I'll or she'll instead of I shall, or she will.
<|det|>text [125, 147, 928, 187]<|/det|>- Shall and will are also used as modal auxiliaries to express the mood or attitude of the speaker. You will learn more about this in a later chapter.
<|det|>title [98, 227, 905, 294]<|/det|>A. Fill in the blanks with correct forms of the verbs given in the brackets. Read each sentence carefully to understand the time reference.
<|det|>text [128, 301, 818, 323]<|/det|>1. I ____ in South Africa for ten years. (live)
<|det|>text [127, 325, 862, 395]<|/det|>2. The girls ____ every day for three hours. They ____ in a competition next month. (exercise, participate)
<|det|>text [127, 395, 951, 439]<|/det|>3. She ____ school last year. She ____ in a boarding school now. (leave, study)
<|det|>text [126, 440, 815, 484]<|/det|>4. Get ready. Here ____ the bus. We ____ right away. (come, leave)
<|det|>text [126, 485, 925, 529]<|/det|>5. Tomorrow ____ Sunday. We ____ him tomorrow. (be, see)
<|det|>text [125, 530, 922, 576]<|/det|>6. My friends ____ the prime minister yesterday. He ____ to look into the matter. (meet, promise)
<|det|>text [125, 577, 890, 645]<|/det|>7. Our maths teacher ____ the sitar very well. He ____ during the Children's Day function last year. (play, perform)
<|det|>text [124, 647, 631, 714]<|/det|>8. Mr Aziz ____ next month.
He ____ for a month after that. (retire, travel)
<|det|>image [668, 616, 986, 720]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [73, 731, 667, 755]<|/det|>B. Rewrite the paragraph in simple past tense.
<|det|>text [120, 761, 903, 806]<|/det|>I am an actor. I am a member of a theatre group. I stage plays regularly. I also train young pupils who wish to become actors.
<|det|>text [120, 808, 988, 855]<|/det|>I need a small flat to train them. I wish I had some more money. I like enthusiastic and committed children. They make fine actors.
<|det|>page_number [94, 919, 125, 935]<|/det|>58
<PAGE><|det|>title [50, 48, 631, 75]<|/det|>C. Rewrite the paragraph in simple future tense.
<|det|>text [114, 78, 898, 214]<|/det|>I walk to school every day. My friends Ananya and Meghna accompany me. They come to my house at 8 a.m. We leave immediately. On the way, we buy the newspaper. Our teacher joins us near the post office. Sometimes, she picks up the mail from the postman. We reach school at 8.30 a.m. The school starts at 8.45 a.m. We always begin with a prayer.
<|det|>text [73, 230, 845, 273]<|/det|>D. Fill in the blanks with the correct form of the verbs given in the brackets.
<|det|>text [116, 281, 811, 305]<|/det|>'What ____ (be) the matter with your tooth, Tom?'
<|det|>text [116, 313, 722, 336]<|/det|>'One of them is loose, and it ____ (ache).'
<|det|>text [114, 345, 885, 459]<|/det|>'There, there now, don't begin that groaning again. ____
(open) your mouth. Well, your tooth is loose, but you're not going to die about that. Mary, ____ (get) me a silk thread and a chunk of fire out of the kitchen,' Aunt Polly ____ (say) calmly.
<|det|>text [114, 467, 909, 535]<|/det|>Tom said, 'Oh, please don't pull it out, it ____ (do) not ____ (hurt) any more. Please don't, Auntie; I don't want to stay home from school.'
<|det|>text [115, 544, 860, 636]<|/det|>'Oh, so all this row ____ (be) because you ____ (think) you would get to stay home from school? Tom, Tom, I ____ (love) you so, and you seem to try every way you can to break my old heart with your outrageousness.'
<|det|>text [113, 643, 905, 711]<|/det|>By this time the dental instruments ____ (be) ready. The old ____ (make) one end of the silk thread fast to Tom's tooth with a loop and ____ (tie) the other to the bed-post.
<|det|>text [113, 718, 855, 789]<|/det|>Then, she ____ (seize) the chunk of fire and suddenly ____ (thrust) it almost into the boy's face. The tooth ____ (hang) dangling by the bed-post.
<|det|>page_number [848, 918, 880, 934]<|/det|>59
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 297, 136]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [509, 38, 978, 119]<|/det|>Monkey Trouble in
Yelahanka
<|det|>text [100, 146, 933, 214]<|/det|>How would it feel if you were to come back home and find your house ransacked by a troop of monkeys? Read the trauma of one such unfortunate victim.
<|det|>text [100, 229, 951, 368]<|/det|>A hapless resident of Yelahanka rubbed her eyes in disbelief when she returned home from work one evening and pinched herself to make sure it was not a nightmare. But for a dozen mangoes that had fallen inexplicably from the tree at the gate, hinting a possible monkey attack, nothing could have prepared her for the vision that was about to unfold on the other side of the door.
<|det|>text [101, 383, 596, 665]<|/det|>Indeed, the work of twenty monkeys who push open a window and spend many happy hours in the house, ripping through every edible item in the kitchen, eating some and saving the rest to play games with, cannot be a pretty picture. A uniform carpet of flour, torn out of a new packet, covered the floor from the kitchen to the dining table, foot-printed by frantic monkey paws. Over this, a generous smattering of moong dal emptied from a bottle. Bottles of pickle and jam, which, to the monkeys' frustration, perhaps could not be opened as easily,
<|det|>image [612, 377, 975, 658]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 666, 954, 777]<|/det|>were effortlessly broken and their insides licked clean. Mango seeds and banana peels, noodles—hakka noodles being their clear favourite—had been munched on, and the rest saved up for a take-away, as an empty packet fluttering on a tree indicated the next morning.
<|det|>text [100, 791, 977, 886]<|/det|>A lesson was learnt: these precocious simians with their opposable thumbs can clearly do more than they will let on. They can cunningly work their way around cartons of badaam milk and orange juice; they can open taps and empty out entire water tanks as they did on this occasion.
<|det|>page_number [132, 926, 161, 943]<|/det|>60
<PAGE><|det|>text [44, 53, 889, 186]<|/det|>And they can most delicately separate chocolates from their individual wrappings without as much as a tear in the foil. Even as the resident, blood boiling, saw visions of bumping them off with a shotgun, a quick consolation came from the fact that even though the monkeys were pitilessly thorough in their destruction of edibles, there was no danger to furniture, upholstery, books or any other property.
<|det|>title [45, 207, 762, 230]<|/det|>A. Complete and rewrite these sentences in your notebook.
<|det|>list [98, 235, 909, 415]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 235, 903, 258]<|/det|>1. A resident of Yelahanka returned home to find ____.
<|det|>text [98, 258, 907, 303]<|/det|>2. As the monkeys could not open the bottles of pickle and jam, they ____.
<|det|>text [98, 303, 722, 326]<|/det|>3. The floor, from the kitchen to the dining table, was
<|det|>text [98, 348, 767, 371]<|/det|>4. The opposable thumbs of the monkeys helped them to
<|det|>text [98, 393, 842, 415]<|/det|>5. The monkeys, however, did no damage to ____.
<|det|>title [45, 439, 673, 461]<|/det|>B. Choose the correct answers for these statements.
<|det|>text [98, 467, 880, 490]<|/det|>1. When the resident of Yelahanka returned home, she thought she
<|det|>list [134, 491, 411, 558]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 491, 388, 513]<|/det|>a. was daydreaming.
<|det|>text [135, 513, 408, 535]<|/det|>b. having a nightmare.
<|det|>text [135, 535, 374, 557]<|/det|>c. had lost her way.
<|det|>text [98, 562, 652, 585]<|/det|>2. The monkeys had entered the house through
<|det|>list [134, 587, 321, 650]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 587, 262, 605]<|/det|>a. a door.
<|det|>text [135, 607, 321, 629]<|/det|>b. the balcony.
<|det|>text [135, 631, 300, 650]<|/det|>c. a window.
<|det|>text [98, 657, 744, 701]<|/det|>3. The next day, the resident, found an empty packet of ____ fluttering on the tree.
<|det|>list [134, 703, 348, 770]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 703, 348, 724]<|/det|>a. hakka noodles
<|det|>text [135, 726, 240, 747]<|/det|>b. pasta
<|det|>text [135, 748, 305, 770]<|/det|>c. moong dal
<|det|>text [98, 776, 569, 797]<|/det|>4. The water tanks had been emptied as
<|det|>list [134, 799, 574, 866]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 799, 552, 819]<|/det|>a. a hole was drilled into the tanks.
<|det|>text [135, 821, 532, 842]<|/det|>b. the monkeys had taken a bath.
<|det|>text [135, 843, 574, 866]<|/det|>c. the monkeys had opened the taps.
<|det|>page_number [851, 916, 882, 934]<|/det|>61
<PAGE><|det|>text [155, 42, 400, 64]<|/det|>5. The monkeys ate
<|det|>list [195, 67, 440, 135]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [196, 67, 440, 87]<|/det|>a. only sweetmeats.
<|det|>text [196, 89, 423, 109]<|/det|>b. all edible items.
<|det|>text [196, 112, 363, 135]<|/det|>c. only fruits.
<|det|>title [103, 155, 816, 180]<|/det|>C. Find suitable words from the passage for these phrases.
<|det|>list [156, 187, 430, 325]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [156, 187, 406, 210]<|/det|>1. frightening dream
<|det|>text [157, 212, 282, 234]<|/det|>2. tearing
<|det|>text [158, 235, 294, 256]<|/det|>3. cleverly
<|det|>text [158, 258, 399, 280]<|/det|>4. act of comforting
<|det|>text [158, 282, 337, 303]<|/det|>5. very careful
<|det|>text [158, 304, 429, 325]<|/det|>6. done without regret
<|det|>image [454, 188, 978, 628]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [135, 930, 168, 947]<|/det|>62
<PAGE><|det|>title [65, 56, 506, 88]<|/det|>Continuous Tenses
<|det|>image [642, 0, 999, 155]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [65, 157, 125, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [137, 156, 843, 202]<|/det|>You have learnt that verbs in the continuous tense are used to denote actions that have not been completed.
<|det|>title [61, 222, 411, 243]<|/det|>Present Continuous Tense
<|det|>text [61, 251, 516, 273]<|/det|>The present continuous tense is used—
<|det|>text [61, 274, 440, 316]<|/det|>◆ to describe an action that is happening now. For example,
<|det|>list [61, 221, 516, 316]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [103, 318, 516, 339]<|/det|>• He is swimming in the lake now.
<|det|>text [104, 341, 440, 362]<|/det|>• Mohini is washing the car.
<|det|>list [103, 318, 516, 362]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 373, 915, 418]<|/det|>◆ to describe an action that has been going on over a period of time, but may not be going on at the time of speaking. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 419, 525, 441]<|/det|>• I am living with my grandparents.
<|det|>text [104, 442, 446, 463]<|/det|>• Murali is learning to drive.
<|det|>list [103, 419, 525, 463]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 474, 843, 496]<|/det|>◆ for an action that will take place in the near future. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 497, 831, 518]<|/det|>• We are going to have dinner at a Chinese restaurant tonight.
<|det|>text [104, 519, 550, 541]<|/det|>• I am going to the theatre tomorrow.
<|det|>list [103, 497, 831, 541]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [96, 565, 155, 588]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>title [81, 603, 411, 622]<|/det|>We do not use continuous tense for—
<|det|>text [99, 626, 526, 646]<|/det|>♥ verbs of sense and perception. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 647, 404, 666]<|/det|>• I hear the noise. (correct)
<|det|>text [175, 666, 480, 685]<|/det|>I am hearing the noise. (incorrect)
<|det|>text [151, 687, 401, 706]<|/det|>• I see the school. (correct)
<|det|>text [175, 707, 482, 726]<|/det|>I am seeing the school. (incorrect)
<|det|>text [99, 740, 625, 760]<|/det|>♥ verbs expressing state of mind or emotion. For example,
<|det|>text [150, 761, 377, 780]<|/det|>• I admire you. (correct)
<|det|>text [175, 781, 444, 801]<|/det|>I am admiring you. (incorrect)
<|det|>text [99, 815, 605, 835]<|/det|>♥ verbs of thinking or other mental activity. For example,
<|det|>text [150, 837, 450, 856]<|/det|>• I think you are wrong. (correct)
<|det|>text [175, 857, 527, 877]<|/det|>I am thinking you are wrong. (incorrect)
<|det|>page_number [864, 920, 895, 937]<|/det|>63
<PAGE><|det|>text [132, 51, 485, 71]<|/det|>♥ verbs of possession. For example,
<|det|>text [192, 73, 408, 91]<|/det|>- I own a car. (correct)
<|det|>text [218, 93, 486, 112]<|/det|>I am owning a car. (incorrect)
<|det|>image [78, 146, 136, 175]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [148, 145, 978, 190]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the present continuous forms of the verb given in the brackets.
<|det|>list [160, 195, 800, 428]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [165, 198, 714, 219]<|/det|>1. Vikram ____ (eat) a huge burger.
<|det|>text [164, 221, 799, 242]<|/det|>2. She ____ (bathe) her cat in the bath tub.
<|det|>text [164, 244, 719, 266]<|/det|>3. When ____ you ____ (go) home?
<|det|>text [163, 267, 770, 288]<|/det|>4. He ____ (talk) on the phone right now.
<|det|>text [163, 289, 928, 311]<|/det|>5. It ____ (rain) cats and dogs since the morning.
<|det|>text [163, 313, 956, 335]<|/det|>6. At present, I ____ (solve) some algebra problems.
<|det|>text [163, 336, 974, 357]<|/det|>7. Which film ____ (run) in the Globe Theatre this week?
<|det|>text [163, 359, 790, 381]<|/det|>8. What ____ you ____ (having) for breakfast?
<|det|>text [163, 383, 604, 404]<|/det|>9. The DJ ____ (play) music.
<|det|>text [151, 406, 701, 427]<|/det|>10. The students ____ (take) notes.
<|det|>title [92, 449, 402, 470]<|/det|>Past Continuous Tense
<|det|>text [90, 479, 514, 500]<|/det|>The past continuous tense is used—
<|det|>text [89, 501, 931, 571]<|/det|>◆ to describe an action going on at a point of time or over a period of time in the past. The time of action may or may not be indicated. For example,
<|det|>list [131, 572, 720, 618]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 572, 543, 593]<|/det|>• I was working till late last night.
<|det|>text [131, 594, 720, 617]<|/det|>• They were watching TV till 1:00 a.m. last night!
<|det|>text [85, 627, 940, 674]<|/det|>◆ to describe an action that was in progress when another action took place. For example,
<|det|>text [128, 675, 645, 698]<|/det|>• They were having dinner, when I arrived.
<|det|>text [127, 699, 736, 721]<|/det|>- When I met him, he was rehearsing for the show.
<|det|>text [82, 722, 934, 770]<|/det|>In the above sentences, the verbs in the past continuous tense describe longer actions while verbs in the simple past describe shorter actions.
<|det|>title [82, 792, 938, 838]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the past continuous forms of the verb given the brackets.
<|det|>text [131, 845, 821, 869]<|/det|>1. I ____ (take) a shower when the bell rang.
<|det|>text [131, 871, 692, 894]<|/det|>2. She ____ (study) late last night.
<|det|>list [131, 845, 821, 894]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [107, 932, 141, 949]<|/det|>64
<PAGE><|det|>text [121, 53, 885, 75]<|/det|>3. It ____ (snow) in Darjeeling when I arrived there.
<|det|>text [122, 76, 903, 97]<|/det|>4. She ____ (make) pudding when the guests arrived.
<|det|>text [122, 98, 916, 138]<|/det|>5. India ____ almost ____ (win) the game when it started to rain.
<|det|>text [122, 141, 900, 185]<|/det|>6. Varun ____ comfortably ____ (spend) his vacations when he was called to report to the front.
<|det|>text [122, 186, 740, 229]<|/det|>7. We ____ (sip) tea by the seaside and ____ (enjoy) a restful weekend.
<|det|>text [121, 230, 885, 252]<|/det|>8. I ____ (plan) to visit the mall, when you came by.
<|det|>text [122, 253, 785, 275]<|/det|>9. Amit ____ (play) cricket at three o'clock.
<|det|>text [115, 275, 878, 317]<|/det|>10. Mother ____ (walk) in the garden when it started to rain.
<|det|>list [115, 53, 916, 317]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [62, 335, 400, 357]<|/det|>Future Continuous Tense
<|det|>text [61, 364, 435, 385]<|/det|>The future continuous is used—
<|det|>text [61, 386, 923, 429]<|/det|>◆ to denote an action that will be in progress at some point of time in the future. For example,
<|det|>text [102, 430, 606, 452]<|/det|>• Rohan will be eating dinner at that time.
<|det|>text [61, 462, 724, 484]<|/det|>◆ to denote future events that are planned. For example,
<|det|>text [102, 485, 733, 507]<|/det|>• The Cabinet will be announcing the annual budget.
<|det|>list [61, 386, 923, 507]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [31, 526, 111, 585]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [111, 527, 905, 571]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the future continuous forms of the verb given the brackets.
<|det|>text [125, 578, 845, 621]<|/det|>1. I ____ (travel) on the Rajdhani Express by this time tomorrow.
<|det|>text [124, 623, 878, 668]<|/det|>2. She ____ (travel) to Paris next week if everything goes as planned.
<|det|>text [125, 668, 842, 689]<|/det|>3. They ____ (study) medicine after high school.
<|det|>text [125, 690, 830, 711]<|/det|>4. We ____ (meet) them tomorrow in Mumbai.
<|det|>text [125, 712, 875, 755]<|/det|>5. As usual, my brother ____ (play) his video game when I arrive home from work.
<|det|>text [125, 757, 812, 778]<|/det|>6. At this time next week I ____ (write) a test.
<|det|>text [125, 779, 923, 801]<|/det|>7. At five o'clock tomorrow, I ____ (work) in my office.
<|det|>text [125, 802, 805, 824]<|/det|>8. This evening we ____ (watch) a talk show.
<|det|>text [125, 825, 746, 846]<|/det|>9. We ____ (have) a quiz this weekend.
<|det|>text [115, 847, 805, 890]<|/det|>10. Rita Jeptoo ____ (inaugurate) the Mumbai Marathon.
<|det|>list [115, 578, 923, 890]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [867, 911, 898, 927]<|/det|>65
<PAGE><|det|>title [65, 32, 123, 61]<|/det|>D
<|det|>text [147, 35, 957, 104]<|/det|>Underline the verbs in these sentences and state whether they are in the simple present (SPr), present continuous (PrC), simple past (SP), past continuous (PC), simple future (SF) or future continuous (FC).
<|det|>list [160, 105, 970, 388]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [165, 109, 461, 130]<|/det|>1. We live in Dehradun.
<|det|>text [164, 132, 655, 153]<|/det|>2. He locked the door and left the house.
<|det|>text [165, 155, 545, 176]<|/det|>3. I am working for the Mehtas.
<|det|>text [164, 178, 696, 200]<|/det|>4. What were you doing at 9 p.m. last night?
<|det|>text [165, 201, 743, 223]<|/det|>5. What will you be doing on Saturday morning?
<|det|>text [165, 224, 657, 245]<|/det|>6. I will be flying to New York next week.
<|det|>text [165, 247, 641, 268]<|/det|>7. I learnt German when I was in Delhi.
<|det|>text [165, 270, 674, 293]<|/det|>8. My children will be playing at that time.
<|det|>text [164, 294, 816, 316]<|/det|>9. We were sitting at the cafeteria when the phone rang.
<|det|>text [151, 316, 968, 339]<|/det|>10. He walks slowly to the station and puts the bag on the platform.
<|det|>text [151, 340, 845, 361]<|/det|>11. I met her last month when she was visiting her mother.
<|det|>text [151, 362, 907, 385]<|/det|>12. While you were waiting for the bus, I walked to the station.
<|det|>image [848, 118, 986, 270]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [149, 407, 931, 454]<|/det|>Change the tense of the verb in these sentences as directed. Add time references and make other changes as required.
<|det|>list [149, 460, 938, 598]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 460, 938, 483]<|/det|>1. The students met the governor of the state. (future continuous)
<|det|>text [150, 484, 740, 506]<|/det|>2. We are listening to the radio. (past continuous)
<|det|>text [150, 507, 722, 528]<|/det|>3. I shall see him tomorrow. (future continuous)
<|det|>text [150, 530, 666, 551]<|/det|>4. He works in a mill. (present continuous)
<|det|>text [150, 553, 783, 598]<|/det|>5. The emperor will give the poor girl a precious ring. (present continuous)
<|det|>image [275, 631, 787, 895]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [127, 924, 157, 941]<|/det|>66
<PAGE><|det|>text [120, 54, 915, 99]<|/det|>6. I studied architecture in Ahmedabad and shall study mass communication in Delhi. (present continuous, future continuous)
<|det|>text [120, 99, 621, 120]<|/det|>7. Ronita went to Delhi. (past continuous)
<|det|>text [120, 121, 903, 163]<|/det|>8. The cashier opens the drawer and takes out the money. (present continuous)
<|det|>text [120, 164, 774, 187]<|/det|>9. As the daylight faded, we felt cold. (past continuous)
<|det|>text [108, 187, 888, 231]<|/det|>10. The snow was falling, the wind was blowing and the cars were skidding off the roads. (present continuous)
<|det|>text [108, 232, 753, 254]<|/det|>11. He has finished his homework. (future continuous)
<|det|>text [108, 254, 905, 297]<|/det|>12. Mr Sharma retires next month and his son joins work next week. (future continuous)
<|det|>list [108, 54, 915, 297]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [854, 914, 885, 931]<|/det|>67
<PAGE><|det|>header [45, 0, 300, 134]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [527, 29, 955, 68]<|/det|>Letter Writing—It
<|det|>title [103, 140, 402, 163]<|/det|>Letters to Newspapers
<|det|>text [99, 168, 949, 261]<|/det|>Letters to newspapers usually involve issues that pertain to more than one person, that is, to society in general or a large group of people. The tone of such a letter is important. It has to be persuasive, but at the same time, there is no scope for rudeness. The language has to be formal.
<|det|>list [102, 262, 936, 402]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [102, 262, 867, 308]<|/det|>- These letters should always be addressed to The Editor and they usually end with Yours truly or Yours faithfully.
<|det|>text [103, 310, 465, 330]<|/det|>• The form of salutation is Sir.
<|det|>text [103, 332, 931, 400]<|/det|>- The letter must address the concerned issue directly without beating about the bush. There should be a suggestion as to how the problem can be tackled or resolved.
<|det|>title [99, 417, 905, 466]<|/det|>Read this sample of a letter to the editor of a well-known newspaper protesting against thoughtless honking.
<|det|>text [121, 497, 348, 520]<|/det|>6 Gurusaday Road
<|det|>text [125, 522, 260, 542]<|/det|>Park Circus
<|det|>text [125, 544, 312, 566]<|/det|>Kolkata 700019
<|det|>text [121, 582, 280, 606]<|/det|>26 July 20XX
<|det|>text [121, 621, 245, 642]<|/det|>The Editor
<|det|>text [121, 645, 288, 668]<|/det|>The Telegraph
<|det|>text [121, 669, 387, 690]<|/det|>6 Prafulla Sarkar Street
<|det|>text [121, 692, 306, 713]<|/det|>Kolkata 700001
<|det|>text [120, 732, 156, 752]<|/det|>Sir
<|det|>text [117, 770, 938, 868]<|/det|>Noise pollution is on the rise in our city. The drivers of public vehicles do not think twice before honking. Even the private vehicles follow suit. Lack of civic sense and awareness on the part of the drivers and gross negligence of the traffic police are responsible for this menace.
<|det|>page_number [130, 935, 161, 953]<|/det|>68
<PAGE><|det|>text [88, 46, 861, 138]<|/det|>Schools on the main roads, senior citizens and patients in hospitals and nursing homes are the silent sufferers. This noise pollution even has an adverse effect on the breeding and migration of animals and birds.
<|det|>text [88, 156, 885, 228]<|/det|>The students of a few leading schools have taken the initiative to curb noise pollution. However, the brazen attitude of certain drivers is a major hindrance to them.
<|det|>text [88, 242, 885, 336]<|/det|>The concerned authorities must rise to the occasion and alleviate this problem at the earliest so as to provide a better environment to all concerned. Perhaps a steep fine for such thoughtlessness will help redress this issue.
<|det|>text [91, 354, 262, 379]<|/det|>Thanking you.
<|det|>text [91, 393, 222, 415]<|/det|>Yours truly
<|det|>text [93, 416, 262, 439]<|/det|>Rohit Agarwal
<|det|>text [94, 440, 329, 462]<|/det|>(ROHIT AGARWAL)
<|det|>title [125, 520, 588, 543]<|/det|>A. Now, attempt these letters to the editor.
<|det|>list [122, 545, 927, 875]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [127, 548, 920, 594]<|/det|>1. Write a letter to the editor of a newspaper drawing attention to the unsanitary condition of the city streets.
<|det|>text [126, 594, 920, 641]<|/det|>2. Write a letter to the editor of a local daily about the rising cases of eve-teasing in your locality.
<|det|>text [126, 641, 900, 686]<|/det|>3. Write a letter to the editor of a newspaper expressing your views on corporal punishment in schools.
<|det|>text [126, 686, 867, 755]<|/det|>4. Write a letter to the editor of a newspaper expressing your displeasure at the frequent strikes called by political parties in your city.
<|det|>text [126, 755, 856, 872]<|/det|>5. Write a letter to the editor of a local newspaper complaining about the disruption of water supply in your town. You must explain in your letter the inconvenience caused to people and request the concerned authorities to restore the supply immediately.
<|det|>page_number [867, 927, 898, 945]<|/det|>69
<PAGE><|det|>list [145, 38, 966, 202]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [145, 38, 966, 131]<|/det|>6. Write a letter to the editor of a local newspaper pointing out that several streetlights are not functioning in your locality. Give reasons as to why this is so and also whom you have informed and with what result. Also point out the risks involved.
<|det|>text [146, 132, 937, 202]<|/det|>7. Write a letter to the editor of a local newspaper complaining about the potholes in your locality. Give your reasons as to why this is so and also point out the risks in such a situation.
<|det|>title [98, 227, 182, 249]<|/det|>Email
<|det|>text [97, 257, 946, 350]<|/det|>Today, the email is fast replacing the art of letter writing. It saves time and reaches faster. Like letters, emails can be both personal and formal. Contrary to a letter, an email is rather brief and the format is also different.
<|det|>text [98, 366, 961, 457]<|/det|>The email has come into vogue due to the advent of the computer. In an email, the salutation, subscription and signature are all to the left. In formal emails, the address of the person to whom the mail is being sent is written in the address box.
<|det|>text [99, 472, 943, 520]<|/det|>Every email, whether personal or formal, should have a subject which is to be written in the line following the address box.
<|det|>title [130, 559, 194, 584]<|/det|>Note.
<|det|>text [120, 597, 911, 660]<|/det|>A personal e-mail is more or less similar to a personal letter. Once again the salutation, subscription and signature are to the left. The address of the writer is not necessary and the e-mail has a subject which follows the address box.
<|det|>title [100, 711, 664, 736]<|/det|>Read the example of an email on the next page.
<|det|>text [98, 749, 962, 821]<|/det|>You won't be able to attend your German class at Max Mueller Bhavan on Thursday, 2 August 2013 as you have a basketball match in school. Send an email to your German teacher.
<|det|>page_number [130, 937, 161, 956]<|/det|>70
<PAGE><|det|>image [207, 51, 803, 368]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [80, 400, 916, 446]<|/det|>B. Identify the mail id of the sender and the recipient and the date and time when this email was sent. Then write a reply to this mail.
<|det|>image [143, 459, 947, 885]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [96, 795, 345, 817]<|/det|>Write to me soon.
<|det|>text [156, 830, 194, 843]<|/det|>Love
<|det|>text [157, 845, 210, 858]<|/det|>Debbie
<|det|>page_number [867, 921, 898, 940]<|/det|>71
<PAGE><|det|>header [92, 38, 147, 83]<|/det|>9
<|det|>header [508, 39, 966, 116]<|/det|>Perfect and Perfect Continuous Tenses
<|det|>image [95, 144, 160, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 143, 953, 213]<|/det|>You have learnt that the perfect tenses show that an action has already been completed. The word perfect implies completed. Let us revise what you have learnt about the perfect tenses.
<|det|>title [94, 225, 396, 247]<|/det|>Present Perfect Tense
<|det|>text [92, 254, 962, 370]<|/det|>Both the simple past tense and the present perfect tense express an action completed in the past. The simple past tense expresses an action completed in the past but not linked to the present. The present perfect tense, however, denotes an action that is completed but connected to the present. The present perfect tense is used—
<|det|>list [94, 378, 970, 784]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [94, 378, 970, 425]<|/det|>◆ to indicate an action that has been completed just now or a little while ago. For example,
<|det|>text [135, 426, 458, 448]<|/det|>• They have bought a car.
<|det|>text [136, 449, 444, 469]<|/det|>• I have called for a cab.
<|det|>text [94, 481, 871, 526]<|/det|>◆ to express an action whose time is not given and is not definite. For example,
<|det|>text [136, 528, 472, 550]<|/det|>• He has lived in Bangkok.
<|det|>text [94, 560, 816, 606]<|/det|>◆ to describe a past event which has its effect on the present. For example,
<|det|>text [135, 608, 545, 655]<|/det|>- I have lost the keys to the house.
(So I cannot open the door.)
<|det|>text [94, 665, 636, 711]<|/det|>◆ to talk about a continuing state, not action. For example,
<|det|>list [135, 712, 696, 782]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 712, 567, 735]<|/det|>• Girish has been ill since Monday.
<|det|>text [135, 735, 465, 758]<|/det|>- Mini has broken her leg.
<|det|>text [135, 758, 696, 782]<|/det|>• We have known the Guptas for twenty years.
<|det|>title [94, 795, 345, 818]<|/det|>Past Perfect Tense
<|det|>text [92, 825, 950, 873]<|/det|>The past perfect tense is used to denote an action that took place before another action. For example,
<|det|>text [132, 873, 697, 897]<|/det|>- When I reached the bus stop, the bus had left.
<|det|>image [732, 595, 999, 808]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 928, 156, 947]<|/det|>72
<PAGE><|det|>text [52, 55, 912, 123]<|/det|>The tenses of the verbs in this sentence show which action happened first. The past perfect (had left) is used for the earlier action and the simple past (reached) for the later action.
<|det|>text [52, 135, 912, 203]<|/det|>The past perfect tense is also used to indicate a past hope or desire that has remained unfulfilled. We use verbs like hope, expect, think in such cases. For example,
<|det|>list [56, 204, 538, 258]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [56, 204, 538, 225]<|/det|>- I had expected to win the tournament.
<|det|>title [54, 241, 336, 262]<|/det|>Future Perfect Tense
<|det|>text [52, 269, 895, 337]<|/det|>The future perfect tense is used to refer to the completion of an action by a certain time in the future. The tense is normally used with expressions like by that time, by then, by next week. For example,
<|det|>list [56, 339, 690, 384]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [56, 339, 690, 361]<|/det|>- I will have completed the assignment by next week.
<|det|>text [56, 361, 581, 383]<|/det|>• The coach will have arrived by 11:00 a.m.
<|det|>image [26, 395, 100, 451]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [105, 404, 880, 428]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks according to the instructions given in the brackets.
<|det|>list [119, 433, 867, 555]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 433, 860, 480]<|/det|>1. We ____ to visit Canada for a long time, so last year we decided to go. (past perfect of want)
<|det|>text [119, 480, 852, 521]<|/det|>2. They ____ me for a long time. (present perfect of know)
<|det|>text [119, 523, 833, 568]<|/det|>3. They ____ a brand new TV set from my shop.
(present perfect of buy)
<|det|>text [119, 568, 876, 613]<|/det|>4. I ____ not ____ the implications of his comment. (past perfect of realise)
<|det|>text [119, 613, 905, 656]<|/det|>5. I ____ my work by the afternoon. (future perfect of complete)
<|det|>text [119, 658, 880, 702]<|/det|>6. Mansi ____ all the curd. (present perfect of eat)
7. I ____ him last five years ago when I went to Delhi.
<|det|>text [119, 702, 905, 767]<|/det|>(smart perfect of see)
8. ____ I ____ it correctly? (past perfect of hear)
<|det|>text [119, 768, 788, 812]<|/det|>9. I was hoping to meet Dad, but by the time I arrived, he ____ for Delhi. (future perfect of leave)
<|det|>text [109, 812, 870, 857]<|/det|>10. I ____ not ____ the work when he arrived. (past perfect of finish)
<|det|>text [110, 857, 844, 903]<|/det|>11. Firdaus ____ the book that he was looking for. (present perfect of find)
<|det|>page_number [848, 919, 883, 937]<|/det|>73
