<PAGE><|det|>text [157, 46, 942, 111]<|/det|>12. Deven ____ already ____ me for two years when he offered to join my organisation. (past perfect of know)
<|det|>text [155, 113, 905, 158]<|/det|>13. The dacoits ____ before the security personnel arrived. (past perfect of escape)
<|det|>text [155, 160, 890, 205]<|/det|>14. I ____ from school by then. (future perfect of return)
<|det|>list [155, 46, 942, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 226, 915, 273]<|/det|>B. Rewrite these sentences using the tense specified in the brackets. At least one verb will have to be changed.
<|det|>text [152, 279, 888, 303]<|/det|>1. When I reached the airport, he was departing. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [152, 304, 797, 326]<|/det|>2. Grandmother is just writing a letter. (present perfect)
<|det|>text [152, 327, 788, 349]<|/det|>3. We know each other for ten years. (present perfect)
<|det|>text [151, 350, 842, 372]<|/det|>4. The patient died before the doctor arrived. (past perfect)
<|det|>text [152, 373, 856, 419]<|/det|>5. By the end of the week, I shall shift to our new apartment. (future perfect)
<|det|>text [151, 420, 664, 442]<|/det|>6. He had not met me yet. (present perfect)
<|det|>text [151, 443, 817, 465]<|/det|>7. Suman finished the canvas right now. (present perfect)
<|det|>text [151, 465, 908, 512]<|/det|>8. The carpenter completed the work before I could instruct him. (past perfect)
<|det|>list [151, 279, 908, 512]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 534, 809, 556]<|/det|>C. Join these pairs of sentences using the past perfect tense.
<|det|>text [149, 557, 615, 580]<|/det|>Make suitable changes where necessary.
<|det|>text [150, 586, 610, 607]<|/det|>1. I ate all the cakes. She asked for one.
<|det|>text [149, 609, 653, 630]<|/det|>2. I reached the museum. I found it locked.
<|det|>text [149, 632, 614, 654]<|/det|>3. The children saw the giant. They fled.
<|det|>text [149, 655, 607, 676]<|/det|>4. I heard the news. I did not believe it.
<|det|>text [149, 678, 599, 722]<|/det|>5. Mother completed the cooking. She called them to the table.
<|det|>text [147, 724, 764, 746]<|/det|>6. The rain stopped. They then went out of the house.
<|det|>text [147, 747, 948, 769]<|/det|>7. The girl refused to admit the truth. She was suspended for a week.
<|det|>text [147, 771, 744, 793]<|/det|>8. I worked regularly. I finished the project on time.
<|det|>list [147, 586, 948, 793]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [82, 808, 143, 852]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [165, 818, 448, 839]<|/det|>Look at these sentences.
<|det|>text [84, 859, 677, 882]<|/det|>• I have been waiting for my mother since 3 p.m.
<|det|>text [84, 882, 948, 927]<|/det|>- The children had been making a lot of noise for quite some time when the teacher entered.
<|det|>list [84, 859, 948, 927]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [82, 829, 143, 852]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [114, 935, 147, 954]<|/det|>74
<PAGE><|det|>text [57, 49, 880, 96]<|/det|>- By the end of this month, I will have been learning to play the piano for five years.
<|det|>text [54, 110, 885, 178]<|/det|>In each of these sentences, we use the perfect as well as the continuous forms of the verb. Such usage is known as the perfect continuous tense. Look at this table which shows the forms of the verb in this tense.
<|det|>table [58, 195, 919, 684]<|/det|><table><tr><td></td><td>First person</td><td>Second person</td><td>Third person</td></tr><tr><td>Present perfect continuous</td><td>I have been playing for an hour.</td><td>You have been playing for an hour.</td><td>He has been playing for an hour.</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>We have been playing for an hour.</td><td>You have been playing for an hour.</td><td>They have been playing for an hour.</td></tr><tr><td>Past perfect continuous</td><td>I had been playing for an hour.</td><td>You had been playing for an hour.</td><td>He had been playing for an hour.</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>We had been playing for an hour.</td><td>You had been playing for an hour.</td><td>They had been playing for an hour.</td></tr><tr><td>Future perfect continuous</td><td>I shall/will have been playing for an hour.</td><td>You will have been playing for an hour.</td><td>He will have been playing for an hour.</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>We shall/will have been playing for an hour.</td><td>You will have been playing for an hour.</td><td>They will have been playing for an hour.</td></tr></table>
<|det|>title [55, 700, 502, 723]<|/det|>Present Perfect Continuous Tense
<|det|>text [55, 741, 525, 763]<|/det|>The present perfect continuous is used—
<|det|>text [55, 764, 850, 809]<|/det|>◆ to denote an action that began at some time in the past and is still continuing. For example,
<|det|>list [81, 810, 666, 856]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [81, 810, 540, 831]<|/det|>• I have been swimming for two hours.
<|det|>text [81, 833, 666, 856]<|/det|>• Kate has been working since six in the morning.
<|det|>page_number [845, 925, 879, 943]<|/det|>75
<PAGE><|det|>text [105, 45, 620, 68]<|/det|>◆ to denote repeated actions. For example,
<|det|>text [126, 68, 790, 90]<|/det|>• I have been getting this journal every week for a year.
<|det|>list [126, 45, 790, 90]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [129, 120, 195, 145]<|/det|>Note.
<|det|>image [536, 111, 574, 145]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [124, 157, 930, 200]<|/det|>There is sometimes very little difference between the meaning of the present perfect and the meaning of the present perfect continuous tense.
<|det|>text [126, 201, 578, 220]<|/det|>• I have been working at this site for three years.
<|det|>text [126, 222, 470, 242]<|/det|>• I have worked here for three years.
<|det|>list [126, 201, 578, 242]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [101, 295, 510, 317]<|/det|>Past Perfect Continuous Tense
<|det|>text [101, 326, 540, 347]<|/det|>The past perfect continuous is used—
<|det|>text [101, 348, 955, 417]<|/det|>◆ to denote that an action that began before a particular moment in the past and was still taking place, or had just finished, at that moment when another action took place. For example,
<|det|>text [121, 418, 890, 440]<|/det|>- When I visited Gwalior, he had been teaching there for a year.
<|det|>text [122, 441, 847, 486]<|/det|>- I had been driving for an hour when I heard a sound in the gear box.
<|det|>list [121, 418, 890, 486]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 497, 773, 520]<|/det|>◆ to talk about repeated actions in the past. For example,
<|det|>text [122, 521, 598, 544]<|/det|>• She had been trying to fix this all day.
<|det|>title [98, 575, 538, 596]<|/det|>Future Perfect Continuous Tense
<|det|>text [98, 604, 558, 626]<|/det|>The future perfect continuous is used—
<|det|>text [98, 627, 678, 671]<|/det|>◆ to indicate an action has been in progress over a period of time and will end in the future.
<|det|>text [134, 674, 294, 696]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [121, 696, 577, 740]<|/det|>- By the end of the year, I shall have been living in Delhi for seven years.
<|det|>text [121, 741, 569, 810]<|/det|>- When their mother returns home at five o'clock, Rita will have been watching television for four hours.
<|det|>image [645, 652, 950, 828]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 937, 152, 954]<|/det|>76
<PAGE><|det|>header [19, 42, 97, 93]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [98, 43, 865, 88]<|/det|>There are three sets of sentences given below. Choose any of the verbs from the box to complete the sentences as directed.
<|det|>table [182, 105, 785, 194]<|/det|><table><tr><td>learn</td><td>wait</td><td>suffer</td><td>organise</td><td>write</td></tr><tr><td>run</td><td>say</td><td>risk</td><td>attend</td><td>save</td></tr><tr><td>work</td><td>live</td><td>hear</td><td>think</td><td>stare</td></tr></table>
<|det|>title [96, 209, 517, 233]<|/det|>SET 1—present perfect continuous
<|det|>text [98, 242, 893, 286]<|/det|>1. The society ____ this competition every year since 1980.
<|det|>text [98, 288, 852, 311]<|/det|>2. How long ____ you ____ for me?
<|det|>text [98, 312, 805, 357]<|/det|>3. Because the children are older and need more space, we ____ of moving to a bigger house.
<|det|>text [98, 357, 878, 402]<|/det|>4. People ____ for years that this building should be pulled down.
<|det|>text [98, 403, 868, 448]<|/det|>5. The situation continues to be serious, and soldiers ____ their lives to rescue people from the floods.
<|det|>list [98, 242, 893, 448]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [100, 480, 476, 503]<|/det|>SET 2—past perfect continuous
<|det|>text [101, 512, 885, 535]<|/det|>1. She ____ from dengue when she was interviewed.
<|det|>text [100, 536, 775, 558]<|/det|>2. Sujata ____ since Christmas to buy a car.
<|det|>text [100, 558, 870, 601]<|/det|>3. They ____ rumours for some time when the news came.
<|det|>text [100, 604, 898, 649]<|/det|>4. I ____ at the sculpture for about two minutes when I realised why it looked familiar.
<|det|>text [100, 650, 903, 694]<|/det|>5. The principal called me to his office because I ____ not ____ classes.
<|det|>list [100, 512, 903, 694]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [98, 725, 502, 748]<|/det|>SET 3—future perfect continuous
<|det|>text [100, 758, 848, 781]<|/det|>1. Next year, I ____ in the company for a decade.
<|det|>text [100, 782, 821, 804]<|/det|>2. On Sunday, we ____ in this house for a year.
<|det|>text [100, 805, 888, 827]<|/det|>3. When he is fifty, he ____ German for fifteen years.
<|det|>text [100, 828, 839, 850]<|/det|>4. By next week, I ____ this book for three years.
<|det|>text [100, 851, 722, 895]<|/det|>5. By the time the last runner starts, the ones in front ____ for fifteen minutes.
<|det|>list [100, 758, 888, 895]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [856, 925, 890, 943]<|/det|>77
<PAGE><|det|>image [65, 28, 138, 82]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 38, 959, 107]<|/det|>Complete these sentences with suitable forms of the verbs given in the brackets. Read each sentence carefully to understand the time reference.
<|det|>list [160, 111, 966, 606]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [167, 115, 710, 137]<|/det|>1. It ____ (snow) for some time.
<|det|>text [166, 138, 960, 162]<|/det|>2. I ____ (learn) a lot about glass painting from him.
<|det|>text [166, 163, 797, 185]<|/det|>3. We ____ (leave) for Delhi tomorrow.
<|det|>text [166, 186, 625, 209]<|/det|>4. I ____ (be) very lucky.
<|det|>text [166, 210, 895, 233]<|/det|>5. At the moment, she ____ (plan) to buy a car.
<|det|>text [166, 234, 970, 258]<|/det|>6. Kavita ____ (wash) the clothes when Gayatri arrived
<|det|>text [166, 258, 865, 303]<|/det|>7. I ____ (do) some work in the garden when I spotted Sue.
<|det|>text [166, 304, 968, 328]<|/det|>8. By the time you get home, I ____ (finish) my studies.
<|det|>text [166, 328, 797, 351]<|/det|>9. I ____ (know) her since I was a child.
<|det|>text [154, 352, 815, 375]<|/det|>10. Bullfighting ____ (be) an ancient sport.
<|det|>text [154, 376, 852, 399]<|/det|>11. He ____ (visit) me on my fiftieth birthday.
<|det|>text [154, 399, 945, 422]<|/det|>12. The plane ____ (fly) at twice the speed of sound.
<|det|>text [154, 422, 898, 466]<|/det|>13. An important document ____ (stole) from my office.
<|det|>text [154, 467, 890, 514]<|/det|>14. Just as I ____ (leave) the house, the fire alarm ____ (go) off.
<|det|>text [154, 515, 860, 560]<|/det|>15. Harish ____ (want) to retire at sixty, but his employer persuaded him to stay on.
<|det|>text [154, 560, 931, 605]<|/det|>16. Scientists ____ (predict) that millions of animals ____ (become) extinct.
<|det|>text [150, 618, 964, 663]<|/det|>Complete the passages with the correct forms of the words provided in the brackets.
<|det|>text [154, 669, 952, 828]<|/det|>1. Within the first hundred yards we ____ (come) to a spot where the animal ____ (lay) down ____ as was ____ (reveal) by the crimson stain that ____ (cover) the grass and the unmistakable outline of the body. From here the animal ____ (slither) down the banks of a nullah. Following up thus ____ (become) terribly difficult and hazardous.
<|det|>text [150, 829, 952, 921]<|/det|>2. A quarter of an hour ____ (slip) by without further signs. In the mean time the usual jungle breeze ____ (spring) up to cause the bamboo shoots to groan and creak. This ____ (arouse) a fresh thought in my mind. What if
<|det|>page_number [130, 932, 161, 950]<|/det|>78
<PAGE><|det|>text [130, 53, 898, 165]<|/det|>one of these ____ (get) dislodged? The cut end may ____ (impale) me to the ground. For a moment this thought ____ (eclipse) all others. Then, I ____ (hear) the crack of a bone. Slowly, I ____ (lift) the rifle. But nothing else ____ (happen).
<|det|>text [37, 185, 867, 230]<|/det|>G. Fill in the blanks with the perfect tense of the of the verb given in the brackets.
<|det|>text [105, 236, 682, 261]<|/det|>1. I ____ (eat) anything since I woke up.
<|det|>text [104, 261, 862, 306]<|/det|>2. How many days ____ you ____ (be) away on tour since the beginning of the quarter?
<|det|>text [104, 306, 867, 347]<|/det|>3. By the end of this year, I ____ (read) three of Dickens' novels.
<|det|>text [104, 349, 885, 393]<|/det|>4. At sixteen years of age, she ____ (publish) two volumes of short stories.
<|det|>text [104, 394, 862, 438]<|/det|>5. After walking down Marine Drive road for two kilometres, we discovered that we ____ (take) the wrong route.
<|det|>text [104, 438, 842, 484]<|/det|>6. When I went to the hospital, my friend ____ already (get discharge).
<|det|>text [104, 483, 890, 526]<|/det|>7. By the age of seven, Mithun ____ (obtain) his black belt in karate.
<|det|>text [104, 528, 729, 551]<|/det|>8. I wish I ____ (listen) to my father's advice.
<|det|>text [104, 551, 870, 573]<|/det|>9. By the end of this year, he ____ (pay) off his mortgage.
<|det|>text [96, 573, 756, 593]<|/det|>10. The doctors ____ (be) on a strike for a week.
<|det|>text [96, 594, 750, 616]<|/det|>11. Your friend ____ (call) thrice since morning.
<|det|>text [96, 616, 880, 660]<|/det|>12. I am saving money to buy a motorcycle. By the end of this year, I ____ (save) enough to buy one.
<|det|>text [96, 660, 856, 683]<|/det|>13. Which films ____ you ____ (see) this month?
<|det|>text [96, 683, 785, 705]<|/det|>14. The birds ____ (fly) away before winter comes.
<|det|>text [96, 705, 714, 727]<|/det|>15. I ____ (finish) my chores. May I go now?
<|det|>text [96, 727, 837, 749]<|/det|>16. ____ you ever ____ (travel) in a hovercraft?
<|det|>text [96, 749, 720, 791]<|/det|>17. By the time I reached the auditorium, the concert ____ (start).
<|det|>text [96, 793, 872, 815]<|/det|>18. How many sums ____ you ____ (solve) so far?
<|det|>text [96, 815, 907, 860]<|/det|>19. The children came to the birthday party at 4 o'clock yesterday, but before that Shena and Taanish ____ (decorate) the room.
<|det|>text [94, 860, 776, 882]<|/det|>20. Uday asked Manish if he ____ (read) Matilda?
<|det|>page_number [840, 914, 876, 931]<|/det|>79
<PAGE><|det|>image [61, 28, 127, 82]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [143, 34, 972, 84]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the perfect continuous tense of the of the verb given in the brackets.
<|det|>list [150, 83, 960, 665]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [157, 86, 948, 132]<|/det|>1. The soup ____ (simmer) for quite some time now. Why don't you switch off the gas?
<|det|>text [155, 133, 933, 179]<|/det|>2. We ____ (wait) for a bus for an hour when a car stopped by and offered us a lift to the railway station.
<|det|>text [155, 179, 968, 247]<|/det|>3. Until last year, our school team ____ (win) the Inter School Football Cup. Last year, they lost to Goethals Memorial School.
<|det|>text [154, 248, 797, 271]<|/det|>4. Someone ____ (try) to pick this lock!
<|det|>text [154, 272, 965, 317]<|/det|>5. He ____ (ring) the bell for five minutes. But no one opened the door.
<|det|>text [154, 318, 943, 364]<|/det|>6. Ms Jaya ____ (act) in films for twenty years when she decided to join politics.
<|det|>text [154, 365, 963, 410]<|/det|>7. The minister ____ (visit) these parts recently. This is quite common before the elections.
<|det|>text [154, 411, 951, 457]<|/det|>8. The candidates ____ (wait) for the interview since ten o'clock. The committee has not called any of them yet.
<|det|>text [154, 457, 844, 501]<|/det|>9. He ____ (work) on this problem for three hours now.
<|det|>text [142, 503, 935, 571]<|/det|>10. For the last six months, he ____ (take) Ayurvedic medicines for his asthma. There is some improvement in his condition.
<|det|>text [141, 572, 940, 617]<|/det|>11. Esha ____ (read) this book for more than a week. But she has not finished it yet.
<|det|>text [141, 618, 878, 664]<|/det|>12. Neelima ____ (do) very well throughout the semester. She hopes to get a scholarship.
<|det|>page_number [108, 925, 143, 944]<|/det|>80
<PAGE><|det|>title [62, 48, 381, 83]<|/det|>An Interview
<|det|>header [695, 0, 999, 134]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>image [60, 146, 132, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 146, 905, 239]<|/det|>Read this conversation between a student, Priyanka, and a journalist. Priyanka is being interviewed by the journalist on the activities of her school's nature club, and the recent 'Green Drive' launched by the club.
<|det|>text [62, 250, 783, 275]<|/det|>Journalist: May I speak to the president of your Nature Club?
<|det|>text [64, 275, 785, 298]<|/det|>Priyanka: I'm the president of the club. How can I help you?
<|det|>text [64, 298, 851, 345]<|/det|>Journalist: I'm Ratan Halder, on behalf of The Teens, the bimonthly English magazine. May I know who I'm interviewing?
<|det|>text [65, 345, 821, 391]<|/det|>Priyanka: Hello! I'm Priyanka Dagar. Are you going to print our picture too?
<|det|>text [65, 391, 901, 481]<|/det|>Journalist: We are actually planning a weekly feature on the extra-curricular activities done by the various schools. The week's feature will focus on the nature clubs of different city schools. And yes, there'll be a few pictures.
<|det|>text [70, 481, 682, 503]<|/det|>Priyanka: Oh! I'm so excited! Where should I start?
<|det|>text [70, 504, 753, 526]<|/det|>Journalist: Let me help you. Have you read our magazine?
<|det|>text [70, 526, 895, 571]<|/det|>Priyanka: Sure, I have. It is such a well-presented and well-researched magazine. I think most of us read it.
<|det|>text [70, 572, 908, 617]<|/det|>Journalist: Now, tell me something about the latest activity organised by your club.
<|det|>text [70, 618, 876, 733]<|/det|>Priyanka: Last week, on Republic Day, we planted one hundred saplings in the neighbourhood of our school. We had also invited a few celebrities like Asha Devi, the famous author, and Manmohan Joshi, the well-known painter, to grace the occasion. Oh! It was a great success.
<|det|>text [67, 734, 860, 778]<|/det|>Journalist: How old is your club? What exactly have been the club's activities till now?
<|det|>text [67, 779, 907, 921]<|/det|>Priyanka: Ours is a fairly new club, about four years old. We meet once a week, every Tuesday, after school. Occasionally, we visit the botanical garden to collect specimens. We paste these and also collect data and write about them for future reference. We have four huge scrap books for the specimens. Would you like to take a look at them?
<|det|>page_number [856, 926, 887, 944]<|/det|>81
<PAGE><|det|>text [92, 39, 942, 87]<|/det|>Journalist: Sure, I'd love to. You are also launching a 'Green Drive'. What is it about, and how are you planning to go about it?
<|det|>text [95, 87, 966, 205]<|/det|>Priyanka: We plan to organise drawing, quiz and creative writing competitions, inviting participation from children of the poorer sections of society; we think it will be a good way of spreading awareness about environmental issues, and also of taking the idea of 'Green Drive' to all sections of society.
<|det|>text [95, 206, 940, 249]<|/det|>Journalist: Are there any other activities that you plan to do as part of the ‘Green Drive’?
<|det|>text [95, 251, 962, 367]<|/det|>Priyanka: Definitely. We will visit different localities and help the local club members to clean their neighbourhood, plant trees and also teach them to care for them. Recently, we organised a quiz competition. It was a great hit, so we'd like to organise more to spread awareness.
<|det|>text [95, 369, 788, 393]<|/det|>Journalist: How about exchanging ideas with other clubs?
<|det|>text [95, 394, 966, 485]<|/det|>Priyanka: Oh, yes! We're already doing that. We've launched a website and are in touch with quite a few nature clubs both within and outside the country. We're surely trying to spread our wings—let's see how far we can reach!
<|det|>text [95, 486, 925, 556]<|/det|>Journalist: Thank you! This was a delightful interview, and all the best. Now, let me take a picture of some of you with your exceptionally well-made scrap book in front.
<|det|>text [95, 556, 833, 580]<|/det|>Priyanka: Thank you! It was a pleasure talking to you as well.
<|det|>title [94, 601, 637, 625]<|/det|>Now, get into pairs and enact these situations.
<|det|>text [95, 630, 961, 699]<|/det|>1. You are a reporter for a children's magazine. One of your neighbours, a young child of twelve, foiled a robbery attempt single-handedly. You have to take an interview of the young boy for your magazine.
<|det|>text [93, 700, 970, 770]<|/det|>2. A fire broke out in an apartment. You were the one to inform the police and the fire department. A journalist interviews you because you were the primary witness to the incident.
<|det|>page_number [130, 935, 161, 953]<|/det|>82
<PAGE><|det|>title [60, 43, 238, 76]<|/det|>Modals
<|det|>header [745, 0, 999, 134]<|/det|>10
<|det|>image [58, 150, 118, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 146, 862, 191]<|/det|>Read this conversation. Observe the function of the highlighted words.
<|det|>text [60, 207, 455, 229]<|/det|>Rakhi: May I come in, ma'am?
<|det|>text [60, 231, 523, 251]<|/det|>Teacher: You must not be late for class.
<|det|>text [60, 253, 893, 299]<|/det|>Rakhi: I'm sorry, ma'am. I had to meet the maths teacher, but I could not find her, so I got late. It will not happen again.
<|det|>text [60, 300, 735, 322]<|/det|>Teacher: Very well. But you ought to excuse yourself first.
<|det|>text [60, 336, 881, 404]<|/det|>The highlighted words above are verbs that are tell us more about the mood or the attitude of the speaker. You have learnt that such words are called modals.
<|det|>text [60, 428, 911, 474]<|/det|>Modals, or modal auxiliaries, are verbs that are used to express the mood or attitude of the speaker.
<|det|>title [94, 515, 155, 538]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [475, 508, 521, 542]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [87, 552, 875, 631]<|/det|>You have learnt that auxiliaries, or helping verbs, are of two kinds—primary auxiliaries and modal auxiliaries. The primary auxiliaries are the verbs be, have and do when they are used with other verbs. Look at this table that lists the forms and functions of these verbs.
<|det|>title [187, 651, 248, 668]<|/det|>Forms
<|det|>text [97, 677, 317, 717]<|/det|>be (present—is, am, are; past—was, were)
<|det|>title [406, 650, 500, 668]<|/det|>Functions
<|det|>text [351, 678, 536, 758]<|/det|>form continuous
tenses, used in the
passive and to denote
a command or a plan
<|det|>text [352, 768, 548, 787]<|/det|>form the perfect tenses
<|det|>title [661, 650, 764, 669]<|/det|>Examples
<|det|>text [572, 677, 791, 737]<|/det|>I am writing a letter. The door was opened by Shyam.
<|det|>text [573, 739, 834, 758]<|/det|>You are to leave right now.
<|det|>text [573, 767, 840, 787]<|/det|>I have completed my work.
<|det|>text [573, 789, 868, 810]<|/det|>Ma had made orange pudding.
<|det|>text [97, 818, 301, 858]<|/det|>do (present—does, do; past—did)
<|det|>text [351, 818, 517, 880]<|/det|>form interrogatives, imperatives and negative sentences
<|det|>text [573, 818, 800, 838]<|/det|>Do you know that girl?
<|det|>text [573, 839, 730, 859]<|/det|>Do be patient.
<|det|>text [573, 860, 768, 880]<|/det|>He doesn't agree.
<|det|>page_number [857, 923, 889, 941]<|/det|>83
<PAGE><|det|>text [117, 55, 892, 78]<|/det|>The verbs be, have and do can also be used as principal, or main verbs. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 78, 540, 99]<|/det|>• I am a student. (reveals a state of being)
<|det|>text [151, 99, 527, 120]<|/det|>• I have a pet dog. (indicates ownership)
<|det|>text [151, 120, 491, 141]<|/det|>• I do yoga every day. (shows action)
<|det|>list [151, 78, 540, 141]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [96, 186, 607, 349]<|/det|>The most commonly used modals are can, could, may, might, shall, should, must, will, would, used, need, dare and ought. These are used to express possibility, certainty, necessity, ability, expectation, permission, advice, wish or obligation. Let us now study the use of modal auxiliaries in detail.
<|det|>image [631, 192, 718, 240]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [645, 238, 934, 320]<|/det|>Modals are always followed by a main verb. They do not change form with the person or the number of the subject.
<|det|>title [98, 366, 269, 387]<|/det|>Can is used—
<|det|>text [98, 388, 692, 411]<|/det|>◆ to talk about ability and possibility. For example,
<|det|>text [140, 412, 614, 435]<|/det|>• She can play tennis very well. (ability)
<|det|>text [140, 435, 705, 458]<|/det|>- Loud noise can damage your ears. (possibility)
<|det|>list [140, 412, 705, 458]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 468, 907, 492]<|/det|>◆ to ask for and give permission in an informal manner. For example,
<|det|>text [140, 492, 815, 515]<|/det|>- Can I join the gym from tomorrow? (seeking permission)
<|det|>text [140, 515, 714, 538]<|/det|>• You can meet me tomorrow. (giving permission)
<|det|>list [140, 492, 815, 538]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [98, 553, 291, 574]<|/det|>Could is used—
<|det|>text [98, 575, 827, 598]<|/det|>◆ as the past equivalent of can in indirect speech. For example,
<|det|>text [140, 599, 495, 621]<|/det|>• Mani said, 'I can help you.'
<|det|>text [178, 621, 562, 644]<|/det|>Mani said that she could help me.
<|det|>text [98, 655, 562, 678]<|/det|>◆ to make polite requests. For example,
<|det|>text [140, 678, 593, 701]<|/det|>• Could you help me carry these bags?
<|det|>text [98, 712, 622, 735]<|/det|>◆ to indicate ability in the past. For example,
<|det|>text [140, 735, 670, 757]<|/det|>• She could do handstands once upon a time.
<|det|>list [98, 575, 670, 757]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [98, 773, 269, 795]<|/det|>May is used—
<|det|>text [98, 795, 601, 840]<|/det|>◆ to ask for permission in a formal manner. For example,
<|det|>text [140, 841, 394, 863]<|/det|>• May I come in, sir?
<|det|>list [98, 795, 601, 863]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 875, 492, 898]<|/det|>◆ to express a wish. For example,
<|det|>text [140, 898, 556, 921]<|/det|>• May you always enjoy good luck!
<|det|>list [98, 875, 556, 921]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [727, 611, 934, 870]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [130, 928, 161, 946]<|/det|>84
<PAGE><|det|>text [44, 43, 596, 68]<|/det|>◆ to indicate a weak possibility. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 68, 427, 88]<|/det|>- It may rain this afternoon.
<|det|>list [44, 43, 596, 88]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [45, 103, 241, 125]<|/det|>Might is used—
<|det|>text [45, 125, 792, 148]<|/det|>◆ to suggest a very weak possibility in the future. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 148, 369, 169]<|/det|>- It might rain tonight.
<|det|>text [92, 169, 670, 193]<|/det|>- Don't play with the knives. You might get hurt.
<|det|>list [92, 148, 670, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [45, 203, 808, 226]<|/det|>◆ as the past equivalent of may in indirect speech. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 227, 456, 249]<|/det|>• Jiya said, 'I may get the job.'
<|det|>text [122, 250, 538, 274]<|/det|>Jiya said that she might get the job.
<|det|>title [45, 288, 220, 309]<|/det|>Will is used—
<|det|>text [45, 309, 862, 355]<|/det|>◆ to talk about future events that are planned or about which we are certain. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 356, 475, 377]<|/det|>• The train will leave at 10 a.m.
<|det|>text [92, 379, 534, 402]<|/det|>• My mother will turn sixty this year.
<|det|>list [92, 356, 534, 402]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [45, 412, 687, 435]<|/det|>◆ to show determination or willingness. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 436, 602, 458]<|/det|>• I will help you complete the assignment.
<|det|>text [45, 469, 525, 492]<|/det|>◆ to make polite requests. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 492, 639, 514]<|/det|>• Will you help me complete the assignment?
<|det|>text [92, 514, 812, 537]<|/det|>• Will you turn down the music, please? I am trying to sleep.
<|det|>list [45, 412, 812, 537]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [45, 551, 228, 572]<|/det|>Shall is used—
<|det|>text [45, 574, 582, 597]<|/det|>◆ to make offers or suggestions. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 597, 434, 618]<|/det|>• Shall I close the door?
<|det|>text [45, 630, 576, 653]<|/det|>◆ to show certainty or promise. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 653, 530, 674]<|/det|>• I shall clean the kitchen tomorrow.
<|det|>list [45, 574, 582, 674]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [45, 686, 845, 731]<|/det|>◆ in the second and third person to express a command, a promise or even a threat. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 732, 571, 754]<|/det|>• He shall not come here again. (threat)
<|det|>text [92, 755, 641, 778]<|/det|>• You shall certainly receive a raise. (promise)
<|det|>list [92, 732, 641, 778]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [45, 792, 248, 813]<|/det|>Would is used—
<|det|>text [45, 816, 523, 839]<|/det|>◆ to make polite requests. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 839, 583, 862]<|/det|>• Would you mind lending me your pen?
<|det|>text [45, 872, 532, 895]<|/det|>◆ to talk about past habits. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 895, 645, 918]<|/det|>• After dinner, we would sit and talk for hours.
<|det|>list [45, 816, 645, 918]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [727, 556, 804, 595]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [624, 601, 890, 662]<|/det|>Shall is usually used with the first person singular (I) or the first person plural (we).
<|det|>page_number [857, 920, 889, 938]<|/det|>85
<PAGE><|det|>text [112, 43, 674, 66]<|/det|>◆ to talk about imaginary situations. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 68, 532, 90]<|/det|>• I would love to go to Morocco.
<|det|>title [112, 105, 304, 126]<|/det|>Should is used—
<|det|>text [112, 127, 772, 150]<|/det|>◆ to express advice, suggestion or obligation. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 151, 460, 171]<|/det|>• You should tell the truth.
<|det|>text [112, 185, 540, 208]<|/det|>◆ to express probability. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 209, 658, 231]<|/det|>• You should find this grammar book helpful.
<|det|>title [112, 248, 283, 268]<|/det|>Must is used—
<|det|>text [112, 270, 667, 293]<|/det|>◆ to express necessity or obligation. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 294, 492, 316]<|/det|>• We must do this right away.
<|det|>text [112, 326, 940, 374]<|/det|>◆ to give advice to someone, especially when we want to emphasise that it is for their own good. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 376, 411, 398]<|/det|>- You must stop lying.
<|det|>title [112, 414, 329, 435]<|/det|>Ought to is used—
<|det|>text [112, 436, 676, 459]<|/det|>◆ to express duty or moral obligation. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 460, 588, 482]<|/det|>• We ought to obey our parents. (duty)
<|det|>text [151, 482, 907, 505]<|/det|>• We ought to protect our national monuments. (moral obligation)
<|det|>text [112, 517, 514, 539]<|/det|>◆ to express necessity. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 540, 507, 562]<|/det|>• We ought to buy a cupboard.
<|det|>title [112, 577, 283, 597]<|/det|>Used is used—
<|det|>text [112, 598, 876, 643]<|/det|>◆ to indicate something that was a habit, but has been discontinued. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 645, 437, 668]<|/det|>• She used to learn yoga.
<|det|>title [112, 682, 283, 702]<|/det|>Need is used—
<|det|>text [112, 705, 651, 728]<|/det|>◆ to denote necessity or obligation. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 729, 556, 750]<|/det|>• Java need not go to the post office.
<|det|>text [151, 751, 436, 773]<|/det|>• Do I need to help him?
<|det|>title [140, 812, 201, 838]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [134, 849, 677, 870]<|/det|>As an ordinary verb, need means want or require. For example,
<|det|>text [157, 871, 378, 891]<|/det|>• I do not need your help.
<|det|>page_number [142, 929, 175, 946]<|/det|>86
<PAGE><|det|>title [60, 37, 236, 57]<|/det|>Dare is used—
<|det|>list [59, 60, 889, 130]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [60, 60, 886, 105]<|/det|>◆ in interrogative and negative sentences where the implication is to be bold enough to.
<|det|>text [110, 107, 390, 129]<|/det|>• How dare you say so?
<|det|>text [60, 140, 495, 163]<|/det|>◆ to imply a challenge. For example,
<|det|>text [109, 164, 585, 187]<|/det|>• I dare you to finish up the whole pizza.
<|det|>title [82, 219, 150, 245]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [468, 214, 516, 248]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [84, 258, 628, 279]<|/det|>Notice how the use of modals affects the meaning of a sentence.
<|det|>list [108, 280, 688, 363]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 280, 604, 300]<|/det|>• You must do it. (means it is necessary that you do it)
<|det|>text [109, 301, 687, 321]<|/det|>- You mustn't do it. (means it is necessary that you do not do it)
<|det|>text [109, 322, 672, 342]<|/det|>• You needn't do it. (means it is not necessary for you to do it)
<|det|>text [109, 343, 687, 363]<|/det|>• You ought to do it. (expresses moral obligation or desirability)
<|det|>title [65, 409, 648, 431]<|/det|>A. Fill in the blanks with will/would/shall/should.
<|det|>list [128, 438, 860, 880]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [129, 438, 535, 460]<|/det|>1. We ____ obey the law.
<|det|>text [129, 462, 617, 483]<|/det|>2. You ____ be punished for this.
<|det|>text [129, 485, 643, 506]<|/det|>3. I ____ tell your mother the truth.
<|det|>text [129, 508, 655, 530]<|/det|>4. He ____ not enter my class again.
<|det|>text [130, 531, 657, 552]<|/det|>5. You ____ have been more careful.
<|det|>text [130, 554, 648, 576]<|/det|>6. I ____ go with you to the movies.
<|det|>text [130, 577, 781, 599]<|/det|>7. Around 4 p.m., we ____ leave for the airport.
<|det|>text [130, 601, 811, 623]<|/det|>8. A child ____ always recognise a mother's voice.
<|det|>text [130, 624, 856, 646]<|/det|>9. We ____ discuss the matter extensively over dinner.
<|det|>text [120, 647, 775, 669]<|/det|>10. She said she ____ help me with the cooking.
<|det|>text [120, 670, 821, 713]<|/det|>11. As soon as he woke up, he ____ get things ready for breakfast.
<|det|>text [120, 715, 790, 761]<|/det|>12. When I was a child, I ____ spend hours in the swimming pool.
<|det|>text [120, 761, 871, 806]<|/det|>13. There was a boy in my class who ____ ask ridiculous questions.
<|det|>text [120, 807, 885, 853]<|/det|>14. I remember how she ____ sit for hours by the window and paint.
<|det|>text [120, 854, 747, 877]<|/det|>15. The parcel ____ have reached my brother.
<|det|>page_number [838, 924, 868, 941]<|/det|>87
<PAGE><|det|>title [105, 8, 963, 37]<|/det|>8. Rewrite these sentences correcting any errors in the use of modals.
<|det|>list [169, 40, 701, 312]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [170, 40, 397, 61]<|/det|>1. It can rain later.
<|det|>text [169, 68, 390, 90]<|/det|>2. Can I come in?
<|det|>text [168, 97, 565, 122]<|/det|>3. I would go out in the evening.
<|det|>text [168, 127, 569, 150]<|/det|>4. I can be thirteen next birthday.
<|det|>text [168, 156, 550, 179]<|/det|>5. May this card be from Rima?
<|det|>text [168, 185, 584, 208]<|/det|>6. You can make a little less noise.
<|det|>text [168, 215, 675, 239]<|/det|>7. May it be that you don't want to resign?
<|det|>text [167, 245, 701, 268]<|/det|>8. I couldn't let him down at the last minute.
<|det|>text [167, 275, 781, 300]<|/det|>9. He is over eighty, but could read without glasses.
<|det|>text [157, 305, 824, 330]<|/det|>10. I thought they were Americans—but I can be wrong.
<|det|>text [157, 335, 905, 360]<|/det|>11. You can't go in now. The principal would not be disturbed.
<|det|>text [157, 365, 864, 414]<|/det|>12. I think I saw Prashant go out, so he couldn't be at home right now.
<|det|>text [157, 419, 955, 469]<|/det|>13. Since I have been in this business for a while, I can be of some help to you.
<|det|>text [157, 474, 474, 499]<|/det|>14. How need you say so?
<|det|>image [65, 528, 141, 580]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [175, 527, 942, 553]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with must, mustn't, need, needn't or ought to.
<|det|>list [168, 557, 965, 898]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [168, 557, 955, 584]<|/det|>1. You ____ lend her money. She has enough right now.
<|det|>text [168, 588, 965, 614]<|/det|>2. I ____ exercise regularly. I ____ gain weight
<|det|>text [168, 618, 955, 665]<|/det|>3. You ____ have waited for me. I would have managed on my own.
<|det|>text [168, 671, 935, 719]<|/det|>4. Do you ____ any help? You ____ hesitate to ask, if you do.
<|det|>text [168, 725, 893, 772]<|/det|>5. Your answer has many errors. You ____ be more careful.
<|det|>text [168, 779, 860, 805]<|/det|>6. She ____ eat peanuts. She is allergic to them.
<|det|>text [168, 810, 915, 835]<|/det|>7. A teacher ____ make his or her lesson interesting.
<|det|>text [168, 841, 898, 866]<|/det|>8. You ____ worry. I will help you with the project.
<|det|>text [168, 871, 868, 897]<|/det|>9. I ____ wake up early tomorrow. It is a Sunday.
<|det|>page_number [131, 924, 161, 941]<|/det|>88
<PAGE><|det|>text [85, 40, 650, 64]<|/det|>10. You ____ have been more careful.
<|det|>text [85, 69, 650, 93]<|/det|>11. Students ____ obey their teachers.
<|det|>text [85, 98, 655, 121]<|/det|>12. You ____ see me before you leave.
<|det|>text [85, 127, 582, 150]<|/det|>13. Does she ____ a new frock?
<|det|>text [85, 156, 590, 179]<|/det|>14. You ____ visit a doctor soon.
<|det|>text [85, 184, 518, 208]<|/det|>15. ____ he work so hard?
<|det|>list [85, 40, 655, 208]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [84, 234, 898, 281]<|/det|>Use the correct modals from the brackets to complete the sentences. Use the hints in the second brackets.
<|det|>text [102, 287, 829, 335]<|/det|>1. You ____ (must/ought to) buy a ticket before you board a train. [necessity]
<|det|>text [101, 340, 880, 388]<|/det|>2. You really ____ (must/should) watch this new sitcom. It's hilarious. [necessary because it's very enjoyable]
<|det|>text [101, 392, 878, 440]<|/det|>3. At the end of the term students ____ (have to/should) take an exam. [obligation]
<|det|>text [101, 444, 860, 492]<|/det|>4. You ____ (should/may) have planned well ahead of time to avoid the mess you are in now. [advice]
<|det|>text [102, 497, 837, 545]<|/det|>5. You look pale. You ____ (had better/should) see a doctor. [advice as to the best thing to do]
<|det|>text [102, 549, 870, 597]<|/det|>6. Too much sugar ____ (is supposed to/would) be bad for you. [for what people say]
<|det|>text [104, 601, 885, 649]<|/det|>7. I ____ (daren't/must not) look at the bill. [afraid to do something]
<|det|>text [104, 653, 862, 701]<|/det|>8. I ____ (used to/have to) have a bicycle, but I sold it.
[past habit]
<|det|>text [104, 705, 900, 752]<|/det|>9. Before we had television, people ____ (used to/would) read books for entertainment. [past habit]
<|det|>text [96, 757, 885, 805]<|/det|>10. I am afraid you ____ (can't/may not) just walk in as if nothing happened. [refuse permission]
<|det|>page_number [843, 924, 876, 940]<|/det|>89
<PAGE><|det|>text [100, 35, 958, 84]<|/det|>Here is a set of instructions that were put up on the notice board in Class VII. Complete this notice using suitable modal auxiliaries.
<|det|>title [507, 106, 611, 128]<|/det|>NOTICE
<|det|>text [456, 147, 660, 168]<|/det|>DN Model School
<|det|>text [415, 170, 700, 192]<|/det|>48, Nehru Nagar, Tarapur
<|det|>text [175, 208, 334, 230]<|/det|>18 April 2013
<|det|>title [401, 244, 708, 264]<|/det|>Rules for Class VII Students
<|det|>list [194, 283, 851, 565]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [194, 283, 757, 305]<|/det|>- You ____ come to school on time.
<|det|>text [194, 311, 714, 333]<|/det|>- You ____ to be neatly dressed.
<|det|>text [194, 338, 817, 382]<|/det|>- You ____ wear the school tie during the summer term.
<|det|>text [193, 388, 848, 411]<|/det|>- You ____ bring expensive items to school.
<|det|>text [193, 416, 817, 439]<|/det|>- You ____ do your home work regularly.
<|det|>text [193, 442, 943, 486]<|/det|>- You ____ carry a cell phone to school. If you do, it ____ be confiscated.
<|det|>text [191, 493, 856, 515]<|/det|>- You ____ maintain silence in the corridors.
<|det|>text [191, 521, 807, 566]<|/det|>- You ____ run or create a ruckus during school hours.
<|det|>title [160, 588, 301, 609]<|/det|>Mona Joshi
<|det|>title [160, 611, 325, 632]<|/det|>Class Teacher
<|det|>page_number [108, 927, 143, 944]<|/det|>90
<PAGE><|det|>title [62, 53, 411, 91]<|/det|>Notice Writing
<|det|>header [720, 0, 999, 146]<|/det|>The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
<|det|>image [60, 153, 123, 198]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 154, 904, 234]<|/det|>A notice is meant to impart some important information to the public, or to give some warning, or to state a rule. The tone of a notice is formal and impersonal. While framing a notice, one must pay attention to the format as well as the content. The content of a notice must
<|det|>text [62, 236, 890, 333]<|/det|>not exceed more than fifty words and all the relevant information must be stated clearly and accurately. Owing to the concise format of a notice, it is important to choose words carefully to ensure that all information is communicated clearly to those concerned.
<|det|>title [98, 370, 165, 395]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [471, 369, 526, 406]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [89, 409, 877, 470]<|/det|>Generally, a notice is always marked out of five. The format is marked out of two and three marks are reserved for the content. A notice which exceeds the word limit will be automatically penalised.
<|det|>text [63, 512, 735, 536]<|/det|>Read this example of a notice. Pay attention to the format.
<|det|>text [264, 564, 714, 610]<|/det|>Sahitya Samiti (name of institute)
24, D N Shah Road, Allahabad (address)
<|det|>text [87, 624, 383, 647]<|/det|>18 December 2013 (date)
<|det|>text [310, 661, 666, 685]<|/det|>Renovation of Library (heading)
<|det|>text [82, 698, 867, 768]<|/det|>(body of the notice) The Samiti library will remain closed to the public from 23 December 2013 till 31 December 2013 owing to repair and renovation work. It will reopen on 1 January 2014.
<|det|>text [82, 769, 655, 792]<|/det|>The inconvenience to all the members is regretted.
<|det|>text [80, 829, 309, 853]<|/det|>J K Joshi (signature)
<|det|>text [80, 854, 347, 877]<|/det|>Secretary (designation)
<|det|>page_number [845, 919, 876, 937]<|/det|>91
<PAGE><|det|>text [101, 38, 968, 87]<|/det|>Now, write notices for the given situations. Remember not to exceed the word limit of fifty words.
<|det|>list [100, 88, 956, 446]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 91, 943, 160]<|/det|>1. A black-and-white waterproof sports watch has been found in the basketball court on Monday, 23 July 2012. Draft out a notice for the school notice board.
<|det|>text [102, 161, 893, 232]<|/det|>2. Your school is organising a cultural evening to showcase hidden talents. Write out a notice asking students to audition for the programme.
<|det|>text [102, 232, 958, 301]<|/det|>3. The basketball match between Martin House and Charnock House has been postponed to a later date for a particular reason. Draft out a notice to inform all concerned.
<|det|>text [101, 302, 958, 372]<|/det|>4. A fancy dress competition has been organised for the students of Class III. Draft out a notice in not more than fifty words to the parents of the concerned children giving the necessary details.
<|det|>text [101, 372, 927, 445]<|/det|>5. Your school has decided to contribute warm clothes, blankets and stationery items to the children of an NGO named Calcutta Social Project. Draft out a notice urging students to contribute generously.
<|det|>page_number [130, 934, 161, 951]<|/det|>92
<PAGE><|det|>title [43, 33, 443, 71]<|/det|>Non-Finite Verbs
<|det|>page_number [836, 32, 906, 73]<|/det|>11
<|det|>image [45, 142, 99, 188]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 152, 720, 178]<|/det|>You have learnt that verbs can be finite or non-finite.
<|det|>text [42, 201, 790, 248]<|/det|>A verb that has a subject and shows tense, person and number is called a finite verb.
<|det|>text [44, 264, 200, 286]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [48, 287, 388, 335]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [48, 287, 312, 308]<|/det|>• I live in New Delhi.
<|det|>text [48, 310, 386, 334]<|/det|>• My sister lives in Mumbai.
<|det|>text [42, 352, 816, 399]<|/det|>A verb that has no subject, and does not show person or number is called a non-finite verb.
<|det|>text [46, 414, 201, 436]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [48, 437, 378, 484]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [48, 437, 353, 460]<|/det|>• I love to go to the mall.
<|det|>text [48, 461, 377, 484]<|/det|>- Reena sat waiting quietly.
<|det|>text [44, 491, 831, 540]<|/det|>There are three kinds of non-finite verbs—infinitives, participles and gerunds. Let us learn about them in detail.
<|det|>title [46, 551, 188, 572]<|/det|>Infinitives
<|det|>text [46, 581, 300, 602]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [46, 604, 470, 720]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [48, 604, 252, 627]<|/det|>- I want to sing.
<|det|>text [46, 627, 470, 720]<|/det|>- She wants to improve her English. In these sentences, the verb want is the finite verb. To sing and to improve are the infinitives.
<|det|>text [42, 728, 467, 822]<|/det|>Infinitives are also called the to-verb because to is often used to form an infinitive. Let us look at the functions of the .infinitive.
<|det|>text [42, 831, 361, 853]<|/det|>An infinitive can be used—
<|det|>text [42, 856, 421, 902]<|/det|>◆ as the subject of the verb in a sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [92, 904, 270, 925]<|/det|>• To lie is bad.
<|det|>image [687, 590, 738, 625]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [518, 634, 760, 654]<|/det|>Infinitives are useful when—
<|det|>list [518, 656, 864, 778]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [518, 656, 863, 675]<|/det|>◆ we wish to state an aim or purpose.
<|det|>text [556, 677, 794, 695]<|/det|>• He called to thank me.
<|det|>text [556, 698, 838, 738]<|/det|>- He wanted to borrow some money.
<|det|>text [556, 740, 852, 777]<|/det|>• They agreed to consider our case.
<|det|>text [518, 782, 783, 801]<|/det|>◆ we wish to indicate result.
<|det|>text [556, 804, 860, 843]<|/det|>- My great-grandmother lived to see me succeed.
<|det|>page_number [843, 930, 876, 948]<|/det|>93

The attention mask and the pad token id were not set. As a consequence, you may observe unexpected behavior. Please pass your input's `attention_mask` to obtain reliable results.
