<PAGE><|det|>text [105, 33, 754, 56]<|/det|>◆ as the object of the verb in a sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [143, 59, 368, 77]<|/det|>- I want to dance.
<|det|>text [104, 80, 900, 128]<|/det|>◆ as the subject of the verb, but it also takes an object of its own. For example,
<|det|>text [143, 130, 592, 152]<|/det|>• To respect our parents is our duty.
<|det|>text [104, 153, 875, 199]<|/det|>◆ as the object of the verb but it also takes an object of its own. For example,
<|det|>text [142, 201, 538, 223]<|/det|>• I expect to complete the task.
<|det|>text [104, 224, 856, 271]<|/det|>◆ as the object of the verb and also be modified by an adverb. For example,
<|det|>text [142, 272, 692, 295]<|/det|>• Many of the singers preferred to sing softly.
<|det|>text [104, 296, 695, 318]<|/det|>◆ to qualify an adjective or a noun. For example,
<|det|>text [142, 320, 503, 341]<|/det|>• This is not the time to laze.
<|det|>text [142, 342, 482, 365]<|/det|>• Quinine is bitter to taste.
<|det|>text [104, 367, 657, 389]<|/det|>◆ as the object of a preposition. For example,
<|det|>text [142, 390, 513, 411]<|/det|>• The concert is about to end.
<|det|>text [104, 413, 643, 435]<|/det|>◆ as the complement of a verb. For example,
<|det|>text [142, 437, 512, 459]<|/det|>• This is to ensure your safety.
<|det|>text [98, 473, 968, 519]<|/det|>We usually use to with the infinitive. However, it is not essential. Look at these instances where we do not use to.
<|det|>text [98, 536, 548, 557]<|/det|>We do not use to with the infinitive—
<|det|>text [99, 560, 455, 582]<|/det|>◆ with modals. For example,
<|det|>text [139, 583, 384, 605]<|/det|>• The child may go.
<|det|>text [98, 604, 860, 628]<|/det|>◆ after verbs of perception like see, hear, watch, know and feel.
<|det|>text [139, 629, 296, 650]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [138, 652, 571, 674]<|/det|>• I saw the clouds gather in the sky.
<|det|>text [98, 674, 634, 696]<|/det|>◆ after verbs like make and let. For example,
<|det|>text [138, 697, 414, 719]<|/det|>• Paula made him cry.
<|det|>text [138, 720, 405, 740]<|/det|>- Let me do this now.
<|det|>text [98, 743, 434, 765]<|/det|>◆ after the conjunction but.
<|det|>text [138, 766, 291, 787]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [138, 788, 543, 811]<|/det|>- The child did nothing but sleep.
<|det|>text [98, 812, 461, 834]<|/det|>◆ after had better, had rather,
<|det|>text [138, 835, 489, 857]<|/det|>sooner than, etc. For example,
<|det|>text [138, 858, 472, 878]<|/det|>• She had better leave now.
<|det|>image [552, 705, 971, 895]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [119, 934, 152, 951]<|/det|>94
<PAGE><|det|>title [73, 73, 135, 98]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [461, 66, 511, 103]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [68, 108, 877, 148]<|/det|>With verbs like need and dare, we use the infinitive with to in affirmative sentences, but not in interrogatives or negative sentences. For example,
<|det|>list [93, 149, 512, 226]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 149, 449, 167]<|/det|>• How dare you create such a ruckus?
<|det|>text [93, 168, 512, 186]<|/det|>• Rina dared her sister to do this reckless act.
<|det|>text [94, 188, 390, 206]<|/det|>• I need not finish the work yet.
<|det|>text [94, 208, 442, 226]<|/det|>• I need to find a good plumber soon.
<|det|>image [31, 270, 96, 308]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 274, 886, 321]<|/det|>Correct the errors, if any, in these sentences. Omit the infinitives if they are not required and add where necessary.
<|det|>list [113, 324, 512, 576]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 327, 433, 349]<|/det|>1. She did nothing but cry.
<|det|>text [116, 350, 443, 371]<|/det|>2. I got somebody help me.
<|det|>text [117, 372, 501, 416]<|/det|>3. 'How did the cat enter?'
'I forgot shutting the window.'
<|det|>text [116, 417, 458, 438]<|/det|>4. He is eager learn dancing.
<|det|>text [117, 439, 441, 459]<|/det|>5. They refused to tell a lie.
<|det|>text [117, 461, 578, 481]<|/det|>6. The boy had better to see the doctor.
<|det|>text [117, 483, 588, 504]<|/det|>7. Can you remind me buy some butter?
<|det|>text [116, 505, 676, 525]<|/det|>8. The sweets are delicious. You must to try one.
<|det|>text [116, 527, 715, 548]<|/det|>9. The captain will not let the truant soldier escape.
<|det|>text [104, 549, 791, 571]<|/det|>10. The teachers are going to participate in the tournament.
<|det|>image [671, 352, 907, 513]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [50, 585, 108, 628]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [124, 587, 882, 631]<|/det|>Look at these sentences where the infinitive has been used to join two simple sentences.
<|det|>list [52, 638, 650, 725]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [52, 638, 562, 658]<|/det|>- It is very humid. We should not exercise.
<|det|>text [92, 660, 400, 679]<|/det|>It is too humid to exercise.
<|det|>text [52, 682, 650, 704]<|/det|>• These are very ripe apples. They cannot be used.
<|det|>text [91, 705, 520, 726]<|/det|>These apples are too ripe to be used.
<|det|>text [51, 727, 838, 748]<|/det|>The adverb too is often used while joining sentences with infinitives.
<|det|>text [51, 765, 397, 785]<|/det|>Now, look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [52, 787, 597, 829]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [52, 787, 597, 807]<|/det|>• We were amused when we heard the news.
<|det|>text [90, 810, 502, 829]<|/det|>We were amused to hear the news.
<|det|>text [50, 831, 895, 875]<|/det|>The verb heard in the first sentence has been replaced by the infinitive to hear in the second sentence.
<|det|>page_number [851, 917, 886, 935]<|/det|>95
<PAGE><|det|>title [111, 38, 960, 111]<|/det|>B. Rewrite each of these sentences by removing the highlighted word or phrase and replacing it with an infinitive. Make necessary changes.
<|det|>list [169, 115, 725, 345]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [170, 115, 722, 137]<|/det|>1. The pages came off when I tried turning them.
<|det|>text [170, 138, 723, 159]<|/det|>2. He went to the hills as he wanted relaxation.
<|det|>text [170, 161, 611, 183]<|/det|>3. Give me something that I can eat.
<|det|>text [170, 185, 631, 206]<|/det|>4. He has some bills that he must pay.
<|det|>text [170, 208, 672, 229]<|/det|>5. He arrived early so that he could swim.
<|det|>text [170, 230, 763, 252]<|/det|>6. He has three sisters whom he must provide for.
<|det|>text [170, 254, 773, 275]<|/det|>7. She started pushing the car when it broke down.
<|det|>text [170, 277, 834, 299]<|/det|>8. I think she began enjoying the work for the company.
<|det|>text [170, 300, 841, 322]<|/det|>9. Health is so important that it should not be neglected.
<|det|>text [160, 323, 893, 345]<|/det|>10. Remember that the keys should be kept in the top drawer.
<|det|>image [58, 365, 132, 425]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [155, 369, 680, 393]<|/det|>Join these sets of sentences using infinitives.
<|det|>list [169, 398, 970, 617]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [170, 398, 852, 420]<|/det|>1. He wants to serve his people. This is his greatest desire.
<|det|>text [170, 422, 748, 444]<|/det|>2. He stopped at the hotel. He wanted some rest.
<|det|>text [170, 446, 645, 466]<|/det|>3. Drink more water. I advised him that.
<|det|>text [170, 468, 727, 490]<|/det|>4. I heard of his misfortune. I was sorry for that.
<|det|>text [170, 492, 722, 513]<|/det|>5. Please do not hesitate. Call me immediately.
<|det|>text [170, 514, 778, 536]<|/det|>6. Did you remember? You had to buy a newspaper.
<|det|>text [170, 537, 831, 558]<|/det|>7. He is going to Mumbai. He will start a business there.
<|det|>text [170, 560, 806, 582]<|/det|>8. He cannot afford higher education. He is very poor.
<|det|>text [169, 583, 842, 605]<|/det|>9. She is very ill. She cannot commence work right away.
<|det|>text [159, 606, 970, 629]<|/det|>10. She stopped work. She stopped so that she could open the door.
<|det|>title [102, 648, 256, 671]<|/det|>Participles
<|det|>image [104, 678, 164, 723]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 678, 436, 698]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [180, 700, 724, 745]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 700, 625, 722]<|/det|>• We saw a man polishing his shoes.
<|det|>text [180, 723, 724, 745]<|/det|>- Noticing a strange animal, we moved away.
<|det|>text [99, 746, 960, 791]<|/det|>The highlighted words are present participles that have been used as verbs and as adjectives connected to the underlined noun and pronoun.
<|det|>text [98, 807, 974, 856]<|/det|>Present participle forms of verbs always end in -ing. Such words represent actions that are going on, or are incomplete.
<|det|>page_number [126, 932, 159, 949]<|/det|>96
<PAGE><|det|>text [67, 55, 411, 75]<|/det|>Now, look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [68, 76, 767, 120]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [68, 76, 687, 97]<|/det|>• Duped by our tour guide, we returned to the hotel.
<|det|>text [68, 98, 760, 119]<|/det|>• We saw a few women bent by the weight of heavy sacks.
<|det|>text [67, 120, 881, 187]<|/det|>The highlighted words are examples of past participles that have been used as verbs and as adjectives modifying the underlined pronoun and noun, respectively.
<|det|>text [67, 199, 840, 245]<|/det|>Past participle forms of verbs usually end in -ed/-t/-n. They describe actions that have been completed.
<|det|>title [94, 273, 160, 298]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [90, 311, 280, 329]<|/det|>Look at this sentence.
<|det|>list [108, 331, 793, 412]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 331, 560, 350]<|/det|>- Walking down the road, amusing sight was seen.
<|det|>text [90, 351, 793, 371]<|/det|>In this sentence, there is no noun or pronoun to which the participle walking refers.
<|det|>text [91, 372, 405, 389]<|/det|>The sentence should be written as—
<|det|>text [110, 391, 548, 412]<|/det|>- Walking down the road, I saw an amusing sight.
<|det|>text [67, 442, 383, 462]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [69, 464, 548, 600]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [70, 464, 548, 486]<|/det|>• The piercing scream broke the silence.
<|det|>text [70, 487, 445, 508]<|/det|>• The barking dogs ran around.
<|det|>text [67, 509, 504, 594]<|/det|>The participles in the above sentences have been used simply as qualifying adjectives. They are known as participial adjectives.
<|det|>title [572, 512, 637, 537]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [568, 550, 850, 590]<|/det|>We may say that a participle is a verbal adjective.
<|det|>text [67, 613, 368, 635]<|/det|>Participles may be used—
<|det|>list [67, 636, 667, 706]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 636, 408, 657]<|/det|>◆ as attributes. For example,
<|det|>text [108, 657, 661, 679]<|/det|>- The broken glass lay scattered on the ground.
<|det|>text [109, 680, 465, 700]<|/det|>• A lost chance never returns.
<|det|>text [67, 701, 862, 725]<|/det|>◆ in the predicate, modifying the subject or the object. For example,
<|det|>list [108, 725, 482, 768]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [109, 725, 478, 747]<|/det|>• We found the child weeping.
<|det|>text [110, 748, 369, 768]<|/det|>• He kept us waiting.
<|det|>text [67, 769, 585, 791]<|/det|>◆ as a subjective complement. For example,
<|det|>list [109, 792, 427, 835]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [109, 792, 436, 813]<|/det|>• The story was interesting.
<|det|>text [109, 814, 424, 835]<|/det|>• The trek was very tiring.
<|det|>text [67, 835, 816, 858]<|/det|>◆ as part of a phrase that functions as an adjective. For example,
<|det|>text [108, 858, 633, 880]<|/det|>• The boy standing at the gate is my brother.
<|det|>page_number [859, 914, 895, 931]<|/det|>97
<PAGE><|det|>text [110, 30, 862, 58]<|/det|>We can join pairs of sentences by using participles. For example,
<|det|>list [113, 58, 697, 128]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 58, 697, 81]<|/det|>• The boy saw a dog. It was coming towards him.
<|det|>text [150, 82, 620, 104]<|/det|>The boy saw a dog coming towards him.
<|det|>text [111, 104, 785, 127]<|/det|>• Sheila opened the box. She took out an old manuscript.
<|det|>text [148, 127, 819, 150]<|/det|>Opening the cupboard, Sheila took out an old manuscript.
<|det|>text [108, 158, 854, 181]<|/det|>In the above examples, both actions take place at the same time.
<|det|>text [108, 182, 945, 228]<|/det|>When we join the sentences, one finite verb is removed. So we use the continuous definite particle.
<|det|>text [107, 237, 966, 308]<|/det|>But sometimes, the second action begins only after the first has been completed. In that case, we convert the verb denoting the first action into a participle. For example,
<|det|>list [109, 309, 811, 354]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 309, 810, 331]<|/det|>• He had lost all his money. He went to his mother for help.
<|det|>text [148, 332, 800, 354]<|/det|>Having lost all his money, he went to his mother for help.
<|det|>text [108, 355, 871, 402]<|/det|>This is an example of perfect participle where the action has been completed at some point of time in the past.
<|det|>text [111, 425, 958, 496]<|/det|>D. Pick out the participle in each of these sentences and state whether it is a participial adjective, present participle, past participle or perfect participle.
<|det|>list [175, 501, 928, 730]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [176, 501, 509, 525]<|/det|>1. The long journey is tiring.
<|det|>text [175, 526, 560, 546]<|/det|>2. Learned men will never agree.
<|det|>text [175, 547, 597, 569]<|/det|>3. I met a girl weeping inconsolably.
<|det|>text [175, 570, 637, 590]<|/det|>4. The selected articles are in this folder.
<|det|>text [175, 592, 701, 614]<|/det|>5. Beaten by the enemy, the soldiers retreated.
<|det|>text [175, 615, 713, 637]<|/det|>6. Drawing out his sword, the prince advanced.
<|det|>text [175, 638, 734, 660]<|/det|>7. Having finished the painting, the artist relaxed.
<|det|>text [175, 661, 711, 683]<|/det|>8. The delightful song echoed across the valley.
<|det|>text [175, 684, 842, 705]<|/det|>9. The inspector transferred to this district is a close friend.
<|det|>text [164, 707, 928, 730]<|/det|>10. Having borrowed some books, he is now ready for the exams.
<|det|>text [111, 752, 715, 776]<|/det|>E. Join these pairs of sentences by using participles.
<|det|>list [160, 781, 744, 921]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 781, 612, 803]<|/det|>1. I knew the answer. I raised my hand.
<|det|>text [160, 805, 588, 827]<|/det|>2. He was disheartened. He gave up.
<|det|>text [160, 828, 704, 850]<|/det|>3. He seized the sword. He threatened the king.
<|det|>text [160, 851, 684, 873]<|/det|>4. They were terrified. They stood in a huddle.
<|det|>text [160, 874, 709, 896]<|/det|>5. The money is collected easily. It has no value.
<|det|>text [160, 897, 744, 921]<|/det|>6. They travelled the whole day. They felt very tired.
<|det|>page_number [133, 926, 168, 944]<|/det|>98
<PAGE><|det|>list [131, 60, 586, 231]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 60, 556, 81]<|/det|>7. He was irritated by the discussion.
<|det|>text [176, 83, 346, 100]<|/det|>He walked out.
<|det|>text [131, 103, 585, 146]<|/det|>8. The lion attacked the hunter. He was driven by rage.
<|det|>text [131, 147, 596, 189]<|/det|>9. I found the door wide open. I entered the precincts of the temple.
<|det|>text [124, 190, 548, 232]<|/det|>10. He took a picture of the bird. The bird was flying overhead.
<|det|>image [631, 60, 907, 222]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [65, 253, 186, 273]<|/det|>Gerunds
<|det|>image [68, 275, 118, 313]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [144, 274, 862, 316]<|/det|>Look at these sentences. Notice how the -ing words are used in these sentences.
<|det|>list [67, 318, 808, 410]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 318, 731, 341]<|/det|>- Wrestling is popular in India. (subject of the sentence)
<|det|>text [67, 342, 731, 364]<|/det|>• I enjoy reading English poetry. (object of the sentence)
<|det|>text [67, 364, 807, 386]<|/det|>- He is fond of drinking cold coffee. (object of the preposition)
<|det|>text [67, 386, 719, 410]<|/det|>• What I quite detest is lying. (complement of the verb)
<|det|>text [64, 423, 908, 467]<|/det|>In these sentences, the -ing words function like nouns. However, all these words are derived from verbs.
<|det|>text [64, 482, 790, 528]<|/det|>When the -ing form of the verb is used as a noun in a sentence, we call it the gerund.
<|det|>title [94, 561, 160, 587]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [474, 557, 526, 595]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [90, 599, 578, 619]<|/det|>The -ing word can be used in different ways. For example,
<|det|>list [91, 620, 648, 680]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [91, 620, 474, 639]<|/det|>- Collecting stamps is her hobby. (subject)
<|det|>text [91, 640, 353, 658]<|/det|>- I don't like writing. (object)
<|det|>text [91, 658, 648, 679]<|/det|>• My favourite hobby is reading. (complement of the sentence)
<|det|>title [66, 708, 375, 730]<|/det|>Gerunds or Infinitives?
<|det|>text [64, 736, 908, 804]<|/det|>As both gerunds and infinitives have the force of a noun and a verb, they have the same uses. Thus, in many sentences either of them may be used. For example,
<|det|>list [67, 805, 463, 895]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 805, 404, 827]<|/det|>• He likes singing. (gerund)
<|det|>text [108, 828, 417, 849]<|/det|>He likes to sing. (infinitive)
<|det|>text [67, 850, 445, 872]<|/det|>• She began laughing. (gerund)
<|det|>text [107, 873, 463, 895]<|/det|>She began to laugh. (infinitive)
<|det|>page_number [861, 914, 894, 931]<|/det|>99
<PAGE><|det|>text [92, 33, 831, 81]<|/det|>However, the only non-finite form of the verb that can follow a preposition is a gerund. For example,
<|det|>list [93, 81, 845, 174]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 81, 740, 103]<|/det|>• They were punished for to lie. (infinitive, incorrect)
<|det|>text [93, 104, 695, 126]<|/det|>• They were punished for lying. (gerund, correct)
<|det|>text [93, 127, 844, 148]<|/det|>• We cannot live without to drink water. (infinitive, incorrect)
<|det|>text [93, 149, 802, 172]<|/det|>• We cannot live without drinking water. (gerund, correct)
<|det|>text [90, 187, 895, 235]<|/det|>The table below lists examples of sentences where we can only use gerunds.
<|det|>title [347, 245, 710, 271]<|/det|>Verbs followed by prepositions
<|det|>text [105, 278, 411, 303]<|/det|>They always disapproved
<|det|>text [544, 280, 660, 304]<|/det|>of boxing.
<|det|>text [104, 310, 346, 334]<|/det|>We have succeeded
<|det|>text [544, 312, 788, 337]<|/det|>in winning the game.
<|det|>title [235, 344, 819, 370]<|/det|>Adjectives with prepositions followed by gerunds
<|det|>text [104, 376, 267, 399]<|/det|>He is fond of
<|det|>text [544, 378, 686, 402]<|/det|>drinking tea.
<|det|>text [104, 409, 351, 433]<|/det|>We were all tired of
<|det|>text [544, 411, 836, 436]<|/det|>listening to his silly jokes.
<|det|>title [140, 459, 789, 484]<|/det|>Identify the participles and gerunds in these sentences.
<|det|>list [139, 489, 661, 905]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 490, 380, 514]<|/det|>1. I prefer walking.
<|det|>text [139, 514, 655, 536]<|/det|>2. Being satisfied, we agreed to buy the car.
<|det|>text [139, 537, 633, 559]<|/det|>3. Hearing the sound, the bird flew away.
<|det|>text [139, 559, 661, 604]<|/det|>4. Children love building castles in the sand and decorating them.
<|det|>text [139, 605, 661, 650]<|/det|>5. We insisted on complaining to the police about the disturbing incidents.
<|det|>text [139, 651, 655, 696]<|/det|>6. We aim at winning the tournament as the winning team will receive a grand prize.
<|det|>text [139, 697, 617, 763]<|/det|>7. The visitors were thrilled to see the glistening trout dancing in the rivulet meandering down the hills.
<|det|>text [138, 765, 650, 833]<|/det|>8. We have succeeded in convincing the authorities about the need to protect the tribes living in remote villages.
<|det|>text [138, 835, 625, 905]<|/det|>9. Noticing the clouds gathering in the distant sky, we decided to leave in the vehicle parked near the gate.
<|det|>image [645, 501, 978, 848]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [107, 929, 152, 948]<|/det|>100
<PAGE><|det|>title [58, 48, 631, 70]<|/det|>G. Correct the errors, if any, in these sentences.
<|det|>list [129, 76, 490, 380]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 76, 384, 99]<|/det|>1. Forgiving is divine.
<|det|>text [131, 100, 482, 121]<|/det|>2. He has some debts to clear.
<|det|>text [131, 123, 482, 144]<|/det|>3. My shoes need be mended.
<|det|>text [131, 145, 478, 168]<|/det|>4. I shall be glad helping you.
<|det|>text [131, 169, 565, 191]<|/det|>5. The parcel was too heavy carrying.
<|det|>text [131, 192, 599, 214]<|/det|>6. He refused playing in the tournament.
<|det|>text [131, 215, 625, 238]<|/det|>7. With a little help, she is sure doing well.
<|det|>text [131, 238, 497, 261]<|/det|>8. This is a stick that is walking.
<|det|>text [131, 262, 678, 284]<|/det|>9. He is averse to invest money in this business.
<|det|>text [120, 285, 743, 330]<|/det|>10. This book is not only interesting read but also easy understanding.
<|det|>text [120, 330, 851, 374]<|/det|>11. To read being his favourite hobby, he read whenever he had some spare time.
<|det|>text [120, 375, 794, 422]<|/det|>12. Who is responsible for breaking this mirror? I want you answering truthfully.
<|det|>image [679, 71, 917, 228]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [65, 443, 905, 490]<|/det|>4. Complete the passage with the correct non-finite forms of the verbs given in the brackets.
<|det|>list [120, 496, 905, 891]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 496, 848, 564]<|/det|>As a child, I did not like ____ (sleep) during the summer afternoons. I would wander in the garden ____ (look) for ____ (nest) birds. It was fun ____ (look) at the
<|det|>text [120, 565, 848, 611]<|/det|>fledglings. ____ (take) one home was of course out of the question. I knew that it was not the correct thing ____
<|det|>text [120, 612, 901, 680]<|/det|>(do). Sometimes when my father spotted me near a nest, I would be scolded for ____ (disturb) the birds. But my objective was not ____ (frighten) the little creatures, but ____ (observe)
<|det|>text [120, 681, 905, 773]<|/det|>them closely. Once I remember ____ (catch) a weaver-bird. It did not seem ____ (mind) my ____ (interfere) nature. In fact, it allowed me ____ (thrust) my hand into its nest and ____ (touch) the sleeping fledgling. Our gardener was a kind
<|det|>text [120, 774, 901, 890]<|/det|>man. ____ (garden) was his passion. He once allowed me ____ (climb) a tree and look into a tree-hole. Neither he nor I had noticed the snake ____ (lurk) on a branch. But my brother did. ____ (scream), he alerted us. ____ (watch) birds is no doubt an interesting hobby!
<|det|>page_number [851, 933, 895, 952]<|/det|>101
<PAGE><|det|>header [19, 12, 297, 155]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [444, 45, 972, 88]<|/det|>Pittypat on a Tippy topo
<|det|>text [98, 157, 972, 207]<|/det|>Read this poem by an American writer who was famous for the poetry he wrote for children.
<|det|>text [98, 222, 485, 247]<|/det|>All day long they come and go—
<|det|>text [99, 248, 354, 271]<|/det|>Pittypat and Tippytoe;
<|det|>text [111, 272, 497, 295]<|/det|>Footprints up and down the hall,
<|det|>text [126, 296, 502, 319]<|/det|>Playthings scattered on the floor,
<|det|>text [113, 320, 440, 342]<|/det|>Finger-marks along the wall,
<|det|>text [126, 343, 495, 365]<|/det|>Tell-tale smudges on the door—
<|det|>text [102, 366, 482, 389]<|/det|>By these presents you shall know
<|det|>text [103, 390, 353, 413]<|/det|>Pittypat and Tippytope.
<|det|>text [100, 426, 414, 451]<|/det|>How they riot at their play!
<|det|>text [101, 451, 388, 473]<|/det|>And a dozen times a day
<|det|>text [117, 474, 506, 496]<|/det|>In they troop, demanding bread—
<|det|>text [128, 496, 455, 518]<|/det|>Only buttered bread will do,
<|det|>text [115, 519, 460, 541]<|/det|>And the butter must be spread
<|det|>text [128, 541, 444, 563]<|/det|>Inches thick with sugar too!
<|det|>text [101, 564, 386, 585]<|/det|>And I never can say 'No,
<|det|>text [101, 586, 361, 609]<|/det|>Pittytat and Tippytoe!'
<|det|>text [98, 622, 517, 644]<|/det|>Sometimes there are griefs to soothe,
<|det|>text [99, 645, 510, 666]<|/det|>Sometimes ruffled brows to smooth;
<|det|>text [113, 668, 397, 690]<|/det|>For (I much regret to say)
<|det|>text [125, 691, 371, 713]<|/det|>Tippytoe and Pittypat
<|det|>text [113, 713, 456, 735]<|/det|>Sometimes interrupt their play
<|det|>text [125, 736, 414, 758]<|/det|>With an internecine spat;
<|det|>text [99, 758, 448, 780]<|/det|>Fie, for shame! to quarrel so—
<|det|>text [99, 781, 350, 803]<|/det|>Pittypat and Tippytoe!
<|det|>text [97, 816, 474, 839]<|/det|>Oh the thousand worrying things
<|det|>text [99, 840, 404, 862]<|/det|>Every day recurrent brings!
<|det|>text [113, 863, 493, 885]<|/det|>Hands to scrub and hair to brush,
<|det|>text [128, 886, 500, 908]<|/det|>Search for playthings gone amiss,
<|det|>image [584, 584, 952, 902]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 931, 168, 950]<|/det|>102
<PAGE><|det|>text [92, 35, 450, 57]<|/det|>Many a wee complaint to hush,
<|det|>text [108, 58, 406, 80]<|/det|>Many a little bump to kiss;
<|det|>text [82, 81, 465, 104]<|/det|>Life seems one vain, fleeting show
<|det|>text [82, 104, 361, 127]<|/det|>To Pittypat and Tippytope!
<|det|>text [80, 140, 388, 163]<|/det|>And when day is at an end,
<|det|>text [82, 164, 414, 185]<|/det|>There are little duds to mend;
<|det|>text [93, 187, 432, 209]<|/det|>Little frocks are strangely torn,
<|det|>text [105, 210, 443, 232]<|/det|>Little shoes great holes reveal,
<|det|>text [93, 233, 430, 255]<|/det|>Little hose, but one day worn,
<|det|>text [105, 256, 436, 278]<|/det|>Rudely yawn at thee and heel!
<|det|>text [78, 279, 485, 301]<|/det|>Who but you could work such woe,
<|det|>text [78, 302, 328, 325]<|/det|>Pittypat and Tippytope?
<|det|>text [77, 340, 489, 363]<|/det|>But when comes this thought to me:
<|det|>text [78, 364, 459, 386]<|/det|>'Some there are that childless be,'
<|det|>text [93, 387, 390, 410]<|/det|>Stealing to their little beds,
<|det|>text [105, 411, 411, 433]<|/det|>With a love I cannot speak,
<|det|>text [90, 434, 437, 456]<|/det|>Tenderly I stroke their heads—
<|det|>text [105, 457, 444, 479]<|/det|>Fondly kiss each velvet cheek.
<|det|>text [77, 480, 461, 503]<|/det|>God help those who do not know
<|det|>text [77, 504, 334, 527]<|/det|>A Pittypat or Tippytoe!
<|det|>text [74, 541, 435, 564]<|/det|>On the floor and down the hall,
<|det|>text [75, 565, 445, 588]<|/det|>Rudely smutched upon the wall,
<|det|>text [87, 589, 430, 611]<|/det|>There are proofs in every kind
<|det|>text [103, 612, 476, 636]<|/det|>Of the havoc they have wrought,
<|det|>text [87, 636, 432, 658]<|/det|>And upon my heart you'd find
<|det|>text [103, 659, 511, 682]<|/det|>Just such trade-marks, if you sought;
<|det|>text [70, 683, 370, 705]<|/det|>Oh, how glad I am 'tis so,
<|det|>text [70, 706, 326, 730]<|/det|>Pittypat and Tippytope!
<|det|>text [761, 728, 929, 752]<|/det|>(Eugene Field)
<|det|>image [31, 772, 120, 821]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [125, 778, 405, 800]<|/det|>Answer these questions.
<|det|>text [122, 806, 929, 830]<|/det|>1. Who do you think are Pittypat and Tippytope? Why are they called so?
<|det|>text [122, 831, 558, 853]<|/det|>2. To what can the poet not say 'no'?
<|det|>text [122, 854, 926, 877]<|/det|>3. What does the poet need to do for Pittypat and Tippytope every day?
<|det|>text [122, 878, 860, 902]<|/det|>4. 'Rudely yawn at toe and heel!' What yawns rudely and why?
<|det|>list [122, 806, 929, 902]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [864, 930, 907, 948]<|/det|>103
<PAGE><|det|>text [152, 40, 896, 64]<|/det|>5. According to the poet, who needs help from God, and why?
<|det|>text [154, 65, 417, 87]<|/det|>6. Explain the lines—
<|det|>list [152, 40, 896, 87]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [361, 88, 699, 110]<|/det|>And upon my heart you'd find
<|det|>text [382, 111, 789, 134]<|/det|>Just such trade-marks, if you sought;
<|det|>text [336, 135, 625, 156]<|/det|>Oh, how glad I am 'tis so,
<|det|>text [336, 157, 582, 180]<|/det|>Pittypat and Tippytoe!
<|det|>image [65, 195, 136, 233]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [152, 203, 698, 225]<|/det|>State whether these sentences are true or false.
<|det|>list [152, 231, 970, 371]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [152, 231, 867, 255]<|/det|>1. At the end of the day, little pants and skirts need mending.
<|det|>text [151, 255, 951, 299]<|/det|>2. The presents that Pittypat and Tippytoe leave behind are broken windows and torn curtains.
<|det|>text [151, 301, 972, 347]<|/det|>3. The poet has no words to explain the love he feels for Pittypat and Tippytoe.
<|det|>text [151, 347, 914, 371]<|/det|>4. Pittypat and Tippytoe always put away their playthings neatly.
<|det|>image [65, 386, 153, 425]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [154, 393, 907, 418]<|/det|>Find suitable phrases from the poem that mean the following—
<|det|>list [151, 422, 312, 468]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [152, 422, 251, 442]<|/det|>1. seek
<|det|>text [417, 422, 537, 442]<|/det|>2. created
<|det|>text [677, 422, 818, 445]<|/det|>3. damage
<|det|>text [152, 446, 274, 468]<|/det|>4. misery
<|det|>text [417, 446, 498, 468]<|/det|>5. tiny
<|det|>text [677, 446, 785, 465]<|/det|>6. show
<|det|>page_number [117, 930, 165, 950]<|/det|>104
<PAGE><|det|>title [65, 58, 485, 132]<|/det|>Transitive and
Intransitive Verbs
<|det|>header [735, 0, 999, 155]<|/det|>12
<|det|>image [65, 157, 130, 206]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [141, 157, 880, 200]<|/det|>We know that there can be no sentences without verbs. A verb tells us—
<|det|>list [63, 212, 816, 282]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [65, 213, 675, 235]<|/det|>• what a person or thing does—She designs clothes.
<|det|>text [65, 236, 815, 258]<|/det|>• what is done to a person—The girl was scolded by her mother.
<|det|>text [65, 259, 790, 281]<|/det|>• what a person or thing is—The tiger is a magnificent animal.
<|det|>text [61, 295, 706, 318]<|/det|>A verb can consist of more than one word. For example,
<|det|>list [63, 319, 496, 364]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [63, 319, 438, 341]<|/det|>• The boys are playing hockey.
<|det|>text [64, 341, 495, 364]<|/det|>• The actors were rehearsing a play.
<|det|>text [61, 378, 378, 399]<|/det|>Now, look at this sentence.
<|det|>list [63, 401, 705, 444]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [63, 401, 444, 422]<|/det|>• My mother polished the vase.
<|det|>text [61, 423, 704, 444]<|/det|>In this sentence, the vase receives the action of the verb.
<|det|>text [61, 469, 903, 493]<|/det|>The person or thing that receives the action of the verb is called the object.
<|det|>text [59, 528, 910, 573]<|/det|>When a verb has an object that receives the action of the verb, it is called the transitive verb.
<|det|>text [61, 599, 352, 620]<|/det|>Now, read this sentence.
<|det|>list [63, 622, 881, 689]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [63, 622, 361, 642]<|/det|>• The child cried aloud.
<|det|>text [60, 643, 881, 688]<|/det|>In this sentence, there is no object. The action stops at the doer. No one receives the action of the verb.
<|det|>text [58, 714, 752, 739]<|/det|>When a verb has no object, it is called the intransitive verb.
<|det|>text [59, 774, 880, 844]<|/det|>Most transitive verbs take a single object. But some transitive verbs like give, ask, promise, tell, etc., take two objects—a direct object and an indirect object. For example,
<|det|>list [63, 845, 825, 890]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [63, 845, 414, 867]<|/det|>• My father gave me a cycle.
<|det|>text [63, 867, 825, 889]<|/det|>The direct object of the verb is cycle and the indirect object is me.
<|det|>page_number [854, 919, 898, 937]<|/det|>105
<PAGE><|det|>text [100, 50, 892, 96]<|/det|>The person or thing that receives the action of the verb is called the direct object.
<|det|>text [99, 138, 946, 187]<|/det|>The person or thing to whom or on whom the action of the verb is done or who receives the direct object is called the indirect object.
<|det|>title [125, 230, 192, 255]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [137, 267, 916, 329]<|/det|>The direct object of the verb can be identified by asking the question who/what after the verb. The indirect object is identified by asking the question to/for/from + whom/what after the verb. For example,
<|det|>text [184, 331, 802, 351]<|/det|>- The students gave the teacher (indirect) a bouquet of flowers (direct).
<|det|>text [137, 364, 923, 406]<|/det|>Sometimes, the subject and the object refer to the same person or thing. We then say that the verb has been used reflexively. For example,
<|det|>text [185, 407, 405, 425]<|/det|>• The man hurt himself.
<|det|>text [137, 439, 918, 481]<|/det|>Do remember that most verbs can be used both transitively as well as intransitively. For example,
<|det|>text [185, 482, 475, 501]<|/det|>• The lions fought the elephants.
<|det|>text [185, 502, 448, 522]<|/det|>• The soldiers fought bravely.
<|det|>text [137, 535, 783, 556]<|/det|>Some verbs like come, go, fall, die, sleep can never be used transitively.
<|det|>title [100, 599, 861, 669]<|/det|>4. Underline the verbs in these sentences and state in each case whether the verb has been used transitively or intransitively. If the verb is used transitively, point out the object/objects.
<|det|>list [167, 675, 585, 905]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [167, 675, 386, 696]<|/det|>1. Do not tell a lie.
<|det|>text [166, 698, 421, 719]<|/det|>2. Time heals wounds.
<|det|>text [166, 721, 474, 743]<|/det|>3. I wrote a letter yesterday.
<|det|>text [166, 745, 470, 766]<|/det|>4. I bought my sister a pen.
<|det|>text [166, 768, 478, 789]<|/det|>5. I know the secretary well.
<|det|>text [166, 790, 519, 813]<|/det|>6. I feel a severe pain in my leg.
<|det|>text [166, 813, 506, 835]<|/det|>7. The children played merrily.
<|det|>text [166, 836, 560, 856]<|/det|>8. The man stood in front of the car.
<|det|>text [166, 858, 580, 881]<|/det|>9. I floated paper boats in the puddle.
<|det|>text [157, 882, 585, 905]<|/det|>10. He spoke rudely. He enjoys cricket.
<|det|>image [699, 745, 838, 832]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [130, 932, 175, 950]<|/det|>106
<PAGE><|det|>list [85, 45, 625, 367]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 45, 593, 69]<|/det|>11. The silver moon shines in the starry sky.
<|det|>text [87, 70, 348, 91]<|/det|>12. The glass cracked.
<|det|>text [88, 93, 377, 115]<|/det|>13. Shiela gave us a bag.
<|det|>text [89, 116, 374, 136]<|/det|>14. I offered him a cake.
<|det|>text [90, 138, 419, 160]<|/det|>15. The car halted suddenly.
<|det|>text [91, 161, 448, 183]<|/det|>16. The teacher told us a story.
<|det|>text [91, 184, 432, 206]<|/det|>17. He drinks milk every day.
<|det|>text [91, 207, 480, 228]<|/det|>18. We talked about the incident.
<|det|>text [91, 230, 450, 252]<|/det|>19. Rima broke my spectacles.
<|det|>text [88, 253, 515, 275]<|/det|>20. I opened the window cautiously.
<|det|>text [88, 276, 558, 297]<|/det|>21. My cat is sleeping on the deckchair.
<|det|>text [88, 298, 569, 320]<|/det|>22. My mother prepared pasta for dinner.
<|det|>text [88, 321, 615, 343]<|/det|>23. The bird picked a worm from the ground.
<|det|>text [88, 344, 615, 367]<|/det|>24. The principal promised me a scholarship.
<|det|>image [607, 70, 831, 255]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [37, 383, 467, 408]<|/det|>Verbs of Incomplete Predication
<|det|>image [55, 424, 108, 466]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 429, 383, 450]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [58, 465, 253, 532]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [58, 465, 245, 487]<|/det|>• A dog barks.
<|det|>text [58, 488, 253, 510]<|/det|>- People laugh.
<|det|>text [58, 511, 251, 532]<|/det|>- Jackals howl.
<|det|>text [54, 546, 871, 592]<|/det|>These sentences make complete sense. We do not need more words to complete the sense of the verbs.
<|det|>text [55, 606, 441, 629]<|/det|>Now, read these groups of words.
<|det|>list [58, 630, 337, 716]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [58, 630, 292, 652]<|/det|>• The boy appears.
<|det|>text [58, 654, 175, 673]<|/det|>- She is.
<|det|>text [58, 676, 223, 695]<|/det|>- She seems.
<|det|>text [58, 697, 334, 716]<|/det|>• The child continued.
<|det|>text [30, 733, 892, 778]<|/det|>• None of these groups of words make sense. We have to add more words to complete the sense of the verbs.
<|det|>text [55, 780, 367, 800]<|/det|>Read these sentences now.
<|det|>list [58, 802, 436, 896]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [58, 802, 395, 825]<|/det|>• The boy appears cheerful.
<|det|>text [58, 826, 280, 847]<|/det|>• She is focussed.
<|det|>text [58, 849, 270, 871]<|/det|>• She seems sad.
<|det|>text [58, 872, 434, 895]<|/det|>• The child continued jumping.
<|det|>image [607, 745, 838, 788]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [848, 921, 892, 940]<|/det|>107
<PAGE><|det|>text [114, 44, 932, 140]<|/det|>Thus, we see that sometimes, intransitive verbs require more words to make complete sense, and to complete the predicate. Such verbs are called verbs of incomplete predication. The word or words required to make the sense complete is called the complement of the verb.
<|det|>text [112, 157, 928, 206]<|/det|>In the above examples, the underlined complements tell us something about the subject. So these are called subjective complements.
<|det|>text [111, 220, 951, 269]<|/det|>We know that transitive verbs have objects. However, sometimes just an object is not good enough. Read this sentence.
<|det|>text [114, 270, 489, 294]<|/det|>• The members elected Gagan.
<|det|>text [111, 307, 864, 331]<|/det|>We need something more to complete the predicate. For example,
<|det|>text [114, 333, 590, 355]<|/det|>• The members elected Gagan secretary.
<|det|>text [112, 355, 911, 402]<|/det|>Here, the word secretary is referring to Gagan, the object of the verb. So secretary is the objective complement.
<|det|>text [112, 424, 945, 472]<|/det|>8. Underline the verbs in these sentences and pick out the subjective and objective complements, if any.
<|det|>list [180, 478, 642, 892]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [181, 478, 370, 499]<|/det|>1. We felt cold.
<|det|>text [181, 502, 393, 525]<|/det|>2. My dog is lazy.
<|det|>text [181, 526, 420, 547]<|/det|>3. We became tired.
<|det|>text [181, 549, 403, 569]<|/det|>4. I feel miserable.
<|det|>text [181, 572, 448, 593]<|/det|>5. He became a writer.
<|det|>text [181, 595, 440, 616]<|/det|>6. They are Buddhists.
<|det|>text [181, 618, 456, 640]<|/det|>7. The building is to let.
<|det|>text [181, 641, 500, 661]<|/det|>8. We named the child Sita.
<|det|>text [181, 664, 482, 686]<|/det|>9. He was elected prefect.
<|det|>text [174, 687, 450, 709]<|/det|>10. The night grew cold.
<|det|>text [174, 711, 458, 732]<|/det|>11. The girl seems gentle.
<|det|>text [174, 734, 444, 755]<|/det|>12. The pill tastes bitter.
<|det|>text [174, 757, 502, 778]<|/det|>13. The machine is of no use.
<|det|>text [174, 780, 516, 802]<|/det|>14. We found the child asleep.
<|det|>text [174, 804, 550, 825]<|/det|>15. Exercise has made him sturdy.
<|det|>text [174, 827, 508, 849]<|/det|>16. I found the child weeping.
<|det|>text [174, 850, 578, 872]<|/det|>17. The committee found him guilty.
<|det|>text [174, 873, 642, 894]<|/det|>18. His account filled us with amazement.
<|det|>image [557, 516, 871, 790]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [145, 934, 192, 951]<|/det|>108
<PAGE><|det|>title [62, 51, 627, 86]<|/det|>Active and Passive Voice
<|det|>page_number [845, 46, 915, 85]<|/det|>13
<|det|>image [67, 157, 118, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [143, 165, 384, 186]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>text [144, 189, 427, 211]<|/det|>• He painted a picture.
<|det|>text [144, 212, 527, 235]<|/det|>• A picture was painted by him.
<|det|>list [144, 189, 527, 235]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 235, 871, 326]<|/det|>Both the sentences have the same meaning but have been constructed differently. In the first sentence, the highlighted verb indicates that the subject (he) did the action. In the second sentence, the function of the verb is to show that the subject (a picture) was acted on.
<|det|>text [61, 345, 908, 414]<|/det|>The verb that expresses that the action is carried out by the subject is said to be in the active voice. The active voice is used when the agent or the doer is to be made prominent.
<|det|>text [62, 447, 862, 494]<|/det|>The verb that expresses what is done to the subject is said to be in the passive voice.
<|det|>text [62, 513, 886, 581]<|/det|>See how the following sentences in the active voice have been changed into passive voice without changing their meanings.
<|det|>text [67, 583, 470, 603]<|/det|>• The referee ordered a re-match.
<|det|>text [111, 606, 550, 627]<|/det|>A re-match was ordered by the referee.
<|det|>text [67, 628, 386, 649]<|/det|>- Shireen bought a house.
<|det|>text [111, 650, 474, 672]<|/det|>A house was bought by Shireen.
<|det|>image [557, 520, 871, 788]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 688, 804, 709]<|/det|>Let us revise the rules of conversion from active to passive voice.
<|det|>text [61, 711, 885, 779]<|/det|>The positions of the subject and the object of the active verb are interchanged. The subject of the verb in the active voice is made the object of the verb in the passive voice.
<|det|>text [61, 780, 865, 803]<|/det|>◆ In the passive voice, the preposition by is added before the object.
<|det|>text [61, 804, 845, 851]<|/det|>◆ The number of the verb is changed according to the number and person of the subject.
<|det|>text [111, 852, 297, 871]<|/det|>- He loves me.
<|det|>text [142, 874, 361, 897]<|/det|>I am loved by him.
<|det|>page_number [854, 924, 898, 942]<|/det|>109
<PAGE><|det|>text [113, 42, 920, 93]<|/det|>Active verbs followed by a preposition retain the preposition. For example,
<|det|>text [151, 93, 593, 115]<|/det|>• The students laughed at the clown.
<|det|>text [151, 116, 687, 139]<|/det|>• The clown was laughed at by the students.
<|det|>list [151, 93, 687, 139]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [112, 154, 951, 205]<|/det|>Not all tenses take the passive form. Study the table to understand how the verb is changed into the passive voice form in different tenses.
<|det|>table [117, 228, 960, 880]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Tense</td><td>Active voice</td><td>Passive voice</td></tr><tr><td>Simple present</td><td>He reads a book.</td><td>A book is read by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Present continuous</td><td>He is reading a book.</td><td>A book is being read by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Present perfect continuous</td><td>He has read a book.</td><td>A book has been read by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Present perfect continuous</td><td>He has been reading a book.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Simple past</td><td>He read a book.</td><td>A book was read by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Past continuous</td><td>He was reading a book.</td><td>A book was being read by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Past perfect continuous</td><td>He had read a book.</td><td>A book had been read by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Simple future</td><td>He had been reading a book.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Future continuous</td><td>He will read a book.</td><td>A book will be read by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Future continuous</td><td>He will be reading a book.</td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Future perfect continuous</td><td>He will have read a book.</td><td>A book will have been read by him.</td></tr><tr><td>Future perfect continuous</td><td>He will have been reading a book.</td><td></td></tr></table>
<|det|>page_number [140, 937, 183, 956]<|/det|>110
<PAGE><|det|>image [65, 28, 121, 106]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [87, 48, 150, 74]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [95, 88, 848, 170]<|/det|>If the active verb has two objects—one direct and the other indirect—either of them becomes the subject of the passive verb, and the other remains as an object in the passive voice. The object that comes after the passive verb is called the retained object. For example,
<|det|>list [140, 172, 686, 235]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 172, 570, 191]<|/det|>• My father gave me a calculator. (active voice)
<|det|>text [140, 193, 642, 213]<|/det|>- I was given a calculator by my father. (passive voice)
<|det|>text [140, 215, 684, 235]<|/det|>• A calculator was given to me by my father. (passive voice)
<|det|>text [58, 270, 911, 318]<|/det|>Study this table to understand how imperative sentences are changed into the passive voice.
<|det|>table [62, 336, 915, 515]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Active voice</td><td>Passive voice</td></tr><tr><td>Take it away.</td><td>Let it be taken away.</td></tr><tr><td>Do it tomorrow.</td><td>Let it be done tomorrow.</td></tr><tr><td>Open the window.</td><td>Let the window be opened.</td></tr><tr><td>Do not leave the food uncovered.</td><td>Let not the food be kept uncovered.</td></tr></table>
<|det|>text [60, 535, 808, 584]<|/det|>- The imperative sentence in the passive voice takes the form—Let + object + be + past participle
<|det|>text [60, 584, 631, 631]<|/det|>- If the sentence is in the negative, the form is—Let not + object + be + past participle
<|det|>list [60, 535, 808, 631]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [58, 651, 743, 675]<|/det|>A. Put the verb in the brackets in a suitable passive form.
<|det|>list [111, 681, 858, 915]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 681, 676, 705]<|/det|>1. The car ____ (park) in the drive.
<|det|>text [111, 706, 770, 752]<|/det|>2. The illegal immigrants ____ (take) to the police station.
<|det|>text [111, 752, 720, 775]<|/det|>3. We ____ (teach) ballet by Mr Harry.
<|det|>text [111, 776, 857, 799]<|/det|>4. The school play ____ (produce) by me this year.
<|det|>text [111, 800, 825, 822]<|/det|>5. Snow white ____ (poison) by her stepmother.
<|det|>text [111, 823, 821, 867]<|/det|>6. I promise the job ____ (complete) before the deadline.
<|det|>text [111, 868, 882, 914]<|/det|>7. The surgery ____ (supervise) by the top doctors of India.
<|det|>page_number [848, 930, 895, 950]<|/det|>111
<PAGE><|det|>text [155, 42, 902, 87]<|/det|>8. As per our arrangement, he ____ (pay) once he finishes the job.
<|det|>text [155, 88, 738, 112]<|/det|>9. The treaty ____ (sign) as we speak.
<|det|>list [155, 42, 902, 112]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [105, 128, 969, 177]<|/det|>B. Underline the verbs in these sentences and state whether they are in the active or the passive voice.
<|det|>list [160, 180, 870, 598]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [167, 183, 429, 204]<|/det|>1. I shall write a note.
<|det|>text [166, 207, 457, 226]<|/det|>2. It was known to them.
<|det|>text [166, 230, 451, 250]<|/det|>3. Do not heat the food.
<|det|>text [166, 253, 471, 275]<|/det|>4. Do the work right now.
<|det|>text [166, 276, 454, 297]<|/det|>5. The jar contains milk.
<|det|>text [166, 298, 474, 320]<|/det|>6. They elected him chief.
<|det|>text [166, 322, 508, 344]<|/det|>7. He has given me his book.
<|det|>text [166, 345, 515, 366]<|/det|>8. He is known to the teacher.
<|det|>text [166, 368, 506, 390]<|/det|>9. One should help the poor.
<|det|>text [157, 392, 533, 414]<|/det|>10. She was reading a magazine.
<|det|>text [157, 415, 575, 437]<|/det|>11. My mother bought me a football.
<|det|>text [157, 438, 567, 458]<|/det|>12. Kiran cooked an excellent meal.
<|det|>text [157, 460, 599, 482]<|/det|>13. The match was not played by them.
<|det|>text [157, 483, 582, 504]<|/det|>14. Hari did not finish his homework.
<|det|>text [157, 506, 629, 528]<|/det|>15. The fine had to be paid by the players.
<|det|>text [157, 529, 657, 551]<|/det|>16. The breakfast was being prepared by her.
<|det|>text [157, 552, 631, 574]<|/det|>17. The children are playing in the garden.
<|det|>text [157, 575, 628, 597]<|/det|>18. You have forgotten the phone number.
<|det|>image [687, 182, 907, 513]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [104, 620, 164, 664]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [178, 614, 890, 661]<|/det|>Study this table to understand how interrogative sentences are changed into the passive voice.
<|det|>title [225, 670, 371, 691]<|/det|>Active voice
<|det|>title [626, 670, 780, 691]<|/det|>Passive voice
<|det|>text [117, 700, 350, 723]<|/det|>Did Ravi invite you?
<|det|>text [481, 700, 775, 723]<|/det|>Were you invited by Ravi?
<|det|>text [117, 730, 438, 753]<|/det|>Do the children like sweets?
<|det|>text [481, 730, 856, 753]<|/det|>Are sweets liked by the children?
<|det|>text [117, 761, 460, 784]<|/det|>Have you finished the project?
<|det|>text [481, 761, 936, 785]<|/det|>Has the project been finished by you?
<|det|>text [117, 792, 277, 815]<|/det|>Who did this?
<|det|>text [481, 792, 756, 815]<|/det|>By whom was this done?
<|det|>text [117, 822, 316, 844]<|/det|>Who hit the ball?
<|det|>text [481, 824, 776, 846]<|/det|>By whom was the ball hit?
<|det|>text [117, 854, 440, 902]<|/det|>When will they announce the date of the wedding?
<|det|>text [481, 854, 940, 902]<|/det|>When will the date of the wedding be announced? (They is not used.)
<|det|>page_number [129, 928, 177, 948]<|/det|>112
<PAGE><|det|>text [75, 35, 900, 127]<|/det|>The passive voice is used when the person or thing acted upon is to be made prominent. The passive voice is thus preferred when the active form would involve the use of a vague pronoun or noun (somebody, they, people, anybody) as subject. For example,
<|det|>list [80, 128, 877, 198]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [81, 128, 767, 151]<|/det|>- My purse has been stolen. (Someone has stolen my purse.)
<|det|>text [81, 152, 668, 174]<|/det|>- I was asked my name. (They asked me my name.)
<|det|>text [81, 175, 874, 198]<|/det|>- I have been invited to the party. (Someone has invited me to the party.)
<|det|>image [114, 211, 415, 386]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [527, 253, 586, 278]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [671, 248, 715, 281]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [521, 292, 837, 333]<|/det|>Do, does and did are not used in the passive voice.
<|det|>title [135, 407, 809, 430]<|/det|>Change these sentences from the active to the passive voice.
<|det|>list [149, 438, 555, 621]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 438, 348, 460]<|/det|>1. Help the poor.
<|det|>text [150, 461, 421, 484]<|/det|>2. Why did he call you?
<|det|>text [150, 485, 430, 505]<|/det|>3. I have selected a coat.
<|det|>text [150, 508, 451, 530]<|/det|>4. I expect you to help her.
<|det|>text [150, 531, 450, 552]<|/det|>5. He had bought a house.
<|det|>text [150, 554, 488, 576]<|/det|>5. Have you finished the play?
<|det|>text [150, 577, 465, 599]<|/det|>7. Do you teach geography?
<|det|>text [150, 600, 475, 622]<|/det|>8. He was painting a portrait.
<|det|>text [150, 623, 475, 645]<|/det|>9. He is observing us closely.
<|det|>text [139, 646, 530, 668]<|/det|>10. I kept him standing for an hour.
<|det|>text [139, 669, 519, 690]<|/det|>11. She does not fear punishment.
<|det|>text [139, 692, 551, 714]<|/det|>12. The painters are mixing the paint.
<|det|>text [139, 715, 533, 737]<|/det|>13. Someone has borrowed my car.
<|det|>text [139, 738, 557, 760]<|/det|>14. The men have planned a meeting.
<|det|>image [556, 458, 930, 700]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [75, 778, 136, 821]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [157, 783, 630, 806]<|/det|>We prefer to use the passive voice when—
<|det|>text [75, 830, 813, 877]<|/det|>◆ the doer is not known or when it is not intended to name him. For example,
<|det|>text [119, 879, 456, 902]<|/det|>• The player was carried out.
<|det|>page_number [848, 930, 892, 950]<|/det|>113

<PAGE><|det|>text [110, 45, 951, 94]<|/det|>we wish to focus on the person/thing acted upon rather than the doer or the agent. For example,
<|det|>text [145, 94, 597, 117]<|/det|>• The shop was destroyed by lightning.
<|det|>text [109, 131, 792, 178]<|/det|>◆ the subject in the active voice is implied from the context. For example,
<|det|>text [145, 179, 725, 202]<|/det|>• He was dropped from the team (by the selectors).
<|det|>title [130, 221, 192, 248]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [127, 259, 926, 325]<|/det|>Since the object of a verb in the active voice becomes the subject of the passive form, it means that only transitive verbs can be used in the passive, because an intransitive verb has no object.
<|det|>text [106, 345, 951, 390]<|/det|>See how sentences in the passive voice can be changed into active voice. The work will be finished by the painters in a week. (passive)
<|det|>text [145, 392, 706, 415]<|/det|>The painters will finish the work in a week. (active)
<|det|>text [108, 427, 517, 450]<|/det|>• What is wanted by you? (passive)
<|det|>text [144, 451, 456, 473]<|/det|>What do you want? (active)
<|det|>text [108, 484, 758, 507]<|/det|>• I have been asked to be present at the meeting. (passive)
<|det|>text [144, 508, 810, 530]<|/det|>Someone has asked me to be present at the meeting. (active)
