<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 287, 142]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [606, 44, 980, 83]<|/det|>Report Writing
<|det|>image [89, 144, 147, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 142, 980, 306]<|/det|>Writing a report for a newspaper or a magazine is very different from other genres of writing since it is largely factual. When covering an event, it is important to mention all the relevant and important facts without adding one's personal opinions. An article written for a magazine may be an opportunity to express one's opinions, but in a report, the idea is to be precise and to the point while holding the reader's attention through an interesting presentation and appropriate use of language.
<|det|>text [87, 318, 980, 526]<|/det|>Reports are always written in indirect speech, unless a person is being quoted as an eyewitness or an authority on the subject. The passive voice should be used and there should be no use of the personal pronoun, 'I'. If necessary, the phrase 'the reporter' or the 'the correspondent' may be used. Personal opinions may be given only if specifically asked for. Start a report directly with the topic; no elaborate introduction is required. The language used in a report is matter of fact and not ornamented with similes and metaphors. All the relevant points should be narrated in the proper sequence. An account by an eyewitness should be included.
<|det|>title [89, 532, 768, 556]<|/det|>Given below is an example of a report. Note the format.
<|det|>title [205, 575, 864, 600]<|/det|>Strike by Delhi Doctors Called Off (a concise heading)
<|det|>text [143, 606, 441, 652]<|/det|>Vinay K Jha (name of the reporter)
<|det|>text [142, 664, 507, 758]<|/det|>New Delhi (name of the place where the event occurred)
Wednesday, 24 November (day and date)
<|det|>text [140, 769, 514, 888]<|/det|>(Body of the report) The impending strike by the doctors in the Capital tomorrow has been called off after the successful talks between the Doctors'
<|det|>text [549, 606, 941, 886]<|/det|>Association of Delhi and the Minister of State for Health, L K Roy. At the end of the talks, which lasted for more than five hours, the minister agreed to look into the demands of the doctors, which included monitoring of hospital appointments and disbursement of salaries, among other issues. The DAD has both private and government hospital doctors as its members.
<|det|>page_number [105, 927, 160, 947]<|/det|>118
<PAGE><|det|>title [84, 38, 150, 65]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>list [111, 78, 870, 226]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 78, 586, 100]<|/det|>• The word limit of a report is usually 100–150 words.
<|det|>text [111, 101, 832, 161]<|/det|>- A report is normally marked out of twenty. The division is fifteen marks for the content and five marks for the format. The format includes the heading, name of reporter, date and the use of paragraphs.
<|det|>text [111, 163, 867, 226]<|/det|>- In a question on report writing, some guidelines will always be given. It is imperative to touch upon all the points and to maintain the sequence of events. Any digression from these rules may result in loss of marks for the format.
<|det|>title [55, 292, 823, 340]<|/det|>Write reports for these situations. You may base the reports on the guidelines provided in the brackets.
<|det|>text [59, 351, 876, 472]<|/det|>1. Mathematics is taught very differently in your school. Recently, there was an exhibition in your school to display the fun way of teaching Mathematics and its advantages. Write a report on the same in the capacity of head boy/girl of your school to be published in the student's section of a well-known newspaper.
<|det|>text [94, 472, 901, 542]<|/det|>(date...venue...name of the exhibition...intention of hosting such an exhibition...a brief description of the display...chief guest...who were the invitees...impact on the visitors...brief interviews of some teachers)
<|det|>text [53, 556, 875, 674]<|/det|>2. Your school has recently felicitated four personalities who have been awarded the Paramveer Chakra. Imagine yourself to be the reporter for a well-known newspaper who had been invited for this occasion. Prepare a report in about 150 words, to be published in the school section of the paper.
<|det|>text [90, 675, 898, 771]<|/det|>(date...venue...name of the programme...purpose...a brief description of the programme...chief guest...how the personalities were felicitated...impact on the audience...brief interviews of some students and teachers)
<|det|>text [50, 785, 900, 882]<|/det|>3. 'Images', the reading club of your school, has recently invited a famous author to address and motivate the members of the club. As the president of your club, prepare a report to be sent to the press. (date...venue...name of the author...a brief history of the author...
<|det|>page_number [834, 935, 879, 954]<|/det|>119
<PAGE><|det|>text [145, 37, 957, 158]<|/det|>occasion...organisers...description of the programme...highlights of the speech made by the author...presence of any other important member apart from the author (club advisors, students)...total number of students who attended the programme...their feedback...how the programme concluded)
<|det|>text [108, 171, 961, 266]<|/det|>4. You are a reporter with a leading newspaper, covering the local city news. You have been asked to cover a rally in the city being organised by a local MLA, preceding the municipal elections in the city. Write a report in not more than 150 words on the same.
<|det|>text [148, 268, 877, 339]<|/det|>(date...venue...turnout at the rally...the highlights of the MLA's speech...reaction of the crowd...any direct quotation that might be relevant)
<|det|>page_number [135, 952, 179, 970]<|/det|>120
<PAGE><|det|>title [73, 45, 636, 85]<|/det|>Subject-Verb Agreement
<|det|>header [715, 0, 992, 145]<|/det|>14
<|det|>image [77, 150, 141, 196]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [148, 148, 797, 196]<|/det|>You have learnt that a verb always agrees with its subject in number and in person. For example,
<|det|>list [75, 204, 474, 321]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [75, 204, 214, 226]<|/det|>He is ill.
<|det|>text [75, 228, 312, 249]<|/det|>• I am an architect.
<|det|>text [75, 251, 383, 273]<|/det|>• Elephants love bananas.
<|det|>text [75, 275, 465, 298]<|/det|>• My mother exercises every day.
<|det|>text [75, 298, 474, 321]<|/det|>• They were going to the museum.
<|det|>text [73, 335, 901, 359]<|/det|>Let us revise the rules that help us determine if the subject is singular or plural.
<|det|>list [73, 359, 801, 550]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [73, 359, 777, 404]<|/det|>◆ If two or more singular nouns or pronouns are joined by the conjunction and, we use a plural verb. For example,
<|det|>list [117, 405, 618, 450]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 405, 600, 426]<|/det|>- Mita and Rita are students of this school.
<|det|>text [117, 428, 618, 450]<|/det|>• The teacher and the student were present.
<|det|>text [73, 461, 890, 507]<|/det|>◆ When two nouns suggest the same idea or refer to the same person or thing, we use a singular verb. For example,
<|det|>list [117, 507, 871, 552]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 507, 871, 530]<|/det|>- The rise and fall of the Mughal Empire is the subject of his study.
<|det|>text [117, 531, 491, 552]<|/det|>- Slow and steady wins the race.
<|det|>text [73, 564, 827, 611]<|/det|>◆ If two subjects together express one idea, the verb may be in the singular. For example,
<|det|>text [116, 611, 878, 657]<|/det|>- The long and short of the matter is that she has decided to return to England.
<|det|>text [67, 668, 836, 715]<|/det|>◆ If the singular subjects are preceded by each or every, the verb is usually singular. For example,
<|det|>text [111, 715, 801, 739]<|/det|>- Every man, woman and child was present at the gathering.
<|det|>text [65, 749, 883, 797]<|/det|>◆ A collective noun takes a singular verb when the group is considered as a unit. For example,
<|det|>text [111, 798, 664, 821]<|/det|>• The committee has accepted your resignation.
<|det|>text [65, 832, 893, 879]<|/det|>◆ However, if we are referring to the members of the unit individually, a plural verb is used. For example,
<|det|>text [111, 880, 748, 902]<|/det|>• The committee members are divided in their opinion.
<|det|>page_number [838, 930, 884, 950]<|/det|>121
<PAGE><|det|>text [97, 18, 952, 69]<|/det|>- Some nouns are plural in form, but singular in meaning. Such nouns take a singular verb. For example,
<|det|>text [136, 70, 540, 93]<|/det|>• Physics is my favourite subject.
<|det|>text [136, 93, 411, 117]<|/det|>• The news was scary.
<|det|>list [136, 70, 540, 117]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [95, 125, 962, 174]<|/det|>- Some nouns are singular in form, but plural in meaning and thus take a plural verb. For example,
<|det|>text [135, 174, 588, 198]<|/det|>• The police have nabbed the culprit.
<|det|>text [95, 208, 935, 257]<|/det|>The words each, everyone, neither...nor, either...or take a singular verb. For example,
<|det|>text [131, 258, 457, 279]<|/det|>• Everyone was welcome.
<|det|>text [131, 281, 532, 305]<|/det|>• Neither he nor I was prepared.
<|det|>list [131, 258, 532, 305]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 315, 974, 386]<|/det|>- When two subjects joined by either...or and neither...nor take different persons, the verb agrees in person with the one nearest to it. For example,
<|det|>text [131, 387, 585, 412]<|/det|>• Either you or she is telling the truth.
<|det|>text [91, 422, 955, 493]<|/det|>- If the subjects joined by either...or and neither...nor are of different numbers, the verb agrees in number with the subject that is closest to it. For example,
<|det|>list [131, 494, 944, 540]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 494, 717, 517]<|/det|>• The boy or his brothers have made the mistake.
<|det|>text [131, 517, 944, 540]<|/det|>- Neither the team members nor the captain has reached the station.
<|det|>text [91, 551, 967, 645]<|/det|>- When two subjects are joined by as well as, with, along with, etc., the verb agrees with the first subject/noun, that is, if the first noun is in the singular, the verb must be singular, even if the second noun is plural. For example,
<|det|>text [131, 646, 708, 669]<|/det|>• Rita, along with her friends, has won the prize.
<|det|>text [91, 680, 871, 726]<|/det|>◆ A relative pronoun always agrees in number and person with its antecedent. For example,
<|det|>text [131, 727, 828, 749]<|/det|>- He is one of those men who know a lot about this matter.
<|det|>text [91, 761, 924, 807]<|/det|>◆ Many refers to number, and is plural. Much refers to amount, and is singular. For example,
<|det|>list [131, 808, 538, 852]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 808, 538, 830]<|/det|>• Many of the bananas were ripe.
<|det|>text [131, 830, 517, 851]<|/det|>- Much of the milk was wasted.
<|det|>page_number [127, 925, 161, 943]<|/det|>22
<PAGE><|det|>text [54, 35, 896, 84]<|/det|>- When a plural number applies to distances, weights, etc., it is taken as a whole, and therefore treated as singular. For example,
<|det|>list [98, 84, 635, 129]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 84, 635, 105]<|/det|>- Two kilos of meat costs five hundred rupees.
<|det|>text [98, 106, 631, 127]<|/det|>• Three hundred miles is not a short distance.
<|det|>text [55, 140, 854, 188]<|/det|>◆ Uncountable nouns such as clothing, news, stationery, cutlery are treated as singular and therefore take a singular verb. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 188, 435, 210]<|/det|>• The furniture is expensive.
<|det|>text [55, 221, 607, 245]<|/det|>◆ Both always takes a plural verb. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 245, 452, 268]<|/det|>• Both the girls were scolded.
<|det|>text [55, 279, 757, 328]<|/det|>- When the subject has the phrase one of the, followed by a plural noun, the verb is singular. The real subject is one.
<|det|>text [98, 329, 245, 351]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [98, 351, 777, 375]<|/det|>• One of the boys in the class was praised by the principal.
<|det|>text [55, 385, 867, 432]<|/det|>◆ When an of phrase follows a percentage, fraction or an amount, the verb agrees with the noun/pronoun closest to it. For example,
<|det|>list [98, 433, 690, 485]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 433, 537, 455]<|/det|>• One-third of the population is poor.
<|det|>text [98, 455, 689, 479]<|/det|>• One-third of the villagers have brick houses here.
<|det|>text [55, 488, 898, 536]<|/det|>◆ With quantifiers like all, some and few, the verb agrees with the noun/pronoun closest to it. For example,
<|det|>list [98, 537, 560, 582]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 537, 560, 558]<|/det|>• Some of the banana cake is in the tin.
<|det|>text [98, 559, 543, 581]<|/det|>• Some of the muffins were very hard.
<|det|>title [80, 604, 145, 628]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [456, 600, 507, 636]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [75, 642, 825, 682]<|/det|>Often a verb is made to agree with a noun near it instead of its proper subject. This is incorrect and should be avoided.
<|det|>list [98, 685, 625, 769]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 685, 625, 706]<|/det|>• The behaviour of the students were excellent. (incorrect)
<|det|>text [128, 707, 612, 726]<|/det|>The behaviour of the students was excellent. (correct)
<|det|>text [98, 727, 563, 747]<|/det|>• The cost of all these goods have risen. (incorrect)
<|det|>text [128, 748, 545, 768]<|/det|>The cost of all these goods has risen. (correct)
<|det|>image [31, 806, 97, 841]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [105, 813, 614, 836]<|/det|>Correct the errors in these sentences, if any.
<|det|>list [102, 842, 616, 915]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [102, 842, 531, 866]<|/det|>1. Every one of them were punished.
<|det|>text [102, 867, 569, 889]<|/det|>2. Much of the truth were not disclosed.
<|det|>text [102, 890, 615, 914]<|/det|>3. Neither Gita nor Hira were present today.
<|det|>page_number [838, 935, 884, 954]<|/det|>123
<PAGE><|det|>list [175, 43, 970, 343]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [175, 43, 775, 65]<|/det|>4. Diamonds, as well as gold, are found in the area.
<|det|>text [176, 67, 710, 87]<|/det|>5. The news of the series of accidents are true.
<|det|>text [176, 89, 710, 110]<|/det|>6. One of the girls in the team were sent back.
<|det|>text [176, 112, 702, 134]<|/det|>7. Two lakh rupees are a lot of money for me.
<|det|>text [176, 135, 740, 156]<|/det|>8. The boys or their teacher have left this behind.
<|det|>text [176, 158, 761, 179]<|/det|>9. Many of the children has passed the written test.
<|det|>text [167, 181, 764, 203]<|/det|>10. Bread and butter were part of his daily breakfast.
<|det|>text [167, 204, 787, 226]<|/det|>11. She is one of those persons who knows the matter.
<|det|>text [167, 228, 725, 249]<|/det|>12. The students, as well as the teacher, are here:
<|det|>text [167, 250, 907, 272]<|/det|>13. The orator and statesman have arrived for the inauguration.
<|det|>text [167, 273, 970, 295]<|/det|>14. A large number of people is waiting to meet the national leader.
<|det|>text [168, 296, 870, 343]<|/det|>15. The scholar and the poet is here for the prize distribution ceremony.
<|det|>title [167, 364, 857, 388]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks choosing from the words given in brackets.
<|det|>list [179, 394, 965, 929]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [180, 394, 714, 416]<|/det|>1. The mob ____ dispersed. (have/has)
<|det|>text [180, 417, 780, 438]<|/det|>2. Neither he nor she ____ to blame. (is/are)
<|det|>text [180, 440, 780, 461]<|/det|>3. What ____ the news on TV today? (is/are)
<|det|>text [180, 463, 842, 484]<|/det|>4. The people ____ aware of the situation. (is/are)
<|det|>text [180, 486, 884, 507]<|/det|>5. Neither of the girls ____ comfortable here. (is/are)
<|det|>text [180, 509, 858, 531]<|/det|>6. I, and not you, ____ going to the market. (is/am)
<|det|>text [180, 532, 915, 554]<|/det|>7. The jury ____ from different parts of the city. (is/are)
<|det|>text [180, 555, 965, 577]<|/det|>8. Mohan, with all his followers, ____ present. (was/were)
<|det|>text [180, 578, 961, 600]<|/det|>9. Each of these metals ____ found in our country. (is/are)
<|det|>text [170, 601, 898, 644]<|/det|>10. Mahima or her brothers ____ to blame for this mess.
(is/are)
<|det|>text [170, 646, 840, 691]<|/det|>11. One of the boys ____ punished for the offence.
(was/were)
<|det|>text [170, 693, 936, 738]<|/det|>12. He is one of the kindest teachers that ____ ever lived.
(has/have)
<|det|>text [170, 740, 898, 785]<|/det|>13. Perseverance, as well as patience, ____ our strength.
(is/are)
<|det|>text [170, 787, 969, 833]<|/det|>14. A series of lectures ____ been arranged by the university.
(has/have)
<|det|>text [170, 835, 925, 881]<|/det|>15. My uncle, as well as the members of his family, ____ settled in Dehradun. (has/have)
<|det|>text [170, 882, 951, 929]<|/det|>16. The notorious dacoit, with his followers, ____ escaped into the jungle. (have/has)
<|det|>page_number [137, 937, 183, 956]<|/det|>124
<PAGE><|det|>list [111, 32, 896, 196]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 32, 832, 78]<|/det|>17. One of the proposals he submitted ____ interesting. (seem/seems)
<|det|>text [111, 80, 895, 104]<|/det|>18. Three-fourths of the city ____ been destroyed. (have/has)
<|det|>text [111, 105, 890, 148]<|/det|>19. Shakespeare was one of the greatest dramatists that ____ lived. (have/has)
<|det|>text [110, 150, 871, 196]<|/det|>20. That night every one of the boys ____ down with fever. (was/were)
<|det|>title [111, 221, 896, 268]<|/det|>Read these passages. There is a verb incorrectly used in each line. Underline the incorrect verb and write the correct one in the blank.
<|det|>text [115, 274, 732, 480]<|/det|>1. There is many traditions associated with the Chinese New Year. On the fifteenth day, much young people go to the river or the sea and throws oranges or apples into the water. Young men hopes to get good wives and young girls hopes to get good husbands. Some peoples throw stones in the hope of getting a good house! Many fishermen put out their nets to catch the falling fruit to resell. Everyone are happy.
<|det|>text [114, 485, 761, 649]<|/det|>2. The dog have been considered man's best friend for so long that it are often forgotten that the family pet were once a wild beast. The origins of our relationship with dogs is lost in prehistory. Probably, primitive wild dogs followed our ancestors for food, much as the hyena today follow lion prides to pick up scraps left of their prey.
<|det|>text [151, 649, 751, 721]<|/det|>The wild dogs of yore was not the only beneficiary of this relationship; prehistoric humans was alerted to danger by the howls of these dogs.
<|det|>text [154, 721, 593, 765]<|/det|>Today, most dogs is domesticated, but they still howls to warn us of danger.
<|det|>image [808, 275, 900, 760]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [838, 930, 884, 950]<|/det|>125
<PAGE><|det|>page_number [100, 28, 168, 70]<|/det|>15
<|det|>header [657, 38, 979, 74]<|/det|>Prepositions
<|det|>image [92, 139, 153, 188]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 139, 953, 184]<|/det|>By now, you have understood that appropriate use of prepositions plays a vital role in speaking and writing English correctly.
<|det|>text [90, 185, 960, 230]<|/det|>Prepositions are usually very small words. Yet, they are very important as they establish a relationship between the different parts of a sentence.
<|det|>text [90, 244, 459, 266]<|/det|>Take a look at these sentences.
<|det|>list [91, 268, 890, 404]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [91, 268, 425, 289]<|/det|>- Put the box on the table.
<|det|>text [130, 291, 664, 313]<|/det|>(relation between two nouns—box and table)
<|det|>text [91, 314, 600, 335]<|/det|>• South Africa is famous for its diamonds.
<|det|>text [130, 336, 882, 359]<|/det|>(relation between the adjective famous and the noun diamonds)
<|det|>text [91, 360, 550, 382]<|/det|>• The king rules over a vast kingdom.
<|det|>text [130, 383, 807, 405]<|/det|>(relation between a verb and a noun—rules and kingdom)
<|det|>text [87, 406, 978, 473]<|/det|>These examples show that a preposition is a word that is placed before a noun (or a pronoun) to show the relation that the noun or the pronoun has with some other word in the sentence.
<|det|>text [87, 486, 938, 533]<|/det|>The noun or pronoun that is used with a preposition is called its object. A preposition can have more than one object.
<|det|>title [89, 564, 953, 611]<|/det|>A. Fill in the blanks with suitable prepositions. Put x where there is no need to put in a preposition.
<|det|>text [140, 617, 970, 687]<|/det|>1. Mr Tiwari has been ill ____ Monday. He plans to join work ____ the weekend. However, his assistant will deliver the documents ____ tomorrow.
<|det|>text [134, 688, 945, 784]<|/det|>2. The school will be closed ____ Monday as a mark of respect to the founder ____ of the institution. A memorial service will be held ____ the school auditorium ____ Sunday ____ 4 p.m.
<|det|>text [131, 785, 898, 875]<|/det|>3. Owing to circumstances ____ their control, the Smiths ran ____ debts.
4. I offered ____ help but they politely refused to accept my offer. However, ____ financial support, their reputed company might collapse.
<|det|>page_number [98, 927, 143, 948]<|/det|>126
<PAGE><|det|>text [122, 48, 891, 138]<|/det|>5. ____ the decline ____ the Roman Empire, Europe split ____ many small nationalities that were governed ____ incompetent ____ monarchs.
<|det|>text [122, 140, 842, 207]<|/det|>6. The residents ____ the locality informed the police that there was a young man ____ a gun ____ the Central Park.
<|det|>text [126, 209, 822, 298]<|/det|>7. We were informed ____ our neighbours that front ____ Monday the road ____ our house would remain blocked ____ a week.
<|det|>text [129, 301, 911, 368]<|/det|>8. ____ giving me her new coat, she gave me some shoes. I was touched ____ her kindness. She was so different ____ her sister.
<|det|>text [132, 369, 921, 462]<|/det|>9. The man was standing ____ a tree in order to escape ____ the heat. He was waiting ____ his friend to arrive. He looked ____ his watch repeatedly.
<|det|>text [80, 487, 920, 536]<|/det|>Let us take a look at the use of three frequently used prepositions in the English language—at, on, in.
<|det|>text [81, 547, 852, 596]<|/det|>All three can be used as prepositions of time as well as prepositions of place.
<|det|>title [82, 610, 363, 634]<|/det|>Prepositions of Time
<|det|>text [81, 637, 905, 704]<|/det|>The prepositions at, on and in can be used to indicate time. Read these sentences and notice how the highlighted words have been used in these sentences.
<|det|>list [85, 706, 922, 821]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 706, 792, 728]<|/det|>- Please meet me at 8 o'clock. (indicates exact point of time)
<|det|>text [85, 729, 922, 751]<|/det|>- This year, Durga Puja falls in September. (indicates a longer period of time)
<|det|>text [86, 752, 744, 773]<|/det|>- You can expect me in a few days. (means at the end of)
<|det|>text [86, 775, 731, 797]<|/det|>• The city museum is closed on Sundays. (indicates day)
<|det|>text [86, 798, 670, 820]<|/det|>- John's birthday is on 20 October. (indicates date)
<|det|>text [81, 833, 895, 857]<|/det|>Time can be indicated by a few other prepositions as well. For example,
<|det|>list [85, 858, 922, 902]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 858, 635, 878]<|/det|>- after his departure
  - within a month
<|det|>text [86, 880, 631, 902]<|/det|>- arrived before him
  - till the evening
<|det|>page_number [856, 922, 901, 940]<|/det|>127
<PAGE><|det|>list [98, 40, 650, 88]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 40, 650, 64]<|/det|>• by three o'clock • from 1 January
<|det|>text [99, 65, 621, 88]<|/det|>- since Sunday
- for ten years
<|det|>title [96, 103, 380, 126]<|/det|>Prepositions of Place
<|det|>text [94, 131, 938, 179]<|/det|>Look at these sentences where the prepositions at, on and in have been used as prepositions of place.
<|det|>list [96, 179, 905, 319]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [96, 179, 794, 201]<|/det|>- The cupboard is in my room. (to show an enclosed space)
<|det|>text [96, 201, 851, 224]<|/det|>- Mr Sinha lives in Canada. (to show position within land areas)
<|det|>text [96, 224, 903, 269]<|/det|>- I placed my bag on the table. (to show position on a horizontal or vertical surface)
<|det|>text [96, 270, 812, 293]<|/det|>• I lived at 56, MG Road. (to show specific place or position)
<|det|>text [96, 293, 867, 318]<|/det|>- Someone is at the doorstep. (to show specific place or position)
<|det|>text [93, 331, 951, 355]<|/det|>Place can be indicated by a few other prepositions as well. For example,
<|det|>list [96, 356, 777, 469]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [96, 356, 394, 377]<|/det|>• stood behind the door
<|det|>text [98, 379, 316, 399]<|/det|>- above the shelf
<|det|>text [98, 402, 334, 425]<|/det|>• stand by my side
<|det|>text [98, 426, 337, 449]<|/det|>- lies near the port
<|det|>text [98, 449, 382, 469]<|/det|>• came from New York
<|det|>list [489, 356, 777, 469]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [490, 356, 736, 378]<|/det|>- lay under the table
<|det|>text [490, 379, 756, 399]<|/det|>- within the classroom
<|det|>text [490, 401, 761, 422]<|/det|>- lies below the surface
<|det|>text [490, 424, 758, 447]<|/det|>- fell into a large crater
<|det|>text [490, 448, 704, 468]<|/det|>- stood beside me
<|det|>title [120, 515, 185, 540]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [504, 504, 556, 545]<|/det|>
<|det|>list [144, 553, 934, 638]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [144, 553, 934, 594]<|/det|>- To indicate cause, reason or purpose, we use prepositions such as of and for. Thus we say, died of cancer, for our good and took medicine for ever.
<|det|>text [144, 597, 865, 638]<|/det|>- To indicate source or origin we use prepositions such as from. Thus, we say from gratitude and comes from practice.
<|det|>image [69, 683, 114, 708]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [140, 683, 914, 731]<|/det|>Rewrite these sentences by inserting prepositions indicating time, place, reason or origin in the appropriate places.
<|det|>list [146, 736, 567, 897]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [146, 736, 478, 758]<|/det|>1. There is a park our house.
<|det|>text [146, 759, 512, 780]<|/det|>2. There is a tea-stall the beach.
<|det|>text [146, 782, 534, 805]<|/det|>3. Be careful. Do not fall the cliff.
<|det|>text [146, 806, 525, 829]<|/det|>4. Cortez came to Mexico Spain.
<|det|>text [146, 830, 560, 852]<|/det|>5. The captain led the army the city.
<|det|>text [146, 853, 556, 876]<|/det|>6. He worked tirelessly many years.
<|det|>text [146, 876, 567, 898]<|/det|>7. I asked the child to stand my side.
<|det|>image [727, 732, 960, 905]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [111, 927, 152, 946]<|/det|>128
<PAGE><|det|>list [139, 42, 888, 293]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 42, 586, 87]<|/det|>8. The plumber has been working early morning.
<|det|>text [140, 88, 599, 109]<|/det|>9. This is an excerpt a novel by Dickens.
<|det|>text [134, 110, 627, 131]<|/det|>10. Do not stand the door. I cannot see you.
<|det|>text [134, 133, 631, 154]<|/det|>11. The team members will meet the airport.
<|det|>text [134, 155, 543, 175]<|/det|>12. The bus reached Haldwani time.
<|det|>text [134, 177, 666, 223]<|/det|>13. I thought that this would be the good of the organisation.
<|det|>text [134, 224, 621, 268]<|/det|>14. I got to the airport time to catch the last flight to Mumbai.
<|det|>text [134, 269, 888, 293]<|/det|>15. Sam will not know what happened as he reached the incident.
<|det|>image [695, 72, 895, 216]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [72, 313, 582, 336]<|/det|>Prepositions of Movement or Direction
<|det|>text [73, 343, 331, 363]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [75, 366, 636, 435]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [75, 366, 636, 387]<|/det|>• My friends arrived and we went to the theatre.
<|det|>text [76, 389, 503, 409]<|/det|>• The train went through the tunnel.
<|det|>text [76, 412, 521, 432]<|/det|>• The swimmer swam across the river.
<|det|>text [74, 435, 627, 458]<|/det|>The highlighted prepositions indicate movement.
<|det|>text [72, 470, 925, 519]<|/det|>Movement or direction can be indicated by a few other prepositions as well. For example, For example,
<|det|>list [75, 521, 301, 587]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [75, 521, 295, 540]<|/det|>- down the road
<|det|>text [75, 544, 301, 566]<|/det|>• over the bridge
<|det|>text [75, 567, 291, 589]<|/det|>- off the ground
<|det|>list [405, 520, 597, 587]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [405, 520, 597, 539]<|/det|>- round the sun
<|det|>text [405, 542, 585, 564]<|/det|>- into the pool
<|det|>text [405, 566, 567, 587]<|/det|>- onto a ship
<|det|>image [65, 611, 127, 639]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [131, 610, 823, 658]<|/det|>Fill in the blanks with the correct prepositions of movement or direction.
<|det|>list [131, 665, 805, 890]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [134, 665, 621, 688]<|/det|>1. I have ____ go home now.
<|det|>text [133, 688, 619, 709]<|/det|>2. Let's walk ____ the beach.
<|det|>text [133, 711, 675, 733]<|/det|>3. Put the clothes ____ the closet.
<|det|>text [132, 735, 800, 758]<|/det|>4. You need to push the car ____ the garage.
<|det|>text [133, 759, 682, 781]<|/det|>5. The cat climbed ____ the fence.
<|det|>text [132, 782, 776, 805]<|/det|>6. The mole made a hole ____ the garden.
<|det|>text [132, 807, 668, 828]<|/det|>7. The earth moves ____ the sun.
<|det|>text [132, 829, 718, 875]<|/det|>8. The car went ____ the cliff and fell ____ the sea.
<|det|>page_number [863, 930, 910, 948]<|/det|>129
<PAGE><|det|>image [88, 42, 150, 90]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [162, 39, 960, 131]<|/det|>Several words are used both as prepositions and as adverbs. Note that when a word governs a noun or a pronoun, it is a preposition. It is an adverb if it modifies a verb, an adjective or another adverb. For example,
<|det|>list [85, 133, 655, 274]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 133, 590, 155]<|/det|>• The wheel of the car came off. (adverb)
<|det|>text [85, 156, 655, 178]<|/det|>- The convict jumped off the van. (preposition)
<|det|>text [86, 179, 507, 200]<|/det|>• The caravan moved on. (adverb)
<|det|>text [86, 202, 563, 224]<|/det|>• The cup is on the table. (preposition)
<|det|>text [86, 225, 530, 247]<|/det|>- Has the teacher come in? (adverb)
<|det|>text [85, 248, 802, 271]<|/det|>- The principal kept his diary in the cupboard. (preposition)
<|det|>title [85, 294, 919, 319]<|/det|>D. State whether the highlighted words are prepositions or adverbs.
<|det|>list [149, 325, 590, 530]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 325, 384, 348]<|/det|>1. Quick, jump in.
<|det|>text [150, 350, 436, 369]<|/det|>2. It is time to move on.
<|det|>text [150, 372, 458, 394]<|/det|>3. The jug is on the table.
<|det|>text [150, 395, 470, 415]<|/det|>4. You should come down.
<|det|>text [150, 418, 470, 438]<|/det|>5. I have heard this before.
<|det|>text [150, 440, 560, 462]<|/det|>6. The chocolates are in the fridge.
<|det|>text [150, 464, 509, 484]<|/det|>7. Children should run about.
<|det|>text [150, 487, 553, 508]<|/det|>8. The squirrels ran down the tree.
<|det|>text [150, 510, 585, 531]<|/det|>9. Ray reached the class before Sam.
<|det|>text [138, 532, 586, 554]<|/det|>10. The wall was finally broken down.
<|det|>text [138, 555, 631, 576]<|/det|>11. He starts for school before ten o'clock.
<|det|>text [138, 578, 663, 601]<|/det|>12. The tired footballers went in immediately.
<|det|>image [631, 338, 936, 518]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [78, 619, 135, 664]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 615, 970, 685]<|/det|>Certain verbs, nouns, adjectives and participles are always followed by particular prepositions. Look at these sentences with examples of some prepositions that are commonly used.
<|det|>list [81, 694, 585, 901]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [81, 694, 581, 717]<|/det|>- This region is inaccessible to foreigners.
<|det|>text [81, 718, 580, 740]<|/det|>- You should not be sensitive to criticism.
<|det|>text [81, 741, 485, 763]<|/det|>- I have a craving for chocolates.
<|det|>text [81, 765, 478, 787]<|/det|>- I must pay attention to details.
<|det|>text [81, 788, 478, 810]<|/det|>• The child is busy with his toys.
<|det|>text [81, 811, 509, 833]<|/det|>- He was found guilty of the crime.
<|det|>text [81, 834, 450, 856]<|/det|>- You must apologise to them.
<|det|>text [81, 857, 557, 878]<|/det|>- He has not recovered from his illness.
<|det|>text [81, 880, 505, 901]<|/det|>• I am convinced of his innocence.
<|det|>page_number [111, 929, 152, 946]<|/det|>130
<PAGE><|det|>text [120, 48, 895, 119]<|/det|>There are some prepositions in Box 1. Match them with the words in Box 2 to form suitable expressions. (You will have to use some prepositions more than once.)
<|det|>text [125, 125, 900, 155]<|/det|>Box 1 of for on to with from in into
<|det|>text [125, 173, 825, 228]<|/det|>Box 2 robbed relate account suffer agree jump
<|det|>text [125, 248, 895, 296]<|/det|>Now, use these expressions to fill in the blanks in these sentences. Make other changes necessary.
<|det|>list [130, 298, 923, 621]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 301, 867, 347]<|/det|>1. Ravi must ____ the huge expenditure incurred during the excursions.
<|det|>text [134, 352, 878, 375]<|/det|>2. I am sorry but I cannot ____ you on this matter.
<|det|>text [135, 380, 790, 402]<|/det|>3. You are mistaken. He does not ____ TB.
<|det|>text [135, 408, 807, 430]<|/det|>4. We have been ____ our ancestral wealth.
<|det|>text [135, 436, 714, 457]<|/det|>5. I cannot ____ this situation at all.
<|det|>text [135, 463, 714, 485]<|/det|>6. I have absolute ____ his abilities.
<|det|>text [135, 491, 704, 513]<|/det|>7. There shall be no ____ this rule.
<|det|>text [135, 520, 724, 542]<|/det|>8. Do not ____ conclusions so soon.
<|det|>text [134, 547, 851, 570]<|/det|>9. The man is ____ finer feelings and emotions.
<|det|>text [125, 574, 918, 621]<|/det|>10. The committee will ____ the matter and submit its report in a month.
<|det|>text [125, 648, 724, 672]<|/det|>Now, try to make sentences with these expressions.
<|det|>list [125, 675, 928, 873]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [135, 678, 312, 700]<|/det|>1. absent from
<|det|>text [135, 706, 294, 727]<|/det|>4. believe in
<|det|>text [135, 733, 312, 756]<|/det|>7. deprived of
<|det|>text [125, 761, 310, 783]<|/det|>10. indebted to
<|det|>text [125, 789, 301, 811]<|/det|>13. famous for
<|det|>text [125, 816, 325, 838]<|/det|>16. indifferent to
<|det|>text [125, 844, 325, 866]<|/det|>19. recover from
<|det|>text [431, 678, 646, 699]<|/det|>2. associated with
<|det|>text [431, 705, 611, 727]<|/det|>5. blessed with
<|det|>text [431, 733, 585, 756]<|/det|>8. despair of
<|det|>text [421, 761, 636, 784]<|/det|>11. equipped with
<|det|>text [421, 789, 636, 811]<|/det|>14. endowed with
<|det|>text [421, 816, 580, 838]<|/det|>17. limited to
<|det|>text [419, 844, 655, 868]<|/det|>20. sympathise with
<|det|>image [636, 336, 936, 519]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [863, 937, 904, 956]<|/det|>131
<PAGE><|det|>title [126, 54, 188, 79]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>list [123, 89, 916, 220]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [123, 89, 916, 154]<|/det|>- Prepositions that consist of one word are called simple prepositions. For example, beyond, out, outside, over, past, but, by, despite, down, to, toward, during, except, between, under, failing, following, for, from, in, inside, into, before, among.
<|det|>text [123, 155, 905, 220]<|/det|>- Prepositions that consist of more that two words are called compound or phrase prepositions. For example, according to, owing to, close to, as far as, by means of, as well as, in spite of, in place of, in front of.
<|det|>image [100, 280, 164, 330]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [97, 279, 957, 445]<|/det|>There is no rule to decide which preposition comes after which verb or adjective. Particular words are followed by particular prepositions, although there are several prepositions that have the same meaning. Do remember, the list given in the previous exercise is merely a list of examples. There are many more combinations with prepositions and it is always important to use the correct prepositions in such expressions. Look at these examples.
<|det|>list [99, 450, 531, 557]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 450, 531, 473]<|/det|>• The captain died of fever. (correct)
<|det|>text [134, 478, 486, 500]<|/det|>He died from fever. (incorrect)
<|det|>text [100, 506, 497, 528]<|/det|>• He was sick of fever. (incorrect)
<|det|>text [134, 534, 502, 556]<|/det|>He was sick with fever. (correct)
<|det|>image [100, 575, 158, 616]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [125, 584, 188, 608]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>list [131, 619, 927, 727]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 620, 927, 641]<|/det|>When two or more words require the same preposition, only one preposition is retained.
<|det|>text [184, 642, 625, 661]<|/det|>• Mahatma Gandhi was loved and respected by all.
<|det|>text [132, 664, 845, 685]<|/det|>◆ Prepositions are not used before certain words and expressions. Thus, we say—
<|det|>text [184, 686, 807, 706]<|/det|>• We go to school every day. ('We go to school on every day' is incorrect.)
<|det|>text [184, 707, 725, 727]<|/det|>- I will leave next month. ('I will leave in next month' is incorrect.)
<|det|>text [98, 766, 885, 790]<|/det|>The table lists some common mistakes that should be guarded against.
<|det|>title [252, 800, 367, 820]<|/det|>Erroneous
<|det|>title [655, 801, 771, 820]<|/det|>Corrected
<|det|>text [110, 831, 414, 854]<|/det|>He ordered for my transfer.
<|det|>text [520, 832, 777, 854]<|/det|>He ordered my transfer.
<|det|>text [111, 863, 468, 907]<|/det|>The police carefully investigated into the case.
<|det|>text [519, 863, 892, 907]<|/det|>The police carefully investigated the case.
<|det|>page_number [124, 937, 171, 956]<|/det|>132
<PAGE><|det|>title [250, 46, 365, 65]<|/det|>Erroneous
<|det|>title [655, 48, 768, 68]<|/det|>Corrected
<|det|>text [113, 77, 452, 122]<|/det|>That thought pervades through Rita's mind.
<|det|>text [516, 78, 895, 100]<|/det|>That thought pervades Rita's mind.
<|det|>text [113, 131, 463, 176]<|/det|>The team comprised of fourteen members.
<|det|>text [516, 132, 834, 176]<|/det|>The team comprised fourteen members.
<|det|>text [113, 186, 438, 233]<|/det|>Sam should not have violated against the rules.
<|det|>text [516, 187, 884, 230]<|/det|>Sam should not have violated the rules.
<|det|>text [113, 242, 377, 265]<|/det|>He was angry upon me.
<|det|>text [516, 242, 770, 265]<|/det|>He was angry with me.
<|det|>text [113, 273, 456, 295]<|/det|>He has an aptitude in business.
<|det|>text [516, 273, 871, 295]<|/det|>He has an aptitude for business.
<|det|>text [113, 304, 424, 328]<|/det|>The pupil insisted in doing it.
<|det|>text [516, 304, 839, 328]<|/det|>The pupil insisted on doing it.
<|det|>text [113, 336, 468, 380]<|/det|>The leopards are confined in the forests of Northern India.
<|det|>text [516, 336, 875, 380]<|/det|>The leopards are confined to the forests of Northern India.
<|det|>text [113, 390, 478, 413]<|/det|>There is no exception of this rule.
<|det|>text [516, 390, 886, 413]<|/det|>There is no exception to this rule.
<|det|>text [113, 423, 438, 466]<|/det|>Byron was a contemporary to Keats.
<|det|>text [516, 423, 844, 465]<|/det|>Byron was a contemporary of Keats.
<|det|>text [113, 476, 497, 500]<|/det|>There is no alternative for this plan.
<|det|>text [516, 476, 900, 498]<|/det|>There is no alternative to this plan.
<|det|>text [101, 533, 907, 581]<|/det|>Remember that the best way to master the correct usage of prepositions is by ample reading.
<|det|>title [100, 609, 903, 656]<|/det|>3. State whether the prepositions have been correctly used in these sentences. If not, rewrite the corrected sentences in your notebook.

<|det|>title [58, 108, 986, 160]<|/det|>H. Rewrite this paragraph after inserting prepositions at the appropriate places.
<|det|>text [111, 168, 968, 414]<|/det|>The arrested man was brought the ruler who sat a throne made gold. The man, a sculptor, stared the king. The courtiers waited expectantly the king's verdict. The young king was a believer the teachings Buddha. He wanted to free the man. But then no one was the law. Meanwhile, thousands had gathered the closed gates. They had been waiting the morning in anticipation a favourable verdict. The chief minister rose his seat. He asked the king whether he was ready to pronounce judgement. The silent king looked thoughtfully the snow peaks the distance. He whole-heartedly sympathised the sculptor but according the laws the land he was guilty. The king was a dilemma.
<|det|>page_number [76, 967, 123, 988]<|/det|>134
<PAGE><|det|>title [72, 50, 651, 85]<|/det|>An Unusual Examination
<|det|>header [732, 0, 999, 142]<|/det|>The image is too blurry to recognize any text content.
<|det|>text [71, 154, 898, 248]<|/det|>To prove oneself in life, one has to go through many trials. The author of this extract was born a slave but went on to become a prominent American educator, author and political leader. Read about an unusual trial that the author went through.
<|det|>text [76, 262, 907, 518]<|/det|>As soon as possible, after reaching the grounds of the Hampton Institute, I presented myself before the head teacher for assignment to a class. Having been so long without proper food, a bath and change of clothings, I did not, of course, make a very favourable impression upon her. I could see at once that there were doubts in her mind about the wisdom of admitting me as a student. For some time, she did not refuse to admit me, neither did she decide in my favour. I continued to linger about her and to impress her in all the ways I could with my worthiness. In the meantime, I saw her admitting other students, and that added greatly to my discomfort. I felt, deep down in my heart, that I could do as well as they, if I could only get a chance to show what was in me.
<|det|>text [78, 529, 923, 577]<|/det|>After some hours had passed, the head teacher said to me, 'The adjoining recitation room needs sweeping. Take the broom and sweep it.'
<|det|>text [78, 590, 895, 638]<|/det|>It occurred to me at once that here was my chance. Never did I receive an order with more delight.
<|det|>text [70, 650, 907, 858]<|/det|>I swept the recitation room three times. Then, I got a dusting cloth and I dusted it four times. All the woodwork around the walls, every bench, table and desk, I went over four times with my dusting cloth. Besides, every piece of furniture had been moved and every closet and corner in the room had been thoroughly cleaned. I had the feeling that in a large measure, my future depended upon the impression I made upon the teacher in the cleaning of that room. When I was through, I reported to the head teacher. She was a 'Yankee' woman who knew just where to look for dirt. She went into the room and inspected the floor and
<|det|>page_footnote [78, 892, 491, 910]<|/det|>* a native or inhabitant of a northern US state
<|det|>page_number [851, 927, 898, 946]<|/det|>135
<PAGE><|det|>text [73, 40, 957, 158]<|/det|>closets; then she took her handkerchief and rubbed it on the woodwork about the walls, and over the table and benches. When she was unable to find one bit of dirt on the floor, or a particle of dust on any of the furniture, she quietly remarked, 'I guess you will do to enter this institution.'
<|det|>text [73, 177, 957, 248]<|/det|>I was one of the happiest souls on earth. The sweeping of that room was my college examination. I have passed several examinations since then, but I have always felt that this was the best one I ever passed.
<|det|>text [684, 264, 977, 289]<|/det|>(Booker T Washington)
<|det|>title [73, 310, 422, 336]<|/det|>A Answer these questions.
<|det|>list [128, 342, 931, 527]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [129, 342, 918, 386]<|/det|>1. What impression did Booker T Washington make on the head teacher when he first met her? What led to such an impression?
<|det|>text [128, 388, 862, 433]<|/det|>2. Something added to Washington's discomfort while he was waiting for admission. What was it?
<|det|>text [128, 435, 862, 479]<|/det|>3. What is the feeling that Washington had while cleaning the recitation room?
<|det|>text [127, 481, 895, 503]<|/det|>4. Which two adjectives would you use to describe Washington?
<|det|>text [128, 505, 930, 527]<|/det|>5. In what way was Washington one of the happiest souls on earth?
<|det|>title [73, 550, 871, 620]<|/det|>B. In not more than 60 words, describe the best examination ever passed by Washington. Give a heading for your summary. Give a reason to justify the choice of the heading.
<|det|>title [73, 646, 926, 692]<|/det|>C. Give the meanings of these words and phrases in the context of the story.
<|det|>list [128, 699, 385, 789]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [129, 699, 292, 720]<|/det|>1. favourable
<|det|>text [129, 723, 295, 743]<|/det|>2. worthiness
<|det|>text [129, 746, 384, 767]<|/det|>3. in a large measure
<|det|>text [129, 769, 280, 788]<|/det|>4. remarked
<|det|>page_number [98, 930, 150, 947]<|/det|>136
<PAGE><|det|>title [53, 49, 372, 88]<|/det|>Conjunctions
<|det|>header [842, 38, 915, 86]<|/det|>16
<|det|>image [55, 155, 118, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 154, 867, 224]<|/det|>You have learnt that a conjunction is a word that is used to join words, groups of words or sentences. There are three types of conjunctions. Let us learn about them.
<|det|>title [54, 245, 421, 270]<|/det|>Coordinating Conjunctions
<|det|>text [53, 285, 911, 333]<|/det|>A conjunction used to join two independent statements or two statements of equal importance is called a coordinating conjunction.
<|det|>text [54, 359, 918, 429]<|/det|>A coordinating conjunction usually comes between the words, phrases or clauses they join. The most commonly used coordinating conjunctions are for, and, nor, but, or, yet, and so.
<|det|>text [57, 444, 676, 466]<|/det|>Coordinating conjunctions perform various functions.
<|det|>text [58, 468, 506, 490]<|/det|>◆ They add one statement to another.
<|det|>text [105, 491, 260, 513]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [105, 513, 576, 535]<|/det|>- I went to the market and bought a cycle.
<|det|>text [105, 535, 406, 556]<|/det|>• She likes tea and coffee.
<|det|>text [58, 569, 620, 613]<|/det|>◆ They add statements expressing opposition or contrast. For example,
<|det|>text [105, 614, 590, 655]<|/det|>- I went to the market, but the shops were closed.
<|det|>text [105, 658, 435, 681]<|/det|>- I was tired, yet I stayed up.
<|det|>text [58, 691, 567, 737]<|/det|>◆ They add statements expressing a choice between two alternatives. For example,
<|det|>text [105, 737, 488, 758]<|/det|>- You must rest or you will fall ill.
<|det|>text [105, 758, 615, 804]<|/det|>- My dog doesn't like meat, nor does he like vegetables.
<|det|>text [58, 813, 772, 836]<|/det|>◆ They add statements expressing an inference. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 837, 580, 858]<|/det|>- He will do well in life, for he is sincere.
<|det|>text [98, 858, 661, 880]<|/det|>- Her father has fallen ill, so she has taken leave.
<|det|>title [667, 479, 730, 504]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [756, 472, 812, 506]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [660, 518, 848, 616]<|/det|>When a coordinating conjunction joins two independent clauses, we usually put a comma before the conjunction.
<|det|>title [667, 655, 728, 680]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [756, 645, 812, 680]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [660, 692, 895, 789]<|/det|>When we use no or to join two independent clauses, the subject and verb order in the second clause is reversed.
<|det|>page_number [848, 925, 895, 943]<|/det|>137
<PAGE><|det|>title [90, 42, 885, 67]<|/det|>A. Fill in the blanks with appropriate coordinating conjunctions.
<|det|>list [160, 72, 926, 551]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [161, 72, 735, 95]<|/det|>1. I am ill, ____ I have to go to work.
<|det|>text [160, 95, 785, 118]<|/det|>2. He studied hard ____ got good grades.
<|det|>text [160, 118, 767, 141]<|/det|>3. Jaya helps me, ____ she cares for me.
<|det|>text [160, 141, 813, 163]<|/det|>4. It was raining, ____ I put on my raincoat.
<|det|>text [160, 163, 807, 185]<|/det|>5. Shiela is talented, ____ she is successful.
<|det|>text [160, 185, 874, 209]<|/det|>6. I was angry, ____ I did not reveal my feelings.
<|det|>text [160, 209, 898, 231]<|/det|>7. Jaideep was feeling cold, ____ he put on a coat.
<|det|>text [160, 231, 921, 274]<|/det|>8. Jayant doesn't like studies, ____ does he like to do work.
<|det|>text [159, 276, 895, 323]<|/det|>9. Rajesh needed some money, ____ he took up a part-time job.
<|det|>text [150, 323, 895, 367]<|/det|>10. Shireen ____ her sister have been invited to the conference.
<|det|>text [150, 369, 956, 414]<|/det|>11. I think the programme will be a huge success, ____ you have all worked very hard.
<|det|>text [150, 414, 956, 461]<|/det|>12. Kamal doesn't like dogs, ____ he loves playing with my pet.
<|det|>text [150, 461, 970, 505]<|/det|>13. Hurry up and close the door, ____ the dust will blow into the house.
<|det|>text [149, 506, 921, 551]<|/det|>14. Mahima drove to office, ____ she did not want to be late.
<|det|>title [87, 575, 755, 599]<|/det|>B. Join these sentences with coordinating conjunctions.
<|det|>list [150, 605, 944, 884]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 605, 565, 627]<|/det|>1. I was exhausted. I had to go out.
<|det|>text [160, 628, 650, 649]<|/det|>2. She was unwell. She went to the doctor.
<|det|>text [160, 651, 621, 673]<|/det|>3. I will go with you. I have a condition.
<|det|>text [160, 675, 645, 696]<|/det|>4. He is not a scholar. He is a good writer.
<|det|>text [160, 698, 697, 721]<|/det|>5. The hotel was not good. The food was good.
<|det|>text [160, 722, 714, 744]<|/det|>6. He gave me a dress. He gave me a chocolate.
<|det|>text [160, 745, 780, 766]<|/det|>7. The concert was cancelled. We went to the theatre.
<|det|>text [160, 768, 865, 790]<|/det|>8. He will succeed in his mission. He is a committed worker.
<|det|>text [160, 791, 807, 814]<|/det|>9. He is an architect. He is interested in creative writing.
<|det|>text [144, 815, 848, 837]<|/det|>10. He was absent for a long time. He did well in the exams.
<|det|>text [144, 838, 942, 861]<|/det|>11. Syed gave me a rare plant. Syed gave me a book on rare plants.
<|det|>text [144, 861, 923, 883]<|/det|>12. Lathika worked hard. She did not succeed in the examination.
<|det|>page_number [105, 928, 152, 946]<|/det|>138
<PAGE><|det|>text [101, 31, 870, 77]<|/det|>13. The students were enthusiastic. The students were eager to take charge of the work.
<|det|>text [102, 79, 888, 124]<|/det|>14. The chairman will not come to the meeting. He will not come to the annual function.
<|det|>list [101, 31, 888, 124]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [50, 145, 423, 171]<|/det|>Subordinating Conjunctions
<|det|>text [50, 185, 886, 211]<|/det|>A subordinating conjunction joins a subordinate clause to a main clause.
<|det|>image [55, 245, 118, 290]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 243, 830, 289]<|/det|>Subordinating conjunctions show relationships between two clauses, so either the main clause or the
<|det|>text [58, 290, 607, 360]<|/det|>subordinate clause can come first. For example, Although we were tired, we continued our journey.
<|det|>text [62, 362, 605, 405]<|/det|>• We continued our journey although we were tired.
<|det|>text [58, 424, 586, 517]<|/det|>The most commonly used subordinating conjunctions are after, although, as, because, before, how, if, once, since, that, than, though, till, until, when, where, whether and while.
<|det|>title [650, 290, 715, 316]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [745, 284, 794, 320]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [644, 330, 879, 472]<|/det|>The subordinating conjunction always comes at the beginning of a subordinate clause. When the subordinate clause comes first, we put a comma after the clause.
<|det|>text [58, 532, 667, 556]<|/det|>Subordinating conjunctions are of the following types.
<|det|>text [58, 556, 777, 601]<|/det|>◆ Subordinating conjunctions of time indicate when an action happened. For example,
<|det|>list [108, 602, 620, 647]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 602, 611, 625]<|/det|>• I left for the school when the rain stopped.
<|det|>text [108, 626, 620, 647]<|/det|>• Seema will leave before the vacation starts.
<|det|>text [55, 659, 772, 706]<|/det|>◆ Subordinating conjunctions of place tell us where an action happened. For example,
<|det|>list [108, 707, 618, 753]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 707, 618, 729]<|/det|>- You can place the trunk wherever you like.
<|det|>text [108, 730, 558, 752]<|/det|>• The students can stay where they are.
<|det|>text [54, 763, 875, 810]<|/det|>◆ Subordinating conjunctions of reason introduce the cause or explanation for an action expressed in the main clause. For example,
<|det|>list [102, 811, 874, 880]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [102, 811, 765, 833]<|/det|>- I cannot dismiss him because he is my oldest employee.
<|det|>text [102, 834, 874, 880]<|/det|>- We were forgiven by our parents since we promised not to repeat our mistake.
<|det|>page_number [830, 932, 876, 950]<|/det|>139
<PAGE><|det|>text [117, 35, 968, 84]<|/det|>◆ Subordinating conjunctions of result tell us that one idea is the resulting or consequence of another. For example,
<|det|>text [158, 86, 775, 109]<|/det|>- The bus was so crowded that we could not get off.
<|det|>text [159, 111, 684, 134]<|/det|>- Such was his condition, that we lost hope.
<|det|>list [158, 86, 775, 134]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [119, 145, 925, 193]<|/det|>◆ Subordinating conjunctions of purpose introduce the objective or purpose of an action expressed in the main clause. For example,
<|det|>text [158, 194, 970, 239]<|/det|>- She is working day and night so that she may complete the project on schedule.
<|det|>text [159, 242, 714, 266]<|/det|>• We work that we may lead comfortable lives.
<|det|>list [158, 194, 970, 266]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [119, 276, 938, 349]<|/det|>◆ Subordinating conjunctions of condition like if, in the event that, in case, unless, even if, unless and whether describe the conditions under which something may or may not happen. For example,
<|det|>text [160, 350, 722, 373]<|/det|>- You will lose your job if you are late every day.
<|det|>text [160, 374, 757, 396]<|/det|>• You will not get any tickets unless you reach early.
<|det|>list [160, 350, 757, 396]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 405, 942, 476]<|/det|>◆ Subordinating conjunctions of contrast or concession like although, even though and though introduce conditions or events that are different from what is expected. For example,
<|det|>text [160, 476, 871, 498]<|/det|>• She has not given up though she knows the obstacles ahead.
<|det|>text [160, 499, 842, 521]<|/det|>• Although I arrived on time, I found no one at the bus stop.
<|det|>list [160, 476, 871, 521]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [119, 531, 942, 578]<|/det|>◆ Subordinating conjunctions of comparison like as...as and than help to compare two persons or things. For example,
<|det|>text [160, 579, 403, 599]<|/det|>• He is as quiet as I.
<|det|>text [160, 600, 574, 621]<|/det|>• Karan is cleverer than her brother.
<|det|>list [160, 579, 574, 621]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [119, 632, 895, 678]<|/det|>◆ Subordinating conjunctions of manner like as, as if and as though indicate how an action takes place. For example,
<|det|>text [160, 679, 628, 700]<|/det|>• She behaved as if she knew everything.
<|det|>text [160, 700, 680, 722]<|/det|>• The people spoke as they had been advised.
<|det|>list [160, 679, 680, 722]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [87, 740, 160, 781]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [165, 744, 773, 768]<|/det|>Join these sentences using subordinating conjunctions.
<|det|>list [167, 774, 563, 906]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [167, 774, 460, 795]<|/det|>1. You say so. I believe it.
<|det|>text [166, 796, 460, 817]<|/det|>2. Hurry. You will be late.
<|det|>text [166, 818, 517, 838]<|/det|>3. You wish it. It shall be done.
<|det|>text [166, 840, 557, 861]<|/det|>4. He finished first. He began late.
<|det|>text [166, 862, 563, 884]<|/det|>5. I thought so. I was younger then.
<|det|>text [166, 885, 543, 907]<|/det|>6. You are tall. My father is taller.
<|det|>page_number [140, 928, 183, 946]<|/det|>140
<PAGE><|det|>list [100, 28, 642, 265]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 28, 642, 54]<|/det|>7. He is leaving. We are giving him a farewell.
<|det|>text [102, 54, 597, 78]<|/det|>8. You eat too much. You will gain weight.
<|det|>text [102, 78, 558, 100]<|/det|>9. Your brother is in London. I know it.
<|det|>text [96, 101, 611, 124]<|/det|>10. He was not there. I spoke to his assistant.
<|det|>text [96, 125, 599, 148]<|/det|>11. He will not pay. He must be compelled.
<|det|>text [96, 148, 620, 171]<|/det|>12. He ran to the station. He missed the train.
<|det|>text [96, 171, 599, 195]<|/det|>13. The bird flew away. The cage was open.
<|det|>text [96, 195, 643, 218]<|/det|>14. The musicians played. The children danced.
<|det|>text [96, 218, 592, 264]<|/det|>15. He apologised to the principal. He was sorry for his behaviour.
<|det|>image [709, 35, 917, 116]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [654, 140, 912, 270]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [45, 279, 102, 323]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [120, 288, 445, 312]<|/det|>Correlative Conjunctions
<|det|>text [45, 331, 867, 355]<|/det|>Correlative conjunctions are conjunctions that are always used in pairs.
<|det|>text [45, 368, 388, 392]<|/det|>Let us look at some examples.
<|det|>list [48, 395, 865, 604]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [48, 395, 443, 418]<|/det|>• As the morning is, so is the day.
<|det|>text [48, 419, 576, 441]<|/det|>• Neither he nor I can accompany you today.
<|det|>text [48, 442, 645, 464]<|/det|>• He spoke so fast that I could not understand him.
<|det|>text [48, 465, 727, 488]<|/det|>- Ronnie will either meet you today or call you tomorrow.
<|det|>text [48, 488, 787, 511]<|/det|>- The student was both applauded and rewarded by the school.
<|det|>text [48, 512, 664, 535]<|/det|>- Such was his motivation that he refused to give up.
<|det|>text [48, 535, 865, 557]<|/det|>- No sooner had I entered the station than I heard the announcement.
<|det|>text [48, 558, 781, 604]<|/det|>- The flood waters not only damaged the earthen dam but also contaminated the drinking water.
<|det|>image [0, 624, 97, 661]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [111, 627, 776, 650]<|/det|>Correct these sentences. Focus on the use of conjunctions.
<|det|>list [119, 656, 865, 913]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 656, 521, 679]<|/det|>1. If I study hard, I will not do well.
<|det|>text [120, 680, 532, 703]<|/det|>2. Take a torch, yet the night is dark.
<|det|>text [120, 704, 589, 726]<|/det|>3. Please meet me lest you have the time.
<|det|>text [120, 727, 593, 749]<|/det|>4. He ran fast, so he missed the first class.
<|det|>text [120, 750, 645, 773]<|/det|>5. He will not come as it rains in the morning.
<|det|>text [120, 774, 678, 797]<|/det|>6. The flat is small and it is surprisingly spacious.
<|det|>text [120, 798, 696, 819]<|/det|>7. I roasted a chicken when he studied for his test.
<|det|>text [120, 821, 726, 844]<|/det|>8. You will not get the recognition since you deserve.
<|det|>text [119, 845, 829, 868]<|/det|>9. You must be cautious about what you say as you meet him.
<|det|>text [108, 868, 862, 913]<|/det|>10. Govind wanted to buy his own house still he had always lived with his relatives.
<|det|>page_number [834, 933, 879, 951]<|/det|>141
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 268, 146]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>title [459, 48, 977, 86]<|/det|>Offering Condolences
<|det|>image [100, 157, 164, 202]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 155, 974, 203]<|/det|>This is a conversation between two boys. One of them has recently lost his grandmother who was very close to him.
<|det|>text [100, 214, 963, 305]<|/det|>Nikhil: Hello, Rohan! What's happened? Why do you look so pale? You haven't been coming for a whole week, and are you aware that you've missed two very important exams?
<|det|>text [102, 306, 538, 370]<|/det|>Rohan: I know, I just couldn't make it. We've had a terrible mishap at home. I lost my grandma. It's so hard to believe. I still think it is an unpleasant dream.
<|det|>text [102, 371, 545, 572]<|/det|>Nikhil: Oh, my God! What are you saying? I just met her the other day when I went to return your notes. She didn't seem ill at all. How did it happen?
Rohan: We too, are not very sure. She died in her sleep. When
<|det|>image [552, 325, 955, 598]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [202, 617, 938, 711]<|/det|>Mother went to her room that morning, she found her still in bed. Mother was a little surprised as my grandma would be the first to rise. It was then, I suppose, that my sister heard her scream and ran to the room to find everything was over.
<|det|>text [98, 711, 506, 733]<|/det|>Nikhil: Oh, but where were you?
<|det|>text [98, 734, 970, 870]<|/det|>Rohan: I had gone to the terrace like every other day to pluck flowers for her puja. Although I had heard the scream, I couldn't fathom a thing. It was only after I came down that I realised something was terribly wrong! I went up to Grandma's room, and thought she was sleeping peacefully. I didn't realise until much later that it was all over.
<|det|>text [94, 873, 958, 921]<|/det|>Nikhil: I'm so sorry! I know it must be so hard to bear, especially since you were so close to her.
<|det|>page_number [120, 924, 165, 943]<|/det|>142
<PAGE><|det|>text [44, 48, 900, 117]<|/det|>Rohan: My mother is totally devastated. She would always run to her for anything and everything. For me, she was always there like a pillar of strength whenever I was scared or confused.
<|det|>text [44, 118, 862, 187]<|/det|>Nikhil: I know...Please don't hesitate to ask for anything. You can call up any time, I'll be there. Please give my condolences to your family.
<|det|>text [44, 187, 436, 211]<|/det|>Rohan: Thank you. I surely will.
<|det|>title [41, 235, 592, 258]<|/det|>Now, get into pairs and enact these situations.
<|det|>text [41, 262, 867, 308]<|/det|>1. Your friend's pet dog has died. Offer him/her your condolences and consolation for his/her loss.
<|det|>text [41, 309, 893, 356]<|/det|>2. Your friend's mother passed away after a serious illness. Offer him/her your condolences and consolation for his/her bereavement.
<|det|>page_number [842, 919, 887, 938]<|/det|>143
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 268, 142]<|/det|>17
<|det|>header [655, 45, 972, 82]<|/det|>Interjections
<|det|>image [94, 151, 160, 198]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 149, 956, 220]<|/det|>Interjections are short utterances that are spoken when a person is very surprised, happy or upset. Interjections are not always whole sentences. Sometimes they are more like a noise than a word.
<|det|>text [96, 222, 255, 243]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [99, 245, 375, 336]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 245, 310, 268]<|/det|>- Ugh! (disgust)
<|det|>text [100, 268, 375, 289]<|/det|>• Wow! (amazement)
<|det|>text [100, 290, 304, 312]<|/det|>- Phew! (relief)
<|det|>text [100, 313, 323, 336]<|/det|>• Huh! (surprise)
<|det|>text [96, 357, 962, 403]<|/det|>A. Fill in the blanks with suitable interjections selected from the words in the brackets.
<|det|>list [160, 406, 970, 688]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [167, 409, 790, 432]<|/det|>1. ____! We won the match. (Hurrah/Ugh)
<|det|>text [166, 433, 968, 455]<|/det|>2. ____! There is a monkey in my bedroom. (Look/Hurrah)
<|det|>text [166, 456, 857, 500]<|/det|>3. ____! Are you calling from the doctor's office? (Wow/Hello)
<|det|>text [166, 502, 770, 547]<|/det|>4. ____! You won the poetry competition.
(Dear me/Bravo)
<|det|>text [166, 548, 740, 571]<|/det|>5. ____! The door is locked. (Oh/Huh)
<|det|>text [166, 571, 912, 593]<|/det|>6. ____! Your mother is making biriyani. (Help/Wow)
<|det|>text [166, 594, 785, 616]<|/det|>7. ____! Silence in the library. (Alas/Hush)
<|det|>text [166, 617, 931, 639]<|/det|>8. ____! You look beautiful in that dress. (Wow/Bravo)
<|det|>text [166, 640, 960, 663]<|/det|>9. ____! The horrible movie is finally over. (Bravo/Phew)
<|det|>text [150, 664, 965, 686]<|/det|>10. ____! That is a nasty cut on your knee. (Dear me/Bravo)
<|det|>text [96, 709, 902, 755]<|/det|>B. The interjections in these sentences have got mixed up. Correct them and rewrite the sentences in your notebook.
<|det|>list [148, 762, 634, 904]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [148, 762, 503, 785]<|/det|>1. Acho! The baby is asleep.
<|det|>text [146, 786, 627, 808]<|/det|>2. Mmm! We have won the tournament.
<|det|>text [146, 809, 615, 832]<|/det|>3. Ouch! We have a holiday tomorrow.
<|det|>text [146, 833, 463, 855]<|/det|>4. Hey! The man sneezed.
<|det|>text [146, 856, 633, 879]<|/det|>5. Hush! You have received a promotion.
<|det|>text [146, 880, 521, 904]<|/det|>6. Hurrah! What a giant spider.
<|det|>page_number [120, 931, 168, 948]<|/det|>144
<PAGE><|det|>list [108, 25, 867, 221]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 25, 520, 51]<|/det|>7. Yippee! You stepped on my toes.
<|det|>text [111, 52, 543, 76]<|/det|>8. Oops! The great poet passed away.
<|det|>text [111, 76, 441, 100]<|/det|>9. Wow! I heard something.
<|det|>text [101, 100, 432, 122]<|/det|>10. Shh! You left me behind.
<|det|>text [101, 123, 427, 148]<|/det|>11. Ugh! What a great idea.
<|det|>text [101, 148, 420, 171]<|/det|>12. Wow! I broke the vase.
<|det|>text [101, 172, 519, 195]<|/det|>13. Alas! I have solved the sum first.
<|det|>text [101, 196, 865, 221]<|/det|>14. Congratulations! This carrot soup is the best I have ever tasted.
<|det|>text [44, 245, 865, 368]<|/det|>You meet one of your school friends after a gap of ten years. Your friend had moved to another town and has recently moved back. What are the things you will discuss? Write the dialogues you would exchange. You can use interjections like Hi!, Hello!, Wow!, Congratulations!, Hurray!, Well!, etc., in your dialogues.
<|det|>page_number [834, 935, 883, 954]<|/det|>145
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 236, 142]<|/det|>18
<|det|>header [662, 37, 962, 72]<|/det|>Punctuation
<|det|>image [104, 149, 164, 196]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 144, 882, 194]<|/det|>The marks of punctuation are used in written text to make the meaning clear. You have learnt about the commonly used
<|det|>text [174, 194, 533, 217]<|/det|>punctuation marks. They are—
<|det|>list [103, 218, 625, 335]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [103, 218, 267, 240]<|/det|>- full stop (.)
<|det|>text [103, 242, 262, 264]<|/det|>- comma (.)
<|det|>text [103, 265, 421, 287]<|/det|>• mark of interrogation (?)
<|det|>text [103, 289, 411, 310]<|/det|>• mark of exclamation (!)
<|det|>text [103, 311, 622, 335]<|/det|>• quotation marks or inverted commas ('...')
<|det|>text [102, 350, 483, 373]<|/det|>Let us study about them in detail.
<|det|>title [102, 394, 223, 417]<|/det|>Full Stop
<|det|>text [101, 420, 950, 468]<|/det|>The full stop, or period, is the most important punctuation mark. It marks the end of an assertive or an imperative sentence. For example,
<|det|>list [103, 469, 558, 515]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [103, 469, 545, 491]<|/det|>• My name is Geetha. I am a teacher.
<|det|>text [104, 493, 255, 515]<|/det|>- Go away.
<|det|>title [102, 536, 206, 556]<|/det|>Comma
<|det|>text [102, 564, 355, 584]<|/det|>The comma is used—
<|det|>list [101, 585, 905, 699]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 585, 842, 631]<|/det|>◆ to separate three or more words or groups of words in a series. For example,
<|det|>text [140, 631, 824, 655]<|/det|>• Jaya, Sujatha, Vijay and Kamala are playing in the garden.
<|det|>text [140, 654, 905, 698]<|/det|>- Rich and poor, young and old, men and women, walked by the monument.
<|det|>list [101, 712, 603, 781]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [101, 712, 603, 735]<|/det|>◆ to set off a noun of address. For example,
<|det|>text [141, 736, 453, 758]<|/det|>• Father, I need your help.
<|det|>text [141, 759, 357, 781]<|/det|>- Sit down, Vinay.
<|det|>text [98, 791, 895, 838]<|/det|>◆ before please and thank ∵ …en they are placed at the end of a sentence. For examη\( ^{r} \)
<|det|>list [139, 839, 603, 885]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 839, 574, 862]<|/det|>• Could you pass me the salt, please?
<|det|>text [140, 862, 558, 885]<|/det|>• Will you have tea? No, thank you.
<|det|>page_number [120, 929, 168, 948]<|/det|>146
<PAGE><|det|>list [65, 29, 893, 150]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [65, 29, 893, 76]<|/det|>- after expressions like for example, however, moreover, that is, in fact. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 77, 630, 100]<|/det|>• She is young. However, she is hardworking.
<|det|>text [65, 110, 803, 134]<|/det|>◆ to separate expressions like yes, no, oh and well. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 134, 471, 155]<|/det|>• Yes, we will arrive next week.
<|det|>text [65, 168, 876, 192]<|/det|>◆ to set off expressions that interrupt the flow of a sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 193, 543, 216]<|/det|>• It is, as far as I can see, a good idea.
<|det|>text [65, 226, 874, 273]<|/det|>◆ to set off introductory phrases or subordinate clauses from the rest of the sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 274, 510, 295]<|/det|>• After dinner, we went for a walk.
<|det|>text [103, 297, 620, 320]<|/det|>• After she baked cookies, she baked a cake.
<|det|>text [65, 333, 597, 356]<|/det|>◆ to separate parts of an address. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 357, 297, 378]<|/det|>• 20, MG Road
<|det|>text [65, 390, 848, 413]<|/det|>◆ to separate parts of a date from the other words following the date.
<|det|>text [103, 414, 252, 437]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [103, 438, 610, 460]<|/det|>• On 12 January 1994, they met each other.
<|det|>text [65, 472, 742, 495]<|/det|>◆ to separate a statement from a question tag. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 496, 476, 518]<|/det|>• You did meet him, didn't you?
<|det|>text [65, 530, 865, 553]<|/det|>◆ to separate direct speech from the rest of the sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 554, 550, 576]<|/det|>• Mamta said, 'I will go to the market.'
<|det|>text [65, 587, 805, 611]<|/det|>◆ after adverbs, if they are placed at the beginning of a sentence.
<|det|>text [103, 612, 250, 634]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [103, 636, 501, 658]<|/det|>• Fortunately, no one was injured.
<|det|>title [61, 679, 348, 703]<|/det|>Mark of Interrogation
<|det|>text [61, 714, 811, 761]<|/det|>◆ The mark of interrogation is used at the end of an interrogative sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [103, 764, 361, 786]<|/det|>• What do you need?
<|det|>text [103, 788, 378, 810]<|/det|>• Can you wait for me?
<|det|>text [61, 821, 854, 845]<|/det|>◆ The mark of interrogation is also used at the end of a tag question.
<|det|>text [103, 847, 250, 869]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [103, 870, 634, 893]<|/det|>• We are meeting them on Sunday, aren't we?
<|det|>text [103, 894, 545, 918]<|/det|>• You didn't finish that cake, did you?
<|det|>page_number [838, 935, 882, 954]<|/det|>147
<PAGE><|det|>title [90, 48, 367, 68]<|/det|>Mark of Exclamation
<|det|>list [89, 69, 836, 182]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [90, 70, 836, 117]<|/det|>The mark of exclamation is used at the end of an exclamatory sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [131, 118, 460, 141]<|/det|>• What a pleasant surprise!
<|det|>text [132, 142, 357, 166]<|/det|>- It was shocking!
<|det|>text [89, 174, 787, 198]<|/det|>◆ The mark of exclamation is also used after an interjection.
<|det|>text [131, 200, 282, 222]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [131, 224, 277, 267]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 224, 260, 242]<|/det|>- Hurrah!
<|det|>text [132, 246, 230, 267]<|/det|>- Alas!
<|det|>title [88, 285, 291, 309]<|/det|>Capital Letters
<|det|>title [88, 315, 382, 338]<|/det|>A capital letter is used—
<|det|>list [88, 339, 890, 816]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [88, 339, 612, 362]<|/det|>◆ at the start of every sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [131, 363, 357, 382]<|/det|>• She is a teacher.
<|det|>text [88, 394, 886, 442]<|/det|>◆ at the start of every sentence within quotation marks. For example, Jugal said, 'We will meet you tomorrow.'
<|det|>text [88, 450, 831, 498]<|/det|>◆ with names of people and places. For example, the Taj Mahal, Australia, Uncle Peter, Dr Raj Kumar, New Delhi.
<|det|>text [88, 509, 485, 533]<|/det|>◆ for the pronoun I. For example,
<|det|>text [131, 533, 414, 556]<|/det|>• I have to get up early.
<|det|>text [88, 565, 783, 608]<|/det|>◆ For the initials in people's names. For example, R K Mehta, P T Usha.
<|det|>text [88, 620, 865, 667]<|/det|>◆ for the names of the days of the week and the months of the year. For example, Tuesday, December.
<|det|>text [88, 677, 895, 725]<|/det|>◆ for the names of festivals and holidays. For example, Independence Day, Christmas.
<|det|>text [88, 733, 821, 779]<|/det|>◆ for the names of books, films and plays. For example, Alice in Wonderland, The Mask of Zorro, Hamlet.
<|det|>text [88, 790, 821, 815]<|/det|>◆ for nationalities and languages. For example, Chinese, British.
<|det|>title [140, 836, 870, 860]<|/det|>Punctuate these sentences and insert capital letters as required.
<|det|>list [139, 865, 816, 914]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 865, 816, 888]<|/det|>1. do you recognise this handwriting do you think it is mine
<|det|>text [140, 888, 799, 911]<|/det|>2. oh dear help me to solve this problem what should I do
<|det|>page_number [115, 931, 159, 948]<|/det|>148
<PAGE><|det|>list [118, 45, 918, 444]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [118, 45, 882, 91]<|/det|>3. mr fogg accordingly tasted the dish but despite its spiced sauce found it far from palatable
<|det|>text [118, 92, 890, 138]<|/det|>4. what should I do should I stay back or should I leave alas I have no one to help me out
<|det|>text [120, 138, 885, 186]<|/det|>5. he pulled a chain and the shining whistle shrieked overhead he pulled it again and again he began enjoying this
<|det|>text [120, 186, 921, 232]<|/det|>6. the train stopped at eight in the midst of a glade some fifteen miles beyond rothal where there were several bungalows
<|det|>text [122, 232, 905, 325]<|/det|>7. let him look from his carriage window across the rich crop laden plains over the fields of speckled cotton of pulses in ordered lines he will see often many miles away the shapes of the distant blue hills on either side of the line
<|det|>text [122, 326, 877, 444]<|/det|>8. aunt mabel took her cat wherever she went to tea parties bridge parties hotels other people's homes much to everyone's dismay and irritation for not everyone is a cat lover the cat also accompanied her on train journeys however she did not buy a ticket for her cat
<|det|>title [61, 463, 573, 486]<|/det|>Inverted Commas or Quotation Marks
<|det|>image [64, 497, 135, 543]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 494, 525, 537]<|/det|>Inverted commas or quotation marks are used to separate direct
<|det|>text [64, 542, 500, 586]<|/det|>speech from the rest of the sentence.
For example,
<|det|>list [67, 586, 480, 632]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 586, 480, 608]<|/det|>• The lady said, 'I shall go home.'
<|det|>text [67, 609, 456, 631]<|/det|>- 'What am I to you?' he asked.
<|det|>title [589, 492, 650, 518]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [732, 488, 780, 523]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [589, 531, 914, 611]<|/det|>Inverted commas are also sometimes used when we are mentioning the name of a book or an article in a sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [608, 612, 892, 631]<|/det|>• Have you read ‘Oliver Twist’?
<|det|>title [61, 658, 920, 681]<|/det|>B. Rewrite these sentences using punctuation marks and capital letters.
<|det|>list [126, 686, 888, 914]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [126, 686, 882, 731]<|/det|>1. she asked how can i reach the station in an hour you must said the team leader
<|det|>text [125, 733, 885, 778]<|/det|>2. how can I a novice lead the team asked mina do not worry you will be fine said mr sen
<|det|>text [126, 779, 885, 825]<|/det|>3. I do not believe that he is a liar I think we are making a mistake said the captain
<|det|>text [126, 825, 888, 870]<|/det|>4. you must remember to take your medicines regularly the doctor said I will promised the patient
<|det|>text [128, 870, 882, 914]<|/det|>5. will you join us for a cup of tea said mother I am sorry but I am in a hurry some other time said mr smith

<PAGE><|det|>list [160, 35, 642, 245]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 35, 597, 59]<|/det|>6. what is electricity the child asked
<|det|>text [160, 60, 636, 104]<|/det|>7. the teacher said promise me that you will work hard for the exams
<|det|>text [160, 106, 631, 151]<|/det|>8. I enjoyed reading in an antique land by amitav ghosh said the teacher
<|det|>text [160, 152, 648, 197]<|/det|>9. when will you be ready to take up the responsibility asked the manager
