<|det|>image [654, 40, 975, 245]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [90, 264, 258, 289]<|/det|>Apostrophe
<|det|>image [91, 298, 147, 343]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [165, 308, 476, 331]<|/det|>The apostrophe is used—
<|det|>list [90, 342, 890, 435]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [90, 342, 890, 388]<|/det|>◆ to show possession or belonging. The position of the apostrophe indicates singular or plural possession. For example,
<|det|>text [130, 390, 686, 413]<|/det|>- The girl's dress is pink. (referring to one girl)
<|det|>text [130, 413, 756, 436]<|/det|>- The girls' dresses are pink. (referring to many girls)
<|det|>text [90, 446, 578, 469]<|/det|>◆ to show joint possession. For example,
<|det|>text [130, 470, 545, 513]<|/det|>- Jane and Jake's mother won the award.
<|det|>text [90, 527, 476, 551]<|/det|>in contractions. For example,
<|det|>list [130, 551, 517, 596]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 551, 386, 573]<|/det|>- It's (It is) hot today.
<|det|>text [130, 574, 517, 596]<|/det|>- Don't (Do not) litter the park.
<|det|>title [90, 628, 241, 648]<|/det|>Semicolon
<|det|>title [620, 451, 685, 476]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [780, 445, 830, 482]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [621, 490, 936, 530]<|/det|>Look at these words that are often confused.
<|det|>list [621, 532, 813, 634]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [621, 532, 778, 551]<|/det|>- we're (we are)
<|det|>text [641, 552, 812, 571]<|/det|>were (past of 'be')
<|det|>text [621, 572, 812, 591]<|/det|>- it's (it is/it has)
<|det|>text [641, 592, 872, 633]<|/det|>its (possessive pronoun/possessive adjective)
<|det|>text [90, 852, 928, 875]<|/det|>The semicolon represents a pause of greater importance than a comma.
<|det|>text [90, 876, 667, 896]<|/det|>Let us learn the use of the semicolon. It is used—
<|det|>list [90, 911, 948, 905]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [90, 911, 903, 933]<|/det|>◆ to separate words or phrases in a list, when they contain commas.
<|det|>text [130, 934, 285, 956]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [130, 957, 948, 802]<|/det|>- She bought vegetables such as potatoes, onions and cabbage; fruits such as bananas, grapes and oranges; and some household items.
\(\spadesuit\) to separate lengthy coordinate clauses where the conjunction is omitted. For example,
<|det|>text [130, 862, 898, 906]<|/det|>- The snow fell; the winds blew; the sky grew dark; we huddled indoors.
<|det|>page_number [111, 927, 160, 946]<|/det|>150
<PAGE><|det|>text [92, 28, 762, 54]<|/det|>to separate a series of loosely related clauses. For example,
<|det|>text [97, 54, 828, 121]<|/det|>- Today we win what tomorrow we lose; today we search what tomorrow we abandon; today we desire what tomorrow we shun.
<|det|>image [55, 150, 127, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [80, 162, 145, 188]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>list [108, 201, 871, 329]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 201, 871, 244]<|/det|>There are two other punctuation marks that can be used to add extra information after a clause. These are the colon (:) and the dash (—). For example,
<|det|>text [140, 245, 714, 265]<|/det|>- There are two parts of a sentence—the subject and the predicate.
<|det|>text [140, 266, 862, 286]<|/det|>• We collected a lot of things from the beach: shells, pebbles, birds' eggs and bottles.
<|det|>text [109, 287, 830, 328]<|/det|>- Hyphens (-) are used to join two or more words together. For example, door-to-door salesman, ninety-four balloons.
<|det|>image [34, 385, 100, 424]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [113, 389, 419, 410]<|/det|>Punctuate these sentences.
<|det|>list [126, 419, 896, 888]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [126, 419, 577, 441]<|/det|>1. mona get me my bag please said kim
<|det|>text [126, 442, 599, 465]<|/det|>2. alas what a loss can we ever get over it
<|det|>text [126, 466, 722, 489]<|/det|>3. we shall come and see you tomorrow they replied
<|det|>text [126, 489, 682, 511]<|/det|>4. to be honest I did not expect him to do so well
<|det|>text [126, 512, 847, 536]<|/det|>5. my sisters husband my nieces and nephews arrived yesterday
<|det|>text [126, 536, 830, 559]<|/det|>6. anita said i am very glad that you could make it to the party
<|det|>text [126, 559, 815, 582]<|/det|>7. there are a few things that i need to tell you said my father
<|det|>text [126, 583, 865, 627]<|/det|>8. by night or by day at home or at work in the country or abroad I never forget it
<|det|>text [126, 628, 855, 674]<|/det|>9. he said congratulations you have done brilliantly your parents will be very proud of you
<|det|>text [115, 675, 876, 719]<|/det|>10. our neighbours car is an old chevrolet and its going to fall apart very soon
<|det|>text [115, 720, 861, 767]<|/det|>11. they had played together as children they had studied together at school they worked together at office
<|det|>text [114, 767, 895, 835]<|/det|>12. it was in 1926 when master bridges twirled his moustache and turned his sights to the area beyond the teesta valley and decided to build railroads
<|det|>text [114, 837, 865, 886]<|/det|>13. mr sen the noted writer the talented artist the fire brand lawyer will preside over the meeting
<|det|>page_number [828, 928, 872, 946]<|/det|>151
<PAGE><|det|>title [98, 38, 768, 63]<|/det|>Punctuate and use capital letters wherever required.
<|det|>text [145, 71, 957, 254]<|/det|>the children were to be driven as a special treat to the sands at jagborough nicholas was not to be of the party he was in disgrace only that morning he had refused to eat his wholesome bread-and-milk on the seemingly frivolous ground that there was a frog in it older and wiser and better people had told him that there could not possibly be a frog in his bread-and-milk and that he was not to talk nonsense he continued nevertheless to talk what seemed nonsense and described with much detail the colouration and markings of the
<|det|>text [146, 256, 611, 576]<|/det|>alleged frog the dramatic part of the incident was that there really was a frog in nicholas' basin of bread-and-milk he had put it there himself so he knew something about it the sin of taking a frog from the garden and putting it into a bowl of wholesome bread-and-milk was talked about at great length but the fact that stood out clearest in the whole affair as it presented itself to the mind of nicholas was that the older wiser and better people had been proved to be in error in matters about which they had been very confident
<|det|>image [626, 270, 960, 525]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [104, 927, 151, 946]<|/det|>152
<PAGE><|det|>title [41, 36, 521, 77]<|/det|>picture Composition
<|det|>image [695, 33, 986, 134]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [47, 137, 105, 185]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [106, 134, 840, 186]<|/det|>In a picture composition, you use the picture as a reference point and write a composition. Like all compositions, a picture composition, too, should have a beginning, a middle and an end.
<|det|>text [46, 188, 800, 253]<|/det|>These are the points you should remember while writing a picture composition.
<|det|>list [48, 253, 874, 416]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [48, 253, 874, 322]<|/det|>- Study the picture carefully and pay attention to all the details such as facial expressions, gestures, clothes and postures of the characters in the picture.
<|det|>text [49, 323, 856, 370]<|/det|>- Your composition must have a basic plot, characters, dialogues and descriptions on the basis of the theme of the picture.
<|det|>text [50, 371, 435, 392]<|/det|>• Always write in the past tense.
<|det|>text [50, 393, 580, 416]<|/det|>- Remember to give your composition a title.
<|det|>text [48, 439, 813, 488]<|/det|>Look at these pictures carefully and write a story, an account or a description of what each picture suggests to you.
<|det|>text [52, 495, 80, 514]<|/det|>1.
<|det|>image [116, 496, 845, 876]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [848, 933, 895, 952]<|/det|>153

<PAGE><|det|>image_caption [97, 45, 120, 62]<|/det|>2.
<|det|>image [134, 45, 571, 339]<|/det|>
<|det|>image_caption [598, 48, 620, 65]<|/det|>3.
<|det|>image [626, 48, 948, 453]<|/det|>
<|det|>image_caption [68, 524, 91, 540]<|/det|>4.
<|det|>image [111, 526, 904, 880]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [80, 925, 131, 946]<|/det|>154
<PAGE><|det|>title [60, 44, 675, 85]<|/det|>Direct and Indirect Speech
<|det|>page_number [830, 38, 907, 83]<|/det|>19
<|det|>image [62, 157, 125, 205]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [139, 153, 904, 224]<|/det|>You have already learnt that there are two ways of reporting the words of a speaker—the direct speech and the indirect, or reported speech.
<|det|>text [61, 252, 890, 301]<|/det|>Direct speech reports the exact words of the speaker. In indirect speech, we report what the speaker said without quoting his/her exact words.
<|det|>title [64, 330, 223, 352]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>list [67, 353, 834, 398]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 353, 735, 375]<|/det|>• Rahul said, 'I want to buy a badminton racquet.' (direct)
<|det|>text [67, 376, 831, 398]<|/det|>• Rahul said that he wanted to buy a badminton racquet. (indirect)
<|det|>text [67, 413, 538, 436]<|/det|>Let us compare the two ways of speaking.
<|det|>title [194, 466, 350, 487]<|/det|>Direct speech
<|det|>text [85, 497, 436, 543]<|/det|>The exact words of the speaker are repeated.
<|det|>text [85, 551, 404, 619]<|/det|>The words of the speaker are enclosed within inverted commas.
<|det|>text [85, 628, 416, 652]<|/det|>The introductory verb is said.
<|det|>text [85, 684, 434, 753]<|/det|>A comma separates the introductory part and the exact words of the speaker.
<|det|>text [85, 786, 448, 854]<|/det|>The speaker uses the first person pronoun to speak for himself/herself.
<|det|>title [586, 466, 764, 489]<|/det|>Indirect speech
<|det|>text [465, 497, 856, 542]<|/det|>The speaker's words are reported by someone in his or her words.
<|det|>text [465, 552, 872, 620]<|/det|>Since the exact words of the speaker are not reproduced, the inverted commas are removed.
<|det|>text [465, 629, 872, 674]<|/det|>The introductory verb is usually said or told.
<|det|>text [465, 684, 860, 775]<|/det|>The subordinate conjunction that is used to join the introductory part to the reported part. The comma is not used.
<|det|>text [465, 785, 870, 832]<|/det|>The first person pronoun is changed to the third person pronoun.
<|det|>page_number [836, 932, 882, 952]<|/det|>155
<PAGE><|det|>text [111, 45, 430, 94]<|/det|>The verbs are usually in the present tense forms.
<|det|>list [116, 95, 270, 194]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [116, 95, 270, 112]<|/det|>• is, am, are
<|det|>text [116, 113, 260, 133]<|/det|>• has, have
<|det|>text [116, 136, 210, 152]<|/det|>can
<|det|>text [116, 153, 264, 173]<|/det|>• shall, will
<|det|>text [116, 175, 212, 193]<|/det|>- must
<|det|>text [502, 44, 941, 94]<|/det|>The verbs are usually changed to the corresponding past tense forms.
<|det|>list [502, 95, 695, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [503, 95, 648, 112]<|/det|>• was, were
<|det|>text [503, 113, 585, 131]<|/det|>- had
<|det|>text [503, 133, 604, 151]<|/det|>- could
<|det|>text [503, 152, 693, 173]<|/det|>- should, would
<|det|>text [503, 174, 609, 193]<|/det|>- had to
<|det|>text [101, 216, 946, 288]<|/det|>All present tense forms of the verb in direct speech are changed into past forms in indirect speech. Study this table to understand how personal pronouns and possessives change in reported speech.
<|det|>table [104, 304, 958, 884]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Direct speech</td><td>Indirect speech</td></tr><tr><td>Simple presentJai said, &#x27;I like eating pizza.&#x27;Present continuousRita said, &#x27;My aunt is baking a cake.&#x27;Present perfectRocky said, &#x27;I have slept for a while.&#x27;Present perfectCorpus said, &#x27;Rita had done this before.&#x27;</td><td>Simple pastJai said that he liked eating pizza.Past continuousRita said that her aunt was baking a cake.Past perfectRocky said that he had slept for a while.</td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Present perfectRita said, &#x27;Rita had done this before.&#x27;</td><td>Present perfectRita said that he had slept a lot while.Rita said that he had been swimming since she was eight years old.</td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td colspan="2">Past perfectRita said that he had bought a book for his mother.</td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td></td><td></td></tr><tr></tr></table>
<|det|>page_number [120, 929, 168, 948]<|/det|>156
<PAGE><|det|>text [70, 33, 867, 81]<|/det|>If the reported speech expresses a universal truth or reports a statement that is still true now, the tense of the verb remains unchanged.
<|det|>list [75, 82, 768, 175]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [75, 82, 768, 126]<|/det|>- The teacher told the class, 'The moon is earth's satellite.'
The teacher told the class that the moon is earth's satellite.
<|det|>text [75, 127, 750, 175]<|/det|>- Pratibha said, 'My mother doesn't like eggs.'
Pratibha said that her mother doesn't like eggs.
<|det|>text [72, 189, 900, 237]<|/det|>When we change direct speech to indirect, expressions of time and place indicating nearness are changed into ones denoting distance.
<|det|>text [71, 250, 902, 300]<|/det|>Study this table that lists some commonly used expressions denoting time and place.
<|det|>table [78, 325, 915, 715]<|/det|><table><tr><td></td><td>Direct speech</td><td>Indirect speech</td></tr><tr><td colspan="3">Adverbs of time</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>nowago todaytomorrowyesterdaylast year</td><td>thenbeforelast day</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>thet yesterday</td><td>thet previous year/the year before</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>next Monday</td><td>the following Monday</td></tr><tr><td colspan="3">Adverbs of place</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>hereherein</td><td>theretherein</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>thisthese</td><td>thatthose</td></tr></table>
<|det|>image [78, 762, 123, 791]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [139, 764, 641, 788]<|/det|>Change these sentences into indirect speech.
<|det|>list [139, 794, 861, 888]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 794, 625, 816]<|/det|>1. I said, 'Priya, you must be more serious.'
<|det|>text [140, 817, 769, 839]<|/det|>2. 'I saw you at the museum yesterday,' the teacher said.
<|det|>text [140, 840, 694, 861]<|/det|>3. Amita said to me, 'I am your sister's classmate.'
<|det|>text [140, 862, 861, 885]<|/det|>4. The professor said to Rahul, 'You are one of my best students.'
<|det|>page_number [851, 932, 895, 950]<|/det|>157
<PAGE><|det|>text [167, 38, 614, 106]<|/det|>5. The owner said to the reporter, 'The restaurant was inaugurated last year.'
<|det|>text [166, 107, 601, 152]<|/det|>6. The students said, 'We will plant a thousand saplings next year.'
<|det|>text [166, 152, 575, 198]<|/det|>7. The athlete said, 'I must win this championship.'
<|det|>text [165, 198, 582, 243]<|/det|>8. Gina said, 'Rahul was rehearsing for the show.'
<|det|>text [165, 245, 621, 291]<|/det|>9. The principal said to us, 'You should be punctual.'
<|det|>text [151, 291, 938, 338]<|/det|>10. The man said to the councillor, 'The supply of drinking water is inadequate.'
<|det|>text [151, 338, 843, 361]<|/det|>11. I said to my friend, 'You are not serious about the work.'
<|det|>text [151, 362, 837, 384]<|/det|>12. The lady said to her daughter, 'You are very insensitive.'
<|det|>text [151, 385, 807, 408]<|/det|>13. Rahim said to me, 'Raja can be trusted to do the job.'
<|det|>text [151, 408, 922, 453]<|/det|>14. He said, 'Hari has been appointed the Director General of the police force in Mumbai.'
<|det|>text [151, 453, 922, 499]<|/det|>15. Rani said to me, 'I shall return from Delhi before you leave for Bangalore.'
<|det|>text [151, 499, 953, 546]<|/det|>16. The secretary said, 'We need to leave for the function by four this evening.'
<|det|>text [151, 546, 870, 590]<|/det|>17. He said to the painter, 'You are taking too long to finish the portrait.'
<|det|>text [151, 591, 945, 637]<|/det|>18. Ratul told me, 'I met our yoga trainer yesterday and he promised to visit us today.'
<|det|>list [151, 38, 953, 637]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [95, 653, 375, 678]<|/det|>Reporting Questions
<|det|>image [97, 680, 164, 727]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 682, 940, 729]<|/det|>Let us see how we should change questions from direct to indirect speech.
<|det|>text [93, 746, 915, 791]<|/det|>◆ The reporting verb must be changed to asked, enquired, wondered, questioned. For example,
<|det|>text [136, 792, 664, 815]<|/det|>- Mani said to Kunal, 'Where are you going?'
<|det|>text [170, 815, 617, 838]<|/det|>Mani asked Kunal where he was going.
<|det|>text [132, 838, 915, 907]<|/det|>Notice that in the direct speech, the subject comes after the helping verb. However, when we report a question, the subject is placed before the helping verb.
<|det|>page_number [120, 924, 168, 943]<|/det|>158
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 44, 791, 88]<|/det|>◆ When we report a wh- question, we use the wh- word as a conjunction. For example,
<|det|>text [108, 89, 717, 135]<|/det|>- Jaya asked Parul, 'What is the name of your dog?' Jaya asked Parul what the name of her dog was.
<|det|>text [65, 145, 929, 191]<|/det|>◆ When we report a yes/no question, we connect the reporting question using if or whether. For example,
<|det|>text [109, 192, 613, 214]<|/det|>• I asked him, 'Will you come for dinner?'
<|det|>text [148, 215, 700, 237]<|/det|>I asked him whether he would come for dinner.
<|det|>text [65, 248, 930, 317]<|/det|>◆ We should keep in mind the tone of the question and the sentiment of the speaker when we change a question from direct to indirect speech. For example,
<|det|>text [110, 318, 692, 363]<|/det|>- My mother said, 'Would you like a cup of tea?' I said, 'Yes.'
<|det|>text [148, 365, 893, 409]<|/det|>My mother asked me whether I would like a cup of tea. I replied that I would.
<|det|>title [65, 434, 645, 458]<|/det|>B. Change these sentences into indirect speech.
<|det|>text [125, 463, 877, 486]<|/det|>1. I said, 'Would you like to accompany us to Mr Lahiri's house?'
<|det|>text [125, 487, 780, 510]<|/det|>2. Ram said, 'When do you expect to finish this project?'
<|det|>text [125, 510, 874, 533]<|/det|>3. Gargi said, 'Who will be the next captain of the cricket team?'
<|det|>text [125, 533, 797, 556]<|/det|>4. Dinaz said to me, 'What can I do for your family, dear?'
<|det|>text [126, 557, 788, 579]<|/det|>5. My mother said to the child, 'Where are your parents?'
<|det|>text [125, 579, 918, 602]<|/det|>6. I asked my sister, 'Are you sure that you have taken the right decision?'
<|det|>text [125, 602, 690, 625]<|/det|>7. The student said, 'When will the show begin?'
<|det|>text [162, 625, 914, 648]<|/det|>'It will begin as soon as the chief guest arrives,' replied the teacher.
<|det|>text [124, 648, 890, 694]<|/det|>8. 'Are you sure that he will come the next week?' I asked Himesh. 'I am certain, he will,' said he.
<|det|>title [65, 710, 510, 735]<|/det|>Reporting Command or Requests
<|det|>image [65, 740, 127, 784]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 740, 825, 787]<|/det|>Let us now take a look at the rules for changing commands, requests, advice, etc., into indirect speech.
<|det|>text [61, 800, 877, 845]<|/det|>We use reporting verbs like tell, warn to report orders and instructions. We also use the to + verb construction. For example,
<|det|>text [123, 846, 901, 892]<|/det|>- The teacher said to the pupils, 'You must apologise to her right now.' The teacher ordered the pupils to apologise to her right then.
<|det|>page_number [845, 927, 890, 946]<|/det|>159
<PAGE><|det|>text [175, 40, 960, 87]<|/det|>- I said to my friend, 'Could you make me a cup of hot chocolate?' I requested my friend to make me a cup of hot chocolate.
<|det|>title [95, 102, 416, 126]<|/det|>Reporting Exclamations
<|det|>text [92, 129, 945, 175]<|/det|>Let us now take a look at the rules for changing exclamations and wishes into indirect speech.
<|det|>list [92, 176, 960, 270]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [92, 176, 862, 198]<|/det|>◆ The reporting verb is changed into ask, advise, tell, request, etc.
<|det|>text [92, 199, 960, 269]<|/det|>Words such as hurrah, alas, oh, etc., are omitted. However, the feeling expressed must be conveyed appropriately by using expressions such as exclaimed with joy, said with a sigh, etc.
<|det|>text [91, 269, 680, 291]<|/det|>◆ The sign of exclamation is omitted. For example,
<|det|>list [131, 292, 714, 339]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [131, 292, 604, 313]<|/det|>- 'Oh no! We've missed the bus,' he said.
<|det|>text [151, 314, 714, 338]<|/det|>He exclaimed sadly that they had missed the bus.
<|det|>image [58, 355, 132, 395]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [140, 361, 658, 385]<|/det|>Change these sentences into indirect speech.
<|det|>list [152, 389, 948, 692]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [154, 390, 597, 413]<|/det|>1. Hema said, 'What a lovely garden!'
<|det|>text [153, 414, 811, 436]<|/det|>2. 'Such a pretty dress!' said the young girl to her mother.
<|det|>text [153, 437, 852, 459]<|/det|>3. She said to me, 'Please lend me your jacket for this week.'
<|det|>text [153, 460, 825, 482]<|/det|>4. I said to Rima, 'Please help me to complete my project.'
<|det|>text [153, 483, 835, 505]<|/det|>5. 'Please don't think that I wish to hurt you,' he said to me.
<|det|>text [153, 506, 945, 529]<|/det|>6. 'Splendid! Keep up the good work,' said the teacher to his pupil.
<|det|>text [153, 530, 911, 571]<|/det|>7. My aunt said to me, 'May the Almighty help you to succeed in life!'
<|det|>text [153, 574, 907, 617]<|/det|>8. The director said to the hero, 'Be careful when you jump from the cliff.'
<|det|>text [153, 620, 823, 663]<|/det|>9. The mother said to her children, 'Do not go out into the rain without raincoats.'
<|det|>text [141, 666, 660, 711]<|/det|>10. My sister said to me, 'At this rate you will not complete your homework.'
<|det|>text [141, 713, 616, 780]<|/det|>11. Her sister said to her, 'You could help mother with the cooking when you have some free time.'
<|det|>text [141, 782, 576, 826]<|/det|>12. 'Alas! To think I should be leading such a life,' said the old man.
<|det|>text [141, 828, 960, 874]<|/det|>13. Swami said to Harish, 'Do not accept the proposal unless you are absolutely sure about it.'
<|det|>text [141, 875, 848, 920]<|/det|>14. The father said, 'Children, do not be so nervous about the tournament next month. I am sure that you will win it.'
<|det|>image [642, 634, 975, 819]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 927, 165, 946]<|/det|>160
<PAGE><|det|>title [57, 48, 695, 70]<|/det|>Conversion of Indirect Speech into Direct Speech
<|det|>image [58, 75, 121, 125]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 77, 880, 121]<|/det|>We know that indirect speech is a means of reporting what somebody has said without using the actual words of the speaker.
<|det|>text [57, 122, 830, 166]<|/det|>The best way to change from indirect to direct speech is to mentally identify the two parts of the sentence.
<|det|>list [57, 167, 870, 277]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [57, 167, 870, 211]<|/det|>The main part will contain a reporting verb like said, tell, suggested, requested, etc.
<|det|>text [57, 213, 844, 258]<|/det|>The other part will contain what is being reported, that is, the part which is the quoted section in direct speech.
<|det|>text [57, 259, 780, 304]<|/det|>The part that will become the quote once we have made the transformation is the object of the reporting verb.
<|det|>list [108, 305, 895, 555]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 305, 799, 348]<|/det|>- He told me that my parcel had reached the previous week. He said to me, 'Your parcel reached last week.'
<|det|>text [108, 350, 783, 393]<|/det|>- The teacher asked the boy whether he had completed his homework.
<|det|>text [142, 395, 895, 418]<|/det|>The teacher said to the boy, 'Have you completed your homework?'
<|det|>text [108, 420, 701, 441]<|/det|>• His mother urged him to take his studies seriously.
<|det|>text [142, 442, 856, 464]<|/det|>His mother said to him, 'You should take your studies seriously.'
<|det|>text [108, 465, 787, 486]<|/det|>• He ordered his servant to be quiet and listen to his words.
<|det|>text [142, 488, 770, 510]<|/det|>He said to his servant, 'Be quiet and listen to my words.'
<|det|>text [108, 511, 591, 533]<|/det|>• He exclaimed that it was a terrible sight.
<|det|>text [142, 534, 532, 556]<|/det|>'What a terrible sight it is!' said he.
<|det|>image [31, 577, 110, 628]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [119, 578, 602, 602]<|/det|>Change these sentences into direct speech.
<|det|>list [131, 608, 870, 910]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [132, 609, 558, 631]<|/det|>1. He requested me to pass the sugar.
<|det|>text [131, 632, 650, 653]<|/det|>2. He asked me whether we felt sorry for him.
<|det|>text [131, 655, 615, 676]<|/det|>3. Lata exclaimed that she was very clever.
<|det|>text [131, 678, 640, 699]<|/det|>4. Nutan said that she preferred tea to coffee.
<|det|>text [131, 701, 817, 746]<|/det|>5. Seema said that there was an accident the night before on the bypass.
<|det|>text [130, 747, 868, 793]<|/det|>6. Rekha said to Radhika that she remembered meeting her three days before.
<|det|>text [130, 794, 729, 817]<|/det|>7. He said that he is glad to be here for the wedding.
<|det|>text [130, 818, 758, 840]<|/det|>8. I asked the pupils what plans they had for Christmas.
<|det|>text [130, 841, 740, 863]<|/det|>9. Katy requested Dona to wait there till she returned.
<|det|>text [117, 864, 808, 887]<|/det|>10. You said to me that you would give me leave on Tuesday.
<|det|>text [117, 888, 833, 911]<|/det|>11. The principal ordered them to get back to their classrooms.
<|det|>page_number [838, 928, 884, 947]<|/det|>161
<PAGE><|det|>list [160, 37, 951, 150]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 37, 867, 59]<|/det|>12. The teacher said that the summer solstice falls on 21 June.
<|det|>text [161, 60, 870, 82]<|/det|>13. He clarified that he had done the deed under compulsion.
<|det|>text [161, 83, 928, 105]<|/det|>14. They enquired politely as to who he was and what he wanted.
<|det|>text [161, 106, 951, 150]<|/det|>15. My mother exclaimed with disappointment that she had lost her favourite watch the day before.
<|det|>title [157, 164, 880, 212]<|/det|>Change this extract into indirect speech. Remember to use the appropriate reporting verbs.
<|det|>text [160, 218, 570, 239]<|/det|>1. 'What time is it?' asked the man.
<|det|>text [202, 241, 667, 263]<|/det|>'Ten minutes past two, sir,' said the butler.
<|det|>text [201, 263, 935, 308]<|/det|>'How horribly late! You must wake me at nine tomorrow. I have some work to do.'
<|det|>text [202, 311, 523, 333]<|/det|>'All right, sir,' said the butler.
<|det|>text [202, 334, 548, 356]<|/det|>'Did anyone call this evening?'
<|det|>text [201, 356, 857, 402]<|/det|>'Only Miss Smith. She took the passenger train to Paris this evening,' the butler said.
<|det|>text [202, 403, 530, 426]<|/det|>'Did she leave any message?'
<|det|>text [201, 426, 882, 448]<|/det|>'Yes, she said she'd be back next week,' answered the butler.
<|det|>title [157, 450, 546, 471]<|/det|>2. 'When will we reach Kolkata?'
<|det|>text [201, 472, 965, 517]<|/det|>'Shiela, I think we will reach at about 6 am tomorrow if the train is on time,' said her father.
<|det|>text [201, 519, 672, 541]<|/det|>'I am sure it will. Don't worry,' said Shiela.
<|det|>text [201, 541, 956, 633]<|/det|>'You are very optimistic,' said Mr Sen, Shiela's father, with a smile. 'Actually, I have travelled by this train twice. In fact last week too, I came to Kolkata by this train. On both occasions it reached before the scheduled time.
<|det|>text [202, 635, 470, 658]<|/det|>'Oh great!' said Mr Sen.
<|det|>title [160, 672, 645, 695]<|/det|>Read these dialogues and fill in the blanks.
<|det|>text [160, 701, 815, 725]<|/det|>1. Sahil: I have invited four of my friends for dinner today.
<|det|>text [201, 725, 648, 747]<|/det|>Radhika: Then I'll invite my friends also.
<|det|>text [201, 748, 546, 771]<|/det|>Sahil: That will be a good idea.
<|det|>text [201, 772, 711, 793]<|/det|>Radhika: So, what should we have for dinner?
<|det|>text [201, 795, 591, 815]<|/det|>Sahil: Let us decide the menu now.
<|det|>text [201, 841, 892, 864]<|/det|>Sahil told Radhika that i) ____. Then Radhika said
<|det|>text [201, 865, 966, 888]<|/det|>ii) ____, which Sahil thought iii) ____. When
<|det|>text [201, 889, 941, 913]<|/det|>Radhika asked iv) ____ Sahil replied v) ____
<|det|>page_number [132, 934, 177, 952]<|/det|>162
<PAGE><|det|>text [111, 56, 528, 78]<|/det|>2. Principal: Where is your mother?
<|det|>text [154, 79, 710, 99]<|/det|>Rhea: I have no mother. She died five years back.
<|det|>text [154, 100, 769, 122]<|/det|>Principal: Who was the lady with whom you came in?
<|det|>text [154, 123, 722, 144]<|/det|>Rhea: She is a stranger. She brought me for a treat.
<|det|>text [154, 145, 760, 167]<|/det|>Principal: You should not accept food from a stranger.
<|det|>text [153, 188, 905, 302]<|/det|>The Principal asked Rhea i) ____. Puzzled, the girl told the Principal ii) ____ back. At this the Principal asked her iii) ____ had come in. Rhea replied that she was a stranger who iv) ____. Then Principal told Rhea v)
<|det|>text [115, 325, 840, 349]<|/det|>3. Maggie: Tom, how much money did you give for the rabbits.
<|det|>text [158, 350, 533, 371]<|/det|>Tom: Five shillings and six pence.
<|det|>text [158, 371, 895, 416]<|/det|>Maggie: I think I've got more than that in my box upstairs. I'll ask mother to give it to you.
<|det|>text [158, 416, 903, 439]<|/det|>Tom: What for? I don't want your money. I've got far more money.
<|det|>text [159, 440, 624, 462]<|/det|>Maggie: I want to buy some more rabbits.
<|det|>text [158, 484, 831, 507]<|/det|>Maggie asked Tom i) ____. Tom replied promptly
<|det|>text [159, 508, 831, 530]<|/det|>ii) ____. Maggie told him iii) ____ she
<|det|>text [159, 530, 805, 552]<|/det|>would ask mother to give that money to him. Tom said iv)
<|det|>text [159, 553, 801, 575]<|/det|>____. Maggie told him that v) ____.
<|det|>text [115, 598, 628, 621]<|/det|>4. Doctor: Hello, How are you feeling now?
<|det|>text [158, 622, 488, 642]<|/det|>Patient: I am much better, sir.
<|det|>text [158, 643, 821, 665]<|/det|>Doctor: Are you not fully well? Don't you take proper rest?
<|det|>text [158, 666, 911, 711]<|/det|>Patient: I am not fully well as I cannot have proper rest because of the busy schedule.
<|det|>text [158, 712, 729, 735]<|/det|>Doctor: Take some tonic to regain your health, ok?
<|det|>text [158, 757, 773, 780]<|/det|>The doctor asked i) ____ the patient replied
<|det|>text [158, 781, 836, 803]<|/det|>ii) ____. The doctor asked iii) ____ the
<|det|>text [158, 804, 854, 827]<|/det|>patient replied iv) ____. Finally, the doctor advised
<|det|>text [158, 828, 496, 850]<|/det|>the patient v) ____.
<|det|>page_number [848, 927, 892, 946]<|/det|>163
<PAGE><|det|>text [145, 37, 586, 59]<|/det|>6. Gina: Why do you look so worried?
<|det|>text [180, 59, 928, 104]<|/det|>Madhu: I have not prepared well for the exams. I have read only general articles.
<|det|>text [180, 105, 951, 174]<|/det|>Gina: Don't worry. There will be no questions from MCB. Reading general articles from magazines or newspapers will help you develop reading skills.
<|det|>text [180, 174, 832, 197]<|/det|>Madhu: Thank you, I am very relieved. Thank you indeed.
<|det|>text [180, 219, 955, 262]<|/det|>Gina asked Madhu i) ____. Madhu told with concern that ii) ____ and that she had read only general articles.
<|det|>text [180, 264, 874, 337]<|/det|>Gina assured her not to worry as iii) ____. Madhu looked relieved at Gina's assurance and thanked her for her consoling words.
<|det|>page_number [119, 939, 161, 956]<|/det|>164
<PAGE><|det|>title [70, 64, 711, 98]<|/det|>From the Berlin Broadcasts
<|det|>header [729, 18, 986, 152]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>text [70, 169, 871, 374]<|/det|>Pelham Grenville Wodehouse is considered to be one the masters of comic writing. At the onset of World War II, Wodehouse and his wife were in France. When the Germans occupied the area where they were living, all male civilians less than 60 years of age were placed in internment camps. Wodehouse, although 59 years, spent a period of almost a year in camps in Loos Prison, Liège, Huy and Tost before the hue and cry in the international community led to his early release. This is an extract from some talks that Wodehouse gave on the radio describing his days of internment.
<|det|>text [72, 386, 918, 501]<|/det|>Life in an internment camp resembles life outside, in that it is what you make it. Nothing can take away the unpleasant feeling of being a prisoner, but you can make an effort and prevent it getting you down. And that is what we did, and what I imagine all the other British prisoners in Germany did.
<|det|>text [72, 510, 905, 645]<|/det|>Lectures and concerts were arranged, and it was also possible for us to learn French, German, Italian, Spanish, first-aid and shorthand, and also to find out all there was to find out about French and English literature. In fact, we were not so much internees as a student body. Towards the end of my stay, we had our own paper—a bright little sheet called The Tost Times, published twice a month.
<|det|>text [72, 659, 611, 879]<|/det|>One great improvement at Tost, from my viewpoint, was that men of fifty and over were not liable for fatigues—in other words, the dirty work. At Liège and Huy, there had been no age limit. We had all pitched in together, reverend elders and beardless boys alike—cleaning out latrines with one hand and peeling potatoes with the other, so to speak. At Tost, for the old dodderers like myself, the arduous side of life was limited to making our beds, brushing the
<|det|>image [616, 631, 925, 873]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [854, 911, 901, 929]<|/det|>165
<PAGE><|det|>text [98, 36, 942, 108]<|/det|>floor under and around them, and washing our linen. When there was man's work to be done, like hauling coal or shovelling snow, we just sat and looked on, while the younger set snapped into it.
<|det|>text [97, 121, 940, 214]<|/det|>There were certain fatigues, like acting as a server at meals and working in the cookhouse, which were warmly competed for. For these, you got double rations. But the only reward of the ordinary chore, like hauling coal, was the joy of labour.
<|det|>text [96, 228, 960, 461]<|/det|>It is a curious experience being completely shut off from the outer world, as one is in an internment camp. One lives principally on potatoes and rumours. To military prisoners, I believe, camp rumours are known for some reason as ‘Blue Pigeons’. We used to call them bedtime stories. These bedtime stories never turned out to be true, but a rumour a day kept depression away, so they served their purpose. Certainly, whether owing to bedtime stories or simply to the feeling, which I myself had, that, if one was in, one was in and it was no use making heavy weather about it, the morale of the men at Tost was wonderful. I never met a more cheerful crowd, and I loved them like brothers.
<|det|>text [96, 475, 944, 592]<|/det|>With this talk, I bring to an end the story of my adventures as British Civilian Prisoner Number 796, and before concluding I should like once more to thank all the kind people in America who wrote me letters while I was in camp. Nobody who has not been in a prison camp can realise what letters, especially letters like those I received, mean to an internee.
<|det|>image [55, 613, 136, 673]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [148, 615, 433, 637]<|/det|>Answer these questions.
<|det|>list [149, 642, 944, 851]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 642, 888, 686]<|/det|>1. Why does the author feel that life in an interment camp is the same as life outside?
<|det|>text [149, 688, 900, 735]<|/det|>2. How was life at the Tost internment camp different from life at other camps at Liège and Huy?
<|det|>text [150, 735, 770, 757]<|/det|>3. When were the prisoners awarded a double ration?
<|det|>text [149, 758, 944, 781]<|/det|>4. What were the 'Blue Pigeons'? How did the author refer to them?
<|det|>text [150, 782, 926, 849]<|/det|>5. 'A rumour a day kept depression away.' Which proverb do these words resemble? Explain these words with reference to the context.
<|det|>page_number [120, 928, 171, 946]<|/det|>166
<PAGE><|det|>title [135, 58, 676, 79]<|/det|>State whether these statements are true or false.
<|det|>list [136, 84, 916, 238]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [136, 84, 829, 105]<|/det|>1. The author was interned at the camp as a criminal prisoner.
<|det|>text [136, 107, 853, 149]<|/det|>2. The author specially thanks the people of Armenia for writing letters when he was at the camp.
<|det|>text [137, 150, 876, 191]<|/det|>3. The author remembers his campmates as a very cheerful crowd whom he loved as brothers.
<|det|>text [138, 193, 916, 237]<|/det|>4. The prisoners at the camp could learn French, German, Spanish or other skills like shorthand and first-aid.
<|det|>image [31, 261, 138, 330]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [145, 260, 766, 285]<|/det|>Find suitable phrases from the passages for these words.
<|det|>list [149, 289, 557, 400]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 290, 452, 312]<|/det|>1. helping with some work
<|det|>text [150, 313, 456, 335]<|/det|>2. doing something quickly
<|det|>text [150, 336, 429, 358]<|/det|>3. to make notes quickly
<|det|>text [150, 358, 376, 379]<|/det|>4. taking more time
<|det|>text [150, 380, 557, 402]<|/det|>5. to be separated from other people
<|det|>page_number [854, 909, 901, 927]<|/det|>167
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 370, 134]<|/det|>20
<|det|>header [562, 35, 962, 72]<|/det|>Kinds of Phrases
<|det|>image [92, 142, 153, 193]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [170, 141, 877, 211]<|/det|>You have learnt that a phrase is a group of words that makes complete sense only when it is a part of a sentence. A phrase does not contain a finite verb.
<|det|>text [91, 228, 604, 251]<|/det|>There are three kinds of phrases. They are—
<|det|>text [91, 252, 331, 275]<|/det|>◆ adjective phrase
<|det|>text [437, 252, 648, 275]<|/det|>◆ adverb phrase
<|det|>text [728, 251, 931, 273]<|/det|>◆ noun phrase
<|det|>title [91, 290, 325, 314]<|/det|>Adjective Phrase
<|det|>text [90, 328, 934, 376]<|/det|>When a group of words does the work of an adjective in a sentence, we call it an adjective phrase.
<|det|>text [90, 395, 903, 442]<|/det|>An adjective phrase modifies a noun and comes immediately after the noun or pronoun it modifies. For example,
<|det|>list [94, 443, 667, 490]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [94, 443, 620, 465]<|/det|>- Kirti is the student with the highest marks.
<|det|>text [94, 466, 655, 487]<|/det|>• The finalist wore a crown made of diamonds.
<|det|>text [91, 498, 878, 545]<|/det|>An adjective can usually replace an adjective phrase and vice-versa. For example,
<|det|>list [94, 546, 769, 592]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [94, 546, 646, 568]<|/det|>• We were surprised to find a deserted village.
<|det|>text [94, 568, 769, 591]<|/det|>• We were surprised to find a village without inhabitants.
<|det|>text [92, 613, 931, 661]<|/det|>A. Replace the adjectives in each of these sentences with an adjective phrase, without changing the meaning.
<|det|>list [160, 667, 714, 900]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [161, 667, 458, 688]<|/det|>1. It was a valuable coin.
<|det|>text [160, 690, 580, 713]<|/det|>2. A wealthy man entered the room.
<|det|>text [160, 714, 545, 735]<|/det|>3. He has done a shameful deed.
<|det|>text [160, 737, 540, 759]<|/det|>4. A glass jar broke accidentally.
<|det|>text [160, 760, 590, 782]<|/det|>5. He was certainly a friendless man.
<|det|>text [160, 784, 580, 805]<|/det|>6. We traversed mountainous roads.
<|det|>text [160, 807, 612, 828]<|/det|>7. Brave deeds deserve our admiration.
<|det|>text [160, 830, 631, 851]<|/det|>8. A loaded truck entered the small lane.
<|det|>text [159, 853, 709, 875]<|/det|>9. A pebbled path was laid around the fountain.
<|det|>text [148, 876, 714, 899]<|/det|>10. They spoke about the colourful world outside.
<|det|>image [642, 655, 950, 819]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 932, 165, 950]<|/det|>168
<PAGE><|det|>title [55, 38, 674, 63]<|/det|>B. Fill in the blanks with suitable adjective phrases.
<|det|>list [114, 68, 805, 253]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [115, 68, 504, 89]<|/det|>1. I like children ____.
<|det|>text [114, 92, 516, 113]<|/det|>2. He is a student ____.
<|det|>text [114, 115, 585, 137]<|/det|>3. A man ____ is loved by all.
<|det|>text [114, 139, 593, 160]<|/det|>4. Clothes ____ are expensive.
<|det|>text [114, 162, 597, 183]<|/det|>5. We met some athletes ____.
<|det|>text [114, 185, 681, 207]<|/det|>6. The book ____ belongs to my sister.
<|det|>text [114, 208, 742, 229]<|/det|>7. The principal submitted a proposal ____.
<|det|>text [113, 230, 802, 253]<|/det|>8. We bought a table ____ from an antique store.
<|det|>title [55, 269, 262, 290]<|/det|>Adverb Phrase
<|det|>text [54, 311, 907, 357]<|/det|>When a group of words does the work of an adverb in a sentence, we call it an adverb phrase.
<|det|>text [54, 379, 862, 403]<|/det|>Study the table with examples of the different kinds of adverb phrases.
<|det|>title [108, 419, 280, 441]<|/det|>Adverb phrase
<|det|>text [62, 451, 167, 470]<|/det|>manner
<|det|>text [62, 481, 125, 501]<|/det|>time
<|det|>text [62, 511, 137, 532]<|/det|>place
<|det|>text [62, 542, 153, 564]<|/det|>degree
<|det|>text [62, 573, 192, 595]<|/det|>frequency
<|det|>text [62, 605, 165, 627]<|/det|>purpose
<|det|>title [512, 419, 720, 441]<|/det|>Example sentence
<|det|>text [334, 449, 802, 471]<|/det|>The fireman opened the door with a kick.
<|det|>text [334, 479, 795, 503]<|/det|>Mother cleans the house in the morning.
<|det|>text [334, 510, 698, 533]<|/det|>Mansi lives in a foreign country.
<|det|>text [334, 541, 717, 564]<|/det|>The dog eats its food very quickly.
<|det|>text [334, 572, 802, 595]<|/det|>You should go to the dentist twice a year.
<|det|>text [334, 603, 564, 627]<|/det|>We play golf for fun.
<|det|>image [55, 648, 114, 692]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [144, 647, 900, 690]<|/det|>It is sometimes possible to replace the adverb phrase in a sentence with an adverb and vice-versa. For example,
<|det|>text [144, 693, 660, 740]<|/det|>- I shall support this cause with all my heart. I shall support this cause whole-heartedly.
<|det|>text [53, 741, 641, 788]<|/det|>- I went through the documents again and again. I went through the documents repeatedly.
<|det|>title [53, 801, 691, 825]<|/det|>C. Replace the adverbs with suitable adverb phrases.
<|det|>list [102, 832, 478, 927]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [102, 832, 417, 854]<|/det|>1. Let us cease work now.
<|det|>text [102, 856, 440, 879]<|/det|>2. The wind blew forcefully.
<|det|>text [102, 880, 476, 904]<|/det|>3. The orator spoke eloquently.
<|det|>text [102, 904, 446, 927]<|/det|>4. The child spoke truthfully.
<|det|>page_number [848, 930, 896, 950]<|/det|>169
<PAGE><|det|>text [150, 45, 485, 66]<|/det|>5. The teacher spoke gently.
<|det|>text [150, 67, 555, 89]<|/det|>6. You should keep the table here.
<|det|>text [150, 90, 618, 111]<|/det|>7. This product is available everywhere.
<|det|>text [150, 111, 825, 134]<|/det|>8. The man finally succeeded in getting back his property.
<|det|>text [150, 134, 912, 158]<|/det|>9. We have to face this kind of traffic daily on our way to school.
<|det|>list [150, 45, 912, 158]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 180, 691, 205]<|/det|>D. Fill in the blanks with suitable adverb phrases.
<|det|>text [150, 212, 653, 235]<|/det|>1. Answer these questions ____.
<|det|>text [150, 236, 548, 258]<|/det|>2. Put these keys ____.
<|det|>text [150, 258, 592, 279]<|/det|>3. The bus will leave ____.
<|det|>text [150, 281, 636, 304]<|/det|>4. We greeted the guests ____.
<|det|>text [150, 304, 786, 327]<|/det|>5. ____ a marvellous monument stood here.
<|det|>text [150, 329, 615, 351]<|/det|>6. I dealt with problem ____.
<|det|>text [150, 352, 639, 375]<|/det|>7. The young girl danced ____.
<|det|>text [150, 376, 750, 398]<|/det|>8. We have shifted to an apartment ____.
<|det|>text [150, 399, 636, 421]<|/det|>9. The shopkeeper spoke ____.
<|det|>list [150, 212, 786, 421]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [98, 442, 275, 464]<|/det|>Noun Phrase
<|det|>text [96, 481, 951, 530]<|/det|>When a group of words does the work of a noun in a sentence, we call it the noun phrase. For example,
<|det|>text [99, 548, 893, 571]<|/det|>- The chairman of the board of governors attended the convention.
<|det|>text [99, 572, 830, 593]<|/det|>- The actor had appeared in innumerable television comedies.
<|det|>text [99, 594, 900, 617]<|/det|>- Leela gave me a piece of insightful and thought-provoking advice.
<|det|>text [99, 618, 542, 641]<|/det|>• The girl in the red skirt is my sister.
<|det|>list [99, 548, 900, 641]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [101, 655, 160, 704]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 655, 945, 701]<|/det|>Sometimes, a noun phrase can begin with a gerund or a participle. For example,
<|det|>text [99, 710, 747, 733]<|/det|>• To be a Bharatmatyan dancer is her wish. (participle)
<|det|>text [99, 734, 777, 757]<|/det|>- Watching cricket on TV is the nation's pastime. (gerund)
<|det|>list [99, 710, 777, 757]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [96, 772, 958, 841]<|/det|>In a sentence, a noun usually acts as the subject or the object of a verb. Likewise, a noun phrase too acts as the subject or the object of the verb in a sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [99, 842, 508, 865]<|/det|>• I like painting portraits. (object)
<|det|>text [99, 865, 725, 889]<|/det|>• Studying music is a wonderful experience. (subject)
<|det|>list [99, 842, 725, 889]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 934, 168, 954]<|/det|>170
<PAGE><|det|>title [106, 31, 636, 55]<|/det|>Pick out the noun phrases in these sentences.
<|det|>list [119, 61, 888, 295]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 61, 400, 83]<|/det|>1. To smoke is harmful.
<|det|>text [120, 85, 425, 107]<|/det|>2. I love going to the zoo.
<|det|>text [120, 108, 495, 130]<|/det|>3. Would you like to buy a car?
<|det|>text [120, 132, 532, 154]<|/det|>4. I enjoy walking in the mornings.
<|det|>text [120, 155, 445, 176]<|/det|>5. He hopes to win a prize.
<|det|>text [120, 178, 555, 201]<|/det|>6. Acting in plays is highly satisfying.
<|det|>text [120, 202, 578, 224]<|/det|>7. Buying a flat in the city is expensive.
<|det|>text [120, 226, 773, 248]<|/det|>From here, you can get a panoramic view of the park.
<|det|>text [120, 249, 770, 271]<|/det|>9. Flying around the world in a private plane is his plan.
<|det|>text [109, 272, 888, 295]<|/det|>10. We are now fed up with waiting for an alternative arrangement.
<|det|>image [768, 40, 904, 216]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [109, 318, 636, 341]<|/det|>Complete these sentences with noun phrases.
<|det|>list [109, 349, 601, 535]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [110, 350, 408, 370]<|/det|>1. I want ____.
<|det|>text [110, 373, 495, 394]<|/det|>2. Do you think ____?
<|det|>text [110, 396, 515, 417]<|/det|>3. I did not expect ____.
<|det|>text [110, 420, 485, 441]<|/det|>4. ____ surprises me.
<|det|>text [110, 443, 483, 464]<|/det|>5. I have found ____.
<|det|>text [110, 467, 515, 488]<|/det|>5. ____ gives me no joy.
<|det|>text [110, 489, 599, 510]<|/det|>7. The child did not know ____.
<|det|>text [110, 512, 575, 534]<|/det|>8. ____ seems the right thing.
<|det|>image [53, 545, 96, 585]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 556, 856, 603]<|/det|>Some phrases have been highlighted in this passage. Say whether they are noun, adjective or adverb phrases.
<|det|>text [110, 608, 888, 746]<|/det|>1. In the evening, the men played cards by the campfire. They discussed how to catch trout. I thought of joining them. At night, they lay on their improvised beds. The rattling of guns woke me. I heard the neighing of horses. The hunters with fearless faces were ready. I offered to join the party. My father, a man of few words, smiled and agreed.
<|det|>text [108, 747, 888, 932]<|/det|>2. The second time I visited India, I decided that I would settle in a hill station and write my novel. I had visions of myself sitting at a table on a large verandah, my notes spread out in front of me next to a steaming cup of tea. Green hills heavy with mists would lie at my feet and the shrill cries of monkeys would fill my ears. The lady who ran the place would tell me stories about the struggle to boot the British out. After my writing day was over, I would go for walks in the rolling hills of the tea estates.
<|det|>page_number [828, 925, 876, 943]<|/det|>171
<PAGE><|det|>title [559, 47, 957, 80]<|/det|>Kinds of Clauses
<|det|>text [95, 150, 907, 198]<|/det|>7 You have learnt that a clause is a group of words that contains a subject and a finite verb.
<|det|>text [93, 198, 951, 268]<|/det|>Some sentences are made up of one clause. Some sentences are made up of two or more clauses. Let us first understand the differences between a phrase, a clause and a sentence.
<|det|>table [95, 291, 966, 589]<|/det|><table><tr><td>Phrase</td><td>Clause</td><td>Sentence</td></tr><tr><td>A phrase is a meaningful group of words.</td><td>A clause is a meaningful group of words.</td><td>A sentence is a meaningful group of words.</td></tr><tr><td>It does not make complete sense by itself.</td><td>It may or may not be dependent on something else for complete meaning.</td><td>It expresses a complete thought.</td></tr><tr><td>It has no subject or predicate.</td><td>It has a subject and a predicate.</td><td>It has a subject and a predicate.</td></tr><tr><td>It has no finite verb.</td><td>It has a finite verb.</td><td>It has a finite verb.</td></tr></table>
<|det|>text [95, 617, 384, 638]<|/det|>Now, read this sentence.
<|det|>text [96, 640, 692, 661]<|/det|>• The girl who came yesterday was Manish's sister.
<|det|>text [94, 663, 961, 733]<|/det|>The highlighted part of this sentence can stand on its own. However, the underlined part of this sentence cannot stand on its own. It has to depend on the rest of the sentence to convey the correct meaning.
<|det|>text [95, 756, 862, 803]<|/det|>A clause that can stand on its own is called the main clause, or the principal clause.
<|det|>text [96, 833, 958, 900]<|/det|>A clause that cannot stand on its own and depends on the main clause to complete its meaning is called the subordinate clause, or the dependent clause.
<|det|>page_number [128, 933, 171, 952]<|/det|>172
<PAGE><|det|>text [57, 26, 341, 51]<|/det|>Now, read this sentence.
<|det|>list [58, 52, 893, 122]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [58, 52, 397, 74]<|/det|>• Mahima is writing a letter.
<|det|>text [58, 75, 893, 122]<|/det|>This sentence has a subject and a predicate and can stand on its own; so we can say that this sentence is a main clause.
<|det|>text [58, 137, 375, 159]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>text [60, 160, 482, 207]<|/det|>- The girl tried. The girl succeeded. The girl tried and succeeded.
<|det|>text [58, 209, 880, 256]<|/det|>By using the coordinating conjunction and, we joined two sentences of equal grammatical importance.
<|det|>text [58, 280, 785, 328]<|/det|>Clauses that are joined by coordinating conjunctions are called coordinate clauses, or coordinate main clauses.
<|det|>text [58, 354, 377, 375]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences.
<|det|>list [60, 377, 562, 448]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [60, 377, 562, 398]<|/det|>• He has a car. He prefers to travel by bus.
<|det|>text [98, 400, 562, 422]<|/det|>He has a car but prefers to travel by bus.
<|det|>text [58, 423, 841, 470]<|/det|>By using the coordinating conjunction but, we joined two sentences showing contrasting ideas.
<|det|>text [58, 491, 862, 536]<|/det|>A. Mark the clauses as main clause (MC), coordinate clause (CC) or subordinate clause (SC) in these sentences.
<|det|>list [128, 543, 585, 590]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [129, 543, 585, 589]<|/det|>1. Jagriti likes painting, but I prefer clay modelling.
<|det|>text [128, 590, 499, 613]<|/det|>2. If I like the sari, I shall buy it.
<|det|>text [128, 613, 625, 635]<|/det|>3. It was raining, so I took out an umbrella.
<|det|>text [128, 636, 653, 658]<|/det|>4. The plane will land after it gets permission.
<|det|>text [128, 659, 650, 681]<|/det|>5. He begged that he be allowed to go home.
<|det|>text [128, 681, 729, 703]<|/det|>6. She left yesterday and she has not returned since.
<|det|>text [128, 704, 711, 726]<|/det|>7. As you are unwell, you should not go to school.
<|det|>text [128, 726, 731, 748]<|/det|>8. The life of a mosquito is brief, but it is very active.
<|det|>text [128, 749, 701, 771]<|/det|>9. He took medicines that he might get well soon.
<|det|>text [115, 772, 711, 794]<|/det|>10. I am fond of chocolates, but I do not like toffees.
<|det|>text [115, 794, 789, 839]<|/det|>11. The book was written by someone who has a thorough knowledge of the subject.
<|det|>text [114, 839, 901, 883]<|/det|>12. The lady embroidered her handkerchief while she waited for her husband.
<|det|>image [668, 525, 936, 726]<|/det|>
