<PAGE><|det|>text [104, 28, 867, 121]<|/det|>You have learnt that a subordinate clause cannot stand on its own. It usually depends on the main clause to complete its meaning. There are three types of subordinate clauses. Let us learn more about them.
<|det|>title [104, 138, 332, 160]<|/det|>Adjective Clauses
<|det|>text [102, 168, 354, 189]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [103, 191, 638, 234]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [103, 191, 529, 213]<|/det|>• This is the book that I am reading.
<|det|>text [103, 215, 638, 236]<|/det|>- There is the woman who took my interview.
<|det|>text [100, 237, 951, 333]<|/det|>The highlighted clauses in these sentences are subordinate clauses that do the work of an adjective—they modify the nouns book and woman. The nouns book and woman are called antecedents to the relative pronouns that and who, respectively.
<|det|>text [99, 348, 858, 395]<|/det|>A subordinate clause that does the work of an adjective is called an adjective clause.
<|det|>text [99, 417, 620, 488]<|/det|>An adjective clause should be placed as close as possible to the noun or pronoun it modifies, ideally right next to it.
<|det|>text [99, 501, 616, 572]<|/det|>An adjective clause about persons begins with the relative pronouns who, whom or whose. These pronouns refer to the person in the main clause. For example,
<|det|>list [99, 574, 600, 641]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 574, 600, 595]<|/det|>• I have a penfriend who lives in New York.
<|det|>text [100, 598, 550, 619]<|/det|>• The artist whom I adored is no more.
<|det|>text [97, 635, 944, 707]<|/det|>An adjective clause about animals or things begins with which, that or those. These refer to the animals or things in the main clause. For example,
<|det|>list [99, 707, 618, 752]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 707, 590, 728]<|/det|>• This is the watch that my uncle gave me.
<|det|>text [100, 729, 618, 751]<|/det|>• This is the road which leads to the stadium.
<|det|>text [95, 765, 929, 833]<|/det|>The words when, where and why can also be used like relative pronouns to begin an adjective clause referring to a place, a time or a reason in the main clause. For example,
<|det|>list [98, 833, 661, 899]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 833, 661, 855]<|/det|>- Do you know the time when the movie begins?
<|det|>text [99, 856, 546, 875]<|/det|>• I went to the town where I was born.
<|det|>text [99, 878, 567, 899]<|/det|>- No one knows the reason why we lost.
<|det|>page_number [114, 920, 165, 938]<|/det|>174
<PAGE><|det|>title [53, 33, 885, 79]<|/det|>B. Underline the adjective clauses in these sentences and point out the noun or the pronoun they qualify.
<|det|>list [119, 80, 888, 405]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [120, 80, 509, 102]<|/det|>1. He laughs best who laughs last.
<|det|>text [120, 103, 512, 125]<|/det|>2. The reason why he failed is this.
<|det|>text [120, 126, 571, 148]<|/det|>3. The house that we lived in is in ruins.
<|det|>text [120, 150, 617, 173]<|/det|>4. The car that is red belongs to Mr Khanna.
<|det|>text [120, 175, 650, 197]<|/det|>5. The man who is at the desk is a senior clerk.
<|det|>text [120, 198, 701, 223]<|/det|>6. I did not understand the question that you asked.
<|det|>text [120, 224, 761, 247]<|/det|>7. I forgot to tell you the time when we will reach home.
<|det|>text [120, 248, 821, 271]<|/det|>8. The committee accepted every proposal that we submitted.
<|det|>text [120, 272, 854, 295]<|/det|>9. He travelled home by the route that his father had once taken.
<|det|>text [111, 296, 807, 341]<|/det|>10. There is a stray dog in our neighbourhood that follows me everywhere I go.
<|det|>text [111, 343, 888, 368]<|/det|>11. The rivers that flow down from the Himalayas are fed by glaciers.
<|det|>text [111, 368, 888, 413]<|/det|>12. There are many scientists who have made outstanding inventions that have changed our lives.
<|det|>title [53, 429, 867, 474]<|/det|>C. Join these sets of sentences by turning the second sentence into an adjective clause.
<|det|>list [111, 476, 761, 710]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [113, 476, 656, 498]<|/det|>1. He bought a plot of land. It is near the airport.
<|det|>text [111, 499, 761, 523]<|/det|>2. I like the Italian footballer. He scored the winning goal.
<|det|>text [111, 524, 619, 546]<|/det|>3. The mangoes are in the box. They are ripe.
<|det|>text [111, 547, 666, 569]<|/det|>4. I would like to visit the town. I was born there.
<|det|>text [111, 570, 790, 593]<|/det|>5. Burglars broke into a shop. The shop sells mobile phones.
<|det|>text [111, 594, 717, 640]<|/det|>6. The train was going to Delhi. Delhi is four hundred kilometres away.
<|det|>text [111, 641, 755, 665]<|/det|>7. My father had a big garden. He grew vegetables there.
<|det|>text [111, 665, 845, 711]<|/det|>8. The young girl wrote a beautiful poem. It was published in the school magazine.
<|det|>title [53, 727, 245, 748]<|/det|>Noun Clauses
<|det|>text [52, 757, 865, 804]<|/det|>A noun clause is a subordinate clause that does the work of a noun in a sentence.
<|det|>image [58, 820, 118, 864]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [140, 835, 383, 856]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [61, 871, 712, 941]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 871, 551, 893]<|/det|>• The teacher said that the earth is round.
<|det|>text [61, 895, 454, 915]<|/det|>- Richa asked me what I wanted.
<|det|>text [61, 917, 706, 941]<|/det|>• I am thinking whether I should go to the party or not.
<|det|>page_number [828, 945, 872, 964]<|/det|>175
<PAGE><|det|>text [115, 42, 948, 90]<|/det|>In these sentences, the clauses act as the object or the complement of the verb. Hence, they function like nouns.
<|det|>text [114, 103, 936, 148]<|/det|>A noun clause can function as the subject of a verb, the object of a verb, the object of a preposition or the subjective complement. For example,
<|det|>list [117, 149, 905, 288]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [117, 149, 585, 171]<|/det|>• What the child said was true. (subject)
<|det|>text [117, 172, 599, 193]<|/det|>• I wish to know how this works. (object)
<|det|>text [117, 194, 903, 240]<|/det|>- My health depends on how well I take care of myself. (object of a preposition)
<|det|>text [117, 240, 882, 288]<|/det|>- The climber's greatest fear was that he might not reach the peak. (subjective complement)
<|det|>text [113, 302, 946, 349]<|/det|>We can combine two independent sentences by changing one to a noun clause by using—
<|det|>text [114, 349, 898, 372]<|/det|>◆ pronouns like who, what, which, whom and whose. For example,
<|det|>text [160, 374, 433, 393]<|/det|>• I know who Karan is.
<|det|>text [114, 405, 710, 428]<|/det|>◆ conjunctions like that, if or whether. For example,
<|det|>text [160, 430, 619, 450]<|/det|>• I wonder if Kavita knows how to cook.
<|det|>text [114, 462, 757, 485]<|/det|>◆ adverbs like when, where, why and how. For example,
<|det|>text [160, 487, 489, 507]<|/det|>• I wonder where Mohan is.
<|det|>text [114, 531, 826, 578]<|/det|>D. Identify the noun clauses in these sentences and state their functions.
<|det|>list [180, 584, 655, 802]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [181, 584, 655, 607]<|/det|>1. That it might snow, seemed a possibility.
<|det|>text [181, 608, 521, 629]<|/det|>2. Ask if he will see me today.
<|det|>text [181, 631, 547, 653]<|/det|>3. Do you know what he wants?
<|det|>text [181, 654, 576, 676]<|/det|>4. My belief is that he will do well.
<|det|>text [181, 678, 624, 699]<|/det|>5. The doctor says that he will be there.
<|det|>text [181, 701, 498, 723]<|/det|>6. Tell me why you are late.
<|det|>text [181, 725, 622, 769]<|/det|>7. Everything now depends on what he decides.
<|det|>text [180, 772, 701, 794]<|/det|>8. The child believes that he has won the race.
<|det|>text [180, 797, 724, 819]<|/det|>9. When he is scheduled to arrive, is not known.
<|det|>text [170, 821, 781, 844]<|/det|>10. My desire is that I may never displease my parents.
<|det|>text [170, 845, 868, 869]<|/det|>11. The teacher guessed what the little child was trying to say.
<|det|>text [170, 869, 726, 893]<|/det|>12. What the soldier did was a true act of bravery.
<|det|>image [664, 560, 964, 755]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [140, 939, 186, 958]<|/det|>176
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 45, 885, 91]<|/det|>- Combine each set of sentences such that the subordinate clause is a noun clause.
<|det|>list [96, 96, 700, 277]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [96, 96, 518, 117]<|/det|>1. She will return soon. She said so.
<|det|>text [96, 118, 529, 139]<|/det|>2. When will the bus arrive? Tell me.
<|det|>text [96, 141, 672, 162]<|/det|>3. Tara is absent today. Do you know the reason?
<|det|>text [96, 163, 672, 185]<|/det|>4. The teacher will accept my apology. I know it.
<|det|>text [96, 186, 699, 207]<|/det|>5. You must work hard. Your success depends on it.
<|det|>text [96, 209, 643, 230]<|/det|>6. Einstein was a genius. Everyone knows this.
<|det|>text [96, 230, 783, 252]<|/det|>7. Malaria is spread by mosquitoes. Everybody knows this.
<|det|>text [96, 253, 790, 275]<|/det|>8. The trekkers reached the camp safely. This is good news.
<|det|>title [94, 296, 846, 343]<|/det|>Complete each of these main clauses by adding a suitable noun clause. The words given in the brackets will help you.
<|det|>list [111, 349, 914, 576]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [111, 350, 877, 371]<|/det|>1. I strongly believe (that) ____.
<|det|>text [111, 373, 821, 393]<|/det|>2. I asked her (what) ____.
<|det|>text [111, 395, 860, 415]<|/det|>3. It is not known (who) ____.
<|det|>text [111, 417, 895, 438]<|/det|>4. I know the reason (why) ____.
<|det|>text [111, 440, 912, 460]<|/det|>5. The police could not figure out (how) ____.
<|det|>text [111, 462, 912, 483]<|/det|>6. Everyone wondered (how) ____.
<|det|>text [111, 485, 912, 506]<|/det|>7. No one understood (why) ____.
<|det|>text [111, 508, 871, 529]<|/det|>8. No one can say (who) ____.
<|det|>text [111, 530, 876, 551]<|/det|>9. Nobody knew (where) ____.
<|det|>text [100, 553, 838, 574]<|/det|>10. I don't know (how) ____.
<|det|>title [47, 607, 262, 628]<|/det|>Adverb Clauses
<|det|>text [45, 643, 905, 714]<|/det|>An adverb clause is a subordinate clause that does the work of an adverb in a sentence. It modifies the meaning of a verb, an adjective or another adverb in the main clause.
<|det|>text [48, 731, 920, 802]<|/det|>7. Adverb clauses show relationships such as time, cause and effect, contrast and condition. Therefore, adverb clauses are classified into the following types.
<|det|>text [48, 801, 920, 869]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of time are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions while, after, when, before, since, whenever, till, as long as, as soon as. For example,
<|det|>text [98, 869, 576, 889]<|/det|>• We must reach before the bus leaves.
<|det|>page_number [867, 905, 910, 925]<|/det|>177
<PAGE><|det|>text [105, 35, 848, 84]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of place are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions where, wherein, wherever. For example,
<|det|>text [148, 84, 618, 105]<|/det|>• Keep the watch where it will be safe.
<|det|>text [105, 116, 881, 163]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of purpose are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions that, so that, in order that, lest. For example,
<|det|>text [148, 165, 603, 188]<|/det|>• We eat so that we may grow strong.
<|det|>text [105, 198, 862, 247]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of reason are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions since, because, as. For example,
<|det|>text [148, 248, 717, 270]<|/det|>• I must leave now because I am feeling unwell.
<|det|>text [105, 280, 938, 354]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of condition are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions if, whether, unless, in case, so long as, provided that. For example,
<|det|>text [148, 354, 571, 377]<|/det|>• If you like it, I shall get it for you.
<|det|>text [105, 387, 974, 435]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of result are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions that, which may be preceded by so or such. For example,
<|det|>text [148, 435, 558, 457]<|/det|>- It was so hot that the plant died.
<|det|>text [105, 468, 928, 515]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of comparison are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions than and as...as. For example,
<|det|>text [148, 516, 540, 538]<|/det|>• The boy is taller than his sister.
<|det|>text [105, 549, 911, 619]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of concession are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions though, although, as, notwithstanding that, even if, however. For example,
<|det|>text [145, 620, 938, 642]<|/det|>• Though I am not wealthy, I will still be a successful businessman.
<|det|>text [105, 653, 862, 701]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of manner are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions like, as, as if, as though, just as. For example,
<|det|>text [145, 701, 648, 723]<|/det|>• She was walking as if she was in a hurry.
<|det|>text [105, 734, 701, 803]<|/det|>◆ Adverb clauses of contrast are introduced by the subordinating conjunctions while and whereas. For example,
<|det|>text [145, 804, 698, 845]<|/det|>• I was reading while my brother was watching television.
<|det|>image [710, 728, 960, 848]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [120, 933, 172, 952]<|/det|>178
<PAGE><|det|>image [17, 51, 77, 83]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [105, 47, 880, 93]<|/det|>Pick out the adverb clauses in these sentences and state what kind each is.
<|det|>list [122, 99, 890, 430]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [123, 100, 461, 122]<|/det|>1. Please do as you deem fit.
<|det|>text [123, 127, 570, 151]<|/det|>2. I played while others were sleeping.
<|det|>text [123, 155, 606, 179]<|/det|>3. The daughter is as pretty as her mother.
<|det|>text [123, 183, 633, 207]<|/det|>4. If you ever go Delhi, do visit the Red Fort.
<|det|>text [124, 211, 615, 235]<|/det|>5. Put the money where you kept it before.
<|det|>text [124, 239, 710, 262]<|/det|>6. The man ran fast lest he should miss the last bus.
<|det|>text [125, 267, 734, 292]<|/det|>7. I shall buy this book provided I get it at a discount.
<|det|>text [126, 295, 672, 319]<|/det|>8. Although I do not know him well, I trust him.
<|det|>text [126, 323, 785, 348]<|/det|>9. As he was in a hurry, he could not stay for the meeting.
<|det|>text [117, 352, 783, 375]<|/det|>10. The sailors refused to sail since the weather was rough.
<|det|>text [117, 380, 875, 403]<|/det|>11. The teacher spoke so fast that I could not take down any notes.
<|det|>text [117, 407, 882, 431]<|/det|>12. The boy worked diligently because he wished to finish the task.
<|det|>text [55, 457, 905, 504]<|/det|>H. Combine each set of sentences with an adverb clause as indicated in the brackets.
<|det|>list [128, 509, 890, 854]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 510, 815, 556]<|/det|>1. The little boy was crying. He had broken his favourite toy. (reason)
<|det|>text [129, 560, 783, 584]<|/det|>2. Stand up straight. I can measure your height. (purpose)
<|det|>text [130, 588, 767, 611]<|/det|>3. Roma felt unwell. Roma went to see a doctor. (result)
<|det|>text [129, 616, 865, 640]<|/det|>4. The squirrel was collecting nuts. I watched the squirrel. (time)
<|det|>text [130, 644, 706, 668]<|/det|>5. It was extremely sultry. I felt dehydrated. (result)
<|det|>text [129, 672, 815, 695]<|/det|>6. It may start raining. We will then stop driving. (condition)
<|det|>text [129, 699, 846, 748]<|/det|>7. The men cut down the tree. They wanted to dig a well there. (purpose)
<|det|>text [129, 751, 741, 775]<|/det|>8. Keep the document. Keep it in a safe place. (place)
<|det|>text [128, 779, 890, 802]<|/det|>9. I am not an athlete. I will still compete in the race. (concession)
<|det|>text [117, 806, 895, 852]<|/det|>10. The children refused to leave the park. They were having a good time. (reason)
<|det|>page_number [843, 939, 890, 958]<|/det|>179
<PAGE><|det|>title [113, 33, 951, 79]<|/det|>1. Combine each set of sentences such that the subordinate clause is an adverb clause.
<|det|>list [159, 86, 682, 311]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 86, 490, 107]<|/det|>1. I shall return. Let it snow.
<|det|>text [159, 111, 611, 133]<|/det|>2. I had left for school. She came later.
<|det|>text [159, 136, 682, 158]<|/det|>3. He cried. It seemed his heart would break.
<|det|>text [159, 163, 657, 186]<|/det|>4. My father is old. He enjoys good health.
<|det|>text [159, 189, 706, 211]<|/det|>5. They made so much noise. I could not study.
<|det|>text [159, 214, 710, 237]<|/det|>6. The thief saw two men approaching. He fled.
<|det|>text [159, 240, 755, 262]<|/det|>7. He was fined. He had kept the book for too long.
<|det|>text [159, 265, 612, 310]<|/det|>8. The police advanced at great speed.
The terrorists fled.
<|det|>image [769, 75, 955, 270]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [157, 336, 908, 405]<|/det|>10. Identify the subordinate clauses in these sentences and state the kind and function of each clause. (There may be more than one subordinate clause.)
<|det|>list [169, 409, 958, 885]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 412, 953, 457]<|/det|>1. When I reached the base camp, I found that the rest of the party had already arrived.
<|det|>text [172, 458, 925, 504]<|/det|>2. Although I like watching movies, I am not very fond of horror films as they leave me feeling tense.
<|det|>text [173, 505, 951, 551]<|/det|>3. After making the necessary purchases at the mall, the group left for the airport that was located at the other end of the city.
<|det|>text [173, 552, 878, 597]<|/det|>4. As I was walking in the garden, I saw an old man who was accompanied by his grandchild.
<|det|>text [173, 598, 881, 667]<|/det|>5. The tigers were ambling through the undergrowth where there was very little light which made it impossible for the photographers to take a good shot.
<|det|>text [172, 668, 912, 714]<|/det|>6. If we could accept the fact that we are imperfect beings, we would be happier.
<|det|>text [172, 715, 938, 758]<|/det|>7. I kept walking across the desert until I came to an oasis where I took rest for a while.
<|det|>text [171, 761, 962, 809]<|/det|>8. If I could remember all the details of my many journeys, I could write a book that would be interesting.
<|det|>text [171, 810, 925, 854]<|/det|>9. I was travelling in the Himalayas when I met a man who had once been a civil servant.
<|det|>text [160, 857, 918, 881]<|/det|>10. I hid the treasure in a large box that was kept under the bed.
<|det|>page_number [127, 925, 177, 946]<|/det|>180
<PAGE><|det|>text [98, 22, 882, 69]<|/det|>11. While the orator made his speech, I looked around for a suitable spot where I could place my camera stand.
<|det|>text [98, 69, 858, 140]<|/det|>12. I perceived that a large crowd had gathered near the field next to the local club that had organised a drawing competition for children who were under the age of ten.
<|det|>list [98, 22, 882, 140]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [284, 174, 640, 771]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [769, 75, 955, 270]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [836, 933, 876, 951]<|/det|>181
<PAGE><|det|>header [19, 0, 220, 136]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [601, 37, 955, 70]<|/det|>Formal Debate
<|det|>text [102, 147, 940, 263]<|/det|>A debate is a persuasive speech. The objective is to influence or change someone's mind or way of thinking about a topic. It is a serious discussion or an argument about a subject on which people have different views or beliefs. The speaker speaks either for the motion or against the motion.
<|det|>text [102, 275, 764, 299]<|/det|>You should organise your debate along the following lines.
<|det|>list [103, 300, 960, 555]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [103, 300, 818, 342]<|/det|>- Reveal your stand in a dramatic or interesting manner in the introduction.
<|det|>text [103, 344, 860, 390]<|/det|>- Your arguments should be addressed to the Chairperson, who is chairing the debate.
<|det|>text [103, 391, 960, 437]<|/det|>- Present effective arguments; provide statistical data, concrete facts, and incidents to support your arguments.
<|det|>text [103, 438, 935, 506]<|/det|>- Anticipate the arguments that may be presented by your opponent speaker and develop methods to analyse them, in order to rebut your opponent's arguments.
<|det|>text [103, 506, 915, 554]<|/det|>- In the last paragraph, clarify your stand in an effective manner once again.
<|det|>title [130, 575, 192, 600]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [124, 612, 905, 698]<|/det|>You should understand that how you say something and how you physically present yourself is just as important as what you say. By understanding the dynamics involved in effective persuasive speaking, you will improve your overall confidence in communicating. You must speak articulately and eloquently.
<|det|>title [102, 727, 955, 751]<|/det|>Read this example of a debate. The speech has been made for the motion.
<|det|>title [254, 766, 791, 790]<|/det|>Should Corporal Punishment Be Banned?
<|det|>text [99, 797, 955, 893]<|/det|>Respected Principal, respected teachers and dear friends, this, as you know is a very current topic and we are all concerned, especially after the media has highlighted certain facts and figures. In short, it has given us an opportunity to examine our notions of corporal punishment.
<|det|>page_number [124, 922, 171, 943]<|/det|>182
<PAGE><|det|>text [65, 35, 915, 106]<|/det|>I wish to speak for the motion because today the saying—'spare the rod and spoil the child' is too outmoded. Everything changes with time and so should these obsolete sayings.
<|det|>text [64, 120, 907, 237]<|/det|>Everyone present here would agree that any form of corporal punishment is violent and humiliating. Parents and teachers have, since centuries, used punishment to discipline or reform children. Some people believe that corporal punishment is an effective deterrent in the classroom, and is essential to discipline students, as they are the future of the country.
<|det|>text [65, 250, 885, 346]<|/det|>Nobody can or will deny the importance of discipline, but there should be alternative ways of enforcing discipline. Beating and spanking little children will hardly help them become remorseful, whereas talking to them or counselling them might just be a better idea.
<|det|>text [66, 359, 892, 475]<|/det|>A very naughty boy who is always ready with a prank, say by throwing 'stink bombs' behind teachers, may be given a chart paper and could be asked to express his thoughts on paper. Perhaps, a psychiatrist could tell us why he does what he does, and help him. After all, a child is a child and always needs attention.
<|det|>text [67, 497, 911, 543]<|/det|>Now write debate speeches for these situations. You may choose to write for or against the motion.
<|det|>list [68, 549, 781, 641]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [70, 549, 625, 571]<|/det|>1. Impact of Television on the Lives of Teenagers
<|det|>text [70, 572, 570, 593]<|/det|>2. Impact of the Internet on Young Children
<|det|>text [70, 594, 583, 615]<|/det|>3. Should We Explore Life on Other Planets?
<|det|>text [70, 616, 778, 639]<|/det|>4. Should Testing of Life Saving Drugs on Animals Be Banned?
<|det|>image [248, 660, 737, 879]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [848, 920, 895, 938]<|/det|>183
<PAGE><|det|>title [106, 44, 177, 83]<|/det|>22
<|det|>title [402, 47, 948, 128]<|/det|>Simple, Compound and Complex Sentences
<|det|>image [100, 147, 160, 196]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [174, 149, 890, 193]<|/det|>You have already learnt that a sentence is a group of words that makes absolute sense.
<|det|>text [97, 207, 948, 252]<|/det|>Sentences can be categorised into three types, based on their grammatical structure. These are—
<|det|>list [99, 254, 368, 321]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 254, 322, 275]<|/det|>◆ simple sentence
<|det|>text [100, 277, 365, 298]<|/det|>◆ compound sentence
<|det|>text [100, 300, 343, 321]<|/det|>◆ complex sentence
<|det|>title [98, 332, 327, 354]<|/det|>Simple Sentences
<|det|>text [98, 362, 345, 382]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [99, 385, 631, 428]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 385, 363, 407]<|/det|>• Seema is a nice girl.
<|det|>text [100, 408, 631, 429]<|/det|>• The captain of the cricket team has fallen ill.
<|det|>text [97, 430, 944, 476]<|/det|>Each of these sentences has only one clause. We can say so because each has a single subject and a single finite verb.
<|det|>text [97, 491, 931, 536]<|/det|>A sentence having only one clause, and therefore, only one finite verb, is called a simple sentence.
<|det|>title [99, 562, 381, 584]<|/det|>Compound Sentences
<|det|>text [99, 592, 348, 612]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [99, 614, 727, 660]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [100, 614, 664, 637]<|/det|>• He looked at the message and began to laugh.
<|det|>text [100, 638, 727, 660]<|/det|>• I was disappointed but I did not express my feelings.
<|det|>text [97, 660, 876, 731]<|/det|>The highlighted parts are independent clauses and can stand on their own. These independent clauses have been joined by coordinating conjunctions like and and but.
<|det|>text [96, 744, 880, 791]<|/det|>A sentence containing two main clauses that are joined together by a coordinating conjunction is called a compound sentence.
<|det|>text [95, 813, 574, 836]<|/det|>Compound sentences can be formed by—
<|det|>list [94, 836, 858, 907]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [94, 836, 858, 884]<|/det|>◆ adding one statement to another with conjunctions such as and, both...and, etc. For example,
<|det|>text [134, 884, 609, 907]<|/det|>• I got into the car and went to the bank.
<|det|>page_number [120, 928, 165, 946]<|/det|>184
<PAGE><|det|>list [54, 6, 905, 335]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [54, 6, 901, 52]<|/det|>- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
<|det|>text [54, 52, 901, 99]<|/det|>- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
<|det|>text[54, 99, 901, 146]<|/det|>- Either you come to see me or I will drop in at your place. (suggests positive choice)
<|det|>text [54, 146, 888, 193]<|/det|>- It is neither possible nor desirable that they should take a month's leave. (suggests negative choice)
<|det|>text [54, 193, 895, 240]<|/det|>- adding two statements indicating contrast, using conjunctions such as but, still, yet, only. For example,
<|det|>list [54, 99, 901, 240]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [54, 240, 717, 264]<|/det|>• She wanted to buy a dress but she did not like any.
<|det|>text [54, 264, 890, 311]<|/det|>- Nadal was suffering from a knee injury, yet he won the match. (to state a contrast of a surprising nature)
<|det|>text [54, 321, 848, 365]<|/det|>● using conjunctions like so, therefore, as a result, for that reason, to suggest that one statement stands as a consequence of another.
<|det|>text [54, 365, 245, 388]<|/det|>For example,
<|det|>text [54, 388, 833, 410]<|/det|>• He did not report on time, so he was dropped from the team.
<|det|>title [54, 429, 316, 453]<|/det|>Complex Sentences
<|det|>text [54, 460, 842, 483]<|/det|>Now, read these sentences. The main clauses have been highlighted.
<|det|>text [54, 483, 543, 505]<|/det|>- When the train arrives, I shall call you.
<|det|>text [54, 505, 570, 528]<|/det|>• I left the house when my mother arrived.
<|det|>list [54, 483, 570, 528]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [54, 528, 890, 582]<|/det|>Each of these sentences consists of a main clause and a subordinate clause. The subordinate clause is dependent on the main clause to make its meaning clear.
<|det|>text [54, 604, 878, 653]<|/det|>A sentence having one main clause and at least one subordinate clause is called a complex sentence.
<|det|>text [51, 679, 838, 775]<|/det|>In a complex sentence, the subordinate clause is joined to the main clause by a subordinating conjunction. That, if, when, where, before, although, since, after, because, as are some subordinating conjunctions.
<|det|>text [50, 790, 908, 863]<|/det|>Words such as who, which, that are used to form adjective clauses. These words do the work of a relative pronoun and function as a conjunction in a sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [54, 864, 635, 890]<|/det|>- I met Dora. Dora has just joined our company.
<|det|>page_number [851, 928, 898, 948]<|/det|>185
<PAGE><|det|>text [100, 32, 540, 54]<|/det|>Now, let us the join the two sentences.
<|det|>text [101, 56, 651, 78]<|/det|>• I met Dora who has just joined our company.
<|det|>text [99, 78, 944, 122]<|/det|>Who is used instead of Dora. So, it does the work of a pronoun. Who also joins the two statements, so it does the work of a conjunction.
<|det|>text [99, 143, 936, 210]<|/det|>A. Take a look at these sentences and identify the main clause and the subordinate clause. There may be more than one subordinate clause in a few sentences.
<|det|>list [160, 215, 954, 500]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [165, 218, 637, 241]<|/det|>1. He said that he wanted to be a lawyer.
<|det|>text [164, 242, 621, 263]<|/det|>2. That he is a criminal, is known to all.
<|det|>text [164, 264, 901, 310]<|/det|>3. The monument that was built by the last emperor needs to be repaired.
<|det|>text [163, 310, 951, 333]<|/det|>4. The car broke down near the building that stands at the crossroads.
<|det|>text [165, 334, 710, 356]<|/det|>5. I will meet you after I come back from Delhi.
<|det|>text [165, 357, 583, 379]<|/det|>6. Iqbal worked while Deepak slept.
<|det|>text [165, 379, 671, 401]<|/det|>7. She found her purse where she had left it.
<|det|>text [164, 402, 907, 425]<|/det|>8. Since it was late, we did not go to the shop that sells antiques.
<|det|>text [165, 426, 799, 448]<|/det|>9. He is the most eloquent speaker that I have ever met.
<|det|>text [154, 448, 895, 470]<|/det|>10. I hope that he will get well soon as I have plans for his future.
<|det|>text [154, 471, 830, 493]<|/det|>11. I stopped going to the gym as I have sprained my ankle.
<|det|>text [154, 493, 816, 539]<|/det|>12. When I return, I will set up a firm that will do business with Europe.
<|det|>text [99, 562, 907, 606]<|/det|>B. State whether these sentences are simple, complex or compound. Underline the finite verbs.
<|det|>list [159, 614, 951, 921]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [160, 614, 520, 637]<|/det|>1. I know what your plans are.
<|det|>text [160, 638, 550, 661]<|/det|>2. She must weep or she will die.
<|det|>text [160, 662, 747, 685]<|/det|>3. After I had taken a shower, I lay down for a nap.
<|det|>text [160, 686, 687, 708]<|/det|>4. You can either come with us or take a taxi.
<|det|>text [160, 709, 890, 755]<|/det|>5. Walking through the woods on a wintry morning, I spotted a musk deer lurking behind a towering pine.
<|det|>text [159, 756, 951, 780]<|/det|>6. John Keats, the noted Romantic poet, composed 'Ode to Autumn'.
<|det|>text [160, 781, 624, 803]<|/det|>7. Either accept my proposal or leave it.
<|det|>text [159, 804, 907, 827]<|/det|>8. Colin agreed to meet me at the coffee shop near the museum.
<|det|>text [159, 828, 885, 871]<|/det|>9. After the rain stopped, we went to the zoo where we ate ice creams.
<|det|>text [150, 874, 861, 921]<|/det|>10. How the prisoner escaped from the police station is still a mystery that I would like solved.
<|det|>page_number [119, 920, 165, 938]<|/det|>186
<PAGE><|det|>text [117, 35, 874, 79]<|/det|>11. The best painting that I have seen is a work by a talented young lady who used to live in Dehradun.
<|det|>text [118, 80, 890, 104]<|/det|>12. Since you are so unhappy, you should look for another employer.
<|det|>text [119, 104, 837, 148]<|/det|>13. The old man walked to the post office where he was greeted cheerfully by the peons who had grown to love him.
<|det|>text [119, 148, 890, 193]<|/det|>14. Priya ordered lunch as it was getting late for the meeting that was to be held at the new office building.
<|det|>text [119, 194, 840, 239]<|/det|>15. Finding the principal's office closed, I walked casually in the direction of the rector's office on the first floor of the annexe.
<|det|>text [119, 240, 880, 286]<|/det|>16. When you have finished typing, you should file the report as we need to complete the project by Monday.
<|det|>text [119, 286, 901, 354]<|/det|>17. When the sun set, the travellers decided to set up camp for the night near a river that was flowing down from the mountain peaks in the distance.
<|det|>text [119, 355, 903, 404]<|/det|>18. Searching through the box, I found a moth-eaten bundle of yellow sheets of paper.
<|det|>list [117, 35, 903, 404]<|/det|>
<|det|>image [298, 478, 741, 810]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [845, 930, 890, 948]<|/det|>187
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 281, 134]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [525, 40, 953, 74]<|/det|>Roads Go Ever On
<|det|>text [151, 134, 421, 156]<|/det|>Roads go ever even or,
<|det|>text [154, 157, 460, 178]<|/det|>Over rock and under tree,
<|det|>text [154, 180, 586, 201]<|/det|>By caves where never sun has shone,
<|det|>text [154, 203, 553, 224]<|/det|>By streams that never find the sea;
<|det|>text [154, 226, 477, 247]<|/det|>Over snow by winter sown,
<|det|>text [154, 249, 605, 271]<|/det|>And through the merry flowers of June,
<|det|>text [154, 273, 465, 294]<|/det|>Over grass and over stone,
<|det|>text [154, 296, 563, 316]<|/det|>And under mountains in the moon.
<|det|>text [151, 335, 421, 357]<|/det|>Roads go ever ever on,
<|det|>text [154, 358, 484, 377]<|/det|>Under cloud and under star.
<|det|>text [154, 380, 545, 402]<|/det|>Yet feet that wandering have gone
<|det|>text [154, 404, 441, 423]<|/det|>Turn at last to home afar.
<|det|>text [154, 426, 556, 447]<|/det|>Eyes that fire and sword have seen,
<|det|>text [154, 449, 515, 469]<|/det|>And horror in the halls of stone
<|det|>text [154, 472, 519, 493]<|/det|>Look at last on meadows green,
<|det|>text [154, 495, 631, 516]<|/det|>And trees and hills they long have known
<|det|>text [151, 532, 506, 554]<|/det|>The Road goes ever on and on
<|det|>text [154, 556, 546, 577]<|/det|>Out from the door where it began.
<|det|>text [154, 579, 548, 599]<|/det|>Now far ahead the Road has gone.
<|det|>text [154, 601, 490, 623]<|/det|>Let others follow, if they can!
<|det|>text [154, 625, 503, 646]<|/det|>Let them a journey new begin.
<|det|>text [154, 648, 468, 669]<|/det|>But I at last with weary feet
<|det|>text [154, 671, 536, 693]<|/det|>Will turn towards the lighted inn,
<|det|>text [154, 695, 550, 716]<|/det|>My evening-rest and sleep to meet
<|det|>text [148, 732, 585, 755]<|/det|>Still 'round the corner there may wait
<|det|>text [148, 757, 457, 779]<|/det|>A new road or secret gate;
<|det|>text [148, 780, 590, 803]<|/det|>And though I oft have passed them by,
<|det|>text [148, 805, 495, 825]<|/det|>A day will come at last when I
<|det|>text [148, 827, 563, 850]<|/det|>Shall take the hidden paths that run
<|det|>text [148, 851, 545, 873]<|/det|>West of the Moon, East of the Sun.
<|det|>image [587, 198, 880, 873]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [98, 926, 138, 944]<|/det|>186
<PAGE><|det|>text [100, 16, 411, 41]<|/det|>A Answer these questions.
<|det|>list [140, 45, 926, 190]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [141, 45, 923, 96]<|/det|>1. What does the word 'road' refer to in the poem? Give a brief description of where it can take you based on the first two stanzas.
<|det|>text [141, 96, 874, 119]<|/det|>2. How will you connect the old and the new through the roads?
<|det|>text [142, 119, 747, 141]<|/det|>3. What does the poet do when he is weary and why?
<|det|>text [141, 141, 895, 189]<|/det|>4. Which is the hidden path that the poet is speaking of and where does it go?
<|det|>text [95, 211, 845, 237]<|/det|>B. Complete these sentences and rewrite them in your notebook.
<|det|>list [141, 240, 761, 345]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [147, 243, 600, 265]<|/det|>1. The poet is waiting to ____.
<|det|>text [147, 266, 657, 288]<|/det|>2. Wandering feet turn at last ____.
<|det|>text [148, 289, 757, 335]<|/det|>3. Travellers return to green meadows and known hills after ____.
<|det|>text [148, 337, 736, 361]<|/det|>4. In summer, the road goes through ____.
<|det|>text [150, 362, 631, 383]<|/det|>5. Around the corner waits ____.
<|det|>text [95, 407, 820, 432]<|/det|>C. Find suitable words from the poem for these words/phrases.
<|det|>list [141, 435, 720, 582]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [150, 439, 301, 460]<|/det|>1. exhausted
<|det|>text [150, 463, 325, 484]<|/det|>2. extreme fear
<|det|>text [151, 487, 301, 509]<|/det|>3. frequently
<|det|>text [150, 510, 717, 533]<|/det|>4. an establishment that provides lodging and food
<|det|>text [150, 534, 607, 556]<|/det|>5. something that is hidden or concealed
<|det|>text [150, 557, 595, 580]<|/det|>6. roaming, moving from place to place
<|det|>page_number [867, 928, 913, 946]<|/det|>189
<PAGE><|det|>title [88, 30, 168, 72]<|/det|>23
<|det|>title [405, 35, 930, 74]<|/det|>Synthesis of Sentences
<|det|>text [84, 135, 890, 203]<|/det|>Synthesis is the process by which a number of simple sentences are combined to form a new sentence which may be simple, compound or complex.
<|det|>title [84, 238, 842, 262]<|/det|>Combining Simple Sentences to Form One Simple Sentence
<|det|>image [85, 268, 139, 315]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [154, 268, 890, 313]<|/det|>Two or more simple sentences can be combined to form another simple sentence—
<|det|>list [85, 316, 613, 430]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 316, 516, 339]<|/det|>◆ by using a participle. For example,
<|det|>text [121, 339, 534, 384]<|/det|>- Girish completed his homework. He went to play.
<|det|>text [121, 384, 613, 430]<|/det|>- Having completed his homework, Girish went to play.
<|det|>text [651, 304, 713, 330]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [768, 300, 811, 333]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [645, 342, 911, 402]<|/det|>The present participle is used when the simple sentences have the same subject.
<|det|>text [84, 440, 521, 464]<|/det|>◆ by using an infinitive. For example,
<|det|>list [121, 464, 740, 533]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 464, 740, 486]<|/det|>• I shall go to my old school. I will meet my teachers.
<|det|>text [121, 486, 558, 533]<|/det|>- I shall go to my old school to meet my teachers.
<|det|>text [84, 543, 470, 590]<|/det|>◆ by using a noun or a phrase in apposition. For example,
<|det|>list [121, 590, 580, 686]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 590, 580, 636]<|/det|>- Nagasaki was once a heap of rubble. It is now a bustling city.
<|det|>text [154, 636, 521, 686]<|/det|>Nagasaki, once a heap of ruins, is now a bustling city.
<|det|>text [85, 694, 798, 719]<|/det|>◆ by using prepositions for/by + gerund + noun. For example,
<|det|>list [119, 719, 815, 861]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [119, 719, 812, 766]<|/det|>- He received the bravery award. He saved his brother from drowning.
<|det|>text [154, 765, 816, 813]<|/det|>He received the bravery award for saving his brother from drowning.
<|det|>text [119, 813, 571, 861]<|/det|>- He sells clothes. He earns his living. He earns his living by selling clothes.
<|det|>text [651, 303, 713, 330]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>image [765, 301, 811, 333]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [647, 342, 907, 402]<|/det|>The present participle is used when the simple sentences have the same subject.
<|det|>text [607, 522, 662, 546]<|/det|>Note
<|det|>text [594, 558, 914, 661]<|/det|>A phrase in apposition describes the noun before it. The word or phrase in apposition is marked off from the thing to which it refers by a pair of commas.
<|det|>page_number [100, 927, 147, 946]<|/det|>190
<PAGE><|det|>text [71, 20, 452, 46]<|/det|>by using adverbs. For example,
<|det|>text [111, 46, 603, 68]<|/det|>- I reached the spot. I reached in an instant.
<|det|>text [128, 69, 441, 93]<|/det|>I reached the spot instantly.
<|det|>text [70, 102, 759, 127]<|/det|>by using too + adjective/adverb + infinitive. For example,
<|det|>text [111, 128, 548, 152]<|/det|>- The box is very heavy. I can't carry it.
<|det|>text [111, 152, 545, 175]<|/det|>- The box is too heavy for me to carry.
<|det|>list [111, 128, 548, 175]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [67, 199, 905, 223]<|/det|>A. Rewrite each of the sets of simple sentences as one simple sentence.
<|det|>list [128, 230, 795, 655]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [130, 230, 576, 254]<|/det|>1. I received no answer. I called again.
<|det|>text [130, 255, 599, 279]<|/det|>2. He hurt his hand. He stopped writing.
<|det|>text [130, 279, 612, 302]<|/det|>3. The boy broke a window. He ran away.
<|det|>text [130, 302, 655, 325]<|/det|>4. Shashank is very busy. He can't work hard.
<|det|>text [130, 326, 657, 348]<|/det|>5. John played chess well. He won the match.
<|det|>text [130, 349, 655, 371]<|/det|>6. The farmer lit a candle. He wanted to read.
<|det|>text [130, 373, 687, 396]<|/det|>7. I lost my passport. I could not go to Sri Lanka.
<|det|>text [130, 397, 667, 420]<|/det|>8. He did all the work. He did it with sincerity.
<|det|>text [130, 421, 691, 443]<|/det|>9. The boy could not come to school. He was ill.
<|det|>text [124, 444, 729, 466]<|/det|>10. Gagan works hard. He has to educate his brother.
<|det|>text [124, 467, 795, 491]<|/det|>11. Tagore was born in 1861. He was a world famous poet.
<|det|>text [124, 491, 714, 515]<|/det|>12. The farmers are very poor. They can't buy grains.
<|det|>text [124, 515, 781, 538]<|/det|>13. The priest arrived at noon. He inaugurated the festival.
<|det|>text [124, 538, 795, 561]<|/det|>14. The drowning boy shouted for help. He shouted loudly.
<|det|>text [124, 561, 841, 584]<|/det|>15. Salim went to Mumbai. He wanted to join the film industry.
<|det|>text [124, 585, 815, 607]<|/det|>16. Manish was very sad. He heard the news of the accident.
<|det|>text [124, 608, 895, 631]<|/det|>17. The young prince won the battle. He came back to his kingdom.
<|det|>text [124, 632, 787, 655]<|/det|>18. The stag ran swiftly. It wanted to escape from the lions.
<|det|>image [238, 671, 745, 905]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [845, 935, 890, 954]<|/det|>191
<PAGE><|det|>title [98, 27, 893, 51]<|/det|>Combining Simple Sentences to Form a Compound Sentence
<|det|>image [101, 58, 157, 107]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [96, 58, 951, 148]<|/det|>Two or more simple sentences can be combined to form a single compound sentence by using coordinating conjunctions like and, not only...but also, as well as, but, yet, nevertheless, however, or, so, for, either...or, neither...nor, otherwise, therefore. For example,
<|det|>list [98, 150, 740, 341]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [98, 150, 517, 171]<|/det|>• He is a teacher. He is an athlete.
<|det|>text [134, 173, 643, 195]<|/det|>He is not only a teacher but also an athlete.
<|det|>text [98, 196, 706, 219]<|/det|>- It is snowing. The ceremony will be held on time.
<|det|>text [134, 220, 740, 242]<|/det|>It is snowing but the ceremony will be held on time.
<|det|>text [98, 244, 540, 265]<|/det|>• He is not a fool. He is not a cheat.
<|det|>text [134, 267, 513, 288]<|/det|>He is neither a fool nor a cheat.
<|det|>text [98, 290, 701, 313]<|/det|>• He did not return the library book. He was fined.
<|det|>text [134, 313, 817, 338]<|/det|>He did not return the library book, therefore, he was fined.
<|det|>title [94, 360, 860, 406]<|/det|>B. Join each set of simple sentences to form a single compound sentence.
<|det|>list [160, 414, 970, 895]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [161, 414, 563, 436]<|/det|>1. Come inside. Have some food.
<|det|>text [161, 439, 588, 461]<|/det|>2. We were strong. We were active.
<|det|>text [161, 462, 658, 485]<|/det|>3. Do not be too early. Do not be too late.
<|det|>text [161, 486, 636, 509]<|/det|>4. He is a good man. He is very helpful.
<|det|>text [161, 510, 663, 533]<|/det|>5. The room was dark. The room was dirty.
<|det|>text [161, 534, 651, 556]<|/det|>6. He is an intelligent boy. He is not vain.
<|det|>text [161, 557, 698, 580]<|/det|>7. I do not like oranges. I do not like peaches.
<|det|>text [161, 581, 714, 604]<|/det|>8. A boat was brought. The goods were loaded.
<|det|>text [161, 605, 711, 628]<|/det|>9. The boy is not lazy. The boy is not insincere.
<|det|>text [151, 628, 836, 653]<|/det|>10. The passengers were delayed. They were not unhappy.
<|det|>text [151, 653, 845, 676]<|/det|>11. I will work hard in school. I will not give up swimming.
<|det|>text [151, 676, 878, 700]<|/det|>12. He did not perform well. He was dropped from the squad.
<|det|>text [151, 700, 928, 724]<|/det|>13. The doctor returned from England. He set up a nursing home.
<|det|>text [151, 724, 895, 747]<|/det|>14. The elders of the family were tired. The children were tired.
<|det|>text [151, 748, 867, 772]<|/det|>15. The store caught fire. Nothing in the store was destroyed.
<|det|>text [151, 772, 968, 797]<|/det|>16. The villagers sowed the seeds on time. The rains washed them away.
<|det|>text [151, 797, 858, 842]<|/det|>17. I like long walks in the mountains. I do not like it during the rains.
<|det|>text [151, 844, 940, 893]<|/det|>18. It was difficult for me to remain calm. I wanted to return home immediately.
<|det|>page_number [120, 939, 168, 958]<|/det|>192
<PAGE><|det|>text [73, 20, 842, 48]<|/det|>combining Simple Sentences to Form a Complex Sentence
<|det|>text [70, 52, 911, 165]<|/det|>Now have learnt that a complex sentence contains one principal clause and one or more subordinate clause. Two or more simple sentences can be combined to form a complex sentence by changing one simple sentence into the main clause and converting the others into subordinate clauses. This can be done—
<|det|>text [70, 168, 542, 192]<|/det|>◆ by using a noun clause. For example,
<|det|>list [108, 192, 805, 285]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 192, 684, 237]<|/det|>• He will make an excellent teacher. I believe it. I believe that he will make an excellent teacher.
<|det|>text [108, 240, 805, 287]<|/det|>- You are the captain of your team. Always remember this. Always remember that you are the captain of your team.
<|det|>text [70, 298, 600, 323]<|/det|>◆ by using an adjective clause. For example,
<|det|>list [108, 323, 774, 417]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 323, 774, 368]<|/det|>- The girl lives in Delhi. She has just joined the college. The girl who has just joined the college lives in Delhi.
<|det|>text [108, 369, 613, 416]<|/det|>- Bring me the bag. It is on the top rack. Bring me the bag that is on the top rack.
<|det|>text [70, 427, 574, 452]<|/det|>◆ by using an adverb clause. For example,
<|det|>list [108, 452, 667, 544]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [108, 452, 607, 473]<|/det|>• The boys won the match. They rejoiced.
<|det|>text [144, 474, 667, 496]<|/det|>The boys rejoiced when they won the match.
<|det|>text [108, 497, 664, 519]<|/det|>- I went to the market. I needed some oranges.
<|det|>text [144, 520, 763, 542]<|/det|>I went to the market because I needed some oranges.
<|det|>text [70, 566, 922, 613]<|/det|>C. Form complex sentences using the subordinating conjunctions given in the brackets. Identify the type of clause in each sentence.
<|det|>list [118, 619, 922, 858]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [118, 619, 885, 667]<|/det|>1. Certain deadly jellyfish sting human beings. The human beings can die. (when)
<|det|>text [118, 667, 920, 690]<|/det|>2. These tigers attack men. Their territories are encroached upon. (if)
<|det|>text [118, 690, 830, 739]<|/det|>3. To lift weights is safe enough. A good warm-up is advised. (although)
<|det|>text [118, 739, 880, 785]<|/det|>4. Daddy took everyone to the fair. He showed us a horror show. (where)
<|det|>text [118, 786, 830, 808]<|/det|>5. It was about two o'clock. We reached the summit. (when)
<|det|>text [118, 808, 922, 858]<|/det|>6. It is very warm and humid on the forest floor. Hardly any sunlight penetrates the canopy. (since)
<|det|>page_number [867, 924, 915, 943]<|/det|>193

<PAGE><|det|>text [145, 36, 815, 59]<|/det|>7. You will never get bored of it. The city is small. (though)
<|det|>text [145, 59, 871, 104]<|/det|>8. Kept in the trunk of the car are three suitcases. They are filled with jewellery and money. (which)
<|det|>text [145, 104, 850, 127]<|/det|>9. He lost his job. He had to quit his expensive habits. (when)
<|det|>text [136, 128, 930, 171]<|/det|>10. Sunil stayed at home and took care of their son. Shayasta worked at a call centre. (while)
<|det|>text [136, 172, 908, 217]<|/det|>11. We met the famous novelist. He had penned quite a number of best-sellers. (who)
<|det|>text [136, 218, 931, 263]<|/det|>12. We wanted to donate the money to the Prime Minister's National Relief Fund. We had collected the money. (which)
<|det|>text [136, 264, 870, 310]<|/det|>13. Why couldn't we get a simple job done on time? I could not understand. (why)
<|det|>text [136, 311, 790, 333]<|/det|>14. We went to the forest. The hermit lived there. (where)
<|det|>text [136, 334, 890, 381]<|/det|>15. The doctors were ready to help. We started organising the free eye check-up camp. (when)
<|det|>title [80, 402, 832, 426]<|/det|>D. Join each set of simple sentences into one complex sentence.
<|det|>text [145, 433, 630, 456]<|/det|>1. It is a secret. I cannot tell you anything.
<|det|>text [145, 457, 588, 479]<|/det|>2. The thief was caught. He stole cars.
<|det|>text [145, 480, 606, 501]<|/det|>3. One must be meticulous. I believe it.
<|det|>text [145, 502, 649, 525]<|/det|>4. I lost the letter. It was sent by my teacher.
<|det|>text [145, 525, 743, 547]<|/det|>5. He studied hard. He wanted to win a scholarship.
<|det|>text [145, 548, 708, 571]<|/det|>6. He paid the money to a clerk. He told me this.
<|det|>text [145, 571, 701, 593]<|/det|>7. I like to visit the zoo. I like observing animals.
<|det|>text [145, 594, 723, 617]<|/det|>8. He helped his mother. He loved her very much.
<|det|>text [145, 617, 631, 639]<|/det|>9. The lion is larger. The lioness is smaller.
<|det|>text [136, 640, 670, 663]<|/det|>10. He was a noted writer. He gave up writing.
<|det|>text [136, 663, 795, 686]<|/det|>11. You should always persevere. You want success in life.
<|det|>text [136, 686, 851, 709]<|/det|>12. I reached the platform. I saw a man. He looked suspicious.
<|det|>text [136, 709, 911, 755]<|/det|>13. The men carried the sacks to the market. They were very heavy. The market was very crowded.
<|det|>text [136, 756, 928, 802]<|/det|>14. He underwent training. It was a very rigorous one. He wanted to be a gymnast.
<|det|>text [136, 802, 938, 826]<|/det|>15. The man fell on the stairs. He hurt his right leg. It began to bleed.
<|det|>text [136, 826, 854, 874]<|/det|>16. Ashoka was the emperor of the Mauryan empire. He was a mighty warrior. He became an advocate of Buddhism.
<|det|>page_number [111, 927, 156, 946]<|/det|>194
<PAGE><|det|>title [54, 22, 490, 105]<|/det|>Transformaiton of
Sentences
<|det|>header [828, 24, 909, 66]<|/det|>24
<|det|>image [55, 136, 114, 183]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 145, 375, 167]<|/det|>Read these sentences.
<|det|>list [58, 185, 757, 256]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [58, 185, 628, 209]<|/det|>• The policeman must run fast to catch the thief.
<|det|>text [58, 210, 757, 233]<|/det|>- The policeman must run fast or he will not catch the thief.
<|det|>text [58, 234, 757, 256]<|/det|>- The policeman must run fast if he wants to catch the thief.
<|det|>text [55, 258, 823, 305]<|/det|>Did you notice that though these sentences have been expressed in different ways, the basic meaning has not changed?
<|det|>text [55, 337, 841, 386]<|/det|>Transformation is the process of changing the structure of a sentence without altering the meaning.
<|det|>title [57, 419, 335, 443]<|/det|>Simple to Compound
<|det|>text [55, 449, 895, 544]<|/det|>Look at the sentences given above once again. The first sentence is a simple sentence. A simple sentence can be transformed into a compound sentence by expanding a word or a phrase into a main clause using a coordinating conjunction. For example,
<|det|>list [61, 545, 755, 591]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [61, 545, 685, 568]<|/det|>• He must work hard to win the scholarship. (simple)
<|det|>text [98, 568, 753, 591]<|/det|>He must work hard and win the scholarship. (compound)
<|det|>text [55, 614, 785, 637]<|/det|>A. Convert these simple sentences into compound sentences.
<|det|>list [122, 642, 757, 856]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [123, 642, 406, 665]<|/det|>1. Being lazy, he failed.
<|det|>text [122, 666, 520, 689]<|/det|>2. Seeing his teacher, he stopped.
<|det|>text [122, 689, 757, 711]<|/det|>3. Wishing to build a school, he started to raise a fund.
<|det|>text [122, 713, 682, 735]<|/det|>4. He must leave immediately to catch the train.
<|det|>text [123, 737, 598, 759]<|/det|>5. Despite his wealth, he is not admired.
<|det|>text [122, 760, 880, 784]<|/det|>6. Besides helping me with money, he gave me a very warm coat.
<|det|>text [123, 785, 799, 808]<|/det|>7. Fearing suspension, the workers withdrew the agitation.
<|det|>text [122, 808, 884, 831]<|/det|>8. When the theft was discovered, the whole house was searched.
<|det|>text [123, 832, 684, 854]<|/det|>9. He must admit the truth to avoid punishment.
<|det|>text [117, 855, 862, 878]<|/det|>10. In spite of our very sincere efforts, we could not save the calf.
<|det|>page_number [848, 927, 892, 946]<|/det|>195
<PAGE><|det|>title [95, 31, 348, 55]<|/det|>Simple to Complex
<|det|>image [95, 60, 150, 109]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [154, 59, 938, 129]<|/det|>A simple sentence can be transformed into a complex sentence by expanding a word or a phrase into a subordinate clause, which could be a noun, an adjective or an adverb clause. For example,
<|det|>text [94, 130, 481, 152]<|/det|>- I saw a beautiful girl. (simple)
<|det|>text [130, 153, 612, 175]<|/det|>I saw a girl who was beautiful. (complex)
<|det|>image [65, 192, 121, 224]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [125, 198, 802, 223]<|/det|>Convert these simple sentences into complex sentences.
<|det|>list [153, 226, 567, 482]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [158, 229, 503, 252]<|/det|>1. We believe in his honesty.
<|det|>text [157, 254, 527, 276]<|/det|>2. The man confessed his guilt.
<|det|>text [158, 277, 532, 297]<|/det|>3. We returned home at sunset.
<|det|>text [158, 300, 563, 321]<|/det|>4. The storm abated around noon.
<|det|>text [158, 324, 529, 345]<|/det|>5. He is a man of many talents.
<|det|>text [158, 347, 543, 370]<|/det|>6. The poor deserve our support.
<|det|>text [158, 371, 627, 393]<|/det|>7. The news of his arrival pleased us all.
<|det|>text [158, 394, 639, 415]<|/det|>8. I know him to be a committed worker.
<|det|>text [158, 416, 717, 439]<|/det|>9. Our teacher will be our guide during the trip.
<|det|>text [148, 440, 935, 485]<|/det|>10. Despite a lot of difficulties, we succeeded in reaching the base camp for the final trek.
<|det|>title [93, 503, 530, 526]<|/det|>Complex to Simple or Compound
<|det|>image [95, 528, 150, 571]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [158, 531, 946, 578]<|/det|>We can convert a complex sentence into a simple or a compound sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [95, 579, 858, 601]<|/det|>- This boy, who is very talented, is also hard-working. (complex)
<|det|>text [130, 602, 722, 625]<|/det|>This boy is talented and very hardworking. (simple)
<|det|>text [130, 625, 944, 649]<|/det|>This boy is not only talented but also very hardworking. (compound)
<|det|>text [91, 664, 945, 734]<|/det|>In order to change a complex sentence into a simple sentence, we must remove the subordinate clause. We must ensure that there is only one verb in the new sentence framed.
<|det|>text [91, 749, 978, 821]<|/det|>When we have to transform a complex sentence into a compound sentence, we must change the subordinate clause into a coordinate clause using a suitable coordinate conjunction.
<|det|>image [65, 836, 123, 875]<|/det|>
<|det|>title [143, 844, 805, 868]<|/det|>Convert these complex sentences into simple sentences.
<|det|>list [141, 875, 670, 922]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [141, 875, 641, 898]<|/det|>1. He is a man who has no sense of pride.
<|det|>text [141, 899, 670, 922]<|/det|>2. When you reach home, you must call me.
<|det|>page_number [111, 925, 165, 946]<|/det|>196
<PAGE><|det|>list [93, 19, 777, 165]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 19, 531, 44]<|/det|>3. If he delays, he might miss the bus.
<|det|>text [93, 44, 635, 69]<|/det|>4. I saw a stag that had a beautiful silken coat.
<|det|>text [93, 69, 506, 93]<|/det|>5. He accepted what we proposed.
<|det|>text [93, 93, 558, 117]<|/det|>6. We exercise so that we may keep fit.
<|det|>text [93, 117, 650, 141]<|/det|>7. Suresh has a house that has been mortgaged.
<|det|>text [93, 141, 777, 166]<|/det|>8. Although I am not very fond of him, I gave him my pen.
<|det|>title [50, 187, 801, 211]<|/det|>D. Convert these complex sentences into compound sentences.
<|det|>list [93, 217, 687, 410]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [93, 217, 431, 241]<|/det|>1. I found the pen that I lost.
<|det|>text [93, 242, 617, 266]<|/det|>2. As I have fever, I cannot go to work today.
<|det|>text [93, 266, 686, 291]<|/det|>3. I will dismiss you if you do not admit your fault.
<|det|>text [93, 291, 643, 316]<|/det|>4. As the day was very hot, we were very tired.
<|det|>text [93, 316, 663, 338]<|/det|>5. Our picnic was cancelled, as it started to rain.
<|det|>text [93, 339, 690, 364]<|/det|>6. When the sun set, we started our return journey.
<|det|>text [93, 364, 643, 388]<|/det|>7. Although we were tired, we did not give up.
<|det|>text [93, 388, 621, 410]<|/det|>8. Although I was on time, I missed the train.
<|det|>title [52, 437, 485, 462]<|/det|>Compound to Simple or Complex
<|det|>text [52, 467, 845, 514]<|/det|>1. A compound sentence can also be converted into a simple or a complex sentence. For example,
<|det|>text [84, 515, 612, 538]<|/det|>He is old but he still works hard. (compound)
<|det|>text [84, 538, 625, 562]<|/det|>Despite being old, he still works hard. (simple)
<|det|>text [84, 562, 636, 586]<|/det|>Though he is old, he still works hard. (complex)
<|det|>text [58, 600, 905, 647]<|/det|>In order to transform a compound sentence into a simple sentence, we must 'replace one of the coordinate clauses and replace with a word or a phrase.
<|det|>text [54, 662, 855, 734]<|/det|>While transforming a compound sentence into a complex sentence, a subordinating clause must take the place of a coordinate clause and a subordinating conjunction must replace the coordinating conjunction.
<|det|>title [58, 755, 780, 780]<|/det|>E. Convert these compound sentences into simple sentences.
<|det|>list [99, 784, 860, 906]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [103, 785, 435, 810]<|/det|>1. He is rich but not happy.
<|det|>text [102, 810, 553, 831]<|/det|>2. I called out but he did not hear me.
<|det|>text [102, 833, 714, 856]<|/det|>3. He finished swimming and got dressed for dinner.
<|det|>text [102, 856, 858, 880]<|/det|>4. The Parliament passed the bill but the people were dissatisfied.
<|det|>text [103, 881, 734, 904]<|/det|>5. You must either pass this exam or leave the college.
<|det|>page_number [838, 931, 884, 950]<|/det|>197
<PAGE><|det|>list [153, 27, 930, 98]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [153, 27, 928, 52]<|/det|>6. They took every precaution but they could not prevent frost bite.
<|det|>text [154, 53, 885, 75]<|/det|>7. He studied very hard, yet he did not score well in the exams.
<|det|>text [154, 75, 847, 97]<|/det|>8. The boy had never been to school, and he could not read.
<|det|>title [100, 118, 846, 143]<|/det|>F Correct these compound sentences into complex sentences.
<|det|>list [153, 148, 952, 335]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [154, 148, 633, 171]<|/det|>1. He saw the snake, but he did not stop.
<|det|>text [153, 171, 710, 193]<|/det|>2. I received the message and I left immediately.
<|det|>text [153, 194, 830, 216]<|/det|>3. Many diseases are caused by bacteria and we know this.
<|det|>text [153, 218, 800, 239]<|/det|>4. You must wear warm clothes or you will catch a cold.
<|det|>text [153, 241, 827, 263]<|/det|>5. They tried to bribe the official, but they did not succeed.
<|det|>text [153, 264, 781, 286]<|/det|>6. The house was burnt down, but no one was injured.
<|det|>text [153, 288, 736, 309]<|/det|>7. He may be at office and I might meet him there.
<|det|>text [153, 310, 951, 334]<|/det|>8. He wishes to secure his position, yet he is not working hard enough.
<|det|>title [100, 360, 575, 382]<|/det|>G. Rewrite these sentences as directed.
<|det|>list [171, 389, 960, 884]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [172, 389, 854, 435]<|/det|>1. But for your temper, we would have secured the contract. (Rewrite using if.)
<|det|>text [171, 435, 931, 482]<|/det|>2. Though the men had lost their leader, they did not abandon the mission. (Rewrite without the subordinate clause.)
<|det|>text [171, 482, 926, 529]<|/det|>3. He has lost all his money, but he has no regrets. (Rewrite using although.)
<|det|>text [171, 529, 915, 573]<|/det|>4. As soon as I had seated myself, the bell rang. (Use no sooner... than.)
<|det|>text [171, 574, 960, 598]<|/det|>5. The team accepted my proposal. (Use the verb form of proposal.)
<|det|>text [171, 599, 926, 643]<|/det|>6. He is too arrogant to listen to advice. (Rewrite using an adverb clause of reason.)
<|det|>text [171, 645, 951, 692]<|/det|>7. I endured the hardship because I had no choice. (Rewrite as two principal clauses.)
<|det|>text [171, 692, 928, 738]<|/det|>8. The young boy entered the hall with much hesitation. (Use the verb form of hesitation.)
<|det|>text [171, 739, 898, 785]<|/det|>9. There was a young architect in our office when the accident occurred. (Begin with At the time.)
<|det|>text [154, 787, 758, 833]<|/det|>10. She laid the table after she had finished cooking.
(Begin: When she...)
<|det|>text [154, 834, 938, 883]<|/det|>11. Mr Roy is correcting the answer scripts of the last examination. (Begin: The answer scripts...)
<|det|>page_number [124, 927, 172, 946]<|/det|>198
<PAGE><|det|>text [125, 35, 856, 84]<|/det|>12. In May I will be going to London with Ma, Baba and Shyam. (Use the word accompanied and rewrite)
<|det|>text [130, 84, 794, 129]<|/det|>13. The weather in Jaipur is much better than it is at home.
(Begin: At home...)
<|det|>text [132, 130, 829, 176]<|/det|>14. She is learning Chinese in order to communicate with her Chinese clients. (Use the word purpose and rewrite)
<|det|>text [133, 177, 821, 223]<|/det|>15. He did not understand fully what he was supposed to do.
(Use the word lacked and rewrite)
<|det|>text [133, 224, 874, 270]<|/det|>16. The child is so naughty that he cannot be left alone even for a minute. (Begin: It is not advisable...)
<|det|>text [133, 271, 710, 318]<|/det|>17. Mr Shetty was mild mannered and humble too.
(Use not only and but also)
<|det|>text [133, 318, 880, 343]<|/det|>18. On being scolded, the pupil started crying. (Begin with when.)
<|det|>image [425, 402, 583, 590]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_number [851, 924, 895, 940]<|/det|>199
<PAGE><|det|>header [0, 0, 182, 117]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [519, 31, 944, 69]<|/det|>Narrative Writing
<|det|>image [78, 130, 141, 179]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [154, 131, 902, 154]<|/det|>A short story is a powerful form of fiction that can leave a strong
<|det|>text [154, 155, 867, 176]<|/det|>impression on the reader even though the writer works with a
<|det|>text [78, 177, 951, 222]<|/det|>limited number of words. There are no set guidelines to being a successful writer, but it helps to keep a few points in mind when writing a short story.
<|det|>text [79, 222, 216, 243]<|/det|>Purpose
<|det|>text [119, 245, 393, 265]<|/det|>to entertain and enthral
<|det|>text [79, 268, 235, 289]<|/det|>Structure
<|det|>list [121, 291, 725, 430]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 291, 657, 315]<|/det|>• a lively opening that will engage the reader
<|det|>text [121, 315, 725, 338]<|/det|>• clear story structure—main characters and setting
<|det|>text [122, 339, 502, 361]<|/det|>• a complication/turning points
<|det|>text [122, 362, 343, 383]<|/det|>• resulting events
<|det|>text [122, 384, 509, 405]<|/det|>• resolution of the complication
<|det|>text [122, 407, 631, 430]<|/det|>• ending has to be linked with the opening
<|det|>text [80, 431, 338, 453]<|/det|>◆ Language features
<|det|>list [121, 454, 907, 638]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [121, 454, 582, 476]<|/det|>• written in first person or third person
<|det|>text [122, 478, 395, 498]<|/det|>• written in past tense
<|det|>text [122, 500, 663, 523]<|/det|>• ideas organised into proper, clear sentences
<|det|>text [121, 524, 907, 590]<|/det|>- a variety of sentence structures (direct speech, passive voice, simple, compound and complex sentences, etc.) to maintain the reader's interest
<|det|>text [121, 592, 725, 616]<|/det|>• largely chronological but may contain flashbacks
<|det|>text [122, 617, 406, 638]<|/det|>• distinctive characters
<|det|>text [80, 663, 759, 688]<|/det|>Now, attempt writing short stories along these guidelines.
<|det|>list [79, 694, 957, 890]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [80, 694, 956, 740]<|/det|>1. Write an original short story to bring out the meaning of the proverb—Blood is thicker than water.
<|det|>text [79, 742, 935, 789]<|/det|>2. Write an original short story which has as its theme—Women can be every bit as brave as men.
<|det|>text [79, 790, 901, 839]<|/det|>3. Write an original short story beginning with, 'I never expected this from you...'
<|det|>text [79, 839, 917, 889]<|/det|>4. Write an original short story where a beggar, a dog and a baby play important roles.
<|det|>page_number [98, 927, 152, 946]<|/det|>200
<PAGE><|det|>list [80, 29, 885, 270]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [81, 30, 762, 56]<|/det|>5. Write an original short story entitled, 'Winds of Change'.
<|det|>text [81, 56, 867, 101]<|/det|>6. Write an original short story concluding with the following line—'I just didn't know what to say!'
<|det|>text [81, 101, 879, 148]<|/det|>- As the sun rose on the changed landscape...'—complete the short story.
<|det|>text [81, 148, 854, 173]<|/det|>8. Write an original short story entitled, 'The Forbidden Cupboard'.
<|det|>text [81, 173, 898, 221]<|/det|>9. Write an original short story ending with the line—'And then I woke up.'
<|det|>text [70, 221, 900, 270]<|/det|>10. Write an original short story where a clown, a doctor and an asylum play important roles.
<|det|>page_number [857, 925, 904, 945]<|/det|>201
<PAGE><|det|>header [84, 33, 287, 121]<|/det|>[Non-Text]
<|det|>header [671, 38, 951, 78]<|/det|>Vocabulary
<|det|>title [120, 140, 912, 171]<|/det|>Same Word Used as Different Parts of Speech
<|det|>text [82, 183, 856, 231]<|/det|>Here are some sentences using the same words in different parts of speech—
<|det|>list [85, 243, 856, 325]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [85, 243, 856, 265]<|/det|>• This small table will break under the weight of that trunk. (verb)
<|det|>text [126, 266, 815, 289]<|/det|>The children have a break between classes at school. (noun)
<|det|>text [85, 304, 582, 326]<|/det|>• He drank two cans of fruit juice. (noun)
<|det|>text [126, 327, 532, 350]<|/det|>You can do it if you try hard. (verb)
<|det|>text [85, 363, 530, 386]<|/det|>• He braked the car suddenly. (verb)
<|det|>text [126, 387, 655, 409]<|/det|>She tried to slam the brakes, but failed. (noun)
<|det|>text [85, 424, 826, 469]<|/det|>- He doesn't want more money; he's content with what he has. (adjective)
<|det|>text [125, 470, 802, 516]<|/det|>You are on a holiday—you can lie in the sun to your heart's content. (noun)
<|det|>text [84, 529, 829, 577]<|/det|>- Why did you desert us when we needed you the most? (verb) Life in a desert isn't easy. (noun)
<|det|>text [84, 590, 657, 613]<|/det|>• Each of the men received a reward (pronoun).
<|det|>text [125, 614, 777, 636]<|/det|>I bought these mangoes for three rupees each. (pronoun)
<|det|>text [125, 637, 701, 659]<|/det|>Each boy was given a suitcase to carry (adjective).
<|det|>text [125, 660, 774, 684]<|/det|>I was away seven days and it rained each day. (adjective)
<|det|>text [82, 698, 676, 721]<|/det|>• A rabbit is usually safe in its burrow. (adjective)
<|det|>text [122, 722, 907, 746]<|/det|>There is a small safe hidden behind the picture on that wall. (noun)
<|det|>text [82, 760, 661, 783]<|/det|>• We had a sail up the coast in his yacht (noun).
<|det|>text [122, 784, 615, 806]<|/det|>Windmills have four different sails. (noun)
<|det|>text [122, 807, 769, 831]<|/det|>Have you ever sailed through the Mediterranean? (verb)
<|det|>text [82, 845, 654, 869]<|/det|>• There has been a drop in temperature. (noun)
<|det|>text [122, 870, 783, 894]<|/det|>I'll just drop a note to my friend while I'm waiting. (verb)
<|det|>page_number [102, 928, 152, 947]<|/det|>202
<PAGE><|det|>text [87, 40, 657, 64]<|/det|>The picture is hanging on the wall. (preposition)
<|det|>text [89, 65, 580, 87]<|/det|>They heaped insults on him. (preposition)
<|det|>text [90, 88, 384, 109]<|/det|>They moved on. (adverb)
<|det|>text [90, 110, 487, 133]<|/det|>Is the party on tonight? (adjective)
<|det|>list [53, 147, 834, 230]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [53, 147, 525, 170]<|/det|>• He played a nasty trick on me. (verb)
<|det|>text [90, 170, 834, 194]<|/det|>At the start of today's play, England scored an easy goal. (noun)
<|det|>text [53, 207, 592, 230]<|/det|>- I hurt myself, but it's only a scratch. (noun)
<|det|>text [90, 230, 526, 254]<|/det|>How did you scratch your leg? (verb)
<|det|>text [53, 268, 801, 291]<|/det|>- It was nearly dawn and the light was getting stronger. (noun)
<|det|>text [90, 291, 636, 314]<|/det|>It was a light meal, but quite filling. (adjective)
<|det|>text [90, 314, 688, 338]<|/det|>When she smiles, it lights up her entire face. (verb)
<|det|>text [53, 352, 585, 375]<|/det|>• I think you need to pepper the soup.(verb)
<|det|>text [90, 376, 596, 398]<|/det|>The soup has too much pepper in it. (noun)
<|det|>text [53, 411, 691, 434]<|/det|>- Tennis players usually have strong forearms. (noun)
<|det|>text [90, 435, 834, 457]<|/det|>He knew he would be questioned, so he was forearmed. (verb)
<|det|>text [53, 471, 585, 494]<|/det|>• I always got good grades at school. (noun)
<|det|>text [90, 494, 633, 517]<|/det|>The teacher graded the papers carefully. (verb)
<|det|>text [53, 531, 537, 553]<|/det|>• They laid flowers on the grave. (noun)
<|det|>text [90, 553, 734, 576]<|/det|>There are some grave decisions to be made. (adjective)
<|det|>text [53, 589, 793, 612]<|/det|>- I'll hide the children's presents behind the book shelf. (verb)
<|det|>text [90, 612, 636, 636]<|/det|>Shoes are usually made of animal hide. (noun)
<|det|>text [53, 650, 583, 673]<|/det|>• A cup of tea will soon perk you up. (verb)
<|det|>text [90, 673, 896, 697]<|/det|>One of the perks in this job is that the company gives you a flat. (noun)
<|det|>title [396, 719, 569, 745]<|/det|>Proverbs
<|det|>text [52, 762, 856, 856]<|/det|>A proverb is a short popular saying, usually of unknown and ancient origin, that effectively expresses some commonplace truth or useful thought. Read the proverbs listed on the next page and pay special attention to the meanings given.
<|det|>page_number [848, 927, 896, 946]<|/det|>203
<PAGE><|det|>table [85, 33, 957, 870]<|/det|><table><tr><td>where there&#x27;s a will, there&#x27;s a way</td><td>when a person really wants to do something, he will find a way of doing it</td></tr><tr><td>a friend in need is a friend indeed</td><td>a friend who helps when one is in trouble is a real friend</td></tr><tr><td>discretion is the better part of valour</td><td>avoiding a dangerous or unpleasant situation is sometimes the most sensible thing to do</td></tr><tr><td>empty vessels make the most noise</td><td>those people who have little knowledge of something usually talk the most and make a lot of fuss</td></tr><tr><td>an idle brain is the devil&#x27;s workshop</td><td>one who has nothing to do will be tempted to do many mischievous acts</td></tr><tr><td>a penny saved is a penny gained</td><td>by being thrifty, one will be able to save up</td></tr><tr><td>a rolling stone gathers no moss</td><td>a person who never settles in one place, who often changes his job, has little money but also has no responsibilities</td></tr><tr><td>as you sow, so you shall reap</td><td>one will either enjoy or suffer the consequences of his earlier actions or inactions</td></tr><tr><td>barking dogs seldom bite</td><td>those who make loud threats seldom ch攻击 them out</td></tr><tr><td>a bird in hand is worth two in the bush</td><td>something that one already has is better than going after something seemingly more worthwhile that one may not be able to get</td></tr><tr><td>birds of a feather flock together</td><td>people who have the same sort of character or belief tend to spend time together</td></tr><tr><td>all that glitters is not gold</td><td>not everything that appears to be appealing or good is actually so</td></tr></table>
<|det|>page_number [105, 933, 152, 950]<|/det|>204
<PAGE><|det|>text [84, 33, 350, 84]<|/det|>every cloud has a silver lining
<|det|>text [84, 135, 350, 180]<|/det|>everyone can find fault,
few can do better
<|det|>text [84, 215, 345, 263]<|/det|>fortune knocks once at every man's door
<|det|>text [84, 271, 301, 319]<|/det|>he laughs best who
laughs last
<|det|>text [84, 363, 361, 386]<|/det|>great haste makes great
<|det|>text [85, 394, 177, 416]<|/det|>waste
<|det|>text [85, 428, 331, 476]<|/det|>all work and no play
makes Jack a dull boy
<|det|>text [84, 507, 361, 552]<|/det|>the pot calling the kettle
black
<|det|>text [85, 563, 370, 608]<|/det|>when in Rome, do as the Romans do
<|det|>text [84, 643, 372, 692]<|/det|>when the cat is away, the mice will play
<|det|>text [84, 699, 372, 747]<|/det|>the grass is always greener on the other side
<|det|>text [84, 755, 350, 801]<|/det|>a bad workman always blames his tools
<|det|>text [84, 811, 370, 857]<|/det|>don't judge a book by its cover
<|det|>text [395, 33, 885, 126]<|/det|>every sad or unpleasant situation has something hopeful about it; silver lining refers to something positive that comes out of a sad or unpleasant situation
<|det|>text [395, 135, 895, 207]<|/det|>it is easier to find fault in other people's actions or methods than to do it properly or correctly
<|det|>text [395, 215, 792, 261]<|/det|>everyone has the opportunity to be successful in life
<|det|>text [395, 271, 834, 364]<|/det|>do not express your joy or pride too soon because someone else might end up being more successful than you in the end
<|det|>text [395, 373, 895, 418]<|/det|>if one does things hastily he will make a lot of mistakes
<|det|>text [395, 429, 866, 498]<|/det|>it is not healthy to spend all your time working, it is necessary to relax and have fun as well
<|det|>text [395, 508, 864, 556]<|/det|>do not criticise another person for a fault that you may have yourself
<|det|>text [395, 564, 890, 631]<|/det|>when one is in a new place, country or situation, he must adapt himself to the new manners and customs
<|det|>text [395, 642, 880, 691]<|/det|>when law enforcers are not present, some people will take advantage of the situation
<|det|>text [395, 699, 856, 742]<|/det|>another person's life always looks better to us
<|det|>text [395, 755, 880, 801]<|/det|>a careless or unskilled person blames bad work on his tools rather than himself
<|det|>text [395, 810, 808, 836]<|/det|>do not be deceived by appearances

<PAGE><|det|>table [92, 33, 949, 901]<|/det|><table><tr><td>a leopard cannot change its spots</td><td>a person&#x27;s nature cannot change</td></tr><tr><td>too many cooks spoil the broth</td><td>too many people doing the same thing at the same time will not be successful</td></tr><tr><td>we never miss the water till the well runs dry</td><td>you only realise the importance of something when it is gone</td></tr><tr><td>you can&#x27;t have your cake and eat it too</td><td>you must choose between two things as it is impossible to have both at the same time</td></tr><tr><td>as you make your bed, so must you lie on it</td><td>you must accept the consequences of your actions</td></tr><tr><td>cut your coat according to your cloth</td><td>live within your income; don&#x27;t be too ambitious in your plans</td></tr><tr><td>an apple a day keeps the doctor away</td><td>it means that apples are so nutritious they keep you in the peak of health</td></tr><tr><td>he who pays the piper calls the tune</td><td>the person who pays for something has the right to say how it should be spent</td></tr><tr><td>you can lead a horse to water but you can&#x27;t make him drink</td><td>we can help, show or encourage someone to do something but we can&#x27;t make him do what he is unwilling or unable to do</td></tr><tr><td>he who keeps company with wolves, will learn to howl</td><td>if we associate with bad companions, we, too, will become bad</td></tr><tr><td>prosperity makes friends, adversity tries them</td><td>if you are rich and successful, you will attract many friends, but if you should suffer hardship or adversity, your false friends will quickly depart</td></tr><tr><td>kindle not a fire you cannot put out</td><td>do not start something you cannot control or resolve</td></tr><tr><td>do unto others as you would others do unto you</td><td>treat others as you would like them to treat you</td></tr><tr><td>fools rush in where angels fear to tread</td><td>foolish people tend to act hastily and do things that wise people would avoid</td></tr></table>

<|det|>title [305, 303, 700, 335]<|/det|>Words Easily Confused
<|det|>list [88, 346, 891, 442]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [88, 346, 802, 393]<|/det|>- ambiguous (not very clear): The prisoner's statement is rather ambiguous in places.
<|det|>text [88, 394, 890, 442]<|/det|>- ambivalent (having a mixed feeling): After what he has done, I was a little ambivalent as to how I should behave.
<|det|>text [88, 466, 847, 512]<|/det|>- appraise (decide the quality or value): It was difficult to appraise her suitability for the job.
<|det|>text [88, 513, 842, 558]<|/det|>- apprise (inform): Have they apprised you of the changes in their circumstances?
<|det|>text [88, 585, 794, 632]<|/det|>- censor (examine and remove anything that is considered offensive): His latest novel was censored by the government.
<|det|>text [88, 633, 855, 681]<|/det|>- censure (officially criticise): The members of the committee were censured for having accepted a bribe.
<|det|>text [120, 705, 875, 754]<|/det|>definite (clearly; fixed): She said that she would give me a definite answer by tomorrow.
<|det|>text [88, 755, 902, 802]<|/det|>- definitive (final; decisive): He thinks that he has written the definitive book on Mother Teresa.
<|det|>text [78, 829, 885, 854]<|/det|>- decent (fairly good): She is being very decent about the whole affair.
<|det|>text [78, 855, 881, 903]<|/det|>- dissent (disagree): I'm afraid this is where I dissent from the general opinion.
<|det|>page_number [854, 928, 901, 948]<|/det|>207
<PAGE><|det|>list [88, 40, 951, 621]<|/det|>
<|det|>text [88, 40, 911, 87]<|/det|>- discreet (cautious; wise): He is very discreet and will never let the secret out.
<|det|>text [88, 88, 951, 135]<|/det|>- discrete (separate and distinct): There are four discrete groups within the class.
<|det|>text [88, 159, 972, 183]<|/det|>- notable (important): There were several notable people at the meeting.
<|det|>text [88, 184, 927, 229]<|/det|>- noticeable (easy to notice): There is a slight stain on the table cloth, but it's hardly noticeable.
<|det|>text [88, 252, 895, 300]<|/det|>- official (approved of or done by someone in authority): You have to get official permission to build a house.
<|det|>text [88, 300, 955, 344]<|/det|>- officious (too eager to boss people): His mother-in-law is so officious that he does not let her visit his house.
<|det|>text [88, 369, 875, 416]<|/det|>- statue (an image made in stone or metal): She stood as still as a statue.
<|det|>text [88, 416, 885, 461]<|/det|>- stature (the degree to which someone is admired): As a scientist, he is a man of international stature.
<|det|>text [88, 461, 873, 507]<|/det|>- statute (a law that has been passed by a parliament or council): You are required by statute to do this.
<|det|>text [88, 529, 911, 574]<|/det|>- vociferous (having loud and strongly expressed opinions): She was vociferous in her criticism of him.
<|det|>text [88, 574, 958, 619]<|/det|>- voracious (eating or wanting large quantities of food): The appetite of most children seems voracious.
